### sound, Krishna, presence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NSljLqQXZc4?t=550
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is fully present and it is Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead with all his features. He is fully present in the sound."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the material understanding of sound with the transcendental nature of the holy name. He uses the example of the word 'table' to illustrate how material sounds are mere references, while the sound of 'Hare Krishna' contains the full presence of Krishna.

So the revelation by the Lord results in that close relationship of love being revived. It is there. It just has to be revived. And Srila Prabhupada says that the sound form of the Lord is completely the Lord himself. It is not just a reference. Just like when I say table, I have a table in front of me. I say table. The sound table communicates to you some object called table. But actually the sound doesn't completely represent the table. You see. Simply by hearing you don't experience what is table. You may ask questions. What is the table made of? How big is the table? Or is it an old table or a new table? So many questions can be asked. Is it your table or somebody else's table? So many questions can be asked. But when we say Hare Krishna, Krishna is fully present and it is Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead with all his features. He is fully present in the sound. So this is a completely different concept for us who are only having material experience of an object or a person and his name being two different things.

---
### name, form, personality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NSljLqQXZc4?t=632
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna means the entire personality. Unlimited personality is there in that sound Krishna, two syllables."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Shakespeare's idea that a rose by any other name would smell as sweet with the concept of Krishna's name. He explains that while material names are arbitrary, Krishna's name embodies His entire unlimited personality, including His forms, qualities, activities, devotees, abode, and energies.

Just like the great poet Shakespeare. He said something like the rose called by any other name would smell as sweet. Of course when we say rose, we understand it's that fragrant beautiful particular flower. But he says what's in a name? That is true in this material because even take the example of the same substance water referred to in different languages by different words. Hindi they say Paani. In Sanskrit they say Jalam. In Kannada we say Neeru. So the same thing, same object water is referred to by different names. Now it is not like that with the Supreme Lord that it's simply a reference. Krishna is not just a reference. Krishna means the entire personality. Unlimited personality is there in that sound Krishna, two syllables. Krish na, two syllables. In that unlimited personality of Krishna is there. Krishna with his names, with his forms, with his qualities, with his activities, with his devotees, pure devotees, with his associates, with his abode, with his paraphernalia, with his energies, everything is just two syllables within this two syllables Krishna.

---
### holy name, purity, material misconceptions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NSljLqQXZc4?t=753
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the power of the holy name and quotes Srila Prabhupada, stating that it is a perfect method for approaching the Lord. He explains that pure contact with the Lord is possible when avoiding offenses against the holy name, which are material misconceptions about the Lord. He states that the Lord wants us to leave material existence and return to His personal abode.

So this sound is very very powerful, as powerful as Krishna. So therefore Srila Prabhupada says here this method is a completely perfect method for approaching the Lord by vibrating the tongue to produce the name, holy name of the Supreme Lord. And by such pure contact with the Lord, see the purity of our uttering the holy name is possible only when we avoid this against the holy name that is these are all offense means material misconceptions about the Lord. What these material misconceptions are I will explain in another different session. I want to have a separate session on that. Now I don't have time. So when the contact with the Lord through this material platform is completely pure, no material misconceptions then the devotee can rise above the material platform and understand the inner meaning of the Vedic literatures including the Lord's existence of the material world. He is the controller of the material world. It is his but he has no other personal interest. He rather wants us to get out of this material existence and go to him in his personal abode in his spiritual holy name. That is the essence of what is explained here.

### Srimad Bhagavatam, Narada's instructions, Vyasadeva
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UTf-Y7PV5jk?t=79
**Verse References:** SB 1.5.40
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So this verse, spoken by Narada Muni to Vyasadeva is the conclusion of this chapter."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of the lecture, focusing on verse 40 of the 5th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, which is Narada Muni's instruction to Vyasadeva to compile Srimad Bhagavatam.

In this session we will be discussing from the 40th verse of the 5th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This is the last verse in this chapter. Tvam Apya Tvam Api Adabhra Shruta Vishrutam Vibho Samapyate Yena Vidam Bubutsitam Prachyahi Dukhayar Muhur Ardhitaatmanam Sanklesh Nirvanam Ushantinanyatha. The meaning of this verse is, please listen. Please, therefore, describe the Almighty Lord's activities which you have learned by your vast knowledge of the Vedas. For that will satisfy the hankerings of great learned men and at the same time mitigate the miseries of the masses of common people who are always suffering from material pangs. Indeed, there is no other way to get out of such miseries. So this verse, spoken by Narada Muni to Vyasadeva is the conclusion of this chapter. The chapter title is Narada's Instructions on Srimad Bhagavatam. Narada Muni has sufficiently justified why Vyasadeva should compile Srimad Bhagavatam.

---
### Absolute Truth, Krishna's activities, Bhagavan

Why? It is clear. It is clear. It is clear. He has revealed exclusive literature describing the glories of Krishna, especially Krishna's activities. The conception of the Absolute Truth as the Supreme Person is important to understand that person means activities, impersonal there is no activity, impersonal there are no qualities, impersonal there are no names, forms, there is no paraphernalia, there is no variety, there is no variegatedness. All this variety. Names, forms, qualities, pastimes, activities, everything, paraphernalia, devotees, relationships, everything is possible only if we try to understand the Absolute Truth's feature as the Supreme Person, Bhagavan. That's why Bhagavata. So here Narada Muni's conclusion is that. This is the conclusion that for helping people identify suffering of material места, the easiest way is to provide the transcendental glow, the transcendental glories of the Supreme Lord's activities. His activities are extraordinary.

### good men, devotion, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UTf-Y7PV5jk?t=289
**Verse References:** BG 7.16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The four classes of good men acknowledge the authority of the Almighty God, of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Srila Prabhupada's perspective on Narada Muni's instructions to Vyasadeva, focusing on the four classes of good men who acknowledge the authority of the Supreme Lord and intuitively take shelter of Him.

So, that is Narada Muni's instruction to Vyasa Deva. Now, Srila Prabhupada very nicely explains in the purport that Narada Muni is instructing Vyasa Deva to compile the Bhagavatam for the good of all classes of people of the entire world. So, Srila Prabhupada says, according to the Bhagavad Gita analysis, there are four classes of good men and four classes of bad men also in this world. Who are the four classes of good men? First of all, the description of the four classes of good men. The four classes of good men acknowledge the authority of the Almighty God, of the Supreme Lord. And therefore, such good men fall under four classes. The first class of good men are those who are in difficulty. The second class of good men are those who are in need of money. The third class of good men are those who are advanced in knowledge. And the fourth class of good men are those who are inquisitive to know more and more about God. All these four classes of men intuitively take shelter of the Lord because they acknowledge the Lord. They acknowledge the authority of the Almighty Supreme Lord.

---
### Vyasadeva, Vedic knowledge, satisfaction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UTf-Y7PV5jk?t=400
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But even that vast learning, if it is not utilized for specifically describing the transcendental activities of the personality of Godhead, until then, he can have full satisfaction in the heart, including those who are learned in the Vedas."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Narada's instruction to Vyasadeva, emphasizing that even with vast Vedic knowledge, one cannot achieve full satisfaction without describing the transcendental activities of the Supreme Lord.

Now, Narada is instructing Vyasa Deva to broadcast the transcendental knowledge of God in terms of the vast Vedic knowledge which he has already attained. If you remember, at the beginning of the fourth chapter, it was described Vyasa Deva, he first of all put the Vedas in writing and he divided the Vedas into four. And then he further summarized the Vedas. He explained the Vedas for less intelligent class of people in an easily understandable way by presenting the Mahabharata. Like that, so much work he did, but he is still feeling a vacuum in his heart. So, he is vastly learned. But even that vast learning, if it is not utilized for specifically describing the transcendental activities of the personality of Godhead, until then, he can have full satisfaction in the heart, including those who are learned in the Vedas. Just like Vyasa Deva himself, he is not feeling satisfaction in his heart. He is fully learned in the Vedas. Because he has not described the transcendental glories of the activities of the Supreme Lord.

---
### bad men, fruitive work, sinful activities, atheism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UTf-Y7PV5jk?t=507
**Verse References:** BG 7.15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The first category of bad men that are described, those who are simply addicted to the mode of progressive fruity work."
**Summary:** The speaker transitions to describing the four classes of bad men, starting with those addicted to fruitive work and progressing to those engaged in sinful activities, those materially advanced but lacking acknowledgment of God, and finally, atheists who hate the very name of God.

Now, as far as the four classes of bad men are concerned, they are also described in the Bhagavad Gita. The first category of bad men that are described, those who are simply addicted to the mode of progressive fruity work. Fruity work means they have to do some work in which they get some results for their material enjoyment, material pleasure, material gain. That is called fruity work. So, there are people who are addicted to progressive fruity work and they keep on doing this. And as a result, they are subjected to the accompanying miseries. Nothing in this material world, any material work done, is always accompanied by miseries. You cannot say, I will do something and I want only pleasure. No, not possible. Always there is pleasure and pain. Or enjoyment and suffering. Sukha and dukkha are two sides of the same coin. Two sides of the same coin. Material activity, fructive activity. So, this is the first class of bad men. The second class of bad men are those who are simply addicted to vicious work, sinful activities. For what? For sense gratification, for sense satisfaction. And naturally they suffer the consequences because they are outright engaging in sinful activity. They have to suffer. Sooner or later, they have to suffer. And the third class of bad men are those who are materially advanced in knowledge but they suffer. In spite of being advanced in knowledge, they suffer because they do not have the sense to acknowledge the authority of the Almighty Supreme Lord. So, what happens? The effect of their knowledge is stolen away by the illusory energy. Māyayā pahṛtaṁ jñānam. And the fourth class of bad men, those who are known as atheists and who therefore purposely hate the very name of God, although they are always in difficulty,

---
### atheism, misconceptions, devotee's vision
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UTf-Y7PV5jk?t=660
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A pure devotee is not thinking, let me try to do good to some good people. No. He is thinking of doing good to all suffering people, all types of people who are suffering material miseries, whether they are good men or bad men."
**Summary:** The speaker continues describing atheists and their misconceptions about God, then highlights the vision of a pure devotee who aims to benefit all suffering people, regardless of their classification as good or bad.

it is natural for somebody who is in difficulty to take shelter of God. But these people, because they have developed a hatred for God with a misunderstanding that God is their enemy, or God is against them, or God is not in their favour, or God is partial to somebody who is their enemy, all kinds of misconceptions, wrong conceptions, misunderstanding. So, they are always in difficulty because they engage in sinful activities one after another, one after another, and they continuously suffer. Still, they will never surrender to God. So, Nārada is advising Vyāsadeva, you please describe the glories of the Supreme Lord to do good to all eight classes of men, both good and bad. See, this is the vision of the Āchārya. This is the vision of the pure devotee. A pure devotee is not thinking, let me try to do good to some good people. No. He is thinking of doing good to all suffering people, all types of people who are suffering material miseries, whether they are good men or bad men. So, please understand, in this world, other than devotees of the Supreme Lord, everyone else can be classified into these eight classes of good and bad men. This is the analysis of the Gita.

---
### Srimad Bhagavatam, transcendental activities, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UTf-Y7PV5jk?t=777
**Verse References:** BG 7.16, BG 7.15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Simply by hearing Bhagavatam, the extraordinary activities of the transcendental Supreme Lord, one can become free from all material miseries, one can become peaceful in the mind, one can become situated transcendentally above the three gunas, no more victimized by the three modes."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing that Srimad Bhagavatam is meant for any sincere soul seeking welfare and peace of mind, and that hearing the transcendental activities of the Supreme Lord can liberate one from material miseries and elevate them beyond the three modes of nature.

Chaturvidhā bhajante mām janā sukṛtino arjuna and namām duṣkṛtino mūḍhā prapadyante narādhamā māyayā pahṛta-jñāna āsuraṁ bhāvam āśritam. So, if people are not devotees of the Supreme Lord in this world, then they are either fruitive workers who perform pious activities or they are sinful people who simply don't care for any pious activities and they simply do all kinds of sinful activities. So, for doing good to all eight classes of men, Narada is advising Vyasa Deva kindly compile the Bhagavatam, in which you exclusively describe the transcendental glories of the Supreme Lord, especially by describing His extraordinary activity. So, the conclusion is, Srimad Bhagavatam is therefore not meant for any particular class of men or any particular section of society. No. It is meant for the sincere soul who actually wants his own welfare and peace of mind. Simply by hearing Bhagavatam, the extraordinary activities of the transcendental Supreme Lord, one can become free from all material miseries, one can become peaceful in the mind, one can become situated transcendentally above the three gunas, no more victimized by the three modes. So, that is what is the Bhagavatam. Hare Krishna. I will stop here. Thank you very much.

---

That is exactly what Vyāsadeva wants to understand in the life of Nārada, from the previous life, previous birth, to the present life, present birth. Because, Srila Prabhupāda says, he wanted to follow in Nārada's footsteps in order to attain to the same perfect stage of life. Nārada has achieved the perfection of spiritual life. So, Vyāsadeva is enquiring from Nārada, what did you do after initiation, exactly, before you got your present spiritual body? This desire to enquire from the spiritual master is an essential factor to the progressive path. This process is technically known as Saddharmaprcchā.

This is described, Prabhupāda is describing from the Nectar of Devotion, where Srila Rupa Goswami explains what are the steps to be taken in executing Sādhanabhakti. For a beginner, in practice of pure devotional service, under the guidance of a bona fide spiritual master, one of the steps is that one has to enquire from the spiritual master. Make proper enquiries. In the Bhagavad Gita also, this is told, tad viddhi pranipātena paripraśnena sevaya upadekṣanti te jñānam jñāninastatva-darśinaḥ. Approach a bona fide spiritual master, surrender to him, pranipātena, and enquire from him, paripraśnena, but in what mood the enquiry should be done, sevaya, in a humble mood, by rendering proper service to the spiritual master. Enquiries with submission and service attitude is required from the spiritual master.

### Narada, spiritual body, transformation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xGrnroIA8r4?t=665
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "How did you pass your life after initiation and how did you attain this body having quit your old one in due course?"
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the third verse, explaining Vyasadeva's inquiry about how Narada passed his life after initiation and how he attained his current spiritual body, given his previous birth as the son of a maidservant.

In the third verse of this chapter, Vyāsadeva further enquires from Nārada, svāyambhuva kaya vṛtya vartitam te param vayah katham chedam udāśrākṣiḥ kāle prāpte kalevaram. The meaning of this verse is O son of Brahma, Nārada is taken birth in this life as a son of Brahma. That's why he is called svāyambhuva, son of svayambhu, son of Brahma. How did you pass your life after initiation and how did you attain this body having quit your old one in due course? See, how did this change of body, this change of body take place? What happened? That you are born in the previous life as a son of an ordinary maid servant. Okay, you executed spiritual life but how did the change of body take place to your present body which is an eternal body? So, in the parport, Śrīla Prabhupāda says, Śrī Nārada Muni in his previous life was just an ordinary maid servant's son. So, how he became so perfectly transformed into the spiritual body of eternal life, bliss and knowledge is certainly important.

### spiritual body, transformation, material body
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xGrnroIA8r4?t=765
**Verse References:** BG 2.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Change from a material body to a spiritual one is not going to happen easily. It doesn't happen automatically."
**Summary:** The speaker references Bhagavad Gita, explaining the concept of the changing body, and emphasizing that the significant question is how Narada transformed into a spiritual body, which doesn't happen automatically.

How did this transformation take place? Bhagavad Gītā says our body is changing always. Dehi nosmin yathā dehe kaumāram yauvanam jarā. We all experience as spirit soul the body which is different from the spirit, the material body is changing from childhood to youth to old age. Similarly, the body changes at death. Of this there is no doubt. Everybody changes their body even at the time of death. tathā dehaṁ taraptiḥ. But, the significant question here is, not whether the change of body has happened or not. It is about how he became so perfectly transformed into the spiritual body of eternal life, bliss and knowledge. That is the important question being raised here. That is not going to happen just like that. Change from a material body to a spiritual one is not going to happen easily. It doesn't happen automatically. So, how did this change happen? That is precisely the question that Vyasa Deva is asking from his spiritual master, Narada Muni.

### history, creation, human nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/i1qa4vYcCXw?t=310
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The history of the universe repeats itself practically in the same way."
**Summary:** The speaker presents Srila Prabhupada's commentary on the verse, emphasizing that human activities like agriculture, mining, and industry have always existed on a similar scale throughout different creations, challenging the notion of a linear progression from primitive to civilized society.

So very interesting purport by Srila Prabhupada a very pertinent observation is made by Srila Prabhupada kindly note this Prabhupada says in the purport man's activities in agriculture mining farming industries gardening etc were all on the same scale as they are now. Sometimes we think when we read so-called history that there was a primitive civilization of man was very primitive condition caveman and then gradually evolved to become the present civilized society but that is not the fact man's activities in all these fields agriculture mining farming industries gardening etc were on the same scale as they are now even previous to the present creation and the same activities will remain as they are even in the next creation cyclically this is going on creation maintenance destruction again creation maintenance destruction in a day of Brahma. Of course there are changes that happen even within one day of Brahma but at the beginning of the day of Brahma there is creation and the end of day of Brahma there is destruction so in between there is there are changes taking place with essentially man's activities at least on the earth are similar to what was there in a previous creation. So, Srila Prabhupada says, after many hundreds of millions of years, one creation is started by the law of nature and the history of the universe repeats itself practically in the same way. It is out of necessity that human beings have to do cultivation of the field and then there are forests which give certain produce without any human endeavor by the arrangement of the Supreme Lord through the nature, mother nature and then there are mines which has got deposits of different minerals, different metals, that mining activity was always there even in the past and it is there now and will be there in the future also. So, the history of the universe repeats itself practically in the same way.

### materialism, spiritual ignorance, purpose of life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/i1qa4vYcCXw?t=486
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The mundane wranglers waste time and energy with archeological excavations without searching into the vital necessities of life."
**Summary:** The speaker criticizes "mundane wranglers" who focus on material pursuits and archeological discoveries without seeking spiritual understanding, emphasizing the importance of inquiring into the vital necessities of life and the spiritual dimension of existence.

As far as major activities are concerned among the different classes of people on different planets, it repeats itself. Now, here is the important observation that Srila Prabhupada is making. The mundane wranglers waste time and energy with archeological excavations without searching into the vital necessities of life. So, mundane wranglers. Mundane means they are only concerned with matter and material things and material existence. They are not have understanding of something beyond matter. They do not know who they are. They do not know their spirit. They don't know there is a spiritual dimension to existence in every human being, every living being actually, particularly in a human being. And there is a spiritual world beyond this material world, which is eternal. Of this, there are people who have no knowledge, no idea, no trace of any understanding. Totally zero when it comes to spiritual understanding. So they are called mundane wranglers.

---
### spiritual inquiry, vedanta, self-realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/i1qa4vYcCXw?t=579
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In just three words in Vedanta Sutra, the whole thing is summarized. Athato Brahma Jijnasa. Vital necessity of life. Inquire into what is spirit."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the futility of focusing solely on material discoveries and emphasizes the importance of spiritual inquiry, referencing the Vedanta Sutra's "Athato Brahma Jijnasa" as the vital necessity of life: inquiring into the nature of spirit, God, and one's true self.

They are simply busy with material things. And they are fascinated if they discover something by excavation. Some ancient civilization, they had beautiful buildings. They had, you know, gold coins. They had a system of mining and purifying. Metallurgy was very advanced even thousands of years back. When they discover all this through excavations, then they waste so much time into rebuilding so-called history. But what they are losing out is the valuable human life. The time we can spend in this human form of life into searching out the vital necessity of this human form of life. What is that? Inquiry into the absolute truth. In just three words in Vedanta Sutra, the whole thing is summarized. Athato Brahma Jijnasa. Vital necessity of life. Inquire into what is spirit. What is Brahman or who is God. Or who you are. Inquire into this.

---
### human potential, spiritual knowledge, problem solving
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/i1qa4vYcCXw?t=671
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Without making this inquiry, human life is completely wasted."
**Summary:** The speaker stresses that without spiritual inquiry, human life is wasted, despite developed intelligence and the ability to contemplate. He asserts that spiritual knowledge is paramount and that realizing one's spiritual nature can solve all problems and end the cycle of birth and death.

Without making this inquiry, human life is completely wasted. All the developed intelligence. The ability to ask fundamental questions and try to get meaningful answers. This opportunity is lost. It's a greatest loss for a human being. In the evolution of the consciousness through different species of life in this material world. The human form of life is the most evolved, developed state of consciousness. So, therefore, it is very well known human being is a rational animal. Can think. Can contemplate. Can inquire. Can learn. Can teach. Can do so many things with this developed intelligence, developed consciousness. So, the most important inquiry. The most important inquiry is inquiry about spirit. Inquiry about God. Inquiry about spiritual reality. That's called Brahma Jijnasa. And that is the most important part of a Vedic knowledge. Vedic knowledge, clearly there are two departments. Material knowledge and spiritual knowledge. Of these two, spiritual knowledge is the most important. Because that is the vital necessity of a human being. Why? Just by studying, understanding, realizing your spirit soul. All your problems are solved. Your life is ultimately a success. You can end the repetition of birth and death. You can solve all your problems.

---
### self-inquiry, detachment, spiritual goal
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/i1qa4vYcCXw?t=802
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But it begins with inquiry into who am I. Essentially, who am I? Am I this body? Am I something different from the body?"
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of self-inquiry, particularly questioning one's identity beyond the physical body. He contrasts this with the futility of dwelling on historical details and concludes by reiterating Narada Muni's focus on spiritual advancement, even as a young boy, and his unwavering pursuit of meditation.

Only by realizing the truth that you are spirit. And of course, the supreme absolute truth is the supreme spirit, Krishna. And you have got a relationship. Three things. But it begins with inquiry into who am I. Essentially, who am I? Am I this body? Am I something different from the body? Or this inquiry is the most important inquiry. So, instead of wasting time inquiring into what was this civilization like 300 years back or 500 years back. What kind of buildings were they building? What were they eating? What were they doing for entertainment? All these kind of things are waste of human energy. That is what is being pointed out. So, after getting his impetus in spiritual life. Narad Muni, even as a 5 year old boy, did not waste time a single moment with economic development. Although he passed through all these towns, villages, mines, industries. He continually went on till he reached that sacred holy place to sit and do meditation. That was his aim. So, we will continue this discussion in the next session. Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.6.12-13 | 27-10-2021
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### detachment, renunciation, spiritual life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/J643v1bHGEQ?t=64
**Verse References:** SB 1.6.12
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Somebody serious about spiritual life will never be attracted to any place which is meant for satisfying the senses."
**Summary:** Narada Muni's travels are described, emphasizing his detachment from material attractions and his focus on spiritual pursuits, highlighting the qualities of a serious spiritualist.

In this session, we will be discussing the twelfth verse of the sixth chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Chitradhatu Vichitradrin Ibha Bhagna Bhujadruman Jalashayan Shivajalan Nalini Surasevitaha. Chitrasvanai Patrarathair Vibhrahmad Bhramara Shriyaha. The meaning of this verse is, "I pass through hills and mountains full of reservoirs of various minerals like gold, silver, and copper, and through tracts of land with reservoirs of water filled with beautiful lotus flowers fit for the denizens of heaven, decorated with bewildered bees and singing birds." Naradamuni is describing his travel. He passed through different kinds of places, and as it will be explained in the next verse, it is the duty of a sanyasi, a renunciant, or one dedicated to spiritual life, to actually travel through different tracts of land which are all the creation of the Supreme Lord in this material world, and none of such places have any attraction for a serious spiritualist like Narada. There is no attraction. He is passing through reservoirs of gold, silver, copper, but he has no attraction for them. Neither is he attracted to a beautiful place filled with clear water and lotus flowers, so beautiful that it is fit for the heavenly inhabitants, singing birds, beautiful bees. He is not at all attracted by any of these because, with one-pointed attention, he is determined to go to a place fit for him, to sit and do meditation on the Supreme Lord, as taught by the bhaktivedantas, his spiritual masters, when they were staying for four months during chaturmasya in that place in the forest where his mother was a maidservant. So somebody serious about spiritual life will never be attracted to any place which is meant for satisfying the senses. Any place which is meant for sense satisfaction, such a place has no attraction for a transcendentalist, a serious spiritualist.

---
### renunciation, duty, preaching
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/J643v1bHGEQ?t=274
**Verse References:** SB 1.6.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The real purpose of going to the house of a householder is not for begging, it is for enlightening them spiritually."
**Summary:** The lecture continues with the description of Narada Muni's travels through dangerous forests and explains the duty of a mendicant or parivrajaka acharya to travel, teach spiritual knowledge, and enlighten householders, emphasizing that their primary purpose is spiritual guidance, not merely begging for sustenance.

The next verse describes further what are the different kinds of places he passed through. The thirteenth verse: nala venu sharastamba kusha ki chaka gahvaram eka evaati yathoham adraksham vipinam mahat ghoram pratibhayakaram vyala uluka shivajiram. The meaning of this verse is, "I then passed alone through many forests of rushes, bamboo, reeds, sharp grass, weeds, and caves, which were very difficult to go through alone. I visited deep, dark, and dangerously fearful forests which were the play yards of snakes, owls, and jackals." So Narada Muni is passing through not only beautiful places, attractive places, but also through some dangerous tracts of land, like forests which are deep, dark, and dangerously fearful because they are inhabited by snakes, owls, and jackals. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains it is the duty of a mendicant. Mendicant literally means a beggar, but it is taken in the sense of a spiritualist who lives a very simple life by being dedicated for spiritual practice and for maintaining his body, he simply begs for some little food from a householder. So such a mendicant is called a parivrajaka acharya. Parivrajaka means he is traveling constantly, wandering, and acharya means he is teaching. What is he teaching? He is teaching spiritual subject matter. He is awakening householders who are lost in material way of life to remind them about their spiritual goal in life. So he enlightens them, and on the plea of begging, he goes to their house, but his real purpose of going to the house of a householder is not for begging, it is for enlightening them spiritually. Of course, in the Vedic culture, the householders welcome a sanyasi or a traveling mendicant or a transcendentalist. They welcome him home because they know that he brings in all auspiciousness, but the real purpose of a sanyasi is not to beg. So just to satisfy some householders, he may take a little food, may take, may not take, but his main purpose is how to enlighten the householder in spiritual subject matter, which is the prime necessity for every human being. So here Srila Prabhupada is telling it is the duty of a parivrajaka acharya.

---
### duty, travel, faith
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/J643v1bHGEQ?t=499
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One's dependence should be on the Supreme Lord, seated in the heart, who is always accompanying every person and is providing, maintaining, is actually taking care of the well-being."
**Summary:** The segment elaborates on the duties of a parivrajaka acharya, emphasizing the importance of experiencing God's creation through travel, gaining faith and strength of mind, and enlightening others with the message of God, highlighting the stages of sanyas and the ultimate dependence on the Supreme Lord.

It is the duty of a parivrajaka acharya to experience all varieties of God's creation. How does he do that? By traveling alone through all forests, hills, towns, villages, traveling alone to gain faith in God and strength of mind, as well as to enlighten the inhabitants with the message of God. This is the program of a parivrajaka acharya. In sanyas, there are four stages of development of a complete renunciation. The beginning stage is called kutichaka. The second stage is called bahudhaka. The third stage is called parivrajaka acharya, and the fourth stage is called paramahamsa. Briefly, I will just mention what are the stages of sanyas. Kutichaka is when one decides to renounce connection with family members, leaves home, but not yet practiced how to maintain himself independently. So he builds a cottage outside his village, and he receives food from home. He is cut off from the family members' social interaction, but as far as his food requirement is concerned, it is taken care of by the former family members. When he is practiced in that way, then he realizes how he can actually maintain himself very easily by begging a little from many different houses. So nobody will feel a burden for feeding one person a little bit, collecting little little from many houses, then nobody feels the burden, and the person is very easily able to get his necessity of daily food. And so that's called bahudhaka, the second stage of sanyas, where one is not dependent only on his one home, the former purvashram family residence, but begs little from the different people of the village outside which he is staying, and in that way, very easily he maintains himself, his requirement of food. Then when he becomes further well established in sanyas, then he starts actually traveling. Traveling is for gaining strength of mind, faith in God, and enlighten the inhabitants as he is passing through different towns, villages, or settlements, human settlements, like that. So these three things are particularly mentioned here: traveling alone, traveling alone to become fearless because a sanyasi knows that God is in my heart, and he is my topmost well-wisher, he is my protector, he is my maintainer. Only due to illusion somebody may think that I have to depend on somebody else or something else outside of me. Actually, one's dependence should be on the Supreme Lord, seated in the heart, who is always accompanying every person and is providing, maintaining, is actually taking care of the well-being. It is only this Supreme Lord who does. So this is the purpose of a sanyasi.

---
### preaching, renunciation, example
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/J643v1bHGEQ?t=768
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What is important is to stop social intercourse and devote life exclusively for preaching the message of God among the common people."
**Summary:** The final segment discusses the example of Lord Chaitanya as a typical sanyasi and emphasizes the importance of preaching Krishna consciousness, especially in this age, highlighting that true renunciation involves dedicating one's life to spreading the message of God.

Now further, Srila Prabhupada says a sanyasi is duty-bound to take all these risks without fear, and the most typical sanyasi of the present age is Lord Chaitanya, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. He took sanyas, and he traveled in the same manner like Narada Muni. Narada Muni traveled in a former age. Chaitanya traveled through the central Indian jungles, enlightening even the tigers, bears, snakes, deer, elephants, and many other jungle animals. What to speak of enlightening human beings, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu even enlightened jungle animals while traveling. Of course, he is the Supreme Lord, so he can enlighten any living being, any living entity, but at least we should attempt to enlighten human beings in the subject matter of sankirtana, bhakti yoga, or practice of devotional service, which is the recommended process in this age. So sanyasi is duty-bound to take all these risks without fear. So Srila Prabhupada further says in this age, Kali, in the age of Kali, sanyasi is forbidden for ordinary people because they cannot maintain sanyasi like in former ages, but for the sake of preaching, one may accept sanyasi simply for the sake of preaching Krishna consciousness because there is a need for preachers to travel and preach. So this sanyasi is more than change of dress. What is important is to stop social intercourse and devote life exclusively for preaching the message of God among the common people. That is the truth. So I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hari Krishna.

---

Parishranta Indriya Atma Aham Trd Parito Bubhukshitaha Snatva Peetva Hrade Nadya Upas Prishto Gata Shramaha. In that holy place, on the way he came across so many different kinds of prosperous towns, gold mines, beautiful lakes filled with lotus flowers and singing birds, but he was not attracted by any of those. His mind was fixed on reaching that sacred spot where he could sit. So what about the demands of the body while travelling? A travelling mendicant can meet the needs of the body, namely thirst and hunger, by the gifts of nature without being a beggar at the doors of the householder. ### spiritual duty, householder life, enlightenment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/20yeZ9vL2Wo?t=173

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the role of a mendicant, stating that their purpose in approaching householders is to enlighten them spiritually, as householders often become engrossed in family affairs.

By Krishna's arrangement, there is sufficient food and water available for any person who just wants to dedicate himself for spiritual life without any need for begging at the door of a householder. So Srila Prabhupada further says the mendicant therefore does not go to the house of a householder to beg but to enlighten him spiritually. There is no need for begging, but then on the plea of begging sometimes is transcendent. A spiritualist may go and approach a householder simply to take the opportunity to enlighten the householder spiritually. That is a real purpose because people, especially in household life, they become busy with their work, family maintenance, providing for the needs of the family members etc. Many responsibilities as regards the maintenance of the family. So they get more or less lost in such family affairs. So to awaken them to spiritual reality is the duty of a traveling transcendentalist Parivrajakacharya.

### Meditation, Paramatma, Bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jbGC1Ri78u4?t=128
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Narada Muni experienced what is called as Bhava. Bhava is the mind transformed in transcendental love for the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Narada Muni's experience, explaining that he meditated on the Paramatma (Supersoul) within the heart, and discusses the changes that occur as meditation progresses correctly, leading to Bhava, or transcendental love for the Lord.

This is Narada Muni's experience after he began meditation. What happened? In the previous verse it was described that he began to meditate on this super soul Paramatma. Super soul is Krishna seated in the heart. As he had learned from the great sages the Bhaktivedantas and as he had actually understood and applying his intelligence he began to search out for the super soul in the heart and fixing his mind he meditated upon the Lord in the heart. Now the changes that take place as the meditation progresses in the correct way in the perfect way as taught by the great authorities. What are the changes that take place? Here it is described Bhava nirjita chetasa. Narada Muni experienced what is called as Bhava. Bhava is the mind transformed in transcendental love for the Lord.

### Anartha Nivritti, Nishtha, Devotional Service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jbGC1Ri78u4?t=562

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Anartha Nivritti involves cleansing the heart of all kinds of misgivings...And when Anartha Nivritti has sufficiently happened, say about 80 percent, then there is nishtha. One becomes fixed up in the practice of devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Anartha Nivritti (cleansing of unwanted things from the heart) and its importance in achieving Nishtha (steadiness) in devotional service. He mentions that about 80% cleansing is needed to become fixed in practice.

Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare. That leads to Anartha Nivritti, the fourth step. Anartha Nivritti involves cleansing the heart of all kinds of misgivings, all kinds of misconceptions, wrong ideas about who I am, who is God, what is my relationship, what is this world, where have I come from, what is my ultimate goal in life, everything. We have some. Some idea. But all the misunderstandings are cleared away and the correct understanding becomes established properly in the heart. And when Anartha Nivritti has sufficiently happened, say about 80 percent, then there is nishtha. One becomes fixed up in the practice of devotional service. In the association of devotees, following the standard procedure as taught by the bona fide spiritual master, without any deviation, without any speculation, without any concoction, and without any doubt. So one makes steady progress. So nishtha is the fifth stage or the fifth step. In the nine step process of perfection in bhakti, nishtha is that one becomes fixed up. There is no more any wavering.

### ecstasy, meditation, spiritual experience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cK9mIsXIS_Y?t=57
**Verse References:** SB 1.6.17
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Being absorbed in an ocean of ecstasy, I could not see both myself and the Lord."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces and translates Srimad Bhagavatam 1.6.17, focusing on Narada Muni's experience of ecstasy during meditation and his inability to perceive himself or the Lord.

Prema Atibhara Nirbhinna Pulaka Ango Ati Nirvrata Ananda Samplave Leeno Na Apashyam Ubhayam Mune. The meaning of this verse is O Vyasa Deva, Narada Muni is speaking to Vyasa Deva, describing the experience he underwent in his previous life when he was instructed by the Bhaktivedantas and he went to a secluded place, a sacred spot in the Himalayas and he sat there firmly and began to do meditation. So, we have heard so far how he experienced symptoms of ecstasy. So, what does it mean to ecstasy? One of the symptoms is described in the 16th verse, that tears started rolling down from his eyes. What else happened? That is being described in this verse. O Vyasa Deva, at that time, being exceedingly overpowered by feelings of happiness, every part of my body became separately enlivened. Being absorbed in an ocean of ecstasy, I could not see both myself and the Lord. This is the 17th verse. I just read the translation of the 17th verse.

### ecstasy, spiritual joy, bhava

**Verse References:** SB 1.6.16, SB 1.6.17
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Spiritual feelings of happiness and intense ecstasies have no mundane comparison."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the unparalleled nature of spiritual joy, contrasting it with material happiness and emphasizing its limitlessness, drawing from Srila Prabhupada's purport.

If you remember, in the 16th verse, Narada Muni had told, he experienced that joy, the ecstasy of having come to the stage of that bhava, bhava nirjita chetasa and tears began to flow from his eyes, tears of joy, extreme joy and he also had mentioned the Supreme Lord appeared on the lotus of his heart. Krishna, who is seated on the heart, gave him darshan. Gave him darshan. Next, what? He experienced, Narada experienced, that he is overpowered by feelings of happiness, exceedingly overpowered. That means the happiness is so much, there is no comparison to any material experience of happiness at all. It is completely different. This is the first point. Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, spiritual feelings of happiness and intense ecstasies have no mundane comparison. There is no comparison with anything material that we experience in this world. There is no material comparison. This is entirely a different experience. It is incomparable because, the amount of joy one experiences is actually unlimited.

Though we are very, very tiny and our experience has got limitation, but still, to the extent we can experience as much joy as we really can, it is possible to experience as much joy as we really can. It is possible by spiritual practice of Bhakti, Sadhana Bhakti, through the stages, as I mentioned in the previous session, Shraddha, Sadhu Sangha, Bhajana Kriya, Anartha Nivritti, Nishtha, Ruchi, Asakti, Bhava. In that Bhava stage, the eighth stage, one stage before Prema, one stage before the ultimate perfection. In that Bhava stage, the eighth stage, one stage before Prema, one stage before the ultimate perfection. In that Bhava stage, the eighth stage, one stage before Prema, one step before the ultimate perfection. In that stage, one is exceedingly overpowered by feelings of happiness. So, there is no mundane comparison. Therefore, it is very difficult to give expression to such feelings. We can just have a glimpse, as far as we are concerned, we are beginners, how we have got to make progress. It will take time. We have to become serious. We have to apply the principles. We have to follow all the rules, regulations. We have to associate with devotees. We have to give up all misconceptions that we have about ourselves, or God, or the relationship, or about this world. Everything we have to. We have to purify our heart. We have to purify our consciousness.

**Summary:** The speaker explains how the senses become awakened to serve the Lord in the stage of Bhava, describing the specific functions of each sense in experiencing Krishna's presence through sight, sound, taste, and smell.

So, we can get an idea in the words of Narada, as he is describing to Vyasa Deva. In the words of Narada, we can get a brief glimpse of the ecstasy that Narada Muni experienced by reaching that eighth stage. Reaching that eighth stage of progress in the practice of Bhakti. Here, Srila Prabhupada says, each and every part of the body or senses has its particular function. See, a living person means there are senses. Senses of perception and senses of action. All these ten senses are centered around the mind. And the mind is called the sense within. Mind is the center. So, in the mind and the senses, one is able to experience joy, spiritual joy. And such experience, what does it do? The spiritual joy of ecstasy, bhava. That after seeing the Lord, the senses become fully awakened to render service to the Lord. Every sense has got a certain function. The eyes for seeing the form of the Lord. The ears for hearing about the Lord. The tongue for tasting food offered to the Lord. Offered tasting the tulsi leaves offered in the lotus feet of the Lord. The taste of prasadam or the taste of remnants of yajna shishta. And the nose for smelling the fragrant incense offered to the Lord. And all, all spiritual fragrances. It's not just that. That is the beginning. In the beginning we offer incense to the Lord when we do worship. But there are other kinds of spiritual fragrances. And just like Krishna's transcendental form has got a spiritual fragrance. Every spiritual form has got a unique spiritual fragrance. So when one is able to come face to face with Krishna, one is able to smell, relish that fragrance of Krishna's transcendental form.
