## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.6.18 | 1-11-2021
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### desire, mind, transcendental form
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xoI1iFCIR8g?t=58
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The transcendental form of the Lord, as it is, satisfies the mind's desire and at once erases all mental incongruities."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains Narada Muni's experience of seeing and then losing sight of the transcendental form of the Lord, and the resulting perturbation, emphasizing that the Lord's form satisfies the mind's desires and removes mental incongruities.

In this session, we will be discussing the 18th verse of the 6th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is: 'The transcendental form of the Lord, as it is, satisfies the mind's desire and at once erases all mental incongruities. Upon losing that form, I suddenly got up, being perturbed, as is usual, when one loses that which is desirable.' So, Srila Narada Muni is explaining his experience to Vyasa Deva. From his previous life, he began meditation. He had a glimpse of the Lord, and he became overjoyed with ecstasy. Suddenly, he lost that form. So then, after losing sight of that form, after getting a brief glimpse of darshan of that form of the Lord in the heart, Narada Muni is describing what was the kind of loss that he really felt when he lost sight of the Lord. The transcendental form of the Lord, as it is, satisfies the mind's desire. The mind is the center of all the senses, and the mind is driving the senses towards sense objects, looking for something. What is that something that each one of us is looking for? It is actually Krishna. Everyone is looking for Krishna, having forgotten Him, and also desiring to get back that which we have lost, the most desirable, the most beautiful, the most wonderful, the dear most, the dearest. We have lost that, so we are looking for that. So, therefore, when we actually get darshan of the transcendental form of the Lord, then immediately our mind's desire is completely satisfied. And also, all the mental incongruities are erased, meaning in the mind we settle for something when we don't get what we are really looking for, and we think maybe this can satisfy me, but then we find something else which may be relatively either better or more beautiful or more pleasing for the mind. Again, it creates in the mind a kind of complex situation that maybe this is what I was looking for. No, no, no, this is not what I was looking for. So there are so many mental incongruities essentially established through contact with so many other forms other than the form of the Lord. So all these incongruities in the mind are completely erased when we actually get darshan of the form of the Lord, and that's what Narada Muni is describing here.

---
### form, spiritual vision, material limitations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xoI1iFCIR8g?t=304
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Nirakar means the Lord does not have a material form. He has a transcendental form."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami elaborates on Narada Muni's experience, emphasizing that the Lord is not formless but possesses a transcendental form, distinct from material forms, and that this form cannot be perceived through material senses or adjustments.

So he says, 'I lost that form which was completely satisfying to my mind, which erased the form of the Lord, which erased all my mental incongruities. That form I lost, and suddenly, on losing the form, all of a sudden I lost that form, then I became very much perturbed in my mind.' And that is to be expected because when somebody loses something which is very, very desirable, then how do they feel? That's how I felt. So Srila Prabhupada makes a very important point in the purport here, that the Lord is not formless is experienced by Narada Muni. A widely prevalent understanding about God is that God is formless, Nirakar. Nirakar is one of the terms that is used to describe God. It is one of the terms that is used to describe the Lord's features, but we should carefully understand what this Nirakar means. Nirakar means the Lord does not have a material form. He doesn't have a form made of flesh and blood and skin and bones. No, He doesn't have such a form. He has a transcendental form. He has a completely spiritual form. That form cannot be seen with these ordinary eyes. These eyes are suitable only for seeing some material forms. Even material forms which are very big we cannot see. Material forms which are too close to the eye we cannot see. We cannot see even our own eyelids. We can't see something very small. We can't see if there is no light to see a particular object or a form. So, these eyes have got limitations. In any case, even if we overcome all kinds of limitations of this eye by some mechanical means, material means, still, spiritual form can never be seen by any material adjustment even for these eyes. You cannot see spiritual form with material vision, even making adjustments, not possible.

---
### spiritual endeavor, transcendental experience, satisfaction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xoI1iFCIR8g?t=480
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What Narada Muni saw was not just another experience of seeing a material form. It's completely different."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains that Narada Muni's vision of the Lord's transcendental form was achieved through spiritual endeavor, not material means, and that this experience is distinctly different from perceiving material forms, as it provides complete satisfaction and erases mental disturbances.

So, here Srila Prabhupada is telling Narada Muni's experience of seeing the form of the Lord is that Narada Muni is able to see the transcendental form of the Lord by his spiritual endeavor, by his spiritual efforts, not by any material efforts. No. But his form is completely different from all forms of our material experience. This is an important point. What Narada Muni saw was not just another experience of seeing a material form. It's not that. It is completely different. So, Srila Prabhupada explains, for the whole duration of our life, we go see different forms in the material world, but none of them is just apt to satisfy the mind, nor can any one of them vanish all perturbance of the mind. Two things Prabhupada is mentioning: none of them can satisfy the mind. No material form can satisfy the mind completely. And none of the material forms have got the power to erase all mental incongruities. That's not possible. So, therefore, what is mentioned by Narada Muni in this verse is very important to note. These are the special features of the transcendental form of the Lord. The transcendental form of the Lord is distinctly different from all material forms, and the special features are being mentioned specifically by Narada Muni, that it completely satisfies the mind. Vision of the transcendental form of the Lord completely satisfies the mind, and it erases all mental incongruities. Further, Srila Prabhupada says, one who has once seen that form is not satisfied with anything else. This is a fact. Having seen the transcendental form of the Lord even once, for a brief moment even, then one can never be satisfied with any other form.

---
### spiritual identity, eternal search, reincarnation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xoI1iFCIR8g?t=632
**Verse References:** BG 2.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As spiritual beings, having eternal relations with that transcendental form of the Lord, we are life after life searching after that form of the Lord."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains that the concept of the Lord being formless refers to the absence of a material form, and that as spiritual beings, we are eternally searching for the Lord's transcendental form, a search that spans lifetimes due to our spiritual nature and the cycle of reincarnation.

The Lord is formless or impersonal means that he has nothing like a material form. This is again being stressed, that Nirakar means the Lord does not have a material form. Even descriptions of the Lord being impersonal means he doesn't have a material personality. Now, Srila Prabhupada is also explaining in the purport, as spiritual beings, having eternal relations with that transcendental form of the Lord, we are life after life searching after that form of the Lord, and we are not satisfied by any other form of material appeasement. See, this is a secret being revealed by Prabhupada based on Narada Muni's statement, that we are always looking, we are searching. But what are we searching? We are searching for the spiritual form of the Supreme Lord. Only that can satisfy our mind. Only that can give us the real joy that we are looking for. Narada Muni got a glimpse of this, but having not seen it again, he became perturbed and stood up all of a sudden to search it out. See, he got a glimpse, and he lost it, so he stood up all of a sudden. He was sitting and doing meditation. He stood up, and he began to search out, 'Where did I lose? How did I lose sight of that beautiful transcendental form of the Lord?' What we desire life after life. See, it is not just this life. From the Bhagavad Gita, we understand we are spirit soul. For the soul, for the real you or real I, or real person, there is no death. There is no birth. So our real nature is spiritual, but we keep changing bodies as long as we don't realize our self, we don't realize the Supreme Lord Krishna, and our relationship with Him. As long as we don't realize these three things, we keep taking birth again and again, simply quitting one body, entering another body. What kind of body? Material body. So that is what is being described here.

---
### spiritual ignorance, deity worship, spiritual vision
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xoI1iFCIR8g?t=818
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Though the deity is Krishna, the deity is completely spiritual, but unless our eyes are trained, unless we have got spiritual training, unless we have developed the spiritual vision, we cannot see the spiritual form of the deity through our ordinary vision."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami concludes by explaining that without spiritual knowledge and training, we remain in ignorance, struggling for existence and unable to recognize the transcendental form of the Lord, even when present in the deity form in the temple, as our vision is limited by our material perspective.

What we desire life after life, if we remain spiritually ignorant, if we are not fortunate enough to come in touch with a pure devotee of Krishna, then we remain in ignorance. We simply are engaged in a hard struggle for existence. We keep searching for that spiritual form, transcendental form of the Supreme Lord, and none of the material forms are like that. Somebody may say that the deity form in the temple is the transcendental form of the Lord, but then when we see the deity form without proper understanding, without proper spiritual training, then we see it as a material statue. We see it as a material statue. We see it as simply a statue made of metal or wood or clay or stone, etc. We don't see it as a spiritual form, though the deity is Krishna. The deity is completely spiritual, but unless our eyes are trained, unless we have got spiritual training, unless we have developed the spiritual vision, we cannot see the spiritual form of the deity through our ordinary vision.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.6.19-20 | 15-11-2021
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### desire, meditation, disappointment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AOpqOuoNX0w?t=62
**Verse References:** SB 1.6.19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I desire to see again that transcendental form of the Lord in the heart, but despite my attempts to concentrate upon the heart, with eagerness to view the form again, he is again wanting to see."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Narada Muni's desire to see the Lord again after a brief darshan, his attempts to concentrate, and his subsequent disappointment at not being able to see Him.

In this session, we will be discussing from the first canto, sixth chapter, nineteenth and twentieth verses of the Srimad Bhagavatam. First, the nineteenth verse, DIDRUKSHU STAD AHAM BHUYAH, PRANIDHAYA MANOHRDI, VIKSHAM ANO PINAPASYAM, AVITRUPTA IVATURAHA. The meaning of this verse is, I desire to see again that transcendental form of the Lord, but despite my attempts to concentrate upon the heart with eagerness, to view the form again, I could not see him anymore, and thus, dissatisfied, I was very much aggrieved. So the background for this verse is, in the previous session, we had heard how Narad Muni began to meditate on the form of the Lord in the heart, as he had been taught by the Bhaktivedantas, and using his intelligence to search out for the Lord in the heart, and very quickly he came to the stage of bhava, tears started flowing down from his eyes, and every part of his body he felt separately enlivened. In that state, the Lord gave him darshan within the heart, a brief darshan, but after a brief moment, when he was in the experiencing that ecstasy of joy of seeing the form of the Lord in the heart, suddenly he lost sight of that form. Then, he again tried to see that form of the Lord. So that is the description here. I desire to see again that transcendental form of the Lord in the heart, but despite my attempts to concentrate upon the heart, with eagerness to view the form again. He is again wanting to see. So he is thinking, let me do the same thing which I did previously. But he could not see the Lord, in spite of very sincere attempts, the same way, exactly the same way, as he had been taught, as he had done already, as he had already seen, he could not again see the form of the Lord, and he was very much dissatisfied and became aggrieved.

---
### causeless mercy, devotional service, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AOpqOuoNX0w?t=243
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are completely dependent upon the causeless mercy of the Lord in trying to see his form. We cannot demand the Lord to be present before our vision."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that seeing the Lord is not a mechanical process but depends on His causeless mercy, using the analogy of the sun rising. He clarifies that this doesn't mean inaction, but sincere effort is required alongside the Lord's grace.

So, Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport that there is no mechanical process to see the Lord, and it completely depends upon the causeless mercy of the Lord. This word causeless means Lord will not give darshan because you are doing something good, because of some act of yours, as it generally happens in this material world, according to the laws of karma, I do some pious activity, I get some pious result, or I do some sinful activity, then I may get a sinful result. But, the way to see the form of the Lord is no mechanical process, is no material method. We are completely dependent upon the causeless mercy of the Lord in trying to see his form. We cannot demand the Lord to be present before our vision. That I did this, I followed the process, I had taken instructions from the masters, the bhaktivedantas, so I should be able to see. Nobody can say that. And Srila Prabhupada gives an example just as we cannot demand the sun to rise whenever we like. Very good example. The sun rises exactly at the time according to its own schedule. The sun doesn't become subject to somebody else's control, or somebody else's demand. No. Same way, the Supreme Lord will give darshan whenever he wills, whenever he wants to give darshan. One should simply await the opportune moment and go on discharging his prescribed duty in devotional service of the Lord. So, this is the meaning of we should actually depend upon the causeless mercy of the Lord and not on our own efforts alone. However, this does not mean that I may do nothing and I simply think that whenever the Lord wants to give me darshan, he will give me. Sometimes when we invite people to the temple, they tell, Jab Bhagwan bulayenge, hum ayenge. Whenever the Lord wants me to come, I'll come. Or whenever he invites me, I'll come. Now, this is not the correct attitude. If we don't take any step at all towards approaching the authorized process, then we'll never be able to see the Lord. We'll never be able to approach the Lord. This is very clear.

---
### devotional service, love, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AOpqOuoNX0w?t=469
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One thing that Krishna really is completely thoroughly pleased when a devotee offers pure devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of sincere effort and the Lord's causeless mercy, explaining that the Lord is bound by unalloyed devotion, like Mother Yashoda's love for Krishna.

So, two things. One is our own sincere efforts according to the scriptural direction, according to the instructions of the bona fide spiritual master, according to the process, authorized process that has to be there. But this alone is not enough. The second thing is the Lord's causeless mercy. So that is being described here that we have to await the opportune moment. We simply wait after doing whatever we have to do properly. Narad Muni thought the Lord could be seen again by the same mechanical process that was successful in the first attempt. But in spite of his utmost endeavor, he could not make the second attempt successful. That was not possible. So, Srila Prabhupada says the Lord is completely independent of all obligations. But one important thing, the most important thing we should know about approaching the Lord or doing the devotional services, he can simply be bound up by the tie of unalloyed devotion. One thing that Krishna really is completely thoroughly pleased when a devotee offers pure devotional service. It is also called devotion unmixed with anything else. Sent person pure devotional service. Only that can actually bind the Lord because he agrees to become bound up. Just like Mother Yashoda, she attempted to tie the Lord her child, whom she thought of as her beloved child when he was becoming little naughty. She wanted to tie him to a grinding mortar with a rope.

---
### love, devotion, spiritual form
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AOpqOuoNX0w?t=593
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is not ropes that can bind the Lord. It is love that can bind the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the analogy of Mother Yashoda tying Krishna, emphasizing that it is love and pure devotion, not material attempts, that can bind the Lord. He also states that the Lord is not visible to material senses.

She was trying and trying and trying and the rope was always falling short by two fingers. That actually Krishna showed to us, it was for demonstrating to us, that he is not bound up simply because, you know, of some mother Yashoda catching him and binding and attempting to bind him with some rope. She got all the rope available in her house but she was somehow not able to tie him. And his form appeared only a limited form but we should know the Lord's form is unlimited and also one cannot bind him simply by some attempt of one's own. The Lord should agree to become bound. So after Mother Yashoda tried and tried and she became exhausted, then Krishna just smiled and immediately she was able to tie him. So that is actually to also teach us that he agreed to become bound up by the love of Mother Yashoda. It is not ropes that can bind the Lord. It is love that can bind the Lord. And what kind of love is this? Love out of pure devotion. That is what can bind the Lord. So, he cannot be bound up simply by some material attempts. Similarly, the Lord is not visible or perceivable by our material senses. Shastras clearly say material senses are totally incapable of actually perceiving the Lord.

---
### incarnation, causeless mercy, grief
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AOpqOuoNX0w?t=747
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He may be seen out of his own accord when he gives darshan, when he gives vision to us. Only then he can be seen."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that although material senses cannot perceive the Lord's spiritual form, He gives darshan out of His causeless mercy, even to those with ordinary vision. He then introduces the 20th verse, describing how the Lord spoke to Narada to mitigate his grief.

Though, when he appears as an incarnation, he gives darshan even though he comes in his spiritual form and these eyes are useless for seeing spiritual form. They are totally incapable of seeing any spiritual form. But still, he gives darshan to even people who have just got this ordinary vision. He gives darshan. That is his causeless mercy. But people mistake him to be another created being of this world. If he appears in a human-like form, as he appears when he appears as Krishna, then they think he is an ordinary covered boy or ordinary prince of this world, as son of Vasudeva. As son of Nanda Maharaj, they think he is just an ordinary covered boy. Or when he appears in the form of a fish or a tortoise or a boar, they think he is another and that creature is another boar or another fish of this world. No! He is completely spiritual and completely transient to everything material. He may be seen out of his own accord when he gives darshan, when he gives vision to us. Only then he can be seen. And then in the 20th verse, it is said, evam yatantam vijane maam aha agochara giram gambhir shrakshanaya vaacha shuchap prashama yan iva. Seeing my attempts in that lonely place, the personality of Godhead who is transcendental to all mundane description spoke to me with gravity and pleasing words just to mitigate my grief. Krishna is very kind. Even though he gave a brief darshan to Narada, still to mitigate Narada's grief because Narada was at grief, he was not able to see again. Just to mitigate his grief, the Lord spoke to him.

---
### mercy, devotional service, separation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AOpqOuoNX0w?t=883
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even hearing the Lord is in no way inferior to seeing the Lord or any other experience."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the Lord's mercy allows one to hear and speak to Him, even though He is beyond mundane perception. He discusses the probationary stage of devotional service and the feelings of separation experienced by devotees, noting that hearing the Lord is as valuable as seeing Him.

Now, in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains, in the Vedas it is said, God is beyond the approach of mundane words and intelligence. avam manasa gochara. Yet, by his causeless mercy, one can have suitable senses to hear him or to speak to him. By his mercy, at the present moment, we are using our material senses to perceive material objects, but the completely spiritual form of the Lord or the spiritual sound of his transcendental vibration can be heard or can be seen by the Lord's mercy. So, this is the Lord's inconceivable energy. We cannot understand with our mundane logic. The Lord was very pleased with Narada Muni and therefore, the necessary strength was invested in him so that Narada could hear the Lord. It is not, however, possible for others to perceive directly the touch of the Lord during the probationary stage of regulative devotional service. Those who are practicing in the probationary stage of regulative devotional service are said to be in the probationary stage of regulative devotional service. That means, we are practicing. It is also called sadhana bhakti, the stage of practice. In the stage of practice, we are yet to reach the perfection. Mind you, Narada had reached perfection. But still, he had not yet become fully purified, 100% purified, yet, though he was very sincerely following and had come to a stage of pretty good advancement stage. So, when he heard the pleasing words of the Lord, the feelings of separation were to some extent mitigated. A devotee in love feels always the pangs of separation, and is therefore always enwrapped in transcendental ecstasy. Even hearing the Lord is in no way inferior to seeing the Lord or any other experience. Though, we always desire if there is a person, we want to see him face to face. We want to interact with him the way we interact with any person in this world. Especially, dealings of love between two persons means there is active reciprocation. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.6.21 | 15-11-2021
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### purity, material contamination, transcendence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TqNMFlPuMuw?t=54
**Verse References:** BG 10
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord doesn't have any tinge of materiality and those in this material world who have any tinge of material affection cannot approach him."
**Summary:** The Lord explains to Narada that one cannot see Him if they are not free from material taints, emphasizing the Lord's absolute purity and the contamination of materiality in conditioned souls.

In this session, we will be discussing from the first canto, 6th chapter, 21st verse of the Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is, O Narada, the Lord is speaking to Narada, I regret that during this lifetime you will not be able to see me anymore. Those who are incomplete in service, and who are not completely free from all material taints, can hardly see me. Now here the Lord is explaining to Narada that Narada is not yet freed from all material taints. That's why one cannot see in spite of all sincerity, sincere attempts and endeavors. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, the personality of Godhead is described in the Bhagavad Gita as the most pure. In the 10th chapter, Param Brahma Param Dhama Pavitram Paramam Bhavan. Pavitram means pure. Paramam Bhavan, Paramam Pavitram, Supreme pure, complete pure, 100% pure. And he is described also as the supreme and the absolute truth, Param Brahma. So there is no trace of a tinge of materiality in this person, unlike our personality, right now, in the material world, there is a contamination of materiality in this person. There is a contamination of materiality in this person. So that is the categorization of this personality. There is a contamination of the illusory influence of the material energy in our consciousness, and that's why we have a material conception of our self, of everything. Whereas the Lord, even when he comes to this material world, He is 100% pure, completely spiritual. There's no tinge of any materiality in this personality. But he is giving us this materiality, the same materiality that the Lord gave us. Even though he appears like a person who is created being of this world, he appears like that, son of Devaki, or he appears in his incarnation as Matsyavatara, he appears as a fish, but he is not touched by the material energy. It's not a material form, it's not a material creation. He comes in his original transcendental form only, though he appears as required, whichever form he desires to appear, to incarnate, to give darshan to his devotees, to mitigate the problems, the sufferings of his devotees. Paritranaya sadhuna. So, therefore, it is said here, thus, one who has the slightest tinge of material affection cannot approach him.

---
### detachment, modes of nature, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TqNMFlPuMuw?t=273
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To be completely freed from all material tinges is to become free from the mode of goodness also."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that to approach the Lord, one must be free from material affection, starting by detaching from passion and ignorance, which manifest as lust and greed, and ultimately transcending even the mode of goodness.

So, this is conversely also explained that the Lord doesn't have any tinge of materiality and those in this material world who have any tinge of material affection cannot approach him. Not possible. The beginning of devotional service starts from the point when one is freed from at least two forms of material modes, namely the modes of passion and ignorance. The result is exhibited by the signs of being freed from karma and lobha. Karma means lust, lobha means greed or covetousness. That is to say, one must be freed from the desires for sense satisfaction and the avarice for sense gratification. Generally material conception of life is I am seeking satisfaction of my senses and I am also desirous or greedy for getting more of sense enjoyment. These two things, kama and lobha, are the symptoms of rajas and tamas. To be completely freed from all material tinges is to become free from the mode of goodness also. As you know there are three gunas, three modes, rajas, tamas and sattva guna. Don't think sattva guna is something spiritual. No, it is also material.

---
### transcendence, spiritual platform, modes of nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TqNMFlPuMuw?t=383
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Unless we rise to the spiritual platform and become fixed up, we will not be able to see the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the need to transcend all three modes of material nature (goodness, passion, and ignorance) to reach the spiritual platform, emphasizing that even activities in the mode of goodness, like solitary meditation in a forest, do not guarantee seeing the Lord.

So we have to become completely free from all the three modes. So it is described here by Srila Prabhupada, to search out the audience of God in a lonely forest is considered to be in the mode of goodness. As is explained elsewhere, a liquor shop is in the mode of ignorance, tamas guna, and a city is in the mode of passion, as a place, talking of different places, influence in different places. And a forest is considered to be in the mode of goodness or sattvic place. One can go into the forest to attain spiritual perfection, but that does not mean that one can see the Lord personally there. Just because I go to the forest, and the forest is in the mode of goodness, simply by attempting to do some meditation or some spiritual practice there, we cannot see the Lord, simply like that. One must be completely freed from all material attachment, and be situated on the plane of transcendence. We have to rise above tamas guna, rajas guna and sattva guna, and come to the plane of transcendence, shuddha sattva, purified goodness, or transcendental platform. This alone will help the devotee to get in personal touch with the personality of God. Because the Lord is on the spiritual platform, unless we rise to the spiritual platform and become fixed up, we will not be able to see the Lord.

---
### deity worship, bhakti yoga, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TqNMFlPuMuw?t=503
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The deity form is completely a spiritual form, though it appears like a material form."
**Summary:** The speaker highlights the importance of deity worship (archana) as a superior method, especially in Kali Yuga, for connecting with the Lord, contrasting it with practices like forest meditation and emphasizing the deity's transcendental nature despite its material appearance.

The best method is that one should live at a place where the transcendental form of the Lord is worshipped. Now here Srila Prabhupada explains the importance of the deity form of the Lord in the temple, or even at one's home. The deity form is completely a spiritual form, though it appears like a material form. After all, the deity is made out of stone, or wood, or metal. But that doesn't mean it is a metallic form, or a clay form, or a stone form. No, it is not. The deity is not a material form. Why? Because the deity is also called Archamurti, or Archavigraha. The Lord is invited after the form of the Lord is prepared, according to Shastra. And then, by the mercy of the bona fide spiritual master, or the Acharya, the Lord agrees to come and reside in that form, and accept the worship, the service, the devotional service, agrees to give darshan to even people who don't have spiritual vision. That is the causeless mercy of the Lord. That's the mercy of the transcendental form of the Lord in his form as the deity. The temple of the Lord is a transcendental place, whereas the forest is a materially good habitation. This difference exists, that a temple of the Lord where the deity form is worshipped regularly is completely a transcendental place. It's not different from Vaikuntha, whereas the forest is a place which is in the mode of goodness, a sattvic place. So, forest still there is some material influence, but in the temple, it is completely a spiritual place. There is no material influence in the temple, just because of the presence of the archavigraha. Devotional service begins from the process of archana, which is better than going out in the forest, especially for us in Kali Yuga, where we are not equipped to do meditation, where we are not properly equipped or not prepared to properly undergo studies, properly engage in other forms of yoga other than bhakti yoga.

---
### bhakti yoga, preaching, devotee
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TqNMFlPuMuw?t=690
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "A pure devotee carries the Lord in his heart."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by advocating bhakti yoga, particularly archana and kirtana, as the most effective path to perfection, highlighting the example of Narada Muni and the transformative power of a pure devotee's presence.

Therefore, in this presentation, it is recommended, simply accept the process of bhakti yoga and don't ever even attempt any other form of yoga. It's not going to be successful. And Narada Muni, in his present life, he is describing his previous life to Vyasa Deva, if you remember this whole description. But in his present life, he is completely free from all material hankerings, because he is a pure devotee. He does not go into the forest, although he can turn any place into Vaikuntha simply by his presence. Simply by his presence, because a pure devotee carries the Lord in his heart. So wherever he goes, he turns that place into a completely transcendental place. So Narada Muni now, in his present life, he travels from one planet to another, throughout the universe he travels, simply to convert all categories of persons, men, gods, that is demigods, kinaras, gandharvas, rishis, munis, anyone who is willing, he wants to convert them into a devotee of the Supreme Lord. And there are examples by his activities, he has engaged many devotees, like Prahlad Maharaj, Dhruva Maharaj, so many others, in the transcendental service of the Lord. A pure devotee of the Lord, therefore follows in the footsteps of great devotees, like Narada Muni and Prahlad Maharaj, and engages his whole time in glorifying the Lord by the process of kirtana. So archana is the worship of the deity form, and it's always to be accompanied by kirtana, the glorification of the Lord, either by singing his holy, glorification of his holy name, or glorification of his activities or pastimes, like that. Such a preaching process is transcendental to all material qualities. So clearly, Srila Prabhupada is explaining, the advantage we have in the process of archana, kirtana, of course, Bhakti Yoga begins with Shravanam Kirtanam Vishnu, but of all the processes for a neophyte, especially in this age, archana is recommended. Either you directly worship, or if you are unable to worship, then you participate in the worship of the Lord in the temple. That's what you should do. In this way, you can actually very easily progress in Bhakti Yoga and come to the stage of perfection very quickly. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

### desire, purification, darshan
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/snqRvsLqR-k?t=87
**Verse References:** SB 1.6.22
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord is speaking and explaining something very important for Narada and for all of us to understand."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse and explains the context of Lord speaking to Narada Muni after giving him a brief glimpse (darshan) and then withdrawing from his sight.

Sakradya Darshitam Roopam Etat Kamayate Anagha Matkamas Shanakai Sadhu Sarvan Munchatih Richhayan. The meaning of this verse is, O virtuous one, you have only once seen my person and this is just to increase your desire for me because the more you hanker for me, the more you desire for me, the more you will be freed from all material desires. So the Lord is speaking to Narada Muni. After Narada Muni began meditation, he reached the stage of bhava, ecstasy. And in that stage of ecstasy, he was able to have a brief glimpse of the Lord in the heart. But then after that brief darshan, he lost sight of the Lord and then he tried again to get that darshan, but he could not. And when he was morose, that is when the Lord spoke to him. And the Lord is speaking and explaining something very important for Narada and for all of us to understand.

---

**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the Lord's words to Narada, explaining that the brief darshan was given to increase Narada's desire for the Lord, as complete purification of the heart is necessary to see the Lord's spiritual form.

So in the 21st verse is the beginning of the Lord's words, Lord's speaking to Narada from within the heart. O Narada, in the 21st verse, the previous session, we discussed this. I regret that during this lifetime, you will not be able to see me anymore. So just the brief darshan was given. So in this verse, the Lord is telling, you have only once seen my person and just to increase your desire for me, I have done it. Because till one is completely purified in the heart, one cannot actually see the Lord, the spiritual form of the Lord. It's not possible. And why did the Lord give darshan, that brief darshan? Even before Narada was fully purified, it is because to increase the hankering, the desire to see the Lord, to associate with the Lord in person.

### desire, purification, spiritual life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/snqRvsLqR-k?t=251
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It means to become free from all material desires. And that is possible not by just trying to negate all material desires, but rather replace material desires with spiritual desires."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srila Prabhupada, explaining that spiritual life doesn't mean becoming desireless, but rather purifying desires by replacing material desires with spiritual desires, specifically the desire to please the Lord.

And that will automatically free Narada from all material desires. So Srila Prabhupada very nicely explains on the purport. He says, a living being cannot be vacant of desires. You would have heard many times, spiritual life means we had to become desireless. Now becoming desireless, we have to understand very carefully. We cannot be completely vacant of all desires. So what does it mean to become desireless in order to advance in spiritual life? It means to become free from all material desires. And that is possible not by just trying to negate all material desires, but rather replace material desires with spiritual desires. Or as Srila Prabhupada explains elsewhere, we have to purify our desires. We have to purify our desires. In our material condition, we are full of material desires. But the same desires can be turned into spiritual desires, when we desire for the Lord. Instead of desiring for ourselves, we desire to please the Lord instead of desiring to please our own senses. So Srila Prabhupada explains a living being cannot be vacant of desires.

### desire, devotional service, entanglement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/snqRvsLqR-k?t=367
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Conversely, when he thinks, feels and wills for the service of the Lord, he becomes gradually freed from all entanglement. This is the wonderful thing about devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker continues quoting Srila Prabhupada, emphasizing that a living being must have desires, and that entanglement arises from material thinking, feeling, and willing. Devotional service, or bhakti, is presented as a positive process that gradually frees one from entanglement by directing these faculties towards the service of the Lord.

He is not a dead stone. What or who is vacant of all desires? It is dead matter. No desire. So living person must have desires. That is symptom of life. So Srila Prabhupada says further, he must be working, thinking, feeling and willing. But when he thinks, feels and wills materially, he becomes entangled. That is our present condition. We are entangled because we are thinking, feeling and willing materially. Conversely, when he thinks, feels and wills for the service of the Lord, he becomes gradually freed from all entanglement. This is the wonderful thing about devotional service, that you start thinking, feeling, willing for the service of the Lord. This is the process of bhakti. Process of devotion. Devotional service. A positive process. Automatically material thinking, feeling, willing will be replaced or purified when you start thinking, feeling, willing for the service of the Lord. And gradually we are freed from all entanglement. Thinking for the service of the Lord itself is spiritual.

### chanting, spiritual sound, transcendental
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/snqRvsLqR-k?t=550
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker contrasts chanting the Hare Krishna mantra with repeating a mundane phrase like 'coca-cola,' highlighting the transcendental nature of the mantra and how it doesn't become boring, unlike material sounds.

In the beginning you may not have taste for it like the way pure devotees have taste but then you increase it and you notice that it is not like a material sound vibration if I tell you keep saying coca-cola coca-cola coca-cola coca-cola how many times can you say that few times you cannot keep repeating that material sound again and again and again it will become hackneyed it will become boring but devotees have been chanting Hare Krishna for so long they chant every day the same mantra so many times but they never find it is boring they never experience any boredom why because this is spiritual sound this is transcendental it is full of spiritual nectar of course we don't experience the nectar immediately but certainly we can experience the difference in terms of not feeling the boredom that itself is a confirmation of the sound being transcendental the very fact that we can repeatedly chant day after day week after week month after month year after year and increase, keep increasing the chanting itself is proof that this is spiritual sound and therefore it increases.

### spiritual service, material service, satiation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/snqRvsLqR-k?t=660
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Material service has satiation... Whereas spiritual service of the lord has neither satiation nor end."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts material and spiritual service, using the analogy of eating chocolate to illustrate satiation in material activities, while emphasizing the endless and non-satiating nature of spiritual service to the Lord.

That is the transcendental nature of godly service, devotional service, spiritual service, spiritual activities like that. It is the transcendental nature of such service. Material service has satiation. What is satiation? You have experience that is eat a small piece of some edible, ordinary edible that you like to eat. Let's say it's a piece of chocolate a child likes. So you give the child a piece of chocolate. So you give the child a piece it'll eat and say very nice. You give more, it'll eat that second piece, very nice. Third piece, fourth piece. How much can the child go on eating, eating, eating, eating and experience more and more pleasure and satisfaction? There's a limit. So that limit is reached that's called satiation. You cannot eat anymore to derive more and more pleasure, or taste, or satisfaction out of eating the sweet. That is the nature of material service, or material activity, or material objects. Whereas spiritual service of the lord has neither satiation nor end. Even the material activity comes to an end with satiation. Even the material activity comes to an end with the body. The body is not permanent. The material body is not permanent. So life in this body is not going to continue forever, no. It is going to end when we have to quit the body at the time of death, we do. Leave this body.

**Summary:** The speaker reiterates the difference between material and spiritual service, emphasizing the eternal nature of spiritual service as it is connected to the soul, contrasting it with the temporary and satiating nature of material activities.

So spiritual service is not of the nature of material activity or material service because spiritual service continues life after life after life it is on the spiritual platform therefore it is eternal it's in the platform of the soul the soul is eternal therefore spiritual service is eternal material service has satiation whereas spiritual service of the Lord has neither satiation nor end what is satiation satiation means something material you cannot repeatedly do that activity to derive more and more pleasure or more and more satisfaction just like eating a piece of sweet meat or a chocolate you eat one piece very nice you eat a second piece okay this is also pleasurable but how much can you keep on eating and deriving more pleasure there's a limit to that everything material is like this that whereas spiritual service of the Lord has neither satiation nor end devotional service just like we chant Hare Krishna there is no satiation we go on chanting more and more and more and more it does not become boring it does not become hackneyed so therefore spiritual service is on the spiritual platform it is eternal one can go on increasing his hankerings for the loving transcendental service of the Lord and yet he will not find satiation or end.

This is the nature of a transcendental loving service of the Lord there is no satiation there is no end because it is not going to end with satiation the body by intense service of the Lord one can experience the presence of the Lord transcendental e therefore seeing the Lord means being engaged in his service because his service and his person are identical so we are to understand this point very carefully that anybody engaged in devotional service is actually seeing the lord through the service because the lord and his devotional service are identical of course it's difficult to understand in the beginning but in the words of the acharya we have to understand this fact about devotional service so the sincere devotee should go on with sincere service of the lord the lord will give proper direction as to how and where it has to be done so narada had no material desire and simply to increase his intense desire for the lord the lord gave him brief darshan and then said now continue your devotional service the way you have been doing and then one day you'll reach perfection you become completely purified then you'll reach perfection I'll stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna.

Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shri Vasadi Gauravakta Vrinda. ### devotional service, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9AXKbTASbBA?t=41
**Verse References:** SB 1.6.23
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By service of the Absolute Truth even for a few days, a devotee attains firm and fixed intelligence in me; consequently, he goes on to become my associate in the transcendental world after giving up the present deplorable material worlds."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces the 23rd verse of the 6th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining that even a short period of service to the Absolute Truth leads to firm intelligence and association with the Lord in the transcendental world, after leaving the material world.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 23rd verse of the 6th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam: Satsevaya Adirghaya Api Jatamai Dridhamatihi Hithwa Avadyam Imam Lokam Ganta Majjanata Masi. The meaning of this verse is Bhutana Madhyanata Masi. By service of the Absolute Truth even for a few days, a devotee attains firm and fixed intelligence in me; consequently, he goes on to become my associate in the transcendental world after giving up the present deplorable material worlds.

---
### spiritual master, service, bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9AXKbTASbBA?t=112
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Serving the Absolute Truth means rendering service unto the Absolute Personality of Godhead under the direction of the bona fide spiritual master."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the meaning of 'service to the Absolute Truth,' explaining, according to Prabhupada, that it involves serving the Absolute Personality of Godhead under the guidance of a bona fide spiritual master, who acts as a transparent medium between the devotee and the Lord.

So this is yet another verse which is spoken by the Supreme Lord, whose unseen but through his transcendental voice he is communicating to Narada. He is telling here, the Lord is telling Narada, by service of the Absolute Truth. So what does it mean, service of the Absolute Truth? That is explained by Prabhupada in the precepts of the Absolute Truth. Prabhupada says serving the Absolute Truth means rendering service unto the Absolute Personality of Godhead under the direction of the bona fide spiritual master. So very clear Prabhupada is explaining what does service of the Absolute Truth mean, Satsevaya. So serving Absolute Truth is having three features: Brahman, Paramatma, and the Supreme Lord are three features. As devotees, we are particularly concerned about the Bhagawan feature, the Absolute Personality of Godhead. So therefore Srila Prabhupada says serving the Absolute Truth means rendering service to the Absolute Personality of Godhead. Now how do you render service to the Absolute Personality of Godhead? Under the direction of the bona fide spiritual master, because we do not know, at least as neophyte devotees, we do not know who is God, or we have no way to approach God directly. So we are approaching God through the bona fide spiritual master. The bona fide spiritual master directs the disciple, directs the aspirant, spiritual aspirant. If the spiritual aspirant agrees to follow the spiritual master, so under the direction of the bona fide spiritual master, one has to render service to Krishna, the Absolute Personality of Godhead.

---
### spiritual master, devotional service, bhakti yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9AXKbTASbBA?t=258
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The bona fide spiritual master is described by Prabhupada as a transparent via medium between the Lord and the neophyte devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the role of the spiritual master as a 'transparent via medium' between the devotee and the Lord, emphasizing the direct experience of offering service through the guru. He then highlights the special feature of Bhakti Yoga, where even a short period of service yields significant results, unlike other yoga systems that require extensive time.

Now the bona fide spiritual master is described by Prabhupada as a transparent via medium between the Lord and the neophyte devotee. What's the meaning of this, transparent via medium? I am there, Lord is there, in between is the spiritual master. So via medium, and what kind of via medium is the spiritual master between the neophyte devotee and the Lord? He is a transparent via medium. Transparent means it is as if I am directly approaching the Lord, but still we should not forget I am approaching through the spiritual master. So the experience, Srila Prabhupada says, is direct. When I offer a flower to the Lord, I am offering through my spiritual master, but it is as good as me does, I am directly offering flower to Krishna. So by service of the Absolute Truth even for a few days, adhirgaya api, not for very long time. So this is the special feature of Bhakti Yoga, unlike the other yoga systems which take a very very very long time for reaching perfection, achieving the ultimate result or goal, takes a very long time. Panthastu Koti Shatavatsara Sampragamyo, see Koti Shatavatsara, hundreds of crores of years, whereas Bhakti Yoga is this lifetime, this lifetime, immediately, as soon as possible. So adhirgaya api, even for a few days, a devotee attains firm and fixed intelligence in me. What is this attaining intelligence in Krishna?

---
### intelligence, spiritual master, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9AXKbTASbBA?t=394
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even for a few days, the training is potent, it is very very powerful, it is effective, and very quickly this intelligence becomes fixed in devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that attaining intelligence in Krishna means that even a neophyte devotee, through the direction and training of the spiritual master, can quickly develop firm and fixed intelligence in rendering service to the Lord, despite their initial imperfect material senses.

That is explained by Srila Prabhupada that the neophyte devotee has no ability to directly approach the Lord because he has got imperfect material senses which are useless for receiving the Lord. Therefore the direction of the spiritual master is essential, and by such direction one gets trained up in serving the Lord. Even if for some days one gets training from the spiritual master, then such a neophyte devotee gets intelligence in rendering service to the Lord. So when he gets intelligence, that is described as devotee attains firm and fixed intelligence in the Supreme Lord Krishna. That is described here as mati means intelligence, firm and fixed intelligence is attained by such training by the spiritual master even for a few days. The training is potent, it is very very powerful, it is effective, and very quickly this intelligence becomes fixed in devotional service.

---
### Krishna, devotional service, material world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9AXKbTASbBA?t=488
**Verse References:** BG 4
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You are all mine, you belong to me, you come back to me, you live with me, you engage always in thinking of me."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the result of devotional service: becoming an associate of Krishna in the transcendental world, leaving behind the deplorable material world filled with miseries. He quotes Krishna stating that all beings belong to Him and should return to Him.

Consequently, as a result of this, he goes on to become my associate in the transcendental world. See the game of rendering service to the Lord. We all belong to the Lord, we all belong to Krishna. We are all Krishnas. In the fourth chapter of Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says you are all mine, you belong to me, you come back to me, you live with me, you engage always in thinking of me. So this closeness of relationship is very very important to understand. So one goes on to become Krishna's associate in transcendental world, in Krishna's personal abode, not in this material world, no, because it is described this happens after giving up the present deplorable material world. Hitwa avadyam imam lokam, this word avadyam, avadyam means deplorable. It is not the perfect place for a devotee, this material world, filled as it is with miseries. Krishna describes this material world, naapnuvanti mahatmanah. If we don't practice devotional service, then we keep taking repeated birth in this material world and suffer the consequences of birth in the material world, suffering different miseries, whereas by devotional service we have become eligible to go to Krishna's personal abode to become one of his associates.

---
### spiritual master, liberation, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9AXKbTASbBA?t=616
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna takes personal charge of his devotees."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain Prabhupada's teachings on the potency of even a few days of training in devotional service under a spiritual master, leading to liberation from the material world and promotion to Krishna's personal abode. He emphasizes Krishna's personal care for His devotees and their complete dependence on Him.

So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport that even if the neophyte devotee gets trained by the spiritual master how to render service for a few days, it is very very potent, effective it is very quickly we are able to perfect our service. This leads ultimately to get free from perpetual inhabitation in the material world. Material existence is such that we have been here for millions and millions of births in ignorance, simply with a desire to enjoy materially, hoping every time to succeed in getting the happiness we are really looking for, which is never available in this world, which is never available in any material activity, but we somehow have some hope, we have false hope, durashaya ye bahirartham anina. In the Bhagavatam elsewhere it is explained durashaya it is a hopeless hope, there is no possibility, but still we are cheated by maya, we have some hope which is never to be fulfilled, durashaya. So here Prabhupada says ultimately we get free from the perpetual inhabitation in the material world, we no longer will have to be in the material world, we have nothing to do with this material existence, and what happens, we are promoted to the transcendental world. Krishna will ensure that we are promoted. Krishna takes personal charge of his devotees. Yogi may transfer himself by the strength of his yogic power, but a devotee he simply is dependent, and Krishna likes that. Krishna is very pleased when we declare our entire dependence upon him, because we belong to him, he is our well wisher, suhurdam sarva bhutanam, he is our everything. So such supreme lord, it is very good, very nice to depend on him completely, and he takes charge, he will transfer us, he will promote us to his personal abode.

---
### liberation, devotional service, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9AXKbTASbBA?t=774
**Verse References:** BG 9.2, BG 2.40
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A little advancement on the path of Krishna consciousness is going to guarantee freedom from all great dangers."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by describing the state of liberated associates in Krishna's abode, emphasizing the complete purification and liberation from material bondage achieved through devotional service. He assures that even small acts of devotion are eternally beneficial and guarantee freedom from degradation, contrasting this with the impermanence of material gains.

And what happens in Krishna's personal abode after we transfer, we are transferred there by Krishna, we become one of the liberated associates of the lord in his kingdom, liberated associate. So that is very important. Such transfer means automatically there is liberation from material bondage, purification. Purification is 100% complete when we are transferred to the spiritual world. By devotional service we become purified, and only when we are fully purified we can go there, but that happens very fast, we don't have to wait for very long time. Of course in the beginning we may sometimes feel I am not making any progress, but the fact is that every chanting of Hare Krishna, every small little service done in devotion to Krishna is actually accounted and it is to our permanent credit, it is eternally beneficial, it is never lost, even little bit is never lost. In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna explains this, a little advancement on the path of Krishna consciousness is going to guarantee freedom from all great dangers. One doesn't have to actually suffer degradation at all, never suffer any degradation, only progressively keep advancing advancing, and unlike material gain, spiritual advancement is never lost. Materially whatever I gain now I have to leave behind, but spiritual advancement is never lost. So this is the assurance by the Lord to Narada. I will stop here, thank you very much Hare Krishna.

---

**Verse References:** SB 1.6.24
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker introduces verses 24 and 25 of the 6th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on the Lord's assurance to Narada that devotion is eternal and protected by divine grace.

In this session, we will be discussing from the 24th and the 25th verses of the 6th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. First, the 24th verse: Matir Mayi Nibaddheyam Na Vipadyeta Karihichit Praja Sarga Nirodhepi Smritischa Mad Anugrahat. The meaning of this verse is intelligence engaged in my devotion cannot be thwarted at any time, even at the time of creation as well as at the time of annihilation; your remembrance will continue by my mercy.

### devotion, eternality, divine grace
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GMX1kIOkX84?t=114
**Verse References:** SB 1.6.24
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everything in relationship with the Lord is eternal; nothing is temporary. There is no question of any loss in devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the verse, explaining that the Lord is speaking to Narada, emphasizing the eternality of devotional service and the absence of loss in such endeavors.

The Supreme Lord is speaking to Narada. He is unseen but he is heard after Narada had a brief glimpse of the Lord in the heart, and he tried to get that darshan once again, but he could not. And the Lord spoke to him to pacify Narada. So in this verse, the Lord is speaking to Narada. He is explaining something very important: intelligence engaged in my devotion cannot be thwarted at any time. So everything in relationship with the Lord is eternal. Nothing is temporary. There is no question of any loss in devotional service.

### eternality, devotional service, material vs spiritual
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GMX1kIOkX84?t=175
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everything spiritual is permanent, eternal, never subject to any loss or deterioration by time. This is a very important principle we have to remember, we have to understand, and we have to apply."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srila Prabhupada's purport, contrasting the temporary nature of material pursuits with the eternal nature of devotional service, urging listeners to dedicate their time to devotional activities.

Sri La Prabhupada explains in the purport devotional service rendered to the personality of Godhead never goes in vain since the personality of Godhead is eternal. Intelligence supplied in his service or anything done in his relation is also permanent. We have experienced materially whatever we may do, it is not going to last forever; it is subject to annihilation by time. Everything material is like that. In fact, the whole creation itself is completely annihilated at the end of Brahma's life. So everything material is subject to destruction by time; everything spiritual is permanent, eternal, never subject to any loss or deterioration by time. This is a very important principle we have to remember, we have to understand, and we have to apply. So how should we be spending our time? We should be very careful to spend our time exclusively in devotional activities and devotional service.

---
### devotional service, progress, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GMX1kIOkX84?t=265
**Verse References:** BG 6
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Anything done in devotional service bears permanent result; it's never lost, and one continues devotional advancement in the next life from where he left off in his previous life."
**Summary:** The speaker references the Bhagavad Gita, explaining that devotional service accumulates birth after birth and is never lost, even if incomplete in the current life.

In the Bhagavad Gita, it is said that such transcendental service rendered unto the personality of Godhead accumulates birth after birth. See, it's not lost. We may make some progress by doing devotional service in this life. Even if we don't complete, there is no loss. Arjuna asked this question in the sixth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. Arjuna asked what happens if somebody takes to devotional service but does not complete? Isn't he a loser both spiritually and materially because for taking up devotional service, one sacrifices material enjoyment, material engagements, so he loses materially as well as spiritually because spiritually he is not successful. So does it mean whatever devotional activity is performed is completely a waste of time? No, Krishna says no, anything done in devotional service bears permanent result; it's never lost, and one continues devotional advancement in the next life from where he left off in his previous life.

---
### spiritual advancement, eternality, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GMX1kIOkX84?t=362
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Unlike material gain in this life which is never carried forward to the next life or to the future, spiritual advancement is always eternally carried forward; it is never lost."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of material wealth versus spiritual advancement, highlighting that spiritual progress is eternally carried forward, unlike material possessions.

This is very important. Supposing you have earned a lot of wealth, do you carry any credit for that wealth earned in this life? If you are unable to exhaust that wealth or spend it off, you have a huge bank balance, so when you quit the body, what happens to that bank balance? It doesn't go with you. You don't get any credit in your next life; you have to begin again. So unlike material gain in this life which is never carried forward to the next life or to the future, spiritual advancement is always eternally carried forward; it is never lost. So therefore, when the devotee is fully matured, the total service counted together makes him eligible to enter into the association of the personality of Godhead. So even if we make some progress, that progress is permanently to the credit of the devotee. Devotional service is associated with the soul, not with the body, so body is replaced, body is changed, but the soul is eternal. The person as spirit soul is eternal; his devotional credit is also eternal.

---
### divine sound, supreme authority, gratitude
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GMX1kIOkX84?t=473
**Verse References:** SB 1.6.25
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Supreme Lord is a supreme authority personified by sound; he could clearly hear the Lord speaking, but he could not see the Lord, unseen by eyes."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces verse 25, describing Narada's experience of hearing the Supreme Lord's voice without seeing Him, emphasizing the Lord's supreme authority and Narada's gratitude.

Verse number 25: etavad uktopara rama tanmahad bhutam nabholingam alingam ishwaram aham chatasmai mahatam mahiyase hirsnavanamam vidadhe anukampitaham. The meaning of this verse is then that supreme authority personified by sound and unseen by eyes but most wonderful stopped speaking feeling a sense of gratitude I offered my obeisances unto him bowing my head. So Narada has heard the Supreme Lord speak and in his own words he is describing it was a most wonderful speech that he heard and he is describing here that it was a supreme authority. The Supreme Lord is a supreme authority personified by sound; he could clearly hear the Lord speaking, but he could not see the Lord, unseen by eyes.

---
### absolute nature, divine sound, identity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GMX1kIOkX84?t=553
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord is fully present in his name. If I say Krishna, in these two syllables, the Lord is fully present as transcendental sound."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srila Prabhupada's purport, explaining the absolute nature of the Supreme Lord, where His name, form, qualities, and instructions are identical to Him, unlike material objects and their representations.

So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport that the personality of Godhead was not seen but only heard does not make any difference in spiritual life. We learn about the absolute nature of the Supreme Lord, the personality of Godhead. What is the meaning of absolute nature? The Lord, the person, personality of the Lord, his name, his form, his qualities, his speech, his instructions, his abode, his paraphernalia, everything is identical with him 100%. That means the Lord is fully present in his name. If I say Krishna, in these two syllables, the Lord is fully present as transcendental sound. We are able to hear that sound Hare Krishna or Krishna, the name Krishna, but in that sound he is fully present, unlike a material sound where we say this is a table. The sound table refers to another object called the table. The sound table and object table are two distinct different entities. We make the association by saying this is called a table. That is not the case with the Lord's name or the Lord's instructions or the Lord's form or the Lord's qualities or his activities, descriptions of his features all are identical with him.

---
### Vedas, transcendental sound, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GMX1kIOkX84?t=684
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He is seen and realized through the transcendental sound of the Vedas. The Vedas are again non-different from him because Vedas are describing the absolute personality of Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the Vedas were produced by the Lord's breathing and that He is realized through the transcendental sound of the Vedas, emphasizing the importance of hearing and realizing Vedic knowledge.

So it is explained here by Srila Prabhupada further the personality of Godhead produced the four Vedas by his breathing. This is what the Vedas say. How did he give the Vedas in the beginning of the book of the creation? He produced the Vedas, the four Vedas, the original Vedic knowledge by his breathing. And he is seen and realized through the transcendental sound of the Vedas. The Vedas are again non-different from him because Vedas are describing the absolute personality of Godhead. Therefore he is seen and realized through the transcendental sound of the Vedas. Formerly there was no need of any written books; simply the teacher would recite the Vedas, the student would hear the recitation by the master, by the teacher, and with full attention the student would realize the subject matter completely.

---
### hearing, Bhagavatam, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GMX1kIOkX84?t=772
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Simply by hearing Bhagavatam, you can realize Krishna, and that's how Parikshit Maharaj realized, that's how Shukadeva Goswami realized; he simply heard the Bhagavatam from his father Vyasadeva."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the power of hearing the Bhagavatam and Bhagavad Gita, explaining that they are non-different from Krishna and can lead to realization when heard from a bona fide spiritual master.

So this is the method. Even Srimad Bhagavatam recommends that you simply hear the Bhagavatam and you can realize Krishna because Bhagavatam is non-different from Krishna. Bhagavatam is the incarnation, the literary incarnation of the Supreme Personality of Godhead Krishna. So simply by hearing Bhagavatam, you can realize Krishna, and that's how Parikshit Maharaj realized, that's how Shukadeva Goswami realized; he simply heard the Bhagavatam from his father Vyasadeva. Vyasadeva heard from Narada the Bhagavatam and realized the subject matter, realized subject matter of Krishna. Vyasadeva heard from Narada. Narada heard from Brahma. Brahma heard from Krishna himself. So it is explained here the Bhagavad Gita is the sound representation of the Lord and there is no difference in identity. Bhagavad Gita is personally spoken by Krishna to Arjuna and through Arjuna to all of us, to everyone, everyone in the whole world. So when Krishna spoke, whatever words came out of Krishna's lotus mouth, that is identical with Krishna himself. So even today we can hear the Bhagavad Gita, we can recite the Bhagavad Gita, we can study the Bhagavad Gita, we can understand the message of the Gita when it is explained by a spiritual master, bona fide spiritual master in parampara. In this way, Bhagavad Gita is non-different from Krishna.

---

So the conclusion is that the Lord can be seen and heard by person chanting of the transcendental sound, the transcendental sound of the Bhagavad Gita when Krishna spoke the lotus mouth sound could be the sound of Bhagavatam, the sound of the Hare Krishna mantra, the sound of Krishna's instructions, anything. So transcendental sound we simply have to chant and in this way by hearing repeatedly, persistently, with full attention, offenselessly, we can fully realize Krishna. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.
