### Dharma Yudha, spiritual benefit, heavenly planets, Svarupa
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C6fL6Ob2lX4?t=309
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We should always remember this is a Dharma Yudha. It is a battle fought according to religious principles. So in such a Dharma Yudha, whoever dies fighting, a Kshatriya who dies fighting in this battle, whichever party they belong to, it doesn't matter, they will at least be promoted to heavenly planets."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of Dharma Yudha, explaining that those who died fighting in the Battle of Kurukshetra attained either promotion to heavenly planets or their Svarupa (original constitutional position) in the spiritual world, especially if they saw Krishna at the time of death.

But here, the description is that Krishna, the fighting was going on for 18 days and whoever was killed on the battlefield, in this battle of Kurukshetra, the dead warriors obtained their deserved destinations. We should always remember this is a Dharma Yudha. It is a battle fought according to religious principles. So in such a Dharma Yudha, whoever dies fighting, a Kshatriya who dies fighting in this battle, whichever party they belong to, it doesn't matter. They will at least be promoted to heavenly planets. Guaranteed elevation in their status in life. And those who died seeing Krishna on the battlefield, they attained their Svarupa, their original constitutional position in the spiritual world. They were directly promoted to the spiritual world. This is what Srila Prabhupada explains in a discourse given on these two verses. So a Dharma Yudha is not at all bad. In fact, it is spiritually beneficial for everyone. And that is exactly what Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita also. While Arjuna at the beginning of the battle of Kurukshetra refuses to fight, Krishna tells him this is a Dharma Yudha and you are a Kshatriya. It is your duty to fight in this Dharma Yudha. Either you die on the battlefield while fighting and you attain minimum the heavenly planets or you enjoy a flourishing kingdom if you win this battle. In either case, there is no loss for Arjuna, for the partners. So here it is described. The warriors who were killed on the battlefield of Kurukshetra attained their deserved destinations. Minimum is promotion to heavenly planets. And others who were fortunate enough to die while dying, they were able to see Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead. They got their Svarupa. They were promoted directly to the spiritual world.

---
### Krishna's plan, Dharma, Yudhishthira
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C6fL6Ob2lX4?t=490
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna's intervention is not partiality on his part. He is not partial to the part of us. Krishna has got a transcendental objective."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the fight between Duryodhana and Bhimasena, Krishna's guidance to Bhima, and Krishna's transcendental objective in the Battle of Kurukshetra, which included reducing the burden of the earth and establishing a God-conscious Kshatriya like Yudhishthira to rule the world.

And in the end, there was a fight between Duryodhana and Bhimasena. So they were fighting with a club in the hand. One on one they were fighting. And Krishna was there present to guide Bhima. Krishna was there to guide Bhima. That this Duryodhana, it's not easy to defeat him or Bhima. It is said that though both were expert in fighting with the club. Still. Bhima had more bodily strength as compared to Duryodhana. And Duryodhana had better skill in fighting with the club. So actually the fighting would have gone on for many many days. A very long time. Had it not been for the intervention of Krishna. We should always remember. Krishna's intervention is not partiality on his part. He is not partial to the part of us. Krishna has got a transcendental objective. Krishna wants this battle of Kurukshetra for some very important reasons. One is that the burden of the earth had to be brought down, reduced. And the second reason is that Krishna wants a God conscious Kshatriya like Yudhishthira to rule the whole world. Because the purpose of Kshatriya. Because the purpose of this material creation is that people are guided towards ultimate liberation from material bondage. And a king like Yudhishthira who is Krishna conscious. He is the perfect ruler to guide the citizens towards spiritual emancipation. So Krishna wanted the Pandavas to win this battle.

---
### Bhagavad Gita, Bhagavatam, Krishna Katha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/C6fL6Ob2lX4?t=661
**Verse References:** BG
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Krishna Katha given in the form of Bhagavatam is beginning from the end of the battle of Kurukshetra."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by describing how Krishna guided Bhima to defeat Duryodhana, leading to Duryodhana's death and Ashwatthama's unlawful killing of Draupadi's sons. He then connects this to the beginning of the Bhagavatam's narration, contrasting it with the Krishna Katha of the Bhagavad Gita, which occurred before the battle.

And it was not easy to defeat, for Bhima to defeat Duryodhana. But still when Krishna advised or indicated to Bhima that you have to actually use your club to beat Duryodhana below the waist. Now beating below the waist is against the rules of Kshatriya conduct in Dharma Yudha. But still because Krishna indicated to Bhima. Bhima did that. And Duryodhana fell down when he was hit below the waist. And his spine was broken. And after that of course Duryodhana died. At that time when he had just fallen down due to that fighting. The son of Dronacharya, Ashwatthama. He in order to please Duryodhana. Ashwatthama was fighting on the side of the Kauravas. So he beheaded the sleeping sons of Draupadi. Draupadi had five husbands. The five Pandavas. So she had one son through each of the Pandavas. And she had five sons. So all of them were killed. Totally unlawfully by Ashwatthama. And he brought those five heads to prove that he has killed them. And he delivered them to Duryodhana. Duryodhana however was not at all pleased. Because it is totally unlawful. And he disapproved of the sinuous act. That is what this verse describes. He was not at all pleased by this. He condemned Ashwatthama for unlawfully killing the five sons of Draupadi. Now this is where the Bhagavatam begins. Narration of Bhagavatam begins. We already have Krishna's instructions to Arjuna and to Arjuna and to all of us. The Krishna Katha in the form of Bhagavad Gita before the beginning of the battle of Kurukshetra. And the Krishna Katha given in the form of Bhagavatam is beginning from the end of the battle of Kurukshetra. So we will hear the narration of the Bhagavatam. How it is connected to Krishna consciousness in the further verses. I will stop here. Thank you very much.

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the context of the lecture, focusing on verses 15 of the 7th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, describing Draupadi's grief over the loss of her sons and Arjuna's attempt to console her.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 15th and the 16th verses of the 7th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. First the 15th verse. Mata Shishunam Nidhanam Sutanam Nishamya Ghoram Paritapyamana Tadarudad Vashpakala Kulakshi Tam Santwayan Ah Kiritam Mali. The meaning of these words: Draupadi the mother of the five children of the Pandavas after hearing of the massacre of her sons began to cry in distress with eyes full of tears trying to pacify her in her great loss Arjuna spoke to her thus. So in these words Arjuna is referred to as Kiritamali. And Arjuna is trying to pacify Draupadi.

It's natural for the mother who has lost all her five children not while they were fighting in the battle but when they were unlawfully killed when they were sleeping it is totally against Kshatriya conduct. So naturally a mother will feel very much. So naturally a mother will feel very much. So naturally a mother will feel very much. So naturally a mother will think that oh my god too much discharge due to the loss of her all five sons and what Arjuna spoke to Draupadi to pacify her is there in the next. The 16th verse Tadashuchaste Pramarjami Bhadre Yatكرam Am Comedy밧시 Yad Brahma Bandho Shira Atatayinaha Gandiva Muktair Vishikhair Upahare Thvakramya Yatsnasya Siddhagdha Putra. The meaning of this 16th verse: O gentle lady. Here is Arjuna pacifying Draupadi. O gentle lady. When I present you with the head of that Brahmana after beheading him with arrows from my Gandiva bow, I shall then wipe the tears from your eyes and pacify you. Then after burning your son's bodies, you can take your bath standing on his head. So these are the words spoken by Arjuna to pacify Draupadi.

### ethics, brahmana, aggression
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IpAhl6FfgmI?t=219
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Brahma Bandhu is one who has taken birth in a Brahmana family from a Brahmana father but he himself does not qualify as a Brahmana."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Arjuna's understanding of the situation, emphasizing that Ashwatthama, despite being the son of Dronacharya, is referred to as 'Brahma Bandhu' because his actions disqualify him from being considered a true Brahmana.

That Arjuna, he understands that Ashwathama is the son of his teacher Dronacharya. So the consideration that he is the son of his teacher, that consideration is lost when he has behaved in a very wrong way. And Arjuna is specifically referring to Ashwathama, the son of Dronacharya, as Brahma Bandho. That means he's not a Brahmana. He is not qualified as a Brahmana. He doesn't have the qualification of a Brahmana. He is called Brahma Bandhu. These terms are used in the Vedic literature, Dvija Bandhu or Brahma Bandhu. Brahma Bandhu is one who has taken birth in a Brahmana family from a Brahmana father but he himself does not qualify as a Brahmana. So such a person is called a Brahma Bandhu.

### aggression, violence, justice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IpAhl6FfgmI?t=350
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "An enemy who sets fire to the house, one who administers poison, one who attacks all of a sudden with deadly weapons, plunders wealth, or usurps agricultural fields or entices one's wife is called an aggressor."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srila Prabhupada's purport, defining an aggressor ('attatayinah') as someone who commits heinous acts like arson, poisoning, or attacking with deadly weapons, justifying punishment even if the aggressor is a Brahmana.

And he is also, he is an aggressor. So that is explained in the purport by Shri La Prabhupada: an enemy who sets fire to the house, one who administers poison, one who attacks all of a sudden with deadly weapons, plunders wealth, or usurps agricultural fields or entices one's wife is called an aggressor. He is called an attatayinah, an aggressor. Such an aggressor, though he be a Brahmana or a so called son of a Brahmana, has to be punished in all circumstances. Punishment is due to such an aggressor, attatayinah.

---
### duty, ethics, kshatriya dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IpAhl6FfgmI?t=406
**Verse References:** BG 1.36
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even if somebody like Bhishma and Drona may attack me, I don't want to retaliate because they are worthy of my respect even though they are aggressors."
**Summary:** The speaker draws a parallel to Arjuna's dilemma in the Bhagavad Gita, where he hesitated to fight against respected elders, even if they were aggressors, highlighting the complexities of duty and ethical considerations.

In the Bhagavad Gita also, this reference is made by Arjuna. In the Bhagavad Gita also, this reference is made by Arjuna that even if somebody like Bhishma and Drona may attack me, I don't want to retaliate because they are worthy of my respect even though they are aggressors. I don't want to retaliate. This is Arjuna's position as described in the first chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. Similarly here, similarly here, Arjuna is referring to the son of Dronacharya Aswathama as Brahma Bandhu and as attatayinah. He is an aggressor and he is not qualified Brahmana. So such an aggressor who is called an aggressor, now here different different situations under which somebody may be called an aggressor, an enemy who sets fire to the house. Even if somebody is enemy, Kshatriya code of conduct is Kshatriya should invite, challenge the enemy and they should have a fight in a proper place according to the code of conduct for Kshatriyas. So even if there is some enmity, that doesn't justify setting fire to the house of the enemy, you know, because in that house the other family members are also staying, so they don't deserve to be killed or they don't deserve to be harmed in any way. That is very important consideration.

### ethics, violence, justice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IpAhl6FfgmI?t=534
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Such aggressors according to Shastra have to be punished and there is no sin involved in giving them punishment."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to define actions that constitute aggression, such as administering poison, attacking unexpectedly, plundering wealth, or enticing someone's wife, emphasizing that such aggressors deserve punishment according to the scriptures.

So how can a Kshatriya who knows the principle set fire to somebody else's house? He becomes immediately an aggressor if he does this. Or somebody who administers poison. Enemy is killed in a proper battle, not killed by other methods, administering poison like this, beheading the children who are sleeping so that they don't have an opportunity to defend themselves. So that is not at all acceptable, administering poison, beheading somebody who is sleeping, this is not acceptable. Or somebody who attacks all of a sudden with deadly weapons, that means immediate death, somebody attacks, that is also unlawful, completely unlawful and such a person is an aggressor. One who plunders wealth, there is no provision in Kshatriya code of conduct for taking away forcibly the enemy's wealth, no they cannot. Or enticing one's wife, the enemy's wife, he cannot entice, that is completely wrong for a Kshatriya, it is completely wrong. So such aggressors according to Shastra have to be punished and there is no sin involved in giving them punishment.

---
### justice, brahmana, villainy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IpAhl6FfgmI?t=652
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "There is no question of sin in killing such a Brahmana son who proved to be a villain."
**Summary:** The speaker reiterates that Arjuna was fully aware of Ashwatthama's Brahmana lineage but was justified in his promise to behead him due to Ashwatthama's heinous act of killing the sleeping sons of Draupadi, comparing him to a butcher and stating that there is no sin in punishing such a villain.

When Arjuna promised to behead the aggressor named Ashwatthama, he knew well that Ashwatthama was the son of a Brahmana. It is not that Arjuna was not conscious that Ashwatthama was the son of a Brahmana, he was very much aware, but then he told Draupadi I am going to cut off the head of this Ashwatthama because that is what he did to the five sons of Draupadi when they were asleep, he cut off their heads. Now here Arjuna is directly going to challenge Ashwatthama and take action against him. So Arjuna knows very well Ashwatthama was the son of a Brahmana, still he is promising Draupadi I am going to cut off his head. Now Prabhupada says here the so called Brahmana acted like a butcher. Like butcher kills some innocent animal the animal cannot even defend itself. So similarly this Ashwatthama has killed those five sleeping sons of Draupadi like a butcher only, cutting off the head. There is no question of sin in killing such a Brahmana son who proved to be a villain. This Ashwatthama proves to be totally unqualified for being shown any mercy.

### ethics, punishment, bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IpAhl6FfgmI?t=758
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even if somebody is a Brahmana if he misbehaves then he deserves to be punished in accordance with the kind of violation he has done."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing the significance of this narration in the Bhagavatam, highlighting that the discussion about Arjuna's actions and the justification for punishing Ashwatthama will be presented in detail, ultimately reinforcing the principle that even a Brahmana deserves punishment for misdeeds.

So this instance that is described here in the Bhagavatam, in the beginning of the Bhagavatam, the narration begins, Suta Goswami is narrating the history, it is very significant and this discussion will be held in great detail about is it proper for Arjuna to kill Dronacharya's son, what kind of punishment does he deserve under the circumstances and what are all the different considerations for either punishing him or for excusing him and not giving him certain punishment. So many considerations and all those will be systematically presented by Vyasa Deva the author of this Bhagavatam very nicely, but in general the principle is even if somebody is a Brahmana if he misbehaves then he deserves to be punished in accordance with the kind of violation he has done. So that is always a important consideration in spite of the person being a son of a Brahmana or being a Brahmana himself, Brahmana has to be very very careful how he conducts himself. So I will stop here, thank you very much Hare Krishna.

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses to be discussed, focusing on Arjuna's pursuit of Ashwatthama for his heinous act and Duryodhana's disapproval.

In this session, we will be discussing the 17th and 18th verses of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. From this chapter, the narration of the Bhagavatam by Suta Goswami begins, and the particular incident that is being discussed in this chapter is the son of Drona punished, Ashwatthama is being punished because he beheaded the sleeping sons of Draupadi. So the 17th verse: Iti-priyaam Valgvi chitra jal payi Sasaantva itva chut緊 mithra Soottha Anvaadyravat damshita ugradhanva Kapitvajo guru putram ratvena. The meaning of this verse is Arjuna, who is guided by the infallible Lord as friend and driver, thus satisfied the dear lady by such statements. Arjuna satisfied Draupadi, then he dressed in armor and armed himself with furious weapons, and getting into his chariot, he set out to follow Ashwatthama, the son of his martial teacher. So Ashwatthama had been rejected even by Duryodhana. Duryodhana did not appreciate this unlawful act of Ashwatthama, and Ashwatthama was actually conscious that his life is in danger, so he had gone away somewhere.

### theology, infallibility, transcendence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IWOOkDe4cmM?t=169
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Supreme Lord and his pure devotees, they never fall down even though they come to the material world, they are always transcendentally situated."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Krishna's role as Arjuna's guide and driver, emphasizing Krishna's infallible nature and the transcendental position of the Lord and his devotees.

Arjuna was having Krishna as his friend and driver. Here, Krishna is described as Achyutaa, infallible Lord. What is the meaning of infallible? He never falls down from his transcendental position unless he is in a deep trance state. Like we, the living entities who desire to enjoy materially, we give up that spiritual platform on which we are eternally situated in our relationship with Krishna. We give up that, and we come to the material platform. So this is called a fall, a fall down, whereas the Supreme Lord and his pure devotees, they never fall down even though they come to the material world. They are always transcendentally situated, whether in the material world or in the spiritual world, or any situation, any time, any place, they are always transiently situated. So Arjuna was guided by the infallible Lord, the Supreme Lord, who is never subject to any deterioration.

---
### narrative, fear, flight

**Verse References:** SB 1.7.18
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ashwatthama the murderer of the princess seeing from a great distance Arjuna coming at him with great speed fled in his chariot panic-stricken just to save his life as Brahma fled in fear from Shiva."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the narrative, describing Arjuna's pursuit of Ashwatthama and Ashwatthama's desperate flight, comparing it to Brahma fleeing from Shiva.

Arjuna satisfied Draupadi by making significant statements. Now, in order to punish Ashwathama, Arjuna has dressed himself in armor and armed himself with furious weapons, and he got into his chariot, he set out to follow Ashwathama. The 18th verse: The meaning of this verse is Ashwatthama, the murderer of the princess, seeing from a great distance Arjuna coming at him with great speed, fled in his chariot panic-stricken just to save his life as Brahma fled in fear from Shiva. So Arjuna is trying to capture Ashwathama to punish him, but Ashwathama, on seeing that Arjuna is coming to capture him, Ashwatthama is himself going in his chariot running away from Arjuna just to save his life.

---
### analogy, puranas, anger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IWOOkDe4cmM?t=350
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That such an exalted personality as Brahma, even he became illusioned by the sight of his beautiful daughter."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the analogy of Brahma fleeing from Shiva, explaining the story from the Puranas where Brahma became infatuated with his daughter, infuriating Shiva.

He knew now his life was in danger, and here the description is given about Ashwathama running away in fear. The comparison is made just as Brahma ran away in fear of Lord Shiva. Now Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport that the word Kaha that is used in the last line of this 18th verse. Now here the word Kaha refers to Brahma. Or if it is read as Yathar Kaha, then Arkaha refers to Surya or the sun god. So both those meanings are taken and explained by Prabhupada. According to the reading matter, either Kaha or Arkaha, there are two references in the Puranas. The Acharyas never speculate. They always refer the Shastra and clarify such matters. So there is a reference for Kaha, and there is also a reference for Arkaha. Arkaha refers to the sun god, and Kaha refers to Brahma. So it is said with respect to Kaha being used in this verse, Kaha means Brahma, who once became elude by his daughter and became a woman and began to follow her. This infuriated Shiva, who attacked Brahma with his trident. Brahma fled in fear of his life. This is described in the Puranas. Elsewhere, that once Brahma became elude by his own daughter. That is the power of the illusory energy, that such an exalted personality as Brahma, even he became illusioned by the sight of his beautiful daughter. He started following her in order to enjoy with her. So that act of Brahma infuriated Shiva. Shiva became very angry. Shiva is also appearing as the son of Brahma. So he became very angry at Brahma. Very angry at Brahma, his own father, in terms of the birth Shiva has taken. But he became angry at Brahma, and he went to attack Brahma with his trident. Shiva carries one instrument called Trishur. With that, he attacks those who are unlawful, those who are unnecessarily giving trouble to saintly persons. So they deserve to be punished. So Shiva became angry against Brahma for the act of Brahma running behind his daughter, and Shiva decided to punish this person. Brahma began to run away from Shiva fearing his life.

Another reference is Arkaha. Arkaha refers to the sun god. So there is a reference to this in the Vaman Purana. Srila Prabhupada quotes from Vaman Purana. There was a demon by the name Vidyunmali who was gifted with a glowing golden airplane which traveled to the back of the sun. So there is this Vidyunmali who is a demon, and he received a gift, and that gift was a glowing golden airplane. So Vidyunmali decided to utilize this glowing golden airplane by traveling to the back of the sun, and night disappeared because of the glowing effulgence of this plane, aeroplane. Aeroplane was very brilliantly shining. So because of that shining of the aeroplane there was no night. So Shiva saw then what happened sun god he became angry against this Vidyunmali that you are disturbing the arrangement of day and night. So what did the sun god do? With his rays he melted the plane in which Vidyunmali was traveling. Now Shiva observed this. He became very very angry against sun god. Shiva he ran away and fell at Kashi or Varanasi. And the fame and the place became famous as Lolarka. For example this description of the planet house. In the short description of the sun god has mentioned that the sun god is famous for his Original form. ### Brahmastra, Ashwathama, Arjuna, Nuclear Weapon
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/v01UZ15OTD4?t=57
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.19
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In the ultimate issue only when there is no alternative the nuclear weapon called the Brahmastra is applied even though this Brahmastra works on the power of mantra."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Ashwathama using the Brahmastra, comparing it to a modern nuclear weapon and explaining the circumstances that led to its use.

Yadasharanam Atmana Aikshata Shranthavajinam Astram Bramma Shiro Mene Atma Tranam Vij atom Jaha. The meaning of this verse is: When the son of the Brahmana Ashwathama saw that his horses were tired he considered that there was no alternative for protection outside of his using the ultimate weapon, the Brahmastra or the nuclear weapon. So Arjuna from the previous few verses, Arjuna when he heard that Draupadi's five sleeping sons were beheaded by Ashwathama, Arjuna pacified Draupadi and told her that I am going to punish him and in that way I will actually pacify you. So Arjuna armed himself and he started on his chariot to chase this Ashwathama. Ashwathama saw that Arjuna is coming very very close and he was going away on his horse, so on his horses I mean on his chariot which was drawn by horses. Now the horses became tired and they would not move any further. Then Ashwathama thought that his life is in danger now so he considered using the ultimate weapon the Brahmastra for his protection. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport to this verse, in the ultimate issue only when there is no alternative the nuclear weapon called the Brahmastra is applied even though this Brahmastra works on the power of mantra. Even though this Brahmastra works on the power of mantra, still it is like modern-day nuclear weapon that means there is intense amount of radiation which this astra gives out so that will be described in the later verses but here Prabhupada is helping us understand this Brahmastra is like a nuclear weapon.

---
### Brahmana, Qualifications, Varna, Heredity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/v01UZ15OTD4?t=229
**Verse References:** BG 18
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The most intelligent man is called a Brahmana, and it is not a hereditary title."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the meaning of 'Dvijatmaja' (son of a Brahmana) and explains that being born in a Brahmana family does not automatically qualify one as a Brahmana; specific qualities and characteristics are required.

The word vij RT from Bridgeana to Brahmastra allashti in Indianlanguage is equal to three words. So that is the basic time to beforded by dharmayani. Atmajah is significant here because Ashwathama, although the son of Dronacharya was not exactly a qualified Brahmana. Dvija means Brahmana. Atmajah means the son. So son of the Brahmana. He is not referred to directly as a Brahmana, but he is Ashwathama is referred to as the son of a Brahmana. Srila Prabhupada explains why is this so? The most intelligent man is called a Brahmana, and it is not a hereditary title. That means the son of a Brahmana does not automatically become a Brahmana. No. He has to qualify. Ashwathama was also formerly called the Brahmabandhu or the friend of a Brahmana. Being a friend of a Brahmana does not mean that one is Brahmana by qualification. Brahmana in the strict sense of the term being used for somebody means the person has got the qualifications of a Brahmana. In the Bhagavad Gita, this is mentioned: Shamo Dhamo Tapasyaucham Shanti Rajyavam Evacha Gyanam Vigyanam Astikyam Brahma Karma Swabhavajam. A Brahmana naturally is endowed with some characteristics by which he is called a Brahmana. So nine qualities by which Brahmanas work are specifically enumerated in the Bhagavad Gita in the 18th chapter. Here Ashwathama because he is not having the qualifications, therefore he cannot be called a Brahmana. Therefore earlier he was referred to as Brahmabandhu, and in this verse he is referred to as Dvijatmaja, the son of a Brahmana. Earlier he was called the friend of a Brahmana. A friend or a son of a Brahmana, well fully qualified can be called a Brahmana and not otherwise. So somebody to be called as a Brahmana should have the qualification, unlike in modern times. Simply somebody is taken birth in a Brahmana family, therefore they are called Brahman, but that is not correct. That is not the proper way in which somebody should be called as a Brahmana. Unless one is born as a Brahmana. Since Ashwathama's decision is immature, he is purposely called herein the son of a Brahmana. He shouldn't have used the ultimate weapon the Brahmastra, but because he immaturely decided to use that, he is being referred to as Dvijatmaja, son of a Brahmana.

---
### Varna, Lakshana, Characteristics, Social Order
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/v01UZ15OTD4?t=438
**Verse References:** SB 7
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One is identified as a Brahmana or any particular varna, Kshatriya or Vaishya, based on lakshana, based on characteristics."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srimad Bhagavatam to emphasize that one's varna (social class) should be determined by their qualities and characteristics (lakshana), not merely by birth.

So Srila Prabhupada explains in another place that the Vedic system is, one is called as a Brahmana only if he has the lakshana, the characteristics of a Brahmana. And in the Bhagavatam it is clearly declared: Yasyaya lakshanam proktam pumso varna abhivyanjakam, Yad anyatra pi drishyeta, Tattenaiva vinirdhishet. One is identified as a Brahmana or any particular varna, Kshatriya or Vaishya, based on lakshana, based on characteristics. If a person has the characteristics of a Vaishya, he should be addressed as a Vaishya or known as a Vaishya. If somebody has the qualities of a Kshatriya, he should be known as a Kshatriya. But without the qualities, without the qualifications, lakshana, characteristics, how can you refer somebody wrongly as a Brahmana or a Kshatriya, whatever. And specifically this verse of the Bhagavatam, the 7th canto, Yad anyatra pi drishyeta. If somebody is born in a Brahmin family, but he doesn't have the characteristics of the qualities of a Brahmana, let's say he has got the qualities of a Vaishya, then Tattenaiva vinirdhishet, he should be designated as a Vaishya because he has got the qualities of a Vaishya even though he is born in a Brahmana family. That is what the verse says. So lakshana means symptoms. One must act as a Brahmana, one must have the characteristics of a Brahmana. If he does not have those characteristics, there is no harm in referring him as the son of a Brahmana, Dujatmaja, because his father is qualified Brahmana. Ashwatthama's father is Dronacharya. Dronacharya is qualified, highly qualified. He was expert, expert Brahmana.

---
### Lineage, Respect, Forgiveness, Compassion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/v01UZ15OTD4?t=577
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Anyone can claim my father is a great person, but he cannot pass off as a great man just because he is a son of a great man."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a heart surgeon's son to illustrate that one cannot claim the same expertise or status as their parent simply by virtue of lineage. He then discusses how Ashwathama was spared capital punishment due to being the son of Dronacharya, highlighting Draupadi's compassion.

So Ashwatthama is not similarly having the characteristics like Dronacharya. Therefore Ashwatthama, it is proper to refer him as Dujatmaja. So here Srila Prabhupada explains, anyone can claim my father is a great person, but he cannot pass off as a great man just because he is a son of a great man. No. Simply because his father is very highly qualified, doesn't mean he necessarily would have acquired all the father's qualifications unless he is trained. Training is required, education is required, culture is required, all that is required. It doesn't simply come off automatically there is no such transfer of qualities. No. From a social point of view, Prabhupada says, we sometimes give respect to somebody just because he happens to be the son of a great person. So we give him respect, that is also okay, giving respect, but he cannot claim that he is like the father. Just like somebody's father is a great heart surgeon, if the son is not qualified as a heart surgeon, how can the son be referred to as a great heart surgeon? It is not proper and it is not to be referred that way. Similarly, even though the father is a great brahmana, the son should not be referred to as a great brahmana. So it will be described in the later portion of this chapter that because he happened to be, Ashwathama happened to be the son of a great personality, Dronacharya, so he was excused from capital punishment. He was excused from being killed. So Arjuna decided not to kill him even later on, it will be described, because he happened to be, the consideration was Guru putra, he is the son of my teacher. If he dies, this Ashwathama is killed, because of the mistake he has done, he deserves to be killed, but if he is killed, his mother, whose Dronacharya's wife, will be unhappy. So it is Draupadi who advised Arjuna, don't kill Ashwathama because then her, his mother, Ashwathama's mother, will be unhappy. She is innocent, she is not at fault. So he spared his life. That is what Draupadi said.

### Brahmastra, Nuclear Weapon, Counteraction, Warfare
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/v01UZ15OTD4?t=777
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Brahmastra is to be used in a very very very extraordinary circumstance. It is the last weapon somebody may use."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the Brahmastra as a last resort weapon, comparing it to a modern nuclear weapon and highlighting the lack of counteraction methods in modern warfare compared to ancient Vedic warfare.

Now he was running on the back of a horse, but when he saw that Arjuna is coming nearer, he thought that the Brahmastra could be the only way he could protect himself. Now Brahmastra is to be used in a very very very extraordinary circumstance. It is the last weapon somebody may use. And Draupadi explains, it is similar to the modern nuclear weapon. It releases lot of radiation. When Ashwathama actually, the description will be there later on, Ashwathama releases Brahmastra, there was so much radiation, people were feeling terrible heat. And Arjuna inquired from Krishna, what is the cause for this terrible heat spreading everywhere. So Krishna informed, this heat is due to Ashwathama releasing the Brahmastra. Krishna, Krishna is the supreme lord, the paramatma, the super soul in everyone's heart, he knows. So Krishna informed Arjuna and Krishna also informed Arjuna, now you counteract this Brahmastra by releasing another Brahmastra to neutralize. Formerly they had this knowledge, if I throw one kind of weapon, somebody else may neutralize that weapon by throwing another weapon, counteraction. But Prabhupada points out, now they have this nuclear weapon, but there is no system of counteracting it. They do not have any counteraction. And even Kshatriyas when they would fight, if one Kshatriya releases a particular weapon, just like a fire weapon, may be counteracted by a water weapon. This is unknown in modern science. It is unknown. There were weapons, not only fire weapons, there were water weapons, there were wind weapons, and there were of course the arrows made of earth, earthly element. Like that, there were different kinds of weapons and they were expert in counteracting a particular weapon. And also formerly when a battle is being fought, then if the opposite party, the Kshatriyas killed, that is the end of the battle. There is no more fighting. So that was the system. It was highly regulated. But now modern warfare is not so much based on principles and regulations, but Kshatriyas used to be regulated. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### Brahmastra, Ashwathama, Mahabharata
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DQGHIRBeKhY?t=67
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.20
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Since his life was in danger, he touched water in sanctity and concentrated upon the chanting of the hymns for throwing nuclear weapons, although he did not know how to withdraw such weapons."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces verse 20 of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Ashwathama's use of the Brahmastra weapon and his incomplete knowledge of its use.

Athopas Prishya Salilam Sandadhetat Samahitah Ajanan Api Samharam Prana Krishra Upasthite. The meaning of this verse as translated by Srila Prabhupada: Since his life was in danger, he touched water in sanctity and concentrated upon the chanting of the hymns for throwing nuclear weapons, although he did not know how to withdraw such weapons. So, the description is that Ashwathama, when he realized his life was in danger after he had beheaded the five sleeping sons of Draupadi and he wanted to please his master Duryodhana, and Duryodhana was not at all pleased with this act of Ashwathama. Arjuna, when he learned from Draupadi that Ashwathama has killed her five sleeping sons, then Arjuna vowed to punish this Ashwathama. So, Arjuna started chasing Ashwathama, and Ashwathama was trying to escape, but his horse became tired. On horseback, his horse became tired, and he realized he couldn't go any further, and Arjuna is chasing on a chariot, and Arjuna is accompanied by Krishna, who was acting as the Sarathi, the chariot driver of Arjuna. This towards the end of the battle of Kurukshetra, so this verse describes that this Ashwathama, since his life was in danger, he decided to use the ultimate weapon, the Brahmastra, to protect his son from the evil of the Kurukshetra, and he decided to use the ultimate weapon, the Brahmastra, to protect himself, and he did not have complete knowledge of using this Brahmastra weapon. He knew how to release this weapon, but he did not know how to withdraw it, so he didn't have complete knowledge, and it is like a nuclear weapon. Srila Prabhupada explains this Brahmastra is like a nuclear weapon; it has got a tremendous amount of radiation that is generated. And what is the kind of power of this Brahmastra? It can destroy a whole universe; that is the kind of power this Brahmastra has.

Of course, this Brahmastra is a weapon that is released by a chanting mantra. This science is unknown in the modern times. This science of utilizing a particular sound to generate an effect. Okay. So, how did he release this weapon, this Ashwatthama? He touched water in sanctity. The Sanskrit says that Upasprishya Salilam. Salilam means water. He touched water. And how did he do it? He did it in sanctity. So, there is a certain procedure for using this weapon, which is a mantra weapon. Mantra weapon. So, chanting the mantra requires that one has to purify oneself by touching water and then chanting that mantra. Now, the chanting is not simply some casual vibration of the tongue; no, it is the technique of chanting this mantra by a qualified chanter. Now, this is the science that is not known in modern times, but it was, it is there even now, it's there, and it was certainly used in the times of the Battle of Mahabharata, battle of Kurukshetra 5,000 years back. It is described in the Ramayana, it is described in the Mahabharata, described in the Puranas how to use mantra weapons, different kinds of mantra weapons.

### Sound, Spiritual Vibration, Hare Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DQGHIRBeKhY?t=695
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If the power of a subtle sound is so much that it can be used to generate a brahmastra which is equivalent of a nuclear weapon it is capable of massive destruction it has got so much destructive power so then we can hardly imagine what is the power of the spiritual sound vibration of properly chanting Hare Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the destructive power of subtle sound vibrations used in weapons with the transformative power of spiritual sound vibrations, specifically the Hare Krishna mantra.

Just by chanting the mantra, mantra means the power of sound. But, it is chanted by using a particular technique. Prabhupada calls it here purification of sound just like we are engaged in chanting the Hare Krishna mantra. This Hare Krishna mantra is a spiritual sound vibration. It is not even a subtle sound. It is a mantra of incorrect meaning. A mantra means a purification of sound. This mantra is a spiritual sound vibration. It is not even a subtle sound it is a mantra is a spiritual sound vibration. It is not even a subtle sound it is a mantra spiritual sound now spiritual sound is beyond gross and subtle matter it is beyond gross and subtle matter but here what is being talked of as the use of brahmastra is a subtle sound vibration utilized to generate this astra this weapon release this weapon and there is a technique of using another mantra for withdrawing it that mantra this aswathama did not know so Prabhupada tells if the power of a subtle sound is so much that it can be used to generate a brahmastra which is equivalent of a nuclear weapon it is capable of massive destruction it has got so much destructive power so then we can hardly imagine what is the power of the spiritual sound vibration of properly chanting Hare Krishna if you chant Hare Krishna right you will learn to chant Hare Krishna from Shriyeila Prabhupada who is the expert spiritual master so Prabhupada says to chant Hare Krishna effectively we should learn how to chant offencelessly we should chant the Hare Krishna mantra avoiding the 10 kinds of offences dasha with the aparatha wait a while so so.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.7.21-22 | 03-01-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### devotion, fearlessness, material existence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BUeduusXOQw?t=56
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Only a devotee is fearless in this material world."
**Summary:** This segment explains that only devotees of Krishna are truly fearless in the material world, which is characterized by the fourfold miseries of eating, sleeping, fearing, and mating.

In this session, we will be discussing the 21st and 22nd verses of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Vishnu is Lord Krishna, and Jishnu is Arjuna. Arjuna began to address Krishna upon seeing that glaring light spreading in all directions, and he thought his own life was in danger. Arjuna said, "Tvam eko dahyamanam apavargo 'si samsruteh." Therefore, only you are competent to instill fearlessness in the hearts of your devotees. Everyone in the flames of material miseries can find the path of liberation in you only. So, by Arjuna's statement, we can understand what is the position of a devotee. Only a devotee is fearless in this material world. All others who are not devotees of Krishna, they are always in a situation where any time there can be fear. There is no cause for fear of some danger, due to some material danger. Krishna is the unlimited Supreme Lord. Therefore, only Krishna can give complete protection to anyone who takes shelter of him. But who takes shelter of Krishna? It's only his devotees. Non-devotees may think that they are safe, or they have some different arrangements for their safety, for their protection. But actually, in this material world, there are four characteristics of material existence: Ahara, Nidra, Bhaya, Maithunam. Everyone is in need of food for their subsistence. They are required to take some rest, sleep. And Bhaya, there is always fear due to some danger, one form or the other. So many different kinds of dangers are there in this world. And Maithunam, the demand for sex, sex indulgence. So, these four things are common for all living beings who have taken birth in this material world under the laws of karma.

---
### devotion, protection, Kurukshetra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BUeduusXOQw?t=297
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is almighty and is especially the cause of fearlessness for the devotees."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes Krishna's role as the giver of fearlessness to his devotees, illustrated by Arjuna's experience in the Kurukshetra war, where Krishna's guidance enabled the Pandavas to overcome a much stronger enemy.

That fear factor is always there. But Krishna is Bhaktanam Abhayankara. He is the giver of fearlessness to those who take his shelter, his devotees. Everyone in the flames of material miseries can find the path of liberation in you only. Shrila Prabhupada explains, Arjuna was aware of the transcendental qualities of Lord Sri Krishna as he had already experienced them during the Kurukshetra war in which both of them were present. Even though Krishna was not fighting in that battle, he agreed to become Arjuna's chariot driver to guide Arjuna and the Pandavas. Not just Arjuna alone, all the Pandavas. So, Arjuna had already experienced how, in spite of big, big generals on the opposite side... This is very nicely described by Shankaracharya. He says, "Bhishma Drona, Tata Jayadrata Jala, Gandhara Neelotpala, Shalya Grahavati, Kripenavahini, Karnena Velakula, Ashwathama Vikarana Ghoramakara, Duryodhana Avarthini, Soktirna Kalu Pandavai Rananadi, Kaivartaka Keshavaha." The Pandavas, in the battlefield of Kurukshetra, they did not have much strength, military strength as compared to the Kauravas. Of course, Bhima and Arjuna were very, very powerful fighters. They were maharathis. But on the opposite side, on the Kaurava side, there were so many maharathis. And among them, Bhishma and Drona, the topmost. Among all those present at that time, the topmost maharathis were on Kaurava's side, Bhishma and Drona. And not only that, Karna and Ashwathama, Vikarna, Duryodhana. So, in this verse, it is listed all these great fighters, maharathis, chariot fighters, single-handed, they could face thousands of other warriors. They could kill in a combat, military combat. So, in spite of so much strength on the Kaurava side, because the Pandavas had Krishna as their guide, they were able to cross this most dangerous ocean. Shankaracharya compares the battlefield to an ocean. And the Pandavas, they had to cross this dangerous ocean full of all kinds of sharks, all kinds of whirlpools, all kinds of crocodiles. Imagine, a person has to cross a water body, which is like an ocean, the vastness of the army strength of the Kauravas. In spite of that, because Krishna was their guide, therefore, they crossed without being vanquished by the Kaurava army. So, Arjuna has already got experience how Krishna protected the Pandavas very nicely.

---
### fearlessness, devotion, shelter
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BUeduusXOQw?t=572
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One who takes shelter of Krishna's lotus feet can be fearless in this material world."
**Summary:** This segment highlights that Krishna is the source of fearlessness for devotees, comparing material existence to a blazing forest fire that only Krishna can extinguish through his mercy.

Therefore, Arjuna's version of Lord Krishna is authoritative. Krishna is almighty and is especially the cause of fearlessness for the devotees. Krishna is the cause of all fearlessness for the devotees. A devotee of the Lord is always fearless. There's another song written by Govinda Das Thakur. He says, my dear mind, always sing the glories of the Supreme Lord Krishna. Nanda Nandana. Krishna has got many names of which this name Nanda Nandana, the beloved son of Maharaj Nanda. Shri Nanda Nandana, Abhaya Charanaravindare, because his lotus feet is the cause of fearlessness. One who takes shelter of Krishna's lotus feet can be fearless in this material world. So, here Prabhupada says, a devotee of the Lord is always fearless because of the protection given by the Lord. Material existence is something like a blazing fire in the forest. What does Arjuna say here? That, Tvam eko dahyamananam. Material world is sometimes compared to a forest fire. Supposing somebody is in the middle of a forest, and suddenly, there is a forest fire. And that fire spreads very, very quickly because, especially if it is summer, and the wood is dry, the trees, it will spread very quickly. And what if a person is caught up in the middle of a forest fire? Every direction he sees, he sees a blazing fire. So, is there a possibility he can escape? Yes, only one possibility. Only if there is a big rainfall which pours down heavily from the sky and then extinguishes this forest fire. See, it is not easy to extinguish a forest fire. Only a huge rainfall from a dense rain cloud which pours down completely extinguishes the forest fire. Then a person is safe. So, Krishna alone can extinguish this forest fire of material existence. Only Krishna can extinguish.

---
### spiritual master, mercy, shelter
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BUeduusXOQw?t=756
**Verse References:** BG 15.7
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Take shelter of Prabhupada's lotus feet."
**Summary:** This segment equates the spiritual master to Krishna, emphasizing that both can extinguish the fire of material existence through their mercy, and encourages taking shelter of Srila Prabhupada's lotus feet.

And he extinguishes by the mercy of rainfall. Same, similar prayer we offer to the spiritual master: samsara-davanala-lidha-loka-tranaya-karunya-ghanaghanatvam. The spiritual master comes to this world, just like Krishna comes, to rescue the conditioned souls who are suffering in the blazing fire of material existence. Material existence is filled with so many miseries. That is the nature of material existence. Therefore, it is also said, manashashtanindriyani prakrutisthani karshati. In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says living entities are simply engaged in a struggle for existence. So, that struggle for existence can be put to an end immediately if we take shelter of the lotus feet of Krishna, who can actually shower, or the lotus feet of Krishna's representative, the bona fide spiritual master, the pure devotee spiritual master, Srila Prabhupada. Take shelter of Prabhupada's lotus feet. Vande gro Sri Charanaravindam. So, we take shelter of Prabhupada's lotus feet. Then Prabhupada also can bring down the shower of mercy of Krishna, because he is always in touch with Krishna. So, he can bring down the mercy rainfall, which will extinguish this blazing fire of material existence. So, we should actually take shelter of Krishna, like Arjuna is teaching us through this prayer.

### shelter, protection, Brahmastra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BUeduusXOQw?t=875
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We should also learn how to take shelter of Krishna and thereby be saved from the dangers of material existence."
**Summary:** The segment concludes by reiterating the importance of taking shelter of Krishna, as Arjuna did when faced with the Brahmastra, to be saved from the dangers of material existence.

Arjuna thought his life was in danger when he suddenly saw. Actually, this was the Brahmastra that was released by Ashwatthama to protect himself. But Arjuna did not know that, that the cause of that radiation suddenly he saw is spreading everywhere, and his own life in danger. He did not know what is the cause. So, he is addressing Krishna, "Only you can protect me. You can save my life. Where is this?" He is going to ask him the next words while addressing Krishna, "Where is this radiation coming from? My dear Lord, I do not know. Please, can you tell me?" And of course, Arjuna has taken shelter of Krishna completely as a devotee. But we should also learn how to take shelter of Krishna and thereby be saved from the dangers of material existence. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

Om Gyanati Miran Dhasya Gyananjani Shalakaya Chaksharun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Namah Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. ### Krishna, supreme personality of Godhead, expansion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EZ5Z7Bi0_uE?t=60
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.23
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You are the originator of the universe. You are the original personality of Godhead who expands himself all over the creations and is transcendental to material energy."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining that it is Arjuna's description of Krishna as the original personality of Godhead, who expands throughout creation and is beyond material energy.

Tvam Adhya Purusha Sakshat Eeshwara Prakrte Paraha Mayam Yudasya Chit Shaktya Kaivalye Sthita Atmani. The meaning of this verse is: You are the originator of the universe. You are the original personality of Godhead who expands himself all over the creations and is transcendental to material energy. You have cast away the effects of the material energy by dint of your spiritual potency. You are always situated in eternal bliss and transcendental knowledge. These are words spoken by Arjuna. Arjuna is seeing suddenly a radiation emitting from some source, and it is spreading all over the sky, and he is inquiring from Krishna what is this radiation that is spreading. Now before he actually puts forward his actual question, as a devotee, he is helping us understand who is Krishna or what is Krishna's position. So he is describing that Krishna's position is that of the supreme personality of Godhead. What does that mean? So he has stated in this specific description here: Adhyaha Purusha Sakshat Sakshat Eeshwaraha Prakriteparaha like that. So Srila Prabhupada explains elaborately in the purport what is Krishna's position.

---
### Krishna, expansion, potency
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EZ5Z7Bi0_uE?t=182
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna expands, therefore Krishna is present everywhere throughout the creation because he expands himself."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Krishna's position as the original personality of Godhead, explaining that all other forms of God are expansions of Krishna. These expansions do not diminish Krishna's original potency, illustrating this with the analogy that in the absolute truth, 1 + 1 = 1 and 1 - 1 = 1.

So the first point here is Krishna is the original personality of Godhead. Original because every other form of God is an expansion of Krishna's original form. That's why Krishna is called original personality of Godhead, and he expands himself all over the creations. Krishna expands, therefore Krishna is present everywhere throughout the creation because he expands himself. Expansion means his power is not diminished. His original form is all powerful. The expanded form is also all powerful. Now this is difficult for us to understand, but Srila Prabhupada says in a very simple way, as far as the absolute truth is concerned, 1 plus 1 equal to 1. 1 minus 1 is also equal to 1. That means the Supreme Lord is present with all his potencies when he expands into another form. Then the original form has all the potencies. The expanded form also has all the potencies. So when he expands into another form, the expanded form also has equally got all the potencies. So this is only possible for the Supreme Lord because he is the absolute truth.

---
### surrender, maya, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EZ5Z7Bi0_uE?t=304
**Verse References:** BG 7.14
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Simply by surrendering to the lotus feet of Krishna, we become free from the influence of maya, because of which we get out of the clutches of ignorance, nescience."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes from Bhagavad Gita and Chaitanya Charitamrita, explaining that surrendering to Krishna frees one from the influence of Maya and ignorance. Krishna is likened to the sun, and Maya to darkness, which vanishes in the presence of Krishna's light.

Now Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport. First of all, Prabhupada quotes the Bhagavad Gita. He says the Lord states in the Bhagavad Gita that the one who surrenders unto the lotus feet of the Lord can get released from the clutches of nescience. Ma mevaye prapadyante maya metam tarantite. So simply by surrendering to the lotus feet of Krishna, we become free from the influence of maya, because of which we get out of the clutches of ignorance, nescience. So further, Srila Prabhupada is quoting the Chaitanya Charitamrita and saying Krishna is just like the sun, and maya or material existence is just like darkness. Krishna suryasama mayahoy andhakar. Wherever there is light of the sun, darkness or ignorance at once vanishes. So therefore, it is now explained here the best means to get out of the world of ignorance is suggested here. What is that best means? That the Lord is addressed herein as the original personality of Godhead. From him all other personalities of Godhead expand. From him, from the original personality of Godhead Krishna, all other personalities expand. The all pervasive Lord Vishnu is Lord Krishna's plenary portion or expansion. Vishnu who is all pervading in this material world, he is an expansion of Krishna.

---
### expansion, energies, source
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EZ5Z7Bi0_uE?t=431
**Verse References:** BG 10.8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna says I am the source of all, everything in existence, and therefore from me only everything emanates."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain Krishna's expansions, differentiating between 'Swamsa' (the Lord himself with all potencies) and 'Vibhinamsa' (separated expansions like living beings). He emphasizes that Krishna is the source of everything, quoting Bhagavad Gita to support this point.

As I told earlier, Krishna expands means Krishna as the original person always remains without any diminishing potency. The Lord expands himself in innumerable forms of Godhead and living beings. Even we living beings are expansions of the Lord, but we are called Vibhinamsa. We are separated expansion. And the forms of the Supreme Lord as Vishnu Tatva, Rama, Narasimha, Varaha, they are called Swamsa. Swamsa means the Lord himself with all his potencies expands in many forms. So the Lord expands himself in innumerable forms of Godhead and living beings along with his different energies. Now at any time the Lord is never without his energy. Energies are always present eternally. As the energy and the Lord is always present as the Lord, and the Lord is always the controller of all the energies. But Sri Krishna is the original primeval Lord from whom everything expands. So that original form from whom everything expands is Krishna. Krishna says this in the Bhagavad Gita: Aham sarvasya prabhavo, Matta sarvam pravartate. Krishna says I am the source of all, everything in existence, and therefore from me only everything emanates.

---
### Paramatma, material nature, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EZ5Z7Bi0_uE?t=553
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is the absolute personality of Godhead. He has nothing to do with the actions and reactions of the material manifestation."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the all-pervasive feature of the Lord is a partial representation, and that Paramatma is included within the Bhagavan feature. Krishna, as the absolute personality of Godhead, is detached from the actions and reactions of the material world.

The all pervasive feature of the Lord experienced within manifested world, this world we are talking about, the all pervasive feature of the Lord is also a partial representation of the Lord. It's a partial representation because the original personality of Godhead is actually the Supreme Lord himself. Supreme Lord himself. Therefore Paramatma is included within him. Paramatma is included within the Bhagavan feature, within the Vishnu Tatva, Paramatma feature is included. He is the absolute personality of Godhead. He means here Krishna. Krishna is the absolute personality of Godhead. He has nothing to do with the actions and reactions of the material manifestation. In this material world constantly there is some interaction between the gunas, producing actions and reactions both. But Krishna is not within this material nature. He is far above the material creation.

---
### darkness, light, transcendence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EZ5Z7Bi0_uE?t=650
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Light exists independent of darkness, but darkness is absence of light."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of darkness and light to explain Krishna's transcendence. Darkness depends on the absence of light, but light exists independently. Similarly, Krishna is full of bliss and transcendental variegatedness, which is dynamic and not static.

Darkness is a perverse representation of the sun, and therefore the existence of darkness depends on the existence of the sun. When we say darkness exists dependent upon the sun means when the sun is shining in the sky, there is no possibility of darkness. When the sun goes out of our vision, then there is darkness. But even that darkness in the when the sun is gone to the other side, still that darkness can immediately be dispelled as soon as the moon rises. The sun sets on the horizon, and the moon rises. Now that moonshine can immediately dispel the darkness. So that is to be also understood that existence of darkness depends on sun or light, but existence of light doesn't depend on darkness. Light exists independent of darkness, but darkness is absence of light. This has to be carefully understood. The sun is never without any trace of darkness. Similarly, the absolute personality of Godhead beyond the material existence is always full of bliss. He is not only full of bliss, but also full of transcendental variegatedness. He is full of spiritual variety. Transcendence is not at all static, but full of dynamic variegatedness.

---
### material nature, energies, control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EZ5Z7Bi0_uE?t=775
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is also simultaneously controller of this material energy by his omnipotence."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna is distinct from material nature, which is complicated by the three gunas. Krishna is independent of material modes but also controls material energy through his representatives, the three directors of the gunas.

And Krishna is also distinctly different from the material nature. It is the material nature which is complicated by the interactions of the three gunas. Therefore Krishna being totally independent of this material modes or material existence, he is independent of that. Therefore Krishna is absolute. Krishna has many many energies, and through these energies only he creates, maintains, destroys the whole material world. He does that. In Krishna's personal abode, there is no creation, there is no matter, there is no material defects. That is particularly explained in the Bhagavad Gita specifically that Krishna is free from all these material material energy, material manifestation. Krishna is free, completely free from that. But Krishna is also simultaneously controller of this material energy by his omnipotence. He simultaneously is aloof from this material energy, and he also controls it through his representatives, the three directors of the gunas. He controls through them. So that is Krishna's specific position.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.7.24 | 05-01-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Krishna's incarnation, Supreme Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/emvbgY9Uu2A?t=56
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.24
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If somebody looks at Krishna, they may be unable to recognize him as the Supreme Lord because he appears like another ordinary person."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the verse under discussion and explains that Krishna, despite appearing as an ordinary person, is the Supreme Lord.

This session will be discussing from the 24th verse of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam: Sa eva jeeva lokasya maya mohita cheta saha vidhatsye svayena viryena shreyo dharmadi lakshanam. The meaning of this verse is, and yet, though you are beyond the purview of the material energy, you execute the four principles of liberation, characterized by religion, and so on, for the ultimate good of the conditioned souls. This is a very important verse which explains when Krishna incarnates, if somebody looks at Krishna, they may be unable to recognize him as the Supreme Lord because he appears like another ordinary person. He appears like another created being, but he is not. He is not a created being. He is not an ordinary person. He is the Supreme Lord.

---
### Causeless mercy, Devotional service, Pure devotee
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/emvbgY9Uu2A?t=118
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord does not consider any material attributes when he has to shower his blessings or mercy upon somebody; he is simply pleased by devotional service."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that Krishna's mercy is causeless and cannot be earned through material qualifications, but only through devotional service and taking shelter of a pure devotee.

That we explained by Prabhupada in the purport. Prabhupada says the personality of God is Sri Krishna. Out of his causeless mercy, Krishna's mercy is described as causeless. That means there is no material cause for that mercy. There is no material cause. So therefore, to attract the attention of Krishna or to get Krishna's mercy, we cannot do that. We cannot expect to get that mercy by having some material qualification, having some material opulence, having some material power, or having some material punya. We cannot expect to get any mercy from the Supreme Lord. No. So it is explained here, then why is it called the supreme Lord? It is called causeless. It is causeless because the Lord does not consider any material attributes when he has to shower his blessings or mercy upon somebody. He is simply pleased by devotional service. If you want his mercy, you have to render devotional service. If you want his mercy, you take shelter of the pure devotee who is himself under the complete shelter and protection of the Supreme Lord. So these are all secrets of Bhakti revealed in the scriptures like Bhagavatam.

---
### Material modes, Transcendental position, Spiritual world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/emvbgY9Uu2A?t=218
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna comes down from the spiritual world, he comes to this material world, but he is never influenced by the material modes of nature."
**Summary:** This segment explains that Krishna descends from the spiritual world without being influenced by the material modes of nature, remaining transcendentally situated.

So here it is told by Prabhupada, the personality of God is Sri Krishna, out of his causeless mercy, descends on the material world without being influenced by the material modes of nature. Krishna comes down from the spiritual world. He comes to this material world, but he is never influenced by the material modes of nature. That means he is not subject to Sattva Guna, or Rajya Guna, or Tama Guna, or any combination. No. Then he is always transcendentally situated, Shuddha Sattva. Even the pure devotees who are coming from the spiritual world, even they are transcendentally situated. They are also transcendentally situated. That is the position of the devotee. That is also the position of the Lord. So Srila Prabhupada further says, Krishna is eternally beyond the material manifestation.

---
### Reclaiming fallen souls, Illusory energy, Spiritual enlightenment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/emvbgY9Uu2A?t=355
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord comes to reclaim the fallen conditioned souls; he wants to take them back to his personal abode."
**Summary:** This segment focuses on Krishna's purpose in descending: to reclaim fallen souls captivated by the illusory energy and bring them back to the spiritual world.

He descends out of his causeless mercy only to reclaim the fallen souls. See this is the reason why he comes. He has got a specific purpose. What is that? To reclaim the fallen souls. That means the fallen souls have come to this material world because of having a desire to enjoy materially. That's all. Therefore Krishna comes to save them, to rescue them, because even if we make plans for enjoying in this material world, we will always be unsuccessful. We are not meant to achieve success through some different methods suggested in the scriptures. No, we are not meant to do that. All those different aspects of illusion in this material world, because of which somebody thinks, I can enjoy, I can become the lord, that is due to the influence of the illusory energy. And accordingly, the supreme lord also makes his arrangement for someone to actually take shelter of his lotus feet and also become free from the influence of the material energy, come out of the illusory influence. So then one becomes actually spiritually enlightened, and then spiritual awakening happens. Of course these all go step by step, but the fact is the Lord comes to reclaim the fallen conditioned souls. He wants to take them back to his personal abode.

---
### Illusory energy, Purification, Mission of reclaiming
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/emvbgY9Uu2A?t=489
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All the different personalities mentioned in the Prema Dvani are actually assisting the Lord in his mission of reclaiming the fallen conditioned souls."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how the illusory energy captivates souls, and how the Lord makes arrangements for them to turn towards Him. It also highlights the roles of Srila Prabhupada, the river Ganga, and Tursi Devi in assisting the Lord's mission of reclaiming conditioned souls.

So therefore, it is described here, these fallen souls are captivated by the illusory energy. They are completely captivated by the illusory energy. They are making so many plans, and they even think that they are safe within this material world. They forget what is the real nature of the material world. So the supreme lord who incarnates for the sake of reclaiming the fallen conditioned souls, he is very very kind to these fallen souls. So he makes so much of arrangements so that somehow the souls will turn their attention towards the Lord, who is present in our heart as Paramatma, who comes from time to time in the form of incarnation visible to the common people. Like that the Lord has got so many different ways in which he actually has made arrangements to reclaim the fallen conditioned souls. We say that Premadwani, that Jai Om Vishnupad Paramahamsa Parivrajakacharya His divine grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada. We are glorifying Srila Prabhupada because Prabhupada has come for the same purpose. Prabhupada has come for reclaiming the fallen conditioned souls. So we glorify the previous acharyas. They also come to this world for the same purpose. We glorify the river Ganga. Why? Because Ganga's appearance in this material world is for the purpose of purifying the conditioned souls so that they become eligible to go back to the spiritual world. You see same mission. River Ganga, river Yamuna, Ganga mai ki jai, Yamuna mai ki jai. Then Bhakti Devi ki jai, Tursi Devi ki jai. Tursi has appeared in this world again for the same purpose for which the Lord comes, to reclaim the conditioned souls. How does Tursi assist the Lord in this reclaiming the conditioned souls? That simply by seeing Tursi, one becomes purified in the heart. Then what is the result of purification? One can easily take to devotional service and prepare oneself to go back to God. Like that all the different personalities mentioned in the Prema Dvani are actually assisting the Lord in his mission of reclaiming the fallen conditioned souls.

---
### Material energy, Illusion, Real enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/emvbgY9Uu2A?t=673
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Our real enjoyment is in the spiritual world; our real enjoyment is spiritual; our real enjoyment is in association with Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment explains how the conditioned souls are influenced by the material energy and seek enjoyment in material nature, which is ultimately illusory. True enjoyment lies in the spiritual world in association with Krishna.

Now the conditioned souls, they are attacked by the material energy. Attacked means they are influenced. Material energy influences, the illusory energy influences them. So under the influence, they simply want to enjoy material nature under false pretext. They make big big plans for enjoying exploiting the resource of material nature. But material nature cannot be the source of pleasure, the source of enjoyment never, because by its inherent characteristic, material energy is actually illusory energy, always presenting something which looks like real, but actually is not real. That is an illusion. So Prabhupada says the living entity is unable to enjoy in this material world because in essence the material energy is simply presenting illusion, presenting illusion. Our real enjoyment is in the spiritual world. Our real enjoyment is spiritual. Our real enjoyment is in association with Krishna. We belong to Krishna.

---
### Faith, Acharya, Merciful nature of the Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/emvbgY9Uu2A?t=775
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The all-merciful nature of the Lord is exhibited when he simply comes for the sake of reclaiming the fallen conditioned souls."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of faith in the words of the Supreme Lord and the Acharya, and how taking shelter of the Acharya helps purify oneself and return to the spiritual world. It concludes by highlighting the Lord's all-merciful nature in reclaiming fallen souls.

So therefore, when we understand these facts, we develop faith in the words of the Supreme Lord. We develop faith in the words of the Acharya, and when we take shelter of the Acharya, then we are easily able to purify ourselves and become eligible to get out of this illusory energy, to go to the spiritual world in our eternal position, eternal existence, full of bliss and knowledge, like the Lord is situated, full of bliss and knowledge. Further also, Prabhupada says, originally everyone is a servant of Krishna, but one forgets this truth when one desires to enjoy the material world. And when one thinks of enjoying, though there is lot of promise, but actually only one in illusion will think this material world as real, as something really enjoyable. No, it is never enjoyable. It is never real. So it is said here, the all-merciful nature of the Lord is exhibited when he simply comes for the sake of reclaiming the fallen conditioned souls. So that is the way the Lord displays his all-merciful nature.

---
