### Devotion, Transcendence, Divine Relationship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/r1aF0zl96po?t=164
**Verse References:** BG 9.18
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord is everyone's father, aham bijapratapitah, that's what Krishna says in Bhagavad Gita. He is the seed-giving Father of all living entities."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the Lord's relationship with everyone, emphasizing His impartiality while highlighting His special inclination towards His devotees, drawing from Srila Prabhupada's purport and Bhagavad Gita.

And are wrapped in meditation upon you, the devotees are entirely dependent upon Krishna for everything. They have no other dependence. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, it appears that the Lord is partial to his devotees. It appears like that, but actually the fact is, He is not partial. But the fact is, everyone is related with the Lord. Everyone has got a relationship with the Lord. It's not just the devotees alone who have relationship. He is equal to everyone. That is the meaning of Supreme Lord. He is equal to everyone, yet He is more inclined to his own men and devotees. He is more inclined to his own men, that is, who are his own men? Devotees. The Lord is everyone's father, aham bijapratapitah, that's what Krishna says in Bhagavad Gita. He is the seed-giving Father of all living entities. So therefore it is described here by Srila Prabhupada, the Lord is everyone's Father. No one can be his father, and yet no one can be his son. This relationship what we understand in the material world, somebody's father or somebody's son. That kind of relationship doesn't apply to Krishna.

### Transcendental Pastimes, Material Conception, Devotional Service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/r1aF0zl96po?t=277
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Materially, we know, somebody is my father, somebody is my son, somebody is my friend, somebody is my relative. Those kind of material idea should not be carried in our mind when we try to understand Krishna's incarnation or descent in this world."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that Krishna's relationships are transcendental pastimes, distinct from mundane material relationships, and emphasizes the purely transcendental nature of devotional service.

Srila Prabhupada further explains, his devotees are his kinsmen and his devotees are his relations. This is his transcendental pastime, it is pastime, this is Leela and it has nothing to do with mundane ideas of relations, fatherhood or anything like that. Materially, we know, somebody is my father, somebody is my son, somebody is my friend, somebody is my relative. Those kind of material idea should not be carried in our mind when we try to understand Krishna's incarnation or descent in this world. As mentioned above, the Lord is above the modes of material nature. He is always transcendental to the modes. And thus there is nothing mundane about his kinsmen and relations in devotional service. Nothing material. Everything is completely transcendental. So therefore it is described as a transcendental pastime.

**Summary:** The speaker explains the proper etiquette for addressing the Lord, emphasizing the importance of offering prayers before making requests, even for intimate friends like Arjuna.

So this is what Arjuna's question is to Krishna. Suddenly Arjuna has seen this dangerous effulgence spreading everywhere. And he does not know the source. So he is asking Krishna, please tell me what is the source of this? So Srila Prabhupada says in the Purport, anything that is presented before the personality of Godhead should be so done after due presentation of respectful prayers. That is the proper etiquette. Krishna is not an ordinary person. So before complain, one wants to ask anything, one has to offer prayers to the Lord. That is the standard procedure. And Shree Arjuna, although an intimate friend of the Lord, is observing this method for general information. Arjuna is actually very, very close friend of Krishna, but still, he's following the etiquette that a devotee should follow when addressing the Lord. Prayers had to be offered.

### Brahmastra, Nuclear Energy, Fear
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/r1aF0zl96po?t=451
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.27
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Know from me that this is the act of the son of Drona. He has thrown the hymns of nuclear energy, Brahmastra, and he does not know how to retract the glare. He has helplessly done this being afraid of imminent death."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 27th verse, where Krishna explains that the effulgence is the Brahmastra released by Ashwathama out of fear, who lacks the knowledge to retract it.

The 27th verse, Shri Bhagavanuvacha, now the Lord's response to Arjuna's question. Shri Bhagavanuvacha, Vethedam Drona Putrasya Brahamam Astram Pradarshitam, Naivasam Veda Samharam Pranabhadha Upastite. The meaning of this 27th verse, the supreme personality of God had said, know from me that this is the act of the son of Drona. He has thrown the hymns of nuclear energy, Brahmastra, and he does not know how to retract the glare. He has helplessly done this being afraid of imminent death. When Arjuna started chasing Ashwathama and Ashwathama's horses became tired. Then Ashwathama thought that his life is in danger. And just to save himself, he resorted to the ultimate weapon, the Brahmastra, which should normally not be used under such circumstances. But he did not care for the rules, regulations. He simply decided how to to release that Brahmastra.

Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Devi Saraswati Shreem Vyasaam Tatho Jaya Mudirayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 28th and the 29th verses of the 7th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 7th canto is entitled the son of Drona punished. ### Krishna consciousness, Bhagavad Gita, Srimad Bhagavatam, Kurukshetra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HpIHVsvirFY?t=90
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, in the Bhagavatam the opening of the description of the topics of Krishna begin with the end of the Battle of Kurukshetra."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the eagerness of the sages to hear about Krishna, Suta Goswami's decision to narrate Srimad Bhagavatam, and the importance of studying Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam for developing Krishna consciousness, focusing on the aftermath of the Kurukshetra war and the killing of Draupadi's sons by Ashwatthama.

So the topics have been developed up to this point that the sages of Naimisha Ranya were very eager to hear about Krishna. Therefore, Suta Goswami decided that he is going to narrate the Bhagavatam which he heard from Sukadeva Goswami when Sukadeva Goswami was speaking to Parikshit Maharaj. So that will come in the second canto. But in the first canto when Suta Goswami says because you are very interested in hearing about Krishna let me narrate the Srimad Bhagavatam to you which is exclusively describing Krishna. Recommends for those who want to develop their Krishna consciousness very very quickly, then you should hear or read or study these two scriptures Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam. So, in the Bhagavatam the opening of the description of the topics of Krishna begin with the end of the Battle of Kurukshetra. And what happened this incident particularly that the sleeping sons of Draupadi were beheaded by Aswatthama, the son of Dronacharya. And it is stated that the sleeping sons of Draupadi were beheaded by Aswatthama, the son of Dronacharya.

### Dharma Yuddha, Brahmastra, Arjuna, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HpIHVsvirFY?t=230
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "O Arjuna, only another Brahmastra can counteract this weapon. Since you are expert in the military science, subdue this weapon's glare with the power of your own weapon."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Arjuna's pursuit of Ashwatthama, Ashwatthama's release of the Brahmastra, and Krishna's instruction to Arjuna to counteract it with his own Brahmastra due to Ashwatthama's inability to retract it.

And it is totally against the principles of Dharma Yuddha. So, now when Arjuna came to know about it, he wants to punish this Ashwatthama. So, Arjuna has chased Ashwatthama. Ashwatthama's horses have become tired when he tried to escape and he raised the Brahmastra. He doesn't know how to retract it. And now, Arjuna has asked Krishna, what is the source of this intense radiation that is spreading everywhere? What is the source of this? What is the cause of this? And Arjuna, of course, has offered nice prayers to Krishna. And now Krishna is replying in this 28th verse, The meaning of this verse is, O Arjuna, only another Brahmastra can counteract this weapon. Since you are expert in the military science, subdue this weapon's glare with the power of your own weapon. In the previous verse, Krishna had explained the source of this radiation as the Brahmastra released by Ashwatthama in order to save his life, his own life. And Krishna also informed Arjuna, this Ashwatthama doesn't know how to retract this. So, it's very dangerous, this radiation of the Brahmastra he has released. So, only another Brahmastra can counteract this radiation. So, only another Brahmastra can counteract it, because he does not know how to retract it. So, you should actually subdue that weapon, that Brahmastra released by Ashwatthama by releasing another Brahmastra, because you know the science.

---
### Brahmastra, Atomic Bomb, Dhanurveda, Kshatriyas, Gurukul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HpIHVsvirFY?t=362
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dhanurveda, Arjuna knew Dhanurveda, he had studied that under Dronacharya. A Kshatriya should know this Dhanurveda, should learn this because for maintaining law and order, this violence is required and it has to be very, very carefully utilized."
**Summary:** The speaker compares the Brahmastra to an atomic bomb, highlighting its immense power and precision. He then discusses Dhanurveda, the science of weaponry, and its importance for Kshatriyas, emphasizing the responsible use of knowledge and training in a Gurukul.

So, Srila Prabhupada explains this in the purport, For the atomic bombs, there is no counterweapon to neutralize the effects. We have heard of atomic bomb, and particularly at the end of the Second World War, America bombed Japan, and there was so much of destruction, Hiroshima and Nagasaki, so much. And the after effects were horrible. But this Brahmastra, even though it has the power to neutralize the effects, it has the power to neutralize the effects. So, it is a very powerful weapon. Like an atom bomb, of course, the power of Brahmastra is many millions of times more than that of the atom bomb. But Brahmastra can be targeted at a very specific target, can be released to destroy a particular target only. That is the complete science of this bomb. So, it is a very powerful weapon. So, it is called Dhanurveda. It is of course not one of the main Vedas, it's called Upaveda. Dhanurveda and Ayurveda, the medical science and Sthapatyaveda, the science of architecture and Gandharva Veda, the science of singing and dancing, classical music, classical dance. So these are all Upavedas and they are highly developed branch of different Vedas. So these are all Upavedas and they are highly developed branch of different sciences. So Dhanurveda, Arjuna knew Dhanurveda, he had studied that under Dronacharya. A Kshatriya should know this Dhanurveda, should learn this because for maintaining law and order, this violence is required and it has to be very, very carefully utilized. Kshatriyas were very responsible. The Brahmanas, Kshatriyas and Vaishyas were very, very responsible, very intelligent in their own fields and they were trained up, they were trained up in a Gurukul to properly learn and also responsibly utilize this knowledge.

Sprishtva Apahatam Parikramya Brahamam Brahmastram Sandadhe The meaning of this 29th verse, Sri Suta Goswami said, Hearing this from the personality of Godhead, Arjuna touched water for purification and after circumambulating Lord Sri Krishna, he cast his Brahmastra weapon to counteract the other one. So, Arjuna, following Krishna's direction, instruction, he did exactly what Krishna told him. Krishna told him, release another Brahmastra to counteract the one released by Ashwathama. Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing from the third verse of the seventh chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam: Tasminsva Ashrame Vyaso Badari Shandamandite Asino Apa Upasprishya Pranidhadyau Manaswayam. The meaning of this verse: in that place, Srila Vyasa Deva in his own ashram, which was surrounded by berry trees, Badari Shandamandite, sat down to meditate after touching water for purification. **Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that perfect vision of the Absolute Truth is only possible through devotional service (bhakti), referencing the Bhagavad Gita to support this point.

So Srila Prabhupada has given a very long introduction to this verse. Perfect vision of the absolute truth is possible only by the linking process of devotional service. Yoga means a linking process. Right now our present condition is we are forgetful of our relationship with the supreme, of our link with the Supreme Lord Krishna. So to reestablish that forgotten relationship or revive that lost connection is the process of yoga. Among the different process of yoga, to have complete vision of the Absolute Truth as the Supreme Personality of God, Krishna, is only possible by Bhakti. This is explained in the Bhagavad Gita, Bhaktiamam Abhijanati. Only by Bhakti we can have vision of Krishna or know Krishna or realize Krishna. There is no other process.

This also confirmed in the Bhagavad Gita. I just now said that. One can perfectly realize the Absolute Truth only by the process of devotional service. And one can enter into the kingdom of God by such perfect knowledge. This is what Bhagavad Gita says. Bhaktiamam Abhijanati Yavanyas Chasmi Tatvatah. Tatomam Tatvatah Vishatetad Anantaram. One can enter into the kingdom of God, kingdom of Krishna, after perfectly getting knowledge about the Absolute Truth as the Supreme Personality of God, Krishna. Imperfect realization of the Absolute by the partial approach of impersonal Brahman or localized Paramatma does not permit anyone to enter into the kingdom of God.

### Brahman, Paramatma, Bhagavan, Narada Muni
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/QC5QC5FmkfY?t=318
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Shri Narada advised Vyasa Deva to become absorbed in transcendental meditation on the personality of Godhead and his activities."
**Summary:** The speaker differentiates between realization of Brahman, Paramatma, and Bhagavan, stating that only Bhagavan realization leads to the kingdom of God. He mentions Narada Muni's instruction to Vyasa Deva to meditate on the Personality of Godhead.

The Jnana process results in realization of the Absolute Truth as impersonal Brahman. And the Ashtanga Yoga process results in realization of the Absolute Truth as Paramatma, localized Paramatma, who is the expansion of the Supreme Lord Krishna situated in the heart of every living being. But this realization of the Absolute Truth as impersonal Brahman or localized Paramatma does not permit a the transcendentalist to enter into the kingdom of God. Shri Narada advised Vyasa Deva to become absorbed in transcendental meditation on the personality of Godhead and his activities. Specifically Narada instructed Vyasa Deva that the activities of the Supreme Lord, Haricharya, are completely transcendental, enlivening to the Self and they can bring about complete satisfaction of the Self while they can enlighten anyone about the Absolute Truth as the Absolute Personality of Godhead, the highest realization of the Absolute Truth.

### Maya, external energy, internal energy, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/QC5QC5FmkfY?t=670
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "There are two kinds of maya or energies of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the external energy (Maya) with the internal energy (Yoga Maya), explaining that the former causes ignorance while the latter is characterized by spiritual knowledge.

The word apashrayam suggests that this energy of the Lord is under full control. This maya is the external energy which is the cause of the living beings to be in the darkness of ignorance. And when one realizes the Supreme Personality of Godhead, then one comes to the light of knowledge, spiritual knowledge, transcendental knowledge, the internal potency or superior energy is also called maya, but it is spiritual maya. There are two kinds of maya or energies of the Supreme Lord. The external energy is the inferior energy. It is responsible for ignorance. And the superior energy, the spiritual energy, yoga maya, is actually always of the characteristic of knowledge, spiritual knowledge, spiritual enlightenment.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.7.30 | 08-01-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Brahmastra, Cosmic Annihilation, Atomic Energy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mp6Hgqg7gO8?t=37
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.30
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The heat produced by the Brahmastra can destroy the whole cosmic situation."
**Summary:** This segment explains the power of the Brahmastra as described in the Srimad Bhagavatam, comparing it to the heat of cosmic annihilation and contrasting it with the power of an atomic bomb.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deviim Saraswatiim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing the 30th and 31st verses of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Samhatya Anyonyam Ubhayoho Tejasi Sharasamvrute Aavrtya Rodhasi Khamcha Vavridhate Arka Vaknivat. The meaning of this verse, this is the 30th verse. When the rays of the two Brahmastras combined, a great circle of fire, like the disk of the sun, covered all outer space and the whole firmament of planets. Arjuna has released the counteracting Brahmastra and the rays of the two Brahmastras combined. It was a great circle of fire, like the disk of the sun. So Prabhupada describes this in the purport. The heat created by the flash of a Brahmastra resembles the fire exhibited in the sun globe at the time of cosmic annihilation. Cosmic annihilation means the entire material existence itself is annihilated. The radiation of atomic energy is very, very insignificant in comparison to the heat produced by a Brahmastra. The atomic bomb explosion can at most blow up one globe, but the heat produced by the Brahmastra can destroy the whole cosmic situation. So there is a lot of difference between the power of Brahmastra and the power of atomic bomb. The comparison is, therefore, made to the heat at the time of annihilation.

---
### Samvartaka Fire, Planetary Systems, Life on Other Planets
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mp6Hgqg7gO8?t=183
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.31
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "No planet therefore is without living beings as less intelligent people tend to think."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the effects of the Brahmastra's heat on the three worlds, comparing it to the Samvartaka fire at the time of annihilation, and emphasizes that life exists on all planets, contrary to common misconceptions.

The 31st verse. Dhrishtva stratejas tu tayo ho tri lo kaan pradahan mahat. Dahya mana praja sarva saam vartakam amamsata. The meaning of this verse, the 31st verse. All the population of the three worlds was scorched by the combined heat of the weapons. Everyone was reminded of the Samvartaka fire which takes place at the time of annihilation. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport to his 31st verse. The three worlds, three lokan, three worlds are the upper, lower and intermediate planets of the universe. In the universe there are many planets but it is divided into three divisions: upper, lower and the middle intermediate planets. Although the Brahmastra was released on this earth, the heat produced by the combination of both weapons covered all the universe, and all the populations and all the different planets began to feel the heat excessively and compared it to that of the Samvartaka fire. At the time of annihilation there is a big fire. That will be explained a little later by Srila Prabhupada further. But here he is telling that the combination of both the weapons produced excessive heat, and everyone in all the planets began to feel the heat. So the conclusion is being made that no planet therefore is without living beings as less intelligent people tend to think. Less intelligent people, they think that only the earth planet is having intelligent human beings. Other planets, they sometimes say there is no life or they say there are no intelligent people, but that's not correct. According to the understanding we get from the Vedas, the perfect knowledge source, there is life on all the planets. There is life on all the planets, and there are intelligent beings on all planets. So Srila Prabhupada explains.

---
### Brahmastra Retraction, Annihilation, Samvartaka Fire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mp6Hgqg7gO8?t=355
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "At the time of universal destruction what happens it is said that the present temperature the Sun will be increased twelve times so naturally there will be fire everything we ablaze in all the planets."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the intensity of the heat from the combined Brahmastras, comparing it to the Samvartaka fire at the time of annihilation, and describes the process of destruction and subsequent torrential rain.

The radiation heat of the radiation when both the brahmastras combined increased many fold because one weapon was released by Ashwatthama, another was released by Arjuna to counteract that first brahmastra on the instruction of Krishna. The heat was so terrible. All the inhabitants came to learn that inhabitants of the different planets, they felt the great heat produced by the two Brahmastras and what did they feel it like? Samvartakam amamsata, the Samvartaka fire, they felt it is like the Samvartaka fire that is explained in the shastra at the end when there is annihilation to take place, there will be no rainfall, everything would be dry. There will be a great fire all over the universe that's called Samvartaka fire, and everything will be ablaze. This fire will take place on account of the heat increase in the atmosphere. There will be no rising or falling. This fire will take place on account of the heat increasing of the Sun. The Sun is actually like a ball of fire, but because it is such a great distance from the earth, we don't feel it like a blazing fire. We don't feel like that except in summer sometimes it becomes too hot, but that is for a short duration and that is also something which you can tolerate. But at the time of universal destruction what happens it is said that the present temperature the Sun will be increased twelve times so naturally there will be fire everything we ablaze in all the planets. Sometimes we have experience that there is a fire that will cause a fire, forest fire and especially in summer when everything is dry in the forest and the forest fire spreads very rapidly and then the animals which are trapped they will all be burnt alive. The forest fire because whichever direction they go they see only fire so they cannot escape they'll be burnt. Similarly the fire will take place when the annihilation is supposed to happen and everything will be burnt to ashes. This is the way the annihilation happens and after that there will be rain and so it is described the intensity of the rain is like trunk of the elephant is falling water falling down is like huge trunk of an elephant and everything will be covered with water that is called pralaya. And by the grace of the Lord, another creation will begin afterwards.

---
### Cyclical Destruction, Controlled Heat, Importance of Guru
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mp6Hgqg7gO8?t=585
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If you want to learn a particular branch of knowledge, you have to approach a proper guru."
**Summary:** The cyclical nature of creation, maintenance, and destruction is discussed, highlighting how Arjuna controlled the Brahmastra's heat and emphasizing the importance of learning knowledge properly from a qualified guru, contrasting Arjuna's expertise with Ashwatthama's lack of proper training.

So this destruction that happens is cyclical. Creation, maintenance and destruction. This cyclically happens. But here, even though the combined heat of the two Brahmastras was like the Samvartaka fire, it was actually highly controlled. It is completely controlled. It was not intended for any destruction to take place. They just felt the heat for a moment. Because after this, it is described in the 32nd verse that Arjuna, at once, he retracted both the Brahmastra weapons as Lord Krishna desired. Arjuna did that. So he did not allow this heat to spread or this radiation to spread. He retracted both the Brahmastras, and he knew the science perfectly. Arjuna knew the science and therefore he was able to retract both the Brahmastras by the knowledge of how to use that Brahmastra. So this knowledge is very, very important. Properly, it has to be learned. This Ashwatthama, he had not properly learned this. Therefore, the scripture's advice: if you want to learn a particular branch of knowledge, you have to approach a proper guru, one who is actually well versed in the knowledge.

---
### Brahmavidya, Spiritual Science, Matter and Spirit
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mp6Hgqg7gO8?t=726
**Verse References:** BG 15.7
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Every living being is a spiritual being, not the body."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of Brahmavidya (spiritual science) as the highest form of knowledge, contrasting it with material knowledge, and explains the difference between matter and spirit, highlighting that living beings are spiritual in nature.

Among all the branches of knowledge, the most important branch of knowledge is called Brahmavidya, Radhyatmavidya, Atmavidya. So Atmavidya, and that is what Krishna is speaking in the Bhagavad Gita. At the end of every chapter of Bhagavad Gita, Vyasa Deva concludes by telling, Iti Srimad Bhagavad Gita Su Upanishad Su Brahmavidyaya, Brahmavidya. So Krishna is speaking the most important branch of the Vedic knowledge, that is Brahmavidya or Brahma Jnana, the spiritual science. Now all this Dhanurveda, everything is material knowledge, but this Brahmavidya, spiritual science, Brahmavidya. What is spirit? It is completely different from the matter, and spirit is the basis for everything material. Matter has got its origin. Of course both matter and spirit are originally energies of the Supreme Lord. He has many varieties of energies of which matter and spirit are two categories of energy. Matter means this material energy coming from material energy. The beginning of the creation material energy is differentiated by the glance of Mahavishnu into 24 elements, and these 24 elements combined in different proportions and different combinations give rise to different objects, material objects, our own body, gross and subtle body, and then the different planets and the varieties of living conditions. Everything is made possible by the differentiation of material energy into the 24 elements and the combinations of the 24 elements. But spirit is different. Spirit is another energy, particularly the spirit, spiritual energy in the form of the living entity. Jeeva bhutam mahabaho yayedam dharyate jagat. The living entities belong to the category of spiritual energy, and they are also spiritual. Every living being is a spiritual being, not the body, and that is the whole teaching of the Gita. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

In this session we will be discussing from the 32nd, 33rd and 34th verses of the 7th chapter of the first canto of Shri Madbhagavatam.

### Brahmastra, divine will, Arjuna's actions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uTZ4IewLiuc?t=61
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Arjuna asked Krishna, when he saw the radiation in the sky spreading everywhere, what is the source of this radiation?"
**Summary:** The speaker explains the context of the verses, focusing on Arjuna's retraction of the Brahmastra weapons as instructed by Krishna, emphasizing the power and danger of the Brahmastra.

Prajopadravam Alaksya Lokavyatikaram Chatham Matamcha Vasudevasya Sanjahararjuno Dwayam. The meaning of this verse, thus seeing the disturbance from the general populace and the imminent destruction of the planets, Arjuna at once retracted both Brahmastra weapons as Lord Sri Krishna desired. So Arjuna asked Krishna, when he saw the radiation in the sky spreading everywhere, what is the source of this radiation? Then Krishna explained how Ashwathama had released that Brahmastra out of fear for his own life and he did not know how to retract it. So it was very dangerous. And Srila Prabhupada explains that Brahmastra is like a nuclear weapon of modern times, but it is much, much more powerful. A Brahmastra can destroy an entire universe. So therefore, seeing the imminent destruction of the planets, Krishna actually advised Arjuna to retract both the Brahmastras by counteracting what Ashwathama had released. By Arjuna releasing his own Brahmastra, simply to counteract, that's all, and retract both of them. So exactly what Arjuna did, he at once retracted both the Brahmastra weapons and there was no more danger from that Brahmastra which Ashwathama had released.

---
### divine control, material nature, supreme will
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uTZ4IewLiuc?t=179
**Verse References:** BG 9.10
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Ultimately it all rests on the supreme will of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srila Prabhupada, emphasizing that atomic weapons cannot destroy the world without the Supreme Lord's sanction, highlighting the ultimate control of the Supreme Lord over material nature and its laws.

So Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, the theory that the modern atomic bomb explosions can annihilate the world is childish imagination. Why? Because Srila Prabhupada says, first of all, the atomic energy is not powerful enough to destroy the whole world. Secondly, ultimately it all rests on the supreme will of the Supreme Lord. Because without His will or sanction, nothing can be built up or destroyed. This we have heard many times, that ultimately everything is under the supreme control of the Supreme Lord. And unless He wills that there should be destruction of the entire world or the entire planet or the entire universe, till then it cannot be destroyed by anybody else. Not possible. So it is foolish also to think that natural laws are ultimately powerful. Yes, there are nature's laws working. But these laws are not blind. They are directed ultimately by the Supreme Lord. Krishna says this in the Bhagavad Gita, Mayadhyakshena prakritihi suyatesa characharam. Mayadhyakshena prakritihi suyatesa characharam. Mayadhyakshena prakritihi suyatesa characharam. By my superintendence, this material nature is working. It is Krishna's superintendence ultimately that is the basis for material nature to work. The laws are there. But the laws have got the law maker and, that is the Supreme Lord, and the law enforcer, that is also the Supreme Lord. So without the Supreme Lord's sanction, without the Supreme Lord's enforcement, nothing will happen in this world. So therefore, natural laws are not ultimately powerful.

---
### divine will, agents of the Lord, insignificance of humans
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uTZ4IewLiuc?t=336
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Unless the Lord wills, it is not possible for anyone to destroy the entire world."
**Summary:** The speaker continues quoting Srila Prabhupada, reiterating that the world can only be destroyed by the will of the Lord, not by the whims of politicians, and that everyone is insignificant compared to the Supreme Lord's power.

It is the supreme will of the Supreme Lord. So further Srila Prabhupada says, Material nature's law works under the direction of the Gita as confirmed in the Bhagavad Gita. The Lord says there that natural laws work under His supervision. The words I quoted, Mayadhyakshena prakritihi suyatesa characharam. The world can be destroyed only by the will of the Lord and not by the whims of tiny politicians. After all, everyone in this world is a very very insignificant person when compared to the supreme power of the Supreme Lord. So everybody is under control of the Supreme Lord and in front of the Lord's power, the power we possess is very very insignificant. However, powerful a person may appear to be in relationship with other persons. But ultimately, in comparison with the Supreme Lord's power, everyone is simply insignificant. So, as Srila Prabhupada says here, Unless the Lord wills, it is not possible for anyone to destroy the entire world. Lord Sri Krishna desired that the weapons released by both Drauni and Arjuna Drauni is Ashwathama, Dronacharya's son. Drauni and Arjuna be withdrawn. And it is carried out by Arjuna at once. So Krishna just instructed Arjuna and Arjuna did accordingly. That is Krishna's will. Similarly, there are many agents of the all-powerful Lord and by his will only can one execute what he desires, what Krishna desires.

---
### Devatas, Vishnu's order, Arjuna's anger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uTZ4IewLiuc?t=470
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All Devatas are Vishnubhaktas."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the Devatas are agents of Krishna, working according to Vishnu's order, and reiterates that insignificant persons cannot destroy the planet. He then introduces the 33rd verse, describing Arjuna's anger towards Ashwatthama for killing Draupadi's sons.

So, Krishna has got many many agents. The Devatas are all agents of Krishna only. Vishnubhaktas vruto daiva. All Devatas are Vishnubhaktas. So, according to Vishnu's order, Vishnu's instructions, Vishnu's directions, the Devatas are working perfectly. There is no reason to think that some insignificant person of this world can destroy the entire planet. That never can happen. In the thirty-third verse, it is described, The meaning of this thirty-third verse is, Arjuna, his eyes blazing in anger, like two red balls of copper, dexterously arrested the son of Gautami and bound him with ropes, like an animal. Pashumrashanayata. So, Arjuna was very angry against this Ashwatthama because Ashwatthama had killed the five sleeping sons of Draupadi. So, Arjuna was very angry against this. He was so angry against Ashwatthama.

---
### Arjuna's actions, Ashwatthama's treatment, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uTZ4IewLiuc?t=574
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It was not wrong on the part of Arjuna to bind Ashwatthama with ropes like an animal."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Arjuna's anger and his treatment of Ashwatthama, explaining that binding him with ropes like an animal was justified as part of Arjuna's duty (Dharma) and because Ashwatthama had degraded himself.

And therefore, Arjuna's description here is, His eyes blazing in anger like two red balls of copper. He was so angry, his red eyes had become so red. And Arjuna arrested this Ashwatthama. He did not kill him immediately. He arrested him. And how did he arrest him? He bound him up with ropes like an animal. Generally a human being is not bound up by ropes like that. But here the treatment meted out to Ashwatthama by Arjuna is justified by Srila Prabhupada in the purport. Srila Prabhupada says, Ashwatthama's mother Krippi was born in the family of Gautama. So she is referred to as Gautami. And Ashwatthama is referred to as Gautami Sutam. The significant point in this shloka is that Ashwatthama was caught and bound up with ropes like an animal. According to Sridhar Swami, Arjuna was obliged to catch this son of a Brahmana like an animal as part of the ritual. As part of his duty, Dharma. So the Acharyas are explaining that Arjuna was just doing his duty. It was not wrong on the part of Arjuna to bind Ashwatthama with ropes like an animal. This suggestion by Sridhar Swami is also confirmed in the later statement of Sri Krishna. Later on Krishna also will tell the same thing. Ashwatthama was a bona fide son of Dronacharya and Krippi. But because he had degraded himself to a lower status of life, it was proper to treat him like an animal and not as a Brahmana. It is proper for Arjuna to have treated Ashwatthama in that way. And the 34th verse.

---
### Krishna's lotus eyes, transcendence, modes of nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uTZ4IewLiuc?t=712
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is not affected by some moods or some influence of some event."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 34th verse, describing Arjuna taking Ashwatthama to the military camp and Krishna looking on with lotus eyes. He explains that Krishna's anger is different from Arjuna's, as Krishna is transcendent and unaffected by the modes of material nature.

Shibiraya Nini Shantam Rajwa Baddhwaripum Balat Praha Arjunam Prakupito Bhagavan Ambujekshanaha. The meaning of this 34th verse is after binding Ashwatthama, Arjuna wanted to take him to the military camp. The personality of God is Sri Krishna looking on with his lotus eyes, spoke to angry Arjuna. So Arjuna wants to take him to the camp, their own military camp. But before that Krishna is now going to speak to Arjuna. And Krishna is described as looking on with his lotus eyes. And Arjuna, he was very angry. His eyes were red. Two red hot balls of copper. That's already described. In the Purport, Srila Prabhupada says that both Arjuna and Lord Sri Krishna are described here in angry mood. But Arjuna's eyes were like balls of red copper whereas the eyes of the Lord were like lotuses. What does this mean? This means that the angry mood of Arjuna and that of the Lord are not on the same level. Why? Because the Lord is transcendence and is absolute in any stage. Krishna is not affected by some moods or some influence of some event. He is unaffected under all circumstances at all times in all places. So he is transcendently situated. Always. Eternally. His anger, that is Krishna's anger, is not like the anger of a conditioned living being within the modes of qualitative material nature. When we become angry, we are influenced by the modes of material nature. Rajoguna, Tamoguna. Because Krishna is absolute, both his anger and pleasure are the same. His anger is not exhibited in the three modes of material nature. It is only a sign of his bent of mind towards the cause of his devotee.

---
### devotee's welfare, transcendental nature, Krishna's position
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uTZ4IewLiuc?t=873
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even if Krishna is angry, the object of anger is blessed."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by stating that Krishna's anger is for the welfare of his devotees, as he felt for Arjuna and Draupadi. Krishna is unchanged and always transcendentally situated, even when angry.

Because that is his transcendental nature. Krishna felt for Arjuna because Ashwatthama had beheaded the sleeping sons of Draupadi. Draupadi is also Krishna's devotee. So Krishna was feeling for his devotee. Therefore, even if Krishna is angry, the object of anger is blessed. He is unchanged in all circumstances. This is Krishna's position. He is unaffected by any situation. He is always transcendently situated. Even when he is angry against somebody, that is Krishna.

Om Agyanati Miran Dhasya Gyananj Nishalakaya Chakshurun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnu Padaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. In this session, we will be discussing from the 35th and the 36th verses of the 7th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam.

### Dharma, Brahmana, Qualification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xIhzblKD8I4?t=65
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.35
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That simply by birth in a Brahmin family, without qualification, nobody can become a Brahmana."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains the meaning of verse 35, focusing on the term 'Brahma Bandhu' and emphasizing that being born in a Brahmana family does not automatically qualify one as a Brahmana.

The 35th verse: Mainam Partharhasitratum Brahma Bandhum Imamjahi Yosav Anagasa Suptan Avadheen Nishi Balakan. The meaning of this verse is Lord Sri Krishna said, 'O Arjuna, you should not show mercy by releasing this relative of a Brahmana, Brahma Bandhu, for he has killed innocent boys in their sleep.' Krishna is justifying punishing Ashwatthama appropriately because Ashwatthama has killed innocent boys in their sleep. He is not observing the rules of Dharma Yudha. And he is being referred to as Brahma Bandhu, relative of a Brahmana. He is not a Brahmana. By his actions, he has proved otherwise. So, in the purport, Srila Prabhupada says the word Brahma Bandhu is significant. Ashwatthama being referred to as Brahma Bandhu is significant. A person who happens to take birth in the family of a Brahmana, but is not qualified to be called a Brahmana is addressed as the relative of a Brahmana and not as a Brahmana. This is a very, very important point to be understood. That simply by birth in a Brahmin family, without qualification, nobody can become a Brahmana.

---
### Analogy, Qualification, Brahmana
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xIhzblKD8I4?t=177
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As the high court judgeship is a post for a qualified man, so also the post of a Brahmana is attainable by qualification only."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a high court judge's son to further illustrate that birth alone does not confer qualification, emphasizing that a Brahmana must acquire the necessary qualities and characteristics.

Prabhupada gives an example. The son of a high court judge is not virtually a high court judge. But there is no harm in addressing a high court judge's son as a relative of the honourable justice. So, Brahma Bandhu, because he is son of a Brahmana, he can be addressed as Brahma Bandhu. Therefore, as by birth only, one does not become a high court judge. So also, one does not become a Brahmana simply by birthright, but by acquiring the necessary qualifications of a Brahmana. So, qualifications are very important. In modern times, there are some people who claim to be Brahmana just by birth in the Brahmin family, but they don't have the qualifications. In the Bhagavad Gita, these qualifications are described. Nine different qualifications: Shamo Damo Tapas Shoucam Shanti Rarjam Mevacha Gyanam Vigyanam Astikyam Brahma Karma Subhavajam. A Brahmana naturally works by these qualities. Control of mind, control of senses, Shamo Damo Tapas Shoucam. Tapa is austerity, and Shoucam is cleanliness. Like that. So, a Brahmana has to have qualification. As the high court judgeship is a post for a qualified man, so also the post of a Brahmana is attainable by qualification only.

---
### Shastra, Qualification, Social Order
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xIhzblKD8I4?t=282
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That only by qualification, we can call somebody a Brahmana."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Shastras dictate that a qualified person from any family should be accepted as a Brahmana, and conversely, one born in a Brahmana family without qualifications should not be considered one.

This is the Shastric injunction, but it is not understood properly or not enforced properly in modern times. The Shastra enjoins that even if good qualifications are seen in a person born in a family other than that of a Brahmana, the qualified man has to be accepted as a Brahmana. Yesiyal lakshanam proktam, that whatever the qualities that are seen in a particular person, irrespective of his birth in some family. Let's say somebody is born in a Vaishya family, but the person displays all qualities of a Brahmana, he has to be accepted as a Brahmana. That's what it is telling. The Shastras say like that. Similarly, if a person born in the family of Brahmana is void of Brahmanical qualification, he must be treated as a non-Brahmana, or in better terms, a relative of a Brahmana, Brahmabandhu. So, without qualification, nobody should be addressed as a Brahmana. And, there is sometimes protest by the so-called Brahmanas if somebody born in a Vaishya family is referred to as a Brahmana because the person is qualified as a Brahmana, the others will protest. Lord Shri Krishna, the supreme authority of all religious principles, the Vedas, has personally pointed out these differences. Krishna actually explains this in several places, in several occasions, in stances. That only by qualification, we can call somebody a Brahmana.

---
### Dharma, Ethics, Warfare
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xIhzblKD8I4?t=394
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.36
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A person who knows the principles of religion does not kill an enemy who is careless, intoxicated, insane, asleep, afraid or devoid of his chariot."
**Summary:** The speaker transitions to verse 36, explaining that it outlines the principles of religious warfare, stating that a Dharmavit (one who knows dharma) does not kill an enemy who is incapacitated or defenseless, nor does he kill boys, women, or surrendered souls.

And a Brahmana, actually, even if he is qualified, he has to behave and conduct himself like a Brahmana. He should display those qualities which are mentioned in the scriptures. So, he is a Brahmana. Now, Krishna is about to explain the reason for referring to Ashwatthama as a Brahmabandhu in the next few shlokas. So, the 36th shloka is again spoken by Krishna: mattham pramatham unmattham suptam bhaatam kalam striyam jadam prapannam viratham bhitham naripum hanti dharmavit. The meaning of this 36th verse is that a person who knows the principles of religion, such a person is called dharmavit, does not kill an enemy who is careless, intoxicated, insane, asleep, afraid or devoid of his chariot. viratham, devoid of chariot, afraid, bheetham, asleep, suptam, then mattham. Mattham means careless and pramatham, intoxicated, unmattham, insane. That's the shastric injunction. Nor does a person who knows the religious principles kill a boy, a woman, a foolish creature or a surrendered soul. Boy is balam, a woman is striyam, then jadam, one who is foolish and prapannam, one who is surrendered soul. So, there are strict principles by which a follower of religious principles conducts himself.

---
### Dharma, Warfare, Ethics
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xIhzblKD8I4?t=528
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Violence carried out on religious principles is far superior to so called non-violence."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the purport, explaining that a warrior who knows dharma does not kill an enemy who does not resist, and that battles were formerly fought on religious principles, not for sense gratification.

So, Shri La Prabhupada explains in the purport, an enemy who does not resist is never killed by a warrior who knows the principles of religion. Somebody may be designated as enemy, but if he is not ready for fighting or he does not resist when attacked then he should not be killed. Formerly battles were fought on the principles of religion and not for the sake of sense gratification. Religious principles, Dharmayuddha, Dharmavit, one who knows the principles of religion. Formerly it was fought like that. If the enemy happened to be intoxicated as sleep etc., as above mentioned, he was never to be killed. Just like this Ashwatthama, he killed the sleeping sons. So, that somebody is sleeping you should not kill them, even if they belong to enemy party. These are some of the codes of religious war, Dharmayuddha. Formerly war was never declared by the whims of selfish political leaders. It was carried out on religious principles free from all vices. No bad habits, no irreligious behaviour. So, that way Dharmayuddha used to take place in the former times. Violence carried out on religious principles is far superior to so called non-violence.

---
### Non-violence, Context, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xIhzblKD8I4?t=662
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When Buddha invented, people were indiscriminately killing animals in the name of Vedic sacrifice."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that violence carried out on religious principles is superior to wrongly applied non-violence, giving the example of Buddha's incarnation to stop indiscriminate animal killing.

Srila Prabhupada concludes this purport by telling one very important thing. Sometimes people think non-violence is to be practiced and followed, but that is got a specific situation where that non-violence has to be applied. When Buddha invented, people were indiscriminately killing animals in the name of Vedic sacrifice. So, that is when Buddha incarnated and he wanted to stop this animal killing. And the genuine followers of the Vedas of our scriptures they recognized and understood yes, animals should not be unnecessarily killed. So, a person who knows religious principles actually is always careful not to kill even the enemy when they are sleeping, when they are when they are without a chariot. If he is a chariot fighter, he has to be on a chariot. If he is without a chariot, they will never attack such a person. And in the course of a war, children and women are never, never, never killed. This is very, very important.

---
### Dharma, Society, Violence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xIhzblKD8I4?t=770
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Principles of religion were followed very strictly in the former times."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing the advanced nature of ancient society in following religious principles, highlighting the protection offered by Kshatriyas and the benefits of violence carried out according to dharma, contrasting it with the sinful reactions of wrongly applied non-violence.

So, we come to know through all these instances described here how in former times the society was very, very highly developed and advanced in following all such religious principles. And these principles are very, very very practical to follow. It is not simply some theory alone. At least 5000 years back, there was strict adherence to the principles of religion by the followers of Dharma, especially the Brahmans, Kshatriyas and Vaishyas used to follow all the religious principles. They never would violate, generally, they never would violate. And therefore society was well organized and very, very peaceful. And the Kshatriyas always protect the citizens against attack by the Brahmans. Some foreign king or foreign person did not allow that the citizens were attacked. So therefore, the principles of religion were followed very strictly in the former times. And when somebody engages in violence, these principles, when it is done, it is actually beneficial for everyone. It is never degrading anyone. And non-violence wrongly applied means there could be sinful reactions. That's what Krishna tells Arjuna in the fourth chapter of Bhagavad Gita.

### Justice, Karma, Punishment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LZpxLDNv3-g?t=54
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.37
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A cruel and wretched person who maintains his existence at the cost of others' lives deserves to be killed for his own well-being, otherwise he will go down by his own actions."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 37th verse of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining that a cruel person who lives at the expense of others deserves punishment for their own benefit, to avoid further degradation.

Swaprananya Parapranayi Prapushnati Agrana Khalaha Tad Vadastasya Hi Shreyo Yad Doshad Yad Yad Pumaan. The meaning of this 37th verse: A cruel and wretched person who maintains his existence at the cost of others' lives deserves to be killed for his own well-being, otherwise he will go down by his own actions.

---
### Punishment, Karma, Liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LZpxLDNv3-g?t=92
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Instead of pardoning him and making him suffer in hell in future, it is better that he be punished now."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna instructs Arjuna that Ashwathama should be punished for his crime to prevent further degradation and suffering. Srila Prabhupada elaborates that just punishment, even capital punishment, can liberate a murderer from future suffering in hell, which would be far more severe.

Now Krishna is instructing Arjuna that Ashwathama has committed a very serious crime, and if he is not punished now, he is going to degrade himself and he is going to suffer the reaction anyway at a later stage, so he has to be punished for his own good, for his own well-being. So Srila Prabhupada elaborately explains this in the purport: A life for a life is just punishment for a person who cruelly and shamelessly lives at the cost of others' lives. So much discussion nowadays is going on that punishment is not justified, capital punishment is to a for a murder. If the laws of the land punish him by giving him a punishment where he'll be able to become free from the reaction when is punished properly, appropriately political morality is to punish a person by a death sentence in order to save a cruel person from going to hell. The punishment in hell is much much much much more severe than just this death sentence now, because he has committed a murder or he has killed somebody unjustly. So instead of pardoning him and making him suffer in hell in future, it is better that he be punished now. That a murderer is condemned to a death sentence by the state is good for the culprit, because in his next life, he will not have to suffer for his act of murder.

---
### Karma, Punishment, Purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LZpxLDNv3-g?t=224
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Men who are punished by the king on the principle of life for a life are purified of all their sins."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that suffering in the present is a result of past actions that were not atoned for or punished. A death sentence is the lowest possible punishment and purifies the murderer of their sins, potentially leading to promotion to heavenly planets.

See, people sometimes say, I can't remember what all I did in my previous life. Why am I made to suffer now? That's because you have to suffer. You have done the mistake and you did not atone for it, nor you received punishment for the mistake you did. So neither you atoned nor you received punishment. Therefore, in the next life, you have to suffer the reaction. Such a death sentence for the murderer is the lowest. Lowest possible punishment offered to him. You see, lowest possible punishment. And it is said in the Smriti Shastras that men who are punished by the king on the principle of life for a life are purified of all their sins. See, they became purified, even though they might have committed a very, very grievous sin of killing another person unjustly. So much so that they may be eligible for being promoted to the planets of heaven. So they are completely freed from the reaction of having for having committed that sin if they are punished appropriately. Now, something very, very important. Srila Prabhupada says,

---
### Non-violence, Karma, Responsibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LZpxLDNv3-g?t=344
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even the killer of an animal is to be considered a murderer."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srila Prabhupada, citing Manu, that even killing an animal is considered murder. He emphasizes that civilized society should not kill animals for food when there are plenty of vegetables, fruits, grains, and milk available. All those involved in the conspiracy of killing an animal are liable to be punished as murderers.

Here, according to Manu, the great author of civic codes and religious principles, Manu is a lawgiver for mankind. Even the killer of an animal is to be considered a murderer. Killer of an animal. Nowadays, people are killing animals so much because animal food is never meant for the civilized man whose prime duty is to prepare himself for going back to Godhead. In a civilized society, when there is plenty of vegetables, fruits, grains, milk, why should people kill animals for food? That is the point being made here. Animal food is never meant for the civilized man. And especially civilized human beings are meant for preparing themselves to go back to Godhead. That is a precious opportunity in this human form of life. So Manu says that in the act of killing an animal, there is a regular conspiracy by the party of sinners and all of them are liable to be punished as murderers, exactly like a party of conspirators who kill a human being combinedly. Just like the state law, if a murder happens, it's not just the person who actually killed is punished. Even the others who may be hatching a conspiracy to kill and they engage somebody to kill. Even the conspirators are equally guilty of this murder. That is the state law. Similarly, in killing of an animal, all those who are responsible directly or indirectly for that animal killing will all actually be liable to be punished. Now here, Prabhupada is quoting Manu that who are all liable to be punished when an animal killing happens? He who gives permission, he who kills the animal, he who sells the slaughtered animal, he who cooks the animal, he who administers distribution of that foodstuff, animal flesh, and at last he who eats such cooked animal food are all murderers. Six kinds of persons involved in animal killing are all murderers.

---
### Karma, Responsibility, Non-violence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LZpxLDNv3-g?t=505
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Six types of people are liable to be punished when animal killing happens in a civilized human society."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the six types of people involved in animal killing who are all considered murderers: the one who gives permission, the one who kills, the one who sells the slaughtered animal, the one who cooks, the one who distributes, and the one who eats the cooked animal food. All are liable to be punished by the laws of nature because no one can create life and therefore has no right to take it.

Even though actual killing is done by one person, but the others, I'll read this again. He who gives permission to kill the animal, somebody is rearing an animal, he actually sells that animal for animal slaughter. Or he may not sell it, but he says, Oh, now this animal is of no use to me. You may take it and you may do whatever you want. You can even kill it. No. He is not absolved of that sin of animal killing. So the person who gives permission, the person who kills the animal, obviously he is liable to be punished. He who sells the slaughtered animal, after the animal is killed, that flesh is sold for those who want to eat meat, animal flesh. He who cooks the animal, somebody is going to cook that meat, because human beings don't eat raw flesh, unlike tigers and lions and all those carnivorous animals, human beings eat cooked flesh. So somebody is cooking that flesh. And those who administer distribution of this cooked meat, somebody is engaged in distributing it. And at last, the person who eats cooked animal food are all murderers. Six types of people are liable to be punished when animal killing happens in a civilized human society. All of them are liable to be punished by the laws of nature. Why? Because no one can create a living being despite all advancement of material science. And therefore, no one has the right to kill a living being by one's independent wills. We cannot create life, so we cannot kill.

---
### Sacrifice, Restriction, Animal Killing
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LZpxLDNv3-g?t=652
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Animal sacrifice is restricted, and such sanctions are there just to restrict the opening of slaughterhouses."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that while animal sacrifice is mentioned in scriptures, it is highly restricted and not meant to encourage animal killing. In ancient times, animals sacrificed in yajnas were revived by brahmanas who knew how to chant mantras and give new life. However, modern animal killings do not revive the animals and are therefore punishable.

So, we should also understand, if an animal is sacrificed in a yajna, then, that animal being sacrificed, it will be revived. After the sacrifice, the animal which was killed will be revived. It will be given a new life. How? By brahmanas who know how to chant mantras and actually give new life to a dead animal. So, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu explains, in a sacrifice, when an animal is killed for the sake of the sacrifice, such animal is given a new life. So, therefore, Srila Prabhupada says, in modern times, when they are killing animals, they are not reviving the animal, they don't know how to give new life, so they are liable to be punished. They cannot kill animals like that. For the animal eaters, the scriptures have sanctioned restricted animal sacrifices only. See, animal sacrifice is restricted. And such sanctions are there just to restrict the opening of slaughterhouses. Just so that people don't systematically kill animals in a slaughterhouse. To prevent that, Shastras give a concession that under highly restricted conditions, an animal flesh eater may sacrifice an animal in a suitable animal sacrifice. It is not meant for encouraging animal killing. This animal sacrifice sanctioned in the scriptures is never meant for animal killing.

---
### Sacrifice, Compassion, Slaughterhouses
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LZpxLDNv3-g?t=775
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The procedure under which animal sacrifices are allowed in the scriptures is both good for the animal sacrificed and also good for the animal eaters."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that animal sacrifice, when performed according to scriptural guidelines, benefits both the animal and the meat-eater. The animal is immediately promoted to a human form of life, and the meat-eater avoids the grosser sins of consuming meat from organized slaughterhouses. Slaughterhouses are described as ghastly places that breed material afflictions for society.

The procedure under which animal sacrifices are allowed in the scriptures is both good for the animal sacrificed and also good for the animal eaters. That's the way the scriptures are advising the animal sacrifice to be performed by meat eaters. It's good for the animal in the sense that the sacrificed animal is at once promoted to the human form of life after being sacrificed. After being sacrificed at the altar, when the rules are followed for animal sacrifice, the animal is immediately promoted to the human form of life. It doesn't have to wait for gradual evolution to the human form. And the animal eater is saved from grosser types of sins eating meat supplied by organized slaughterhouses. One is never meant to purchase flesh from a slaughterhouse. No. If at all they want to eat the sanction is there for animal sacrifice to be performed and in that way one may under restricted conditions indulge in meat eating. Not otherwise. These slaughterhouses Srila Prabhupada describes here are ghastly places for breeding all kinds of material afflictions to society, country and the people in general. When there is a slaughterhouse there is a wholesale sinful reaction that the country, the society, the people in general have to suffer. So it is very very dangerous to have a slaughterhouse. It's not good for the society.

---
### Sin, Calamities, Karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LZpxLDNv3-g?t=884
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By encouraging animal slaughter the whole atmosphere becomes polluted more and more."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by stating that the material world is full of anxieties, and encouraging animal slaughter pollutes the atmosphere, leading to war, pestilence, famine, and other calamities. These calamities are a result of sinful activities, and there is a direct link between them. There is no accident or chance in this world, as explained in the scriptures. The lecture ends with a thank you and the Hare Krishna mantra.

The material world is itself a place always full of anxieties. And by encouraging animal slaughter the whole atmosphere becomes polluted more and more. How does it become polluted? By war, pestilence, famine and many other unwanted calamities. Calamities are because people become sinful in this society. Otherwise there will be no calamities. So there is a link between some calamity and natural calamity and the sinful activities indulged in by the people in general. So they are linked up. It is not something accident or by chance something happened. No. There is no chance, there is no accident in this world. That's what the scriptures explain. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.
