### Promise, Retaliation, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5gwzfdNVcDU?t=63
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.38, SB 1.7.16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Furthermore I have personally heard you promise Draupadi that you would bring forth the head of the killer of her sons."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces verses 38 and 39 of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Krishna reminding Arjuna of his promise to Draupadi to avenge the killing of her sons by Ashwatthama.

In this session we will be discussing from the 38th and the 39th verses of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 38th verse: Pratishrutam Chabhavata Panchalyei Shunvatomama Aharishye Shrastasya Yaste Maanini Putraha. The meaning of this verse is Krishna is telling Arjuna, 'Furthermore I have personally heard you promise Draupadi that you would bring forth the head of the killer of her sons.' This was what Arjuna had told Draupadi when Draupadi informed Arjuna that Ashwatthama has killed her five sleeping sons. So what was it that Arjuna had promised? In the 16th verse of the same chapter, Arjuna had said, 'O gentle lady, when I present you with the head of that brahmana, after beheading him with arrows from my Gandiva bow, I shall then wipe the tears from your eyes and pacify you. Then after burning your sons bodies, you can take your baths standing on his head.' Arjuna was very angry, angry at the act of Ashwathama killing, beheading, just cutting off their head when they were sleeping, the five sleeping sons of Draupadi, totally against all principles of Dharma Yudha. And so if Arjuna had promised to Draupadi, that's what Krishna is reminding Arjuna, 'I personally heard you promise Draupadi that you will bring forth the head of the killer of her sons.'

---
### Aggressor, Punishment, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5gwzfdNVcDU?t=185
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "An enemy who sets fire to the house administers poison attacks all of a sudden with deadly weapons plunders wealth or usurps agricultural fields or entices one's wife is called an aggressor such an aggressor though he be a Brahmana or a so-called son of a Brahmana has to be punished in all circumstances."
**Summary:** Srila Prabhupada's purport explains that an aggressor, even if a Brahmana, must be punished. Arjuna knew Ashwatthama was a Brahmana's son, but his actions warranted punishment, and there is no sin in killing such an aggressor.

Now Srila Prabhupada explains the purport: 'An enemy who sets fire to the house, administers poison, attacks all of a sudden with deadly weapons, plunders wealth, or usurps agricultural fields, or entices one's wife is called an aggressor. Such an aggressor, though he be a Brahmana or a so-called son of a Brahmana, has to be punished in all circumstances.' So there's no question of sparing an aggressor; he has to be punished. When Arjuna promised to behead the aggressor named Ashwatthama, he knew well that Ashwatthama was a son of a Brahmana. Arjuna was quite aware who Ashwatthama is; he's the son of Dronacharya, son of the teacher of Arjuna. But because the so-called Brahmana acted like a butcher, a butcher simply cuts off an animal which is meant for slaughter, so this Ashwatthama acted like a butcher. Therefore Ashwatthama was taken as such, and there is no question of sin in killing such a Brahmana son who proved to be a villain. So there is no sin involved in killing an aggressor.

---
### Murder, Justice, Degradation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5gwzfdNVcDU?t=277
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.39
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This man is an assassin and murderer of your own family members... Kill him immediately."
**Summary:** Krishna instructs Arjuna to kill Ashwatthama, describing him as an assassin, murderer, and someone who dissatisfied his master, Duryodhana, and degraded himself, deserving immediate punishment.

Atatai. Now Krishna will use that word Atatai in the next verse, 39th verse. Krishna says. The meaning of this 39th verse: 'This man is an assassin and murderer of your own family members.' Assassin and murderer. Not only that, but he has also dissatisfied his master. He is but the burnt remnants of his family. Kill him immediately. So this is Krishna's instruction to Arjuna, that Krishna is telling this, 'Ashwatthama, he is a murderer of your own family members. He also dissatisfied his master.' Who was Ashwathama's master? It was Duryodhana, and when Ashwathama revealed to Duryodhana that 'I have killed,' Duryodhana couldn't understand. This Ashwathama has not fought against them and killed them in a fair battle. He has killed them while they were sleeping. So Duryodhana did not approve of this heinous act at all. Duryodhana was totally dissatisfied with this. He said, 'You have not done the right thing.' So therefore Ashwathama is described as the burnt remnants of his family. He has completely degraded himself, shamelessly has degraded himself. Therefore Krishna tells Arjuna, 'Kill him immediately. He deserves this kind of punishment.'

---
### Respect, Duty, Dharma Yudha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5gwzfdNVcDU?t=406
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dronacharya the teacher of Arjuna is on the opposite side of the Pandavas so Dronacharya had to be respected and Arjuna did that how did he do that before fighting with Dronacharya Arjuna first of all threw one arrow on his lotus feet indicating I am paying my obeisances to my teacher my guru this is called etiquette my dear sir Prabhupada explains you have taught me this fighting science Dhanur Vidya now it is being used against you because it is my duty to fight in this Dharma Yudha so I offer my obeisances as a Kshatriya standing in the battlefield."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how Dronacharya was respected even on the opposing side, and Arjuna showed respect by shooting an arrow at his feet before fighting, demonstrating duty and etiquette in Dharma Yudha.

So in the purport Srila Prabhupada explains the son of Dronacharya is condemned here as the burnt remnants of his family. How is Ashwathama the burnt remnants of his family? That's explained by Prabhupada. The good name of Dronacharya was very much respected. Although Dronacharya joined the enemy camp, the Pandavas held him always in respect, and Arjuna saluted him before beginning the fight. There was nothing wrong in that way, fighting on the opposite sides. Arjuna is obviously on the Pandavas side, but Dronacharya, he is the teacher of Arjuna. He is on the side of the Kauravas, the enemy side, but still Arjuna never lost any respect for Dronacharya, neither Dronacharya was having any inimical feeling towards Arjuna. That is explained by Srila Prabhupada in another discourse. Prabhupada says Dronacharya, the teacher of Arjuna, is on the opposite side of the Pandavas, so Dronacharya had to be respected, and Arjuna did that. How did he do that? Before fighting with Dronacharya, Arjuna first of all threw one arrow on his lotus feet, indicating, 'I am paying my obeisances to my teacher, my guru. This is called etiquette, my dear sir.' Prabhupada explains, 'You have taught me this fighting science, Dhanur Vidya, now it is being used against you because it is my duty to fight in this Dharma Yudha, so I offer my obeisances as a Kshatriya standing in the battlefield.' How did Arjuna offer obeisances to Dronacharya? By shooting one arrow at the feet of Dronacharya, and Dronacharya also, he is a military teacher, he is standing ready for fighting against the enemy on the battlefield, and he is not ready to fight against the enemy on the Kaurava side, so what did Dronacharya do? He threw another arrow touching the head of Arjuna, 'My dear boy, become blessed. I mean, you have my blessings.' That was Dronacharya indicated to Arjuna, and Srila Prabhupada explains this is duty.

---
### Dharma Yudha, Kshatriya Dharma, Twice-born
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5gwzfdNVcDU?t=590
**Verse References:** BG 2.31
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Fighting in a Dharma Yudha is a duty of a Kshatriya."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Dharma Yudha, where death leads to elevation, and the duty of a Kshatriya to fight. He then discusses the concept of 'twice-born' (dvijas) and the cultural purification (Samskara) process.

Now how is Dronacharya blessing Arjuna by killing? You are given blessings to kill me? Will anybody bless another person that you kill me? No, but this is in the matter of performing duty of fighting Dharma Yudha. This is called the Dharma Yudha. Why? Because in a Dharma Yudha, whether one is victorious or one is killed, either case it is not fair to kill one or one is not, they are elevated to a higher status in their next life. That's why Dharma Yudha is duty. Fighting in a Dharma Yudha is a duty of a Kshatriya. That's what Krishna explains, sorry, Krishna explains in Bhagavad Gita to Arjuna. Swadharmam, it is your Swadharma as a Kshatriya to fight in this Dharma Yudha. The disciple is fighting, touching the feet of Dronacharya. It is my duty. And disciple is telling the teacher, 'Please give me your blessing to fight.' And Dronacharya, he is telling, 'Yes, you have my blessings to fight in this Dharma Yudha.' So continuing the purport of Srila Prabhupada, but the son of Dronacharya degraded himself by committing acts which were never done by the dvijas or the twice-born higher castes. Who are the twice-born higher castes? The dvijas or twice-born higher castes belong to the first three varanas: Brahmana, Kshatriya, and Vaishya. They are the first birth from their parents, and there is a second birth. How is the second birth possible? By accepting the spiritual master as the father and Vedas as the mother. Veda Mata, Guru Pita. During the second birth, which is meant for beginning the learning of the Vedas, they go to Gurukul. In the Vedic culture, they are sent to Gurukul to be trained up. Brahmanas, Kshatriyas, and Vaishyas. They are trained up by a qualified teacher, qualified Brahmana. This is called cultural purification. Samskara. It is called Samskara. It is cultural purification. Human life is meant for purification. So even by performing Samskaras, this purification happens.

---
### Spiritual Initiation, Murder, Duryodhana's disapproval
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5gwzfdNVcDU?t=753
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By birth from parents everyone is born a Shudra. But by spiritual initiation one becomes a Vidya."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that everyone is born a Shudra, but spiritual initiation transforms one into a Vidya. He contrasts Kshatriya fights in Dharma Yuga with Ashwatthama's murder of sleeping sons, which even Duryodhana disapproved of.

The scriptures declare Janmana Jayate Shudra Samskarat Vijam Uchyate. So by birth from parents everyone is born a Shudra. But by spiritual initiation one becomes a Vidya. That initiation happens when one goes to a Guru and submits to the Guru. 'I am your disciple. Please accept me as your disciple and train me, teach me Vedic knowledge.' So continuing the purport. Then murder by killing the five sleeping sons of Draupadi. This is murder. This is not like Kshatriya fights in a Dharma Yuga and kills the enemy. It is not like that. In that there is no sin. It is not to be considered as murder, fighting in a Dharma Yuga. But here the sleeping sons of Draupadi were killed by Ashwathama. And by this act of killing the sleeping sons of Draupadi, this Ashwathama dissatisfied his master Duryodhana. Duryodhana never approved of the heinous act of killing the five sleeping sons of the Pandavas. Now Duryodhana also was fighting the battle of Kurukshetra following the principles of Dharma Yuga.

---

**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5gwzfdNVcDU?t=867
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker concludes by reiterating that killing someone sleeping is against Dharma, making Ashwatthama an aggressor deserving of punishment, as instructed by Krishna to Arjuna.

This is explained earlier by Krishna. Suptam means sleeping. One who is sleeping is not to be killed. So therefore Ashwathama became an assaulter of Arjuna's family members and thus he was liable to be punished by Arjuna. And therefore Krishna tells Arjuna, 'Now you must actually kill this aggressor Ashwathama. You should kill him.' I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

Arjuna is aware of his duty and will act responsibly, which is the difference between a devotee and a non-devotee, as non-devotees can be swayed by material energy and fail in their duties.

Arjuna has to punish Ashwathama. But what kind of punishment? That is elaborately discussed in the rest of the chapter. It is not that Arjuna was incomplete in the sense of his duty. Nor was Lord Sri Krishna unaware of Arjuna's sense of duty. Krishna knew. Arjuna is aware of his duty and he will act responsibly. This is the difference between a devotee and a non-devotee. A non-devotee under all circumstances acts responsibly. A non-devotee may or may not be responsible in all his actions because a non-devotee under the influence of the glaring material energy is carried away sometimes and will fail in properly doing his duty or fail in taking responsibility. Now here Ashwatthama was bound with ropes like an animal, but what is Ashwatthama's position? Ashwatthama was bound up because he did not behave like a Brahmana. He did not behave like a Brahmana even though he is the son of a Brahmana, son of Dronacharya, son of the Acharya, teacher, military teacher of Arjuna, and he deserves to be respected as such. But he behaved most degrading manner, he degraded himself. So therefore Arjuna bound him up, but when he was brought in front of Draupadi she showed him due respects as a Brahmana. ### Custom, Shastra, Respect
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TRjchKH11tE?t=375
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is customary to offer the same respect that is offered to the father is offered to his son."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Draupadi's perspective with that of Krishna and Arjuna, explaining that she acted out of custom, respecting Ashwatthama as the son of Dronacharya. He clarifies that while society generally respects a Brahmana's son, the shastras dictate that one should only be respected as a Brahmana if they possess the qualities of one, regardless of birth.

Considered not from the angle of shastric vision but as a matter of custom. It is customary to offer the same respect that is offered to the father is offered to his son. Especially when the father is a teacher, a brahmana, deserves all respects. So the son is also offered similar respect that is a custom. Why? Because people generally accept the son of a brahmana as a brahmana. But the shastras say the son of the brahmana if he is not qualified as a brahmana he should not be accepted as a brahmana. You see a lakshanam proktam. This is the verse in the Bhagavatam. Whatever the symptoms that are displayed by a person irrespective of the family in which he is born, irrespective of whose son he is, if he displays the symptoms of a shudra, he should be accepted as a shudra. In terms of social interaction, what is the respect that is due? What is the respect that is due? The respect that is due to a brahmana who is qualified is different. The respect that is due to a shudra who is not qualified as a brahmana but having the symptoms of a shudra is different. So Draupadi accepting Ashwathama who has degraded himself, behaved in the most degrading manner in killing Draupadi's own sons in a most inglorious way. He is a murderer. He is a criminal. So he should not be treated like a brahmana.

He explains that Arjuna spared Ashwatthama because he was the son of his guru, and Draupadi, being soft-hearted, didn't withdraw the respect due to Dronacharya's son.

So Srila Prabhupada is explaining elsewhere that in the Vedic culture they know how to offer respect to the proper persons. They know how to offer respect. You consider Arjuna's position. Krishna told Arjuna after Ashwathama was arrested by Arjuna, Krishna told Ashwathama, kill him. He doesn't deserve to actually be excused for this most inglorious murder he has committed. But Arjuna did not kill him. Arjuna could have killed him but he did not kill him. Why? Because Arjuna thought he is a murderer. Why? Why? Because Arjuna is a son of the brahmina. The son of my guru. So as son of guru, of course, he is not a brahmana. Son of a brahmana is not a brahmana when he is not behaving according to the brahmanical standard. So he is considered Brahma bandhu. He is a relative of a brahmana. He is a son of the brahmana. But son of a Brahmana, Arjuna did not kill him. Similarly, Draupadi, considering that he is a son of a Brahmana, she actually being very soft hearted, Vamasvabha, previous verse explained that Vamasvabha. She did not withdraw the respect that is due to Ashwatthama as the son of Dronachali. So the important point is that Arjuna did not kill him and Draupadi did not even withdraw the respect. This is a very, very advanced culture in human society, very advanced culture.

### Dhanurveda, Vedic knowledge, weapons
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cgnQKK7lYx8?t=53
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.44
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Gross military science is dependent on material weapons but finer than that is the art of throwing arrows saturated with Vedic hymns which act more effectively than gross material weapons like machine guns or atom bombs."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam (1.7.44) and explains Srila Prabhupada's purport, focusing on the Dhanurveda, the Vedic military science, and how it uses mantras to control weapons, a science lost in modern times.

In this session we will be discussing from the 44th verse of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Sarahasyo Dhanur Vedaha Savisargo Pasamyamaha Astra Gramascha Bhavata Shikshito Yad Anugrahat. The meaning of this verse is it was by Dronacharya's mercy that you learnt the military art of throwing arrows and the confidential art of controlling weapons. Sarahasyo Dhanur Vedaha. So Shri La Prabhupada explains in the purport Dhanur Veda or military science was taught by Dronacharya with all its confidential secrets of throwing and controlling by Vedic hymns. This is unknown in modern times. Control a weapon after releasing it by mantra, sound. Gross military science is dependent on material weapons but finer than that is the art of throwing arrows saturated with Vedic hymns which act more effectively than gross material weapons like machine guns or atom bombs. See the difference? That art, or the science, is lost now. It's not there. The control is by Vedic mantras or the transcendental science of sound.

---
### Shabda Vedi, Ramayana, skill
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cgnQKK7lYx8?t=178
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "He could pierce his target with his arrow by only hearing the sound without seeing the object."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the power of sound in controlling weapons, referencing Maharaj Dasaratha from the Ramayana, who possessed the skill of Shabda Vedi, hitting targets by sound alone, and explains the meaning of Shabda Vedi.

Vedic mantras, they are not ordinary sounds. The sound can be used to direct a weapon, to control a weapon, to target a particular person when you release a weapon. That is lost now. That science is lost. So in the Ramayana, it is said that Maharaj Dasaratha, the father of Lord Sri Rama used to control arrows by sound only. He could pierce his target with his arrow by only hearing the sound without seeing the object. So Srila Prabhupad explains it is called Shabda Vedi. Just like I cannot see where is a bird, but the bird is chirping. And by Shabda Vedi, I can release an arrow. It will go wherever the bird is by the dint of the sound the bird is making. And it will go and kill that bird. So, this is called Shabda Vedi. Now Maharaja Dasharatha used it. He knew this science and he used it.

---
### mistake, consequences, Shravan Kumar
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cgnQKK7lYx8?t=279
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So, he released an arrow aimed at whoever was actually making that sound while fetching water or drinking water."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of how Maharaj Dasaratha mistakenly killed a Brahmachari named Shravan Kumar while hunting, illustrating the potential for misuse of the Shabda Vedi skill.

And once he made a mistake of instead of killing an animal in the forest, he killed a boy, one Brahmachari. It is described in the Ramayana. Because that Brahmachari had come, he was taking his parents on a pilgrimage and they were old. He was carrying them in a palanquin and when they were thirsty on the way, so he stopped and he went to fetch water. So, he went looking for a water source and he found a pond with clear water. So, he tried to take some water in a pot. So, when he dipped that pot in the pond to get water, it made sound and this Dasharatha was actually hiding. And when he heard that sound, he thought here is some animal that has come to drink water in the pond. So, he released an arrow aimed at whoever was actually making that sound while fetching water or drinking water. And the arrow went and actually pierced that Brahmachari. His name was Shravan Kumar. That is described in the Ramayana.

---
### repentance, curse, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cgnQKK7lYx8?t=391
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Then they were ecstatic. They were extremely aggrieved because only his son, they were dependent on him."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Shravan Kumar's death, describing Dasharatha's remorse, his encounter with the Brahmachari's parents, and the curse they placed upon him due to their grief.

And Shravan Kumar almost immediately was killed by the arrow released by Dasharatha. Then Dasharatha, after having released the arrow, when he heard a human being is crying out, then he went and saw, Oh, I have killed a Brahmachari. He never intended to kill a Brahmachari. No. But he mistook by the sound of that pot he was trying to fill. He mistook that sound to be the sound of an animal drinking water. And because he knew the science without seeing the target, He actually shot the arrow, Shabda Vedi, and it went and pierced that target, the Brahmachari who made that sound while filling the pot. So when he came and saw, he was very, very repentant. Then just before that boy died, he said, my parents are waiting nearby. They are elderly. They are in a palanquin. So kindly give them water. They are very thirsty. So this Dasharatha, and saying this, he died. Dasharatha took that pot of water and actually went to give that water to nearby. He saw a palanquin and two elderly people. Obviously, the parents of that Brahmachari, Shravan Kumar, were sitting. And he went and tried to give them water. They asked, who are you? He said, I am actually a king and I had come for hunting and I am very sorry to inform you that by mistake, instead of killing an animal, I killed your son. So I have come to give you water. Then they were ecstatic. They were extremely aggrieved because only his son, they were dependent on him. And they cursed this Dasharatha that even Dasharatha would die being aggrieved by the loss of his son.

---
### promise, grief, consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cgnQKK7lYx8?t=553
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And in that grief he died."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the story of Dasharatha, explaining how the curse manifested in his grief over Rama's exile and ultimately led to his death, highlighting the consequences of his actions and the importance of properly utilizing knowledge.

And what happened? Because of that curse, Dasharatha, at the time of coronation of Lord Ramachandra, his eldest son, he was obliged to his youngest queen, Kaikeyi, that she demanded that Rama be banished, sent to the forest in exile, 14 years and that her own son, Bharata, be enthroned as the king. And because Dasharatha was bound by a promise to this queen, Kaikeyi, he had to actually fulfill that promise. And then even though he was reluctant, when Rama came to know that his father had promised his stepmother, so he agreed to actually fulfill that demand of his stepmother because he wanted to keep his father's promise intact. He did not want to break that promise. So Rama was, he went away to the forest and Dasharatha could not tolerate that his son was actually banished just when he was about to be coronated and in that grief he died. Of course, because Rama is no ordinary son, dying while constantly thinking and calling out his name, that was good for Dasharatha because Rama is the Supreme Lord. So that was his blessing that he got the Supreme Lord as his son and therefore, even if, whatever be the case, he was actually fortunate. But still, Dasharatha did commit a grave mistake by not properly utilizing the Shabda Vedi. So they knew this science. And that's how Arjuna was taught all this by Dronacharya.

---
### Vedas, Brahmavidya, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cgnQKK7lYx8?t=690
**Verse References:** BG 15.15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Vedas ultimate goal is to know or understand Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the Dhanurveda as one of the Upavedas, subsidiary to the main subject of the Vedas, which is Brahmavidya or spiritual science, emphasizing that the ultimate goal of the Vedas is to understand Krishna and our relationship with Him.

The science called Dhanurveda. It is one of the Upavedas. Dhanurveda, Ayurveda, Sthapatyaveda, Gandharvaveda, the science of dance and music, the science of architecture. That's how they used to build beautiful temples, nice Gopurams, nice Vimanam, very intricately carved, exact dimensions, etc. So all these sciences are there in the Vedas. Upaveda. It is subsidiary because the main subject matter of the Vedas is Brahmavidya, Atmavidya, spiritual science. Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, Adhyatmavidya Vidyanam. So, don't be carried away by some subsidiary subject matter of the Vedas because the main aim of the Vedas Vedaischa Sarvair Ahameva Vedyam. The Vedas ultimate goal is to know or understand Krishna. So this is very important. As I mentioned earlier in a previous session, that Chaitanya Mahaprabhu teaches us the main teaching of the Vedas is Who am I? Who is Krishna? And what is my relationship with Krishna? This is the ultimate goal of life to re-establish our lost relationship with Krishna in devotional service. In devotional service.

---
### detachment, devotion, Pandavas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cgnQKK7lYx8?t=816
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They are not carried away by those skills."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of the Pandavas to illustrate that even with skills and achievements, the ultimate goal is devotion to Krishna, emphasizing their detachment from worldly power and their focus on remembering the Lord at the time of death.

So that way, if somebody learns even a subsidiary subject matter in the Vedas, they understand the ultimate goal is not merely the use of that particular skill or that subject matter. Just like the Pandavas. They won the battle of Kurukshetra, but they ruled for very short time because they were ruling because it was Krishna's instruction to them because they were divine natured. So they should be ruling the people because they are divine natured, not demoniac natured persons like Kauravas. So they were ruling. But when they came to know that Krishna has actually departed for his personal abode, immediately they retired. Immediately they retired and went on Mahaprasthana. They renounced. Of course before renouncing they handed over charge of ruling to the grandson, Arjuna's grandson Parikshit Maharaj. So Yudhishthira handed over charge to Parikshit Maharaj and all the five Pandavas they went away, Mahaprasthana. They keep walking while remembering the Supreme Lord and in that consciousness of remembrance of the Lord they will quit the body whenever it is time to quit the body. They keep walking, walking, walking. So they are conscious of the ultimate goal is to prepare oneself to quit the body while remembering the Lord and therefore when they learnt that Krishna has gone back to his abode and Narada had told you remain here on the earth till Krishna is here and after that you go back to Krishna because they are all belonging to they are all belonging to Krishna. They are associates of Krishna, personal associates. So they go back to Krishna. They came for the sake of participating in Krishna's pastimes as Krishna's assistants and they go back. This is the position of a pure devotee. So as pure devotees they never forget they teach us by their exemplary behavior what is the proper goal in spite of studying these other subjects. They are not carried away by those skills. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

Om Agyanati Mirandasya Gyananjanishalakaya Chaksharunminitam Yena Tasmait Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatyade Shatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam. Devi Saraswati Vyasa Tato Jayam Udi Raye. In this session we will be discussing from the 45th and the 46th verses of the 7th chapter of 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam.

---
### Dronacharya, Ashwatthama, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Cgn6KZhkQxY?t=64
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.45
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So in either case, Ashwathama was a representative of Dronacharya, and therefore killing Ashwathama would be like killing Dronacharya."
**Summary:** The speaker explains verse 45, focusing on Draupadi's argument against killing Ashwatthama, emphasizing that Ashwatthama represents Dronacharya and his continued existence through his son.

The 45th verse is Saesha Bhagavang Dronaha Praja Rupena Vartate Tasyatmano Urdham Patnyaste Nanvagad Veerasu Kripi. The meaning of this verse is that is Dronacharya is certainly still existing being represented by his son. His wife Kripi did not undergo a sati with him because she had a son. Gravapati is explaining the reason why Ashwatthama should not be killed. She is coming up with so many different arguments that here she is saying that she is not a good person. She is telling Dronacharya he died in the battle, but he is still existing because he is represented by his son, Praja Rupena Vartate, and Dronacharya is referred out of respect by Draupadi as Bhagavan Dronaha Saesha Bhagavata. So it is explained by Srila Prabhupada in the purport, the wife of Dronacharya, Krippi, is a sister of Krippacharya, a devoted wife, who is according to the revealed scripture, the better half of her husband, is justified in embracing voluntary death along with her husband, if she is without issue. But in the case of the wife of Dronacharya, she did not undergo such a trial, because she had her son, the representative of her husband. So Srila Prabhupada explains, a widow is a widow only in name, if there is a son of her husband existing. So in either case, Ashwathama was a representative of Dronacharya, and therefore killing Ashwathama would be like killing Dronacharya. That was the argument.

---
### respect, family, grief
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Cgn6KZhkQxY?t=201
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.46
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A slight insult for a respectable family is sufficient to invoke grief. Therefore, a cultured man should always be careful in dealing with worshipful family members."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces verse 46, highlighting Srila Prabhupada's explanation that even a slight insult to a respectable family can cause grief, emphasizing the importance of careful conduct towards worshipful family members.

The argument of Draupadi against the killing of Ashwathama. Draupadi was against killing Ashwathama. The 46th verse. The 46th verse. The 46th verse. The meaning of this verse is, O most fortunate one, who knows the principles of religion, it is not good for you to cause grief to glorious family members, who are always respectable and worshipful. So Srila Prabhupada says, in the purport to this 46th verse, a slight insult for a respectable family is sufficient to invoke grief. Therefore, a cultured man should always be careful in dealing with worshipful family members. So Srila Prabhupada has explained elaborately these two verses in a discourse he had given earlier.

---
### Guru, Bhagavan, Dronacharya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Cgn6KZhkQxY?t=282
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "She herself is referring to Dronacharya as Bhagawan Drona."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Dronacharya's position in the Pandavas' lives as their Guru, highlighting Draupadi's respect for him and her reference to him as Bhagavan Drona, and then discusses the meaning of Bhagavan.

Now, the point explained. The point explained by Prabhupada, especially with reference to these two verses, is that what is the position of Dronacharya in the life of the Pandavas. Dronacharya is accepted as Guru by the Pandavas. They were trained up by Dronacharya. They were trained up by Dronacharya. And Dronacharya, especially as required by the Pandavas. As regards Arjuna, Dronacharya taught all the secrets. And already Draupadi has pointed out, it is only by the mercy of Dronacharya that you learnt all the secrets about Dhanur Vidya or Dhanur Veda. So Draupadi is not an ordinary woman. You should always remember this. She knows everything about religious principles. And therefore she is teaching the assembly of respectable learned persons how the spiritual master should be respected. She herself is referring to Dronacharya as Bhagawan Drona. Anyone who is extraordinarily powerful is addressed as Bhagawan. Sometimes Narada is addressed as Bhagawan. Sometimes Lord Shiva is addressed as Bhagawan. They are all extraordinarily powerful personalities. The original Bhagawan is Krishna.

---
### Bhagavan, Krishna, Opulence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Cgn6KZhkQxY?t=395
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One who possesses the sum total of all the wealth is held only by one person, Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the definition of Bhagavan according to Parashara Muni, emphasizing the possession of all opulences, particularly by Krishna, and differentiating between Krishna as the supreme Bhagavan and others who possess Bhagavan qualities to a lesser extent.

The definition given by Parashara Muni in the Shastra is who is Bhagawan. One who possesses the sum total of all the wealth and the other opulences. One who possesses the sum total of all the wealth and the other opulences. Six opulences. Sum total of all the six opulences possessed by Krishna, Bhagawan Krishna. He is the supreme Bhagawan. But there are others who are not exactly in the position of Krishna as supreme. But they are also having certain extent the same qualities as Krishna. The qualities of Bhagawan they have. So in that sense they also can be addressed as Bhagawan because they are extraordinarily powerful compared to other persons. But they are not having Samagra Aishwarya. They are not having the sum total of all the wealth. So that position possessing the sum total of all the wealth is held only by one person. Krishna. Nobody else can equal him. Nobody else can excel him. So she is telling he is extraordinarily powerful personality. Dronacharya. Saesha Bhagawan Drona. Praja roopena vartate. He is still existing. Praja. In the form of Praja. Praja means offspring son or daughter. So one who is born of the father. Is called Praja. Now without father there is no possibility of any birth taking place. Either son or daughter. So one who gives birth. Prajayate. Praja is the product of the body of the father. That's what Prabhupada explains.

---
### Father, Son, Vedic Culture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Cgn6KZhkQxY?t=544
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The father's body is actually present in the form of the son or daughter."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a seed and a tree to explain the relationship between father and son, emphasizing that the father's essence is present in the son. He then discusses the Vedic concept of a woman not being considered a widow if she has a son, even if her husband is deceased.

The father's body is actually present in the form of the son or daughter. Praja. The Shastra says. Yatha yoni yatha bijam. Father is the bijam. The seed is given by the father. And just like you sow a seed in the ground. Then a huge tree comes up. So the seed itself is generated by the tree. But that seed again grows up to be a tree. So the seed is tree later on. Similarly the seed is put by the father in the womb of the mother. Implanted. And when you sow a seed in the ground, you take care to for it to properly grow. Similarly the seed is sown in the womb of the mother. And the mother takes care of the seed and becomes a big body and comes out. So this is the process of birth. In one sense the seed and the tree there is no difference. Similarly there is no difference between the son and the father. No difference. The seed of the father and the father himself. There is no difference. So Dronacharya is still present in the form of his son, Ashwathama. So according to Vedic culture this is very important to understand. A woman who has got a son, even if her husband is dead, she is not considered a widow. Widow which actually means a woman who doesn't have a husband, who has lost her husband. Husband is dead. But if the husband's representative is there, the son, then the woman is not a widow.

---
### Ardhangini, Draupadi, Dronacharya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Cgn6KZhkQxY?t=686
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dronacharya is still existing. So killing the son of Dronacharya means killing Dronacharya."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the concept of the wife being the 'better half' (Ardhangini) and reiterates Draupadi's argument that Dronacharya is still existing through his son, making the killing of Ashwatthama equivalent to killing Dronacharya.

So in English also it is said the wife is considered half the body. In Sanskrit also. In English, better half. So where is this concept coming from. So Prabhupada explains this concept is coming from the absolute truth. The ultimate source of everything is the absolute truth, Krishna. So we have finished that. Now, we have to 1962. We have done this. Now, we have to specifically explain to understand we completely understand it in abstract language. Why as we have heard it coming up grilled. Hannah and zyangorji. Yes this tradition is also like it was before. These ideas. So Radha and Krishna have combined in the form of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. So in the same way, the wife is considered the better half of the husband, Ardhangini. And the son is there. In so many ways, Draupadi is trying to convince, don't think Dronacharya is dead and gone. Dronacharya is still existing. So killing the son of Dronacharya means killing Dronacharya. So do not do this. This is Draupadi's argument.

### Dharma, Vaishnavi, Compassion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Cgn6KZhkQxY?t=805
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Being a Vaishnavi, being a devotee, a pure devotee, not just a devotee, a pure devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of acting in a way that glorifies the family and adhering to religious principles. He highlights Draupadi's compassion as a Vaishnavi, who is concerned about the grief that killing Ashwatthama would cause Kripi.

And one should act in such a way that the action will always be glorified in the family. Now Pandava's family is a very respectable family. So Draupadi is telling also, if you know the principles of religion, you will not perform any action which will bring down the glory of the family. You should not do. You should not do such an action. So therefore, you should take care in dealing with the family members of Dronacharya. Especially Krippi. So there will be further reasons given by Draupadi why Ashwathama should not be killed in the next two verses. Now Draupadi is a Vaishnavi. That will be explained in the next verse. She is a great devotee. Earlier also Prabhupada had mentioned in the Purport that she is a pure devotee. Therefore, even though she is aggrieved because of the loss of her sons, she is thinking not just of her own problem. She is thinking how Krippi will be put into so much of grief. She has already lost her husband. Further if you kill Ashwathama, she is going to be put into so much more grief. So Draupadi has all such considerations. Being a Vaishnavi, being a devotee, a pure devotee, not just a devotee, a pure devotee. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### compassion, Draupadi, Vaishnava qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aGEnnkH1794?t=55
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.47
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "See how very considerate is Draupadi."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami begins discussing Srimad Bhagavatam 1.7.47, focusing on Draupadi's compassionate nature and her plea to Arjuna not to make Dronacharya's wife suffer as she has.

In this session we will be discussing Narayanam Namaskritaya Naram Chai Vanarottamam from the 47th and the 48th verses of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 47th verse: Ma Rodid Asya Janani Gautami Patidevata Yathaham Brta Vatsarta Rodimi Ashrumukhi Muhuhu. The meaning of this verse is, 'My Lord,' this is Draupadi speaking to Arjuna, 'Do not make the wife of Dronacharya cry like me. I am agreed for the death of my sons. She need not cry constantly like me.' See how very considerate is Draupadi. So in the purport Srila Prabhupada said that, 'Sympathetic good lady as she was, Srimati Draupadi did not want to put the wife of Dronacharya in the same position of childlessness,' both from the point of motherly feelings and from the respectable position held by the wife of Dronacharya. From two perspectives she is saying that why should she suffer like me? And also she is a respectable lady as the wife of the Guru. So why should she be made to suffer?

---
### Parashurama, anger, Vaishnava qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aGEnnkH1794?t=328
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.48
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They don't deserve to be treated the way you are thinking of punishing Ashwathama."
**Summary:** The speaker transitions to discussing the rage of Brahmanas, using the example of Parashurama and then connects it to Draupadi's plea to Arjuna not to enrage the Brahmanas by punishing Ashwatthama. He emphasizes Draupadi's Vaishnava qualities, particularly her compassion for others' suffering.

The Kshatriyas had become very puffed up, very proud, and they were not doing their duties properly as Kshatriyas. So he decided to kill all these miscreant Kshatriyas. So he simply took one chopper, Parashu. He is called Parashurama. His name is Rama, but he is called Parashurama because he took one axe. Parashu means axe and simply went around the world 21 times, Trisaptaha, and simply went on chopping the heads of all these miscreant Kshatriyas. 21 times he went around the world to get rid of all these miscreants. So the rage of the Brahmanas is very dangerous for the lower persons, especially the Kshatriyas. So that is exactly what Draupadi is pointing out here. Don't enrage them. She is telling Arjuna, 'Don't enrage them. They don't deserve to be treated the way you are thinking of punishing Ashwathama.' No. And Srila Prabhupada explains further, in addition to the purport, he has given a very nice discourse on these two verses. He says in this discourse that Draupadi is sympathetic to Krippi. Why? Because Draupadi is a Vaishnavi. This is the attitude of all Vaishnavas, all pure devotees. What is that? Para Dukha Dukhi. Vaishnava is having some special qualifications. One of them is Para Dukha Dukhi. On their own account, they are never unhappy, but they are Dukhi, they are unhappy only if others are unhappy, others are put into distress, then they become distressed. They can't tolerate seeing others suffer. This is the quality of a Vaishnava.

---
### compassion, suffering, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aGEnnkH1794?t=493
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He could not tolerate to see the suffering of the conditioned souls."

Just like Prabhupada, he took so much trouble travelling all over the world and preaching Krishna Consciousness. Why? Because he could not tolerate to see the suffering of the conditioned souls. And how can their suffering be relieved? Only by Krishna Consciousness. That's why he was so painstakingly untiring efforts he put for preaching Krishna Consciousness to all sections of people all over the world. So here, now Prabhupada is explaining, look at the character, to give an example, Prahlad Maharaj. Prahlad Maharaj says, Naivodhvijeparaduratyavaitaranyas Tvadvirya Gayana Mahamruta Magna Chittaha Shochetato Vimukachetasa Indriyartha Mayasukhayabharam Udvahato Vimudhan. Prahlad Maharaj.

### devotion, tolerance, divine protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aGEnnkH1794?t=561
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The devotee is tolerant, but Supreme Lord Krishna, He never tolerates even a little insult done to his devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Prahlad Maharaj and Hiranyakashipu, highlighting Hiranyakashipu's anger towards Prahlad's devotion and the subsequent torture. He emphasizes that while devotees are tolerant, the Supreme Lord Krishna cannot tolerate any insult to his devotees, leading to the appearance of Narsimha and the killing of Hiranyakashipu.

Before explaining what is quoted about Prahlad, that he was harassed by his father, who was a demon. Hiranyakashipu was a demon. Prahlad was a devotee. Now, just because Prahlad was just chanting the name of the Supreme Lord, as a devotee, it's natural, but Hiranyakashipu became very very angry at Prahlad, his own son, small boy, five year old boy, just because he is chanting the name of the Supreme Lord. That enraged Hiranyakashipu to what extent he became angry is that he actually tortured this boy, Prahlad, tortured the boy. And because Hiranyakashipu was torturing Prahlad, therefore the Supreme Lord, He cannot tolerate. The devotee is tolerant. Devotee is tolerant, but Supreme Lord Krishna, He never tolerates even a little insult done to his devotee. So the Supreme Lord appeared in the form of Narsimha and He, Narsimha killed this Hiranyakashipu. Now after that Narsimha Deva, He blessed Prahlad, very pleased with his devotion and He said, 'You have undergone so much trouble for my sake. Now you ask me any benediction that you want.'

### humility, devotion, self-satisfaction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aGEnnkH1794?t=681
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I have learned how to be fully satisfied and happy simply by singing the glories of Your Lordship."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Prahlad Maharaj, focusing on Prahlad's humility and devotion. Prahlad declines any material benedictions from Lord Narsimha, stating that he has learned to be fully satisfied and happy simply by singing the glories of the Lord.

Now Prahlad is telling in his prayers to Lord Narsimha, 'My dear Lord, I am born in a family which is filled with these qualities of Rajoguna and Tamoguna. Asuras, they are influenced by these two lower qualities, Rajoguna and Tamoguna, Rajas, Tamo Bhava. Asuras, they are influenced by these two lower qualities, Rajoguna, Tamoguna. And Devatas, they are influenced by Sattva Guna.' So as compared to those influenced by Sattva Guna, these Rajoguna, Tamoguna people, they are very much carried away by wealth, power, by position, etc. So Prahlad is telling Lord Narsimha Deva, 'Since I have taken birth in this Asura family, please don't allure me by offering me some benediction.' And Prahlad is also telling, 'Actually, I have learned how to be fully satisfied and happy simply by singing the glories of Your Lordship, Tvad Veerya Gayana Mahamrita Magna Chitta.' The glories of the Supreme Lord when chanted and that nectarian glories of the Supreme Lord as enumerated by pure devotees is capable of fully immersing a devotee's mind. A devotee when he hears or he hears that nectar, he is able to immerse himself completely and that way he is always situated in a happy condition. So on his personal account, there is no one who is not happy because of that. Now she is thinking what about Kripy.

**Key Quote:** "She need not cry constantly like me. So don't punish Ashwatthama."
**Summary:** The speaker returns to the example of Draupadi, emphasizing her compassion and forgiveness. Despite the immense grief caused by the death of her sons, Draupadi does not seek revenge or punishment for Ashwatthama, demonstrating the Vaishnava quality of being Para Dukha Dukhi.

Ashwatthama is the one who is actually beheaded the five sleeping sons of Draupadi, but she didn't kill this Ashwatthama in retaliation or in terms of punishment. Why? Because like I have been put into so much grief by loss of my sons, in the death of my sons, she need not cry constantly like me. So don't punish Ashwatthama the way you have bound him up and you are having some plan to succeed. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### compassion, Dharma, forgiveness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IlCwipSzzgg?t=115
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Draupadi particularly reasoned and told Arjuna not to kill Ashwatthama as a punishment for whatever Ashwatthama had done."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the story of Ashwatthama, Draupadi's compassion, and her reasoning for sparing his life, emphasizing her consideration for Dronacharya's position and the suffering of Ashwatthama's mother.

So, Sut Goswami is describing the incident, the first incident from where Bhagavatam begins, the narration of the Bhagavatam begins by Sut Goswami. The son of Dronacharya, Ashwatthama, he beheaded the five sleeping sons of Draupadi and Draupadi was very much aggrieved and Arjuna decided to punish this Ashwatthama. So, Arjuna went chasing and Ashwatthama released a brahmastra just to save his life but under Krishna's guidance Arjuna was able to neutralize that brahmastra and he anyway, Arjuna was able to capture this Ashwatthama. He bound him like an animal and brought him to his own camp and presented him to Draupadi. Draupadi was very conscious, this Ashwatthama, even though he is an aggressor, atatai, still, he is the son of Dronacharya. And who is Dronacharya? Dronacharya is the teacher who taught Arjuna and all the Pandavas for that matter. Particularly Arjuna was taught all the secrets of Dhanur Vidya, Dhanur Veda. So Draupadi always remembered that the Pandava family has to be always grateful to Dronacharya. So considering Dronacharya's position and also Dronacharya was killed in the battle and Dronacharya's wife, Ashwatthama's mother had become a widow. Widow means she had lost her husband. She is not a widow because her son is still surviving. So she had become, she had lost her husband. So Draupadi did not want Krippi to suffer the same way she was suffering the loss of her sons. So therefore it is described here, Draupadi particularly reasoned and told Arjuna not to kill Ashwatthama as a punishment for whatever Ashwatthama had done.

### Dharma, human life, material desires
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IlCwipSzzgg?t=287
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The difference between human life and animal life as described in the scriptures is that human life is not just meant for satisfying the demands of the body."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that King Yudhishthira supported Draupadi's statements and then elaborates on the meaning of 'Dharmya,' emphasizing the importance of understanding and practicing religious principles, contrasting human life with animal life, which is primarily focused on satisfying bodily demands.

So Asutu Goswami is telling King Yudhishthira fully supported the statements of the queen. King Yudhishthira fully supported the statements of the queen Draupadi. Now what is described about the statements of Queen Draupadi very nicely it is told here. Dharmya means they are in accordance with the principles of religion. This is very important, that should become conversant with the principles of religion and conduct themselves accordingly. They should be conversant with the principles of religion. The difference between human life and animal life as described in the scriptures is that human life is not just meant for satisfying the demands of the body. These four demands of the body are there whether animals or humans. This is the material platform, bodily platform that when we have got this body there is the need of aahara. We have to take food so many times a day to maintain the body. Nidra, rest, sleep is required to rejuvenate this body and bhaya, one has to defend oneself against so many dangers, so many problems, so many threats. One has to defend oneself and maithuna, one has to procreate. There is sex life and the sex life is meant for procreation, for creating progeny. So these four demands are there of the body, whether animals are humans. Samanya metad pashubhi narana, but there is one extra special function of the human being that's called Dharma. The principles of religion. The principles of religion. That is called Dharma. The principles of religion.

### Dharma, service, intrinsic nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IlCwipSzzgg?t=443
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Dharma is the eternal, inseparable, intrinsic nature of something."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that Dharma is not a sectarian system of faith but the intrinsic nature of something, using analogies of fire, river, and sugar to explain the concept, and then defines the Dharma of a human being as rendering service.

Now religion should not be understood as some sectarian system of faith. Many times Srila Prabhupada explains this because that is a misconception in the society in general among the people. That when you say Dharma, immediately they take it like some kind of faith, some system. No, it is not a system of faith. Dharma is the eternal, inseparable, intrinsic nature of something. Like the Dharma of fire is heat and light. Wherever there is fire, there has to be heat and light. You cannot have fire where there is no heat. You cannot have fire where there is no light. Not possible. It is intrinsically the quality of fire. So Dharma of fire is heat and light. Dharma of a river is to flow. River means it keeps flowing, flowing, flowing till it reaches the ocean. So then only it is called a river, Nadi. And similarly, Dharma of sugar is the sweet taste of sugar. So you cannot have sugar which is not sweet in taste. Then it is not sugar. Sugar means it should be sweet in taste and it gives that sweet taste to whatever you mix sugar with. It gives the sweet taste, right? So that, like that, what is the Dharma of a human being? Human being has got Dharma. What is the Dharma of the human being? So that is explained in the scriptures. Dharma of the human being is to render service, to render service. So one should render service to Krishna.

Somebody may think, I am master. I am not the servant of anyone. But then he may think like that, but actually he is servant of lust. He is servant of his senses. He is servant of greed, anger, so many things. That means when there is anger, he has to satisfy the demand of anger. Of anger demands of anger when there is when there is lust lusty desire he cannot resist fulfillment of their lusty desire so one is forced to be the servant of illusion if one does not engage in service of Krishna this is the whole this is the perfect and proper understanding of Dharma so an intelligent person will think if mine intrinsic quality or nature is to serve why should I serve the illusory energy and in in exchange for the service of the illusion what do we get only misery you can see that the person who is not engaged in service of the illusory energy and in exchange for the service of the illusion what do we get only misery illusory energy only keeps giving us misery after misery after misery sometimes there is some respite or break from misery and we think that is some kind of happiness so relief from misery is considered or thought of as happiness in this material world for all those who are busy serving illusion of course people do not know that they are busy serving illusion know this they are in ignorance that's another quality nature of being in the material world everybody is ignorant of this fact that I am servant if I don't serve Krishna then I am forced to serve illusion and the consequence of serving illusion is misery but if somebody serves Krishna then there is only transcendental bliss.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.7.5 | 07-12-2021
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### material misery, spiritual identity, external energy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yG4t5Q1rvFs?t=86
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are not this body, but we are spirit soul."
**Summary:** This segment explains that the root cause of suffering is identifying with the material body due to the influence of the external energy, and that the verse being discussed contains the root cause of suffering, the remedy, and the ultimate perfection.

A person of material nature thinks of himself as a material product and thus undergoes the reactions of material miseries. This is the explanation of what is explained in the Bhagavad Gita, that we are not this body, but we are spirit soul. For the spirit soul, there is no death, there is no birth, there is no disease, there's no old age, there's no suffering, there are no miseries. Why are we experiencing whatever we are going through? We go through suffering that is due to the external energy, the inferior energy of the Lord, the material energy. So that material energy, although the living entity is transcendental to the three modes of material nature, the influence of the external energy makes the living being think that he is a product of material nature, he is a material product, and in that way, he undergoes the reactions of material miseries. Now, in the purport, Srila Prabhupada elaborately explained this point because the central point for our understanding is that we are not the body, we are spirit soul. This is very, very important to understand. And then there is also, as Prabhupada explains, the root cause of suffering by the materialistic living beings is pointed out. What is the root cause of suffering? This influence of the external energy because of which I think I am a material product, and the remedial measures which are to be undertaken, what is the remedy for this, and also the ultimate perfection to be gained. All three are mentioned in this particular verse. Let us see how.

---
### transcendental nature, imprisonment, unholy contact
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yG4t5Q1rvFs?t=233
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Due to this unholy contact, what is unholy contact? The contact of matter and spirit, the pure spiritual entity suffers material miseries under the modes of material nature."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the transcendental nature of the living being, how it is imprisoned by external energy, and how the contact between matter and spirit causes suffering.

These three things are actually mentioned in the verse and explained wonderfully. The Living Being by Constitution is transcendental to material engagement. Engagement means we are trapped inside this body. Each one of us, spirit soul, is trapped in this body. The body is like a cage, it is like a bird is locked up in a cage. Same way, we are locked up in this body, but he is now imprisoned by the external energy and therefore he thinks himself one of the material products. Even though we are transcendental, we are beyond this material body, our existence is beyond this material body, still the imprisonment is by the influence of the external energy, and that influence makes us think that I am a material product. And due to this unholy contact, what is unholy contact? The contact of matter and spirit, the pure spiritual entity suffers material miseries under the modes of material nature. I am pure spirit. Each one of the living beings is pure spirit, but the contact with this external energy creates a situation where I think, due to the influence of external energy, I think I am a product of matter and this body, and that is the cause for suffering material miseries.

---
### misunderstanding, misidentification, perverted thinking
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yG4t5Q1rvFs?t=351
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The living entity misunderstands himself to be a material product; it is a case of misunderstanding."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that identifying as a material product is a misunderstanding and misidentification, leading to perverted ways of thinking, feeling, and willing under material conditions, which is not the natural state of the spirit soul.

The living entity misunderstands himself to be a material product. It is a case of misunderstanding. It is totally a phenomenon because of misconception, misunderstanding, misidentification. These are the terms used everywhere in the scriptures. This means that the perverted person is a material product of matter, and this is a case of misunderstanding. The perverted way of thinking, feeling, and willing under material conditions is not natural for him. We think materially, we feel only in terms of matter, thinking, feeling, willing, we act materially. All this is only simply under material conditions. It is the way we think, feel, and live, and this is not natural. This is not our original situation. This is not our situation as Spirit Soul, but Sheila proper says he has his normal way of thinking, feeling, and willing. The present perverted way of thinking, feeling, and willing is going on, but...

---
### illusory energy, supreme lord, reformation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yG4t5Q1rvFs?t=511
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord does not interfere with the task of the illusory energy because this influence of the illusory energy is necessary for reformation of the conditioned soul."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the illusory energy's role in influencing the living being, the Supreme Lord's aloofness from it, and how this influence is necessary for the reformation of the conditioned soul who desires independence from the Lord.

Absolute impersonal Brahman, that is completely wrong because the absolute truth is never under any influence of the energy, never. Whereas the living being who is not the absolute truth can come under the influence of the illusion. This cannot be because the living entity has his own way of thinking in his original unconditional state also. The original state is an unconditional state. It is not a state in which there is an influence of this external energy. The present energy, sorry, the present conditional state actually is due to the influence of the external energy, which means that the illusory energy takes the initiative, while the Supreme Lord is aloof. Whose energy is it, this external energy or material energy? It is the Supreme Lord's energy, Krishna's energy, and the Supreme Lord, even though he is the supreme controller, he is always aloof from the external energy. And as a tiny spirit soul, every living being in this material world is conditioned. Conditioned means influenced to act in a material way. That is the conditioning. The Lord does not desire that a living being be illusioned by external energy. He is always aloof from the external energy, and as a tiny spirit soul, every living being, external energy, it's not the Lord's desire that we should be illusioned, but the external energy, aware of this fact, still accepts a thankless task of keeping the forgetful soul under illusion by her bewildering influence. That is why, why should the external energy do this task of keeping the forgetful soul in illusion? That is explained. The Lord does not interfere with the task of the illusory energy because this influence of the illusory energy is necessary for reformation of the conditioned soul. What does the need for reformation? The conditioned soul desires independent of the external energy of the soul. So this is why the Lord is not interfering.

---
### illusory energy, surrender, relief
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yG4t5Q1rvFs?t=674
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Although the ways of illusory energy are very stiff to overcome, very difficult to overcome, still one who surrenders under the lotus feet of the Lord is set free by the order of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of a father disciplining his children to explain why the Lord allows the illusory energy to act. It also explains that the Lord descends to offer relief through the Bhagavad Gita, emphasizing that surrendering to Krishna is the way to overcome the illusory energy.

Energy, and then they may think they are independently enjoying, while the fact is they are completely under the control of the illusory energy. So to reform the conditioned soul, this is necessary. Prabhupada gives an example: an affectionate father does not like his children to be chastised by another agent, but he puts his disobedient children under the custody of a severe man just to bring them to order. So similarly, and all of it, the all-affectionate Almighty Father Krishna, he desires relief for the conditioned soul, and what kind of relief? Relief from the clutches of the illusory energy. The Supreme Lord for this purpose, he descends from his kingdom upon the kingdom of the illusory energy and personally gives relief in the form of Bhagavad Gita. Krishna speaks the Bhagavad Gita for helping us to get relief from the influence of the illusory energy. So what does it say in the Bhagavad Gita? He suggests that although the ways of illusory energy are very stiff to overcome, very difficult to overcome, still one who surrenders under the lotus feet of the Lord is set free by the order of the Supreme Lord. You can get out of this form of change, out of this form of change by overcoming illusory energy. Still, we can surrender, and then the lotus feet of the Lord is set free by the order of the Supreme Lord. We can get out of it, this illusion simply by surrendering to the lotus feet of Krishna. That's all.

---
### surrender, remedial measure, loving relationship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/yG4t5Q1rvFs?t=827
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The highest perfection to be achieved is not merely that I become free from the influence of this energy, no, there is an original position in a loving devotional relationship with Krishna, the Supreme Lord, in his personal abode as his loving devotee. That is the perfection to be attained."
**Summary:** This segment summarizes the lecture by reiterating the cause of suffering, the remedial measure of surrendering to Krishna, and the ultimate perfection of attaining a loving devotional relationship with Krishna in his personal abode.

This surrendering process is the remedial measure for getting relief from the bewildering ways of the illusory energy. So first thing is why the suffering? Because under the influence of the illusory energy, we are thinking in terms of I am a material product, I identify with this body, and the body undergoes so many changes, and then I think I am suffering, while actually I am aloof from the body. Then what is the remedial measure for this? Surrender to the lotus feet of Krishna. And then finally, the highest perfection to be achieved is not merely that I become free from the influence of this energy, no, there is an original position in a loving devotional relationship with Krishna, the Supreme Lord, in his personal abode as his loving devotee. That is the perfection to be attained. So it is not just relief, it is relief and reinstating in our original constitution position. So that is also explained here. I'll stop here. Thank you very much.

In this session we will be discussing from the 50th, 51st and 52nd verses of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 50th verse: Nakula Sahadevascha Yuyudhano Dhananjayaha Bhagavan Devaki Putro Ye Chanye Yascha Yoshitaha. The meaning of this 50th verse: Nakula and Sahadeva, the younger brothers of the King, and also Satyaki, Arjuna, the personality of Godhead, Lord Shri Krishna, who is the son of Devaki, and the ladies and others all unanimously agreed with the King here. The King refers to Yudhishthira after this verse is spoken after Draupadi. Draupadi.

Draupadi. Draupadi. Thirapanti. Draupadi. Tell Arjuna not to kill Ashwatthama. And it is described that Yudhishthira fully supported Draupadi's decision not to kill Ashwatthama. And now Yudhishthira was further supported, that means Draupadi is being also supported not only by Yudhishthira but Nakula, Sahadeva and Satyaki. Satyaki is the chariot driver of Krishna. He belongs to the Yadu dynasty. And Arjuna agreed with Draupadi. Yes, what Draupadi is telling is correct. Even though Arjuna wanted to kill Ashwatthama. And even Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, the son of Devaki, he also agreed with Yudhishthira who was supporting Draupadi's decision. And the ladies and all others unanimously agreed with the king. Now kindly note, here practically everyone is mentioned except one person that is Bhima.
