### anger, violence, justice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/pzsERirf8TM?t=210
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.51
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhima however disagreed with them and recommended killing this culprit who in an angry mood had murdered sleeping children for no purpose and for neither his nor his master's interest."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces verse 51, focusing on Bhima's disagreement with the others regarding Ashwatthama. Bhima advocated for killing Ashwatthama due to his heinous act of murdering sleeping children in a fit of anger, an act that served no purpose or benefit to anyone, including Duryodhana.

In the 51st verse we will see why Bhima's name is not mentioned. The 51st verse. Now about Bhima, what is being said here. The translation of this 51st verse: Bhima however disagreed with them and recommended killing this culprit who in an angry mood had murdered sleeping children for no purpose and for neither his nor his master's interest. See Bhima's reasoning. Bhima disagreed with everyone else. For what reason? Because Ashwatthama is a culprit. He has committed a crime. And what kind of crime? In an angry mood he had murdered sleeping children. The five sons of Draupadi. And he had done it for no purpose. What was his gain by doing it? His specific gain, nothing. And for neither his nor his master's interest. It is not even in his interest that he had killed the five sleeping sons of Draupadi. Neither Duryodhana was pleased with this act of Ashwatthama. Because as soon as Ashwatthama killed them, he took the five heads which had been chopped off and presented them to Duryodhana thinking that Duryodhana will be pleased. But Duryodhana was not at all happy about it. He thoroughly disapproved of this and said this is totally abominable. So Duryodhana did not approve it at all. Ashwatthama was fighting on the side of Duryodhana. So he thought, Duryodhana will be pleased. But Duryodhana was not at all pleased by it. So for whose interest had he done it? That's what Bhima is questioning. For whom? For whose sake? It was not his interest. It was not his master's interest. And what other purpose can simply be somebody killed like that? By whom? By Ashwatthama who is fighting in a Dharma Yudha on religious principles. It is totally against all rules. All religious principles.

---
### anger, discrimination, Krishna's role
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/pzsERirf8TM?t=379
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.51, SB 1.7.52
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When somebody becomes angry, then they lose their sense of discrimination. Anger actually makes a person lose his intelligence."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining Bhima's perspective, emphasizing that Ashwatthama's actions were driven by anger, leading to a loss of discrimination and intelligence. He then introduces verse 52, describing Krishna's reaction to the conflicting opinions of Bhima and Draupadi, as Krishna looks at Arjuna.

And even if let us say somebody violates a religious principle, for whose benefit is it being done? Nobody's benefit. Nobody's benefit. And he had done it in an angry mood. When somebody becomes angry, then they lose their sense of discrimination. Anger actually makes a person lose his intelligence. Anger makes a person lose his intelligence. So Bhima is pointing out that this Ashwatthama, he deserves to be killed because he is a culprit. Don't spare his life. And then the 52nd verse, it describes when Bhima said this, then the reaction of Krishna. The 52nd verse, nishamyabhimagaditam draupadyascha chaturbhujah alokyavadanam sakhyuh idamahasannivah. The meaning of this verse is chaturbhujanam the four armed personality or the personality of Godhead chaturbhujaha. After hearing the words of Bhima, Draupadi and others, Bhima and Draupadi's words are contradictory. Chaturbhujah, the personality of Godhead, saw the face of his dear friend Arjuna and he began to speak as if smiling.

---
### dilemma, friendship, contradiction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/pzsERirf8TM?t=495
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.52
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is looking at Arjuna, his friend, his dear friend and Krishna is now going to speak something very very significant to Arjuna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Arjuna's dilemma, caught between Draupadi's plea for mercy and Bhima's demand for justice. Krishna, understanding Arjuna's predicament, looks at his friend and prepares to speak. The speaker then introduces Srila Prabhupada's purport and quotes Shridhara Swami, highlighting the opposite views of Bhima and Draupadi.

Now, what is Arjuna's position? That's what Krishna is considering. Arjuna has captured this Ashwatthama, brought him to his own camp and he had bound him up like an animal because he was a culprit. He had arrested him. But now Draupadi is telling Arjuna don't kill him. And she gave a reasoning. And Yudhishthira fully supported Draupadi and everyone except Bhima did support Draupadi. But Bhima disagreed. So what is Arjuna supposed to do? Bhima is not agreeing to let go this Ashwatthama to be excused. And Draupadi is telling don't kill him. What is Arjuna supposed to do? So, Krishna is looking at Arjuna, his friend, his dear friend and Krishna is now going to speak something very very significant to Arjuna. And Krishna is also smiling because he can understand the dilemma Arjuna is having that on the one hand Draupadi is telling don't kill and Bhima is telling he should be killed. And Arjuna had taken a vow to kill. All that will be mentioned by Krishna. So in the purport Srila Prabhupada says Lord Sri Krishna has two arms. Krishna is Vibhujan. And why is he designated as four armed? Chaturbhujaha. Krishna is being referred to as Chaturbhujaha in this verse specifically. Why? That is explained by one of our Acharyas. Shridhara Swami. So, Shridhara Prabhupada is quoting Shridhara Swami. Shridhara Swami is telling both Bhima and Draupadi held opposite views about killing Ashwatthama. Very clear. Bhima wanted Ashwatthama should be killed. And Draupadi said don't kill him. So opposite views of Bhima and Draupadi. Bhima wanted him to be immediately killed. Whereas Draupadi wanted to save him so.

---
### Krishna's forms, spiritual planets, Vaikuntha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/pzsERirf8TM?t=630
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.52, BG 11.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In order to prevent both of them the Lord discovered another two arms. Originally the primeval Lord Sri Krishna displays only two arms. But in his Narayana feature he exhibits four hands."
**Summary:** The speaker continues quoting Srila Prabhupada and Shridhara Swami, explaining that Krishna can manifest different forms, including the four-armed Narayana form. He discusses the spiritual planets, Vaikuntha, where Narayana resides, and Krishnaloka (Goloka Vrindavana), where Krishna resides in his two-handed form, emphasizing that Krishna and Narayana are the same Supreme Personality.

Srila Prabhupada says quoting Shridhara Swami we can imagine Bhima ready to kill while Draupadi is obstructing him. And in order to prevent both of them the Lord discovered another two arms. Originally the primeval Lord Sri Krishna displays only two arms. Vibhujarupa. Manusham roopam. That's what is described in Bhagavad Gita. But in his Narayana feature he exhibits four hands. In his Narayana feature he resides with his devotees in the Vaikuntha planets. While in his original Sri Krishna feature he resides in the Krishnaloka planet and far far above the Vaikuntha planets in the spiritual sky. This is explained elsewhere that beyond this material universes is the spiritual sky. And in that spiritual sky there are many many spiritual planets. Now the spiritual planets are called Vaikuntha Loka free from all anxiety. It's the abode of the pure devotees. The pure devotees of the Supreme Lord. In all the Vaikuntha planets the Supreme Lord is present as the predominating deity in his form as Narayana. Four handed form Narayana. But above the Vaikuntha planets among all the spiritual planets one particular planet is called Krishnaloka. It's also called Goloka Vrindavana. In that planet alone the Supreme Lord is present as Krishna in two handed form and Krishna is the predominating deity of Krishnaloka. Of course Krishna and Narayana are one person. The Supreme personality you have got it. But still there is distinction also.

### Krishna's power, Arjuna's dilemma, sympathy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/pzsERirf8TM?t=776
**Verse References:** BG 11.5, SB 1.7.51, SB 1.7.52
**Content Type:** philosophy

**Key Quote:** "Krishna only displayed two more arms in this situation. Therefore the Supreme Lord is now sympathizing with Arjuna that Arjuna is in a dilemma."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the explanation of the verses, stating that Krishna can manifest any number of arms, as he did in his Vishwaroopa shown to Arjuna. He reiterates Arjuna's dilemma, caught between Draupadi's and Bhima's conflicting opinions, and emphasizes Krishna's sympathy for Arjuna's situation, which is why Krishna is smiling at Arjuna. The speaker announces that he will stop here and continue in the next verse.

So here if Sri Krishna is called Chaturbhuja there is no contradiction because Krishna only expands as Narayana. If need be Krishna can display hundreds of arms as he exhibited in his Vishwaroopa shown to Arjuna. He can display any number of arms. Therefore one who can display hundreds and thousands of arms can also manifest four arms whenever required. So this is the explanation given by Sridharaswami. Krishna only displayed two more arms in this situation. So therefore the Supreme Lord is now sympathizing with Arjuna that Arjuna is in a dilemma. He is not he is getting contradictory suggestions. Draupadi telling Arjuna don't kill him. And Bhima is telling he must be killed because he is a culprit. For no purpose he has killed the sleeping sons of murdered not just killed murdered the sleeping sons of Draupadi. Neither his interest nor his master's interest is being served by this killing. So he deserves to be killed. This is Bhima's opinion. And what is Arjuna supposed to do? So Krishna is looking at Arjuna and he is sympathizing therefore he is also smiling at Arjuna. Now Krishna is going to speak to Arjuna something. That we will see in the next verse. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### Scriptural Interpretation, Dharma, Renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DuSFo72mcIE?t=289
**Verse References:** BG 18
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whenever somebody tries to refer the scriptures, then in certain circumstances, to apply the scriptural injunction, it is difficult."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the difficulty of applying scriptural injunctions in specific situations, using the example of a sanyasi's duties and the seemingly contradictory instructions in the scriptures.

So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada says that Arjuna was perplexed because Ashwatthama was to be killed as well as spared according to different scriptures cited by different persons. This is a fact. Whenever somebody tries to refer the scriptures, then in certain circumstances, to apply the scriptural injunction, it is difficult. In the 18th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, it is explained that for somebody who is a sanyasi, who is in the renounced order of life, there are no duties to be performed. No duties to be performed because he is completely renounced. He is simply engaged in devotional service to Krishna, exclusively dedicated for that. That is the meaning of enunciation. At the same time, Shastra says that activities which are of the nature of yajna, dana and tapa should never be given up under any circumstances under any circumstances. For a civilized human being, in all stages, one should never stop performing sacrifice because sacrifice is specifically activity meant for pleasing the Supreme Lord. So, in that way, we can understand sacrifice should never be given up. Though, material activities, or material duties even, a sanyasi has got no material duties. A brahmachari has got duties, a grihasta has got duties, a vanapurasta has got duties, but a sanyasi has no material duties. So, it is difficult to understand whether a sanyasi should perform a sanyasi, should perform a sanyasi, should perform a sanyasi, should perform any activities, or not do any activities at all. So, like this, in the scriptures, there are injunctions which seemingly are contradictory in certain circumstances, in certain situations where one has to apply. Different people will give different circumstances, cite different shastras and give different rulings. Just like here, it's happening.

---
### Dharma, Conflict, Scriptural Interpretation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DuSFo72mcIE?t=473
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhima is telling you must kill, because Bhima is citing the shastra only. And Draupadi is telling, don't kill. And she has given her reasoning."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to discuss the complexities of applying scriptural injunctions, using the example of Bhima and Draupadi's differing opinions on Ashwatthama's fate, and highlighting the various perspectives and rulings from different scriptures.

Bhima is telling you must kill, because Bhima is citing the shastra only. And Draupadi is telling, don't kill. And she has given her reasoning. So here, Prabhupada is telling, as a brahma bandhu, or a worthless son of a brahmana, Ashwatthama was not to be killed. Brahma bandhur nahantavya. This is the ruling of the shastra. Brahma bandhu, because son of a brahmana. Even though he is not a brahmana, but he is son of a brahmana still, he should not be killed. But he was at the same time, this Ashwatthama was an aggressor. According to the rulings of Manu, an aggressor, even though he be a brahmana, and what to speak of a brahma bandhu, unworthy son of a brahmana, such an aggressor is to be killed. This is what Manusmriti says. Manusmriti. Dronacharya was certainly a brahmana in the true sense of the term. But because he stood in the battlefield, he was killed. He was fighting. A dharma yudha. In the battle he got killed. So when, because Dronacharya was on the side of the Kauravas, and obviously he was killed by the Pandavas. But such killing of Dronacharya was justified because Dronacharya was engaged in battle. But although Ashwatthama was an aggressor, now look at this complication. As an aggressor, it is said in the Shastra, aggressor, even if he is a brahmana, he is to be killed. So, even though Ashwatthama was an aggressor, he stood without any fighting weapons. He stood without any weapons. He was bound up. He didn't have any weapons with him. The ruling is that an aggressor, when he is without weapon or chariot, cannot be killed. Now look at this. Brahmabandhu should not be killed. Aggressor, even if he is a brahmana, he has to be killed. An aggressor, when he is standing without any weapon, he should not be killed. So what is Arjuna supposed to do? Besides all this, now these were all perplexities for Arjuna. And besides all this, to further complicate matters, Arjuna had to keep the promise he had made before Draupadi just to pacify her. As soon as Draupadi began crying after learning that her five sons had been all killed by Ashwatthama, she was crying, and Arjuna just to pacify her had said, I am not going to spare this Ashwatthama. I am going to cut off his head and bring that head for you. In order to pacify you. So he has to, he had made a promise. Kshatriya's promise cannot be an empty promise. And he also had to satisfy both Bhima and Krishna, who advised killing Ashwatthama. So Arjuna has promised Draupadi, I will kill him. Bhima and Krishna have advised Arjuna to kill Ashwatthama. The Shastra says, a Brahmabandhu should not be killed. Aggressor has to be killed. But an aggressor without any weapons should not be killed. So what is Arjuna supposed to do? Certainly very, very, very perplexed in situation. This dilemma was present before Arjuna and the solution was awarded by Krishna. Now how Krishna awards the solution is going to be explained in the next verse. But the lesson for us to be learnt from this is that in order to, even if one is conversant with the scriptural injunctions, it is not easy to apply the injunctions in different circumstances. It is not so simple. Always. In some cases it is very simple.

---
### Guru, Guidance, Krishna Consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DuSFo72mcIE?t=756
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In order to learn the scriptures and more importantly in order to learn how to apply the scriptural directions or injunctions in one's own personal life, one has to approach a bona fide spiritual master."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing the necessity of approaching a bona fide spiritual master for guidance in understanding and applying scriptural injunctions, drawing a parallel to Arjuna's surrender to Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita.

But in some situations or circumstances it is not so simple. So therefore Shastra says, in order to learn the scriptures and more importantly in order to learn how to apply the scriptural directions or injunctions in one's own personal life, one has to approach a bona fide spiritual master. One has to approach a bona fide spiritual master. The beginning of the Bhagavad Gita, Arjuna first says, I am not going to fight in this battle. How can I kill my own kinsmen? How can I kill Bhishma and Drona, my respectable superiors? For the sake of a kingdom I am going to kill them? Never. But at the same time, Arjuna understands this is a Dharma Yudha and it is a duty of a Kshatriya like Arjuna to fight in this Dharma Yudha. So considering these two aspects, Dharma Yudha, Arjuna should fight, it is his duty to fight and somebody like Bhishma and Drona who are respectable superiors should not be killed. So Arjuna is confused about his duty and Arjuna, what does he say? Now I am confused about my duty and therefore I surrender to you my dear Lord Krishna. Kindly instruct me what is to be done in these circumstances. And this is exactly what is taught in the Bhagavad Gita. We have to actually seek solution to our perplexities by approaching a bona fide spiritual master. A bona fide spiritual master is situated completely in Krishna consciousness and he is able to direct everyone perfectly in such a way that they are able to do their duty at the same time not commit any sin and also make advancement for ultimate liberation in Krishna consciousness. So this is the special position of a bona fide spiritual master. Therefore one has to actually approach a bona fide spiritual master and learn from him and seek his guidance in all such situations. Hare Krishna. Thank you very much.

---

### intelligence, Krishna consciousness, compromise

**Summary:** This segment discusses Arjuna's actions in dealing with Ashwatthama, highlighting how Arjuna used his intelligence, guided by Krishna consciousness, to find a compromise that satisfied conflicting demands.

The fifty-fifth verse, Suta Vacha. Now, Suta Goswami is describing this whole incident, narrating this historical incident to the sage Suna Misharanya, he is telling, Arjuna ha sahasa agnyaya here hardam athasina manim jahar murdhanyam Drijasya sah murdhajam Drijasya ah sah murdhanyam Drijasya such mood adjust sitting Arjuna hot To the entrance of Arizombur, it is'll give you Аthas participants that the adoration of thegers is where the meaning of this verse, The meaning of this verse is that, verses. Just then Arjuna could understand the motive of the Lord by his equivocal orders and thus with a sword he severed both hair and jewel from the head of Ashwatthama. Equivocal means ambiguous. Krishna said that you have got two contradictory things to be settled and you have to act appropriately. So Arjuna could understand what is Krishna's motive in giving him such an ambiguous order. You have to satisfy both Bhima and Draupadi. You should keep your promise. You should act according to the rulings of the scriptures. So what did Arjuna do? With his sword he cut off the hair and the jewel from the head of Ashwatthama. So in the purport Srila Prabhupada says contradictory orders of different persons are impossible to carry out. Therefore a compromise was selected by Arjuna by his sharp intelligence. This is where the intelligence is, one takes help of one's intelligence. Of course here we have to understand taking help of intelligence means intelligence directed by the super soul seated on the heart. Super soul is Krishna, Paramatma. And a devotee like Arjuna is able to take help of his intelligence because he is Krishna conscious. He is Krishna conscious therefore he is able to take help of his intelligence and understand what is Krishna's direction in this regard. What should he do?

Now we should not think that we can also simply take help of our intelligence under all circumstances and do whatever is according to our judgement using our intelligence, whatever the help whatever is right we should do that that seems like a common sense thing to do but we should also understand that if we are not 100% Krishna conscious we are unable to properly utilize our intelligence the way Paramatma or Krishna directs it why because the Supreme Lord as Paramatma directs the intelligence according to our desires according to our karma and according to our position whether we are devotee or non-devotee for a devotee Krishna one was 10% Krishna conscious Krishna directly gives perfect instructions from within the heart through the intelligence directly Krishna gives instructions for somebody's a non-devotee Krishna is neutral he's neutral as Paramatma he actually considers any living being who is not Krishna conscious ordinary people in this world most people Krishna will as Paramatma consider what is the desire of the particular person then what is his karma based on which what he deserves and accordingly Krishna will direct the intelligence for the person to do in a particular act in a particular way now.

### karma, desire, intelligence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FDMYKWt4060?t=373
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Paramatma will always consider the desire, the karma, and it is according to the laws that are working."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on how Paramatma directs intelligence based on a person's desires and karma, emphasizing that even with good intentions, past sinful activities can lead to incorrect directions, while pious actions can lead to proper guidance.

Now supposing somebody actually desires to do something which is correct, then if his karma is to not do things right, because of his previous sinful activities, then the direction will be given to do something wrong and not the right thing. So he will not get the correct direction through the intelligence, even though he wants to do something right. You see, his desire is to do things right, but his karma is to do something wrong. And therefore, the intelligence will work in doing something wrong. Now consider the other case. Somebody doesn't actually know what is to be done, whether it is right or wrong. But his karma is that he should do the right thing. His karma is that he should be doing the right thing because he is a pious person. He doesn't have sinful reaction of doing the wrong thing. So then he is given the proper direction by the intelligence because of his karma and his desire is to do the right thing and he is not sure what to do. He is not sure what to do. He is seeking direction. So when we seek direction, we refer our intelligence. So he is directed to do the right thing. So Paramatma will always consider the desire, the karma, and it is according to the laws that are working. Laws of nature. There is no partiality. There is no partiality in the part of the Supreme Lord. It is less like a programmed machine. It works regardless of who operates according to the program, according to the way it is designed. It will work.

---
### Krishna consciousness, gunas, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FDMYKWt4060?t=527
**Verse References:** BG 14.26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The real way to ensure that we always do the right things and never do any sinful activity, never commit any mistake in our life, is to become completely Krishna conscious."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of Krishna consciousness as the means to ensure righteous actions and avoid sinful activities, highlighting the influence of the three gunas and the path to transcendence through devotional service.

So when we are not Krishna conscious, we cannot always be sure, even if we want to do the right thing. We can't always be sure we will be able to do the right thing. Therefore the real way to ensure that we always do the right things and never do any sinful activity, never commit any mistake in our life, is to become completely Krishna conscious. That is the only way because we are very very very tiny as spirit soul, individual spirit soul. We are very tiny. We are subject to influence of the illusory energy. And under the influence of the illusory energy, if the mode of goodness is acting, then there is knowledge and proper direction, enlightened state. There is proper direction to do the right things. In the mode of passion, we are confused. In the mode of ignorance, we do always the wrong things. So these gunas are always interacting and producing so many effects. Sometimes sattva guna dominates, sometimes raja guna dominates, sometimes tamma guna dominates. So it is very very difficult under the influence of the gunas to always do the right thing. There is no guarantee. So therefore the shastras say if you want to do the right things, you should actually rise above the gunas. How can you rise above the gunas? How can you be situated without influence, without being affected by the influence of the gunas? You should come to the spiritual platform above the material platform of the three gunas. And to come to that spiritual platform is possible only if you engage in pure devotional service. This is told in the Bhagavad Gita: mam chayo avyabhicharena bhakti-yogena sevate sagunan samatityaitaan brahma bhuyaya kalpate. So here.

### punishment, rituals, compassion

**Summary:** The lecture concludes by revisiting Arjuna's actions towards Ashwatthama, explaining the prescribed punishments for a fallen Brahmana, and describing the Pandavas' performance of rituals for the dead, marking the end of the chapter.

Arjuna, he separated the jewel from the head of Ashwatthama and also cut off his hair. Now this was as good as cutting off his head and yet his life was saved for all practical purposes. See how Arjuna actually was able to satisfy both Draupadi's request to save his life and Bhima Sena's suggestion that Ashwatthama should be killed. So certainly Ashwatthama is twice born because he is indicated here as Dvija. Dvija. Saha murdha jam. Dvija. He is twice born and he is twice born but he fell down from his position and he is dead and therefore he was properly punished. So the 56th verse, Vimuchya Rashana- meatballs Baal-a-hatya-hatam-prabham Tejasaa-Maniina-heenam-sibiraan-nirayaa-payatt. He had already lost his bodily luster due to infanticide and now moreover having lost the jewel from his head he would lost even more strength he was unbound and driven out of the camp 57th verse wapanam dravina adhanam sthanan niraya nirya panam tatha eshahi brahma bandhunam badho nanyo asti dai hi kaha cutting the hair from his head depriving him of his wealth and driving him from his residence are the prescribed punishments for the relative of a Brahmana there is no injunction for killing the body so Arjuna did not kill him but at the same time he cut his hair from his head deprived him of his wealth his jewel was taken away and driving him from his residence he was driven out of the of the camp so these are prescribed punishments and Arjuna gave those proper punishments the last words are 58th verse of this chapter the closing words putra shoka tura sarve Pandava sahakrishna swanam nutanam yatkrityam chakrur nirharana adhikam the meaning of this verse the last verse thereafter the sons of Pandu and Draupadi overwhelmed with grief performed the proper rituals for the dead bodies of their relatives so they undertook the the duty of performing the rituals for the departed souls that is the end of this chapter thank you very much Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.7.6 | 08-12-2021
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Vyasadeva's vision, energies of the Lord, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5PXy_ny0POc?t=58
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.6
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is the perfect remedy for the suffering of the conditioned souls: the process of devotional service."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Vyasadeva's vision of the Lord and His energies, highlighting devotional service as the remedy for conditioned souls, beginning with hearing and chanting.

In this session, we will be discussing the 6th verse of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. It is complete and perfect in all respects. When it is described that Vyasadeva saw the Personality of Godhead, it is qualified: he saw the all-perfect Personality of Godhead. Srila Prabhupada further explains what this means. This statement suggests that the complete unit of the Personality of Godhead includes His parts and parcels also. The Lord is always complete, and He is never without His parts and parcels. He saw, therefore, his different energies, namely the internal energy, the marginal energy, and the external energy. The Lord is always with His energies, so Vyasadeva, when he saw the Lord, he saw His different energies also. He also saw His different plenary portions and parts of the plenary portions, namely His different incarnations also. Even though the Supreme Lord is one, one and only Supreme Personality of Godhead, He is not a limited person. He has many different forms of incarnations, plenary portions, and parts of plenary portions. Vyasadeva also specifically observed the unwanted miseries of the conditioned souls who are bewildered by the external energy. This was explained in the previous verse, and here the remedial measure is given. At last, he saw the Lord, and here the remedial measure is given. This is the perfect remedy for the suffering of the conditioned souls: the process of devotional service. It is the process of devotional service. This devotional service is a great transcendental science and begins with the process of hearing and chanting the name, fame, glory, etc., of the Supreme Personality of Godhead: shravanam, kirtanam, vishnom. This is the beginning of the process of devotional service.

---
### devotional service, causeless mercy, mitigation of miseries
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5PXy_ny0POc?t=370
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Bhakti is something that is bestowed by the Supreme Lord when the Lord is fully satisfied with the sincere efforts of the devotee."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that devotional service should not be mechanical but involve the full mind and spirit, depending on the Lord's causeless mercy, and that even a beginner can experience mitigation of material miseries.

Revival of the dormant affection or love of Godhead does not depend on the mechanical system of hearing and chanting. Generally, when people do something repeatedly, the process tends to become mechanical, just like routinely we do so many things very mechanically. But the process of devotional service is never a mechanical process because it is the full involvement of the mind, the full involvement of the spirit soul, of the spiritual personality, 100% involved in this process. Then only the process is effective to the extent of achieving perfection. Even if somebody does devotional service in a mechanical way, there is benefit, but it is not sufficient to take us to perfection. So Srila Prabhupada says this transcendental process solely and wholly depends on the causeless mercy of the Lord. Bhakti is something that is bestowed by the Supreme Lord when the Lord is fully satisfied with the sincere efforts of the devotee. He may endow him with his loving transcendental service. So the complete perfection comes after the devotee has rendered devotional service with all his efforts sincerely, trying to please the Lord, and when the Lord is pleased, He bestows the perfection of love of God. But even with the prescribed forms of hearing and chanting, that is just one has begun, there is at once mitigation of the superfluous and unwanted miseries of material existence. The effect on the heart of the chanting and hearing is immediately felt even by the beginner before he is able to even make advancement, immediately. What is the immediate effect? The mitigation of material miseries. This is very important to understand, and it is a very great encouragement for anyone that immediately they begin to feel the benefit of this process.

---
### devotional service vs. knowledge, purification, material miseries
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5PXy_ny0POc?t=570
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Devotion helps us to awaken knowledge within our heart."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts devotional service with the impersonal pursuit of knowledge, stating that devotion is independent of knowledge and helps awaken it, emphasizing the need for sincere and patient engagement in devotional service to cleanse material contamination accumulated over lifetimes, and describing material miseries as superfluous.

Such mitigation of material affection does not wait for development of transcendental knowledge. This is what the impersonalists think, that unless there is knowledge, there is no progress in spiritual life. That is for the jnana process. Bhakti process does not depend on development of transcendental knowledge. So Prabhupada says, rather knowledge is dependent on devotional service for the ultimate realization of the supreme truth. So knowledge is dependent on devotion, but devotion is independent of knowledge. Devotion helps us to awaken knowledge within our heart. So the process of devotional service directly acts on the contaminated consciousness to purify, but the devotee has to sincerely engage in the process and patiently engage in the process and patiently wait for complete purification. The complete purification takes some time because we have been associating with this material world for a very, very, very long time, millions of lifetimes, and we have accumulated so much of this material contamination, life after life after life after life after life. In ignorance, we have been simply engaging in sense gratification, so that has to be cleansed away before we can actually experience the transcendental love in reciprocation with the Supreme Lord. Before that, we have to cleanse the heart. These material miseries are described as superfluous. Superfluous means they are actually not giving any benefit for us. They are simply useless stuff. That's why they are called un-earth. In fact, un-earth means unwanted, something which is unwanted. Un-earth this body itself, material body itself is un-earth. It itself is un-earth. But in the material conception, we think we cannot exist without the body, why? Because the body is the means for our sense gratification, for our material enjoyment. That material enjoyment, yes, we can have to whatever extent we can get some material pleasure by the means of the body, but as spirit soul, we are not meant to enjoy materially, neither material pleasures give any satisfaction for the soul. The Bhagavatam raises this point.

---
### soul satisfaction, material vs. transcendental happiness, pure bliss
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5PXy_ny0POc?t=772
**Verse References:** BG 5.22
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Material pleasure is simply a sensation, and it is a temporary thing; it is also called chapala sukha, momentary happiness, and it eventually leads to miseries only."
**Summary:** This segment explains that true satisfaction comes from the soul, not material pleasures, which are temporary and lead to frustration, contrasting this with transcendental happiness, which is pure bliss and never momentary, experienced through transcendental senses.

What ensures that there is complete satisfaction for the soul? The satisfaction of the soul, of the real self, that is to be considered. The satisfaction of the soul is never possible by any material pleasure, material means, material methods, material pleasures, material arrangements. It never even touches the soul in any way. So, therefore, people who are seeking real happiness, they are frustrated when they make different arrangements for enjoying materially, or they seek material happiness, and they never are satisfied within their heart, never are satisfied. So this point is explained in the Bhagavad Gita: yehi samsparishya ja bhoga dukha yonaya evate aadyantavan takaunteya nateshu ramate budhah. One more verse that explains what is the nature of transcendental happiness: sukham aatyantikam yatta buddhikrahyam atindriyam. You see, this transcendental happiness is an experience of boundless transcendental joy, which is experienced with transcendental senses, atindriyam, not with these material senses. These material senses are useless for experiencing any spiritual happiness or transcendental happiness. So material pleasure is simply a sensation, and it is a temporary thing; it is also called chapala sukha, momentary happiness, and it eventually leads to miseries only, whereas transcendental happiness is completely pure bliss, and it is never momentary. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Harikrishnan.

---

### hearing, Srimad Bhagavatam, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Dez1DeOI9rc?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.7
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Simply by giving oral reception to this Vedic literature, referring to the Srimad Bhagavatam, the feeling for loving devotional service to Lord Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, sprouts up at once to extinguish the fire of lamentation, illusion and fearfulness."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam, emphasizing the power of hearing the text to awaken love for Krishna and dispel material anxieties.

Om Agyana Timiran Dhasya Gyanan Janishalakaya Chakshurun Militam Yena Tasvai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gauravatta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udhirayet. In this session, we will be discussing from the 7th verse of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam: Asyamvai Shruyamanayam Krishnay Parama Purushe Bhaktir Utpadyate Pumsaha Shokamoha Bhayapaha. The meaning of this verse is simply by giving oral reception to this Vedic literature, referring to the Srimad Bhagavatam, the feeling for loving devotional service to Lord Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, sprouts up at once to extinguish the fire of lamentation, illusion and fearfulness. See how effective Srimad Bhagavatam is.

### hearing, absorption, nectar

**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of Pariksit Maharaj hearing from Sukadeva Goswami to illustrate the power of attentive hearing and absorption in the Srimad Bhagavatam.

Simply you have to hear this called giving oral reception. You have to give oral reception means you have to hear attentively. And simply you have to do that. What did Pariksit Maharaj do? He simply sat and heard Shukadev Goswami speak for 7 days and nights continuously. That's all he did. He didn't do any gymnastics, but he was hearing with complete attention. He was fully absorbed in the subject matter, so much so that Shukadev Goswami said, 'Do you want to take a break?' He said no. There is no need for any break because through my ears, I'm drinking the nectar of the words of the Srimad Bhagavatam spoken by Shukadev Goswami. So that nectar makes one indifferent to thirst, hunger, sleep, all these things cannot disturb a person who's completely immersed in hearing the Srimad Bhagavatam.

### illusion, material pangs, suffering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Dez1DeOI9rc?t=232
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Material life means constant lamentation and everyone is after the mirage of illusory things."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how hearing the Bhagavatam extinguishes lamentation, illusion, and fear, quoting Srila Prabhupada on the effectiveness of the ear and describing material life as full of lamentation and the pursuit of illusory happiness.

It extinguishes the fire of lamentation, illusion and fearfulness. This is explained by Srila Prabhupada in the purport. Prabhupada says, there are various senses of which the ear is the most effective ear. This sense works even when a man is deep asleep. One can protect himself from the hands of an enemy while awake, but while asleep, one is protected by the ear only. The importance of hearing is mentioned here in connection with attaining the highest perfection of life, namely getting free from three material pangs. Everyone is full of lamentation at every moment. Everyone is full of lamentation at every moment. Everyone is full of lamentation at every moment. Material life means constant lamentation and everyone is after the mirage of illusory things. The whole material creation is a creation of the illusory energy. And so many objects are there which bewilder the mind and people are simply running after this mirage. Mirage means something, a phenomenon or a death. Mirage means something, a phenomenon or a death. In a desert, there is no water, but it appears like there is a body of water at a distance and one chases that body of water and never gets any water because there is no real water there in that mirage. Similarly in this material world, so many objects seem to be promising so much of happiness, but actually none of those objects can give us any real pleasure. And thirdly, he is always afraid of his supposed enemy, Shoka, Moha, Bhaya, these are three material pangs, which everyone in this material world who is born under the force of karma has to face.

### material disease, symptoms, pure devotee

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

These are the primary symptoms of material disease, Bhavaroga, it is called Bhavaroga, the disease of repeated birth and death. Everyone is affected by this disease in this material world and the primary symptoms just like if somebody has got a viral infection, then they have a symptom called fever or some body pain. Vomiting, loose motion, whatever, like that, what is the symptom of this material disease that is described as these three symptoms Shoka, Moha, Bhaya. Shoka is lamentation, Moha is illusion and Bhaya is fear. And it is definitely suggested here in that simply by hearing the message of Srimad Bhagavatam, one gets attachment for the supreme personality. Krishna is very, very attractive, but you have to hear about Krishna from the pure devotee. Because the pure devotee presents factually the actual personality of Godhead, not some speculated theory or some mental concoction, no, real Supreme Lord is presented by the pure devotee. And as soon as this is affected, the symptoms of the material diseases disappear.

The ultimate result of devotional service is to develop genuine love for the first Supreme personality. We have love for Krishna, but this is awakened only by this process of Bhakti. It cannot be awakened by the process of Jnana, Karma or Yoga. No material methods are possible to be practiced in order to awaken our genuine devotion or genuine love for Krishna. ### Prakriti, Parama Purusha, relationship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Dez1DeOI9rc?t=562
**Verse References:** BG 7.5
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord is absolute in all respects and thus there is no difference between him and the subject matter heard about him."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the relationship between Krishna and the living entities as one of love, comparing it to the affection between male and female, and emphasizing that hearing about Krishna is direct contact with Him.

Why is that? Because the living entities are described as Prakriti in the Bhagavad Gita. Apareyam itasthu anyam prakritim viddhime paraam. Material energy is described as Apara Prakriti, living entities are described as Para Prakriti. So, we are Prakriti. Prakriti is feminine. Prakriti is female. Prakriti is feminine. Prakriti is feminine. Prakriti is feminine. The Lord is always described as the Parama Purusha, the supreme male personality. Thus, the affection between the Lord and the living entities is something like the affection between the male and the female in this material world. See why this is being said is because we have experienced what is the kind of affection that exists between a male and a female. So the term love of God. Love of God is quite appropriate. If you understand from this perspective, we are feminine in terms of being Prakriti and the Lord is the supreme male. So the relationship is one of love. That is how Prabhupada explains in this purport. Loving devotional service to the Lord begins with hearing about the Lord. Shravanam. Shravanam. Shravanam. Shravanam. This process for executing devotional service is hearing. There is no difference between the Lord and the subject matter heard about him. This is the absolute nature of Krishna. His descriptions are identical with his personality. The Lord is absolute in all respects and thus there is no difference between him and the subject matter heard about him. The Lord is an absolute in all respects and thus there is no difference between him and the subject matter heard about him.

### Srimad Bhagavatam, Shukadeva Goswami, Vyasadeva
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/l0Ygpbdfdb4?t=55
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That Vyasadeva, he compiled the Srimad Bhagavatam under the instruction of Narada."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the verse being discussed, focusing on Vyasadeva compiling and teaching the Srimad Bhagavatam to his son, Shukadeva Goswami.

In this session, we will be discussing the 8th verse of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is: The great sage Vyasadeva, after compiling the Srimad Bhagavatam and revising it, taught it to his own son, Shri Shukadeva Goswami, who was already engaged in self-realization. It's a very, very important verse. What is this verse telling us? That Vyasadeva, he compiled the Srimad Bhagavatam under the instruction of Narada. He revised it because it's a very big work, 18,000 verses. And he also taught it to his own son, Shri Shukadeva Goswami. Now, what is the special thing about Vyasadeva teaching his own son Shukadeva Goswami?

---
### Shukadeva Goswami, Liberation, Brahma Sutras
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/l0Ygpbdfdb4?t=123
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srimad Bhagavatam is the natural commentary on the Brahma Sutras compiled by the same author."
**Summary:** This segment explains Shukadeva Goswami's unique position as a liberated soul from birth and Srila Prabhupada's explanation of the Srimad Bhagavatam as a natural commentary on the Brahma Sutras by Vyasadeva.

If you remember, the beginning of the Bhagavatam, it is explained, Shukadeva Goswami walked away from home without caring to undergo any samskaras, including the Upanayana samskara. He just ignored because only after Upanayana one approaches a bona-fide spiritual master to learn the Vedas, to learn the scriptures, to learn the spiritual knowledge. But Shukadeva Swami just didn't care for any of those formalities. Why? Because he was already engaged in self-realization. He was a liberated soul by birth. It's very, very, very rare somebody is born liberated. Generally, everyone is born as a conditioned soul. The very reason for birth is that one is conditioned by material energy or material nature. But Shukadeva Swami was a liberated soul by birth, so he didn't have to undergo any study of the Vedas. Now, why did he actually learn the Srimad Bhagavatam? That's the question that will be raised in the next words. But here, Srila Prabhupada is the one who explains about the significance of Vyasadeva compiling the Bhagavatam and teaching it to his own son. So Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, Srimad Bhagavatam is the natural commentary on the Brahma Sutras compiled by the same author, natural commentary on the Brahma Sutras. If you are aware, the Brahma Sutras or the Vedanta Sutras are the most scholarly presentation by Vyasadeva. The essence of the Vedas is given in the Upanishads. The essence of the Upanishads is given in the Vedanta Sutra or the Brahma Sutra. So Brahma Sutra is like the cream of the cream, and that Brahma Sutra is not easy to understand even for learned Vedic scholars. So Vyasadeva himself wrote a commentary on this Vedanta Sutra in the form of Srimad Bhagavatam, and it's called natural because the author of both the Vedanta Sutra and its commentary, the Srimad Bhagavatam, is the same person, Srila Vyasadeva. This Brahma Sutra or Vedanta Sutra is meant for those who are already engaged in self-realization.

---
### Material Bondage, Freedom, Limitations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/l0Ygpbdfdb4?t=437
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Material bondage means we have lost our freedom, natural freedom, which is there in our spiritual existence."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the concept of material bondage, explaining how it limits our natural freedom and subjects us to the laws of material nature.

Material bondage, material bondage means we have lost our freedom, natural freedom, which is there in our spiritual existence. We are bound up by this body. We are bound up by the laws of material nature. Just like I cannot eat whatever I want, anytime I want, as much as I want. I cannot eat. If I don't obey or follow the laws of material nature, then overeating causes indigestion, or eating the wrong thing, it infects some part of the body, and we get diseased, and we suffer in so many ways due to diseases. So like this, we have lost our natural freedom because of material bondage. And what is this proof of material bondage? This very material body. Every body, material body, is a limitation. The soul is completely free to travel anywhere in the whole creation, but being bound up by this body, we cannot just go anywhere and everywhere. And the means of travel also is fixed according to the body, is it not? Birds can fly, human beings cannot fly unless they use a machine called an airplane or they use some yogic siddhi, otherwise they cannot fly. The aquatics can live in the water, but they cannot live on the land. We live on the land, but we cannot live in water. So like this, there are so many limitations, so many limitations. So this is called bondage.

---
### Sense Gratification, Liberation, Bhagavatam

**Verse References:** —

**Summary:** This segment continues the discussion on bondage, linking it to sense gratification and highlighting the Bhagavatam as the solution, emphasizing Shukadeva Goswami's liberated status.

Bondage also dictates sense gratification. We are under illusion or this thing that sense gratification will solve my problem, but the remedial measure for all these problems is there in the Bhagavatam. Shukadeva Swami was a liberated soul from the very beginning of his life, but his father taught him because this is the subject matter for the Paramahamsas, for those who are liberated souls.

### Bhagavatam, Kali Yuga, Compilation Date
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/l0Ygpbdfdb4?t=608
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Srimad Bhagavatam is compiled not less than five thousand years ago."
**Summary:** This segment addresses the dating of the Bhagavatam, contrasting mundane scholarship with the Bhagavatam's internal evidence, concluding it was compiled at least 5000 years ago.

These mundane scholars, they have their own empirical methods of determining how old is some scripture or how many years back something was spoken. About history itself, there are so many opinions according to their own theories. But from the text of the Bhagavatam, it is very clear that this Bhagavatam is compiled before the disappearance of King Pariksit and after the departure of Lord Krishna. So this is all described in the Bhagavatam itself very clearly. When Maharaj Pariksit was ruling the world as the king of Bharata Varsha, he chastised the personality of Kali, Kali Purusha. That is described in the first canto of the Srimad Bhagavata. According to revealed scriptures and astrological calculation, the age of Kali is in its five thousandth year now. Five thousand years have already passed since Kali Yuga began. Therefore, Srimad Bhagavatam is compiled not less than five thousand years ago.

---
### Vedic Literature, Krishna, Impersonal Brahman
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/l0Ygpbdfdb4?t=680
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Mahabharata was compiled before Srimad Bhagavatam and the Puranas were compiled before Mahabharata."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the chronological order of Vedic literature compilation, emphasizing the importance of the Puranas, Mahabharata, and Bhagavatam in describing Krishna's activities and the subordination of the impersonal Brahman.

See how Srila Prabhupada is arriving at this figure of five thousand years old based on the description in the Bhagavatam itself. Mahabharata was compiled before Srimad Bhagavatam and the Puranas were compiled before Mahabharata. Vyasadeva is the one who compiled all this. So he compiled the Puranas, then he compiled the Mahabharata, then he compiled the Srimad Bhagavatam. That is an estimation of the date of compilation of the different Vedic literatures. Especially the Puranas, Mahabharata, and Bhagavatam are very important because they describe the activities of the personality of the Srimad Bhagavatam called Krishna, the activities of the person is very rarely found in very literature. Lot of description is there about the impersonal feature, but that is the subordinate aspect of the absolute truth.

---
### Nivritti Marga, Pravritti Marga, Liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/l0Ygpbdfdb4?t=742
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The theme of the Srimad Bhagavatam is the cure of the materialistic disease of the human being or stopping completely the pants of material existence."
**Summary:** This segment explains the synopsis of Srimad Bhagavatam, the path of nivritti marga (liberation), contrasting it with pravritti marga, and emphasizing the Bhagavatam's role in curing materialistic disease.

The impersonal Brahman is subordinate to the personality of God. The synopsis of Srimad Bhagavatam was given before the detailed description under instruction of Narada. Narada gave the synopsis of Bhagavatam to Vyasadeva, and Vyasadeva expanded that and presented the Srimad Bhagavatam for our understanding in an elaborate way. Srimad Bhagavatam is the science for following the path of nivritti marga. That's why it was said, nivritta niratam munihi. Shukadeva Swami was a liberated soul. It is the path of liberation. The path of pravritti marga was condemned by Narada. Narada, while instructing Vyasadeva, he said this pravritti marga simply increases, aggravates the diseased condition, whereas nivritti marga relieves one of the disease, material disease, and also on the path of liberation, one becomes freed from all the material miseries that was described in the previous two verses. So this path of nivritti marga is the natural, sorry, the path of pravritti marga is the natural inclination for all conditioned souls. The theme of the Srimad Bhagavatam is the cure of the materialistic disease of the human being or stopping completely the pants of material existence. See when we take up this nivritti mark then immediately the cure is.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.7.9 | 12-12-2021
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### self-realization, Shukadeva Goswami, Srimad Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xmODOjLkvvY?t=52
**Verse References:** SB 1.7.9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srimad Bhagavatam is a post-graduate study even for the atmaramas who have to surpass all the studies of vedic knowledge."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses why Shukadeva Goswami, already self-realized, would study the vast Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining that it is transcendental literature even beyond liberation, meant for those already self-satisfied.

In this session, we will be discussing the 9th verse of the 7th chapter of the 1st canto of Shreemad Bhagavatam. Now, after Suta Goswami said that Vyasadeva taught the Shreemad Bhagavatam to his own son, Shaunaka immediately interrupts Suta Goswami and asks a very, very important question. Kindly hear this question very attentively. Shri Shaunaka asked, "Shri Shukadeva Goswami was already on the path of self-realization, and thus he was pleased with his own self. Why did he take the trouble to undergo the self-realization, the study of such a vast literature? Bhagavatam is no small literature, 18,000 verses. Why did he undergo the trouble when he was already on the path of self-realization?" So, in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains, "For the people in general, the highest perfection of life is to cease from material activities and be fixed on the path of self-realization." Ceasing from material activity means that these material activities are activities which are aimed at sense gratification. Sense gratification means the senses are always demanding some pleasure, some pleasing object of the senses. For the eyes, it is a beautiful form; for the ears, it is some melodious music or some praise; for the tongue, it is some palliative beauty. Soul pleasure of the self, atma means self and arama means to take pleasure, everyone is searching after the highest pleasure, but the standard of pleasure of one may be different from the standard of another. Of course, everybody wants pleasure, but the standard may not be the same. Therefore, the standard of pleasure enjoyed by the karmis, the materialistic persons, is different from that of the atmaramas, the transcendentalists. The atmaramas are completely indifferent to material enjoyment in every respect. Why is Srila Prabhupada mentioning this? Because he is trying to explain what is the need for Shukadeva Goswami to undertake study of this vast literature when he was already self-satisfied, what is the impetus? So, Srila Prabhupada says atmaramas are indifferent to material enjoyment. They are not interested in material enjoyment. Srila Shukadeva Goswami had already attained that stage, the stage of atmarama, taking pleasure in the self or the soul, and still he was attracted to undergo the trouble of studying the great Bhagavatam literature. So, there is an important conclusion here from this stage that Srimad Bhagavatam is strongly affecting the atmarama's behavior. This means that Srimad Bhagavatam is a post-graduate study even for the atmaramas who have to surpass all the studies of vedic knowledge. Atmaramas are not interested in any material stuff, so why would they study Bhagavatam? Because Bhagavatam has got nothing to do with material sense enjoyment. It's completely transcendental.

---
### liberation, Vedanta philosophy, Srimad Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xmODOjLkvvY?t=421
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srimad Bhagavatam is not merely a transcendental literature which will award liberation; those who are already liberated again, they won't take interest in some literature which describes how you can get liberation."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Srimad Bhagavatam is not just about liberation, which wouldn't interest someone like Shukadeva Goswami who is already liberated, and contrasts it with Vedanta philosophy, which is for those seeking liberation from material bondage.

It is transcendental, and not only that, it is transcendental. It is not merely a transcendental literature which will award liberation. Those who are already liberated again, they won't take interest in some literature which describes how you can get liberation. That is the Vedanta philosophy. Vedanta philosophy is meant for people who want to get out of material bondage and put an end to the repetition of birth and death, and thereby put an end to all material miseries by becoming liberated by following the process of self-realization as described in the Vedanta philosophy or Vedanta literature. But Shukadeva Goswami is already liberated, so Shukadeva Goswami will be neither interested in material sense enjoyment nor will he be interested in that literature which describes how to get liberation. Both these kinds of literature are not meant for persons like Shukadeva Goswami. So, therefore, somebody who is already liberated is certainly not interested in any material enjoyment or material sense pleasures, and certainly not also interested in literature which teaches how you can get liberation. Then what kind of literature will such a person study? That is described here as Srimad Bhagavatam. That's why Srila Prabhupada uses the term Srimad Bhagavatam is described as Srimad Bhagavatam.

---
### devotional happiness, liberation, Krishna Consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xmODOjLkvvY?t=701
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If you were to be able to get an ocean of spiritual happiness, then that ocean of spiritual happiness cannot equal even one drop of devotional happiness."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts spiritual happiness with devotional happiness, stating that devotional happiness, derived from service to Krishna, is far superior and is the essence of the Bhagavatam, and that pure devotees even deride liberation in favor of serving Krishna.

Happiness is much, much, much, much more than spiritual happiness. What is the comparison? But there is something to say is that if you were to be able to get an ocean of spiritual happiness, then that ocean of spiritual happiness cannot equal even one drop of devotional happiness. There is a comparison in the book that is something. So, devotional happiness is the happiness that is the happiness derived by devotional service to Krishna when Krishna reciprocates the devotees and service, devotional service. So, that is billions and trillions of times more than any spiritual happiness also. So, that happiness is what is there in the Bhagavatam, that devotional happens. And there are so many examples given in the Bhagavatam how different great devotees, pure devotees, they experience this bliss of devotional happiness. And such pure devotees who experience devotional happiness, they deride even the conception of liberation. One great devotee, Bilvamangala Thakura, he says, "Ever since I am engaged in devotional service and I'm experiencing devotional happiness, I don't care for liberation, but liberation comes and waits as my maidservant, how can I serve?" So, this is the position of a pure devotee, that a pure devotee is uninterested in liberation. Certainly, a pure devotee is never interested in any material happiness. A pure devotee is not even interested in any kind of liberation. There are five kinds of liberation, but a pure devotee is not interested in any of the kinds of liberation. He is simply interested in serving Krishna because that is the source of unlimited transcendental bliss called the devotional happiness. So, Bhagavatam is filled with that. Simply by sitting and hearing Srimad Bhagavatam from the proper source, one has access to this devotional happiness. We get a glimpse of that when we begin Sankirtana. In Sankirtana, there is some kind of experience of happiness which one has never experienced before, and that is very, very, very unique, but that is only a brief glimpse we get in the beginning of our Krishna Consciousness. We have to cleanse our heart and become fixed up in devotional service to be able to experience real devotional happiness. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

The first verse: Suta Vacha Atate Samparetanam Svanam Udakam Ichchatam Datum Sa Krishna Gangayam Kritya Yayostriyaha. The meaning of this verse, the first verse of this 8th chapter of the first canto of Bhagavatam: Suta Goswami said, thereafter the Pandavas, desiring to deliver water to their dead relatives who had desired it, went to the Ganges with Draupadi. ### Hindu customs, death, Ganges
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OdTVPl6yqmo?t=207
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Today it is the custom in Hindu society to go to the Ganges or any other sacred river to take bath when death occurs in the family."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Srila Prabhupada's explanation of the Hindu custom of going to the Ganges or other sacred rivers to bathe after a death in the family and pouring water for the departed soul.

So Srila Prabhupada explains, this particular ritual. Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, today it is the custom in Hindu society to go to the Ganges or any other sacred river to take bath when death occurs in the family. Death is an inevitable part of life. That life is an inevitable part of life. That life in this body has always got a beginning and an end. So the end of life in this body is called death. And according to Bhagavad Gita, the real person inside the body, the soul, quits the body or is forced to quit the body at the time of death, at the end of this life, the end of life in this body. So when death occurs, it is the tradition and custom to go to some sacred river and take bath. And moreover, it is described here by Srila Prabhupada, each of the family members pours out a pot full of the Ganges water for the departed soul and walks in a procession with the ladies in the front.

So those of you who are familiar with this custom, the rituals that are performed, one of the important rituals is called Dharmodaka, offering water. Udaka means water, offering water for the departed soul according to the particular described method in the Dharmashastras. The Pandavas also followed the rules more than 5000 years ago. So these customs and traditions and the particular rituals mentioned in Dharmashastra are not something new that has been invented now; it has always existed in human society, and it was followed by the Pandavas 5000 years ago because this is a historical record, the Srimad Bhagavatam's historical record of what happened 5000 years back when the Pandavas and Kauravas were present on this earth.

So therefore, Vasudeva's son Krishna and Kunti's sons, the Pandavas, were cousins. That's how it is described. Krishna being a cousin of the Pandavas was also amongst the family members. ### Ganges, Vamana, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OdTVPl6yqmo?t=484
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It is because the water of the Ganges is mixed with the dust of the lotus feet of Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the sanctity of the Ganges, relating it to the pastime of Lord Vamana and how the Ganges originated from washing the lotus feet of Vamanadeva.

Now what is a special thing about the Ganges River? We all know it is very, very sacred, it's very holy, it's sanctified. Why is that being mentioned here in the Bhagavatam in this verse? It is because the water of the Ganges is mixed with the dust of the lotus feet of Lord Krishna. So the Bhagavatam itself describes a pastime of Lord Krishna in his incarnation as Vaman Dev, when Vamana wanted to restore the kingdom of the heavenly planets to Indra, the son of Aditi. Aditi prayed to the Supreme Lord Vishnu and said, 'My sons have lost their kingdom, kindly restore it to them.' So the Lord, being pleased with the worship of Aditi, agreed to simply become her son, appear as her son, and that was the incarnation of the Lord as the son of Aditi. His name was Vamana. Vamana incarnation of the Lord is Vamana. He appeared as a small dwar brahmachari boy. That was the form in which he appeared. That's why Vamana.

### Vamana, Bali Maharaj, donation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OdTVPl6yqmo?t=571
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Vaman Dev said I should be satisfied with what is the bare minimum necessity and he spoke very brilliantly telling that there is no end to greed."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Vamana, describing how Vamana approached Bali Maharaj for a donation of three paces of land.

Vamana means a dwarf in some script and then this Vamana went to Bali Maharaj who was ruling the heavenly planets having conquered the heavenly kingdom and on behalf of the asuras Bali Maharaj is ruling so he and Bali Maharaj is performing sacrifices so Vamana disguised as a brahmachari boy the Supreme Lord Vishnu disguised as a brahmachari boy he simply went to that arena sacrificial arena of Bali Maharaj and asked him I want a donation, three paces of land measured according to my footstep so Bali Maharaj initially was hesitant to give that small quantity of donation which he thought small boy doesn't have experience asking donation. So, Bali Maharaj said I can give you so much donation, why are you asking for just three steps of land. Then Vaman Dev said I should be satisfied with what is the bare minimum necessity and he spoke very brilliantly telling that there is no end to greed. And anyway Bali Maharaj thought this Brahmachari boy is speaking very intelligently, very nicely. So, he actually agreed to give that donation.

### Vamana, Ganges, universe
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OdTVPl6yqmo?t=688
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The water of the Ganges is sanctified because it is mixed with the dust of the lotus feet of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker describes how Vamana expanded himself to cover the universe in two steps, and how the Ganges originated when the water from the Karna ocean washed the lotus feet of Vamanadeva after his toe pierced the covering of the universe.

And what did Vaman Dev do? He expanded himself to cover the entire universe in two steps. And, when he stretched out his leg to cover the upper portion above the earth all the planets, until the covering of the universe the first step. Then the toe of his foot pierced the coverings of the universe that's very significant because the universe is covered by seven layers. Each layer being 10 times thicker than the previous layer. It's a huge covering. The universe itself is 4 billion miles in diameter. And these coverings are 40 billion miles thick, 400 billion miles thick, second layer, third layer, 4000 billion miles thick, 4 trillion miles thick. So, like that 4 quadrillion miles, like that the thickness of the layers. So, all those layers, the Supreme Lord pierced by merely pushing his toe into the shell of the universe. And what happened? Because these universes are situated in the Karna ocean, causal ocean. So, the water from the Karna ocean leaked inside this universe. And that water, as it came into the universe, through the hole created by Vamana piercing the shell of the universe, that water washed the lotus feet of Vamandev, who is Vishnu, who is the Supreme Lord. And that is called Ganga. So, therefore, here it is said, the water of the Ganges is sanctified because it is mixed with the dust of the lotus feet of the Supreme Lord.

So, therefore, Ganga gel is spiritually potent. It is spiritually potent because it contains the dust of the lotus feet of the Supreme Lord Krishna. And that's the reason why anybody takes bath in the Ganges. People swim in the Ganges River. They are completely purified of all sinful reactions. When they offer that Ganges water to the departed soul, the departed soul also is actually benefited in several ways. Actions are actually cleared away when the soul is offered Ganga Jal, because the Ganga Jal is sanctified water because of being mixed with the dust of the lotus feet of the Supreme Lord. The Lord supports his devotees because there is a great necessity of this for everyone's well-being. Devotees are meant for the well-being of everyone in the world. Because devotees do the greatest welfare activity. There are many people who are engaged in different kinds of welfare activity. But which welfare activity is the best? That which reinstates every living being in his or her original position as the loving devotee of the Supreme Lord in his personal abode. We all belong to Krishna. There is no reason for us to suffer in this material world other than our foolishness of becoming forgetful of Krishna or being rebellious, envious of Krishna, trying to compete with Krishna, trying to assume Krishna's position or trying to be independent of Krishna. ### devotion, welfare, equality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kyTH8xwBuc4?t=662
**Verse References:** BG 5.29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord is the best well-wisher of all living beings. Similarly his pure devotees are also the best well-wishers of all living beings."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna and his devotees are the best well-wishers of all living beings, contrasting this with the discriminatory mentality of non-devotees.

This is what is told in the Bhagavad Gita. Krishna is described in the Bhagavad Gita as surdasarvabhutana and the devotees are described as surdasarvadehina. Same meaning. Sarvadehina means all living beings in different forms of bodies. Sarvabhutana means all devotees. Sarvabhutana means all different living beings in different forms of bodies. Same meaning. So the Lord is the best well-wisher of all living beings. Similarly his pure devotees are also the best well-wishers of all living beings. So both the Lord and his pure devotees are similarly the well-wishers. That was the reason he vanquished the rule of the company of Duryodhana and established the rule of Maharaja Yudhishtira. It's not a question of just competency in ruling. Duryodhana was competent to rule. We can't say he was incompetent. But Duryodhana was lacking that devotion. Therefore Duryodhana was typically having different kinds of feelings in his heart for different categories of people. So and so is my enemy. So and so is my friend. So and so is my well-wisher. Somebody else is my competitor. All kinds of this enviousness. A mentality of differentiation based on externals without looking into the core of the heart that every living being belongs to Krishna. A non-devotee himself doesn't know that he belongs to Krishna. So how will he understand others connection with Krishna. He will never understand by himself if he continues to be in material existence.

---

He also wanted to protect Maharaja Pariksit who was lying in the embryo for the same reason. The successor to Yudhishtira is Pariksit Maharaj. The only surviving descendant in the entire Pandava era. He did not like the idea that the world should be without the Pandavas, the ideal family of devotees. Apandavam idam kartum. Apandavam. This Ashwathama wanted to finish off the last life in the Pandava family. So that the world will be without any descendants of the Pandavas. But Krishna did not like that. Because he wanted pure devotees to rule the world and continue to successively rule. Their descendants who are also pure devotees should successfully rule the world. That's what Krishna wants. And even preaching Krishna consciousness is the only way to do that. Everyone, especially the leaders, become Krishna conscious. That will do good to the entire world.
