### sincerity, surrender, Krishna's kindness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/vQVNU9ZAuXs?t=586
**Verse References:** BG 9.26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Supreme Lord is so kind that he does not show special favor only to a great philosopher."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of sincere surrender to Krishna, highlighting that women's natural inclination to bow down in reverence makes them easily accessible to Krishna's mercy.

And therefore, women in general, they offer obeisances to the Supreme Lord without any reservation. Now, this is a very important qualification for somebody to actually know and understand Krishna's personality. If somebody bows down to Krishna without reservation, yes, my dear Lord, you are the Almighty. You are the Supreme Personality of Godhead. I bow down to you. So, Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, that fourth point, you bow down to me, offer your obeisances to me. That is being pointed out by Srila Prabhupada. Women very easily, they agree to bow down to the Supreme Lord Krishna because he is the Almighty Supreme Lord. And because of this reason, Srila Prabhupada says, the Supreme Lord is so kind that he does not show special favor only to a great philosopher. As is mentioned in the verse, he knows the sincerity of purpose. Therefore, it is observed generally that in any religious function, women assemble in great numbers as compared to men. And this is not true just in some particular place or some particular sect of some religion, no. In every country and in every sect of religion, it appears that the women are more interested than the men in such religious functions where there is an assembly of religious minded people. This simplicity of acceptance of the Lord's authority is more effective than showy insincere religious fervor. Men may try to show off. We are great religionists. Or they may try to speculate or try to analyze or try to reason out. But it's not easy for them to understand Krishna. Krishna is beyond the range of understanding of even the most learned or greatest philosophers. But when women bow down with sincerity to the Supreme Lord, then Krishna reveals himself to such sincere souls without any discrimination. Without any discrimination. For Krishna, every living being is important as a spirit soul, as his own part and parcel. Krishna does not see the external bodily features. Krishna does not see that.

---
### soul vs. body, devotional practice, accessibility of Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/vQVNU9ZAuXs?t=770
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To understand Krishna is easily accessible for the sincere soul who follows Krishna's instructions."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by explaining that Krishna sees the soul within the body, regardless of its form, and that sincere devotion, particularly through simple acts like bowing down, makes Krishna accessible, especially for women who readily embrace this practice.

And why these different bodies? Only because the particular soul who is interested in enjoying this material world is given a particular body suitable for the enjoyment. So whether it is a body of a woman or a man or a devata or an animal or a bird or an insect or a reptile or whatever. 8,400,000 different varieties of bodies. But these are just meant for accommodating the desires to enjoy in this world. But as Krishna is concerned, he sees every living being within the body, whatever be the external form. He sees the living being who is actually a spirit soul, spiritual by nature, completely different from the body. Krishna sees the soul. And that is also the vision of pure devotees. They see living beings as spirit souls trapped in a body, trapped in a misconception that they are seeking some material enjoyment in different ways. So it is important that to understand Krishna is easily accessible for the sincere soul who follows Krishna's instructions, the instructions of the devotional literature in simply approaching the Deity form of the Lord and just bowing down, Krishna, you are the Supreme Lord and I offer my respectful, sincere obeisances to you just because you are the Supreme Lord and you deserve the greatest respect in this world. So just this alone is enough for Krishna to reveal himself as the Supreme Personality of Godhead to a sincere soul. And in this regard, women are fortunate because without wasting time in trying to speculate and differentiate between matter and spirit and trying to philosophize, they simply bow down and they are recipients very easily of Krishna's mercy by which they can understand Krishna. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.8.21 | 12-02-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### prayer, Krishna's names, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/00nTQBG_8_I?t=36
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.21
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Now every name referred to by Queen Kunti is very meaningful."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the verse SB 1.8.21 and explains the meaning of the names of Krishna used in Queen Kunti's prayer.

Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare, Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya, Narayanam Namaskritya, Naram Chai Vanarottamam, Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 21st verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Queen Kunti. Krishnaaya Vasudevaya Devaki Nandanayacha, Nanda Gopakumaraya Govindaya Namo Namaha. The meaning of this verse is, let me therefore offer my respectful obeisances unto the Lord, who has become the son of Vasudhara, Vasudeva, the pleasure of Devaki, the boy of Nanda, and the other cowherd men of Vrindavan, and the enlivener of the cows and the senses. Now every name referred to by Queen Kunti is very meaningful.

---
### Krishna's names, incarnation, meaning
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/00nTQBG_8_I?t=102
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Govinda means one who is enlivener of the cows and the senses."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the meaning of each name of Krishna mentioned in the verse, including Vasudeva, Devaki Nandana, Nandagopakumara, and Govinda.

Krishnaaya, the Supreme Lord Krishna, who is Vasudevaya, he has appeared as the son of Vasudeva, therefore he is called Vasudeva. So, Kunti is identifying this personality who has appeared as the son of Vasudeva, and therefore called Vasudeva, he is actually the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Krishna. Krishnaaya Vasudevaya Devaki Nandanayacha, he is also identified as the son of Devaki, Devaki, Devaki, he appeared as the son of Devaki and Vasudeva, Devaki as his mother and Vasudeva as his father. So, therefore Devaki Nandana, another name for Krishna. He is the same Supreme Lord who has appeared as the son of Devaki and Vasudeva. He is not an ordinary person, his birth is not ordinary, it is transcendental. Then, Nandagopakumaraya, Nandagopakumaraya, Nandagopakumaraya, Nandamaharaj was the king of the cowherd men in Vrindavan. So, Krishna also appeared as the son of Nandamaharaj and Yashodhamayi, as the cowherd woman, wife of Nandamaharaj in Vrindavan. So, he is also identified as Nandagopakumaraya, Kumara means a small boy, five-year-old boy. So, Krishna appeared as a cowherd boy also, as the son of Nanda and Yashoda. And, he is Govindaya Namo Namaha, Govinda. Govinda means one who is enlivener of the cows and the senses. This Go, the word Go means the senses, Go also means the cows.

---
### Krishna, devotion, senses
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/00nTQBG_8_I?t=231
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whenever the senses come in contact with Krishna or the cows, when they are in touch with Krishna, they become enlivened."
**Summary:** This segment explains how Krishna enlivens the cows and the senses, emphasizing the special relationship between Krishna and the cows, and between Krishna and devotees engaged in devotional service. It also introduces Srila Prabhupada's explanation that material qualifications are useless for understanding Krishna.

So, the cows and senses are enlivened by Krishna. Whenever the senses come in contact with Krishna or the cows, when they are in touch with Krishna, they become enlivened. That's why Krishna as the cowherd boy is always tending the cows. That's a very, very special thing. A very, very special relationship of love between Krishna and the cows. Or, between Krishna and the devotees whose senses are very nicely engaged in devotional service to Krishna because of which the senses become enlivened. So, Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport that the Supreme Lord, as has been explained in the previous verse by Kunti Devi, is unapproachable by any material assets. Whatever be the material qualification of any person in any position in this material world, those material assets are useless when it comes to understanding the personality of Krishna. I may be a very great philosopher, but that doesn't qualify me to know and understand the personality of Krishna. I may be the most charitable, beneficent person, that doesn't qualify me to understand Krishna. I may be a very powerful devata, that doesn't qualify me to understand Krishna. No material position is, or material assets are qualification for understanding Krishna.

---
### mercy, demons, incarnation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/00nTQBG_8_I?t=349
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna wants to show His causeless mercy."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how Krishna can be understood through His causeless mercy, His appearance in His original form, and His purpose in diminishing the influence of demoniac persons and protecting devotees.

Then how can Krishna be understood? He is understood when out of His unbounded and causeless mercy He comes down to this earth as He is. He comes in His original form. When we say Krishna, He appears in His original form. Not that He is assuming a human-like form. No. He is coming in His original form. His original form resembles a human-like form. But it is not a human form. It is not a material form. It is not a human form. And He does so just for the sake of showing special mercy upon His pure devotees. Pure devotees. Krishna wants to show His causeless mercy. Unbounded. That means there is no limit to how much mercy He showers on His pure devotees. Unlimited mercy. And also He appears to diminish the upsurges of the demoniac persons. These demons, they become too much troublesome. They want to create havoc in the life of religious people or saintly persons or law-abiding citizens. These demoniac persons, they are law-breakers. They are against God in general. They are sometimes too materialistic or they are atheistic or they are openly against God, like that. So all such demoniac persons when they become too much of a disturbance and they create too much trouble for the saintly persons, for the devotees, then Krishna appears and Vinashaya Chidushkrutam He destroys, He kills them and protects the devotees.

---
### incarnation, mercy, accessibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/00nTQBG_8_I?t=495
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Therefore, Krishna is more merciful than Rama."
**Summary:** This segment explains why Queen Kunti specifically adores the incarnation of Krishna above all other incarnations, highlighting Krishna's accessibility and mercy compared to other incarnations like Rama. It emphasizes how everyone in Vrindavan can directly approach Krishna and render service to Him.

Now, Srila Prabhupada says, Queen Kunti specifically adores the incarnation or descent of Lord Krishna above all other incarnations. Krishna appears in many, many different incarnations. Rama, Varaha, Narsimha, Vamana, etc. But among all the incarnations of the Supreme Lord, the one and only Supreme Lord, the incarnation in His original form as He is, the incarnation of Krishna is the topmost. Why? Because in this particular incarnation, Krishna is very easily approachable by everyone. So Prabhupada compares in the incarnation of Rama, He remained a king's son and from His very childhood, He became a king's son. But in the incarnation of Krishna, although He appeared as the son of a king, son of King Vasudeva, He at once left the shelter of His real father and mother, Vasudeva and Devaki, and went to the lap of Yashoda Mai to play the part of an ordinary cowherd boy in the holy land of Vrindavan. And that Vrindavan has become sanctified because of Krishna performing His childhood pastimes there, which are very, very, very pleasing to all devotees. Therefore, Krishna is more merciful than Rama. The incarnation of Krishna is more merciful than the incarnation of Rama. Why? In Vrindavan, everyone is a devotee of Krishna. Everyone can approach Krishna. Not only the parents of Krishna, Yashoda Mai and Nanda Maharaj, but the cowherd boys, the cows, the peacocks, the birds, the bees, the river Yamuna, the trees, the plants, the insects, every creature in Vrindavan is able to directly approach Krishna and actually render service to Him. In whatever way they can, they render service in their own way. And Krishna accepts it very, very mercifully. That is Krishna's greatness. So therefore, the scriptures declare Krishna's incarnation is the topmost because He is easily approachable by everyone.

---
### relationship, pastimes, Vrindavan
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/00nTQBG_8_I?t=680
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Nanda and Yashoda are more fortunate because they could relish the childhood pastimes of Krishna which Vasudeva, Devaki, Kunti, they could not relish."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Krishna's kindness to Vasudeva, Kunti's relationship with Krishna, and the fortune of Nanda and Yashoda in relishing Krishna's childhood pastimes. It also introduces the concept of Vrindavan as a prototype of Krishna's eternal pastimes in Krishnaloka.

Now, Srila Prabhupada also points out Krishna was very kind to Kunti's brother Vasudeva because he appeared as the son of Vasudeva. Now, because he appeared as the son of Vasudeva, Kunti was able to have that affectionate relationship with Krishna as being Krishna's aunt, father, sister. So, Kunti is able to address Krishna in paternal affection. She has all different kinds of relationships between the Supreme Lord and the devotee. So here, Kunti is able to affectionately address Krishna as her relative. But, Nanda and Yashoda are more fortunate because they could relish the childhood pastimes of Krishna which Vasudeva, Devaki, Kunti, they could not relish. They could not relish the childhood pastimes of Krishna. And among the childhood pastimes of Krishna, of course, all the childhood pastimes of Krishna and Vrindavan are very special. They are described by Srila Prabhupada here as prototypes of his eternal pastimes in original Krishnaloka. Krishna has got his personal abode in the spiritual world. That's called Chintamani Dhamma, Vrindavan Dhamma, Goloka Dhamma, different names, same abode of Krishna, Krishnaloka. But that Krishnaloka, eternally Krishna's pastimes is covered by or going on. And Krishna sometimes comes to this world to display those pastimes, the replica of those pastimes he enacts in this earthly planet, in the tract of land called Rajabhoomi, Rajabhoomi, Gokula Vrindavan.

---
### Vrindavan, invitation, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/00nTQBG_8_I?t=829
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is inviting us, come, join me, come with me to Krishnaloka and enjoy pastimes like these covered boys are enjoying with me."
**Summary:** This segment differentiates between Goloka Vrindavan in the spiritual world and Gokula Vrindavan on Earth, emphasizing the spiritual nature of the latter. It concludes with an invitation from Krishna to join Him in Krishnaloka by becoming devotees and chanting Hare Krishna.

So there are two Vrindavans, Goloka Vrindavan in the spiritual world and Gokula Vrindavan on this earth planet. But Gokula Vrindavan is not material. It is completely spiritual. Though ordinary eyes see it as a material, another material place, but actually it is not a material place. Now Krishna comes to Gokula Vrindavana with all his spiritual entourage and paraphernalia. Now Krishna's mother, Yashoda Mai, she also belongs to Krishnaloka. But she also comes. Nanda Maharaji belongs to Krishnaloka but he also comes. The covered boys, Shridham and Madhumangala, all the covered boys, they belong to the spiritual world but they also come with Krishna. That is for Krishna to display his wonderful, loving pastimes with all his devotees. And Krishna is inviting us, come, join me, come with me to Krishnaloka and enjoy pastimes like these covered boys are enjoying with me, like the covered men and covered women, the eternal associates are enjoying. You also come. That is why Krishna comes to display and to attract us. And Krishna invites so many ways. So it is up to us to actually accept this invitation and actually prepare ourselves to just, what is the preparation? Become devotees. Manmana abhava madbhakto madhyaji ma namaskuru Chant Hare Krishna. Become devotee of Krishna and go to Krishnaloka. Never again to come to this world which is filled with miseries. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.8.22 | 15-02-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### prayer, transcendental form, lotus
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FnrM5RkGbjg?t=35
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.22
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Each line has got the word Pankaja, means a lotus."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami discusses Queen Kunti's prayer to Krishna, focusing on the significance of the word 'Pankaja' (lotus) and its connection to Krishna's transcendental form.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing the 22nd verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This verse contains one more prayer by Queen Kunti. These prayers are being offered by Queen Kunti to Krishna when Krishna is just about to depart from Hastinapura to Dwaraka. Nama Pankaja Nabhaya, Nama Pankaja Maline, Nama Pankaja Netraya, Namaste Pankajangraye. This verse has been described by Srila Prabhupada as the vision of lotuses. Each line has got the word Pankaja, means a lotus. So the translation of this verse: My respectful obeisances are unto you, O Lord, whose abdomen is marked with a depression like a lotus flower, Pankaja Nabhaya, who are always decorated with garlands of lotus flowers, Pankaja Maline, whose glance is as cool as the lotus, Pankaja Netraya, and whose feet are engraved with lotuses, Pankajangraye. Angri means the bottom of the feet. Pankajangraye, and whose feet are engraved with lotus flowers.

---
### transcendental form, spiritual body, devotee
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FnrM5RkGbjg?t=151
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna's original form is a form which looks like a human form but it is not a human form it is completely transcendental original form of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Srila Prabhupada's purport, explaining the specific symbolical marks on the spiritual body of the Personality of Godhead that distinguish Him from other spiritual forms, emphasizing Krishna's transcendental nature and Kunti's perceived unfitness to see the Lord.

In the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains, here are some of the specific symbolical marks on the spiritual body of the Personality of Godhead, which distinguishes his body from the bodies of all others. We often hear the Supreme Lord has got transcendental spiritual form, Satchidananda vigraha, but his spiritual form is distinctly different from the spiritual form of the other devotees in the spiritual world. All the inhabitants have spiritual form, all the devotees in the spiritual world. Only the Supreme Lord and his devotees, nobody else is there, everyone is a devotee, but specifically there are some marks on the transcendental form or body of the Lord which distinguish him from the spiritual bodies of all others. So what are those specific marks are being described, four of them are described in this verse. These marks are all special features of the body of the Lord. Even though there is similarity in the form of the Lord and the living beings, the spiritual form, you're talking with the spiritual form, not the material form. These spiritual forms are similar, but still there are differences. The Lord may appear as one of us, but he is always distinct by his specific bodily features. These features of the Supreme Lord distinguish him from the other living entities. Krishna's original form is a form which looks like a human form but it is not a human form it is completely transcendental original form of the Supreme Lord. Srimati Kunti claims herself unfit to see the Lord because of her being a woman. In a previous shloka she stated so, 'Katham pasye mahi sriyeha,' this was the prayer by Queen Kunti in her previous verse. This is claimed because women, shudras, that is the worker-class people, and the dvija bandhus, who are the wretched descendants of the hierarchy classes, they don't come up to the dvija qualities or qualifications of the twice born, though they are taken birth in such a family. All such classes of people are unfit by intelligence to understand transcendental subject matter concerning the spiritual name, fame, attributes, forms, etc. of the supreme absolute truth. Now, this is a very, very specific description here.

---
### archavigraha, deity worship, mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FnrM5RkGbjg?t=373
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord descends or comes down to this material world to distribute favors to the fallen souls, including the above-mentioned women, shudras and vijabandhus."
**Summary:** The lecture explains that while women, shudras, and dvijabandhus may lack the intelligence to understand the Vedas directly, they can still perceive the Lord through the archavigraha (deity form) in temples, which is an incarnation of the Lord who descends to the material world to bestow blessings upon all fallen souls.

When it is said, 'sthreeshudra dvijabandhunam trai na sruthi gochara,' that sthreeshudra and dvijabandhu are unable to understand the sruthi, the Vedas, what does it mean? It means that they don't have sufficient intelligence to understand the transcendental subject matter concerning the spiritual name, spiritual form, spiritual attributes, qualities of the supreme absolute truth, Krishna. That is the sense in which it is said. That is a specific point that is made. Such persons, although they are unfit to enter into spiritual affairs of the Lord, can see him as the archavigraha. Archavigraha means the worshipable deity form, the archamurthi in the temples, especially in the temples. So, the archamurthi, the archavigraha, the transcendental form of the deity who descends on the material world just to distribute favors to the fallen souls. So, this archamurthi or archavigraha or the deity form is an incarnation of the supreme Lord in the material world who appears in a form which we can see with ordinary eyes, the deity form. So, the Lord descends or comes down to this material world to distribute favors to the fallen souls, including the above-mentioned women, shudras and vijabandhus. He comes specifically for favoring these people who are unfit, who don't have sufficient intelligence, who do not take interest in spiritual subject matter, who are in that sense unfortunate, but even they can become fortunate by simply visiting the temple and having darshan of the Lord. Because such fallen souls cannot see anything beyond matter, the Lord condescends to enter into each and every one of the innumerable universes as the garbhodaka shayi vishnu who grows a lotus stem from the lotus-like depression in the center of his transcendental abdomen. And thus Brahma, the first living being in the universe, is born.

---
### condescension, pure devotee, archavigraha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FnrM5RkGbjg?t=561
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord also comes down for delivering the fallen souls."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the Lord's mercy in descending to the material world and sending pure devotees like Srila Prabhupada to deliver fallen souls, explaining the significance of Pankajanabhi and the various forms the Archavigraha can take, all made from different elements.

See, the Lord's mercy upon us is that he personally comes. Here condescends means he agrees to come down to our level. We are in a lower level. We are in the material world. We are fallen souls. But he comes down. Though he has nothing to do with this material world, he doesn't belong to the material world. He belongs to the spiritual world. He is always in transcendence. But even when he comes down, he is unaffected by the material energy. He remains in transcendence. That is very, very important to understand, to note. So, he comes down personally. And he sends his pure devotees like Srila Prabhupada also, who also come down, who don't belong to this world. They belong to the spiritual world eternally. But they come down for the sake of delivering the fallen souls. The Lord also comes down for delivering the fallen souls. Therefore, the Lord is known as Pankajanabhi. The depression in the center of his transcendental abdomen, which is like a lotus. That is why he is called Pankajanabha. The Pankajanabhi Lord accepts the Archavigraha, his transcendental form in different elements, namely a form within a mind, a form made of wood, a form made of earth, a form made of metal, a form made of jewels, a form made of paint, a form drawn on sand. So, when he said form made of earth, it could be either clay form, or it could be a stone form. So, all these different elements, from these elements, a particular specific form is either carved out, or cast in the case of a metal, or chiseled from wood, a block of wood, just like Jagannath Baldev Subhadra. From neem wood, the forms are made, or the marble forms of Radha Vrindavan Chandra, or the metal form of Radha Krishna Chandra. These forms are specifically made according to Agama Shastra, according to the scriptures, which describe how to make the form of the Lord. All such forms of the Lord are always decorated with garlands of lotus flowers, especially lotus, because lotus is the most beautiful flower among all the flowers. And as soon as we see lotus, we should remember Krishna. We should remember Krishna. That's why he is always garlanded with lotus flowers. He has got a depression, lotus-like flower depression in the center of his transcendental abdomen.

---
### temple worship, deity, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FnrM5RkGbjg?t=771
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "A deity is a Sakshat Bhagawan in his transcendental form."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing the importance of a soothing atmosphere in temples to attract non-devotees, clarifying the difference between idols and deities, and encouraging neophyte devotees to visit temples, worship the Lord, and become Krishna conscious.

And it is said here by Srila Prabhupada, there should be a soothing atmosphere in the temple of worship to attract the burning attention of the non-devotees who are always engaged in material wranglings. Material wrangling means that the mind is absorbed in material affairs. And they are completely, in that sense, materialistic. But even their attention can be attracted towards Krishna simply by the soothing atmosphere in the temple where the beautiful deity form of the Lord is always nicely decorated with beautiful flower garlands, especially lotus garlands. The meditators worship a form within the mind. Therefore the Lord is merciful even to the women, shudras and vijabandhus, provided they agree to visit the temple and worship the different forms made for them. Such temple visitors are not idolaters. Idolaters means those who worship idols. They are not supposed to worship idols. Idol means an imaginary form of the Lord, whereas deity is the Lord himself incarnating in the form of the deity. That means the Lord is personally present. A deity is a Sakshat Bhagawan in his transcendental form, whereas idol is an imaginary form. So there is a difference between idol and deity. All the great Acharyas establish such temples of worship in all places just to favor the less intelligent. And one should not pose himself as transcending the stage of temple worship while one is actually in the category of shudras and women or less. All people who are neophyte devotees are not conversant with the science of Krishna consciousness should visit temple and actually worship the Lord, bow down before him and actually have darshan of the Lord and that way become very easily Krishna conscious. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.8.23 | 16-02-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### prayers, protection, divine grace
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n3VsxrCg9JQ?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.23
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "O Hrishikesha, master of the senses and lord of lords, you have released your mother Devaki who was long imprisoned and distressed by the envious king Kamsa and me and my children from a series of constant dangers."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with prayers and then transitions into an explanation of Queen Kunti's prayer to Lord Krishna, focusing on the protection He provides to Devaki and Kunti herself.

Om Agyanati Miran Dhasya Gyananjan Shalakaya Chakshurun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pachatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Devi Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi. In this session, we will be discussing from the 23rd verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Queen Kunti. Yatha Hrishikesha Khalena Devaki Kamsena Ruddhati Chiram Shucharpita Vimochitaham Cha Sahatma Javibho Tvayaiva Nathena Muhur Vipadganath. The meaning of this verse: O Hrishikesha, master of the senses and lord of lords, you have released your mother Devaki who was long imprisoned and distressed by the envious king Kamsa and me and my children from a series of constant dangers. Muhur Vipadganath.

---
### divine intervention, protection, demoniac nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n3VsxrCg9JQ?t=100
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna could not be killed as soon as he was born by Kamsa because Vasudeva was in anxiety about protecting Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the story of Devaki's imprisonment by Kamsa, the prophecy of Krishna's birth, and how Krishna was protected by Vasudeva, highlighting the contrast between the demoniac nature of Kamsa and the divine intervention to protect Krishna.

So in the purport, Shri La Prabhu Par explains, Devaki the mother of Krishna and sister of King Kamsa was put into prison along with her husband Vasudeva because the envious king was afraid of being killed by Devaki's 8th son. Who is Devaki's 8th son? Krishna. So there was a prophecy at the time of Devaki's marriage that the 8th son of Devaki will be killing Krishna. So just because of this prophecy, immediately Kamsa wanted to kill Devaki immediately. And it is at the time of her marriage, his own sister, just because there was a prophecy, an unseen voice from the sky. This is the nature of the demoniac. Demoniac people are always envious. Kaleena Kamsena by this envious king Kamsa. This king Kamsa, he killed all the sons of Devaki who were born before Krishna. Six children. Six children who were born. But Krishna escaped the danger of child slaughter because he was transferred to the house of Nanda Maharaj, Lord Krishna's foster father. So Krishna could not be killed as soon as he was born by Kamsa because Vasudeva was in anxiety about protecting Krishna. And Krishna instructed Vasudeva that you actually transfer me because you are always in anxiety about the protection of your children, which is natural for every parent.

---
### divine grace, devotion, helplessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n3VsxrCg9JQ?t=248
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The conclusion is that Krishna bestows more favor upon a devotee who is in greater dangers."
**Summary:** The segment shifts to Kunti Devi's story, emphasizing her widowhood and the dangers faced by her and her children due to Dhritarashtra's greed. It highlights Krishna's special favor towards Kunti because of her helplessness and dependence on Him.

Now as regards Kunti Devi, Shraddha Prabhupada says, Kunti Devi along with her children were also saved from a series of dangers. Kunti Devi, she was a widow when her children were very small. And so she was in the house of, in the palace of Dhritarashtra. But this Dhritarashtra who was very much desirous of enthroning his own sons, even though they did not have the right, the right to rule. Dhritarashtra did not have the right to rule. Neither his sons had the right to rule the kingdom of the whole world being ruled from Hastinapura. The seat of the King of Hastinapura was the seat of the Emperor of the entire world those days. So in spite of that, being very very greedy, greed of the Emperor of that era, greed for power, so they don't hesitate to do anything. So these Kauravas they always were attempting to kill the Pandavas but Krishna protected the Pandavas so that's what Kunti is telling that Kunti Devi along with her children were also saved from a series of dangers by Krishna. Srila Prabhupada says Kunti Devi was shown more favor because Lord Krishna did not save the other children of Devaki whereas he saved the children of Kunti Devi. Kunti had five sons and they were all saved. Kunti her five sons Draupadi all of them were saved by Krishna. Parikshit the grandson of Arjuna all of them were saved by Krishna. Why? This was done because Devaki's husband Vasudeva was living whereas Kunti Devi was a widow and there was none to help her except Krishna. So what is the meaning of this? Prabhupada says the conclusion is that Krishna bestows more favor upon a devotee who is in greater dangers.

---
### attachment, Krishna consciousness, material existence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n3VsxrCg9JQ?t=436
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Sometimes, Krishna puts his pure devotees in such dangers because in that condition of helplessness, the devotee becomes more attached to the Supreme Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment explains that Krishna sometimes puts devotees in difficult situations to increase their attachment to Him. It contrasts material existence, where people rely on their own arrangements, with the reality that Krishna is always there to protect and foster devotion.

Sometimes, Krishna puts his pure devotees in such dangers because in that condition of helplessness, the devotee becomes more attached to the Supreme Lord Krishna. Actually, material existence is such that we forget Krishna and we think that we have to personally make our own arrangements for our protection because material world is full of dangers. But then the reality is Krishna is always there to protect us and Krishna wants us to depend on him for all protection so that we become attached to him. So that simply by developing our, reviving our memory of Krishna and developing our attachment, which is natural for us, because in our heart there is dormant Krishna consciousness, then we become eligible to transfer ourselves to the personal abode of Krishna where we actually belong. This is the whole description in the Bhagavad Gita. The more the attachment is there for the Lord, the more success is there for the devotee. See, due to material contact, we develop material attachments and that perpetuates our material existence. But if we develop attachment for Krishna and if that attachment increases more and more and more and more and more, just like it happened for Kunti.

---
### divine nature, Krishna's supremacy, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n3VsxrCg9JQ?t=544
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna agrees to become the son only of the most advanced devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the extraordinary nature of Devaki as the mother of Krishna, highlighting that Krishna only becomes the son of the most advanced devotee. He asserts Krishna's unparalleled supremacy, stating that no one is equal to or greater than Him, and refutes the idea that everyone is God.

So, Prabhupada is clarifying, Devaki is not an ordinary woman. Devaki, the devotee who became the mother of Krishna, was not an ordinary woman. After all, who can become the mother of the Supreme Personality of God? Krishna agrees to become the son only of the most advanced devotee. In their previous lives, Devaki and her husband Vasudeva underwent severe austerities and when Krishna therefore appeared before them wanting to give them a benediction, they told him they wanted a son. But where can there be a person like a god like Krishna like Krishna? But where can there be another person like Krishna? That is not possible. Krishna or God is Asaamardhva. That is, no one is equal to him. No one is greater than him. Everyone is God. No. One who says this, Prabhupada explains, he is actually totally ignorant about God. He is like an animal. He is like a dog. Prabhupada says, not God, but dog. Not G-O-D, but D-O-G, dog. For God is great and he is no competitor. No one is equal to him. Everyone is subordinate to him. Everyone is lower than him. And in the Chaitanya Charita Amritha, it is said, Ekale Ishwara Krishna Arsaba Bhrithya. Everyone is a servant. Only Krishna is the only master. Even the greatest of demigods like Brahma, Shiva and even Vishnu, of course Vishnu is not a demigod, but everyone is subordinate to him. Everyone is subordinate to him. Everyone else is a servant of Krishna.

---
### demigods, Krishna's power, material world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n3VsxrCg9JQ?t=670
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lord Krishna is offered respect even by Lord Shiva and Lord Brahma, the topmost of demigods."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the hierarchy of beings, from humans to demigods, emphasizing that even the most powerful demigods like Brahma and Shiva are subordinate to Krishna. It explains their roles in controlling aspects of the material world and highlights that their power comes from Krishna.

Shiva Virenchi Nutam, in the Shastra, the various scriptures, it is said, Lord Krishna is offered respect even by Lord Shiva and Lord Brahma, the topmost of demigods. Krishna is offered respect by everyone else. That's a fact. Now, above the beings, there are demigods. Demigods are empowered personalities in this world, material world, who are many, many, many millions of times more powerful than human beings. That's a fact. They control the wind. All over the universe, the wind is blowing. One of the devatas controls the sun planet, which is supplying heat and light to all the millions of other planets in this universe. So, they are universal level controllers. But even they are subordinate to Krishna. They are empowered by Krishna. The power is given to them by Krishna. Krishna is only all powerful. Everyone else is dependent on Krishna for their power. As we human beings are above the lower animals, above us, there are demigods, devatas. The most important of demigods are Lord Brahma and Lord Shiva. Lord Brahma is the creator of this universe. That is the power of Brahma. And Lord Shiva is the destroyer of this whole universe. As Lord Vishnu, who is Krishna himself in another form, for the sake of certain functions, Krishna appears in the form of Vishnu. So, Vishnu is the maintainer of the entire universe. So for the sake of this material world. There are three guna's. Satwa Guna, Raja Guna, and Tama Guna. lord vishnu takes charge of sattva guna brahma takes charge of rajoguna and shiva takes charge of tamo guna now these three controllers of these three gunas they are not under the influence of the gunas prabhupada gives the example just like a superintendent of a jail is himself not a prisoner even though he may be in the prison but he is there as the controller of the prison not as a prisoner similarly these three personalities brahma vishnu and shiva are transcendental to the mouths.

But Krishna is a supreme controller above everyone else and therefore he is known as Hrishikesha. Hrishika means senses and Isha means the master. Actually Krishna is the master of everyone's senses of all the senses so we have got senses and we think we control our senses actually we don't control the example is given i've got this hand i think i can lift my hand whenever i want but if there is the control of krishna is there that he withdraws the to move my hand that happens when I may get a paralytic stroke then I cannot move my hand independently so therefore Krishna is the controller of everyone's senses and therefore it is called vishikesha what does it mean it means that I must utilize my senses for Krishna's service my senses are not meant for my enjoyment or my utilization no they are meant for serving Krishna and that is the perfect situation proper situation as soon as we recognize Krishna is the controller of my senses I use it for Krishna's service I become completely free from material bondage I reinstate myself in the position of Krishna's devotee and I am eligible to go back to God I'll stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna.

### protection, devotion, divine intervention
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/YnNAfgvo_e4?t=52

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "My dear Krishna, Your Lordship has protected us from a poison cake, from a great fire, from cannibals, from the vicious assembly, from sufferings during our exile in the world."
**Summary:** Queen Kunti's prayer listing the dangers Krishna saved her and her sons from is introduced.

In this session, we will be discussing the 24th verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is a prayer by Queen Kunti to Lord Krishna: "My dear Krishna, Your Lordship has protected us from a poison cake, from a great fire, from cannibals, from the vicious assembly, from sufferings during our exile in the world. You have saved us from the forest and from the battle where great generals fought, and now you have saved us from the weapon of Ashwatthama." So, here is a list of different dangerous encounters that Kunti Devi, along with her sons, had to face.

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** Srila Prabhupada's comparison of Devaki's and Kunti's situations is explained, highlighting Kunti's continuous difficulties and Krishna's constant protection.

Srila Prabhupada explains this very nicely in the purport. The list of dangerous encounters is submitted here in Devaki, because in the previous verse there is a reference to Devaki was protected as much as Kunti was also protected. Prabhupada is comparing Devaki's situation with Kunti's situation. Devaki was once put into difficulty by her envious brother. Otherwise, she was well. Kunti and her sons were put into one difficulty after another for years and years together. See the difference? Devaki, after she was imprisoned and you see Krishna was born and Krishna was transferred to Vrindavan. And then, Devaki's problems were over. But, Kunti Devi and her sons were put into so many different dangerous situations, so many difficulties. They were put into trouble by Duryodhana and his party due to the kingdom. And each and every time, the sons of Kunti were saved by Lord Krishna. This is the reciprocation of the Lord with his devotees.

### poison, divine intervention, protection

**Key Quote:** "Bhima was given special medication to increase his strength to that of 10,000 elephants so Bhima became very very very strong and then the Nagas again brought him back to that river and left him on the bank of that river and then Bhima was missing from the palace for some days and Kunti was very worried and so."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Duryodhana poisoning Bhima, Bhima's subsequent rescue by the Nagas, and his return with increased strength.

Because the Pandavas were always protected by Krishna, without any doubt. So, it begins with visha. Visha means, once Bhima was administered poison in a cake. Bhima was administered poison in a cake. The Pandavas and Kauravas, they grew up in the same palace in Hastinapura. And Dhritarashtra was the caretaker king. And the grandfather of the Pandavas and Kauravas, Bhishma, he appointed Dronacharya to teach and coach the princes. Both the Kauravas, the hundred sons of Dhritarashtra and the five sons of Pandu, the Pandavas, 105 students Dronacharya had. And he coached them very nicely in military science because they were Kshatriyas. Now, in such training time, it was very clearly evident that Bhima was the strongest among all the 105 students. Now, it is said that Bhima was very successful. Duryodhana with an evil, asuric mentality, he decided, "Let me kill this Bhima." And he administered a trick. He organized a kind of a picnic for all the princes and they all went and they were playing and then he arranged that food be served in the picnic spot and he had made sure secretly that one of the items, the cake which was to be served to all the princes, Bhima's cake was poisoned. So Bhima was given that cake and he didn't suspect anything. He just ate it and after eating a little while he started feeling very very drowsy and Bhima tried to remain awake and somehow he was feeling extremely sleepy so he went off somewhere and just completely slept off. All the princes then were going back and suddenly the Pandavas, the five of them or four of them other than Bhima, they noticed Bhima is missing so they are searching and searching and searching and searching where is Bhima. What has Duryodhana done? He has arranged that secretly Bhima be thrown into a river. And because he was completely asleep, the effect of the poison was that Bhima was thrown into a river. He was poisoned so he was kind of unconscious and then he was thrown into the river he drowned in the river and he went to the bottom of the river he was carried away by the waves and he ended up in one of the lower regions of this universe.

Glover Yudhishtira Nakula Sahadeva Arjuna all of them were worried what happened and the Kauravas then Duryodhana knew of course what was going on but he went oh where is Bhima where is Bhima let's search for him. Let's search for him maybe he is somewhere in the woods maybe he is been taken by somebody else maybe he has gone away for hunting so many things this they just made a cover up and finally Bhima suddenly showed up. And then Kunti was very very pleased. Duryodhana was very apprehensive. How did he come back? What happened? Duryodhana did not know. Then Bhima revealed secretly that there is a plot to kill me. There was a plot and by Krishna's mercy I was saved. And Bhima in this way was actually saved from being killed by Krishna's arrangement. It was Krishna's arrangement. Then once they were put into a house made of shellac. Now each of these dangers that are mentioned, it is elaborately explained in the Mahabharata. So I will not be able to explain each and every particular danger like that. But in brief I may state that another attempt was made to burn the Pandavas alive. By sending them to a new palace that was called Kunti. It was constructed but it was made of combustible material, shellac. And Duryodhana's plan was to set fire when they were all settled inside nicely one particular night. So Vidura came to know about it and Vidura informed Yudhishthira that you are being sent to a palace which is going to set fire. Be careful. So Yudhishthira through Vidura's help, he dug a tunnel as soon as they went into the palace. He dug a tunnel. And before escaping through the tunnel, underground tunnel, Yudhishthira himself set fire to that palace. And then after that the Pandavas with Kunti escaped.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.8.26 | 20-02-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### humility, devotion, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/miGfEiJovzg?t=46
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One who is on the path of material progress, trying to improve himself with respectable parentage, great opulence, high education, and bodily beauty, cannot approach you with sincere feeling."
**Summary:** Queen Kunti reveals that material opulence hinders sincere devotion to Krishna, emphasizing the importance of humility in approaching the Lord.

In this session, we will be discussing the 26th verse of the 8th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Queen Kunti. The meaning of this verse is: One who is on the path of material progress, trying to improve himself with respectable parentage, great opulence, high education, and bodily beauty, cannot approach you with sincere feeling. So Kunti Devi is revealing a secret about the way to approach Krishna: the simple ways to call out Krishna's name and in this way directly come in contact with Krishna, approach Krishna. But there is a quality to such utterance of the name or calling out the name. One can approach with sincere feeling if they are materially exhausted. If they are puffed up with material opulence, then they cannot sincerely call out the Lord's name. And Prabhupada elaborately explains that in the purport.

---
### materialism, illusion, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/miGfEiJovzg?t=163
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Being materially advanced means taking birth in an aristocratic family and possessing great wealth, education, and attractive personal beauty."
**Summary:** Srila Prabhupada explains that material advancement, characterized by wealth, education, beauty, and aristocratic birth, leads to artificial pride and intoxication, hindering spiritual progress.

Srila Prabhupada says, being materially advanced means taking birth in an aristocratic family and possessing great wealth, Janma, Aishwarya, and education, Shruta, and attractive personal beauty, Shri, Janma, Aishwarya, Shruta, Shri. These four are described here. All materialistic men are mad after possessing all these material opulences, and this is known as the advancement of material civilization. The whole world practically is in ignorance about the spiritual truths, spiritual reality. Therefore, people are mad after material opulence. But the result is that by possessing all these material assets, one becomes artificially puffed up. We are being intoxicated by such temporary possessions. Why is it said artificially puffed up? Because there is no real asset to be proud of. It is simply some illusory possession that people become proud of. How long can you have your wealth? How long can you have bodily beauty? How long can you have material uprightness? Education only as long as the body lasts. At the most, after that, everything is separated from you. Can you take your wealth with you when you put the body to your next life? Can you take your material education? Can you take any material possessions? No, you cannot. So therefore it is described here, these material assets make one materially opulent, meaning artificially puffed up, artificially proud, and it's also intoxicating.

---
### chanting, sincerity, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/miGfEiJovzg?t=297
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By once uttering the holy name of the Lord, the sinner gets rid of a quantity of sins that is unable to commit."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the power of sincerely uttering the holy name of the Lord, contrasting it with insincere chanting driven by material satisfaction.

Consequently, such materially puffed up persons are not able to live in the material world. They are incapable of uttering the holy name of the Lord by addressing him feelingly, 'Oh Govinda, Oh Krishna.' You see the quality of uttering the name that is important also. It's not just that somebody is able to say the name. Srila Prabhupada explains a very important point here. He says it is said in the shastras, he's quoting from the scriptures, that by once uttering the holy name of the Lord, the sinner gets rid of a quantity of sins that is unable to commit. That means even once uttering the name of the Lord is so powerful, so effective, it can destroy any quantity of sin. It doesn't matter what the quantity of sin is. Such is the power of uttering the holy name of the Lord, chanting Hare Krishna, simply vibrating the tongue and producing the sound Hare Krishna. It's very, very, very powerful. Such is the power of uttering the holy name of the Lord. There is not the least exaggeration in this statement. What the shastras are telling, that any quantity of sin can be destroyed completely by just one utterance of the holy name, is not an exaggeration. It's a fact. It's a 100% fact. Actually, the Lord's holy name has such powerful potency. There's a secret that is known to the pure devotees and to those who are associated with the pure devotees. But there is a quality of such utterances also. It depends on the quality of feeling. So there is a difference between a sincere devotee calling out for Krishna by chanting his name, by uttering his name, and there is a difference between a sincere devotee and a person who is not so sincere but simply makes an external show that I can also chant. No, it's not going to be effective if one is not a sincere devotee, if one doesn't utter with feeling.

---
### helplessness, sincerity, spiritual advancement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/miGfEiJovzg?t=490
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A helpless man can feelingly utter the holy name of the Lord, whereas a man with great material satisfaction cannot be so sincere."
**Summary:** Sincerity in chanting comes from a feeling of helplessness, like a child crying for its mother, and material opulence can be a disqualification for spiritual advancement.

That is explained by Prabhupada: a helpless man can feelingly utter the holy name of the Lord, whereas a man with great material satisfaction cannot be so sincere. So sincerity is being explained specifically. What does it mean to be sincere in chanting Hare Krishna? It means to have within the heart the feeling of utter helplessness. Srila Prabhupada says in another place that just like a very, very small child cries for the mother, the child cries helplessly. So the mother is particularly attentive to the child who cries helplessly. Immediately she attends to the child. Similarly, when man utters the Lord's Holy Name in helpless state of feeling, then the Lord immediately appears and actually takes care of the devotee. A materially puffed up person may utter the Holy Name of the Lord occasionally, but he is incapable of uttering the name in quality. So quality of the Lord is the state of feeling that is not the state of reality is not there in the utterance by materially satisfied people. Therefore, the four principles of material advancement which are mentioned by Kunti Devi in this prayer, which are the four principles: high parentage, good wealth, high education, attractive beauty, are so to speak disqualifications for progress on the path of spiritual advancement.

---
### detachment, spiritual advancement, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/miGfEiJovzg?t=627
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "One should realize the worthlessness of all material opulences when one wants to actually take to spiritual life."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that while material possessions aren't inherently disqualifying, one must recognize their worthlessness for genuine spiritual progress, comparing material coverings to a fever that needs to be reduced.

Sometimes people think that I can make spiritual advancement and also be materially rich or opulent and materially educated, but actually the two don't go together. Though it's not a disqualification for somebody who has a lot of material wealth to engage in devotional service, they cannot engage in devotional service with the feeling that I am materially very, very satisfied because of my wealth or education or beauty or whatever. No, that is not possible. One should realize the worthlessness of all material opulences when one wants to actually take to spiritual life. In spiritual life, these are not positive qualifications, though they may be there or not there. Something else is required for spiritual success, and that is already explained by Prabhupada: utter helplessness. There is something mental, how to warfare between good and bad, of another political understanding that all these material opulences are useless for spiritual practice. The material covering of the pure spirit soul is an external feature. All the material opulences are like an external covering, as much as the body, our material mind, material intelligence, all these are external coverings of the spirit soul. Here is an example Prabhupada gives: as much as fever is an external feature of the unhealthy body, a very good example. The general process is to decrease the degree of the fever and not to aggravate it by maltreatment. If somebody gets fever, it is an indication of unhealthy body, so what should one do? One should try to decrease the fever, not increase it.

---
### detachment, spiritual advancement, illusion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/miGfEiJovzg?t=776
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The principle of life should always be to decrease the degree of material intoxication which leads one to be more and more illusioned about the aim of life."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by stating that material impoverishment can be a good sign of spiritual advancement, emphasizing the need to reduce material intoxication to realize the true aim of life and avoid illusion.

Similarly, it is sometimes seen that spiritually advanced persons become materially impoverished, and this is no discouragement. This is not discouraging if they become materially very poor after making spiritual advancement. On the other hand, such impoverishment is a good sign, as much as the falling of temperature is a good sign. Nobody wants to increase the fever. When somebody gets fever, they want to reduce it. Similarly, material wealth, there is no need of increasing material wealth when one spiritually advances. There's no need. If one's material wealth reduces, that's a good sign. It is not a sign of misfortune. No, the principle of life should always be to decrease the degree of material intoxication which leads one to be more and more illusioned about the aim of life. All these material assets, a high parentage and high education, material beauty, material wealth, all these are intoxicating, so that intoxication results in one becoming illusioned about the aim of life. So if somebody is really illusioned, then they are not fit for entrance into the kingdom of God. So we have to reduce this intoxication and become sober. Dheera, the Sanskrit word is Dheera. Krishna says Dheera Stratra Namukhyati. One who is sober, he or she is not bewildered by this material opulence or material wealth, etc. So I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.8.27 | 22-02-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### prayer, obeisances, humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JCME3k_SHaQ?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "My obeisances are unto you who are the property of the materially impoverished."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with prayers and obeisances, setting the stage for a discussion on Queen Kunti's prayers and the Lord's relationship with the materially impoverished.

Om Agyana Timiran Dhasya Gyananjan Shalakaya Chakshurun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnu Padaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatyade Shatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shri Vasadi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jaya Mudhirayet. In this session, we will be discussing from the 27th verse of the 8th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. This is the 8th chapter of the first canto of Shri Madbhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Queen Kunti. Namo Akinchanavittaya Nivritta Gunavrittaya Atma Ramaya Shantaya Kaivalya Pataye Namaha. The meaning of this verse is, my obeisances are unto you who are the property of the materially impoverished. You have nothing to do with the actions and reactions to the material modes of nature. You are self-satisfied and therefore you are the most gentle and you are the master of the monas.

---
### renunciation, materialism, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JCME3k_SHaQ?t=102
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Supreme Lord is the property of the materially impoverished."
**Summary:** This segment explains Kunti Devi's prayer, clarifying that the Lord belongs to the materially impoverished and that true renunciation involves giving up something for a greater spiritual gain, not simply becoming empty-handed.

So this is yet another prayer by Queen Kunti. It is a sequel to the previous two prayers. In the previous prayer, Kunti Devi had explained that people who are materially opulent are perfect, and they cannot feelingly utter the holy name of the Supreme Lord. But here, Kunti Devi is telling the Supreme Lord is the property of the materially impoverished, Akinchanavittaya. So Shri La Prabhupada explains in the purport that a living being is finished as soon as there is nothing to possess. What does it mean? One is akinchanavitha without any material possessions. So it is not to be misunderstood. So Prabhupada is clarifying the sense in which this is used by Kunti Devi, akinchanavitha. The Supreme Lord is the property of the materially impoverished. What is the meaning of that? How do we understand it? So a living being is finished as soon as there is nothing to possess. Therefore, a living being cannot be in the real sense of the term a renouncer. Sometimes people think I have to renounce. I have to give up. I have to give up my wealth. I have to give up my house. I have to give up my family. I have to give up my possessions. I had to give up my possessions, I had to give up my everything. But we should also understand that we cannot really be a renouncer in that way. Why? Because the principle of renunciation...

---
### renunciation, sacrifice, spiritual gain, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JCME3k_SHaQ?t=192
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A devotee renounces the material world, not for nothing, not to become empty handed, without any possessions, but for something tangible in spiritual value."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the principle of renunciation, explaining that it involves sacrificing something for a more valuable spiritual gain, using the examples of students and servants, and highlighting the Goswamis of Vrindavan who renounced worldly pomp for the service of the Lord.

The principle of renunciation is explained by Prabhupada as a living being renounces something for gaining something more valuable. For example, a student sacrifices his childish proclivities to gain better education. As children are sent to school, initially, they may be very, very playful. But as they become mature and serious, then they actually try to gain better knowledge. So, they are given up their playful tendency. Similarly, a servant gives up his job for a better job. So, in the same way, a devotee renounces the material world, not for nothing, not to become empty handed, without any possessions, you know, but for something tangible in spiritual value. So, devotee gives up material possessions to gain some spiritual, spiritually tangible value, something of spiritual value and it is quite tangible, it is not intangible. So, the example is given of the Goswamis of Vrindavan who were disciples of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. It is said here, Srila Rupa Goswami, Srila Sanatana Goswami and Srila Raghunath Das Goswami and others gave up their worldly pomp and prosperity for the sake of service of the Lord. Devotion is service. For Krishna Bhakti, for Krishna Seva for that reason. They were big men in the worldly sense.

---
### renunciation, detachment, wealth, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JCME3k_SHaQ?t=329
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Don't think that devotees are renouncing and giving up and becoming totally impoverished."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to illustrate the concept of renunciation by describing the high social status of the Goswamis before they renounced everything for Krishna consciousness, and then introduces the story of Sanatana Goswami and the touchstone to emphasize that devotees give up material wealth for spiritual wealth.

The Goswamis were ministers in the government service of Bengal. Rupa Goswami and Sanatana Goswami are ministers of Nawab Hussain Shah in the then government of Bengal during that time. And Srila Raghunath Das Goswami, who was the son of a big zamindar of his time. Very, very rich. His father was very rich. But they left everything to gain something superior to what they previously possessed. So, don't think that devotees are renouncing and giving up and becoming totally impoverished. They give up material wealth to gain some spiritual wealth. The devotees are generally without material prosperity, but they have a very secret treasure house in the lotus feet of the Lord. So, this treasure possessed by the devotees is unknown to materialistic persons. They can't evaluate. There is a nice story about Srila Sanatana Goswami. Prashupa tells a story. Now, Sanatana Goswami had a touchstone with him. A touchstone can convert any base metal to gold simply by touching. In Hindi they say paraspathar. In Sanskrit, they say chintamani. So, it is such a touchstone. If you touch iron with a touchstone, it become gold. So, Sanatana Goswami, he had a touchstone with him and he had left this stone, touchstone in a pile of refuse, in some garbage. He had thrown it in the garbage. A needy man took it.

---
### detachment, spiritual wealth, holy name, value
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JCME3k_SHaQ?t=466
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna Nama is the spiritual touchstone. It is not a material touchstone. It is a spiritual touchstone."
**Summary:** The story of Sanatana Goswami continues, highlighting the poor man's realization that the touchstone was not the most valuable thing Sanatana Goswami possessed, leading to Sanatana Goswami giving him the holy name of the Lord, which is the true spiritual touchstone.

So, somebody approached Sanatana Goswami, I am a very poor man. So, can you give me something so that I can get rid of my poverty? So, Sanatana Goswami said, you want some touchstone? It is there. You can take it. Where is it? Oh, it is in the garbage heap. So, this poor man, he took it and he thought, oh, now my poverty problem is solved completely. But later on, he wondered, why was this valuable stone, this touchstone kept in such a neglected place? Why was it kept there? He was an intelligent person also. He was considering that what kind of thing I have taken from the Goswami, from Sanatana. He therefore asked Sanatana Goswami for the most valuable thing. He was intelligent enough to understand, if touchstone is the most valuable thing Sanatana Goswami has in his possession, then he would not have left it in the garbage. Nobody leaves in the garbage something very, very valuable that they possess. So, why had he kept it in the garbage? So, therefore, he went and asked Sanatana Goswami that can you give me the most valuable thing in your possession? Sanatana Goswami said, yes, I can give you. And what did he give? Sanatana Goswami gave him the holy name of the Lord. That is why in one of the scriptures, it is said, Nama Chintamani Krishna. Krishna Nama is the spiritual touchstone. It is not a material touchstone. It is a spiritual touchstone.

---
### devotion, materialism, spiritual vision, renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JCME3k_SHaQ?t=586
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Material hankerings and spiritual advancement go ill together; they don't go together."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the meaning of Akeenchana, emphasizing that a true devotee gives the supreme asset, Krishna, and that material desires hinder spiritual progress. He uses the example of Sanatana Goswami to illustrate that material opulence and spiritual advancement are incompatible unless one has a spiritual vision.

Akeenchana means one who has nothing to give materially. This is the meaning of the word Akeenchana. It should not be misunderstood. A factual devotee or Mahatma does not give anything material to anyone. Just like Sanatana Goswami, he gave the holy name. That is not a material commodity. It is 100% spiritual. Now, Sanatana Goswami or any Mahatma, any factual devotee does not give material, anything material to anyone, because the Mahatma himself or the pure devotee himself, he has already left all material assets. He has given up himself. So, he does not have material assets to give you. He has given them up. He can, however, deliver the supreme asset. What is supreme asset with a pure devotee? It is the personal of Godhead Krishna. Krishna is the property of the pure devotees. Krishna can be delivered by a pure devotee. Krishna can delivers sorry because Krishna is the only property of a factual devotee. So Krishna can be delivered by a devotee to anybody who desires to have Krishna with the touchstone of Sanatana Goswami which is thrown in the rubbish was not the property of the Goswami otherwise it would not have been kept in such a place. So this specific example is given to neophyte devotees just to convince them that material hankerings and spiritual advancement go ill together; they don't go together. Material opulence and spiritual advancement don't go together unless one is able to see everything as spiritual in relation with the Supreme Lord. One must always distinguish between spirit and matter. A neophyte devotee should not artificially try to see the spirit and matter as one and same. A neophyte devotee should make distinction between what is spirit and what is matter, should make a distinction. A spiritual master like Sanatana Goswami although personally able to see everything as spiritual set this example for us only because we have no such spiritual vision. We have no such spiritual vision. We don't have spiritual vision at the present moment to understand that even matter being emanation from Krishna is also ultimately spiritual only but then a such a vision is not possible for a neophyte devotee in the beginning of Devotional Service so what happens is. what happens.

---
### materialism, spiritual advancement, entanglement, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JCME3k_SHaQ?t=794
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Material advancement is Anartha. It is not required."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by warning against the dangers of material advancement, which leads to entanglement and miseries, and emphasizes the importance of giving up material pursuits and taking to Krishna consciousness under the guidance of pure devotees.

When somebody advances materially, material advancement is civilization. Such material advancement is a great stumbling block for spiritual advancement. The more one becomes materially advanced, the more one becomes puffed up. And consequently, such a puffed up person cannot make spiritual advancement even if such a person takes to chanting Hare Krishna, because the chanting will be done without the sincere feeling. That is not possible for a materially intoxicated person. Such material advancement entangles the living being in the bondage of material body followed by all sorts of material miseries. See, material advancement comes with a price. What is that? With material advancement, there is entanglement and entanglement means material miseries. So, this has to be carefully understood. So, therefore, in Sanskrit, material advancement is called Anartha, unwanted things, unwanted. Actually, this is so. This is a fact. Material advancement is Anartha. It is not required. So, Prabhupada gives an example that in the present context of material advancement, one uses lipstick at a cost of 50 cents and there are so many unwanted things which are all products of material conception of life. So many material possessions people have. But that makes them only entangled. And subsequently, they have to face so many unwanted miseries. Everybody understands miseries are unwanted, but that's a result of possessing so many material things which are actually Anartha. So, Prabhupada's instruction is give up this business of material advancement and take to Krishna consciousness under the guidance of pure devotees. So, Prabhupada's instruction is give up this business of material advancement and take to Krishna consciousness under the guidance of pure devotees.
