## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.8.29 | 25-02-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### transcendental nature of Krishna, impartiality, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFSDaKHg3wM?t=34
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Kunti is helping us understand what is the nature of Krishna's personality."
**Summary:** This segment introduces Queen Kunti's prayer and explains that Krishna is transcendental, without enmity or special liking, and not partial, despite appearances.

Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare, Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya, Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchevanarottamam, Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 29th verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Queen Kunti. The meaning of this verse is, no one can understand your transcendental pastimes which appear to be human and are so misleading. You have no specific object of love nor do you have any object of envy. People only imagine that you are partial. This is a prayer. So Kunti is helping us understand what is the nature of Krishna's personality.

---
### transcendental nature of Krishna, material contamination, conditioned souls
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFSDaKHg3wM?t=104
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The very conception of the personality of Godhead as a human being is misleading."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on Krishna's transcendental nature, emphasizing that He is not a human being and is untouched by material contamination, unlike conditioned souls who desire material enjoyment.

Krishna is completely transcendental which means that he has no enmity. He has no special liking for somebody. He is not partial. It appears sometimes like he is partial to his devotees. It appears like that. So that will be explained here in the purport by Srila Prabhupada. The Lord's mercy upon the fallen souls is equally distributed. He has no one as the specific object of hostility. Now this point is very important. The very conception of the personality of Godhead as a human being is misleading. Krishna is not a human being. Krishna is the Supreme Unlimited Lord, full of all good qualities. He is absolute. He is all good. Every living being is his part and parcel and therefore every living being is dear to him, as the part and parcel living being. His pastimes in this material world especially appear to be exactly like a human being's activities, but actually they are transcendental. They are without any tinge of material contamination, because Krishna does not come in contact with material nature. The material modes are not able to influence Krishna. The material modes always are influenced by Krishna. Only the conditioned souls.

---
### material desire, devotional service, impartiality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFSDaKHg3wM?t=227
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Foolish people think that devotional service is something like flattering Krishna to get some special mercy."
**Summary:** This segment explains why conditioned souls are influenced by material modes, contrasting this with pure devotees who serve Krishna without expectation, using the analogy of the sun to illustrate Krishna's impartiality.

Why? Because the conditioned souls want to enjoy materially. Therefore the material modes will act on them. Only then they can try to enjoy, though there is no enjoyment. Now here one point Srila Prabhupada explains, that Krishna is known to be partial to his pure devotees, but actually he is not partial. He appears to be partial to his pure devotees. Just like Prabhupada gives the example of the sun. The sun is never partial to anybody. By utilizing the sun rays, sometimes even the stones become valuable, brilliant jewels, whereas a blind man cannot see the sun, although there are enough sun rays before him. So darkness and light are two opposite conceptions, but this does not mean that the sun is partial in distributing its rays. In spite of the sun being present in the sky, why is there darkness in some places? Because even though the sun rays are open to everyone, the capacity of the receptacles are different. Foolish people think that devotional service is something like flattering Krishna to get some special mercy, whereas the fact is that the Lord has so many unlimited number of pure devotees, and those pure devotees are always engaged in the transcendental loving service of the Supreme Lord Krishna, always. And they are not like a mercantile community. Now mercantile community, people are merchants. They render some service in exchange for value. They always want something in exchange. That's the nature of a commercial transaction.

---
### pure devotion, reciprocity, unconditional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFSDaKHg3wM?t=390
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You simply want to serve Krishna out of love, Krishna wants to provide you everything that you require out of love for you."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts pure devotees, who serve Krishna without expecting anything in return, with those who serve with material motives, highlighting the reciprocal relationship where Krishna provides for those who serve Him unconditionally.

So a pure devotee, even though he is always engaged in serving Krishna lovingly, he never does any service with expectation of something in return from Krishna. Never. Never. Never. Now just because the pure devotee renders loving service to Krishna, Krishna's full mercy is open for him. Then there are other people who serve the Lord but with a material motive, so when the purpose is over then they don't continue their service, just like somebody is in need of money and they approach Krishna and pray to him, 'I am in need of money, kindly help me.' So after Krishna fulfills their desire then they forget Krishna, how they are not continuing their prayer to Krishna. So such people they are conditionally rendering service to Krishna, they have a material motive, whereas pure devotees they render service to Krishna without any tinge of any material motive, pure service. So the Lord correspondingly is always taking care of them. It is reciprocal, you simply want to serve Krishna out of love, Krishna wants to provide you everything that you require out of love for you. You serve him unconditionally, Krishna takes care of you without putting any conditions. So it is not that Krishna is partial to the Lord and he performs service to Krishna.

---
### universal devotion, demoniac nature, mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFSDaKHg3wM?t=527
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The mercy of the Supreme Lord Krishna is open for everyone, all living beings, especially all human beings, but people are reluctant to receive the mercy of Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that Krishna's mercy is available to everyone, but some people, particularly those with a demoniac nature, are reluctant to receive it, contrasting pure devotees with mixed devotees who have material motives.

So Lord Krishna is so strong which only a traditional consumers are able to apply it to such people who render him loving transcendental service. No, he does not say some select people can become my devotee, can render service to me. He says everyone is my devotee, means everyone is Krishna's devotee, so everyone should render service to Krishna because you are his devotee, everyone without exception. But some people they are very much against Krishna, they are very very very demoniac natured, they envy Krishna, so such people it is very difficult for them to understand Krishna or relate to Krishna properly, it is very difficult. So Prabhupada concludes this description by telling the mercy of the Supreme Lord Krishna is open for everyone, all living beings, especially all human beings, but people are reluctant to receive the mercy of Krishna. Many times they refuse. So this is the difference between a pure devotee and a mixed devotee. Mixed devotee means there is some material motive for such a devotee to render service to Krishna, and pure devotee on their hand, me other hand means no material motive and without any material motive constantly engaged in Krishna's service always. So it is a very exacted position to constantly render service without any envy towards somebody or without any expectation of anything in return. It is very very very very rare. This is the difference between a pure devotee and pure devotional service. It's very rare.

---
### pure devotion, mercy, receptivity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFSDaKHg3wM?t=670
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Those who are cent percent engaged in devotional service to Krishna are the full recipients of the Lord's mercy."
**Summary:** This segment discusses who can receive the Lord's mercy, explaining that those completely against Krishna's service are in darkness, those who ask only in need are partial recipients, and those fully engaged in devotional service are full recipients, emphasizing the importance of chanting Hare Krishna.

But anybody associates with a pure devotee they can easily develop this pure devotion to Krishna. So Prabhupada explains in terms of who can receive the mercy of the Lord. Lord is freely distributing but who can receive. Prabhupada says those who are compelled to receive the mercy of the Lord are those who are compelled to receive the mercy of the Lord, who are completely against the service of the Supreme Lord Krishna are considered to be in abject darkness, they're completely in darkness. Those who ask for the Lord's favor only at the time of necessity are partial recipient recipients of the mercy of the Lord. The atheist, agnostics, demoniac natured people who never worship God, they are completely in darkness about Krishna and they remain in material existence till they change their ways. And those who are asking for the Lord's favor but they are rendering some devotional selves though it's not pure devotional service but still they're rendering some service to Krishna and asking for some help, they are actually partially partial recipients of the mercy of Krishna because how will Krishna reciprocate with them. They are asking for something, Krishna will give that. But devotees, pure devotees who don't ask anything from Krishna, Krishna provides everything they require. They are always protected by Krishna, always sheltered by Krishna, always provided for by Krishna. So the conclusion is that those who are cent percent engaged in devotional service to Krishna are the full recipients of the Lord's mercy. So it is just open for everyone. Anybody who sincerely serves Krishna, just sincerely chants, vibrates the tongue and says Hare Krishna, the Lord's mercy is available fully for such a person. But people refuse to even chant the name of Krishna.

---
### reciprocity, liberation, free will
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nFSDaKHg3wM?t=830
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "He is simply reciprocating with each person exactly according to that person surrender to Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment concludes by reiterating Krishna's impartiality and reciprocity, explaining that even those who die in Krishna's presence attain liberation, but ultimately, individuals have the free will to accept or reject the opportunity to return to the spiritual abode.

They will not chant the name of Krishna, so unfortunate people miss this opportunity. Even though when the Lord descends to this material world he plays like a human being, it appears like he is partial to his devotees, but he is not at all partial. He is simply reciprocating with each person exactly according to that person surrender to Krishna. That's all. Now in the battlefield of Kurukshetra, over died in the presence of Krishna, they got salvation without necessary qualification. Salvation means liberation without having even the qualifications, because when somebody dies in the presence of Krishna then they become purified of all sinful reactions. Therefore such a person is able to get some place in the spiritual abode of the Supreme God Krishna, provided they are willing to go to the supreme abode of the Supreme God. If people don't want to go, Krishna does not force. Sometimes people who don't have proper knowledge or information, they refuse to go to this about the Lord because only those who are purified can go and Appar.

### grief, family, Kurukshetra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oXd5gVY0iQ4?t=50
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.3
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "There sat the king of the Kurus Maharaj Yudhishthira along with his younger brothers and Dhritarashtra Gandhari, Kunti and Draupadi all overwhelmed with grief. Lord Krishna was also there."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of ego and begins discussing Srimad Bhagavatam 1.8.3, describing the scene after the battle of Kurukshetra, focusing on the grief of the family members and the presence of Lord Krishna.

In this session we will be talking about the process of getting rid of the ego. We will be discussing from the third verse of the eighth chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The third verse is as follows: Tatrasenam Kurupatim Dhritarashtram Sahanujam Gandharim Putrashokartam Pratham Krishnam Chamadhavaha. The meaning of this third verse of the eighth chapter of the first canto of Bhagavatam: There sat the king of the Kurus Maharaj Yudhishthira along with his younger brothers and Dhritarashtra Gandhari, Kunti and Draupadi all overwhelmed with grief. Lord Krishna was also there. Now Srila Prabhupada explains this situation that in the purport of the third verse of the eighth chapter of the ninth chapter of the third chapter Ganga Mahaprabha talks about the battle of Omneshwarinha in Kurukshetra as we read about in her report. Srila Prabhupada says that the battle of Kurukshetra was fought between family members and thus all affected persons were also family members like Maharaj Yudhishthira and brothers Kunti, Draupadi, Subhadra and Dhritarashtra and Gandhari. Her daughters in laws were on one side with Kauravas and on the other side there were. So, therefore, the description given by Srila Prabhupada here is, all the principal dead bodies were in some way or other related with each other. And therefore, the family grief was combined.

They had lost all different grades of relatives. This is described in the Bhagavad Gita in the beginning also. When Arjuna is surveying the battlefield against whom he has to fight, the enemy party, he sees all grades of relatives on the enemy side. Of course, on his side also there were relatives. But he is seeing the enemy side. There are so many grades of relatives. And he is overjoyed. And he is overjoyed with compassion for them. And he refuses to fight. And then Krishna instructs him in the Bhagavad Gita and tells him how he has to do his duty of fighting in this Dharma Yudha as a Kshatriya. So, after the battle, the family grief of the surviving family members on both sides, the grief was combined. Lord Krishna was also one of them as a cousin of the Pandavas and nephew of Kunti as well as brother of Subhadra, etc. See how Krishna is related in so many ways. He is the nephew of Kunti. He is a cousin of the Pandavas. He is the brother of Subhadra. Subhadra was the wife of Abhimanyu. Abhimanyu was the son of Arjuna. Abhimanyu died in the battle. So, Subhadra was... lost her son, Abhimanyu, not her husband. Subhadra's husband was Arjuna. Arjuna and Subhadra's son was Abhimanyu. Abhimanyu died in the battle. So, Subhadra lost her son, Abhimanyu. So, Krishna was the brother of Subhadra. The Lord... Who is the Lord? The Lord... Supreme Lord Krishna. Therefore, he was equally sympathetic toward all of them and therefore he began to pacify them befittingly.

### dharma, divine law, material nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oXd5gVY0iQ4?t=445
**Verse References:** —

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that the laws of nature are under the control of the Supreme Lord and cannot be altered. He defines Dharma as the laws given by God, a universal religion applicable to all, irrespective of background.

altered by any living entity the laws of nature are something which nobody can alter it nobody can alter it so therefore it is described here the living entities are eternally under the subjugation of the Almighty Lord now who is the maker of this laws it is Supreme Lord Krishna and who enforces it is Supreme Lord enforces it and therefore everyone is eternally under the subordinate control of the Supreme Lord who is the supreme control now it is clearly explained here by Srila Prabhupada the Lord makes all the laws and orders and these laws and orders are generally called Dharma or religion this is a very very important point what is Dharma or what is religion Dharma or religion means the laws given by the supreme law nobody else can make these laws you cannot have your own religious system as is fashionable nowadays no there is a universal religion or Dharma the real Dharma that is the laws of God which is acting on every living being irrespective of their belonging to some particular country or some religious sect or some belief system all that doesn't matter just like when death comes there is no consideration of whether this person is a Hindu or somebody is believing in God or somebody doesn't believe there is no such consideration when there is disease especially when there is a there's a epidemic or a pandemic there is no consideration whether somebody believes or does not believe these are laws stringent laws of material nature which are actually laws of God so no one can create any religious formula bona fide religion is to abide by the orders of the Lord.

in our material stage we can see neither ourselves nor the lord for want of spiritual vision in our present condition we lack spiritual vision this vision is simply some material vision which is not useful for seeing the spiritual reality we cannot see god we cannot because god is completely spiritual in form and we cannot see our self the real self inside this body we can't see anything spiritual but when we are liberated from material affection and when we are situated in the original spiritual form we can see both ourselves and the lord face to face so therefore what is called as mukti or moksha or liberation actually means to be reinstated in our original spiritual status after giving up the material concept of life we are all having a material concept that i am this body all the bodily connections are mine but we are not the body bodily connections are not mine so to give up this material conception is to practice dharma properly and become liberated from misconceptions material misconceptions therefore human life is specifically meant for qualifying ourselves for the spiritual liberty spiritual liberty real liberation is spiritual liberation materially however much people may try to become free the bondage is always there and because of the body and after this body is finished we don't die but we accept another material body so if you want to become free from this material covering material trap material entanglement then you should aim for spiritual liberation spiritual liberty.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.8.30 | 26-02-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### inconceivability of God, divine attributes, Kunti's prayers
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/3qsJNeMCsbg?t=50
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.30, BG 9.4, BG 9.16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though the Supreme Lord is all pervading super soul of all existence, still he appears in the form of a boar among animals, in the form of a human being among inhuman society as Rama, Krishna, etc."
**Summary:** Kunti's prayer highlights the seemingly contradictory nature of the Supreme Lord, who is both unborn and takes birth, active yet inactive, and manifests in various forms, which is ultimately inconceivable to the limited human mind, but can be understood through pure devotees.

In this session, we will be discussing the 30th verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Queen Kunti. Kunti Devi is saying, "Of course it is bewildering, O soul of the universe, that you work, although you are inactive, that you take birth, though you are the vital force and the unborn. You yourself descend among animals, men, sages and aquatics. Verily this is bewildering." In this particular prayer, Kunti is helping us understand something about the characteristics of the Supreme Lord, which seem to be self-contradictory. Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport that the transcendental pastimes of the Lord are not only bewildering, but also apparently contradictory. In other words, they are all inconceivable to the limited thinking power of the human being. What is the reality? We cannot conceive of a personality who is simultaneously unborn and still takes birth. He is active, yet he has nothing to do. So we cannot conceive what kind of a personality is that. Even though the Supreme Lord is all pervading super soul of all existence, still he appears in the form of a boar among animals, in the form of a human being among inhuman society as Rama, Krishna, etc., or he appears in the form of a rishi like Narayana Rishi, or he appears in the form of an aquatic like a fish, Matsyavatara. Still, Kunti is saying he is unborn. Even the scriptures declare Aja. In Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says Ajo Pisannabhya Yatma. He is Aja, he is unborn. And Name Parthasthi Kartavyam Trishulokeshu Kinchana. Krishna says in Bhagavad Gita, here also it is said, there is nothing to do. And even in the Vedas, Natasyakaryam Karanam Javidhyate. This is in Upanishads. So, if we properly hear from the right source, like pure devotees, like Kunti, and all the other Acharyas, then we can get some understanding about Krishna's personality as inconceivable for our tiny brain.

---
### divine forms, spiritual nature, Krishna as Vishwatma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/3qsJNeMCsbg?t=252
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Although he appears among animals like another animal, or he appears like a man, or a rishi, or a fish, he is eternally the Supreme Lord under all circumstances."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that everything is possible for Krishna, and his actions are extraordinary and bewildering to the common man; pure devotees understand that despite appearing in various forms, Krishna is eternally the Supreme Lord, and his activities prove this; Kunti addresses Krishna as Vishwatman, the vital force of the universe, present in everyone's heart and controlling the universal workings.

Everything is possible for him because everything is reconciled in his personality. Every action of the Lord actually is bewildering for the common man, because none of his actions are ordinary. They are all extraordinary. Lord means performer of extraordinary activities, always, not sometimes, always. Now in the Brahma Samhita it is said, he cannot be understood by Vedic knowledge, Vedesh Udurlabham, but he can be easily understood by the pure devotees, Adurlabham Atma Bhaktao. It is very easy to understand for the pure devotees. So, therefore, it is the pure devotees who know that, although he appears among animals like another animal, or he appears like a man, or a rishi, or a fish, he is eternally the Supreme Lord under all circumstances. And his activities prove that. Activities prove that. When the Lord appeared as a fish, he instructed Satyavrata. Satyavrata was a great king. He was a devotee also. So, the Lord instructed him in transcendental knowledge. You see. So, which fish will instruct transcendental knowledge to somebody else? That is because he is the Supreme Lord in that form. The form may appear like a fish, or a boar, or a human, or half man, half lion, all kinds of inconceivable forms, simply for the sake of his pastimes, he appears in such forms. But all of those forms are completely spiritual. There is not the least tinge of any materiality in any of those forms. So, therefore, Kunti addresses the Supreme Lord Krishna here as Vishwatman, the vital force of the universe. In everybody's body, there is vital force. There is Atma. But, just as this whole body works because of the presence of that vital force in the heart of the living being, similarly, the whole universe is working because Krishna has entered this universe as Vishwatma. So, he is the supreme vital force. Supreme vital force. We are all present in this universe, but we are not in the center of the universe. We don't control the universal working. We have no influence to do anything in this universe.

---
### transcendental birth, Krishna's omnipresence, understanding Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/3qsJNeMCsbg?t=486
**Verse References:** BG 4.9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is within everyone and everything, and he is outside also."
**Summary:** Krishna's actions are meant for our understanding, and though he is the supreme vital force, his birth is transcendental; understanding this leads to liberation; Krishna is both within and without everything, present in every atom and yet untouched by material nature; therefore, understanding Krishna requires hearing from devotees, not through ordinary logic or material observation.

But Krishna's actions, they are meant specifically for our understanding. If Krishna is present as the supreme vital force in the center of the universe, where is the question of his taking birth? So, he will not take birth. Why he has to take birth? So, therefore, in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna clarifies, I take birth and I perform activities, but they are all transcendental. They are not ordinary. Janma karma cha me divyam and evam yo veti tattvatah. One who understands this real nature in truth about the activities and appearance, or birth of Krishna, such a person, upon leaving the present body, need not take birth again in this material world, but instead, attains Krishna's eternal abode. In the beginning of our prayers, we said that Krishna is within and without everything. But still, he is invisible. Now, for example, Krishna is in everyone's heart. He is entered into every atom also. Andantrastha paramanu chayantrastha. Paramāṇu means atom. So, he entered into every universe. He enters into every planet. He enters into every living being's heart. He enters into every material atom. So, Krishna is within everyone and everything. And he is outside also. As Vishnu, he is outside of everything. He never comes in touch with the material nature, material world. So, he is outside of everything also. So, therefore, Krishna we cannot understand by some ordinary logic, by some material calculations, by descriptions given in terms of some material observation. That will never help you understand Krishna. We have to hear devotees like Venkati.

---
### Vishwaroopa, greatness of God, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/3qsJNeMCsbg?t=656
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is simultaneously greater than the greatest Mahato Mahiyan and he is smaller than the smallest Anoranyan."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Krishna's Viratrupa or Vishwarupa, explaining how the scriptures depict the universal form with hills as bones and oceans as holes; he emphasizes that God's greatness surpasses material understanding and warns against being misled by limited conceptions; Krishna is simultaneously greater than the greatest and smaller than the smallest, a characteristic that can be understood through hearing from proper souls.

Now, there is a description of Krishna's Viratrupa or Vishwarupa, one form of the Lord. The external body of Krishna as described in the description given of the Viratrupa or Vishwarupa. The hills and mountains are described as the bones of the Lord. So many hills are there. So many mountains are there. They are all described as the bones of the Virat Purusha. The great oceans have been described as the different holes in the Lord's universal body. The planet Brahmaloka has been described as the upper portion of his skull. Like this, the universal form has been described in so many ways. But then, the earth and the sky are intended to be the upper portion, because they are the set of the earth. The certain part of the earth and the sky is where the earth is. This is the only thing about which the universe is. Let us veer from the present state. In Brahma's view, the matter of the universe is to have run. What makes the universe and God's existence both greater than the matter of the universe. Now, it is said here, Brahma Shankar Prabhupada, there are people who can simply think of God as being great, but they do not know how great he is. They are thinking in terms of what they consider as great. So then they are misguided. So, then they are misled to believe some Devatas as God or some other powerful person they think is God. So, therefore, the Vishwaroopa description of the Lord, Supreme Lord is given in the scriptures that if you think of greatness of the Supreme Lord, think of the hills and mountains as the bones of the universal form. So, how many hills are there? How many mountains are there? You cannot count. So, this form is the universal form is very, very, very, very huge. Nobody can see. Arjuna was given special eyes to see that universal form. Otherwise, nobody can see this form of the Supreme Lord. So, Krishna is simultaneously greater than the greatest Mahato Mahiyan and he is smaller than the smallest Anoranyan. That is Krishna's wonderful characteristic. Simultaneously, he is greater than the greatest and smaller than the smallest. So, the Vishwaroopa is the biggest form of anyone in this entire universe. And the form of the Supersoul who has entered into every atom is much, much smaller than the atom. That form of the Lord is actually the Lord being smaller than the smallest Anoranyan. So, this is also greatness of the Lord. Simultaneously, he is present as greater than the greatest and smaller than the smallest. So, in this way, we hear from the proper souls, we can understand Krishna to some extent.

Om Agyana Timiraandasya Gyananjanishalakaya Chakshuranmelitam Jena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam. Devim Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Raiyet. Indusarayana. In this session, we will be discussing from the 31st verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Queen Kunti. Gopyada Detva Ikrataagasi Dama Tavad Yate Dashashru Kalilanjana Sambhram Aksham Vaptram Niniya Bhaya Bhavana Yastitasya Samam Vimohyati Bhirapiyat Vibheti. The meaning of this prayer by Queen Kunti: My dear Krishna, Yashoda took up a rope to bind you when you committed an offence and your perturbed eyes overflowed with tears which washed the mascara from your eyes and you were afraid. Though fear personified is afraid of you, this sight is bewildering to me.

---
### supreme lord, devotion, love
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WlHwTBnwFPQ?t=107
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "And when the Supreme Lord is reciprocating with his pure devotee, he also accepts the loving service of the devotee more relishably when the devotee is rendering that service out of pure affection, spontaneously, without any reverential admiration."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Srila Prabhupada's purport, emphasizing Krishna's eternal supremacy and the bewilderment caused by his pastimes. He highlights the pure devotee's unalloyed love and selfless service, where they forget Krishna's supreme position, and how Krishna relishes this spontaneous affection.

So, in the previous verse, Kunti Devi has said his activities, Krishna's activities are bewildering. So, she is giving an example of a particular pastime which is bewildering. So, Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, here is another explanation of the bewilderment created by the pastimes of the Supreme Lord. The Supreme Lord is the supreme in all circumstances as already explained earlier. It is not that sometimes he is supreme and sometimes he is not. No, always eternally supreme under all circumstances, all places, always, eternally. Here is a specific example of the Lord's being the supreme, at the same time a plaything in the presence of his pure devotee. The pure devotee being referred to here is Yashoda, Mother Yashoda. Now, we have to remember that the pure devotees are rendering service to Krishna only because of their unalloyed love, pure love. There is no expectation of anything in return. And while discharging such devotional service to the Supreme Lord, the pure devotee forgets that Krishna is actually the Supreme Lord. They forget that. And when the Supreme Lord is reciprocating with his pure devotee, he also accepts the loving service of the devotee more relishably when the devotee is rendering that service out of pure affection, spontaneously, without any reverential admiration.

---
### devotion, love, reverence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WlHwTBnwFPQ?t=246
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna as a Supreme Lord, one and only one Supreme Lord, he relishes more if his devotees render him spontaneous loving service, than when the devotees render him devotional service out of reverence for him as the greatest."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the devotion in Vaikuntha, characterized by reverential admiration for Krishna's opulence, with the spontaneous love in Goloka Vrindavan, where devotees forget Krishna's supreme position. Krishna relishes the latter more.

See, in spiritual world there are two kinds of devotees. The devotees in the Vaikuntha Loka are also pure devotees, as much as devotees in Goloka Vrindavan are also pure devotees. But the devotees in Vaikuntha, they have appreciation of the Supreme Lord, knowledge, understanding, as the supreme person full of all opulences, the greatest. So, they have reverential admiration for the Supreme Lord. Whereas in Goloka Vrindavana, devotees, they forget that Krishna is the Supreme Lord, and they render service to him simply out of their love for him. So, this is without reverential admiration. This is spontaneous love. This is different than what devotion that is expressed in Vaikuntha. Now, Krishna as a Supreme Lord, one and only one Supreme Lord, he relishes more if his devotees render him spontaneous loving service, than when the devotees render him devotional service out of reverence for him as the greatest.

---
### love, pastimes, subordination
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WlHwTBnwFPQ?t=349
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna accepts the chastisement by his devotees like his parents, more lovingly than he accepts the prayers of the Vedas, which glorify him in so many ways as the greatest."
**Summary:** The speaker provides examples of Krishna's friends and parents in Vrindavan, who are not conscious of his supreme position. He emphasizes that Krishna values the chastisement from his loving devotees more than the glorification from the Vedas, and that Krishna descended to show the pastimes of Goloka Vrindavana.

Now, Prabhupada gives examples here. The friends of Krishna, the covered boyfriends, they consider Krishna as one of them. They are not conscious that, oh, here is Krishna who is the Supreme Lord. You have to be very careful in our dealing with him. No. The parents of Krishna, they are like Yashoda, Nanda Maharaj, and elderly gopis and elderly gopas. In Vrindavan, they consider Krishna as a child only. Again, they are not conscious. Here is Krishna, who is the Supreme Personality of God, full of all powers. No. They forget that. Now, one important point here is that Krishna accepts the chastisement by his devotees like his parents, more lovingly than he accepts the prayers of the Vedas, which glorify him in so many ways as the greatest. So, therefore, Krishna, when he came to this material world, he wanted to show to the people of this world what are the kind of pastimes that go on between himself and his pure devotees in Goloka Vrindavana. He wanted to show that. So, in front of this material world, in front of his mother Yashoda, he displayed a unique picture of subordination. Though, he does not become subordinate. He plays the role of a subordinate child, but he does not become subordinate.

---
### pastimes, love, fear
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WlHwTBnwFPQ?t=477
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, this picture of Krishna is adored by Kunti Devi."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts the pastime of Krishna breaking butter pots and being threatened by Yashoda, leading to him crying and washing away his mascara. This display of apparent fear by Krishna, who is feared by fear personified, is adored by Kunti Devi.

So, Srila Prabhupada explains, in his natural, childish, playful activities, Krishna used to spoil the stock of butter that was kept by mother Yashoda, by breaking the pot of this butter and distributing it to his friends and monkeys. And that made mother Yashoda very angry. Why? Because, I have churned this butter for you. And what are you doing? Spoiling the stock of this nice delicious butter and distributing to the monkeys? So, simply out of love for her child, Yashoda wanted to make a show of punishment. So, what did she do? She took a rope and threatened to actually tie him up, tie his hands. So, Krishna, when he saw mother Yashoda is angry and she wants to tie him, he began to cry, just like a small child, ordinary children. When mother is angry, they also become very afraid and they begin to cry. Now, when tears were rolling down Krishna's eyes, they were washing the mascara, which Yashoda used to decorate his eyes with. Krishna is already very beautiful, but the devotees like to decorate him. So, the beautiful eyes, the mascara was being washed when he was crying out of fear of mother Yashoda. So, this picture of Krishna is adored by Kunti Devi.

---
### love, knowledge, yogamaya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WlHwTBnwFPQ?t=605
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is the loving exchange that Krishna relishes."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Kunti Devi is aware of Krishna's exalted position, while Yashoda is not, because Krishna uses Yogamaya to make her forget his supremacy so she can chastise him out of love. Krishna relishes this loving exchange, which is a unique kind of anger possible only in the spiritual world.

And Kunti Devi is simultaneously remembering this child who is crying as a son of mother Yashoda. She is, this child is not ordinary child. He is a Supreme Lord. And he is feared by whom? By Sakshatbhayamurthy, by the fear personified, who creates fear in everyone, in his entire universe creation. That Bhayamurthy is always afraid of Krishna. Then how can Krishna be afraid of Yashoda? So, Srila Prabhupada explained, this means Kunti was conscious of the exalted position of Krishna, whereas Yashoda was not conscious of the exalted position of Krishna as the Supreme Lord. Why? Because if Yashoda were to be conscious of Krishna's position, she could not have chastised Krishna. And it is said here that Krishna makes Yashoda forget that he is the Supreme Lord, so that Yashoda will not treat him as a Supreme, and thereby, she will actually, out of love, even chastise him. So, what does Krishna relish? It is the loving exchange that Krishna relishes. It is only out of love that mother chastises. Mother Yashoda chastises Krishna. It is not out of any real anger. It is anger of love. Anger of love. So, this is actually a different kind of anger than what we know as anger in this material world. It is only possible in the spiritual world, this kind of anger. And it is only because of the loving exchange between Mother Yashoda and Krishna. The same love, Mother Yashoda sometimes will kiss Krishna. Same love, she will embrace him. Same love, she will sometimes feed him. Sometimes she will send him for taking the cows for grazing in the forest. The same love, but variety of exchanges of rasa. This is subject matter of the Bhagavatam. Subject matter of one book written by Srila Rupa Goswami, Bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu.

---
### devotees, knowledge, love
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WlHwTBnwFPQ?t=780
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They all know Krishna is the Supreme Personality of God."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the exalted position of Krishna's pure devotees, clarifying that they are not ignorant of his supreme position, even when they forget it in their loving exchanges. He illustrates this with the story of Yashoda seeing the universe in Krishna's mouth and then being covered by Yogamaya again.

So, this is a very very exalted position of the devotees of Krishna. Pure devotees. They are among the topmost devotees. And don't think that because they forget Krishna as Supreme Lord, don't think they are in ignorance about Krishna's actual position. They all know Krishna is the Supreme Personality of God. They all know. Like it happens when Yashoda once told Krishna, I heard you have eaten some mud. Open your mouth. So, Krishna said, No, I have not eaten any mud. So, Yashoda said, Okay, okay, if you have not eaten, let me just see. Let me open your mouth. You open your mouth, let me just see whether there is any mud in your mouth. So, Krishna opened his mouth and then Yashoda began to see the whole universe in his mouth. So, then she became completely conscious of Krishna's exalted position as the Supreme Personality of Godhead. And she began to think actually under the influence of illusion, I am thinking this, Krishna is my child. Actually, Krishna is my child. Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead. So, then Krishna does not like Mother Yashoda to treat him as the Supreme Lord. So, therefore, Krishna covered Mother Yashoda with Yogamaya, which hides Krishna's greatness and supremacy. And Mother Yashoda after that said, Okay, okay, now don't ever again attempt to eat mud. I will give you nice sweets. Don't eat mud. Go and play. So, she came back to her position as the loving mother of Krishna, the small child Krishna. And this is the eternal position of Krishna in the spiritual world, in his loving exchanges with his devotees. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.8.32 | 28-02-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Krishna's appearance, Karma, Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FJHr2o0VsVc?t=60
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.32, BG 4
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is the master always, the controller, the Lord, and we are eternally the servant."
**Summary:** Queen Kunti glorifies Krishna as unborn yet born, explaining that while spirit souls are forced to take birth due to karma, Krishna incarnates by His own internal potency to reestablish dharma, protect the pious, and annihilate the impious, appearing out of His own will and not bound by karma.

From the 32nd verse of the 8th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Queen Kunti: kecidahur ajam jatam punya shlokasya keertaye yadopriyasyaanvavaye malayasyeva chandanam. The meaning of this verse is some say that the unborn is born for the glorification of pious kings, and others say that He is born to please King Yadu, one of your dearest devotees. You appear in his family as sandalwood appears in the Malaya hills. So here the description is being given by Queen Kunti glorifying the Supreme Lord Krishna as unborn is born. Krishna is unborn. Ajopisannabhya yatma. Krishna has a transcendental form which never deteriorates and He is always unborn. He eternally exists in the spiritual world. Eternally He is the controller of everyone and everything in the entire cosmic manifestation. Still He incarnates. Incarnates means He takes birth in this material world. But He is born. He is born as the Supreme Lord. His position doesn't change. He appears in a transcendental form. He doesn't take birth like we take birth. Even spirit soul, the living entity is Ajop. He is unborn, but the spirit soul is forced to take birth, accept a material body, a temporary material body, because of the force of karma. Such karmic force does not act on the Supreme Lord because the Supreme Lord is the controller of all such laws of karma. He is transcendental always. He is the master always, and we are eternally the servant. Either we serve Krishna as devotees, or we serve the illusory energy as non-devotees. So servant we are, always. Krishna is the master always, the controller, the Lord. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport because the Lord's appearance in this material world is bewildering, there are different opinions about the birth of the unborn. In the Bhagavad Gita, the Lord says that He takes His birth in the material world, although He is the Lord of all creations and He is unborn. So there cannot be any denial of the birth of the unborn because He Himself establishes the truth, Krishna's own statement in the Bhagavad Gita. But still there are different opinions as to why He takes His birth. That is also declared in the Bhagavad Gita, in the fourth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. Krishna declares that He takes birth in this material world, and He also declares the purpose of such birth. He appears by His own internal potency. Krishna has got three kinds of potencies: internal potency, external potency, internal potency. So that internal potency is the spiritual potency. It is Krishna's personal energy. Potency means energy. It is His personal energy for His purposes exclusively. And when Krishna takes birth in this world, it is the action of the internal potency. Energies are working always, but we take birth under the external potency, under the material energy. Therefore we get a material body. Krishna does not accept the material body. So in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says He comes to reestablish the principles of religion, dharmasamsthapanarthaya. So how does He establish these principles? He speaks the Bhagavad Gita. He follows the rules and regulations, the rules and regulations of a householder while He was living in Dwaraka as the king of Dwaraka, by His own behavior, by His actions, by His words, by His teachings, by His instructions, He establishes, rather reestablishes the principles of religion. And He also comes to protect the pious, paritranaya sadhunam, and to annihilate the impious, vinashaya chadushkrutam. So three purposes clearly He says in the Bhagavad Gita for which He appears, or He takes birth in this material world. That is the mission of the appearance of the unborn. Srila Prabhupada uses this word appearance to distinguish Krishna's birth as transcendental. He is always present, but He becomes visible. Therefore we say He appears. His birth is not exactly birth. Birth means we are accepting a material body. Krishna does not accept a material body. Still it is said that the Lord is there to glorify the pious king Yudhishthira. Now Yudhishthira, one of the Pandavas, the eldest Pandava, he was a pure devotee of Krishna. Not only Yudhishthira, all the five Pandavas are pure devotees of Krishna. Kunti is pure devotee of Krishna. Draupadi is pure devotee of Krishna. All the members of the family of Yudhishthira, the Pandavas, are pure devotees of Krishna. So Krishna appears to glorify Yudhishthira. Yudhishthira is a pious king. He rules exactly according to the directions given in the Shastra, the scriptures. He follows the instructions of Krishna. He rules for the welfare of the entire world. He teaches the citizens what is the goal of life, and he guides them so that they may be delivered from this material existence. That's why devotees are required to rule this world. So Srila Prabhupada further says Lord Sri Krishna certainly wanted to establish the kingdom of the Pandavas for the good of all in the world. The Mahabharata describes when Pandavas were ruling, everyone was happy. There was plenty of supply of all the basic needs. That's described in the Mahabharata and the Bhagavatam both. King Yudhishthira's rule is very very good. So Prabhupada says when there is a pious king ruling over the world, the people are happy. That's a fact. When the ruler is impious, the people are unhappy. This is the opposite. So there cannot be happiness simply by some artificial arrangements. No. The people in this world can be happy only when they have a devotee ruling over them. Only such a devotee can ensure the people will be happy. In the age of Kali, in most cases, the rulers are impious, and therefore the citizens are also continuously unhappy. Kali Yuga is the age of quarrel, hypocrisy, and the rulers are generally impious. So people are not happy in Kali Yuga. But in the case of democracy, the system of government that we have at present in our country and many other countries also, the democratic form of government, Srila Prabhupada says in the case of democracy, the impious citizens themselves elect their representative to rule over them. Mostly people are not pious in Kali Yuga, and such impious citizens, they elect an impious person to rule. Therefore they cannot blame anyone for their unhappiness. When there is an impious ruler, people have to be unhappy. They cannot be happy. So they want to be happy. So who is to blame? They themselves are to blame because they elect their representative to rule. So Srila Prabhupada gives another point here that Maharaj Nala was also celebrated as a great pious king, but he had no connection with Lord Krishna. Therefore Maharaj Yudhishtira is meant here to be glorified by Lord Krishna. Maharaj Nala was also a pious king. He was ruling nicely. He was ruling nicely, but he was not a pure devotee of Krishna. So therefore Yudhishtira was a pure devotee of Krishna. He is meant to be glorified by the appearance of Krishna in this world.

---
### Krishna's independence, Analogy, Mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FJHr2o0VsVc?t=660
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The conclusion is that the Lord is ever unborn like the sun, and yet He appears as the sun rises from the eastern horizon."
**Summary:** Krishna also glorified King Yadu by appearing in his family, although He is independent and not obligated to do so, unlike conditioned souls bound by karma; Krishna's appearance is likened to sandalwood growing in the Malaya hills and the rising of the sun, emphasizing His eternal presence and merciful manifestation.

Krishna also glorified King Yadu, having taken birth in his family. So Krishna appeared in Yadu dynasty. Yadu was a former king, and from him the dynasty of kings is coming down. So Krishna's father Vasudeva was a descendant of the Yadu dynasty. Krishna appeared in that Yadu dynasty, the son of Vasudeva. So therefore it is said here Krishna is known as Yadava, Yadu Veera, Yadu Nandana. Many names are there like this, although the Lord is always independent of such obligation. Krishna is identified as a descendant of the Yadu dynasty, but Krishna has no obligation to appear in the Yadu dynasty. When we take birth in this world in a certain family or a certain lineage, we have no choice. We are forced to take birth according to karma. Krishna is not forced like that. So Krishna chooses where He wants to appear, how He wants to appear, as whose son He wants to appear. He chooses that. Therefore He is completely independent. He is not bound like us. So the example is given. Krishna is just like the sandalwood that grows in the Malaya hills. Trees can grow anywhere and everywhere, yet because the sandalwood trees grow mostly in the area of Malaya hills, the name sandalwood and the Malaya hills are interrelated. Sandalwood is famous. It has got very very nice fragrance, but such sandalwood trees grow mostly in the Malaya hills, and therefore Malayasyeva Chandanam. Sandalwood is known as Malayan sandalwood. So the example is given to help us understand. Krishna need not appear in the Yadu dynasty just to glorify his devotee King Yadu. Krishna appears in that dynasty. So Srila Prabhupada says the conclusion is that the Lord is ever unborn like the sun, and yet He appears as the sun rises from the eastern horizon. Now another example. The sun is in its orbit, and it is just rotating in its orbit, but to our eyes on the earth, it appears like sun is rising on the eastern horizon in the morning, and sun is setting in the evening, and there is a period of darkness. Again next morning the sun rises. So the sun appears to rise and set, but actually sun is always in its orbit in its position. So just as the sun is never the son of the eastern horizon, similarly the Lord is no one's son, but He is the father of everything that be. Krishna is the Lord of everything, everyone, but out of His causeless mercy, He appears as the son of Vasudeva, Devaki, as a descendant of Yadu dynasty, out of His mercy. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

### Krishna's appearance, divine purpose, incarnation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kF-oaFcfY74?t=52
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.34
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When he appears for apparently one purpose, actually he solves so many problems."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam and discusses the various reasons for Krishna's appearance, emphasizing that the Lord solves multiple problems even when appearing for a specific purpose.

In this session, we will be discussing from the 34th verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto, Srimad Bhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Queen Kunti. The meaning of this verse is: Others say that the world being overburdened like a boat at sea is much aggrieved, and that Brahma, who is your son, prayed for you, and so you have appeared to diminish the trouble. So yet one more reason why Krishna appeared. Krishna had appeared. Krishna had incarnated, and all these reasons are valid reasons. So the Lord is so expert, but that when he appears for apparently one purpose, actually he solves so many problems. All these problems are valid reasons, all the problems of all the people. That's the Lord's way of incarnation.

---
### Brahma's birth, Narayana's omnipotence, creation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kF-oaFcfY74?t=118
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Without spiritual contact, matter cannot create."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Brahma felt the earth was overburdened by demoniac forces and prayed for the Lord's incarnation. Srila Prabhupada explains Brahma is the direct son of Narayana, who incarnates as Garbhodakashayi Vishnu. Without spiritual contact, matter cannot create. Brahma was born on a lotus flower grown out of the transcendental abdomen of Vishnu. Vishnu is also known as Padmanabha, and Brahma is known as Atma Bhuvu because he was begotten directly from the father without any contact of Mother Lakshmi ji.

Here another reason is being given that Brahma, he felt that the earth was overburdened by demoniac forces and he was unable to deal with it, so he prayed for the Lord's incarnation, and the Lord appeared to diminish this burden. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains Brahma, or the first living being born just after the creation, is the direct son of Narayana. Narayana as Garbhodakashayi Vishnu. Garbhodakashayi Vishnu is a form of Narayana incarnating as Vishnu as the second Vishnu form in this world. Garbhodakashayi Vishnu, first of all, this Vishnu enters the material universe why? Because without spiritual contact, matter cannot create. We think that material changes are taking place automatically. No, nothing happens automatically. Behind matter, there has to be spirit. So Srila Prabhupada says, without spiritual contact, matter cannot create. This principle was followed from the very beginning of the creation. The supreme spirit, that is the supreme Lord, entered the universe, and the first living being, Brahma, was born on a lotus flower grown out of the transcendental abdomen of Vishnu. Garbhodakashayi Vishnu has got a lotus-like depression in his navel region. From that lotus navel, a lotus flower was born. Therefore, he is called Padmanabha. What I've heard, this name Padmanabha, he from whose navel nabi or nabha Padma, a lotus appeared. Brahma was born on that lotus. So Srila Prabhupada says Vishnu is also known therefore as Padmanabha. Brahma is known as Atma Bhuvu because he was begotten directly from the father without any contact of mother Lakshmi ji. Brahma is also known therefore as Padmanabha. Brahma is known as Atma Bhuvu because he was begotten directly from the father without any contact of Mother Lakshmi ji without any contact of Mother Lakshmi ji.

---
### Narayana's omnipotence, mayavada philosophy, transcendental nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kF-oaFcfY74?t=295
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Narayana is not an ordinary living being."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain Brahma's unique birth from Narayana without a mother, emphasizing Narayana's omnipotence. He then contrasts this with the mayavadi philosophy, which incorrectly equates all beings with Narayana, calling it blasphemy. Narayana is transcendental and never becomes conditioned or accepts a material body, unlike ordinary living beings.

Vishnu's consort is Lakshmi. Generally, anybody takes birth from a father and mother. That's our experience in this world. But Brahma, he was born directly from the father without any contact with the mother. Srila Prabhupada explains, Lakshmiji was present near Narayana engaged in the service of Lord Narayana. And still, without contact with Lakshmiji, Narayana begot Brahma. So, therefore Srila Prabhupada says, this is the omnipotency of Lord Narayana. Omnipotency means he is the father. He has got all power, all kinds of potencies, and those potencies are working. They are active, actively engaged in producing so many different changes. One who foolishly considers Narayana like other living beings should take a lesson from this. There are so many millions and billions of living beings including powerful devatas, but no devata can produce a son without contact between the male and female. Not possible. Never happens. Therefore, the conclusion is, Narayana is not an ordinary being. Narayana is not an ordinary living being. Especially the mayavadis. They wrongly think or consider everyone is Narayana. So, between sanyasis, they address each other as Narayana. When one mayavadi sanyasi meets another mayavadi sanyasi, he says, Namo Narayana. So, he is addressing the other sanyasi as Narayana. Then the other sanyasi will also address this first sanyasi as Narayana. This is blasphemy because Narayana is not an ordinary living being. Ordinary living being can never become Narayana. Narayana never becomes conditioned. In this material world, Narayana never accepts a material body. So, these mayavadis who accepted a material body, it is because they are jiva. They are ordinary living being. Living being is subject to bewilderment by the illusory energy. Living being has to be, may be forced to take birth and accept a material body. But Krishna does not take such birth. Krishna does not accept material body. Narayana is always transcendental. So, the conclusion or the proper understanding is that.

---
### Narayana's potencies, Brahma Samhita, creation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kF-oaFcfY74?t=499
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Each part or each sense can act, and perform the activities of all other senses."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Narayana is the Personality of Godhead with all potencies in his transcendental body, referencing the Brahma Samhita. He emphasizes that Narayana can perform the functions of all senses with any part of his body, unlike ordinary beings. He reiterates that Narayana is not bound by material conditions and that Brahma, created by Narayana, is responsible for further creation in the universe.

Narayana is the personality of God in himself, and he has all the potencies of all the senses in all the parts of his transcendental body. So, this is a statement in the Brahma Samhita. As regards the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Brahma Samhita states, his different parts of the body are capable of doing the work of all other parts. Each part or each sense can act and perform the activities of all other senses. Just like Narayana can eat by seeing through his eyes. We can only see with our eyes. We cannot eat with our eyes. We cannot see with our mouth. We can only eat with our mouth. Like that. So, Srila Prabhupada explains, an ordinary living being begets a child by sexual intercourse, and he has no other means to beget a child other than the one designed for him. But Narayana, being omnipotent, is not bound to any condition of energy. Supreme Lord Narayana. He is complete and independent to do anything and everything by his various potencies, very easily and perfectly. Everything the Lord does, through his energies, through his potencies, easily and perfectly. Just by his will, things happen. That's all. Nothing more. And whatever he does, he does it perfectly. Brahma is therefore directly the son of the father and was not put into the womb of a mother. Therefore, Brahma is called Atma Bhuv, self-born, because he doesn't have a mother. Brahma doesn't have a mother. Doesn't enter a womb of a mother. No. This Brahma, who is thus created by Narayana without any contact with the woman, this Brahma is in charge of further creations in the universe. Further creations in the universe. So the initial work of creation is done by Vishnu, but afterwards, after Brahma is born, Brahma does the further work of creation.

---
### creation, Vishnu's forms, prayers to Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kF-oaFcfY74?t=679
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If Brahma were not empowered by Narayana, Brahma could not create."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Brahma's role in secondary creation, empowered by Narayana. He introduces Kshirodakashayi Vishnu, residing in Shwetadvipa, and explains how demigods approach Brahma for problems, who then consults Kshirodakashayi Vishnu. He recounts how Brahma and other devatas prayed to the Kshirodaka ocean due to Kamsa's troubles, learning that Krishna would descend as Vasudeva and Devaki's son. The devatas then prayed to Krishna in Devaki's womb, known as Garbhasthuti prayers.

And Prabhupada explains here, secondarily reflected by the potency of the omnipotent. What is the meaning of this? That Brahma does further work of creation. Like Brahma creates the Prajapatis. Now that is also due to the potency of the Supreme Lord, which is invested in Brahma to do the further creation. If Brahma were not empowered by Narayana, Brahma could not create. If Brahma were not empowered, he could not create. Within the halo of the universe, there is a transcendental planet known as Shwetadvipa, which is the abode of the Kshirodakashaya Vishnu. This is the third Vishnu form. Kshirodakashaya Vishnu. He is residing in a planet called Shwetadvipa, in which there is a milk ocean, and in the center of the milk ocean, there is an island. On that island, the Lord resides at Kshirodakashaya Vishnu, the maintainer Vishnu of this whole universe. Whenever there is trouble in the universe that cannot be solved by the administrative demigods, they approach Brahma. And if Brahma also cannot solve their problem, then Brahma consults and prays to Kshirodakashaya Vishnu, the maintainer Vishnu. So when Kamsa and others were giving trouble, then earth became overburdened by the misdeeds of these demoniac people. So Brahma, along with the other devatas, prayed at the shore of the Kshirodaka ocean, and they were advised that the Supreme Lord himself is going to descend in his avatar as Krishna. And they also knew that Krishna would appear as the son of Vasudeva and Devaki. So therefore, some people say the Lord appeared because of the prayers of Brahmaji. Brahma prayed. It's a fact. Brahma prayed, and he got the response from the Lord within his heart that the Supreme Lord himself will personally appear in the Yadu dynasty as the son of Vasudeva and Devaki. Therefore, when Krishna appeared in the womb of Devaki, then the devatas came to pray to Krishna within the womb of Devaki. It's called Garbhasthuti prayers, Tenth canto, second chapter. Beautiful prayers are there which are offered by the demigods to Krishna in the womb of Devaki. They knew because Brahma was informed.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Devim Saraswati Bhyasam Tatho Jayam Udhirayet.
