### justification, pacification, responsibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dT2QRHTaFRU?t=329
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna and these sages headed by Vyasa, they all tried to pacify and explain and convince Yudhishthira that Yudhishthira is not responsible for this killing."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna and other sages tried to convince Yudhishthira that the war was justified and that he was not responsible for the deaths, but Yudhishthira remained unconvinced.

Prabhupada further explains in the purport: This mass killing simply to enthrone Maharaja Yudhishthira was too mortifying for whom? For Yudhishthira. So, he tried to be convinced with evidences from histories by great sages like Vyasa and by the Supreme Lord Krishna himself, that the fight was justified, because the cause was just. So, justifying the fighting in spite of the huge number of people who died in this battle, in spite of that big number of deaths, the sages headed by Vyasa and Krishna himself, both of them, they wanted to pacify Yudhishthira and explained to him by historical evidence of previous instances where such big battles were fought for the sake of justice to prevail in this world. Duryodhana had occupied the post by cheating the Pandavas, by banishing them to the forest, by his so many tricks and by force, though Yudhishthira was powerful, but he was just being very morally upright in every instance that Duryodhana cheated the Pandavas, headed by Yudhishthira. So, after the battle, Krishna and these sages headed by Vyasa, they all tried to pacify and explain and convince Yudhishthira that Yudhishthira is not responsible for this killing, but Maharaj Yudhishthira could not be satisfied, even though he was instructed by the greatest personalities of his time. Somehow, Yudhishthira was not convinced, and that will be described in the rest of this chapter, towards the end of this chapter, how Yudhishthira was not convinced that this battle was justified.

### divine plan, superhuman action, Bhishma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dT2QRHTaFRU?t=495
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna's purpose of Yudhishthira not getting convinced is that Krishna wanted to perform still more superhuman action."
**Summary:** The speaker suggests that Krishna's inability to convince Yudhishthira was part of a larger divine plan, as Krishna wanted Yudhishthira to receive instructions from the dying Bhishma.

Now, Krishna is being described in these words as the performer of superhuman actions, Adbhuta Karmana, Krishnena, Adbhuta Karmana. But in this particular instance of trying to convince Yudhishthira, neither Krishna nor Vyasa could convince King Yudhishthira. They could not. So, does it mean Krishna failed to be a superhuman actor? The answer is no, certainly not. How do we understand this? Krishna did not fail in his attempt to convince Yudhishthira. Why? Because Krishna is the supreme lord. He is the Ishwara, supreme controller, and he is seated as a super soul in the hearts of both King Yudhishthira and Vyasa Deva. So, Krishna is in the background, but he is the supreme controller. He is the supreme controller. So, Krishna's purpose of Yudhishthira not getting convinced is that Krishna wanted to perform still more superhuman action. He wanted to perform still more superhuman action as super soul of King Yudhishthira. Krishna did not allow King Yudhishthira to become convinced by the words of Vyasa and others including himself, because Krishna's desire was that Yudhishthira should hear instructions from the dying Bhishma Dev, from the dying Bhishma Dev. Now, Bhishma was also another great devotee of the supreme lord Krishna. Also another great devotee of the supreme lord Krishna, even though Bhishma was fighting on the opposite side. Even though Bhishma was fighting on the opposite side.

Still, Bhishma had to fight on the side of Duryodhana. On the side of Duryodhana. Now the supreme lord Krishna also wanted that at the last stage of his material existence, that at the last stage of his material existence, the great warrior Bhishma Deva should see Krishna personally at the time of his quitting the body, and Bhishma should also see his beloved grandchildren, the Pandavas, and thus pass away very peacefully, because Bhishma Deva was not at all satisfied to fight against the Pandavas. Why? Because he knew Pandavas cause is just, to fight against the Pandavas when he knows their cause is just. So Prabhupada explains the reason why Bhishma Deva was constrained to fight on the side of the Kauravas. The reason is that Kshatriyas are very stern people, and therefore Bhishma Deva, he was obliged to take the side of Duryodhana. Why was he obliged to take the side of Duryodhana? Because Bhishma was maintained at the expense of Duryodhana.

### sacrifice, lineage, brahmacharya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dT2QRHTaFRU?t=749
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Bhishma actually was the one who was supposed to rule the whole world, but he voluntarily gave up for the sake of his father."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts the story of Bhishma's sacrifice, explaining how he renounced his claim to the throne and vowed to remain a brahmachari to allow his father to marry Satyavati, thus ensuring the lineage of the Kauravas and Pandavas.

Now elsewhere Prabhupada explains that actually the kingdom belonged to Bhishma Deva. How is that? Bhishma was the eldest son of the emperor Shantanu. And the emperor, when he actually retired, this Shantanu was a, he was a forefather of the Pandavas and Kauravas. When he retired, Bhishma should have taken charge of ruling the whole world as the emperor, but because Shantanu wanted to marry one daughter of a fisherman, one Satyavati. Hmm. It's a very long history. So, Shantanu wanted to marry the daughter of a fisherman, Satyavati, but Satyavati's father refused, because he calculated that the son born of my daughter through this emperor will not be able to inherit the post of the emperor, because the elder son has got a son through his first wife. So what is the use of marrying my daughter to this emperor. So he refused. This fisherman refused Shantanu. Shantanu said okay. But when Bhishma found out that my father has been refused, he went to the fisherman and said what is the reason you are refusing to marry your daughter to my father. So then the fisherman explained. Bhishma said I forsake the claim to the throne. The son born of your daughter will be the next emperor. Then Bhishma thought that the fisherman will accept. The fisherman said okay, the son born of my daughter will be the emperor, but after that son, your son will claim the throne of the whole world, because you are forsaking, but he may not forsake. So then Bhishma agreed to remain a brahmachari, not to marry, and that way Shantanu was able to marry that fisherman's daughter Satyavati. So, Bhishma actually was the one who was supposed to rule the whole world, but he voluntarily gave up for the sake of his father. Therefore Krishna, Bhishma is not actually a dependent, not really a dependent, but he assumed a humble position that Duryodhana maintained me, and so he fought on the side of the Kauravas. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.8.47-48 | 19-03-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### delusion, divine arrangement, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wmgRv-_hOD0?t=57
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Both Yudhishthira and Arjuna are pure devotees, so there is no question of them becoming deluded by Maya."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that King Yudhishthira's grief, like Arjuna's delusion, was arranged by Krishna to facilitate instruction on Dharma.

In this session, we will be discussing two verses, the 47th and the 48th verses of the 8th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of the 47th verse is: King Yudhishthira, son of Dharma, overwhelmed by the death of his friends, was aggrieved just like a common materialistic man. O sages! Thus deluded by affection, he began to speak. Suta Goswami is describing how Yudhishthira was aggrieved and how he expressed himself. So he was overwhelmed just like a common man. Now Srila Prabhupada explains something very significant in the purport. Srila Prabhupada says, King Yudhishthira, though he was not expected to become aggrieved like a common man, became deluded by worldly affection by the will of the Supreme Lord Krishna, just as Arjuna was apparently deluded on the battlefield of Kurukshetra before the war began, again by the arrangement of Krishna only. Both Yudhishthira and Arjuna are pure devotees. All the five Pandavas are pure devotees. So there is no question of them becoming deluded by Maya. So it is Krishna's arrangement for Arjuna to become deluded, so that Krishna could speak Bhagavad Gita for our benefit. Similarly, Krishna wanted Bhishma to instruct Yudhishthira, and through Yudhishthira, Krishna wanted Bhishma to instruct the whole world about the intricacies of Dharma. And for that purpose, he was able to tell the whole world about the intricacies of Dharma. Krishna arranged that Yudhishthira should become overwhelmed by worldly affection for the loss of so many people in this battle.

---
### detachment, body vs soul, duty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wmgRv-_hOD0?t=186
**Verse References:** BG 2.13, BG 2.22
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We are spirit soul, we are not the body, we are not the mind."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Prabhupada's purport, emphasizing the distinction between the body and the soul, the importance of occupational duties (swadharma/varnashrama dharma), and Krishna's plan behind Yudhishthira's delusion.

Shila Prabhupada explains in this purport, a man who sees knows well that the living entity is neither the body nor the mind, but is transcendental to the material conception of life. You have heard this many times in the Bhagavad Gita and all the scriptures it is explained, we are spirit soul, we are not the body, we are not the mind. Both the gross body and the subtle body are coverings for the real person within the body, that is the spirit soul. The common man thinks of violence and non-violence in terms of the body, but that is a kind of delusion. Just considering the body alone is a big mistake. Everyone is duty bound according to one's occupation. Occupational duties, swadharma or varnashrama dharma. A kshatriya is bound to fight for the right cause regardless of the opposite party. In this case, Yudhishthira was fighting against his own cousins, the Kauravas, but for the right cause, a kshatriya should not refuse to fight. That is what Krishna explains in the Bhagavad Gita to Arjuna also. In discharge of duty, one should not be disturbed by annihilation of the material body, which is only an external dress of the living soul. In the Bhagavad Gita in the second chapter, Krishna explains, just like this cloth we are wearing is simply an external covering for the real person within the dress. Similarly, the physical body is simply an external covering of the spirit soul, and when it is worn out, then one discards that. That is what is called death, discarding this body and accept a new body. All this was perfectly known to Yudhishthira Maharaj, but by the will of the Supreme Lord Krishna, he became just like a common man, because there was another great idea behind this delusion. Krishna had another plan, why Yudhishthira should become deluded.

---
### instruction, responsibility, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wmgRv-_hOD0?t=377
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.48
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "O my Lord, I am the most sinful man. Just see my heart, which is full of ignorance."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Krishna's plan for Yudhishthira to be instructed by Bhishma, then translates and explains verse 48, focusing on Yudhishthira's lament and the definition of an akshauhini.

What is that great idea? King Yudhishthira would be instructed by Bhishma, as Arjuna was instructed by Lord Krishna himself. This was the idea for Yudhishthira to become deluded because of the mass massacre. The 48th verse: Aho me pashyata ajnanam, hridhirudham duratmanah, parakyasyaiva dehasyah, aho me akshauhinir hatah. The meaning of this verse is, King Yudhishthira said, O my Lord, I am the most sinful man. Just see my heart, which is full of ignorance. This body, which is ultimately meant for others, has killed many many phalanxes of men. Phalanx means akshauhini in Sanskrit. Then the purport should be appropriate to explain what is the count of a phalanx or akshauhini division. A solid phalanx of 21,870 chariots, 21,870 elephants, 109,650 infantry, and 65,600 cavalry is called an akshauhini. Chariots here mean chariot fighters, and elephants means on elephant back there are powerful warriors who fight from elephant back, and infantry means those on foot, soldiers on foot, and cavalry means those who are on horseback, fighters on horseback. So, these four kinds of fighters are there in a battle in the Vedic times, in the Vedic culture. So, akshauhini division is very very big. 21,000 and odd chariots, 21,000 and odd elephants, 109,000 infantry, and 65,000 odd cavalry. So many. And like this how many divisions were there totally on the battlefield of Kurukshetra? 11 on the Kaurava side, 11 akshauhini divisions on the Kaurava side, and 7 akshauhini divisions on the Pandava side. Totally 18 akshauhini divisions were fighting in this battle of Kurukshetra. So, Prabhupada further explains.

---
### responsibility, piety, consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wmgRv-_hOD0?t=556
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This body is after all meant for others. It is meant for others. While there is life in the body, it is meant for the service of others."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining Prabhupada's purport, focusing on Yudhishthira's sense of responsibility for the killings and his understanding of the body's temporary nature.

Many akshauhinis were killed on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. That's a fact. Maharaj Yudhishthira as the most pious king of the world takes for himself the responsibility for killing such a huge number of living beings because the battle was fought to reinstate him on the throne. Yudhishthira is seeing from this perspective. After all Duryodhana was ruling very nicely, even though he was not the rightful claimant, but he was ruling. He was a Kshatriya ruling properly. He was guided by proper Brahmanas who were expert in state administration. So, just for the sake of me to become the emperor, so much of killing. It's not justified. So, Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport the thinking of Yudhishthira. What was his thinking like? This body is after all meant for others. It is meant for others. While there is life in the body, it is meant for the service of others, paropakaara. And when it is dead, it is meant to be eaten by dogs and jackals or maggots. After all the body will be when the soul leaves the body after death, the body is either burnt away to ashes in a fire, cremated, or it is buried and it will be eaten away by maggots underneath the earth, or some people just leave the body for being eaten by other birds or vultures or other animals. So, in any case, the body is going to be finished. So, but Yudhishthira is sorry because for such a temporary body, such a huge massacre was committed. In terms of Yudhishthira seeing from a perspective that after all the body is meant for doing some welfare activity while I am living, and what have I done? Just because I want to be the emperor, such a huge massacre. This is not at all justified, not at all justified. So, this is Yudhishthira's way of thinking. And further verses will explain how he is the most pious king and he is very sensitive to what has happened. So, Yudhishthira is behaving like this because he is a pure devotee.

---
### conscientiousness, welfare, purpose of life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wmgRv-_hOD0?t=744
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It's meant for doing welfare activity for the other people, paropakaara. That's what it is meant for."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Arjuna and Yudhishthira's reactions, highlighting the importance of using the human body for welfare activities (paropakaara) rather than sense gratification.

A pure devotee is very very conscientious. Arjuna, he is thinking, okay, we are all ready to fight, but what are the consequences of such a ghastly war? What are the consequences? He is thinking about it. Is it really necessary to fight in this battle? And whom am I going to kill? My own kinsmen? And for what? For just getting a throne, ascending a throne for ruling the world? And I am also simply going to fight against my own grandfather and teacher, Drona, Bhishma, who are my respectable superiors? I don't want to fight. This is the way Arjuna responded upon seeing the army of the Kauravas before the battle was about to begin. Now, Arjuna was overwhelmed before the battle began. Krishna spoke Gita, and Yudhishthira was overwhelmed after the battle. And both of them were instructed. Arjuna was instructed by Krishna, and Yudhishthira was instructed by Bhishma. Bhishma is a Mahajana. He is an authority. So, it is correct to say that after all the body is not meant for simply enjoyment as emperor or some enjoy some power. No, it's not meant for that. It's meant for doing welfare activity for the other people, paropakaara. That's what it is meant for. And a human being has got this sense of reasoning, developed intelligence by which he can understand what is the real purpose of this existence in this body. That a human being can understand. The lower creatures cannot. So, human beings should not simply utilize the body for sense gratification. That's the conclusion. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

### responsibility, consequences of war, leadership

**Key Quote:** "Though I live millions of years, I will not be relieved from the hell that awaits me for all these sins."
**Summary:** Yudhishthira Maharaj is overwhelmed by the massacre at Kurukshetra and feels responsible for the deaths, despite being the rightful heir to the throne.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Laram Chaiva Narottamam Deviim Saraswatiim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udhirayet. In this session, we will be discussing the 49th and 50th verses of the 8th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of the 49th verse is: 'I have killed many boys, brahmanas, well-wishers, friends, parents, preceptors, and brothers. Though I live millions of years, I will not be relieved from the hell that awaits me for all these sins.' So Yudhishthira Maharaj is affected by the massacre that happened in the battle of Kurukshetra. It was a World War. The whole world was divided into two parties: those on the side of the Pandavas and those on the side of Kauravas. The Mahabharata says 640 million people were dead at the end of 18 days of the battle of Kurukshetra. So when Yudhishthira Maharaj was killed by the massacre, he went to the scene of the battlefield. He saw the number of dead people, and he was overwhelmed with a feeling that I have been responsible for this entire killing because the whole battle was fought because of Yudhishthira Maharaj as the emperor of the world. Of course, it was the rightful claim of the Pandavas to rule the world after the death of Pandu, who was the ruling emperor.

### devotion, divine plan, material entanglement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lwNrAcADhF4?t=179
**Verse References:** BG 1.1
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Only a pure devotee can properly rule this world and actually guide the people to become Krishna conscious, to get out of this material entanglement."
**Summary:** Dhritarashtra's desire for his sons to rule led to Duryodhana's evil plans, but Krishna protected the Pandavas, intending for them to rule as pure devotees and guide people towards Krishna consciousness.

Dhritarashtra being the caretaker king while the Pandavas were very small, their father King Pandu, Emperor Pandu, he died untimely, early. So then Dhritarashtra was made the caretaker king because there was nobody else to take charge of ruling till Yudhishthira grew up and became sufficiently powerful. He was trained and an adult to actually take charge of ruling the kingdom, ruling the entire world. So Dhritarashtra as the caretaker king developed the desire that his sons, the Kauravas, headed by Duryodhana, should rule and not the Pandavas. So Duryodhana being evil-minded, it is described in the Bhagavad Gita, Dhritarashtra durbuddhe yudhe priyochikiri shavaha. Durbuddhi, evil-minded Duryodhana, he planned and plotted to kill the Pandavas and eliminate them before they could even become adults and could actually stake the claim for ruling the entire world. But Duryodhana was unsuccessful because the Pandavas, being pure devotees, were always protected by Krishna. It was Krishna's plan that the Pandavas should actually be ruling the entire world because they are the proper representatives of Krishna, the Supreme Lord, being pure devotees of Krishna. Only a pure devotee can properly rule this world and actually guide the people to become Krishna conscious, to get out of this material entanglement. That's the whole plan of the Supreme Lord in this material world.

---
### purpose of creation, liberation, spiritual guidance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lwNrAcADhF4?t=320
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The purpose of this creation is to give a chance to the conditioned souls."
**Summary:** The purpose of creation is to liberate conditioned souls from material bondage by realizing their spiritual nature and returning to the spiritual world, guided by a proper Kshatriya king.

Sometimes people wonder, what is the purpose of this creation? The purpose of this creation is to give a chance to the conditioned souls. All of us are called conditioned souls. We are conditioned by this material nature. We develop desires to enjoy, thinking that we are this body, we belong to this world. And our life continues birth after birth after birth because we have so many varieties of desires for enjoying. But actually Krishna's purpose is different. Krishna wants us to realize we don't belong here because we are spirit, soul, we are not the body. And as spiritual beings, we can never be really satisfied and happy by material pleasures, by material enjoyment, by material lordship. We can never be satisfied or happy. So Krishna wants us to realize this. And for this purpose, Krishna has created this world in such a way that in the human form of life, we have developed intelligence. And if we are guided on the spiritual path of emancipation, of liberation from material bondage, then very easily we are able to understand and act appropriately under proper guidance and become completely free from all entanglements and go back to our original position in the spiritual world to enjoy eternal life of bliss and knowledge. This is the whole plan of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. This is the purpose of creation: to liberate all the living beings, especially the human beings. And a proper Kshatriya ruling the human beings, ruling this world and particularly guiding the human beings on this path of liberation. It is possible that everyone can be liberated if they are guided by a king like Yudhishthira.

---
### war, sin, responsibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lwNrAcADhF4?t=462
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Yudhishthira Maharaj accepts responsibility for this mass killing because only for the sake of enthroning Yudhishthira the whole battle was fought."
**Summary:** A Krishna conscious king can guide everyone to liberation. Srila Prabhupada says war always involves the killing of innocents, which is a great sin. Yudhishthira accepts responsibility for the war's mass killing.

A Krishna conscious king. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada says, 'Whenever there is a war, there is certainly a massacre of many innocent living beings, such as boys, brahmanas and women, whose killing is considered to be the greatest of sins. Innocent people should not be killed. They are all innocent creatures and in all circumstances killing of them is forbidden in the scriptures.' Maharaj Yudhishthira was aware of these mass killings. Similarly there were friends, parents and preceptors also on both sides and all of them were killed. It was simply horrible for him to think of such killing and therefore he was thinking of residing in hell for millions and billions of years. Yudhishthira Maharaj accepts responsibility for this mass killing because only for the sake of enthroning Yudhishthira the whole battle was fought. And so many people were killed.

---
### duty, violence, devotee sensitivity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lwNrAcADhF4?t=533
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A pure devotee is very thoughtful, is very sensitive, he is very very careful in every action and he always considers the consequences of any action that he performs."
**Summary:** Verse 50 explains that a king has a duty to kill miscreants to protect the innocent, but Yudhishthira questions if this applies to him. He feels responsible for the sins of the war, highlighting the thoughtfulness and sensitivity of a pure devotee.

The 50th verse: naino rājña prajābhartur dharma-yuddhe vadho dviṣāṁ iti me natu bodhāya kalpate śāsanam vachaha. The meaning is, there is no sin for a king who kills for the right cause, who is engaged in maintaining his citizens. But this injunction is not applicable to me. A king has to engage in violence when there is a necessity of protecting innocent creatures, of protecting the weaker sections of society, of protecting women, of protecting saintly persons, of protecting brahmanas. Then he has to actually kill the miscreants. That's the duty of the king. But Yudhishthira Maharaj is thinking that even though a king has got a duty to kill the miscreants, but this killing in this battle is not applicable, is not proper for King Yudhishthira. That's what he's thinking. The Purport Shrila Prabhupāda says, 'Maharaj Yudhishthira thought that although he was not actually involved in administration of the kingdom, just being carried on well by Duryodhana without any harm to the citizens, he caused the killing of so many living beings only for his personal gain of the kingdom from the hands of Duryodhana. The killing was committed not in the course of administration but for the sake of self-aggrandizement, and as such he thought himself responsible for all the sins.' This is Yudhishthira Maharaj's thinking. A pure devotee is very thoughtful, is very sensitive, he is very very careful in every action and he always considers the consequences of any action that he performs. Before performing the action there is so much of thinking, there is so much of precautions taken.

---
### peace, negotiation, obstinacy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lwNrAcADhF4?t=667
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To avoid that world war Yudhishthira tried peace negotiations."
**Summary:** Yudhishthira tried to avoid the war through peace negotiations, even reducing his request to five villages, but Duryodhana's obstinacy made war inevitable.

As you are aware in the Mahabharata it is described that to avoid this battle, Yudhishthira Maharaj tried peace negotiations with Duryodhana and the Kauravas. In so many ways he tried to avert this battle. He did not like the idea of fighting against the Kauravas in order to get back his rightful claim for the kingdom. But Duryodhana was obstinate and he would not give the kingdom back to the Pandavas. Of course it is a very long story. Story in the sense it is a historical incident that really happened. It is not a fictitious story. Actually the Kauravas headed by Duryodhana, they usurped the kingdom of the Pandavas by cheating them in a gambling match. And after they promised to return the kingdom after the Pandavas had completed 13 years of exile living in the forest, when they came back Duryodhana refused to give back their kingdom. So then war became inevitable because a Kshatriya when challenged he cannot refuse to fight. So Duryodhana said you fight and defeat us and then you take your kingdom. So Kshatriya never begs for kingdom. Of course it was a rightful claim. Yudhishthira was not begging Duryodhana. But Yudhishthira did not like the idea of fighting and killing the Kauravas and so many others. He knew that it is going to be a world war. So to avoid that world war Yudhishthira tried peace negotiations. The Pandavas reduced their request for peace by averting the battle by just asking five villages for the five brothers. Even Duryodhana was not willing to give this much to the Pandavas.

---
### devotee simplicity, detachment, responsibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lwNrAcADhF4?t=820
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A devotee maintains his body simply for the sake of doing some devotional service to Krishna."
**Summary:** The Pandavas, as devotees, lived simple lives detached from material comforts, focused on serving Krishna. Despite Duryodhana's obstinacy, Yudhishthira still felt responsible for the war's massacre.

Why Pandavas were asking? Because they are duty bound to maintain themselves by ruling over citizens, by ruling over people. So they collect taxes and maintain themselves. So the Pandavas as devotees their life is very very simple. Anyway thirteen years they have spent in exile. They are so magnanimous souls. They are as pure devotees they are completely free from the desire for sense enjoyment. A devotee maintains his body simply for the sake of doing some devotional service to Krishna. That's all. He maintains his existence only for serving Krishna. And for serving Krishna what is required? Krishna is happy even if any devotee offers him in worship a leaf, a flower, a fruit or little water. So devotees live a very simple life and they are engaged in a devotional service. So the Pandavas did not have even though they were born in a royal family and they were living in a palace before they were sent to exile. They were living in royal comforts as Kshatriyas ruling half the world. But as devotees they did not want any of these comforts. So the Pandavas reduced their request and they asked Duryodhana for five villages and Duryodhana refused. So war was inevitable. So even though Duryodhana's obstinacy was the cause for the entire battle Yudhishthira is now thinking that this massacre that has happened he considers himself responsible and therefore he is very much feeling guilty of being the cause for this massacre, mass killing. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the fifth verse of the eighth chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining how Duryodhana usurped Yudhishthira's kingdom and the consequences of insulting Draupadi.

Om Agyanati Miran Dhasya Gyanan Janishalakaya Chakshurun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gaurabhakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Devim Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Jaya Mudirayet. In this session, we will be discussing from the 5th verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Shri Madbhagavatam. Sadha Itva Ajata Shatro Swam Rajyam Kitavay Ritam Ghatayitva Asato Ragnaha Kachasparsha Kshata Ayushaha. The meaning of this verse is: The clever Duryodhana and his party cunningly usurped the kingdom of Yudhishthira who had no enemy. By the grace of the Lord, the recovery was executed and the unscrupulous kings who joined with Duryodhana were killed by him. Others also died, their duration of life having decreased for their rough handling. The story of the hair of Queen Draupadi. In the purport, Shrila Prabhupada explains: Insulting the chaste lady Draupadi cost them their very life.

### Vedic Culture, Protection, Social Order
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oTK21_CjRFY?t=115
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In the Vedic culture, the King protects all the citizens impartially, but gives special care or protection for five categories of persons."
**Summary:** Srila Prabhupada explains that in Vedic culture, the king protects all citizens impartially but gives special care to five categories: brahmanas, cows, women, children, and old men.

It's very very dangerous and Prabhupada explains very nicely in the purport. In the glorious days or days before the advent of the age of Kali, the brahmanas, the cows, the women, the children and the old men were properly given protection. So, five categories of persons are mentioned who require special protection. Of course, in the Vedic culture, the King protects all the citizens impartially. But, in spite of having given them protection, in spite of being impartial to all the citizens, the king gives special care or protection for five categories of persons, the brahmanas, the cows, the women, the children and old men.

---
### Brahmanas, Cows, Spiritual Life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oTK21_CjRFY?t=194
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Cow's milk is especially meant for human beings in order to nourish their brain tissues to understand the spiritual goal of life."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on why brahmanas and cows should be protected, explaining that brahmanas guide society towards spiritual life, and cow's milk nourishes brain tissues for understanding higher aims.

Now why? That is explained by Srila Prabhupada very nicely. Number one, why protection should be given specially for brahmanas? It is explained here, the protection of the brahmanas maintains the institution of varana and ashrama, the most scientific culture for attainment of spiritual life. The brahmanas are the ones who give direction to the rest of the society for everyone to attain spiritual life. Having got the human form of life, everyone is eligible as a human being. So brahmanas are protected means rest of the society will get proper direction for spiritual advancement to attain spiritual life. Number two, why cows have to be protected? That is explained as follows. The protection of cows maintains the most miraculous form of food. That is milk for maintaining the finer tissues of the brain for understanding higher aims of life. Human intelligence is special, because a human being has developed intelligence to understand spiritual aim of life or higher aim of life than merely satisfying the senses in gross or subtle ways. So this higher aim of life is missed out if the brain tissues are not properly nourished. Therefore cow's milk is especially meant for human beings in order to nourish their brain tissues to understand the spiritual goal of life. And Srila Prabhupada many times has explained this point that cow's milk is a miraculous form of food. For whom? For the human beings, for the progressive human beings.

---
### Women, Children, Progeny
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oTK21_CjRFY?t=348
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The protection of women maintains the chastity of society by which we can get a good generation for peace, tranquility and progressive life."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the importance of protecting women and children, stating that protecting women maintains chastity and leads to a good progeny, while protecting children gives them the best chance to prepare for liberation from material bondage through garbhadana samskara.

Number three, this describes why women should be protected. The protection of women maintains the chastity of society by which we can get a good generation for peace, tranquility and progressive life. The future depends on the quality of the progeny that human beings are born with, producing now. Now how can we ensure that there is a good progeny? By protecting the women. Number four, why children should be protected? The protection of children gives the human form of life its best chance to prepare the way of liberty from material bondage. Such protection of children begins from the very day of begetting a child by the purificatory process of garbhadana samskara, the beginning of pure life. So, certainly in modern society many people do not know what is this garbhadana samskara or the beginning of pure life. That before a man and woman combine, they perform this garbhadana samskara or preparation of their consciousness in order to attract a spiritually advanced soul to take shelter of the parents as their child. So, in this way, good population will be there in the society, resulting in a good generation being produced for a good future.

### Animal Life, Human Life, Varna and Ashrama
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oTK21_CjRFY?t=576
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker contrasts human life with animal life, emphasizing that human beings should strive for spiritual life and a permanent solution to material problems through the varna and ashrama system.

Human life is no better than animal life if one is simply engaged in the same for animal activities ahara nidra bhaya maithunam cha samanyam etat pasubhi naranam there are four common activities for both the animals pasubhi and naranam and for the human beings what are the four common activities ahara food we all require food our living is based on food nidra sleep bhaya defense mechanism against some danger for the body and maithunam for procrastination creation there is a need of engaging in sex life. So these are common activities of the animals and human beings but a human being is distinguished because of possessing a developed intelligence by which the human being can make a permanent solution to all problems of material existence by attaining spiritual life. So for attaining spiritual life, there has to be a proper organized effort in the society, in the human society. And that organized effort is called the scientific system of varna and ashrama. Every human being is classified according to guna and karma to engage in a particular type of occupational activity so that they can contribute to the society and also get their means of livelihood.

### Brahmanas, Insult, Duration of Life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oTK21_CjRFY?t=696
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By insulting any of the five categories of persons, by insulting a brahmana or a cow or a chaste woman or innocent children or old men, by insulting any of them, our duration of life is shortened."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of brahmanas in guiding society towards spiritual life and explains that insulting any of the five protected categories shortens one's duration of life.

And importantly, all are guided by the qualified brahmanas in order to attain spiritual life by practicing certain basic principles meant for the human being, civilized human being. Now, Srila Prabhupada says, the killing of the above mentioned innocent creatures is totally for the benefit of the human being. It is forbidden because even by insulting them, one loses one's duration of life. We have a fixed duration of life when we are born, but this duration of life will not remain with us throughout our life intact. It is not going to remain intact. We can reduce our duration of life by insulting. By insulting any of the five categories of persons, by insulting a brahmana or a cow or a chaste woman or innocent children or old men, by insulting any of them, our duration of life is shortened. And here is an example that Prabhupada gives. In the age of Kali, they are not properly protected. And therefore, the duration of life of the present generation has shortened considerably.

### Women, Varna Sankara, Draupadi
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oTK21_CjRFY?t=824
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker explains that when women are not protected, unwanted children (Varna Sankara) are born, degrading society. He uses the example of Dushasana insulting Draupadi to illustrate how insulting a chaste woman shortens one's life.

Why are we seeing shortening of the duration of life nowadays? Because the above five categories of personalities are not given proper protection. That is well known. In the Bhagavad Gita, it is stated that when the women become unchaste, for want of proper protection, there are unwanted children called Varna Sankara. Varna Sankara means children born of unprotected women. Such children are actually born with so many demoniac qualities and they actually spoil the entire human society because of their bad behavior. And the whole society becomes degraded because of such unwanted children. So that is because women are not protected. To insult a chaste woman means to bring about disaster in the duration of life. Dushasana, a brother of Duryodhana, insulted Draupadi, an ideal chaste lady, and therefore the miscreants died untimely. All the Kauravas, 100 sons of Dhritarashtra, they all died simply because Draupadi was insulted in that assembly when there was a gambling match. So they tried to strip her naked and they were unsuccessful because Krishna protected Draupadi. But nonetheless, their duration of life was shortened and they all died untimely. Akala Mrtyu. So, duration of life is shortened when one insults a chaste lady.

### guilt, responsibility, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M73IIcYUqC0?t=61
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.51
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I have killed many friends of women and I have thus caused enmity to such an extent that it is not possible to undo it by material welfare work."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam (1.8.51) and explains Yudhishthira Maharaj's feelings of guilt and responsibility after the Kurukshetra war, despite his devotion.

In this session we will be discussing from the 51st verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Streenam Madhata Bandhunam Droho Yosav Ihotthitaha Karmabhir Grihamedhiyair Naham Kalpo Vyapohitum. The meaning of this verse is, 'I have killed many friends of women and I have thus caused enmity to such an extent that it is not possible to undo it by material welfare work.' This is continuing from the previous few verses where Yudhishthira Maharaj, after visiting the battlefield after the war was over, his son was killed. He had seen the massacre that has taken place. Millions, 640 million people were dead, and he is feeling very guilty that just for me to be enthroned as the emperor of the world, this huge killing has happened. Therefore he is expressing his feelings as a conscientious devotee. This is his behavior or his reaction as a devotee.

### materialism, sin, Grihamedhi
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M73IIcYUqC0?t=166
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A materialist is sure to commit sins even unintentionally in the course of discharging material duties."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the concept of 'Grihamedhi' as those focused solely on material prosperity, inevitably leading to sinful activities due to the nature of the material world.

He is assuming responsibility for killing different kinds of people as a result of this battle. So, Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, the Grihamedhis are those whose only business is to perform welfare work for the sake of material prosperity. There are two words that are used in the scriptures, Grihamedhi and Grihastri. Grihamedhi is a person who doesn't have any spiritual understanding of life, simply works on the basis of 'I am the body' and simply works for maintenance of the body, for giving pleasure to the body, for maintaining the body, and the false ego dictates that 'I am this body and all the body is mine.' The bodily connections are mine. This is the outlook of a Grihamedhi, and such a Grihamedhi, whatever work he does, he does only for the sake of material gain. He has no understanding of spiritual perspective of life itself. So, such Grihamedhis they perform welfare activity only for the sake of material prosperity. Now, these material prosperity is sometimes hampered by sinful activities. Why? Why is it hampered by sinful activities? Because a materialist is sure to commit sins even unintentionally in the course of discharging material duties. That's the nature of this material world. Even if a person knows what is sin and wants to avoid sinful activities he cannot avoid that simply by being engaged in material duties.

---

So it is said here to get relief from such sinful reactions the vedas prescribe several kinds of sacrifices. For unintentionally committed sins there is a prescription in the vedas. Different kinds of sacrifices are prescribed for those on the path of material gain through the scriptural directions. So further Srila Prabhupada says it is said in the vedas that by performing the ashwamedha yagna or horse sacrifice one can get relief from even brahma hatya, killing of a brahmana. Among all the types of sinful activities a person can commit in the human form of life the most grievous type of sinful activity is this killing of a brahmana, killing of a cow, and killing of a brahmana. Krishna is worshipped as a brahmana hita yacha. Krishna is worshipped as the topmost well-wisher of the cows and the brahmanas. The brahmanical culture, human society can progress spiritually only if this brahmanical culture is maintained and all saintly kings, raja rishis, maintain this brahmanical culture based to protect the brahmanas and cows especially.

### karma, enmity, war

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In war either the husband or the brother or even the father or sons go to fight when they are killed a fresh enmity is created and thus a chain of actions and reactions increase which is not possible to be counteracted even by thousands of ashwamedha yagnas."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Yudhishthira's thinking with the general efficacy of yagnas, highlighting the unique situation of war where endless cycles of enmity are created, making atonement difficult.

But Yudhishthira Maharaj's thinking is different. So Yudhishthira Maharaj actually performed this ashwamedha yagna in order to atone for unintentional sinful activities, but he thinks that even though by performing such yagnas one can generally become free from sinful reactions, he thinks that for him it is not possible to get relief even by performing ashwamedha yagna from the great sins he has committed. What happens when a war takes place? Srila Prabhupada explains in war either the husband or the brother or even the father or sons go to fight when they are killed a fresh enmity is created and thus a chain of actions and reactions increase which is not possible to be counteracted even by thousands of ashwamedha yagnas. In a particular battle one particular family people are killed, kshatriyas then the women are so much the surviving family members the women and children unprotected and then there is enmity. Lelakshmi may be described as the first family in violence but in the great days There is that a woman named lelaki or a woman called lelaki who is a nice lady and a very kind person the child killed her son left her child to his mother-in-law also with her husband and his wife the two lovers had a very good relationship and now they say that they are not sharing the battle and that is why they say that the FX is not that they can not make it in this way when they find out that they can not do it in their own way then the gravedigger of the vale will say that he has no power over them and son of Yadav is again leaving them alone after their loss become enemies of the new ruling king. Like this, this enmity continues and there is a chain of action and reaction.

### karma, devotional service, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/M73IIcYUqC0?t=556
**Verse References:** BG 9.27, BG 9.28
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Such actions and reactions on the path of work can be checked only when work is done on behalf of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the way out of the cycle of karma is to perform actions as an offering to Krishna, citing verses from the Bhagavad Gita.

So, Srila Prabhupada says the way of karma is like that. Material activity or work in this material world is like that. We are always entangled in a chain of action and reaction. It creates one action and another reaction simultaneously and thus increases the chain of material activities, binding the performer in material bondage. So, what is the way out of this chain of actions and reactions? In the Bhagavad Gita, Srila Prabhupada says, in the 9th chapter, 2 verses, 27th and 28th verses, the remedy is suggested that such actions and reactions on the path of work can be checked only when work is done on behalf of the Supreme Lord. Only by offering the work or doing the work on behalf of the Supreme Lord or offering the results of the work to Krishna, can one be freed from the reactions.

---

So actually Yudhishthira Maharaj is a pure devotee and it is Krishna's intention that a king like Yudhishthira Maharaj rules the whole world because he is the proper representative of Krishna in this material world therefore factually no sin whatsoever touched the Pandavas who were only the order carriers of the Lord. For the Pandavas there is no sin because they are pure devotees they do everything for pleasing Krishna they do everything on behalf of Krishna they carry out Krishna's orders. ## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.8.52 | 22-03-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### sacrifice, karma, responsibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E1Ol0Bu32FA?t=39
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.52
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As it is not possible to filter muddy water through mud, or purify a wine-stained pot with wine, it is not possible to counteract the killing of men by sacrificing animals."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam about the futility of trying to purify sinful acts with further sinful acts, particularly in the context of Yudhishthira's lamentation over the killings in the battle.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 52nd verse, the last verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Yatha Pankena Pankambhaha Surayava Surakritam Bhutahatyam Tathai Vaikam Nayajnehir Marshtum Arhati. The meaning of this verse is, as it is not possible to filter muddy water through mud, or purify a wine-stained pot with wine, it is not possible to counteract the killing of men by sacrificing animals. Again, this is Yudhishthira's lamentation that so many people were killed in the battle because he feels for his own sake the battle was fought and he is responsible for the killing.

---
### sacrifice, Vedic knowledge, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E1Ol0Bu32FA?t=126
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A sacrifice recommended in the scriptures is never meant for killing animals."
**Summary:** This segment explains that Vedic sacrifices involving animals were not meant for killing but for rejuvenating them, proving the efficacy of Vedic hymns, and that qualified brahmanas were needed to revive the sacrificed animals.

So, Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, Ashwamedha Yajnas or Gomeda Yajnas, sacrifices in which a horse or a bull is sacrificed, were not, of course, for the purpose of killing the animals. This is a very important point to be understood here. A sacrifice recommended in the scriptures is never meant for killing animals. Even though, actually, in such a sacrifice, a horse or a bull is killed, it is not meant for killing. How do we understand this? So, Lord Chaitanya explains that such animals sacrificed on the altar of Yajna were rejuvenated and a new life was given to them. See, in all such sacrifices, even though there is a horse or a bull, that killed horse or bull is rejuvenated by giving it new life. In some cases, the soul in the animal's body is promoted immediately to the human form of life. It was just to prove the efficacy of the hymns of the Vedas. In the Vedas, in the Vedic sacrifice, there are four kinds of priests. There is the priest who chants the mantras, and the mantra chanting should have the power, the purity of the brahmana, who has perfected his mantra chanting, because of which the chanting will be effective. And in case the brahmana who is chanting the mantras is unable to revive an animal, because his chanting is not sufficiently powerful, then there is a referee priest who actually takes over and actually he chants the mantras to revive the animal, the animal which is sacrificed, and he also chants for the sake of the sacrifice. So, the whole process is to give new life to an animal, and for the testing of the chanting of the mantra, such an act is done. Kill an animal and give it a new life.

---
### Kali Yuga, sacrifice, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E1Ol0Bu32FA?t=285
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In this age of quarrel and hypocrisy, that is Kali Yuga, there is no possibility of performing the yajnas perfectly, for want of expert brahmanas who are able to conduct such yajnas."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the importance of qualified brahmanas in performing sacrifices and how the lack of such qualified individuals in Kali Yuga makes proper execution of yajnas impossible, leading to negative karmic consequences.

By recitation of the hymns of the Vedas in the proper way, certainly the performer gets relief from the reactions of sins. In such sacrifices, when mantra chanting is done by qualified brahmanas, then the yajaman, the performer of the sacrifice, he gets relief from reactions of sins. But, in case of such sacrifices improperly done under inexpert management, surely one has to become responsible for animal sacrifice, like it is happening in Kali Yuga. They kill animals, but they don't have qualified brahmanas to chant mantras to revive the animal. There are no qualified brahmanas who can chant mantras in this way. In the scriptures it is explained, there is an arani mantra, which a brahmana who performs sacrifices, yajnik brahmana, chants. By chanting the arani mantra, he is able to generate fire by the power of the mantra. To light a sacrificial fire, a brahmana does not use a matchbox, or some other device, to spark a fire. No. He uses arani mantra. So, that's the power of sound. Subtle sound, vibration is like that. But, in Kali Yuga, we don't have qualified brahmanas who can chant in this way. This is one of the main reasons why all sacraments, all sacrifices of this kind, are particularly forbidden in the age of Kali. That's what Srila Prabhupada says here. In this age of quarrel and hypocrisy, that is Kali Yuga, there is no possibility of performing the yajnas perfectly, for want of expert brahmanas who are able to conduct such yajnas.

---
### Kali Yuga, meat eating, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E1Ol0Bu32FA?t=416
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Such meat eating is never pardoned."
**Summary:** This segment uses the example of Kali Prasad meat shops to illustrate how improper animal sacrifices in Kali Yuga lead to karmic repercussions for everyone involved, comparing it to a gang of conspirators committing murder.

So, Srila Prabhupada says elsewhere, that in Calcutta, there are some meat shops which sell animal flesh. Now, they say Kali Prasad. What is their, what is the reason why they call their shop as Kali Prasad, meat shop? Because, they make a show of killing a goat, and offering a piece of the goat flesh to Kali. But, there is no qualified brahmana who can actually chant the mantras to revive the goat in performing this sacrifice of killing the goat. So, in the absence of a qualified brahmana chanting properly the mantras to revive the killed animal, the killer and the person who actually partakes the flesh of the killed animal, actually six kinds of people are responsible for this killing, and are punishable by the laws of karma. The person who rears the goat, the person who transports the goat, the person who sells the goat, the person who kills the goat, the person who purchases the flesh, the person who cooks the flesh, the person who eats the cooked meat, all of them are punishable for the killing of the goat. It's exactly like a murderer is punished by state laws. Exactly like that. So, people are unaware. In modern times, people are unaware what is the kind of punishment that awaits them for indulging in such meat eating. Such meat eating is never pardoned. The animal killing involved in such meat eating. And not only the person who kills the animal, everyone else involved in that are all equally responsible for the killing. They are like a gang of conspirators who commit a murder. Exactly like that.

---

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In Kali Yuga, there is no possibility of anybody properly executing Ashwamedha Yajna or Gomidha Yajna or even the goat sacrifice to Kali."
**Summary:** This segment reiterates the impossibility of properly performing animal sacrifices in Kali Yuga due to the lack of qualified brahmanas and uses analogies of muddy water and wine-stained pots to illustrate the principle that sinful acts cannot be purified by further sinful acts.

So, in Kali Yuga, there is no possibility of anybody properly executing Ashwamedha Yajna or Gomidha Yajna or even the goat sacrifice to Kali, which is called Ashwamedha Yajna. So, anybody properly executing Ashwamedha Yajna or Gomidha Yajna or even the goat sacrifice to Kali, which is recommended in the scriptures for meat eaters, there is no possibility that they can do that properly because there is no qualified Brahman. Now, Prabhupada says further in purport, Maharaj Yudhishthira therefore gives a hint to performing sacrifices in the age of Kali. Yudhishthira Maharaj is telling that in the age of Kali, there is no possibility that simply because somebody has killed men by killing animals in a sacrifice, they cannot get rid of the sinful reaction of killing men. He gives examples, two examples he gives, just like it is not possible to filter muddy water through mud. So, you have to have a filter which is clean, which is free of mud. Then only you can filter muddy water and get clean water. But if the cloth itself has got mud, the filtering cloth, then how can you filter muddy water through that and get clean water? Similarly, he gives another example, you cannot purify a wine stained pot by washing it with wine. You have to wash it with pure water. Then only you can cleanse away the wine stained pot. Similarly, simply by sacrificing animals, they can purify water. So, you can purify water by washing it with clean water. Similarly, you can purify the blood stained pot with pure water. Similarly, you can purify water with clean.

---
### Harinama Sankirtana, sin, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E1Ol0Bu32FA?t=744
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One of the offenses is, do not perform any sinful activity on the strength of chanting Hare Krishna, thinking that even if a sinful person has committed a sin, by chanting Hare Krishna, his sinful reaction is nullified."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of avoiding sinful activities, especially the four regulative principles, and leading a responsible life while chanting Hare Krishna, explaining that chanting is not a license to sin.

Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu particularly taught this Harinama Sankirtana Yajna. This is what he taught. But one should not indulge in animal killing and counteract it by performing the Harinama Yajna. There are some offenses to be considered in chanting Hare Krishna, in performing Sankirtana Yajna. What are the offenses? One of the offenses is, do not perform any sinful activity on the strength of chanting Hare Krishna, thinking that even if a sinful person has committed a sin, by chanting Hare Krishna, his sinful reaction is nullified. That is only for unintentional, inadvertent sins committed in spite of taking all care to avoid sin. Only such care. So, a devotee who chants Hare Krishna should be very responsible, should lead a responsible life, should completely avoid all sinful activities, especially avoid four types of sinful activities in Kali Yuga, meat eating, intoxication, gambling and illicit sex. Completely avoid them. And be careful not to perform any other sinful activities. So, then only, while chanting Hare Krishna, if accidentally there is some sin committed, then Krishna will pardon. This sinful reaction is nullified by chanting Hare Krishna. Krishna pardons. Those who are devotees of the Lord never kill an animal for self-interest. For their personal sake, they never kill an animal. Never. And, as the Lord ordered Arjuna, they do not refrain from performing the duty of a Kshatriya. The principle of Ahimsa is, do not harm other creatures by indulging in violence.

---
### dharma, devotional service, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E1Ol0Bu32FA?t=874
**Verse References:** BG 2.31
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The whole purpose, therefore, is served when everything is done for the will of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment clarifies that devotees act according to the will of the Lord, citing Arjuna's duty as a Kshatriya and emphasizing that actions performed for the Lord's pleasure are free from sinful reactions.

But, that does not mean a Kshatriya. A Kshatriya will not do his duty of killing miscreants. That is within the purview of Dharma. Laws of Dharma. So, Krishna told Arjuna that by fighting in a Dharma Yudha, you will not incur sin. By not doing your duty. By avoiding. By not participating in a Dharma Yudha, you will incur sinful reactions. That is what Krishna tells Arjuna in Bhagavad Gita. That is being cited by Prabhupada here. The whole purpose, therefore, is served when everything is done for the will of the Lord. This is possible only for the devotees. Who acts according to the will of the Supreme Lord, it is devotees. Devotees' life is dedicated to Krishna. Therefore, they act only according to the will of the Lord. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### invocation, verse explanation, context
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/g_bpM5E5wRU?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 1.8.6
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lord Shri Krishna caused three well-predicted well-performed Ashwamedha yajnas or horse sacrifices to be conducted by Maharaj Yudhishthira and thus caused his virtuous fame to be glorified in all directions like that of Indra who had performed 100 such sacrifices."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with traditional Vaishnava prayers, followed by the reading and translation of Srimad Bhagavatam 1.8.6, focusing on Yudhishthira's performance of three horse sacrifices and the comparison of his fame to Indra's.

Om Agyanati Miran Dasya Gyanan Janishalakaya Chakshurun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnu Padaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pachatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Devim Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayamudhiya. Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam. In this session, we will be discussing from the 6th verse of the 8th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Yajayitva Ashwamedhaistam Tribhir Uttamakalpakaihi Tadyasya Pavanam Dikshu Shatamanyor Iva Tanot. The meaning of this verse is Lord Shri Krishna caused three well-predicted well-performed Ashwamedha yajnas or horse sacrifices to be conducted by Maharaj Yudhishthira and thus caused his virtuous fame to be glorified in all directions like that of Indra who had performed 100 such sacrifices.

---
### historical context, devotee glorification, Bhagavatam purpose
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/g_bpM5E5wRU?t=103
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So everything is glorious about the exalted Mahabhagavata Parikshit Maharaj."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the historical context of the verse, emphasizing Suta Goswami's narration of past incidents related to the Pandavas and Parikshit Maharaj, highlighting Parikshit's exalted status as a Mahabhagavata and the Bhagavatam's focus on the Lord's relationship with His devotees.

So the comparison is made here. Yudhishthira made, Yudhishthira performed only three horse sacrifices, but his fame equaled that of Indra who performed 100 Ashwamedha yajnas. Another name for Indra is Shatakrtu, one who has performed Shata, Shata means 100, 100 horse sacrifices. So how is this? Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, this is something like the preface to the performances of Ashwamedha yajna by Maharaj Yudhishthira. Now here we should remember Suta Goswami is describing a past incident, a historical incident about the Pandavas. This is actually based on the questions asked by the sages of Naimisharanya to give the background, how is it that Parikshit Maharaj met Shukadev Goswami and heard the entire Bhagavatam from Shukadev Goswami. So in order to describe the birth of this Parikshit Maharaj, the incidents leading to that are being described here because they will help us understand, better understand the fact that who is Parikshit Maharaj, how he is a Mahabhagavata by birth itself, and how his activities are glorious, even his preparation for death is very very very glorious. So everything is glorious about the exalted Mahabhagavata Parikshit Maharaj.

---
### devotee glorification, Krishna's plan, pure devotee leadership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/g_bpM5E5wRU?t=234
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna actually conducted this battle of Kurukshetra in order to enthrone his pure devotee as the emperor of the world."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to glorify the Pandavas as pure devotees and explains that the Bhagavatam concerns the Supreme Lord in relationship with his variegated devotees. He states that Krishna orchestrated the battle of Kurukshetra to enthrone Yudhishthira, a pure devotee, as emperor, because a pure devotee carries out the mission of the Supreme Lord.

So the description, because Parikshit is the grandson of the Pandavas, therefore the description is being given here. Pandavas themselves are Mahabhagavatas, they are pure devotees of the Supreme Lord, all the five of them. So therefore the Bhagavatam concerns not only description of the Supreme Lord and one or two devotees, but actually concerns the Supreme Lord in relationship with his variegated devotees, yes, unlimited devotees all relating with him in their own particular relationship. So we understand that from the Srimad Bhagavatam because all such descriptions are there. So therefore the historical incident of Yudhishthira Maharaj, the end of the battle of Kurukshetra, this narration began when Ashwatthama attempted to kill, sorry, Ashwatthama killed the five sleeping sons of Draupadi and he was punished, that was the seventh chapter, and the eighth chapter begins by telling that after that Yudhishthira, Yudhishthira was enthroned as the emperor of the world, and Krishna actually conducted this battle of Kurukshetra in order to enthrone his pure devotee as the emperor of the world. Why? Because a pure devotee carries out the mission of the Supreme Lord in this material world.

---
### Krishna's mission, devotee leadership, Ashwamedha yajnas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/g_bpM5E5wRU?t=353
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna wants pure devotees to rule this world."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the Lord's mission to give conditioned souls a chance to return to Krishna consciousness, emphasizing the king's role in guiding citizens towards Godhead. He reiterates that Krishna desires pure devotees to rule the world, leading to the Pandavas' reign and their performance of three Ashwamedha yajnas.

What is that mission of the Supreme Lord in this world? The Lord has created this world in order to give a chance to the conditioned souls to try to enjoy materially and prepare themselves, especially in the human form of life, to actually return to their original consciousness, Krishna consciousness. So this is the whole program and to facilitate this understanding and acting properly and preparing oneself to return to the original consciousness, the king has to guide the citizens of his kingdom in such a way that they all prepare themselves to go back to Godhead and therefore Krishna wants pure devotees to rule this world. So the Pandavas are pure devotees of Krishna, therefore Krishna wants the Pandavas to rule the world. The Kauravas are not devotees of Krishna, therefore Krishna arranged the battle of Kurukshetra in which the Kauravas were completely destroyed. So here the after Yudhishthira was enthroned as the emperor of the world, he performed three Ashwamedha yagnas again by the mercy of the Supreme Personality of Godhead Krishna.
