## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.9.25 | 22-04-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Dharma, Mahabharata, Bhishmadeva
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6wNh-stHqnQ?t=57
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhishmadeva is the most authoritative personality to speak on religious principles."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the context of the lecture, focusing on the importance of Bhishmadeva's teachings on dharma to Yudhishthira in the Mahabharata.

In this session, we will be discussing the 25th verse of the 9th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Sutavvacha Yudhishthiristadakarnya Shayanam Sharapanjare Aprichhadvividhan dharman Rishinamcha Anushunvatam. The meaning of this verse is: Suta Goswami said Maharaj Yudhishthira, after hearing Bhishmadeva speak in that appealing tone, asked him in the presence of all the great rishis about the essential principles of various religious duties. Now here is the most important part of the Mahabharata. In the Mahabharata, the conversation between Yudhishthira and Bhishmadeva on principles of religion, dharma, is the bulk of the Mahabharata. Three parvas cover this conversation and the instructions Bhishmadeva gives to Yudhishthira about dharma. Bhishmadeva is one of the twelve great authorities in matter of understanding dharma. The Mahabharata says, Sattvam Nihitam Guhayam. The most confidential understanding of what is dharma is hidden in the heart of such great personalities called Mahajanas. Mahajana Yena Gata Sapanta. So Bhishmadeva is the most authoritative personality to speak on religious principles.

---
### Yudhishthira's Grief, Krishna's Arrangement, Pacification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6wNh-stHqnQ?t=172
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna inspired Yudhishthira to ask him of the principle of peace and principles of religion."
**Summary:** This segment describes Yudhishthira's grief after the battle of Kurukshetra and how Krishna arranged for Bhishmadeva to pacify him and impart knowledge of dharma.

Yudhishthira was put into a situation where he was aggrieved at the massacre that took place just for Yudishthira to be enthroned as the Emperor of the world. Six forty million Kshatriyas were killed in that battle of Kurukshetra which lasted just 18 days because huge armies, totally 18 Akshohini divisions of armies, were participating in that battle which is actually a world war. So Yudhishthira after the battle was over and he had won, the Pandavas had won the battle, but seeing the Massacre Yudhishthira was aggrieved and he could not be pacified by so many other saintly persons, sages, and even Krishna himself could not pacify Yudhishthira. He was thinking, 'I am the most sinful person on the surface of this planet earth. Look at my greed for becoming the Emperor. I have become responsible for this huge massacre.' So Yudhishthira was to be pacified by Bhishma Deva. This is Krishna's arrangement. So Srila Prabhupada explains this in the purport that Bhishma Deva speaking in that appealing tone convinced Maharaja Yudhishthira that he was very soon passing away, who he, it is Bhishma Deva. Bhishma Deva soon going to pass away and Maharaja Yudhishthira was inspired by Lord Shri Krishna to ask him of the principle of peace and principles of religion.

---
### Krishna as Paramatma, Guidance from Within, Liberated Souls
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6wNh-stHqnQ?t=292
**Verse References:** BG 3.17
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Shastras are meant for those who are not in touch with Krishna within the heart, the conditioned souls."
**Summary:** This segment explains that Krishna resides in the hearts of all beings as Paramatma, guiding devotees, and that liberated souls receive constant direction from Krishna within.

It is Krishna who seated in the heart of Yudhishthira and Krishna who seated in the heart of Bhishma Deva. Krishna seated on everyone's heart as the Paramatma and particularly devotees are guided by the supreme Lord in the heart always. This is explained in the Bhagavad Gita in the third chapter, 17th verse, that it is explained for the liberated souls there are no duties to be performed as prescribed in the scriptures. So does it mean that liberated souls don't follow any authority, they whimsically act? No, the liberated souls are not required to go by the principles of the Shastra because they are constantly in touch with Krishna within the heart. It is Krishna only who has given us the Shastra. The Shastras are meant for those who are not in touch with Krishna within the heart, the conditioned souls and the liberated souls, they are receiving direction from Krishna within the heart every moment.

---
### Krishna's Inspiration, Auspicious Departure, Bhishma's Benediction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6wNh-stHqnQ?t=375
**Verse References:** BG 3.17, BG 8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhishma had a benediction that he could quit his body according to the time of his choice."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on Krishna's inspiration to Yudhishthira and Bhishmadeva, and Bhishma's unique benediction to choose the time of his death.

This is explained in the Bhagavad Gita in the third chapter, 17th verse. So it is said here by Srila Prabhupada that Krishna inspired Yudhishthira to ask Bhishma Deva about the principles of religion and Krishna inspired Bhishma Deva to speak even though Bhishma was in a very, very difficult situation. Bhishma spoke about the principles of Dharma elaborately to Yudhishthira. This also coincided with Bhishma waiting for the most auspicious time to quit his body because he had such a benediction from his father. Bhishma had a benediction that he could quit his body according to the time of his choice. This particular choice is not available for most souls who are forced to quit their body at a particular time of death when the lifespan is exhausted. Our lifespan is taken away with every sunrise and sunset. We have a fixed duration and we have to leave the body which is called death at a particular time when our lifespan is exhausted. But somebody like Bhishma Deva, they have this perfection, Siddhi, it's called Siddhi, that they can quit the body whenever they choose to. That means they can select the most auspicious time for quitting the body. This is explained in the 8th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita.

---
### Importance of Bhagavad Gita, Guidance of Spiritual Master, Essence of Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6wNh-stHqnQ?t=497
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Bhagavad Gita is the complete knowledge for every human being on the surface of the earth in every aspect of their duties in different stages."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of studying the Bhagavad Gita under a bona fide spiritual master and highlights that it contains the essence of dharma.

You see everything is there in the Bhagavad Gita, but unfortunately people don't study Bhagavad Gita under the guidance of a bona fide spiritual master, therefore they are misled into thinking Bhagavad Gita is some kind of scripture which is merely talking about do your duty. Don't be attached to result. Most people if you ask them what do you know about Bhagavad Gita, they only tell these two things. They have no understanding that Bhagavad Gita is the complete knowledge for every human being on the surface of the earth in every aspect of their duties in different stages. Now Bhishma will explain, it will be told in a later verse, he explained Dharma pertaining to four varanas and four ashramas, varana dharma ashrama dharma. So those details are there in the Mahabharata, but Bhagavad Gita contains the essence. The essence is given in the Bhagavad Gita. So Bhishma Deva spoke about Dharma to Yudhishthira in the presence of many people.

---
### Bhishma's Condition, Krishna Consciousness, Srila Prabhupada's Example
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6wNh-stHqnQ?t=617
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lord Krishna wanted to prove that his pure devotee like Bhishmadeva is always sound in body and mind by dint of spiritual enlightenment."
**Summary:** This segment describes Bhishma's physical condition and how Krishna demonstrated his devotee's unwavering Krishna consciousness, drawing a parallel with Srila Prabhupada's dedication even in his final days.

Vyasadeva was present also when Bhishma was instructing Yudhishthira. Now Bhishmadeva was lying on a bed of arrows at the time when Yudhishthira approached Bhishmadeva. So he was greatly aggrieved in that condition lying on a bed of arrows. So one should not have asked him any question at that time. But Lord Krishna wanted to prove that his pure devotee like Bhishmadeva is always sound in body and mind by dint of spiritual enlightenment. See somebody is sent person Krishna conscious then irrespective of the condition of the body or the mind they are perfected. So inside this house, they are tightly situated in Krishna consciousness always. So they are ready to speak on the principles of Dharma, on the principles of Krishna consciousness at any time you may approach them. This was demonstrated by Srila Prabhupada also. Towards the end of his manifest presence in this world Srila Prabhupada has had over a lifetime of service to the Brahmins in the Brahmins. He was always in every place to meet the Brahmins. So in that time, the Brahmins and the weak and initiated but still he was surrounded by devotees who were constantly chanting the Hare Krishna mantra and Prabhupada used to receive reports about preaching all over the world in so many centers and he would give directions when they were when the disciples were asking questions and he was dictating purpose of bhagavatam the last chapter the Srila Prabhupada translated and gave purpose of the bhagavatam was the 10th canto 13th chapter you can go through that 10th canto 13th chapter translation and purpose by Srila Prabhupada he was dictating he was very very weak but the consciousness was perfectly clear. Devotees have actually recorded that then they have transcribed as they usually did always were doing that even before so Prabhupada was giving perfect knowledge coming in the parampara perfect knowledge.

### Devotee's Perfection, Bhishma's Auspicious Departure, Krishna's Glory
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6wNh-stHqnQ?t=778
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Worship of his devotee is more valuable than the worship of Lord Krishna himself."
**Summary:** This segment concludes by reiterating that a devotee of the Lord is always ready to speak on the right way of life, highlighting Bhishma's rare privilege and Krishna's desire to glorify his devotees.

It is described here in any circumstances a devotee of the Lord is in perfect order to speak the right way of life and Maharaj Yudhishthira he preferred to solve this problematic questions by asking Bhishma Deva rather than ask anyone else present there because many great saintly persons and sages arrived there understanding that Bhishma Deva is going to quit his body at the most auspicious time and that privilege that Bhishma had not everyone has got that it's very rare and this is the perfection of life to quit the body to be able to go back to Godhead to be able to go back to Godhead that is the that is the perfection of one's life no more taking birth again. So Bhishma Deva was ready to quit his body actually because he was fully Krishna conscious he could have quit any time but taking a humble position he decided to quit his body at the time at the most auspicious time, Uttarayana Punyakala it's called and while he was waiting he spoke to Yudhishthira about the principles of religious duties and Prabhupada says Krishna says that worship of his devotee is more valuable than the worship of Lord Krishna himself. So Krishna wants to glorify his devotees while devotees want to glorify Krishna. Krishna wants to glorify his devotee and Krishna wanted to glorify Bhishma therefore Krishna inspired Yudhishthira to ask questions about Dharma to Bhishma and Bhishma answered them. I'll stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna.

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse to be discussed, SB 1.9.26, and explains that it summarizes Bhishma Deva's teachings to Yudhishthira about Varna Dharma and Ashrama Dharma, including counteraction by detachment and interaction by attachment.

In this session, we will be discussing from the 26th verse of the 9th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Purushasvabhava Vihitam Yathavarnam Yathashramam Vairagya Rago Padibhyam Amnatobhaya Lakshanam. The meaning of this verse is, at Maharaj Yudhishthira's inquiry, Bhishma Deva first defined all the classifications of caste and orders of life in terms of the individual's qualifications. Then he systematically in two-fold divisions described counteraction by detachment and interaction by attachment. So the description continues by Suta Goswami to the sages of Naimisharanya, that this conversation between Yudhishthira making inquiry about caste and orders of life, about dharma, and Bhishma replying, is given in great details in the Mahabharata, but the summary is given here.

### Varna Dharma, social order, qualification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JAhmv5_fU6Y?t=126
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everything goes by quality, not by birth, not by some hereditary title. No. It goes by qualification."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Varna Dharma, explaining the four divisions of society (Brahmanas, Kshatriyas, Vaishyas, and Shudras) based on their acquired nature and occupational duties, emphasizing that qualification, not birth, determines one's Varna.

So Bhishma first of all explained Varna Dharma. A civilized society is divided into four Varanas based on occupational duties, occupational engagements, according to their acquired nature. So those in Sattva Guna, their acquired nature is suitable for doing Brahmanical duties, Brahmanas. Kshatriyas, their acquired nature is Rajoguna. So they are suited for doing occupation of a Kshatriya, administrative duties, protection against harm of the innocent people, saintly persons, weaker sections of society, and the Vaishyas, their acquired nature is a mixed mode of passion and ignorance. So they are suited for doing Vaishya duties. They are the economic generators of the society, and the Shudras are mostly in the mode of ignorance, and they are mentioned in the Mahabharata. They are meant for doing some labor. They are meant to engage in the service of the upper three divisions, Brahmanas, Kshatriyas, and Vaishyas. Everything goes by quality, not by birth, not by some hereditary title. No. It goes by qualification. So in terms of the individual's qualifications, he described Varana Dharma. What is Brahmana Dharma? What is Kshatriya Dharma? What is Vaishya Dharma? What is Shudra Dharma?

---
### Ashrama Dharma, spiritual progress, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JAhmv5_fU6Y?t=244
**Verse References:** BG 4.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The goal is that everybody should be freed from bondage, not just the brahmanas, not just some few educated or learned people. No. Everyone should get free from bondage."
**Summary:** The speaker continues with Ashrama Dharma, outlining the four spiritual divisions (Brahmachari, Grahastha, Vanaprastha, and Sanyas) and emphasizing that the ultimate goal of Varnashrama Dharma, as planned by the Lord and described in the Bhagavad Gita (Chatur Varnyam Mayasrishtam Gunakarma Vibhagshah), is to liberate everyone from material bondage, not just a select few.

And he described the Dharma of the different Ashramas. Ashrama is spiritual division, Brahmachari, Grahastha, Vanaprastha, Sanyas. So Brahmachari Dharma, Grahastha Dharma, Vanaprastha Dharma, Sanyas Dharma. Everything Bheeshma Deva described systematically. Then he also described counteraction by detachment and interaction by attachment. So Srila Prabhupada explains these things in the purport. Prabhupada has written elaborate purport. I don't have time to go through the whole purport. I will try to cover some important points, that the conception of the four Varanas and four Ashramas as planned by the Lord Himself, as described in the Bhagavad Gita, Chatur Varnyam Mayasrishtam Gunakarma Vibhagshah, is to accelerate transcendental qualities of the individual person, so that he may gradually realize his spiritual identity and thus act accordingly to get free from material bondage with your conditional life. See the goal is that everybody should be freed from bondage, not just the brahmanas, not just some few educated or learned people. No. Everyone should get free from bondage, and that's possible by following the Dharma. Anyone and everyone can actually become liberated. That is the whole idea.

### Varnashrama Dharma, misinterpretation, self-realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JAhmv5_fU6Y?t=355
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Varnashrama Dharma is prescribed for the civilized human being just to train him to successfully terminate human life. What is successful termination of human life? Self-realization, to attain self-realization and thereby become free from material bondage."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the common misunderstanding of Varnashrama Dharma as a system of artificial domination, clarifying that it is actually meant to train individuals for self-realization and liberation from material bondage, distinguishing human life from the animalistic tendencies of eating, sleeping, fearing, and mating.

In the Mahabharata, it is most elaborately explained by Bhishma Deva in the Shanti Parva, beginning from the 60th chapter. Prabhupada has given reference. Where do you get details? Here it is briefly explained. It is just mentioned. It is not even explained. But Prabhupada gives a little explanation here. Now the Varnashrama Dharma is most often misunderstood, especially in the modern times, that this caste system has created artificial domination of Brahmanas or Brahmanas and Kshatriyas over the other sections of society. No. That is not Varnashrama Dharma. That is a misinterpretation. That is the degradation of the Varnashrama Dharma. When unqualified people claim to be Brahmanas or Kshatriyas, they tend to exploit the weaker sections of society. That is not Varnashrama Dharma. So Varnashrama Dharma is prescribed for the civilized human being just to train him to successfully terminate human life. What is successful termination of human life? Self-realization, to attain self-realization and thereby become free from material bondage.

---
### qualifications, civilized society, duties
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JAhmv5_fU6Y?t=455
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One cannot be called a civilized person without acquiring the above mentioned preliminary qualities."
**Summary:** The speaker outlines nine general qualifications advised by Bhishma Deva for all human beings to be considered civilized, followed by specific occupational duties for each Varna as mentioned in the Vedas.

So self-realization is distinguished from the life of the lower animals who are simply engaged in four activities: eating, sleeping, fearing, and mating. So Bhishma Deva advised for all human beings, not just for Brahmanas or not just for some people. No. 9 qualifications. What are they? Not to become angry, not to lie, not to equally distribute wealth, to forgive, to beget children only by one's legitimate wife, to be pure in mind and hygienic in body, not to be inimical toward anyone, to be simple, and the ninth one is to support servants or subordinates. It is explained here by Srila Prabhupada based on the description given by Bhishma Deva in the Mahabharata. One cannot be called a civilized person without acquiring the above mentioned preliminary qualities. Then there are specific qualities by which Brahmanas work, the Kshatriyas work, the Vaishyas work, and the Shudras work. Everybody has got specific occupational duties mentioned in the Vedas.

### Varna Dharma, duties, charity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JAhmv5_fU6Y?t=547
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So if an intelligent man abuses his position, if he does not follow the Vedic way of life, he should seriously study Vedic literatures, especially Bhagavatam and Bhagavad Gita."
**Summary:** The speaker details the specific duties of each Varna, emphasizing sense control for Brahmanas, charity for Kshatriyas, and the importance of supporting Brahmanas so they can provide guidance and enlightenment to society.

Now briefly Prabhupada says for the intelligent men, that is the Brahmanas, controlling the senses is the most essential qualification. Now it is the basis of morality. Why control the senses? It is the basis of morality. So if an intelligent man abuses his position, if he does not follow the Vedic way of life, he should seriously study Vedic literatures, especially Bhagavatam and Bhagavad Gita, and it is imperative that such a person willing to study should approach a bona fide spiritual master. He should not do anything which is forbidden in the Shastra. For the Kshatriya it is especially advised to give charity and never accept charity in any circumstances. Kshatriya especially should give charity. So the Kshatriyas were meant to support the Brahmanas by giving them profuse charity, and thereby the Brahmanas could give free service to everyone in the society in matter of guiding them, giving them direction, enlightening them, and helping them whenever they were in difficulty. So this is the perfect society as described in the Shastras.

### Varna Dharma, protection, economic roles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JAhmv5_fU6Y?t=655
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One of the important duties of the Kshatriya is to actually protect the weaker sections of the society."
**Summary:** The speaker continues describing the duties of each Varna, focusing on the Kshatriya's role in protecting society, the Vaishya's role in protecting cows and producing grains, and the Shudra's role in serving the upper three classes.

Now one of the important duties of the Kshatriya is to actually protect the weaker sections of the society, the rest of the society, by not pretending to become non-violent when there is a need to chastise the miscreants, the rogues, the thieves, the plunderers. They should be severely chastised by using violence by the Kshatriyas. Then the Vaishyas, they should particularly protect cows and grow sufficient grains and agriculture and distribution of foodstuff, especially meant for the Vaishyas, and Kshatriyas were given charge of protection of the citizens. Vaishyas were given charge of protection of the cows, Goraksh, and they used to produce profusely agricultural produce and milk and milk products, and then the Shudras, they have no independence because they are not sufficiently intelligent enough to use their independence, so they are meant to render sincere service to the upper three sections so that they can get all their comforts of life simply by rendering service to the higher classes because as soon as a Shudra accumulates wealth he should never be given money because as soon as he accumulates wealth it will be misused for sinful activities in wine, women and gambling, and that's what we see happening today.

### Kali Yuga, social degradation, Ashrama Dharma, self-realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JAhmv5_fU6Y?t=773
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Brahmachari Ashram is especially meant for training both the attached and the detached, so that the goal is self-realization."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the degradation of society in Kali Yuga, where the population becomes like Shudras, and reiterates the importance of providing for the Shudras' needs without giving them money. He concludes by explaining the progressive nature of the Ashramas in developing spiritual qualities and achieving self-realization, emphasizing that the goal of both attached householders and detached renunciates is self-realization.

Whoever is not intelligent enough to utilize accumulated wealth, they will misuse it for sinful activities, and that's what is happening. Population becomes degraded. In Kali Yuga it is said population becomes degraded to the level of Shudras or even lower than that, Chandala. The Shudras should always be provided with all the necessities without giving them money so that they are satisfied in all respects and they don't have accumulated wealth to misuse them, and the upper three sections, they should maintain the Shudras under all circumstances. Never should the Shudras be abandoned by any of the upper sections, and the Ashramas, the student life, Brahmachari, Grahastha, the householder life, the retired life Vanaprastha, and the renounced life, it is progressively meant for developing spiritual qualities and progressing in self-realization systematically, and that is also explained by Bhishma Deva in detail, and the household life is for one who is attached, and Vanaprastha and Sanya's orders of life are meant for those who are detached. Householders are generally attached to family and so many family paraphernalia, but the Brahmachari Ashram is especially meant for training both the attached and the detached, so that the goal is self-realization, either somebody is attached or somebody is detached but the goal is self-realization that should not be forgotten. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### charity, dāna, daya, varnashrama
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W6ib-kP6yrg?t=111
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "See, try to understand the difference between dāna and daya."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the importance of charity (dāna) within the varnashrama system, emphasizing that it should be directed towards brahmacharis, vanaprasthas, and sannyasis engaged in spiritual activities, and differentiating it from compassion (daya) shown to the poor or handicapped.

These are explained elaborately in the Mahābhārata. It is given. So, here he says, to give charity is one of the householder's main functions among the four āśramas: Brahmachārī, Grahastha, Vānaprastha and Sannyāsa. The Brahmachārī's Vānaprastha and Sannyāsa is, don't earn money, don't have a means of living, don't have a means of getting their livelihood. So, they are dependent on bhikṣa. They beg or live by whatever is available easily in nature. So, the Grahasthas have to do this charity, giving dāna to whom? To Brahmachārīs, to the Vānaprasthas and Sannyāsas. All three are engaged in spiritual activities exclusively. So, charity is to be given to somebody exclusively devoted to spiritual activities. Charity is not to be given to somebody who is poor, somebody who is handicapped. No, that is not charity. That's not dāna. That is different. What is that called? That's called daya. See, try to understand the difference between dāna and daya. Prabhupāda explains this elsewhere. Daya means to show compassion to somebody who is less privileged.

### charity, varnashrama, Krishna consciousness, energy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W6ib-kP6yrg?t=250
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Therefore the resultant actions of such energy must be given to the Lord in the shape of transcendental loving service rendered to Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to elaborate on the concept of dāna, emphasizing that it is specifically for those engaged in spiritual activities within the varnashrama dharma, and connects it to the idea that all energy originates from Krishna and should be returned to Him through devotional service.

Dāna is specifically the householder or those having wealth giving charity only to those who is exclusively engaged in spiritual activities and in the very culture, the varnāśrama dharma is followed strictly. In such a society, the brahmachāris, the vāna-prasthas and sannyāsīs, they don't do any activity other than spiritual activities. So a brahmachāri has got specifically the duty to learn, to get trained up how to practice spiritual life. That is the main duty of a brahmachāri. A householder should give charity and a person in the retired and in the renounced order of life, Vāna-prasthas and sannyāsīs should practice penances and austerities. These are general functions for all the ashramas. Actually the brahmachāri training is such that they should understand all the property in the entire world belongs to Krishna, the supreme personality of Godhead. No one can claim any proprietorship over anything. Therefore the life of the householder is meant to give charity, especially to the brahmachāris, vāna-prasthas and sannyāsīs. So Prabhupāda explains the concept is that everyone's energy is generated or borrowed from the reservoir of energy of the Supreme Lord. Therefore the resultant actions of such energy must be given to the Lord in the shape of transcendental loving service rendered to Krishna.

### Krishna consciousness, charity, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W6ib-kP6yrg?t=378
**Verse References:** BG 9.27
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Similarly our energy is borrowed from the Supreme Source, the Lord's energy, it must be returned to the Lord."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of rivers returning to the sea, the speaker explains that our energy is borrowed from Krishna and should be returned to Him through devotional service, emphasizing that Krishna is the source and reservoir of everything, and that giving charity to those engaged in spiritual activities is a way of giving to Krishna.

The example is given, the rivers draw water from the sea through the clouds and again go down to the sea. Similarly our energy is borrowed from the Supreme Source, the Lord's energy, it must be returned to the Lord. That is the perfection of our energy. And Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, whatever we do, whatever we undergo as penance, whatever we sacrifice, whatever we eat, whatever we give in charity, must be offered to Krishna. Yad karoṣi yad aṣṇāsi yad yahoṣi, Dadāsi yad yad tapasyaśi kaunteya, Tath kurusva madharpanam. Now why Krishna is telling that we should give it to Krishna? Krishna is not in need. He is the source. He is the reservoir of everything. He's got unlimited wealth. So why we should give in charity to Krishna? It is actually through giving to actually the Brahmacharis and the Vanaprasad sannyasis, we are giving to Krishna because they are going to utilize it for spiritual activities. In that way, it goes back to Krishna. So when you give charity, actually.

### charity, detachment, Rajadharma, self-realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W6ib-kP6yrg?t=455
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So the proper utilization is minimum required for your own maintenance."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that giving charity is not renouncing something that belongs to you, but rather returning something that ultimately belongs to the Lord, emphasizing the importance of utilizing wealth for maintenance and returning the balance to Krishna through spiritualists, and then transitions to discussing Rajadharma, the science of ruling to guide citizens towards self-realization.

It is going back to the original source from where you have got your wealth. So giving charity is not that something which belongs to you, you are renouncing. No, you are actually possessing something which does not belong to you. So the proper utilization is minimum required for your own maintenance. You definitely do utilize whatever you are earning or whatever your wealth you have inherited or earned or got from nature. You definitely utilize a portion of that for your own maintenance, for your subsistence, yourself, your family members, and the balance should be returned to the Lord to whom it belongs through the spiritualist, transcendentalist. That way it is going back to the Lord because the transcendentalist will only use whatever bhiksha they get for the Lord's service and not for any other purpose. Rajadharma is a great science. It is the science of ruling perfectly the kingdom, guiding the citizens so that they understand the goal of human form of life is self realization and not sense enjoyment.

---
### Rajadharma, leadership, sacrifice, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W6ib-kP6yrg?t=556
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The greatest duty of the king was to ensure that he leads the citizens in attaining ultimate salvation from material bondage."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Rajadharma, contrasting it with modern political diplomacy, emphasizing that kings were trained to be munificent in giving charity to saintly persons and to lead citizens towards liberation from material bondage through sacrifices and proper governance.

The kings were systematically trained to become not merely tax collectors, but to become munificent in giving charity to the saintly persons. So for the prosperity of the subjects, the kings were supposed to do sacrifices. When sacrifices are performed, there is plenty of supply by the arrangement of the Supreme Lord through the agents called the Devatas. And the greatest duty of the king was to ensure that he leads the citizens in attaining ultimate salvation from material bondage. So this is Rajadharma. This is totally different from modern training in political diplomacy. This is not Rajadharma, political diplomacy. It is mainly how to govern the citizens so that ultimately the citizens are going to attain complete liberation from material bondage.

### Rajadharma, taxation, Vedic culture, Moksha Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W6ib-kP6yrg?t=624
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "People were not grudging in parting with one-fourth of their produced wealth because that wealth which the king collected as taxes was used for providing for the citizens in so many ways."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the taxation system in Rajadharma, where the king took one-fourth of the production, which was willingly given by the citizens because it was used for their welfare, contrasting it with the modern misuse of wealth for sense gratification, and then transitions to Moksha Dharma, emphasizing the importance of conquering lust, anger, and unlawful desires for salvation.

And it is also explained here that the taxation law was very simple. The king had a right to take one-fourth of the production made by the citizens. And who would produce mainly the vices. And people were not grudging in parting with one-fourth of their produced wealth because that wealth which the king collected as taxes was used for providing for the citizens in so many ways. And the citizens also were rich in agriculture and animal husbandry. Therefore they had enough grains, fruits, milk without any need for artificial ingredients like soaps and toilets and cinemas and bars. None of these things are required. Neither people had any of those in the Vedic culture. So this is what people were directed to live a life mainly aimed at self-realization, not that they have wealth, they misuse that for sense gratification in so many sinful ways as is happening in the modern times. No. So that the Rajadharma was then Moksha Dharma. Salvation is ultimately possible by conquering the principles of lust, anger, unlawful desires, avarice, and bewilderment. So this is.

### Bhagavad Dharma, devotional service, bhakti, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W6ib-kP6yrg?t=881
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So we should understand every one of us ultimately is eternal servant of Krishna and simply by worshiping the Lord but with simple ingredients but with devotion if one offers even one leaf or little water to the Supreme Lord Krishna that is the easiest way of pleasing the Lord but the important thing is bhakti devotion."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing that everyone is an eternal servant of Krishna and can please Him through simple devotional service, even offering a leaf or water with devotion, which guarantees ultimate liberation.

How to please the Lord? So we should understand every one of us ultimately is eternal servant of Krishna and simply by worshiping the Lord but with simple ingredients but with devotion if one offers even one leaf or little water to the Supreme Lord Krishna that is the easiest way of pleasing the Lord but the important thing is bhakti devotion. So in this way one will actually guarantee ultimate liberation by worshiping the Lord and pleasing him through our activities. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.9.28 | 25-04-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### scriptural authority, history, Vedic literature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TH-LqX2z6lg?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 1.9.28
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The correct understanding is that the Puranas, Itihases, Mahabharata, Ramayana contain factual historical narrations."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of understanding the Puranas, Itihasas, Mahabharata, and Ramayana as factual historical narrations, countering the British propaganda that they are mere mythology.

Om Agyanati Miran Dhasya Gyananjan Shalakaya Chakshurun Militam Jena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Namah Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Devi Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayam. In this session we will be discussing from the 28th verse of the 9th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Dharmartha Kama Mokshamscha Sahopayan Yathamune Nanakhyana Itihaseshu Varanaya Masatatvavit. The meaning of this verse is: Then he described the occupational duties of different orders and statuses of life, citing instances from history, for he was himself well acquainted with the truth. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport to this verse. Incidents mentioned in the Vedic literatures such as the Puranas, Mahabharata and Ramayana are factual historical narrations that took place sometime in the past, although not in any chronological order. So this is a very very important understanding for all of us that the British propaganda was that our Vedic scriptures, especially the Puranas, Itihases, like Ramayana, Mahabharata, are all mythology. The word mythology comes from the word myth. Myth means something which is not a fact. As opposed to the British propaganda that all these are imaginary stories, the correct understanding is that the Puranas, Itihases, Mahabharata, Ramayana contain factual historical narrations. Puranas, Itihasas, Mahabharata, Ramayana contain factual historical narrations. They are all cent percent fact.

### spiritual reality, ignorance, scriptural understanding
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TH-LqX2z6lg?t=89
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The descriptions in the Puranas and the Itihasas is meant to eradicate our darkness of ignorance, enlighten us about reality, about facts, not some imaginary stories."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that descriptions in the Puranas and Itihasas may not align with our present-day experiences because they describe events on different planets, universes, and the spiritual world, aiming to dispel ignorance and reveal reality.

Though many of the descriptions may not match our present day experience of the world we live in now, we should always remember that the descriptions in the Puranas and the Itihasas, Ramayana, Mahabharata, etc., concern descriptions of incidents that take place in different planets of this universe, and sometimes the descriptions are there of incidents that take place in other universes. And of course there is description of incidents that happen in the spiritual world beyond all the material universes. So that will never tally with our experience because we do not know what is the spiritual world. We do not know what are the upper planets. We do not know what are hellish planets. That is because of our ignorance. So the descriptions in the Puranas and the Itihasas is meant to eradicate our darkness of ignorance, enlighten us about reality, about facts, not some imaginary stories. So this has to be very properly understood and digested.

Just like to give in the Bhagavatam, the disappearance of Krishna is described in the 14th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 14th chapter is entitled The Disappearance of Krishna. The Disappearance of Lord Krishna. And the appearance of Krishna is described in the 10th canto of the first chapter, or 10th canto of the third chapter. First, second and third. So it is not chronologically arranged that first this happened, then this happened, then this happened. Sometimes there is a description which is not necessarily in the chronological order, not necessarily in the order in which they occurred but the important point is there is some lesson for us to learn and that lesson is important besides that Srila Prabhupada continues to explain they happen on different planets nay in different universes and thus the description of narrations is sometimes measured by three dimensions our experience of life on this planet in the present age is limited in former ages the demigods used to visit the earth planet and they were visible to ordinary men but in Kali Yuga the demigods are not visible they generally don't come to this earth planet.

### spiritual practice, Varna Ashrama, self-realization

**Summary:** The speaker discusses the importance of spiritual practice for perceiving reality, the Varna Ashrama system for civilized society, and the need for occupations aligned with self-realization, avoiding sinful activities to advance spiritually and return to Godhead.

And they are giving us knowledge about things which is beyond the range of our experience or understanding. And when we qualify, we are able to perceive this reality described in the scriptures. When we qualify, there is a way of acquiring these qualifications by spiritual practice. And that is what is being taught in the Vedic literature. Dheeshma Deva described such narrations before Maharaj Yudhishtira in reply to his different questions. Yudhishtira asked so many questions about Dharma. So, Varna Dharma and Ashrama Dharma. This is for civilized people. Varna Ashrama system. The society is divided into four parts. Four occupational divisions according to the acquired nature. We have discussed this in the previous verses. And Brahmana Dharma, Kshatriya Dharma, Vaishya Dharma, Shudra Dharma. So, that is way of dividing the society for the sake of everyone having a common goal or aim of self-realization. That is the reason the particular occupations are recommended. People should not simply do anything and everything for the sake of earning money. No, they should not do anything sinful. So, to avoid sinful activities means to prepare oneself to advance in spirituality. To go back to the spiritual life. And prepare oneself to go back home, back to Godhead. That is the whole program.

### accountability, karma, spiritual correction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TH-LqX2z6lg?t=640
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The goal itself is missing in the modern education system."
**Summary:** The speaker criticizes the modern education system for lacking the goal of self-realization and awareness of our eternal nature and accountability for actions, explaining that sinful activities have consequences in this or future lives, but humans have the opportunity to correct themselves through Prayas Chitta.

Now, the goal itself is missing in the modern education system. They do not know that we are eternal. Each one of us is eternal. They do not know this. So, the goal itself is missing. So, then where is the relevance? Studying the scriptures and living a life based on the goal of ultimate self-realization. They do not understand this. They don't even know that we are accountable for every activity we do. Not only now because of the government or the police force or some material laws that we know are enforced, also, after quitting this body, next life we are accountable. All our actions are accountable. If I do some sinful activities in this life, I may have to suffer in this life itself the reactions for those sinful activities. I may not suffer in this life. But I will have to suffer in the next life or in future lives. So, these are certain laws that are working. Now, why do the scriptures ordain that certain sinful activities, the reactions are given only in the future lives? Because in the human form of life, if I have done a sinful activity, I have got an opportunity to correct myself. Because human being has got developed intelligence. So, he can, he or she, any human being can correct their sinful activity done unknowingly or done accidentally, done inadvertently. Somehow, by force of circumstances, somebody did something wrong. They can correct themselves. That's called Prayas Chitta. And the best Prayas Chitta as described in the Bhagavatam is simply to chant the holy name of the Supreme Lord.

### spiritual sky, material world, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/G7Gq3iVZ7BY?t=218
**Verse References:** BG 8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Secondly darkness also means ignorance. That is a characteristic of material existence."
**Summary:** The speaker references the Bhagavad Gita and explains the concept of the spiritual sky (Paravyoma) beyond the material sky, emphasizing the contrast between the illuminated spiritual world and the dark, ignorant material world.

In the Bhagavad Gita, Srila Prabhupada says in the 8th chapter, 'It is said that self-realized souls who are exactly identified themselves with the interest of the Supreme Lord can generally leave the material body during the time of the fire gods effulgence and when the Sun is in the northern horizon and thus achieve the transcendental sky.' See, there is a spiritual sky beyond this material sky. Parastasmatubhavonyo vyakto vyaktatsanatanah, Paravyoma, the spiritual sky. The material sky is what we can see with these eyes, and we understand there are so many other planets in the material sky. Some are called upper planets, some are called lower planets where the conditions of living are different than those on the earth. But beyond all these material planets, beyond this universe, beyond all the material universes, there is a spiritual sky, and in the spiritual sky, there are spiritual planets and the spiritual sky is illuminated by the personal bodily effulgence of Krishna. It's called Brahma Jyoti effulgence, spiritual effulgence. So there is no darkness. Then in the spiritual world, the material world is characterized by its being always dark, and therefore there is a necessity of illumination either by sunlight or moonlight or by fire or electricity. There is a need of illumination in this material world, otherwise by itself the material world is dark, a dark place. Darkness means the absence of light because of which we cannot see in darkness. Our eyes are able to see only when there is light illumination in darkness. Secondly, darkness also means ignorance. That is a characteristic of material existence. We are always in ignorance unless we become enlightened by acquiring the proper knowledge from the perfect authority, the perfect source.

### Bhagavatam, Bhishma, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BchZMGcPIMU?t=42
**Verse References:** SB 1.9.3
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna wanted to glorify Bhishma, Bhishma is one of the Mahajanas, and he is such a great personality that he instructed Yudhishthira in the intricacies of what is Dharma, what is real religious principles."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam (1.9.3) and explains the context: Yudhishthira Maharaj is going to consult Bhishma, who is lying on a bed of arrows, about Dharma, and Krishna wants to glorify Bhishma.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing the third verse of the ninth chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Bhagavanapi Viprarshe Rathena Sadhananjayaha Satair Vyarochata Nripaha Kuvera Iva Guhyakaihi. The meaning of this verse is, O Sage amongst the Brahmanas Viprarshe, Lord Sri Krishna, the personality of Godhead, also followed, seated on a chariot with Arjuna. Thus, King Yudhishthira appeared very aristocratic, like Kuvera surrounded by his companions, the Guhyakas. So the description continues as I mentioned in the previous session, Yudhishthira Maharaj is going to the site of the battlefield where Bhishma is lying on the bed of arrows, ready to quit his body at his chosen time, and Yudhishthira is very much affected by the massacre, so he is going to consult Bhishma, and it is Krishna's will that Bhishma should instruct Yudhishthira, specially at the last moment before he quits his body. Krishna wanted to glorify Bhishma. Bhishma is one of the Mahajanas, and he is such a great personality that he instructed Yudhishthira in the intricacies of what is Dharma, what is real religious principles, in all details he instructed, and that is the bulk of the Mahabharata, Bhishma instructing Yudhishthira, and Yudhishthira asked so many questions about Dharma. So the description here is that Krishna also accompanied the Pandavas.

---
### Krishna, Arjuna, Devotion, Materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BchZMGcPIMU?t=172
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna was on the side of the Pandavas, therefore the Pandavas were most powerful, that Duryodhana could not understand; he was a materialistic person."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Krishna's role as Arjuna's chariot driver and explains why the Pandavas were ultimately victorious despite the Kauravas' apparent strength. Duryodhana's materialistic calculations prevented him from understanding Krishna's influence.

Now Krishna was seated on a chariot with Arjuna because in the battle of Kurukshetra, Krishna voluntarily agreed to be the chariot driver of Arjuna. This is because of Arjuna's friendship with Krishna. Krishna said he is not going to fight on any side in the battle, but whichever side he is going to be present, he is just going to give the instructions or the strategies, how to fight, but he himself is not going to fight, he is not going to use any weapon in the battle. So Arjuna requested Krishna to be his chariot driver and to instruct him and direct him in the battle, in fighting the battle, and Krishna's army, the Yadu army, was on the Kaurava side. They were all well equipped to fight. So it seems like it was not so much of a powerful support that the Pandavas had, but actually the reality is, the truth is, Krishna was on the side of the Pandavas, therefore the Pandavas were most powerful. That Duryodhana could not understand. He was a materialistic person, so he was making some material calculations. Oh, we are very strong, Duryodhana was thinking, that Bhishma is on our side, Dronacharya is on our side, Karna is on our side, the whole Yadu army is on our side, even though Krishna is on the side of the Pandavas, Krishna is not going to fight. So he is making material calculations, but as Sanjaya says, even before the battle begins, in Dhritarashtra.

---
### Bhishma, Pandavas, Justice, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BchZMGcPIMU?t=350
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhishma always felt very sorry for the Pandavas suffering injustice at the hands of Kauravas. Bhishma knew the Pandavas cause is just."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna wanted to give darshan to Bhishma at the time of his death, showing mercy to his devotee. He discusses Bhishma's sympathy for the Pandavas' suffering and his obligation to the Kauravas, despite knowing the Pandavas' cause was just.

In any case, Krishna accompanied the Pandavas, who were actually going to visit Bhishma. Now Krishna wanted to actually give his darshan to Bhishma, especially at the time of Bhishma quitting his body. That is Krishna's mercy on his devotee. Bhishma is a pure devotee, the Pandavas are pure devotees, so therefore Krishna was accompanying the Pandavas, and Yudhishthira, it is described here, appeared very aristocratic, like Kuvera surrounded by his companions, the Guhyakas. Now that is explained by Srila Prabhupada in the purport. Lord Sri Krishna wanted the Pandavas to be present before Bhishma Deva in the most aristocratic order, so that he might be pleased to see them happy at the time of his death. Bhishma always felt very sorry for the Pandavas suffering injustice at the hands of Kauravas. Bhishma knew the Pandavas' cause is just, but still why was he fighting on the side of the Kauravas? Because he was obliged to the Kauravas who were maintaining him. Actually, the entire kingdom belonged to Bhishma, he was the inheritor, but he voluntarily gave up, and because he voluntarily gave up, Dhritarashtra's father, Vichitravirya, became the emperor. That is Bhishma's brother. Bhishma gave up, so but still Bhishma, because he was, he had renounced the claim for the throne, he was being maintained as the elder member of the family in the royal family. He was looked after nicely, but he considered himself maintained by Duryodhana, Dhritarashtra, by the Kauravas when they were caretakers, and the Kauravas never gave the kingdom to the Pandavas. So Kauravas were all the while ruling, and they were giving the maintenance to the kingdom to Bhishma and Drona. So Bhishma and Drona, though they knew the Pandavas' cause is just, still because they were maintained by the Kauravas, they had to fight on the side of Kauravas. This is etiquette. So Bhishma was not very happy with the injustice that the Pandavas had to suffer at the hands of the Kauravas. So at the last moment of his life, he was very happy to see the Pandavas get back their rightful claim to the throne of Hastinapura, the ruling of the entire world, and he also was might have been pleased to see the Pandavas in a very aristocratic order because they deserve that. They are the proper claimants to the throne, and their cause is just, and they are very, very righteous in their conduct, and they are very law abiding. They are followers of Dharma. Yudhishthira particularly, he was very, very meticulously following the principles of Dharma.

---
### Kuvera, Wealth, Sacrifice, Detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BchZMGcPIMU?t=575
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When somebody becomes very rich, there is a possibility that they may go on accumulating more and more wealth unnecessarily, just out of greed they may try to accumulate more and more wealth."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna wanted to satisfy Bhishma by showing him the Pandavas' aristocratic position. He then discusses the comparison to Kuvera, the treasurer of the demigods, and how Yudhishthira needed wealth to perform the Ashwamedha Yagna.

So Krishna wanted to actually make Bhishma very satisfied, seeing the Pandavas very aristocratically taking charge of ruling the world at the end of the battle of Kurukshetra. Now the comparison is made to Kuvera, why? Because Kuvera is the richest of all the demigods, he is the treasurer of the demigods. Kuvera is very rich, so Yudhishthira, he was very, very rich at the end of the battle of Kurukshetra. So it is explained that Yudhishthira was to perform Ashwamedha Yagna as advised by the sages, and for that he required money, and he sent his brothers to fetch wealth for performing the sacrifice because at the end of the battle of Kurukshetra, the treasury was empty, so they had to fetch wealth. So Krishna instructed Arjuna that there is a lot of gold lying somewhere in the Himalayas. Now Krishna knew this because Krishna is of course the supreme personality who has got the super soul in everyone's heart. Formerly, there was one, a king, one emperor of this earth planet, Maruth Maharaj. He had performed a very, very grand Ashwamedha Yagna, and in that Yagna he had spent a lot of money. He had given a lot of gold in charity to the Brahmanas who were performing the sacrifice, and those Brahmanas, they were given so much gold that the Brahmanas were completely detached. They used to live a very simple life, even though they were respected the most in the society, they were given a lot of charity, specially by the Kshatriyas and the Vaishyas, but they never were attached to living a aristocratic life. No, they kept their life very simple because they were devoted to culture, to cultivating knowledge. They were devoted to worshipping Krishna, worshipping God, and they were actually always very, very careful not to be overpowered by greed or lust or anger or enviousness. You see, when somebody becomes very rich, there is a possibility that they may go on accumulating more and more wealth unnecessarily, just out of greed they may try to accumulate more and more wealth, but Brahmanas want to be free from greed.

---
### Brahmanas, Society, Dharma, Arjuna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BchZMGcPIMU?t=771
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Brahmanas want to be free from greed, kama, krodha, loba, moha, mada, matsarya, then only they are in a proper position to guide and direct the entire society."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the role of Brahmanas in guiding society towards spiritual goals and avoiding material entanglement. He explains how Arjuna got the name Dhananjaya by fetching the gold for Yudhishthira's Ashwamedha Yagna, which contributed to Yudhishthira's aristocratic appearance.

Kama, krodha, loba, moha, mada, matsarya, then only they are in a proper position to guide and direct the entire society. The rest of the society, the Kshatriyas, Vaishyas, and Shudras, are guided by Brahmanas towards the higher aim of life, the spiritual goal, self-realization. Otherwise, the material world is entangling, and people easily forget what is the final goal of life, and they simply remain in material existence, simply suffering repeated birth and death. So the Brahmanas always are enlightened, they maintain their position in that enlightened state of having cultivated spiritual knowledge. They never want to be overpowered by greed or envy, so therefore these Brahmanas left behind all that golden utensils and golden plates and golden different varieties of golden paraffin that was given to them as dana, as charity, that was left behind, and that was lying there. Nothing happens to gold, it will be lying there, so Krishna directed Arjuna, go to that place and fetch all that gold and give it to your brother Yudhishthira for performing Ashramida Yajna. So that's how Arjuna got the name Dhananjaya. He is the one who fetched wealth for gold, especially gold, for Yudhishthira to perform the Ashramida Yajna. So therefore he got the name Dhananjaya, Arjuna got the name. So Yudhishthira was able to actually get so much gold for the sake of performing Ashramida Yajna, and that's the way Yudhishthira appeared very, very rich when he was visiting Bhishma, accompanied by all the sages and also accompanied by his brothers who were who were riding on chariots driven by horses which were decorated with golden ornaments. So that's the description here. I'll stop here, thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

Bhishma has stopped speaking. He was instructing the Pandavas. The Uttarayana Punikala has arrived, the auspicious time for him to quit the body, and he stopped speaking. Now after stopping speaking he withdrew his mind from everything else and fixed his eyes and thereby even his mind upon the original personality of God at Sri Krishna. upon the original personality of God at Sri Krishna. upon the original personality of God at Sri Krishna. Sri Krishna was right in front of Bhishma. Remember, Krishna came along with the Pandavas to that spot where Bhishma was lying on the battlefield on the bed of arrows. So Krishna is front of Bhishma. Bhishma fixed his attention, his eyes, his sight completely on Krishna. Now how did Krishna appear to Bhishma Deva? Krishna appeared in a four-handed form to Bhishma Deva. Why? Because Bhishma's relationship with the Supreme Personality of Godhead is as the four-handed Narayana dressed in yellow garments. Four-handed Narayana dressed in yellow garments. That is the relationship of the Lord and Bhishma Deva. That is the relationship of the Lord and Bhishma Deva. That is the relationship of the Lord and Bhishma Deva.

The same Krishna is present as a small child. The same Krishna is present as a small child. The same Krishna is present as a small child. The same Krishna is present as a small child. So the same supreme Lord is present in different forms according to the relationship of the devotees with the Lord. ### human life, misery, spiritual liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6sYfD262dn0?t=326
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The most important form is the human form... It is very rare because there are millions and billions and trillions of living beings in so many different forms... And it is very precious because in the human form of life the most important function is to prepare ourselves to get out of this material existence, the essence of which is misery."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Prabhupada, emphasizing the rarity and importance of human life as an opportunity to escape the inherent misery of material existence.

In the momentous hour of leaving this material body Bhishma Deva set the glorious example concerning the important function of the human form of life. Of all the different species of life in this material world the most important form is the human form. The human form of life. The Bhagavatam says, Durlabham manusham janma. It is very very very rare that a living being in this material world gets the human form. It is very rare because there are millions and billions and trillions of living beings in so many different forms. Insects and birds and animals and aquatics and plants and trees and shrubs and reptiles and all kinds of other life forms are there and they outnumber the human form. Just like if I stand in a particular place, just say I occupy about 6 inches by 6 inches square flat land. Now if I dig just 1 inch down into that 6 inches by 6 inches square there will be millions of microbes inside that. There will be so many worms. There could be so many insects. So many worms. So we have no idea that the human form of life is very rare. It is very rare and it is very precious because in the human form of life the most important function is to prepare ourselves to get out of this material existence the essence of which is misery. The essence of material existence is misery. In any form, in any place, in the material world there are always miseries.

---
### happiness, eternal life, consciousness at death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6sYfD262dn0?t=477
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The subject matter which attracts the dying man becomes the beginning of his next life. This is the Science described in the Bhagavat Gita."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that humans naturally desire happiness and eternal life, which can be attained by preparing oneself in the human form to leave the body in proper consciousness, and that the thoughts at the time of death determine the next life.

And we don't want miseries. We are always, we are always desiring happiness. We always want to be joyful. We always want to be satisfied. We want eternal life. So that is possible by preparing ourselves in the human form of life to quit the body in the way Bhishma Deva is teaching us by preparing ourselves to quit the body in proper consciousness. Then we go to the spiritual world for eternal life without any difficulty. We are directly transferred immediately if we are in the right conscious in the time of death. So it is explained by Prabhupada further, the subject matter which attracts the dying man becomes the beginning of his next life. This is the Science described in the Bhagavat Gita.

**Summary:** The speaker quotes Prabhupada and references the Bhagavad Gita, stating that absorption in thoughts of Krishna at the time of death ensures return to Godhead, ending material suffering and attaining eternal life.

It is said, that ils invecousوا In this body there is a final moment of life in this body. That is called the moment of death. At that time whatever is the subject matter that is attracting the person who is quitting the body, who is dying, that becomes the basis of the beginning of the next life. So Prabhupada says, therefore if one is absorbed in thoughts of the Supreme Lord Sri Krishna, he is sure to go back to Godhead without any doubt. So how is perfection attained in this uniformed life? To be absorbed in thoughts of the Supreme Lord Sri Krishna always, so that even at the time of death we are absorbed in thought of Krishna. We go back to Krishna Loka where we belong. Otherwise if we have some other thoughts we have to take birth again in this material world. Again we will take birth which means that continuation of suffering the miseries of material existence. So if you want to put an end to all material miseries and want eternal life full of bliss and knowledge in this spiritual world in association with Krishna then you should practice absorbing your mind in thoughts of Krishna. And how is this done?

### chanting, bhakti yoga, remembering Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6sYfD262dn0?t=644
**Verse References:** BG 8.5-15
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This is most easily practiced by practicing chanting of Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that chanting Hare Krishna is the easiest way to practice absorbing the mind in Krishna, referencing the Bhagavad Gita's teachings on remembering Krishna at all times, even while performing duties.

Srila Prabhupada explains in the introduction to the Bhagavad Gita, this is most easily practiced by practicing chanting of Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare. So in the Bhagavad Gita this is explained very elaborately in the 8th chapter verses 5 to 15. 5 to 15 Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, whoever at the time of death quits his body remembering me alone, that means remembering Krishna, at once attains my nature. Of this there is no doubt. Krishna's nature is completely transcendental. So one will get spiritual nature. And the general rule is whatever state of being one remembers when he quits his body that state he will attain without fail. So Krishna tells Arjuna, therefore Arjuna should always think of me in the form of Krishna and at the same time carry out your prescribed duty of fighting with your activities dedicated to me and your mind and intelligence. And if your mind is fixed on me you will attain me without doubt. Tasmat sarveshu kaaleshu all the time if we are practiced thinking of Krishna by mind being absorbed in Krishna even while doing our routine duties. Maa Manusmara Yudhya Cha. Krishna says two things, you remember me and you also do your duties. So that is to be practiced. That has to be practiced. So Prabhupada says you practice chanting Hare Krishna. So even while you are doing ordinary work you practice chanting Hare Krishna. It is a matter of the consciousness, the background consciousness with which we do any activity. That is important. He who meditates on the Supreme Personality of Godhead, his mind constantly engaged in remembering me, undeviated from the path he, O Arjuna is sure to reach me.

### bhakti yoga, jnana yoga, ashtanga yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6sYfD262dn0?t=784
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Bhakti Yoga is the easy process. So therefore the Bhagavad Gita recommends practice Bhakti Yoga."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Bhakti Yoga with Ashtanga Yoga and Jnana Yoga, highlighting Bhakti Yoga as the easiest path to Godhead, particularly through chanting the Hare Krishna mantra, and concludes the lecture.

So Krishna is describing and in the scriptures there are two different recommendations. One is the method of Ashtanga Yoga. Other one is the method of Bhakti Yoga. So Ashtanga Yoga method is one who at the time of death fixes his life air between the eyebrows, between the eyebrows and in full devotion engages himself in remembering the Supreme Lord will certainly attain to the Supreme Personality of Godhead. This is the Ashtanga Yoga method. And then there are the Jnanis who practice chanting Om. For them it is described, persons learned in the Vedas, Jnanis, they should study the Vedas, they should study the Vedas, who utter Omkara and who enter into Brahman and who are great sages in the renounced order enter into Brahman. So this is three different, the Jnana process, the Yoga process, Ashtanga Yoga process and the Bhakti process. The Bhakti process is simply to be engaged in devotional service and in that state of always being engaged in devotional service one simply quits the body whenever, anytime and simply goes back to Godhead. So that is the advantage for the Bhakti Yogis as opposed to the Ashtanga Yogis and the Jnana Yogis. The Jnana Yogis very very difficult process. Ashtanga Yoga also very difficult process. Bhakti Yoga is the easy process. So therefore the Bhagavad Gita recommends practice Bhakti Yoga. So therefore the Bhagavad Gita recommends practice Bhakti Yoga. And in Bhakti Yoga the easiest method of practicing Bhakti is also described as chanting the Hare Krishna Mantra. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.
