**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### devotion, transcendentalism, Krishna's leela
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IFYbl2ZtakQ?t=35
**Verse References:** SB 1.9.38
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "May He, Lord Sri Krishna, the personality of Godhead who awards salvation, be my ultimate destination."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces verse 38 from the 9th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Bhishma Deva's prayers to Lord Krishna and the transcendental nature of their exchange on the battlefield.

Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare, Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya, Narayanam Namaskritya, Naram Jai Vanarottamam, Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tatho Jayam Udhirayet. In this session, we will be discussing the 38th verse of the 9th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Bhishma Deva. Shita Vishikha Hato Vishirna Damsaha Shataja Paripluta Atatayinome Prasabham Abhisasara Madvadhartham Sabhavatume Bhagavan Gathir Mukundaha. The meaning of this verse is, May He, Lord Sri Krishna, the personification, the personality of Godhead who awards salvation, be my ultimate destination. On the battlefield, He charged me as if angry because of the wounds dealt by my sharp arrows. His shield was scattered and His body was smeared with blood due to the wounds.

---
### devotion, Krishna's leela, enemy as devotee
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IFYbl2ZtakQ?t=106
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The astounding feature of such dealings between the Lord and his devotees is that a devotee can please the Lord by playing the part of an enemy."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the apparent enmity between Bhishma and Krishna is a leela, a transcendental exchange of love, where Bhishma serves Krishna even by acting as an enemy, a concept elaborated upon by Srila Prabhupada.

So this whole description we should remember that that exchange between Bhishma and Krishna is an exchange between the Lord and His beloved devotee. Krishna's form is completely transcendental. There is no possibility of any injury for Krishna's transcendental body. But apparently it looked like Bhishma's arrows were striking Krishna's body and there were some wounds because of the arrow piercing the body. It is actually a leela of the Lord because Bhishma relates with Krishna in the virya rasa relationship. So therefore this show of apparently Bhishma enduring making wounds on the body of Krishna. So Srila Prabhupada explains this in the purport, The dealings of Lord Krishna and Bhishma Deva on the battlefield of Kurukshetra are interesting because the activities of Lord Sri Krishna appeared to be partial to Arjuna and at enmity with Bhishma Deva. But factually all this was especially meant to show special favor to Bhishma Deva. A great devotee of the Lord. You see. For a common man, the battle is going on between Pandavas and Kauravas. They are enemies. Krishna is on the side of the Pandavas and Bhishma is on the side of the Kauravas. So Krishna, Bhishma are enemies for a common man's understanding. But the confidential understanding is Krishna is the Supreme Lord and his beloved devotees Arjuna and Bhishma both are there on the battlefield. So Krishna by his inconceivable potency he is able to show favor special favor to Bhishma and at the same time protecting Arjuna completely because Arjuna is his devotee also. So Srila Prabhupada says the astounding feature of such dealings between the Lord and his devotees is that a devotee can please the Lord by playing the part of an enemy.

---
### devotion, Krishna's supremacy, unconquerable
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IFYbl2ZtakQ?t=285
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord being absolute can accept service from his pure devotee even in the garb of an enemy."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on how Krishna, being absolute, accepts service from devotees in any form, even as an enemy, because devotion is what matters; Krishna is unconquerable and no one can harm Him, so the apparent danger is part of His pastimes.

See the wonderful description here. That a devotee is serving the Lord by becoming the Lord's enemy. The Lord being absolute can accept service from his pure devotee even in the garb of an enemy. Devotee whatever role he plays. Krishna can accept the service because it is with devotion. A devotee serves Krishna always with devotion. Krishna wants that bhakti. Whether somebody serves Krishna as an enemy or as a friend in any capacity. The Supreme Lord cannot have any enemy. Nor can a so-called enemy harm Krishna. Why? Because Krishna is Ajita or unconquerable. He is infinitely powerful. And everyone else is if at all they have any power that is given by Krishna and it is very very very insignificant power that Jeevas have. So how can Krishna's life be in danger? Krishna is immortal. Krishna is unconquerable. Krishna is infinite power. So this should always be remembered. Don't make the mistake of making material calculations and thinking that Krishna's life was in danger. No. But still Krishna's pastimes are such that he takes pleasure when his pure devotee beats him like an enemy or rebukes him from a superior position. Although no one can be superior to the Lord.

---
### devotion, Krishna's humility, loving exchange
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IFYbl2ZtakQ?t=393
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In Krishna's dealings with his devotees, when Krishna assumes the inferior position, Krishna doesn't become inferior. He remains the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of Mother Yashoda scolding Krishna to illustrate how Krishna assumes an inferior position in His dealings with devotees out of love, without diminishing His supreme status; these confidential exchanges are understood only by devotees.

In Krishna's dealings with his devotees when Krishna assumes the inferior position Krishna doesn't become inferior. He remains the Supreme Lord. Just like Mother Yashoda sometimes scolds Krishna. So Krishna as a small child begins to cry apparently being very much affected by Yashoda's scolding. But actually has Krishna's position changed? He appears to be an inferior. An inferior position as a small child. A helpless child. But actually he is the Supreme Lord. And Yashoda is always the devotee who out of love wants to serve Krishna as a mother. It is a loving exchange between Krishna and his devotee. So this is not easily understood by everyone. Only a devotee can understand this confidential dealings of the Lord with his devotees on the platform of pure love.

---
### devotion, Bhagavatam, pure devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IFYbl2ZtakQ?t=475
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhagavatam is the particular exclusive literature which is telling us about pure devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that these exchanges are rooted in love between the Lord and His devotees, and that Srimad Bhagavatam is unique in its exclusive focus on pure devotional service, devoid of karma, jnana, or ashtanga yoga.

Mind you these exchanges are simply happening out of the Lord's love for his devotee and the devotee's love for the Lord. These are some of the transcendental reciprocatory dealings of the devotee with the Lord. Bhagavatam is the particular exclusive literature which is telling us about pure devotional service. That's why it's called Amala Purana. The entire subject matter of the Bhagavatam is only this pure devotional service. And nothing else. There is nothing about karma, jnana, ashtanga yoga, no. It's only pure devotional service. Because throughout the Bhagavatam the description is of the Supreme Lord and his interactions with his pure devotees. He has innumerable devotees. And particularly Bhagavatam describes the transcendental activities which are completely free from any material contamination. Those who have no information of pure devotional service cannot penetrate into the mystery of such dealings.

---
### devotion, Bhagavatam, Krishna's supremacy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IFYbl2ZtakQ?t=577
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna's supremacy is eternally true under all circumstances. He remains the supreme Lord always."
**Summary:** The speaker stresses the importance of learning Bhagavatam from pure devotees like Prabhupada, who can explain the mysteries of these dealings; Bhishma's role was to bewilder non-devotees, who misunderstand Krishna's supremacy as mere sentiment.

So you have to hear this Bhagavatam from the pure devotees. Bhagavatam has to be learned from a Bhagavata. Those who know the mystery of such dealings only they can explain properly. Like Prabhupada is explaining for us. The elaborate purports Prabhupada has written is to help us understand what is the nature of pure devotional service. Bhishma Deva played the part of a valiant warrior. And he purposely pierced the body of the Lord so that to the common eyes it appeared that the Lord was wounded. I have already explained this point. But factually all this was to bewilder the non-devotees. Non-devotees always think that the devotees are sentimental people who think that Krishna is some supreme God. No, it is not the devotee's sentimental feeling because of which Krishna is considered as the supreme. No, Krishna's supremacy is eternally true under all circumstances. He remains the supreme Lord always. In the material world, in the spiritual world, before the creation, during the maintenance, during destruction, after the destruction. He is always the supreme Lord.

---
### Krishna's pastimes, devotee bewilderment, Krishna's power
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IFYbl2ZtakQ?t=671
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The non-devotees are always bewildered when Krishna appears in any incarnation."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna's position is eternally supreme, and His pastimes are for the pleasure of His devotees; non-devotees are often bewildered by Krishna's incarnations and actions, but Krishna proves His power, as illustrated by the story of Indra and the Govardhan hill.

That's his unique position. Eternally true. So what happens to Krishna's position? Nothing happens to his position. But as part of his pastimes he assumes different forms, he has different interactions, different types of reciprocation with his beloved pure devotees. Only with his pure devotees he has his exchanges. And the non-devotees are always bewildered when Krishna appears in any incarnation. The non-devotees are bewildered. They can't make out who is this Krishna. Because they make material calculations and they think Krishna is an ordinary person. But some actions of Krishna prove even to the non-devotees that Krishna is not an ordinary person. Indra, he sent torrential rain, devastating rainfall. That's the correct explanation, description. Devastating rain in Vrindavan. And Krishna simply lifted the Govardhan hill and held it up like an umbrella to protect the inhabitants against devastating rainfall. So then Indra came to his senses. Who is this small boy? He is my lord. He is my master. He is a supreme personality of Godhead. I am foolishly thinking he is an ordinary boy. So, every time a non-devotee or even sometimes the devatas they mistake Krishna to be an ordinary person or a person of this world. Krishna proves to them by his actions that he is the ultimate controller, supremely powerful personality of Godhead.

---
### devotion, Krishna's beauty, Bhishma's love
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IFYbl2ZtakQ?t=796
**Verse References:** SB 1.9.38
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "May that Lord, Bhagavan Mukundaha be my ultimate destination."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by reiterating that Krishna's all-spiritual body cannot be wounded, and a devotee cannot be His enemy; Bhishma's prayer to have Krishna as his ultimate destination proves his devotion, and Krishna's appearance with wounds was for Bhishma's transcendental pleasure.

The all-spiritual body of the Supreme Lord cannot be wounded. This we should always remember. Never forget this. And a devotee cannot become the enemy of the Lord. Bhishma Deva cannot become the enemy. Had it been so, Bhishma Deva would not have desired to have the very same Lord as the ultimate destination of his life. What is the prayer here? Sabhavatume Bhagavan Gathir Mukundaha. That very Lord who on the battlefield was on the opposite side, enemy's side. May that Lord, Bhagavan Mukundaha be my ultimate destination. Had Bhishma Deva been so, had Bhishma Deva been an enemy of the Lord, Lord Krishna could have annihilated him even without moving. Does Krishna require to take one wheel of a chariot, break his promise, get down from the chariot and attack Bhishma? Is it necessary for Krishna, the Supreme Lord to do that? No. So there was no need to come before Bhishma Deva with blood and wounds. But he did so because the warrior devotee wanted to see the transcendental beauty of the Lord decorated with wounds created by a pure devotee. Krishna is beautiful regardless of the form he assumes. Now this is the form he has assumed for the sake of his devotee. And this form is also very very beautiful especially for Bhishma Deva. So, Bhishma Deva is adoring that very scene where Krishna appeared in front of Bhishma with wounds and carrying a weapon to attack Bhishma. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### Bhishma, Krishna, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/qKXMx2cNzHw?t=41
**Verse References:** SB 1.9.39
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "At the moment of death, let my ultimate attraction be to Shri Krishna, the personality of Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam (1.9.39) which contains Bhishma Deva's prayer expressing his desire to focus on Krishna at the time of death, specifically as the chariot driver of Arjuna, and explains that those who saw Krishna on the battlefield attained their original forms after death.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Devi Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jaya Mudhirayet. In this session, we will be discussing from the 39th verse of the 9th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, continuing the prayers by Bhishma Deva. Vijaya Ratha Kutumba Aatta Totre Dhritahaya Rashminita Sriyekshaniye Bhagavati Rati Rastume Mumurshor Yamiha Nirikshahata Gataswaroopam. The meaning of this verse is, at the moment of death, let my ultimate attraction be to Shri Krishna, the personality of Godhead. I concentrate my mind upon the chariot driver of Arjuna, who stood with a whip in his right hand and a bridle rope in his left, who was very careful to give protection to Arjuna's chariot by all means. Those who saw him on the battlefield of Kurukshetra attained their original forms after death.

### Krishna, devotion, samadhi
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/qKXMx2cNzHw?t=118
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, this is the perfection of life to attain one's Swaroopa, the original form."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the verse, explaining that everyone has an eternal relationship with Krishna and the goal of life is to attain that relationship through Bhakti Yoga. He contrasts the difficult Ashtanga Yoga process with the ease with which a devotee attains Samadhi by constantly remembering Krishna.

Just try to picturize, Krishna is having a whip in his right hand, he is having the reins of the horses in his left hand, he is driving the chariot of Arjuna, and whoever saw Krishna on the battlefield, they attained their original forms after death. So, this is the perfection of life to attain one's Swaroopa, the original form. Each one of us, we belong to the spiritual world. In the spiritual world, we have a specific eternal relationship with Krishna, and our goal of life should be to attain that eternal relationship after quitting this body, by preparing through practice of Bhakti Yoga. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains, a pure devotee of the Lord constantly sees the presence of the Lord within himself, because of being transcendentally related by loving service. Such a pure devotee cannot forget the Lord for a moment. Even for a moment, a pure devotee does not forget the Lord. And the conditioned souls have almost completely forgotten the Supreme Lord, even though they also have a relationship, eternal relationship. So, a pure devotee always being Krishna conscious is called trance, Samadhi. The mystic yogi tries to concentrate upon the super soul by controlling the senses from all other engagements, and thus he ultimately attains Samadhi. The Ashtanga Yoga process is a difficult process, very very difficult process. But with great austerity, Ashtanga Yogis try to control their senses, their mind, withdraw the mind from all sense objects, focus it upon the form of the Lord in the heart, and fix it on this super soul, and become absorbed in meditation on this form of the super soul in the heart. And in that way, gradually, they attain Samadhi. A devotee more easily attains Samadhi or trance, by constantly remembering the Lord's personal feature along with His holy name, fame, pastimes etc. What is Vishwadevar doing now?

### pastimes, universes, eternity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/qKXMx2cNzHw?t=456
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is continuously performing his pastimes in one universe after another universe after another universe after that, another universe, like that."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of the sun rising and setting in different parts of the world to explain how Krishna's pastimes are continuously occurring in different universes. He describes the sequence of Krishna's pastimes, from his birth to his departure, and how they move from one universe to another.

After it is over in this universe, in then another universe the same past time continues. Then after it is over in that universe, then another universe. So, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu explains this eternal past times in the material world as just like the sun is continuously rising in different parts of the world as the time is progressing. Let us say now it is evening 7.40. Okay. In Bangalore or India 7.40 p.m. But in some other part of the world it is 6.40 p.m. Another part of the world it is 8.40 p.m. So, the sun appears to be moving but actually it is all relative motion. And the sun is continuously rising or setting in different parts of the world as time progresses. So similarly, Krishna is continuously performing his pastimes in one universe after another universe after another universe after that, another universe, like that. The pastimes of the Lord beginning from his birth at the prison house of Kamsa up to the Leela at the end. This is described in the eleventh canto, the pastime of the Lord, ending his pastimes in this material world and going back to his spiritual above. All these pastimes all move one after another in all the universes, just as the clock hand moves from one point to another.

### devotees, Krishna, death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/qKXMx2cNzHw?t=586
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "At the time of quitting the body, at the time of death, last moment of life in this body, whatever the state of consciousness, that same state one will attain without fail in the life after death."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that not everyone on the battlefield of Kurukshetra was an eternal companion of Krishna, only select devotees like the Pandavas and Bhishma. He highlights Bhishma's unique perspective of Krishna as Parthasarathi and explains that Krishna displays different forms for different devotees. He then discusses how seeing Krishna at the time of death allows one to attain their original spiritual form, referencing the importance of one's consciousness at the time of death.

And in such pastimes his associates like the Pandavas and Bhishma are constant eternal companions. Not all the persons on the battlefield of Kurukshetra are eternal companions, only some select a few devotees are eternal companions, just like the Pandavas and Bhishma as mentioned here. So Bhishma Deva never forgot the beautiful feature of the Lord as Partha Sarathi, which even Arjuna could not see. Arjuna was being the Lord. So Arjuna could not see that form as Partha Sarathi, which Bhishma saw. Arjuna was behind the beautiful Partha Sarathi while Bhishma Deva was just in front of the Lord. As far as the military feature of the Lord is concerned, Bhishma Deva observed this with more relish than Arjuna. So this is an important point to note. For the sake of Bhishma, Krishna is displaying the particular form which Bhishma relates with. The Lord has so many forms, unlimited number of forms, because he has unlimited devotees, and each devotee relates with the Lord in a particular form, and that form the Lord displays for that particular devotee. Now Srila Prabhupada explains the point that, everyone attained their original spiritual form after death, because Krishna was present on the battlefield when they were dying. So Srila Prabhupada says, all the soldiers and persons on the battlefield of Kurukshetra attained their original spiritual form, like the Lord, after their death. Because by the causeless mercy of the Lord, they were able to see him face to face on that occasion, at the time of death. yamyam vapi smaranbhavam tyajati ante kalevaram At the time of quitting the body, at the time of death, last moment of life in this body, whatever the state of consciousness, that same state one will attain without fail in the life after death. The soul is eternal. But what life will the soul attain after quitting this body? That depends on the consciousness in time of death. So, whoever saw the Lord at the time of his death face to face, because the Lord is completely transcendental, spiritual, they all attained their original eternal spiritual form.

The meaning of this verse is: seeing him, that means seeing Bhishma lying on the ground like a demigod fallen from the sky, the Pandava King Yudhishthira along with his younger brothers and Lord Krishna bowed down before him. Sreelah Prabhupada explains Lord Krishna was also a younger cousin of Maharaja Yudhishthira as well as the intimate friend. Lord Krishna and a better beloved Rama Yudhishthir as well as the intimate friend Devasini as well of Arjuna. Krishna was related to the Pandavas as a younger cousin because by age Krishna was younger than Yudhishthira Maharaj and Yudhishthira Maharaj's mother Kunti and Krishna's father Vasudeva were sister and brother, Kunti and Vasudeva. So therefore Krishna was the younger cousin of Yudhishthira Maharaj. Krishna was also related to Arjuna in the same way as a cousin but more importantly Krishna and Arjuna were intimate friends, intimate friends. So in addition to being a cousin Arjuna was also intimate friend of Krishna. But all the family members of the Pandavas knew Lord Krishna as the Supreme Personality of Godhead. All the five brothers, Yudhishthira, Bhima, Nakula, Sahadeva, Arjuna, everyone knew Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead and even Draupadi, Kunti, everyone knew Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

### humility, Krishna, behavior, example

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker addresses the apparent contradiction of Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, bowing down to Bhishma, explaining that Krishna behaves in a humanly way to set an example.

The Lord, although conscious of His supreme position, always behaved in a humanly custom and so He also bowed down before the dying Bhishma Deva as if He, that means Krishna, were one of the younger brothers of King Yudhishthira. Now this is very significant that when the Pandavas have come to see Bhishma, Bhishma is elderly person compared to the Pandavas, so Pandavas grandfather, Pandu's father, father's brother. So in that way the Pandavas are perfectly right or correct in bowing down to Bhishma Deva, grandsons they are offering their respects to the elderly grandfather, well-wisher and affectionate grandfather. But why is Krishna bowing down before Bhishma when Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead? Now we have to carefully understand Srila Prabhupada explains even though Krishna is always conscious He is the Supreme Personality of Godhead, He is eternally so, but still He behaves in a humanly way when He comes to this material world for the sake of His pastimes. So Krishna behaves in a particular way in a particular situation which we have to understand from the scriptures as explained by the acharyas.

Now Krishna in the morning when He wakes up, He wakes up very early even though He is lying comfortably with His wife, the queen but He wakes up and He performs His duties as a householder. Now these duties are described in the Srimad Bhagavatam 10th cantum, these duties are performed by Krishna to teach how an ideal householder to perform householder's duties. He would go and pay respect to His mothers. Of course, His mother is Devaki. But Vasudeva, His father had 16 queens. So, the other queens of Vasudeva were stepmothers of Krishna. Nonetheless, Krishna would respect all the stepmothers on an equal level with Devaki, His own mother. So, this is ideal behavior of Krishna. They all know Krishna is the Supreme Personality of God. But they also know Krishna is behaving in this way to teach by His own example how an ideal householder should carry out the duties of a householder. Grahasta Ashrama. Krishna is teaching by His own example. He would give cows in charity to the brahmanas. Now, the brahmanas know Krishna is the Supreme Personality of God. Krishna has no obligation to anybody. He is in fact the maintainer of everyone. So, what obligation can He have for any brahmana or for any other living being? He is the maintainer of all the living beings. Eko bahunam yo vidhati kama. Krishna is teaching by His own example, but He must teach by His own example. Acharya. Acharya means one who teaches by his own behavior, by his own actions.

**Key Quote:** "When Brahma stole the calves and the cowherd boys, Krishna actually expanded Himself as a calves and cowherd boys... So like this, Krishna's second set of activities are those to help and meant for helping us understand Krishna is the Supreme Personality of God."
**Summary:** The speaker provides another example of Krishna's divine activities, recounting the story of Brahma stealing the calves and cowherd boys, and concludes by reiterating that Krishna's bowing down to Bhishma teaches respect for elders.

We understand such activities of Krishna as those of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. When Brahma stole the calves and the cowherd boys, Krishna actually expanded Himself as a calves and cowherd boys. Now Brahma came back after hiding those calves and cowherd boys who were stolen by Brahma using Brahma's mystic power. He was hiding them somewhere and when Brahma came back after one moment of his time, already one year had passed on the earth and Krishna had expanded Himself so nobody knew that the cowherd boys and the calves, original cowherd boys and calves were kidnapped by Brahma and they were hidden by Brahma in some place away from the earth planet and they were all sleeping under the mystic power of Brahma. A spell cast by Brahma by Brahma's mystic power but nobody knows in Vrindavan. How is that? Krishna has expanded exactly resembling each of the cowherd boys and each of the calves and Brahma came and saw there is a second set of the same cowherd boys and calves playing with Krishna. So Brahma was bewildered. He knows he has hidden there, cows and cowherd boys, he has stolen in his particular place and he knows by his mystic power they are all sleeping under the spell of his mystic power. But Brahma also seeing a second set and who are these second set of cowherd boys and calves. Brahma is wondering. So like this, Krishna's second set of activities are those to help and meant for helping us understand Krishna is the Supreme Personality of God so we have to understand when Krishna bows down to Bhishma Deva it is teaching us how a younger one should show respect to an elderly superior worship of personality. I'll stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.9.40 | 09-05-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### bhakti, Gopis' devotion, pure devotion, conjugal love
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jR_9igBGf2Y?t=37
**Verse References:** SB 1.9.40
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They simply want to please Krishna. That's all."
**Summary:** This segment explains Bhishma Deva's prayer to Krishna, focusing on the Gopis' unparalleled pure devotion characterized by selfless love and a desire only to please Krishna, contrasting it with other forms of worship motivated by personal gain or knowledge.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Jai Vanarottamam Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jaya Mudhirayet. In this session, we will be discussing the 40th verse of the 9th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Lalitha Gati Vilasa Valguhasa Pranaya Niriksana Kalpitoru Manaha Krita Manu Krita Vatya Unma Dandhaha Prakritim Agan Kilayasya Gopa Vadhvaha. The meaning of this verse is, let my mind be fixed upon Lord Sri Krishna, whose motions and smiles of love attracted the damsels of Raja Dharma, the Gopis. The damsels imitated the characteristic movements of the Lord after his disappearance from the Rasa dance. This is yet another precept. This is the same prayer offered by Bhishma Deva to Lord Krishna. Bhishma is fixing his mind upon Lord Sri Krishna and remembering the different pastimes of Krishna. Smaranam, remembering the pastimes of Krishna, remembering the form of Krishna, remembering the name of Krishna, remembering the qualities of Krishna. This is bhakti. This is the practice of bhakti. Here, specifically, Bhishma Deva is remembering Krishna's pastimes with his topmost devotees, the Gopis of Vrindavan. So, in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains what is the characteristic of the Gopis' devotion to Krishna. It is explained by Srila Prabhupada in the purport, by intense ecstasy in loving service, the damsels of Vraja Bhumi, that is the Gopis, attained qualitative oneness with the Lord by dancing with him on an equal level, by embracing him in nuptial love, by smiling at him, by looking at him in joke, and by looking at him with a loving attitude. The Gopis are in a relationship with Krishna in conjugal love. Conjugal love means love between opposite genders. Actually, Krishna is the only male, and everyone else is female. Krishna is the only Purusha, everyone else is Prakriti. So, this love between Krishna and the devotees is natural, is our constitutional position. So, among the devotees of Krishna, the topmost devotees are the Gopis. Now, Srila Prabhupada says, the relation of the Lord with Arjuna is undoubtedly praiseworthy for devotees like Bhishma Deva, because Arjuna's friendship with the Lord was very, very intimate friendship. It was transcendental relationship, not material friendship. But, the relation of the Gopis with the Lord is still more praiseworthy because of their still more purified loving service. Now, how is Gopis' devotional service more purified? To understand that, Srila Prabhupada explains, that according to Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, ramyakachit upasana vrajavadhu vargena yakalpita. The meaning of this is, this is what Chaitanya Mahaprabhu says about the Gopis' devotional service rendered to Lord Krishna. There is no better method of worship than the system invented by the Gopis. Worship of Krishna. The best method of worship is that which was performed by the Gopis of Vrindavan. So, the Gopis' invention of worshipping Krishna was to remain always Krishna conscious without any material profit. That is the super excellence of the Gopis. Super excellent devotional service. They never expected any return from Krishna. That was not their business. Their attitude was not like, Krishna, we have done so much for you. Can you give us some benediction, Krishna? No. They never expected any benediction from Krishna. Though Krishna is the Supreme Lord, who is the only giver of all kinds of benedictions, but the Gopis, they never expected any benediction from Krishna. Gopis' attitude was that you take whatever you like from us, but we do not ask anything in exchange, in return. Nothing. We want nothing. They simply want to please Krishna. That's all. They only want to see Krishna happy. That's all. We have got everything to give you, and we have no need to ask you anything at all. This is the Gopis' worship.

---
### devotion, pure devotion, motives for worship, Gopis' devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jR_9igBGf2Y?t=481
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whereas, the pure devotees do not want anything in return for their worship or in return for their service to Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the Gopis' pure devotion with other types of devotees who approach Krishna with specific desires or motives, such as relief from distress, wealth, knowledge, or curiosity, highlighting that pure devotees seek nothing in return for their service.

Generally, people worship the Supreme Lord either because they are Artha, Artha means they are in distress, or they are in need, or they are Artharthi, or they are in desiring some wealth, some money. So, they want some relief from some suffering, or they want some money. Two classes of people, Artha and Artharthi. And another two classes of people who also worship God, Jignasu and Jnani. Jignasu means they are inquisitive to know who is this God, or who is God. Curiosity. And Jnani are the ones who are after pure knowledge of God. In any case, all these four categories of persons who approach Krishna, they are classified as devotees, but they want something in exchange for their worship, for their devotion. They want something. Whereas, the pure devotees do not want anything in return for their worship or in return for their service to Krishna. Because they are approaching Krishna, even such devotees who want something in exchange are classified as devotees. And they do get some spiritual benefit because of approaching Krishna. But they are not pure devotees. Whenever the service is rendered, devotional service is rendered with some motive, expectation of something in return, then the service is not considered pure devotional service. It is mixed devotional service. So, the gopis' activities in devotional service are on the supreme platform of pure devotion. This is the position of gopis. This is the position of the gopis. Therefore, Prabhupāda says, the loving service of the gopis is the topmost, is most praiseworthy. And, the gopis, by their service, they practically became one with the Lord of attaining equal footing with the Lord.

---
### Gopis' devotion, mercy, individuality, selflessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jR_9igBGf2Y?t=677
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Devotees generally want mercy of Krishna and his pure devotees."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Bhishma's desire for the Gopis' mercy, emphasizing that Krishna is pleased when his devotees are glorified. It contrasts the Gopis' selfless devotion with the impersonalists' desire to merge with God, highlighting the importance of retaining individuality to serve Krishna, and Krishna's unwavering support for his devotees.

Now, Bhīṣma's aspiration to remember the gopis at the last stage of his life is to have their mercy also. Devotees generally want mercy of Krishna and his pure devotees. Bhīṣma is teaching us. The Lord, Supreme Lord Krishna, is always more satisfied when his pure devotees are glorified. And, therefore, Bhīṣma Deva not only glorified the activities of Arjuna, but he also glorified the activities of the gopis, the topmost devotees of Krishna. Now, the gopis, their equality, which is being described here, is one of perfect ecstasy, in which the differential conception is completely eradicated. Why? Because, in the gopis' service to Kṛṣṇa, the centrist interest of the lover and beloved all respects that is Krishna has nothing to expect from his devotees and the devotees have nothing to expect from Krishna so in this way both are unconditionally loving each other and that is the way their interest becomes one now the Maya bodies they also want to merge into the existence of God they also want to become one but that oneness is an attempt to extinguish their own individuality in a sense they want to become God so it is actually a selfish interest the Maya bodies interest is a selfish interest because they want to become God in a sense whereas the devotees they want to retain their individuality which is always eternally existing and they want to serve God by determining whether or not they should do that so they want to look at their individuality in a idea of that which is a personal project dreams and their lives and their health their their demands or their desires Krishna maintains his devotees Krishna maintains everyone it's not just his devotees he maintains he maintains everyone but particularly his devotees who are completely dependent on him he never lets down his devotees he never lets down anybody but he never lets down his devotees certainly even if the devotees don't have any demands so such devotees are being glorified by Bhishma Deva I'll stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.9.41 | 10-05-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Bhishma's prayer, Krishna's pastimes, Rajasuya Yajna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Zii_8m1Wi4M?t=37
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is yet another prayer by Bhishma Deva to Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the context of Bhishma Deva's prayer to Lord Krishna, focusing on his remembrance of Krishna's pastimes, particularly the Rajasuya Yajna performed by Yudhishthira.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing from the 41st verse of the 9th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Munigana Nripavarya Sankhulentaha Sadasi Yudhishthira Rajasuya Esham Arhanam Upapedha Ikshaniyo Mamadrishi Gochara Esha Aaviratma. The meaning of this verse is: At the Rajasuya Yajna performed by Maharaj Yudhishthira, there was the greatest assembly of all the elite men of the world, the royal and learned orders, and in that great assembly, Lord Sri Krishna was worshipped by one and all as the most exalted personality of Godhead. This happened during my presence, and I remembered the incident in order to keep my mind upon the Lord. This is yet another prayer by Bhishma Deva to Lord Krishna.

---
### Rajasuya Yajna, Yudhishthira, Emperor
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Zii_8m1Wi4M?t=125
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Yudhishthira wanted to glorify Krishna, therefore he performed this Rajasuya Yajna."
**Summary:** This segment explains the Rajasuya Yajna, its purpose for emperors to establish supremacy, and how Yudhishthira's intention was to glorify Krishna, describing the challenge horse tradition and Yudhishthira's acceptance as emperor.

Bhishma is remembering Krishna's different pastimes. So one of the pastimes was the performance of Rajasuya Yajna by Yudhishthira. Generally, the Rajasuya Yajna is performed by a king who is on the level of being the emperor of the world, and he wants to establish his supremacy by performing this Rajasuya Yajna. But Yudhishthira's aim was different because Yudhishthira is a pure devotee of Krishna. Yudhishthira wanted to glorify Krishna, therefore he performed this Rajasuya Yajna. So it is explained by Sri Lakshmi that after gaining victory in the battle of Kurukshetra, Maharaj Yudhishthira, the emperor of the world, performed a Rajasuya sacrificial ceremony. The emperor in those days, upon his ascendance to the throne, would send a challenge horse all over the world to declare his supremacy, and any ruling prince or king was at liberty to accept the challenge and express his tacit willingness either to obey or to disobey the supremacy of the particular king of the world. The world was ruled in different provinces by different kings, but one king was the emperor of the whole world. Generally, the king whose capital was Hastinapura, at least 5000 years back, that was the system. So one who accepted the challenge of the emperor, whichever king, king of a smaller province, he had to fight with the emperor and establish his own supremacy by victory. The defeated challenger would have to sacrifice his life, making a place for another king or ruler. This was the system. So Maharaj Yudhishthira also dispatched such challenging horses all over the world, and every ruling prince and king all over the world accepted Maharaj Yudhishthira's leadership as the emperor of the world in terms of the dynasty, Kuru dynasty. Yudhishthira was the descendant of the emperors of the past.

---
### Vedic system, Social order, Rajasuya Yajna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Zii_8m1Wi4M?t=274
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This was the Vedic system."
**Summary:** This segment describes the invitation of rulers and sages to Yudhishthira's Rajasuya Yajna, highlighting the expense and difficulty of such ceremonies in the current age, and emphasizing the importance of qualified brahmanas and the Vedic social system.

So the ruling kings and the princes, they all knew Yudhishthira to be very very powerful emperor. So they accepted his leadership. After this, all the rulers of the world under the regime of Maharaj Yudhishthira were invited to participate in the great sacrificial ceremony of Rajasuya. Such performances required hundreds of millions of dollars, and it was not an easy job for a petty king. Such a sacrificial ceremony being too expensive and also difficult to perform under the present circumstances is now impossible in this age of Kali. This is something that happened in Dwapar Yuga. Nor can anyone secure the required expert priesthood to take charge of the ceremony. It's not just a question of money alone. Even qualified brahmanas are unavailable for performing such sacrifices because the qualified brahmana has to chant the mantra with purity. Then only the mantra will have the potency to give the benefit to the performer of the sacrifice. After being invited, all the kings and great learned sages of the world assembled in the capital of Maharaj Yudhishthira in Hastinapura. The learned society included the great philosophers, religionists, physicians, scientists, and all great sages were invited. That is to say, the brahmanas and the Kshatriyas were the top most leading men in society, and they were all invited to participate in the assembly. This was the Vedic system.

---
### Social change, Krishna's beauty, Assembly
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Zii_8m1Wi4M?t=399
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Everyone wanted to see Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the changing social dynamics, the dominance of Vaishyas in the modern world, and the unparalleled beauty of Krishna in the Rajasuya assembly, where everyone's attention was drawn to Him.

The Vaishyas and Shudras were unimportant elements in society, and they are not mentioned here. The Vaishyas and Shudras were not mentioned due to change of social activities. In the modern age, the importance of men has also changed in terms of occupational positions. Today, practically the whole world is run by the Vaishyas. The whole world is run by Vaishyas. There are no qualified brahmanas or Kshatriyas to occupy the positions of power or to guide those in positions of power. So the times have changed, and Prabhupada explains that during that time, Yudhishthira Maharaj invited all the kings and the learned sages and the great brahmanas for this Rajasuya Yajna. In that great assembly, Lord Sri Krishna was the cynosure of all the people. Everyone wanted to see Lord Krishna, and everyone wanted to pay his humble respects to the Lord. Now elsewhere it is described that when Krishna was elected the president of the assembly in that Rajasuya sacrifice, where all great personalities had assembled, everyone wanted to see Lord Krishna. Everyone's eyes were glued on the beautiful, exceptionally beautiful form of Krishna. They all began to wonder, probably this beauty is not to be found anywhere in the whole universe. So very beautiful, they thought this personality is the most beautiful in the whole universe. This personality is the most beautiful creation of Brahma. That's what they thought.

---
### Krishna's beauty, Transcendental, Impersonal meditation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Zii_8m1Wi4M?t=541
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is the only exception in that assembly who is not a creation of Brahma."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts relative material beauty with Krishna's original transcendental beauty, emphasizing that Krishna is not a creation of Brahma and highlighting the difficulty of impersonal meditation compared to meditating on Krishna's form and pastimes.

Actually, beauty in the material world is relative. That's a fact. But Krishna is not a created person. He is not a creation of Brahma. Krishna is the only exception in that assembly who is not a creation of Brahma. Krishna's beauty is his original transcendental beauty, his transcendental supreme beauty. So it is not at all surprising for devotees that Krishna was the center of attraction in that entire assembly. Now Bhishma Deva was also present in that assembly, and he remembered all this and was glad that his worshipful Lord, the personality of Godhead, was present before him in his actual formal presence. Krishna, the Supreme Lord, Bhishma knew the Supreme Lord. He was actually present in front of Bhishma, and Bhishma remembered this incident. So to meditate on the Supreme Lord means to meditate on the activities, the form, the pastimes, the name and fame of the Supreme Lord. Now this type of meditation is what is performed by the devotees of Krishna. Now this type of meditation in Bhakti is easier than the imaginary meditation on the impersonal feature of the Supreme. This is stated in the Bhagavad Gita that to meditate upon the impersonal feature of the Supreme is very very very difficult.

---
### Impersonal meditation, Difficulty, Devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Zii_8m1Wi4M?t=664
**Verse References:** BG 12.5
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees
**Key Quote:** "To meditate upon the impersonal feature of the Supreme is very, very difficult."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the difficulty of meditating on the impersonal feature of the Supreme, describing it as a negative process of denying material attributes and ultimately a waste of time, contrasting it with the easier path of devotional meditation.

There are people who are attached to the impersonal feature of the Supreme, and they attempt to meditate on the impersonal feature. That is very very difficult because the impersonal feature is described in a negative way in the Bhagavad Gita. Also, it is mentioned to meditate on the Supreme, who is inconceivable, who is not having any material form, is formless, is not having any material attributes, is not having any material body, like that, denying all material attributes, qualities, material form, material names, etc. So what can they meditate upon really speaking? So they imagine, they try to speculate and imagine something which is beyond everything material, and that is the impersonal meditation, and that is very very difficult. Srila Prabhupada says it is practically no meditation at all or simply a waste of time because very seldom is the desired result obtained. Meditation means to fix the mind completely upon the Supreme.

---
### Devotional meditation, Bhakti Yoga, Lord's pastimes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Zii_8m1Wi4M?t=772
**Verse References:** BG 12
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Thinking of the Lord's pastimes is same as thinking of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts impersonal meditation with devotional meditation, explaining how devotees can easily meditate on the Lord's form, pastimes, and qualities, making the Lord easily approachable, and emphasizing the non-difference between the Lord and His transcendental activities.

Now when there is no definite understanding of the features of the Supreme, the spiritual mind, the spiritual attributes, then it is very difficult to fix the mind. The devotees' meditation, however, upon the Lord's factual form, factual pastimes, factual qualities is very easily done by the devotees, and therefore the Lord is easily approached by the devotees. This is the way it is also explained in the Bhagavad Gita in the twelfth chapter itself, the chapter on devotional service, Bhakti Yoga. Devotees very easily approach the Lord. The Lord is non-different from his transcendental activities in the spiritual platform. The Lord is non-different from his transcendental activities. The Lord is absolute. The Lord is absolute means he is identical with his name, with his form, with his qualities, with his pastimes, with his paraphernalia, with his pure devotees. He is identical. So thinking of the Lord's pastimes is same as thinking of the Lord. So that is what Bhishma, he was doing, remembering Krishna's pastimes.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.9.42 | 11-05-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### prayer, transcendence, meditation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n1imoer6Oe0?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 1.9.42
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Now I can meditate with full concentration upon that one Lord Sri Krishna now present before me because now I have transcended the misconceptions of duality in regard to his presence in everyone's heart, even in the hearts of the mental speculators."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam 1.9.42, explaining it as Bhishma Deva's last prayer before leaving his body, focusing on transcending duality and meditating on Krishna.

Om Agyana Timiraandhasya Gyananjan Shalakaya Chakshurunmeelitam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Svamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatyade Shatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Srivasa Adi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Devi Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jaya Mudhirayet. In this session, we will be discussing from the 46th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, from the 2nd verse of the 9th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam: Tamimam Aham Ajam Sharira Bhajam Hridi Hridi Dishtitam Atmakalpitanam Pratidrisham Iva Naikadharkham Ekam Samadhi Gatosvi Vidhuta Bhedam Ohaha. The meaning of this verse is, now I can meditate with full concentration upon that one Lord Sri Krishna now present before me because now I have transcended the misconceptions of duality in regard to his presence in everyone's heart, even in the hearts of the mental speculators. He is in everyone's heart. The sun may be perceived differently, but the sun is one. This is the last prayer by Bhishma Deva in this series of prayers given in this chapter.

---
### misconceptions, Krishna's expansions, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n1imoer6Oe0?t=117
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lord Shri Krishna is the one absolute supreme personality of Godhead. God is one, Krishna is one, supreme absolute truth is one, but he has expanded himself into his multi-plenary portions by his inconceivable energy."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Bhishma Deva's prayer, explaining common misconceptions about Krishna's presence in everyone's heart and how pure devotees understand Krishna's expansions and energies.

Bhishma is offering this prayer to Krishna while preparing to quit his body because he had the benediction that he could quit his body at a chosen auspicious moment. As you are aware, he chose the auspicious Uttarayan Punyakala, generally, which comes during the Shankranti Makara Shankranti. So, here, the last prayer Bhishma Deva is offering to the Supreme Lord, clarifying that people have some misconceptions about the presence of the Lord in everyone's heart. There is some misconception, so that is explained by Prabhupada in the purport, what is that misconception and how pure devotees don't have that misconception. Prabhupada says in the purport, Lord Shri Krishna is the one absolute supreme personality of Godhead. God is one, Krishna is one, supreme absolute truth is one, but he has expanded himself into his multi-plenary portions by his inconceivable energy. Krishna can expand and Krishna does expand into unlimited forms of his personal self.

---
### inconceivable energy, Paramatma, duality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n1imoer6Oe0?t=288
**Verse References:** BG 9.11, BG 15.15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is one, but he appears in so many different forms, and he is present in everyone's heart, the Paramatma feature of the Lord is an expansion of his plenary portions."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how Krishna, though one, manifests in various forms and is present in everyone's heart as Paramatma, using the analogy of the sun being perceived simultaneously by many.

The conception of duality is due to ignorance of his inconceivable energy. That means to think that Krishna is different, Rama is different, Paramatma is different, Narsimha is different, Vishnu is different. This conception is due to ignorance. All one supreme Lord, though they are present as if they are different persons because their activities are variegated, their forms appear different, and they have their own devotees, they have their own planets in the spiritual sky. They appear on their own schedule in the material world. So the people in general who do not exactly know who is God or what is the nature of his personality, that even though he is one, he is simultaneously present in so many different forms, all transcendental, all equally powerful, though the source of all these forms is Krishna, the original form of which the other forms are expansions, that original form is Krishna. Govindam Adipurusham Tamaham Bhajami. So that is explained in the Brahma Samhita. That is explained in the Bhagavatam: ete chamsh kalapumsaha krishnastu bhagavan svayam. So all the incarnations and the expansions are plenary portions of Krishna, but Krishna himself is the original personality of God, svayam bhagava, he is the original person. In the Bhagavad Gita, the Lord says that only the foolish take him to be a mere human being. Foolish people think Krishna is an ordinary person, avajananti mamudha manushin tanumashritam. That is the verse in the Bhagavad Gita. Such foolish people are not aware of Krishna's inconceivable energies, achinthya shakti. Krishna has achinthya shakti. By his inconceivable energy, he is present in everyone's heart. Krishna has entered into every person's heart. He says that in the Bhagavad Gita, sarvasya chaham hrithi sannyi vishto, and the example is being given just like the sun is present before everyone all over the world. Sun is one. If the sun appears overhead, you go in any direction hundreds and hundreds of times and thousands of miles, they will all say sun is above their head. How can the sun simultaneously appear overhead for so many people? So that is an example. Krishna is one, but he appears in so many different forms, and he is present in everyone's heart. The Paramatma feature of the Lord is an expansion of his plenary portions. Paramatma feature is the Lord in the form of the Lord in the heart of every living being. He expands himself as Paramatma in everyone's heart by his inconceivable energy.

---
### Brahmajyoti, oneness, devotee's perspective
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n1imoer6Oe0?t=476
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vasudeva Sarvamiti, Vasudeva is all in all in everything, and there is no existence of anything without Vasudeva."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining Krishna's expansions, including the Brahmajyoti, and emphasizes the oneness of Krishna, Brahmajyoti, and Paramatma from the perspective of a devotee, referencing Brahma Samhita and Bhagavad Gita.

He also expands himself as the glowing effulgence of the Brahma Jyoti by expansion of his personal glow. Krishna's form has got unlimited light coming out of it, spiritual light, so that light expands as Brahma Jyoti in the entire spiritual sky. That Brahma Jyoti is the source of illumination in this material world. The sunshine, the moonshine, the light of the fire, the light of electricity, every illumination is an expansion of that Brahma Jyoti, is an expansion of the personal glow of Krishna. This is confirmed in the Brahma Samhita that Brahma Jyoti is the personal glow, Yasyaprabha Prabhavato Jagadanda Koti is the statement of the Bhagavad Gita, Brahma Samhita. Therefore, there is no difference between him, that is Krishna, his personal glow, the Brahma Jyoti, or his plenary portion as all are identical. Vadantita tattva vidaha tattvam yajnanam advayam, advayagyana non different, brahmeti paramatmeti bhagavaniti shabhyati. Less intelligent persons who are not aware of this fact consider Brahma Jyoti and Paramatma features to be different from Krishna. This misconception of duality is completely removed from the mind of Bhishma Deva. So Bhishma Deva knows that Krishna is the original personality of Godhead. He has expanded himself and entered the heart of everyone as Paramatma, and his personal glow is expanded as Brahma Jyoti. That Bhishma Deva knows, and all the pure devotees of Krishna know this. So Bhishma is fully satisfied that Krishna only is all in all in everything, only one who is all in all in everything, so only Krishna who is the ultimate reality in everything in existence.

---
### authority, devotional path, Mahajana
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n1imoer6Oe0?t=634
**Verse References:** BG 7.19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We follow the spiritual authorities. We don't concoct, we don't try to imagine, we don't try to discover by our own strength. We never do that because we are limited, we are prone to defects, we are affected by ignorance by the gunas."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of following spiritual authorities like Bhishma Deva on the devotional path, highlighting the limitations of individual speculation and the need to adhere to established knowledge.

This enlightenment of the truth about Krishna is attained by the great Mahatmas or the great devotees, as it is stated in Bhagavad Gita, Vasudeva Sarvamiti Samahatma Sudurlabha. Vasudeva Sarvamiti, Vasudeva is all in all in everything, and there is no existence of anything without Vasudeva. So Vasudeva or Lord Sri Krishna is the original supreme person as confirmed now by a Mahajana, Bhishma Deva is a Mahajana, one of the twelve great authorities in this universe who know the secret or confidential knowledge of Dharma, what is Dharma or who is God, what is the absolute truth? Now both the pure devotees and the neophytes, everyone should try to follow in the footsteps of Bhishma Deva. That is the devotional, that is the way of the devotional line. In Bhakti always we follow the authorities, the spiritual authorities. We don't concoct, we don't try to imagine, we don't try to discover by our own strength. We never do that because we are limited, we are prone to defects, we are affected by ignorance by the gunas. So therefore it is impossible for us to attempt to discover or find out by our own strength individually or collectively. We follow the spiritual authorities.

---
### Parthasarathy, Shama Sundara, impersonal vs. personal
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/n1imoer6Oe0?t=772
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though all are identical with Krishna, still in his personal feature as Krishna, either as Parthasarathy or Shama Sundara, Krishna is present as the person, but in the impersonal feature as Brahmajyoti or in the feature as Paramatma, Krishna is not personally present."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Bhishma Deva worships Krishna as Parthasarathy, while the Gopis worship him as Shama Sundara, clarifying that both forms are the same Krishna, and distinguishing between Krishna's personal presence and his impersonal manifestations as Brahmajyoti and Paramatma.

Now Ravupan explains another important point here that the worshipable object of Bhishma Deva is Lord Krishna as Parthasarathy. This is described earlier because Bhishma is situated in the relationship of Vediyarasa with Krishna, and the worshipable object of the Gopis is the same Krishna in Vrindavan as the most attractive Shama Sundara. Gopis, they relate with Krishna as Shama Sundara, and Bhishma relates with Krishna as Parthasarathy. Now Prabhupada says less intelligent scholars sometimes make a mistake and think that the Krishna of Vrindavan and that of the battle of Kurukshetra are different personalities. This is not correct. Both the forms of Krishna, Parthasarathy and Shama Sundara, are one person, the supreme person, one and only supreme person, Krishna. Even the impersonalist object of destination is Krishna as the impersonal Brahmajyoti, that is also Krishna only, and the yogis destination of Paramatma, yogis meditate on Paramatma in the heart, that is also Krishna only. So even though all are identical with Krishna, still in his personal feature as Krishna, either as Parthasarathy or Shama Sundara, Krishna is present as the person, but in the impersonal feature as Brahmajyoti or in the feature as Paramatma, Krishna is not personally present. This we have to understand. So therefore devotees object is always the personality of God is Krishna. I will stop here, thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### Nirvikalpa Samadhi, Devotional Service, Absorption in Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Myj43RT1ytI?t=112
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is the highest stage of perfection, this total absorption. And it is possible for everyone to attain this stage by practice of devotional service."
**Summary:** This segment explains the concept of Nirvikalpa Samadhi, the state attained by Bhishma Deva, and emphasizes that this state of total absorption in Krishna is attainable by anyone through devotional service.

Because his mind, his speech, his sight and actions are all completely absorbed in Krishna. Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, this verse, the stage attained by Bhishma Deva while quitting his material body is called Nirvikalpa Samadhi. This is a technical term. What is the meaning of this term Nirvikalpa Samadhi? That is also explained by Srila Prabhupada. Nirvikalpa Samadhi, it is called Nirvikalpa Samadhi because he, that is Bhishma Deva, merged himself into thinking of the Lord and his mind into remembering, remembering his different activities. He chanted the glories of the Lord, that is his speech. And by his sight, he began to see the Lord personally present before him. And thus, all his activities became concentrated upon the Lord without deviation. Samadhi means total absorption. Total absorption of the mind, of the thinking, that is the mind, activity of the mind, the speech, and the sight, and the very self. So, thinking, remembering, glorifying, and seeing, in all these ways, Bhishma was totally absorbed in thought of Krishna. This is the highest stage of perfection, this total absorption. And it is possible for everyone to attain this stage by practice of devotional service. So here, Prabhupada is teaching us, there is the highest perfectional stage to be attained in the practice of devotional service. Devotional service means practicing, focusing our attention on Krishna. Krishna means the Supreme Personality of Godhead. We are focusing our attention on Krishna.

---
### Devotional Service, Krishna, Service Attitude
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Myj43RT1ytI?t=273
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So every possible way if we can absorb our self in thought of Krishna, in thinking of Krishna, in glorifying Krishna, in hearing about Krishna... this is the highest perfection to be attained and it is possible for anyone provided one practices devotional service."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on devotional service, emphasizing the importance of absorbing oneself in thoughts of Krishna through various means, and introduces the nine principles of service activities.

Krishna means the Supreme Personality of Godhead. He has so many different features. So every possible way if we can absorb our self in thought of Krishna, in thinking of Krishna, in glorifying Krishna, in hearing about Krishna, in seeing Krishna's form either as the Deity or as the personally Krishna is present in the case of Bhishma. He is personally present. So Bhishma is directly seeing Krishna before quitting his body. And the beautiful form. He is describing, he has already described in his prayers the beautiful form of Krishna. So this is the highest perfection to be attained and it is possible for anyone provided one practices devotional service. Now devotional service itself, how is it performed? So Srila Prabhupada says based on the explanation in the seventh canto of the Bhagavatam. The devotional service of the Lord Krishna. The devotional service of the Lord consists of nine principles of service activities. Devotional service is not something idle. Simply sitting and doing nothing. No. Devotional service. Bhakti means devotional service. So it consists of service activities. Activities rendered while having a service attitude. I want to serve Krishna to please him. I want to serve Krishna in a loving attitude. And these nine principles of service activities are described in the Bhagavatam seventh canto as.

---
### Nine Principles of Devotional Service, Shravanam, Atma Nivedanam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Myj43RT1ytI?t=392
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Any one of them, any one of the nine or all of them are equally competent to award the desired result."
**Summary:** This segment details the nine principles of devotional service: hearing, chanting, remembering, serving the lotus feet, worshipping, praying, executing the orders, fraternizing, and fully surrendering, providing examples for each.

Number one. Hearing. Hearing about Krishna. Shravanam. Number two. Chanting. Kirtanam. Number three. Remembering. Remembering in Sanskrit it is called Smaranam. Number four. Serving the lotus feet of Krishna. Padashevanam. Number five. Worshipping. Archanam. Deity worship. Specifically Deity worship. Number six. Praying. Vandanam. Offering prayers. Just like Bhishma did. He was remembering Krishna's pastimes. He was praying. Offering prayers. He was chanting the glories of Krishna. And like that he was engaged in all possible ways in service activities before quitting his body. Totally absorbed in that. So number seven. Executing the orders. It's called Dasyam. Dasyam. A servant is specially meant for simply carrying out the orders of the master. Just like Hanuman. Whatever Rama ordered, Hanuman simply carried out that order. So that is Dasyam. Then eighth one of the list of nine service activities. Eighth one is fraternizing. Sakhyam. Acting as a rendering service rather. As a friend of the Lord. Friend of Lord Krishna. Just like Arjuna. Rendering service as a friend of Krishna. And the final one, ninth one. Fully surrendering. Atma Nivedanam. Total self surrender. Total self surrender. Bali Maharaj. Each one of these, we have an ideal described in the Bhagavatam. Any one of them, any one of the nine or all of them are equally competent to award the desired result.

---
### Guidance, Practice, Hearing, Bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Myj43RT1ytI?t=549
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhakti begins with hearing."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the need for persistent practice of devotional service under the guidance of a spiritual master, highlighting the importance of hearing (shravanam) as the most important item of all.

That is the highest perfection of total absorption of oneself in the Supreme Lord Krishna. But they require to be practiced persistently under the guidance of an expert devotee of the Lord. Who is that expert devotee of the Lord? The bona fide spiritual master. So the bona fide spiritual master gives guidance, gives instructions, helps the disciple to actually practice one or more of these nine items, nine principles of service activities rendered for Krishna by a devotee. And they have to be practiced. In the beginning, it will not be very nicely done. But by repeatedly doing, again and again, abhyasa yoga yuktena cetasa na anyagamina without deviation of the attention. Persistently focused. The attention, mainly the attention. Completely focused on Krishna. So of these nine, Srila Prabhupada says, the first item, hearing, is the most important item of all. Bhakti begins with hearing. Bhakti begins with hearing. So the most important item is hearing, shravanam. And therefore, hearing of the Bhagavad Gita and later on Srimad Bhagavatam is essential for the serious candidate who wants to attain the stage of Bhishma Deva at the end.

So practice hearing Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam. From whom should you hear? From Srila Prabhupada. For all of us now, we should hear from Prabhupada. Prabhupada has given discourses and he also has written Bhagavad Gita translation and purport. Bhagavatam translation and purport. So we should read or we should hear somebody else reading Prabhupada's book, Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam. And in this way, we should completely become absorbed in the subject matter that is described in the Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam. Both are Krishna katha. Bhagavad Gita is personally spoken by Krishna and Bhagavatam is spoken by Shukadeva Goswami and other great devotees about Krishna. So they are both Krishna katha and Chaitanya Mahaprabhu has instructed everyone to hear these two scriptures. Krishna katha as given in these two scriptures, Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam and thereby perfect our devotional selves. The unique situation at Bhishma Deva's time of death can be attained even though Lord Krishna may not be personally present. In the case of Bhishma, he was very very fortunate and it's rare that Krishna is personally present before the devotee who is quitting his body. That's very special, very rare. Bhishma Deva was blessed by Krishna. But even if Krishna is not personally present in front of us, when we are quitting the body, we can attain that situation that Bhishma was himself in. In that Nirvikalpa Samadhi,

### Sound Incarnation, Bhagavad Gita, Srimad Bhagavatam, Ideal Death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Myj43RT1ytI?t=822
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Quitting the body like Bhishma Deva is the ideal. Not dying in ignorance, not dying in thought of something else or someone else, but dying in thought of Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment explains how the words of Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam are identical to Krishna and are sound incarnations of the Lord, enabling one to attain the stage of Bhishma Deva and achieve an ideal death in Krishna consciousness.

total absorption of the self in Krishna is possible. How? So Prabhupada says, His words of the Bhagavad Gita or those of Srimad Bhagavatam are identical with the Lord. This is the meaning of Krishna is absolute. Krishna, the person, his form, his qualities, his pastimes, his words, his descriptions, his paraphernalia, his abode, all are identical 100% with Krishna. And there is no difference at all between them. So Prabhupada describes Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam as sound incarnations of the Lord. Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam are sound incarnations of the Lord. And one can fully utilize them to be entitled to the stage of Sri Bhishma Deva, who was one of the eight Vasus. Bhishma Deva, before this birth, he was one of the eight Vasus in the upper planets. He took birth as Bhishma and he attained this perfection. Everyone has to quit the body.
