### Bhishma Deva, Vasus, Mahajanas, dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/vOjC2S9dsxc?t=427
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Picking up the most essential fundamental religious principles and exactly following it so as to be able to achieve the perfection of this human form of life, that is the challenge."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Bhishma Deva as one of the eight Vasus and one of the twelve Mahajanas, emphasizing the challenge of discerning and following the essential religious principles from the vast details in the Vedas.

Now Vishwadeva, he is described as Vasuttama. He is the best of the eight Vasus. Vasus are one category of personalities in the upper planets. So Vishwadeva had appeared on the earth as the son of Shantanu and Ganga for the sake of participating in Krishna's prasadams because he is one of the pure devotees of Krishna. He is also one of the twelve Mahajanas. Mahajanas are authorities in dharma. They know the principles of dharma and they are able to guide and instruct others in how to follow religious principles. There is a verse that says dharmasya tatvam nihitam guhaya, the verse in the Mahabharata that the eternal religious principles are a very big mystery for even the most learned Vedic scholars because the Vedas describe so many different details about practicing dharma. Now out of these so many details, picking up the most essential fundamental religious principles and exactly following it so as to be able to achieve the perfection of this human form of life, that is the challenge. If somebody becomes very much preoccupied with some preliminary things, then they miss out the essential principles which are required for making advancement.

---
### Srila Prabhupada, essential principles, cleanliness, Hare Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/vOjC2S9dsxc?t=587
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Srila Prabhupada has taught us the most essential principles which are absolutely required to make advancement in Krishna Consciousness without spending too much time in preliminaries."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes Srila Prabhupada's teachings on focusing on essential principles for advancement in Krishna consciousness, using the example of cleanliness and chanting Hare Krishna.

So Srila Prabhupada has taught us the most essential principles which are absolutely required to make advancement in Krishna Consciousness without spending too much time in preliminaries, without spending too much time in preliminaries, without spending too much time in preliminaries. Just like we have to maintain cleanliness, external and internal cleanliness before we do Deity worship. So Prabhupada explained this that you maintain cleanliness externally by taking bath, wearing fresh clothes, keeping your surroundings clean, and internal cleanliness by chanting Hare Krishna. So he gave the very very basic fundamental principle of cleanliness without getting into the unnecessary details which are anyway not practically possible in every place at all times. The scriptures say apavitra pavitrova sarvavasthanga topiva yasvare kundari kaksham sabhajya abhyantara suji, one becomes completely purified externally and internally by simply remembering the lotus side supreme god. So how do we remember the lotus side supreme god? By chanting Hare Krishna. So Srila Prabhupada taught us to chant Hare Krishna and thereby purify ourselves and be fit for serving Krishna, for doing Deity worship. Like this Prabhupada has given the essential principles so that we can execute the follow those principles and achieve the purpose of properly practicing the religious principles to make advancement in Krishna consciousness to achieve perfection of purifying ourselves completely of all material contamination and going back to god.

---
### Bhishma Deva, Mahajanas, religious principles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/vOjC2S9dsxc?t=753
**Verse References:** SB 6.3.20
**Content Type:** philosophy

**Summary:** The speaker reiterates Bhishma Deva's position as one of the twelve Mahajanas, quoting a verse from Srimad Bhagavatam spoken by Yamaraj, and highlighting his knowledge of religious principles and ability to adjust them according to time and place.

So it is said here Bhishma Deva, he is one of the twelve great authorities, mahajanas. Twelve mahajanas svayambhu narada shambhu kumaran kapilao manubhu praladha janaka bheeshma valir vayasakhi vayam. This verse in the sixth canton of the Bhagavatam is spoken by Yamaraj. Yamarajis describe the twelve mahajanas. Yamaraj is one of them. Bhishma Deva is also one of the twelve mahajanas, so he perfectly knew all the religious principles and he could make adjustments according to time and place. So therefore he could receive and welcome all the powerful sages assembled there at his death bed from all parts of the universe.

### Bhishma Deva, benediction, auspicious time, devotees
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/vOjC2S9dsxc?t=809
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "For devotees, whenever they quit their body, it doesn't matter because they are Krishna conscious. Whenever they quit their body, they are definitely going back to Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Bhishma Deva's benediction to choose the time of his death and contrasts it with the situation of devotees, for whom time and place of death are irrelevant due to their Krishna consciousness.

Now one special thing about Bhishma is he had a benediction from his father that he could quit the body whenever he desired to by choosing an auspicious moment. That's the sense in which that particular benediction was used because in the eighth chapter of the Bhagavatam Bhishma describes different auspicious and inauspicious times which are applicable for quitting the body. If somebody quits the body at an auspicious time, they are able to get out of this material existence and get liberation beyond this material existence. If they quit at an inauspicious time then they cannot go out of this material world letting rebirth again. But that is only applicable to non-devotees. Why? Because devotees, whenever they quit their body, it doesn't matter because they are Krishna conscious. Whenever they quit their body, they are definitely going back to Godhead. They are getting ultimate liberation whenever they quit their body, wherever they quit their body, it doesn't matter time and place. It doesn't matter for the devotees.

---
### Bhishma Deva, pure devotee, auspicious moment, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/vOjC2S9dsxc?t=899
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhishma Deva was successful in quitting his body to attain ultimate liberation."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by stating that Bhishma Deva, as a pure devotee, didn't need to choose an auspicious time but did so to teach others, and that he successfully attained liberation by quitting his body in the presence of Krishna, glorifying Him.

So Bhishma Deva as a pure devotee, he need not choose an auspicious time for quitting the body, neither he has to choose an auspicious place because he is a pure devotee. He is guaranteed to go back to Godhead. He is already liberated because he is a pure devotee, but still in order to teach others because he was an expert yogi, so he was waiting for the most auspicious moment to quit his body and the great sages knew that Bhishma is an authority in Dharma, so they all assembled to witness Bhishma quitting his body at the most auspicious time, Uttarayana Punyakala. But Bhagavatam also describes in this very chapter that Bhishma Deva, he quit his body in the presence of Krishna, glorifying Krishna, fixing his complete attention on Krishna at the time of his death. So both ways Bhishma Deva was successful in quitting his body to attain ultimate liberation. I will stop here. Thank you very much.

### governance, scripture, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FTUe61Bkfx0?t=52
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.12, BG
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The kings or the executive heads of all states must know the codes of Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami discusses verses 1.17.12-13 of the Srimad Bhagavatam, emphasizing the importance of rulers following the codes of Lord Krishna as found in the Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam, especially in Kali Yuga.

In this session, we are discussing two verses, the twelfth and the thirteenth verses of the seventeenth chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Ko avrishchat tavapadam strin Saurabheya chatushpada Mabhuvams tvadrisha rashtre Raghanam krishnanu vartinam. The meaning of this twelfth verse is, He, Maharaj Parikshit, repeatedly addressed and questioned the bull, O son! O son of Surabhi, who has cut off your three legs? In the state of the kings who are obedient to the laws of the Supreme Personality of God Krishna, there is no one as unhappy as you. So Parikshit was very pained to see the condition of the bull suffering, because it had lost three of its legs, somehow wobbling along on one leg. So he is questioning, who has done this? In the purport by Srila Prabhupada, Prabhupada says in the purport, the kings or the executive heads of all states must know the codes of Lord Krishna. And the codes of Lord Krishna generally mean the Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam. Two scriptures are enough, though there are varieties of scriptures, many, many scriptures, but for the present age of Kali Yuga, these two scriptures are very important and enough for understanding the codes of the Supreme Lord Krishna. The Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam are both described by Chaitanya Mahaprabhu as Krishna Katha. Bhagavad Gita is Krishna Katha spoken by Krishna himself. Devotees, so these are two scriptures which are very important.

---
### suffering, human life, scriptural guidance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FTUe61Bkfx0?t=184
**Verse References:** BG 8.16, SB 3
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "To put an end to these miseries is the mission of human life by following the codes given in the Bhagavad Gita and the Bhagavatam."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the mission of human life, which is to end the miseries of material existence (birth, death, old age, and disease) by following the codes in the Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam. He describes the suffering in the mother's womb and emphasizes that understanding and following Krishna's codes can lead to a perfect life.

So the king or the executive head must act accordingly in order to fulfill the mission of human life, which is to make an end to all miseries of material conditions. Human life is a definite goal and a definite purpose as described in scriptures, that we should put an end to all miseries of material existence. Bhagavad Gita summarizes these miseries under four headings: janma, mrityu, jara, vyadhi. Janma means the misery of birth, taking birth in this material world. Mrityu means death, the misery of death. Jara means the misery of old age, invalidity due to old age. Old age doesn't mean just having some wrinkled skin or some general weakness, no, it means the invalidity, and there are many symptoms of old age which are making life very, very difficult. And the fourth one is vyadhi, vyadhi means disease. The construction of the body itself is such that there has to be these four miseries pertaining to the body we have acquired. It's after all in the mother's womb, the development of the body takes place beginning with us very, very tiny p-shaped small two particles, the sperm and the ovum combine to form that embryo, and then it starts developing in the mother's womb and develops into a nice baby boy or baby girl. Nine months in the mother's womb the development takes place. It's not at all, though it's the most conducive for the development of the body for the child, the mother's womb is most conducive, but it is not at all pleasant, and that's described in the Bhagavatam, third canto, what are the conditions inside the mother's womb which the child experiences in terms of suffering, acute pain, acute pain, that is a misery of birth. We all have gone through it, but we forgot after coming out of the mother's womb. So to put an end to these miseries is the mission of human life by following the codes given in the Bhagavad Gita and the Bhagavatam. One who knows the codes of Lord Krishna can achieve this end without any difficulty.

---
### scriptural study, leadership, historical context
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FTUe61Bkfx0?t=380
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhagavad-gita is the preliminary study of bhagavatam or bhagavatam is the elaborate description of the life full of Brahma."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to emphasize the importance of Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining that the former is a synopsis and the latter an elaborate description of the codes of Godhead. He then discusses verse 1.17.13, highlighting the reputation of pious kings like Maharaja Ramachandra and the Pandavas, whose kingdoms ensured the well-being of all living beings.

Perfect life, leading a perfect life is possible by following the instructions of the Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam. In the Bhagavad Gita, in a synopsis, we can understand the codes of Godhead, and in the Srimad Bhagavatam, the same codes are explained further. Many times Srila Prabhupada says Bhagavad Gita is the preliminary study of Bhagavatam, or Bhagavatam is the elaborate description of the life full of Brahma. Such quotes are presented in the pages of Srimad Bhagavatam for the guidance of the blind persons who happen to be at the helm of all affairs. Kalau nashtat drishamesha puranarko adhunoditaha. Bhagavatam, it is said, in Kali Yuga people have lost the vision of the goal of human life because of the dense darkness due to the influence of Kali, and this darkness is dissipated by the Srimad Bhagavatam, which is the horizon like the sun at the beginning of Kali Yuga to give direction to all those people who have lost the direction for the future, making progress in human life. The 13th verse, aakhya hi vrishabhadram vaha sadhunam akrita agasam, atma vairupya kartaram parthanam kirti dusanam. Meaning of this 13th verse is, O bull, you are offenseless and thoroughly honest, therefore I wish all good to you. Please tell me of the perpetrator of these mutilations, which blackmailed you and made you feel the reputation of the sons of Pritha, kirti dusanam. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada says, the reputation of the reign of Maharaja Ramachandra and that of the kings who followed in the footsteps of Maharaja Ramachandra, like the Pandavas and their descendants, are never to be forgotten because in their kingdom, offenseless and honest living beings were never in trouble.

---
### governance, dharma, historical truth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FTUe61Bkfx0?t=556
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "They are facts, historical facts, cent percent true."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the ideal governance during the reigns of Rama and Yudhishthira, emphasizing the correlation between Dharma and societal well-being. He refutes the notion of Ramayana and Mahabharata as mere mythology, asserting their historical accuracy and the importance of reading them.

Ramaraja, you have heard of Ramaraja? So when Rama was ruling, everybody was happy. People were getting all their necessities in plenty. When Yudhishthira was ruling also, that was the situation. So there is a correlation between the way a king governs the state or the kingdom and the codes of Dharma given in the scriptures, there is a correlation. Exactly according to the Dharma Shastra, the Samadhi rules, there is peace, prosperity, plenty of supply, and people will be able to live very nicely. And these are described in the authorized scriptures. How people were living during Maharaja Yudhishthira's time is described in the Mahabharata. How people were living during Ramachandra's time is described in the Ramayana. So you should read that. And they are not just stories. They are not fiction stories. They are facts. That also people do not know. They use the term mythology to describe Ramayana, Mahabharata, our ancient scriptures. But mythology means something which is false, myth, just imaginary, which is not true. But that is a wrong description of Mahabharata, Ramayana, the Puranas. Mythology is wrong.

---
### scriptural authority, cow protection, leadership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FTUe61Bkfx0?t=683
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The ignorance about Dharma Shastra is a very, very serious lacuna in modern society."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of scriptures as perfect guidance, highlighting the value of cows and bulls as symbols of offenselessness and the utility of their byproducts. He contrasts the pious kings of the past with modern leaders who lack knowledge of Dharma and fail to protect animals, stressing the need for leaders to gain knowledge from liberated souls.

They are facts, historical facts, cent percent true. Great sages performed severe tapasya in order to compile these literatures for the perfect, for giving perfect direction to people in general. So they did, they had no business writing fiction stories. They simply documented the facts and figures and the principles perfectly without any trace of any mistake in these scriptures. That is the standard of these scriptures. They say Shastra. They say shastra. The bull and the cow are the symbols of the most offenseless living beings. The bull and the cow completely offenseless. And the value of these animals, Prabhupada explains, is that even the stool and urine of these animals are utilized to benefit human society. No other animal stool, except the cow, is useful for human society. Even the human being's stool also is not useful. But the cows, cow dung, gober, gomedhya. So that is very, very valuable. How many ways it can be used. Nowadays, of course, people do not know so much. But if you go to some villages even now, multiple ways they use this cow dung and cow urine. So, if the stool and the urine are also useful for human society, how valuable are these animals for human society? You can only imagine. The descendants of the sons of Pritha, like Maharaj Parikh, are afraid of losing their reputation when the animals are not protected. They don't think they will be able to retain the reputation of giving proper protection to the Praja. But in modern days, the leaders are not even afraid of killing such offenseless animals. So, therefore, Prabhupada says, herein lies the difference between the reign of pious kings and the modern states ruled by irresponsible executive heads without knowledge of the codes of conduct. The ignorance about Dharma Shastra is a very, very serious lacuna in modern society. So, they can only rule properly if they have proper knowledge. And this knowledge should be based on taking the knowledge from the perfect source, not imperfect theories or speculations given by conditioned souls. No. You take knowledge from liberated souls. So, I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

### governance, kali yuga, leadership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/893sOXDBR3c?t=48
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dishonest miscreants flourish because of cowardly and impotent executive heads of state. This is the present situation."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces the verses to be discussed and explains Srila Prabhupada's commentary on the decline of qualified leadership in Kali Yuga, leading to the flourishing of miscreants.

In this session we will be discussing from the 14th and the 15th verses of the 17th chapter of the 1st canto of Shri Mataji. The 14th verse is as follows. The meaning of this 14th verse is as follows: Whoever causes offenseless living beings to suffer must fear me. Anywhere and everywhere in the world, by curbing dishonest miscreants, one automatically benefits the offenseless. These are words spoken by Parikshit Maharaj. Parikshit Maharaj is addressing the bull dharma in the shape of the bow. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains, 'Dishonest miscreants flourish because of cowardly and impotent executive heads of state. This is the present situation.' As Kali Yuga progresses, there will be no qualified Kshatriyas, no qualified Brahmanas, except for the devotees, who are anyway most qualified persons in the entire world. Other than devotees, who will also be not so many in number, there will be few devotees, but majority of the population, as Kali Yuga progresses, will be people who are sinful.

---
### kali yuga, leadership, sin
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/893sOXDBR3c?t=173
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Because they are themselves miscreants, they won't really protect the innocent, the honest, the weaker sections."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the characteristics of people in Kali Yuga, their ignorance, and their desires, leading to the rise of unqualified leaders who are unable to protect the innocent and honest.

People who are ignorant about who they are, about what is this world, about who is God, people who are full of desires for material enjoyment. Among such people, some person who can garner some votes or get some popularity will somehow become the executive head of the state. And they will not be sufficiently strong or powerful to curb the miscreants because they themselves will be mostly miscreants. Because they are themselves miscreants, they won't really protect the innocent, the honest, the weaker sections. They won't do that.

### governance, justice, leadership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/893sOXDBR3c?t=247
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When the miscreants are punished in an exemplary manner, automatically all good fortune follows."
**Summary:** The lecture emphasizes the importance of strong and qualified leaders who can curb miscreants and administer appropriate punishment to deter others, leading to good fortune and protection for the peaceful citizens.

Srila Prabhupada says further, 'When the executive heads are strong enough to curb all sorts of dishonest miscreants in any part of the state, certainly they cannot flourish.' So if there is a strong person as the head of the state who is qualified, then he can easily curb all dishonest miscreants. Further, Srila Prabhupada says, very important point, 'When the miscreants are punished in an exemplary manner, automatically all good fortune follows.' The punishment given should be appropriate for the mischief performed by the dishonest miscreant. It should be suitable punishment which will deter the other miscreants. That is called punishing in an exemplary manner. It will send a proper signal to all the other miscreants that better stop your nefarious activities, otherwise you are going to be severely punished. As said before, Srila Prabhupada is further explaining, 'It is the prime duty of the king or the executive head to give protection in all respects to the peaceful, offenseless citizens of the state.' It is a prime duty. To be in a position of responsibility means to actually carry out one's duties properly, being equipped with the right qualifications, being equipped with the right knowledge and the right support of the able assistance.

---
### devotion, preaching, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/893sOXDBR3c?t=389
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Sankirtana Yajna is the most recommended process for cleansing the hearts of the people in general."
**Summary:** The speaker highlights the importance of Krishna consciousness and Sankirtana as a means to purify the hearts of the people, leading to a peaceful and offenseless society.

The devotees of the Lord are by nature peaceful and offenseless, and therefore it is the prime duty of the state to arrange to convert everyone to become a devotee of the Lord. So how is it possible for a king or an executive head of state to actually rule properly for the citizens? There should be a program to preach Krishna Consciousness, by which the material contamination in the hearts of the people in general will be cleansed. Like Chaitanya Mahaprabhu says about Sankirtana, what is he saying? Cheto darpana marjanam. The first effect of Sankirtana, public Sankirtana, is that the heart of the public, simply if the holy name falls on their ears, whether they hear it willingly or unwillingly, knowingly or unknowingly, anyhow it will work, it will act, it will purify their heart. Of course it's a gradual process, it takes time, but the purification begins as soon as the holy name, the vibration of the holy name enters their ears. So this is the primary program in the present age. Yajnai Sankirtana Prayi Riyajantihi Sumedha Saha. Sankirtana Yajna. Sankirtana Yajna is the most recommended process for cleansing the hearts of the people in general. Thus automatically there will be peaceful, offenseless citizens.

---
### kali yuga, lust, governance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/893sOXDBR3c?t=522
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Then the only duty of the king will be to curb the dishonest miscreants."
**Summary:** The lecture discusses the contamination of people's hearts by Rajoguna and Tamoguna in Kali Yuga, leading to lust and greed, and emphasizes the king's duty to curb miscreants even after implementing Sankirtana programs.

There has to be some program because people are contaminated or polluted in their heart by association with other spirits, other sinful people, bad association. By association with the gunas, which are particularly in Kali Yuga, Rajoguna and Tamoguna is very prominent. So people become polluted in their consciousness with this Rajoguna and Tamoguna. Rajoguna and Tamoguna effect is mostly lust and greed. Very lust and greed will dominate in the hearts of so many people. So they will be unable to control their senses, control their mind and they will act in so many sinful ways, being forced to actually endeavor to satisfy their lusty desires, satisfy their greed, satisfy their crazy material desires like that. Then the only duty of the king will be to curb the dishonest miscreants. In spite of this program, Sankirtana program, some demoniac natured persons will not mend themselves. They will not correct themselves. They will have to be punished by the able administrator king. That will bring about peace and harmony all over the human society.

---
### governance, heaven, punishment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/893sOXDBR3c?t=646
**Verse References:** BG 8.16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "An upstart living being who commits offenses by torturing those who are offenseless shall be directly uprooted by me even though he be a denizen of heaven with armor and decorations."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 15th verse and explains that even denizens of heaven will be punished if they commit offenses, and discusses the relative deathlessness of beings in higher planets.

So Prabhupada has outlined a very clear method for the executive head of state to curb these miscreants. The 15th verse. The meaning of this 15th verse: 'An upstart living being who commits offenses by torturing those who are offenseless shall be directly uprooted by me even though he be a denizen of heaven with armor and decorations.' So Parikshit Maharaj is warning the miscreants even if they are persons from the upper planets. Still they cannot create any nuisance or any mischief on this earth planet as long as Parikshit is ruling. They are very very powerful and capable and qualified and conscientious. So in the Purport Shlap Prabhupada says the denizens of heavenly kingdom are called Amar or deathless due to their possessing a long span of life. Actually they are not deathless in terms of the body they have, material body they have, but they are like deathless because of the long span of life they have, far greater than that of the human beings. For a human being who has only a maximum 100 year duration of life, a span of life spreading over millions of years is certainly considered to be deathless. So it is relative. It is not exactly deathless. It is relative. Relatively very long life span.

---
### governance, justice, equality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/893sOXDBR3c?t=766
**Verse References:** BG 8.16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It should be the principle of a state executive head that the offender of the codes of God is always punished."
**Summary:** The lecture continues to explain the long lifespans on Brahmaloka and other heavenly planets, emphasizing that even those with long lives are not truly deathless. It stresses that a strong executive head must punish offenders regardless of their position.

For example Prabhupada gives the example from the Bhagavad Gita. We learn that on the Brahmaloka planet the duration of one day is calculated to be 4,300,000 times 1,000 solar years. 12 hours of Brahma's time scale. The top most planet in this universe is some 4 billion years. 4 billion years is 12 hours of Brahma. Similarly in other heavenly planets one day is calculated to be 6 months on this planet and the inhabitants get a life of 10 million of their years. Therefore in all higher planets since the span of life is far greater than that of the human being, the denizens are called deathless by imagination. See this is an important point. Although actually no one within the material universe is deathless. Ultimately Brahma also has to die. Everyone has to die. Die means they have to give up the present material body. Now Maharaj Pariksit challenges even such denizens of heaven if they torture the offenseless. He says even if somebody is a resident of a higher planet I don't care for your position or power. This means that the state executive head must be as strong as Maharaj Pariksit, so that he may be determined to punish the strongest offenders. It should be the principle of a state executive head that the offender of the codes of God is always punished. There is no discrimination when somebody is committing an offence. There is no discrimination as to who that person is. Whatever be his position. If he is an offender he has to be punished.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | SB 1.17.16-17 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 22-02-2023
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Kali Yuga, Protection, Pious Life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LcVYkn2Czwo?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It gives us a clue as to how we can protect ourselves against the evil influence of Kali, this age, this time."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the topic of discussion, verses 16 and 17 of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on how Parikshit Maharaj dealt with Kali Yuga and how individuals can protect themselves from its negative influences by leading a pious life.

Om Agyanati Miran Dhasya Gyananjan Shalakaya Chaksharun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnu Padaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadhadhara Shri Vasadi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jaya Mudhirayet. In this session, we are discussing the 16th and 17th verses of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The title of the 17th chapter is Punishment and Reward of Kali, how the Kali Yuga, the Kali Purusha, the personality who is the personification of this age, Kali Yuga of this period, how he is dealt with by Parikshit Maharaj when Kali Yuga is about to begin. He is trying to enter Parikshit's kingdom when Dwapar Yuga is ending and the Kali Yuga is going to begin, and how he is dealt with is very instructive for all of us. It gives us a clue why it is important for us. It gives us a clue as to how we can protect ourselves against the evil influence of Kali, this age, this time, this atmosphere, the prevailing external conditions, the bad influence. How can we protect ourselves, provided, of course, pious people want to lead a life free from sinful activities, want to lead an honest life, a clean life, a pious life? Then how do they protect themselves against the evil influence? That is the reason why this is being discussed here.

---
### Dharma, Swadharma, Punishment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LcVYkn2Czwo?t=153
**Verse References:** BG 18.48
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The duty of the king is to see that everyone strictly follows his occupational duty as prescribed in the scriptures."
**Summary:** This segment focuses on the 16th verse, explaining the supreme duty of a king to protect law-abiding citizens and punish those who deviate from scriptural ordinances. It discusses the concept of apadharma (emergency duty) and emphasizes the importance of following one's prescribed duty (Swadharma) as outlined in the scriptures, with the king ensuring its adherence and punishing violators.

The 16th verse, Ragnyohi paramo dharma ha swadharma sthanu palanam shasato anyan yatha shasram anapadi utpathan iha. Let me read this. Okay. The meaning of this verse is, the supreme duty of the ruling king is to give all protection to law-abiding persons and to chastise those who stray from the ordinances of the scriptures in ordinary times when there is no emergency. So, in the purport, Srila Prabhupada says, in the scriptures, there is mention of apadharma or occupational duty at times of extraordinary happenings. It is said that sometimes the great sage Vishwamitra had to live on the flesh of dogs in some extraordinary dangerous position. Flesh-eating at a normal circumstance is completely forbidden. Or if somebody, somehow or the other, because they are very much desirous of enjoying eating some flesh, animal food, animal flesh, then if they do so, then they are liable to be punished by the laws of God, the laws of nature, laws of karma. But under extreme circumstances, when there is no food available except for flesh, nothing else is available, then one may eat flesh just to survive. That is called apadharma, emergency time. So, Srila Prabhupada says, in cases of emergency, one may be allowed to live on the flesh of animals of all description, but that does not mean that there should be regular slaughterhouses to feed the animal eaters and that this system should be encouraged by the state. This is completely sinful. When plenty of fruits, vegetables, grains, milk is available, then why should people eat flesh? Why should they kill animals for getting the flesh? So, that is very, very sinful and severely punishable. So, Srila Prabhupada says, no one should try to live on flesh in ordinary time simply for the sake of the palate. Palate means simply for satisfying the tongue. Oh, I like to eat. No. Just because you like to eat, you cannot kill other animals. As much as you have a desire to live, you want to subsist in this particular, in this particular present body. Similarly, every living being has got the desire to live and should be allowed. Should not be, there should be no unnecessary killing of even an ant, even a small plant, no unnecessary killing. So, it is said here, Srila Prabhupada further, if anyone does so, it means they simply kill animals for the sake of satisfying their tongue. The king or the executive head should punish him for gross enjoyment. There are regular scriptural injunctions for different persons engaged in different occupational duties, and one who follows them is called Swadharmastha or faithful in one's prescribed duties. Everybody has got a prescribed duty given in the scriptures. Hmm. In the Bhagavad Gita, 18.48, it is advised that one should not give up his occupational prescribed duties even if they are not always flawless. Swadharma, what is prescribed for you according to your acquired nature, according to your nature. Hmm. You have to perform a certain type of occupational duty to maintain yourself. Such Swadharma might be violated in case of emergency, but not in normal circumstances should you violate. If one is forced by circumstances, one may violate, but they cannot be violated in ordinary times. The state executive head is to see that such Swadharma is not changed by the follower, whatever it may be, and he should be given all protection. The follower of the Swadharma should be given all protection by the king.

---
### Law, Scriptures, Duty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LcVYkn2Czwo?t=396
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The duty of the king is to see that everyone strictly follows his occupational duty as prescribed in the scriptures."
**Summary:** This segment continues the discussion on Swadharma and punishment, drawing an analogy between scriptural law and modern civil and criminal laws. It emphasizes that the severity of punishment should correspond to the severity of the violation and reiterates the king's duty to ensure everyone follows their prescribed occupational duty according to the scriptures, which are considered perfect and divinely inspired.

The violator is subject to punishment in terms of the Shastra. Now what punishment who should get according to the particular violation. Hmm. Just like in ordinary law, there are civil laws and criminal laws. Civil laws is for some minor transgression, transgression of some particular rule or law of the land. Hmm. Just like somebody is driving, they are just exceeding the speed limit. So that's not a very serious violation of the road rules or the law of the road driving, driving laws. So such a person may be just, he may be just warned or he may be just given some small fine. He has to pay. That's all. But somebody is driving rashly and killing another person or bringing about severe accident, severe damages, then such a person is subject to criminal code of conduct. Criminal laws are applicable in such cases. So, there is a difference depending on the kind of violation. The duty of the king is to see that everyone strictly follows his occupational duty as prescribed in the scriptures. Shastras should be the basis. Perfect. They are perfect in all respects. They are not made by some human beings with defects. Shastras are given by the Supreme Lord and further elaborated upon by liberated souls, saintly persons who are liberated personalities.

---
### Devotion, Power, Parikshit Maharaj
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LcVYkn2Czwo?t=583
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He was not falsely proud for a devotee of the Lord is equally as powerful as the Lord or sometimes more powerful by the Lord's grace."
**Summary:** This segment transitions to the 17th verse, where Dharma praises Parikshit Maharaj. It explains that Parikshit's assurances and challenges are not exaggerations but reflect his real power as a devotee. It highlights that devotees can be as powerful as the Lord due to His grace and that Parikshit's strict rule and enforcement of scriptural law extend even to the devatas.

The 17th verse, dharmavacha, because Pariksit was speaking to dharma, to the personality of religion. So dharma is now responding to Pariksit Maharaj. etad vaha pandavayanam yuktam aartabhayam vachaha yesam gunaganai krishno dhautyadau bhagavan krutah. The meaning of this verse is the personality of religion said, he is replying to Pariksit Maharaj. These words just spoken by you, befit a person of the Pandava dynasty. Captivated by the devotional qualities of the Pandavas, even Lord Krishna, the personality of Godhead, performed duties as a messenger. Something wonderful. Dharma is telling about Pariksit Maharaj. He is glorifying Pariksit Maharaj. So in the Purpose, Srila Prabhupada says, the assurances and challenges made by Maharaj Pariksit are never exaggerations of his real power. He is speaking, I will punish anybody who violates. I don't care even if that person who is violating is a denizen of the heaven. I don't care. So these words are befitting his actual position and they are not simply some exaggerations. The Maharaja said that even the denizens of heaven could not escape his stringent garment if they are violators of religious principles. He is a strict ruler and he has the power to rule and enforce the law of the scriptures, the rules and regulations and laws of the scriptures, even upon the devatas, even upon superior beings. He was not falsely proud for a devotee of the Lord is equally as powerful as the Lord or sometimes more powerful by the Lord's grace. And any promise made by a devotee, though it may be ordinarily very difficult to fulfill, is properly executed by the grace of the Lord. You see, that's the position of a devotee. Pariksit Maharaj is a pure devotee of the Lord, so he has got extraordinary power. Nobody else can have it. Not even devatas can have as much power as Pariksit has because Pariksit is a pure devotee.

---
### Krishna, Devotee, Protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LcVYkn2Czwo?t=748
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord wants to serve his devotees also without any hesitation, without any consideration that he is the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the Pandavas' devotion, which allowed Krishna to serve them as a chariot driver and messenger. It emphasizes Krishna's desire to serve His unalloyed devotees and highlights Parikshit Maharaj's lineage as a pure devotee, protected by Krishna even in his mother's womb. It concludes by reiterating that Parikshit's assurances are backed by the Lord's special protection.

So further, Srila Prabhupada says, Pandavas, by their unalloyed devotional service, unalloyed devotional service, pure devotional service, and full surrender to the Supreme Lord, made it possible for the Lord to become a chariot driver or sometimes their letter messenger. See, Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, worshipable by everyone, including the devotees, everyone. Everyone is subservient to Krishna and Krishna is supremely powerful, unlimitedly powerful. But that Krishna, being captivated by the Pandavas' wonderful qualities, devotional qualities, he became their servant. Voluntarily, he became their servant. He became the chariot driver of Arjuna in the battle of Kurukshetra. He became the messenger of Yudhishthira. He carried a message, a peace message on behalf of Yudhishthira to Duryodhana. Toward the war, the battle of Kurukshetra, before the war was actually fought, Yudhishthira wanted to avert this big world war. So Krishna personally carried this message from Yudhishthira to Duryodhana, personally went. So he was a letter messenger of Yudhishthira. Such duties executed by the Lord for his devotee are always very pleasing to the Lord because the Lord wants to render service to his unalloyed devotee whose life has no other engagement than to serve the Lord with full love and devotion. The devotee wants to serve the Lord unconditionally. The Lord wants to serve his devotees also without any hesitation, without any consideration that he is the Lord. Maharaj Pariksit, the grandson of Arjuna, the celebrated friendly servitor of the Lord, was a pure devotee of the Lord, like his grandfather. Who is his grandfather? Arjuna. Pariksit is the grandson of Arjuna. Arjuna's son was Abhimanyu. Abhimanyu's son was Pariksit. So Pariksit was also a pure devotee like Arjuna. Therefore the Lord was always with him even from the time when he was helplessly lying in the womb of his mother and was attacked by the blazing Brahmastra weapon of Ashwathama. This is the history of Pariksit. We discussed this earlier. He was saved in his mother's womb when he was an infant. Not even an infant. He was not even born. Not even come out of the mother's womb. He was protected by Krishna because he was a devotee. So when Pariksit makes an assurance, it is always backed up by the special protection of the Supreme Lord Himself. So the personality of religion, Dharma, accepted this fact and thanked the King for his being true to his exalted position. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### suffering, philosophy, bewilderment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eLaes4xJ7BY?t=46

**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam and explains Dharma's reply to Pariksit Maharaj about the difficulty in ascertaining the cause of suffering due to the many conflicting philosophical opinions.

In this session we will be discussing from the 18th verse of the 17th chapter of the 1st canto of Shri Madbhagavatam Navayam Klesha Bijaanam Yathasya Purushar Shabha Purushamtam Bijaanemo Vakyabhedavimohitaha. Dharma is replying to Pariksit Maharaj. Pariksit Maharaj asked Dharma, who is the cause of your suffering? So now Dharma's particular reply to Pariksit Maharaj. What is he telling? So greatest among human beings, it is very difficult to ascertain the particular miscreant who has caused our sufferings, because we are bewildered by all the different opinions of theoretical philosophers.

---
### suffering, material existence, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eLaes4xJ7BY?t=102
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Material existence means there will be suffering."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the nature of suffering in the material world, highlighting the universal desire to avoid suffering and the inevitability of it in material existence.

So what does this mean? It's difficult to ascertain the actual cause of our suffering, because so many different theories exist. There are many theoretical philosophers in the world who put forward their own theories of cause and effect, especially about the cause of suffering and its effect on different living beings. See, one thing is very common among all of us. What is the cause of suffering? What is the cause of suffering? What is the cause of suffering? All living beings, all human beings at least, nobody wants suffering. Everybody wants to enjoy without any suffering. But even if somebody does not want suffering, there is suffering in this world which practically everybody has to go through to different degrees, of course. Not that everyone is suffering. Not that everyone suffers the same extent. But suffering is definitely there in this world. This is the truth about material existences. Material existence means there will be suffering. So, what is the real cause of suffering and how to get rid of this suffering? That is the whole discussion that is now going to happen.

---
### philosophy, Vedic knowledge, Srila Prabhupada
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eLaes4xJ7BY?t=195
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Generally, there are six great philosophers."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces Srila Prabhupada's explanation of the six great philosophers and their respective philosophies: Kanada, Gautama, Patanjali, Kapila, Jaimini, and Vyasa Deva.

So, Srila Prabhupada says about the statement of Dharma that there are many theoretical philosophers. Prabhupada says, Generally, there are six great philosophers. Who are the six great philosophers? The first one is Kanada, the author of Vaisheshika philosophy. I will just briefly explain what these philosophies are. First, let me read this out in Prabhupada's purport. The first one is Kanada Rishi. He was a great sage in former times, Vedic times. He is the author of Vaisheshika philosophy. Then, there is the second one, the second sage, Rishi, Gautama Rishi, the author of Logic. Logic means Nyaya Shastra. The third one, the third great philosopher is Patanjali. The present trouble, if we are suffering now, is due to the fructifying of seedling sins. There is Papa Bija within our heart, everybody's heart. Everyone who has taken birth in this material world by force of karma has got in their heart what is called as Papa Bija, seedling sins. But even those seedlings... Even those seedlings... Even those seedling sins also gradually fade away by execution of pure devotional service. Even those seedlings... Anybody suffering is due to their own sinful activities performed in the past. And those sinful activities which they performed in the past resulted in Papa Bija being put in their heart. And that Papa Bija will gradually develop into Papa Phala. And that Papa Phala is what we experience as suffering. Different kinds of suffering depending on different kinds of Papa Phala. So, but somebody who performs pure devotional service... All... All the Papa Bijas are completely destroyed before they can fructify.

In this session, we will be discussing from the 19th verse of the 17th chapter of the 1st canto of Shri Madbhagavatam. Kechit Vikalpa Vasana Aahur Aatmanam Aatmanah Daivam Anya Apare Karma Swabhavam Apare Prabhum. The meaning of this verse is some of the philosophers who deny all sorts of duality declare that one's own self is responsible for his personal happiness and distress. Others say that superhuman powers are responsible, while yet others say that activity is responsible, and a gross materialist maintains that nature is the ultimate cause. So dharma, the personality of religion, is replying to Pariksit's question: Who is the cause of your suffering? So in the previous verse, the 18th verse, we discussed that dharma says there are different theories and disciplines in a particular spirit. There are different theories that God has created them in the form of his own existence, theoretical philosophers who put forward different theories as to the ultimate cause of suffering, and dharma says general people are confused, we do not know whom to actually accept as correct. So in the purport, the previous verse purport proper, it said generally there are six great philosophers, and I mentioned in the previous session that I shall tell you briefly what are their philosophies. The six great philosophers, now that is being described by dharma himself, those six philosophers are mentioned by dharma, four are mentioned here in this verse, the first one is one's own self is responsible for his personal happiness and distress.

### karma, philosophy, Jaimini
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/aeHNUd-evoo?t=155
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Jaimini's explanation is that even if somebody is a devotee of some supreme god or devotee of some devata or believer of some some higher controller, still that controller or controllers or supreme lord has to award the results of one's past activities and those results are awarded in terms of suffering or happiness enjoyment."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces Jaimini and his Karma Mimamsa philosophy, explaining that according to Jaimini, one's own actions (karma) are the cause of suffering and enjoyment, and even gods are bound by the laws of karma.

So in the purport, Prilapravapad explains as referred to above, philosophers like Jaimini and his followers establish the fructive activity is the root cause of all distress and happiness and that even if there is a superior authority, some superhuman powerful god or gods, he or they are also under the influence of fructive activity because they reward result according to one's own action. The first philosopher being mentioned by dharma among the many philosophers, six great philosophers, first one is Jaimini. Jaimini is one sage who has put forward a philosophy, a theory that the cause of suffering is one's own actions, and he calls it karma meemamsa philosophy. What is this karma meemamsa philosophy? It says that if I am suffering, it is my own past specific action which I performed that is the cause of my suffering, and not only suffering, if I am enjoying, then the enjoyment also is my own past pious activity I have performed, and if I am suffering, it is my own past sinful activity. So Jaimini analyzes people can perform either sinful activity or pious activity. If they perform pious activity, they are going to get enjoyment or happiness. If they perform sinful activity, they are going to get happiness, and if they do not, they are going to get suffering or pain or some distress. This is Jaimini's analysis. He says beyond this, there is nothing more. This is Jaimini's analysis, so his analysis is based on karma meemamsa philosophy that he has put forward. He says ultimately even if there is some god or godless god or gods devatas, even if there are some powerful beings, they are all bound up ultimately by the laws of karma, and according to law of karma, everybody's suffering or enjoyment is according to the law, and that law states you suffer your own past misdeed and you also enjoy your own past pious activities.

### suffering, philosophy, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4nqwMXez2dM?t=112
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "There are so many different theoretical philosophers who put forward different philosophies trying to explain what is the cause of our suffering, ultimately."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Maharaj Pariksit questioned Dharma about the cause of suffering, and Dharma replied that it's difficult to ascertain due to various philosophical theories, including those attributing suffering to oneself, superhuman powers, activity, or nature.

So this is the question between Dharma and Maharaj Pariksit. Maharaj Pariksit directly questioned Dharma as to who is the cause of your suffering. And Dharma replied that it is very difficult to ascertain the particular miscreant who has caused our sufferings, because there are so many different theories. There are so many different theoretical philosophers who put forward different philosophies trying to explain what is the cause of our suffering, ultimately. So here, four different kinds of philosophers are mentioned specifically. One class of philosophers say ultimately we ourselves are responsible for our own suffering or happiness, suffering or enjoyment. We ourselves are responsible for our own suffering or happiness, suffering or enjoyment. And some people say superhuman powers, it's not within our control, something beyond us is responsible, we simply have to accept whatever is coming our way, just like fatalists. In English, there's a word fatalists. In Kannada they say, hane baraha, whatever is destined is going to happen, destiny is everything, superhuman powers. Then some, the third category philosophers say activity is ultimately responsible. If you do good work, you get good results, you're happy, you enjoy life, everything is nice. If you do bad activities, if you do sinful activities, then you have to suffer, you have to undergo distress, and you will have a very bad time. So they attribute the ultimate cause of one's own enjoyment of suffering to whatever actions a particular person does. The fourth category of philosophers, they say nature is the ultimate cause. They attribute nature as the ultimate cause of everything.

### atheism, Sankhya philosophy, Kapila

**Summary:** The speaker discusses the six great philosophers in the Vedic tradition and then focuses on Sankhya philosophy, distinguishing between the devotional Sankhya of the original Kapila (an incarnation of the Supreme Lord) and the atheistic Sankhya of Nirishwaravadi Kapila, who considers material nature the ultimate cause.

So in the Vedic tradition, there are six great philosophers who have written very elaborate thesis on the ultimate cause of suffering that is there in this world. And those six philosophers have been named Granada, Gautama, then Patanjali, Kapila, Jaimini, and Vyasa Deva. So now, in the previous session, I described Jaimini's philosophy of karma Mimamsa philosophy, and now I will try to describe this atheistic materialistic Sankhya philosophy. Sankhya philosophy. The Sankhya philosophers, the Nirishwaravada Sankhya philosophers, because I mentioned this earlier also, I am repeating, there are two Kapilas in history. The recent one is the Nirishwaravadi Kapila, Atheist Kapila, who does not accept the theistic conclusion of the Srimad Bhagavatam or the theistic conclusion of the Vaishnava tradition. This Atheistic Kapila, because the incarnational Supreme Lord Shiva is not a Kapila. The Srimad Bhagavatam or Kapila, the first Kapila, who appeared many millions of years back, he actually is teaching us the devotional Sankhya philosophy, which analyzes everything in existence and establishes the Supreme Personality of God, Krishna, as the ultimate cause of all causes. But this Atheistic Kapila, Nirishwaravada, is not a Kapila. The Nirishwaravadi Kapila, he has got his own materialistic Sankhya philosophy, which says material nature is the ultimate cause of all causes.

### material elements, Sankhya philosophy, atheism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4nqwMXez2dM?t=654
**Verse References:** BG 7, BG 13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, this Atheist Kapila analyzes matter as consisting of 24 elements."
**Summary:** The speaker details the 24 elements of matter according to the atheistic Sankhya philosophy, as also described in the Bhagavad Gita, including the five great elements, ten senses, five sense objects, mind, intelligence, false ego, and the unmanifested material energy.

So, this Atheist Kapila analyzes matter as consisting of 24 elements. What are the 24 elements? First of all, this is described in the Bhagavad Gita by Krishna himself in the 13th chapter. In the 7th chapter and 13th chapter, Krishna describes, in the 13th chapter particularly, Mahabhutani ahankara buddhir avyakta mevaca. That Mahabhutani, the five great elements, earth, water, fire, air and sky. These are called the five great elements. And then there are the ten senses. The five knowledge acquiring senses. Gyanendriya. The eyes, the ears, the nose, the tongue and the sense of touch. Then the five karmendriyas or the five working senses. The hands, the legs, the voice, the anus and the genitals. So, totally that makes it 15. And then five more senses. The sense objects. For the eyes, there are form. And for the ears, there is sound. For the nose, there is smell. Different kinds of fragrances or different kinds of smells. And then for the tongue, there are different kinds of tastes. And for the sense of touch, there are different kinds of feelings of softness. Hardness, whatever the kind of sensation of touch. So, these five sense objects called indriya visaya. Totally, five fours are 20. And then four more for making it 24 total elements, which is what matter is constituted of. Mind, intelligence, and false ego. Mind is manas, buddhi, intelligence, ahankara, false ego. And finally, there is the total stock of the unmanifested material energy, avyakta. Avyakta. I'm sorry. Avyakta is the total stock of the material energy from which these elements are differentiated at the beginning of creation. This is what Krishna explains in the Bhagavad Gita, 13th chapter.

---
### atheism, materialism, spirit soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4nqwMXez2dM?t=844
**Verse References:** BG 2
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Any intelligent person can understand these people are ignorant about the total reality."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the atheistic Sankhya view, which sees matter as everything, with the theistic understanding that material energy is controlled and directed. He emphasizes the ignorance of atheists regarding spiritual nature and the existence of the individual spirit soul, as explained in the Bhagavad Gita.

But this atheistic Sankhya philosophers headed by atheist Kapila, they say this matter is everything. That's all that is there in existence. So, matter is manifest as these 24 elements, and these 24 elements combine, and so many varieties are produced, and they remain for some time, and afterwards everything is destroyed. So, material energy goes back to its unmanifested state, avyakta. But then who is actually causing material energy to come from the unmanifested state? From the unmanifested state to the manifest state, and remain for some time in the manifest state. In the manifest state, there are changes that are taking place. While this world exists as a material world, as a material universe, there are so many changes that are taking place. There are no explanations for this. They say it's automatic. And then, finally, what about destruction? Who brings about this destruction? How does destruction take place? How does it take place? But all the manifestations, they go back to their elemental state of the unmanifested condition of material energy, even before the five elements are formed. So, who is doing all this? How is it happening? There are no explanations. They say it's all nature. Nature. Who is behind nature? Whose nature? We had asked that question. Whose nature? Where is the nature? Where is the direction coming from? Where is the direction coming from for all these changes to take place in a definite way, in a systematic way? So, that they have no logical explanation. They have no reasonable, intelligent explanation. So, any intelligent person can understand these people are ignorant about the total reality, that beyond material nature, there is spiritual nature. And the individual person, individual soul is not material. It's non-material. This is elaborately explained by Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita, second chapter. That each living being is a spirit soul of a different quality, deathless, changeless and beyond matter. So that these atheistic philosophers don't explain. So I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### karma, materialism, atomic theory
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jcuUrWCPcWw?t=33
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everything ultimately can be broken down only into an atom."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with an introduction to the atomic philosophy of Kanada, contrasting it with karma mimamsa and atheistic Sankhya philosophies.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will continue discussing the 19th verse of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This is part 3 of the discussion. In the previous two sessions, we discussed the karma mimamsa philosophy, that karma is everything. You do good actions, everything is fine. If you do sinful activity, you will suffer. Then the second kind of philosophers, the atheistic Sankhya philosophers who analyze material nature as the ultimate cause. Material nature consists of matter. Matter consists of 24 elements and that they say is everything. Now the third kind of philosophers, the philosophers headed by Kanada. They accept the combination of atoms as the original cause of the creation. Ultimately, they analyze entire material existence consists of material. Atoms and that atom is the fundamental particle and the combination of atoms there are varieties of objects, persons, things, whatever, everything. Everything ultimately can be broken down only into an atom. So kana, kana means atom in Sanskrit. So this philosopher's name, the sage's name also Kanada because he is explaining everything in terms of atoms, so atomic philosophy. It's also called atomic philosophy, the third kind.

### atomic theory, Vedic science, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jcuUrWCPcWw?t=151
**Verse References:** SB 3.11
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, the Vedic conception of a material atom is different than the understanding of a material atom as given by today's scientists."
**Summary:** The speaker critiques the atomic philosophy, highlighting the difference between the Vedic concept of an indivisible atom and the modern scientific understanding of atoms with subatomic particles, referencing the Srimad Bhagavatam's description of atomic time.

So let's analyze what is the defect in this atomic philosophy. Yes, matter at the fundamental level is definitely the atom. Not the atom that is the fundamental. Not the atom that the modern day scientists and physicists call as atom because according to the Bhagavatam, the scriptures, our Vedic scriptures, atom is the indivisible particle, you cannot divide it any further, but these, our present day scientists, they say atom consists of subatomic particles. Subatomic particles like neutron, proton, electron, and so many more, now they have so many more. Photons, so many things they have. So they have not yet come to the atom, which is described in our scriptures, which is indivisible, cannot be divided any further. It is the smallest particle. So in the, very nicely, in the third canto, in a, this, discussion between Vidura and Maitreya Muni, Maitreya Rishi. There is a clear description. Calculation of time from the atom. This is chapter, the eleventh chapter of the third canto. There, the calculation of time is given beginning from atomic time up to universal time. See, the smallest division of time that is given in our Vedic literature, in our scriptures is atomic time. And the largest division of time is the duration of this entire universe. So, atomic time to universal time is nicely explained in the third canto of the Bhagavatam and other places in the Vedic scriptures. So, the Vedic conception of a material atom is different than the understanding of a material atom as given by today's scientists. Of course, the scientists, when they make further advancement, they will one day come to understand what is this atom, which is described in the Vedic scriptures as the indivisible particle, ultimate particle, which is indivisible, cannot be divided.
