## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | SB 1.17.20 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 01-03-2023
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Paramatma, Karma, Evidence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UyBOQsPXI3k?t=54
**Verse References:** BG 15.15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Paramatma is there in everybody's heart who has existed eternally in the past, who remains to continue to exist now, and who will remain after everything is finished."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains that even though we may not remember our past lives or have material evidence of our past actions, Paramatma, the Supreme Soul, is always present within us as a witness and director of our karma.

In this session, we will be discussing the 20th verse of the 17th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is, there are also some thinkers who believe that no one can ascertain the cause of distress by argumentation, nor know it by imagination, nor express it by words. O sage amongst kings, judge for yourself by thinking over all this with your own intelligence. So, this is the reply given by Dharma, the personality of religion, to Pariksit's question. In the purport, Srila Prabhupada explained, the Vaishnavites, the devotees of the Lord, do believe as above explained, that nothing can take place without the sanction of the Supreme Lord. He is the Supreme Director, for he confirms in the Bhagavad Gita, 15.15, that he, as all-pervading Paramatma, stays in everyone's heart and keeps vigilance over all actions and witnesses all activities. Atheists have got this argument that if you are saying that I am suffering because of some sinful activities I did in my previous life, where is the proof that I was existing in a previous life and what is the evidence for my having performed some sinful activity in some previous life? This is their argument, but even though I don't remember or I cannot produce an evidence or any living being of this world cannot produce evidence in terms of material evidence in terms of one's past life, still Paramatma is there in everybody's heart who has existed eternally in the past, who remains to continue to exist now, and who will remain after everything is finished. So he is the constant companion of every living entity in this material world. He is the perpetual witness of everything that is happening and it is he only who remembers, knows everything we have done, and who exactly directs Kala and directs material nature to inflict suffering or give enjoyment exactly according to our karma and according to the time the karma is rectified. So everything is perfectly explained in the Vedanta philosophy.

---
### Paramatma, Karma, Upanishads
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UyBOQsPXI3k?t=312
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This body is like a tree and there are two birds sitting within or on this tree: the individual spirit soul, the jiva soul, and the super soul, Paramatma."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains the relationship between the individual soul (Jivatma) and the Supreme Soul (Paramatma), using the analogy of two birds sitting on a tree from the Upanishads, where one bird enjoys the fruits of karma while the other witnesses.

Srila Prabhupada further says a living being may forget all that he might have done in his past or present, but one must know that in the same tree of the material body, the individual soul and the supreme soul as Paramatma are sitting like two birds. This is the verse of the Upanishad. The Upanishad says this body is like a tree and there are two birds sitting within or on this tree: the individual spirit soul, the jiva soul, and the super soul, Paramatma. Jivatma, Paravātma. Both of them are sitting. So the Upanishad says, one of them, the living being, is enjoying the fruits of the tree. Whatever the result of our actions is given to us as the fruits. And it is also described in the Upanishads that some of the fruits are bitter, some fruits are sweet. So the bitter fruits are the distress we suffer, and the sweet fruits are the enjoyment we get as the result of our actions in the past. Some actions yield enjoyment as sweet fruits, and some of our previous actions yield us bitter fruit, which is nothing but distress or suffering. The Supreme Being is there inside to witness the activities. Therefore the Paramatma feature, the Supreme Soul, is actually the witness of all activities of the living being, and only by His direction can the living being remember or forget what He might have done in the past. So He remembers everything and He gives direction to the living being to remember or forget.

---
### Brahman, Paramatma, Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UyBOQsPXI3k?t=452
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If Paramatma is always active and Paramatma exactly gives us the result of our own activities of the past, according to Kala, then why should we be anxious about the result of any activity we do?"
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami differentiates between the impersonal Brahman and the localized Paramatma, explaining that devotees, knowing Paramatma witnesses all, perform their duties sincerely without being overly anxious for rewards, trusting in the proper result according to their actions.

He is there for both the all-pervading impersonal Brahman and the localized Paramatma in everyone's heart. So what is the meaning of this? The Supreme Absolute Truth, Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, has got a feature called Brahman feature: impersonal, all-pervading Brahman. Impersonal because Brahman has no personal features. Brahman is energy. Brahman is the varieties of energy of the Supreme Lord. That is Brahman. And Paramatma is localized. It is in the heart of every living entity. Paramatma is seated, so localized expansion of the Supreme Lord. And that Paramatma actually is the knower of all past, present and future, and nothing can be hidden from Him. The devotees know this truth, and therefore they discharge their duties sincerely, without being overly anxious for rewards. If Paramatma is always active and Paramatma exactly gives us the result of our own activities of the past, according to Kala, then why should we be anxious about the result of any activity we do? If we do properly some activity, Paramatma will give us the proper result. We have to do it properly, but we don't care whether we do actions properly or not, and then expect that somehow I'll get success or I'll get the result I desire. No. That's never going to happen.

---
### Karma, Free Will, Service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UyBOQsPXI3k?t=571
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everyone is engaged in his service in different ways. In the conditioned state, that means our present state of existence, such services are endured by the living being under force of the material nature."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains that one cannot estimate the Lord's reactions by speculation or scholarship. Everyone is engaged in the Lord's service, either directly through devotion or indirectly through the dictation of material nature, which is also directed by Krishna.

Besides that, one cannot estimate the Lord's reactions either by speculation or by scholarship. See, what exactly we have done in the past, we do not know. Even if somehow somebody were to find out what is the particular reaction for every action, exactly cannot be determined accurately by any human being or any devata. Any created being cannot estimate, cannot find out. Why does he put someone into difficulty and not others? He is a supreme knower of the Vedic knowledge and thus he is a factual Vedantist. Same time he is a compiler of the Vedanta. No one is independent of him. Everyone is engaged in his service in different ways. This we have to also understand. What is the meaning of everyone is engaged in his service according to his will? That is, if I don't serve Krishna, which is the case in this material world with most people, then I am forced to serve the dictation of the illusory energy who is directed by Krishna. So indirectly I will have to follow Krishna, indirectly. So therefore Prabhupada says everyone is engaged in his service in different ways. In the conditioned state, that means our present state of existence, such services are endured by the living being under force of the material nature. The material nature is forcing us.

---
### Material Nature, Devotional Service, Liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UyBOQsPXI3k?t=699
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When any activity is done for the sake of pleasing Krishna, then such activities are all above the gunas, gunatit."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami elaborates on how material nature forces engagement, even in laziness, and contrasts this with devotional service, which is performed to please Krishna and is above the influence of the material modes (gunas).

We cannot say I won't do anything. We may say like that and sit down, but then we will be forced to do something by the influence of material modes. Just like a lazy person also is active mentally, is active mentally or can be active even through speech. So actions are performed through the mind, through the words, and through the body, external body. So all three types of actions are there. So even a lazy person may be physically not working, but mentally is active, vocally is active. So even the lazy person also is active. We cannot deny that. So the devotees, they are engaged in devotional service in order to please Krishna, and that is the liberated state of the devotee. When any activity is done for the sake of pleasing Krishna, then such activities are all above the gunas, gunatit. So living beings, either they do devotional service and serve the Supreme Lord for pleasing Him, they are in the liberated state, or somebody doesn't want to do devotional service, wants to be independent of Krishna, then they are forced by material nature to engage in some material activities according to the dictation of the three gunas, the illusory energy, the material energy. So in His actions, the Supreme Lord's actions, there is no incongruity or inebriety. Inebriety means there is no defect, and incongruity means there is nothing illogical or unreasonable or meaningless. Everything is full of meaning. All are on the path of the Absolute Truth. Everyone, without exception.

---
### Dharma, Leadership, Pariksit Maharaj
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UyBOQsPXI3k?t=861
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Maharaj Pariksit was put to test for His greatness and how He solves this problem of who is the cause for the suffering of the bull and the cow, that is Dharma and Mother Earth."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami concludes by stating that the sufferings of Dharma and Mother Earth were planned to demonstrate Maharaj Pariksit's ideal leadership, as he protected cows, the earth, Brahmanas, and religious principles, and that Pariksit was being tested.

The conclusion is, therefore, that the sufferings of the representative of religion and the representative of the earth, as present before Maharaj Pariksit, were planned to prove that Maharaj Pariksit was the ideal executive head because he knew well how to give protection to the cows, the earth, and the Brahmanas, religious principles, the two pillars of spiritual advancement. Maharaj Pariksit as a Rajarshi, he knew perfectly. Everyone is under the full control of the Lord, and the Lord Himself is always correct in His action when He desires something to be done by someone, irrespective of the consideration of the particular case. So Maharaj Pariksit was put to test for His greatness and how He solves this problem of who is the cause for the suffering of the bull and the cow, that is Dharma and Mother Earth. That we will see in the next verse. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### narrative, context
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lZMMEdN_tMU?t=48
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.21
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So Suta Goswami is narrating the events leading to Pariksit Maharaj meeting Shukadev Goswami."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses to be discussed (SB 1.17.21-22) and provides context by explaining that Suta Goswami is narrating the events leading up to Pariksit Maharaj meeting Shukadev Goswami to the sages of Naimisharanya.

In this session, we will be discussing from two verses, verses 21 and 22 of the 17th chapter to the first canto of Shri Madbhagavatam. The 21st verse is as follows: Suta Vacha Evam Dharmepravadati Samrat Dvijasattamah Samahitena Manasa Vikhedaparyachashtatam. The meaning of this verse is, Suta Goswami said, O best among the brahmanas, the emperor Pariksit, thus hearing the personality of religion speak, was fully satisfied and without mistake or regret, he gave his reply. Suta Goswami is addressing the brahmanas, the sages of Naimisharanya, assembled in that holy place for hearing about Krishna from Suta Goswami. So Suta Goswami is narrating the events leading to Pariksit Maharaj. Pariksit Maharaj.

Let's try to understand what does this verse mean in detail in the purport. The bull has said that it is very difficult to ascertain the particular miscreant who is responsible for our suffering why because there are multiple causes it's not just one cause this has been discussed in the Vedanta philosophy in very detailed way and we went through some of those philosophies in the previous sessions that some philosophers say the self is responsible some philosophers say that superhuman powers are responsible some say nature is the ultimate cause some say only God is the cause so all kinds of different philosophies do exist but Bhagavatam declares the proper understanding of all the causes and particularly Bhagavatam declares the supreme cause of everything that is Krishna sarva karana karanam the supreme cause yet we cannot blame God for our suffering even though ultimately God is the cause of all causes the supreme cause of everything but still we cannot blame how we will understand from the purport in the purpose will a Prabhupada says a devotee's conclusion is that no one is directly responsible for being a benefactor or mischief-monger without the sanction of the Lord.

### karma, divine will, devotion

A devotee understands that both benefit and loss are God-sent and are His grace. While devotees readily accept benefits as God's mercy, they may doubt how the Lord could allow them to suffer.

You see any action that has been committed to the Lord even in this life that takes place there is a background for that action because that action can take place only when the supreme lord seated as paramatma in the heart of everyone has to sanction that act, good or bad, harmful or beneficial. Therefore Srila Prabhupada says further he means the devotee does not consider anyone to be directly responsible for such action so nobody is directly responsible because there are a chain of causes and effects but in both the cases he takes it for granted that either benefit or loss is god sent because in both cases god is a sanctioner Krishna is paramatma in the heart of the performer of the action is sanctioning that action so therefore a devotee understands whether the result of the action is beneficial for me or it is not beneficial it is harmful or it is a loss. Both are God sent and thus it is his grace, God's grace. In case of benefit, no one will deny that it is God sent because especially devotees, they always are dependent on the mercy of the Lord and they are very pleased to receive God's mercy in the shape of so many nice benefits that they derive in any situation. ### suffering, tolerance, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lZMMEdN_tMU?t=679
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That is the way of accepting a thing either favorable or unfavorable."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the question of why God would sanction a devotee being harmed, using the example of Jesus Christ's crucifixion. He explains that pure devotees do not blame the immediate cause of their suffering, accepting it as God's will. He states that the one being harmed is equally responsible for the suffering as the one inflicting it, as both must take the reaction for their sins.

So how to understand, especially how can a devotee understand that if I am suffering due to somebody's mistreatment, ill treatment, just like the bull was directly being beaten by Kalipurusha. So how can Kalipurusha beat a devotee? Because unless God sanctions Kalipurusha's intention or desire to beat, Kalipurusha cannot do any harm independently. So then why would God sanction a devotee being beaten by a miscreant? Prabhupada gives an example. Jesus Christ was seemingly put into such great difficulty being crucified by the ignorant, but he was never angry at the mischief mongers. This is a fact. Pure devotees, acharyas and great saintly persons, they know that in any difficult circumstances, they never blame the person who is the immediate cause of the problem. That is the way of accepting a thing either favorable or unfavorable. Thus for a devotee, the identifier is equally a sinner like the mischief monger. That means, if I am being harmed by another person, I am equally responsible for getting the suffering, as much as the person who is inflicting the suffering also is responsible, has to take the reaction for that sin. And I deserve to get that. That is the way of accepting a sin. That is the way of accepting a sin. This is the way of getting the great sin. This is how the person who is suffering from that sin can get a sense of loosing the sense of love, and this is the way of getting the great evil. See, the great evil, the right way to get rid of evil is not just to have the wrong action. This is a great way to end it. Life has a heart, the right way, the left way, the right way, and the right way to face the situation. So, by God's grace,

### tolerance, Krishna consciousness, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lZMMEdN_tMU?t=822
**Verse References:** SB 10
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "My destination is to go back to Godhead. And I simply have to be Krishna conscious."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes a verse from the tenth canto of the Bhagavatam, explaining that a devotee tolerates difficulties by thinking that they deserve much more suffering due to past sinful activities, but by Krishna's grace, they receive only a small token of punishment. He emphasizes the importance of remaining Krishna conscious in all circumstances, with the ultimate goal of returning to Godhead. He concludes by reiterating that Pariksit Maharaj understood the bull was the personality of religion and that devotees see God in everything, experiencing no suffering. He summarizes that devotees remain Krishna conscious through all situations and return to Godhead at the end of life.

The devotee tolerates all reverses. There are some words in the Bhagavatam, in a later canto, in the tenth canto, Tatenu Kampam Susamikshamano Bhunjana Evatmakrutam Vipakam. A devotee tolerates all difficulties or dangers or any harm by thinking that due to my own past sinful activities in the past, my own past sinful activities, I deserve to suffer much more. But by Krishna's grace, I am getting a very small token of punishment. So let me just suffer. Let me not forget Krishna, even in the midst of difficulty or danger. And let me never forget Krishna, even if I am going through some happiness or going through some period where I have lot of benefits or lot of good things coming my way. In either case, I don't want to forget Krishna. My destination is to go back to Godhead. And I simply have to be Krishna conscious. That's all under all circumstances. Therefore, Maharaj Pariksit could understand that the bull was no other than the personality of religion himself. In other words, a devotee has no suffering at all because so called suffering is God's grace for a devotee who sees God in everything. The cow and bull never die. The cow and bull never placed any complaint before the king for being tortured by the personality of Kali, although everyone lodges such complaints before the state authorities. The extraordinary behavior of the bull made the king conclude that the bull was certainly the personality of religion, for no one else could understand the finer intricacies of the codes of religion. So therefore, the conclusion is that there are mudras. There are multiple causes and a devotee depends upon Krishna under all circumstances and by Krishna's grace, the devotee is able to go through all situations in Krishna consciousness and ultimately go back to Godhead, irrespective of whether there was suffering in the course of living life in this body or there was some enjoyment or there was some peace or there was some chaos. Whatever be the situation, devotees simply are Krishna conscious and they go back to Godhead at the end of life. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### inconceivable energies, karma, responsibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xsLpu-f0ZTY?t=51
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Knowing the ultimate doer, one should not pose himself as ignorant of the actual performer."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the inconceivable nature of the Lord's energies, the role of karma, and the responsibility of individuals for their actions, even when the Lord is the ultimate doer.

In this session, we will be discussing the 23rd verse of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this 23rd verse is, thus it is concluded that the Lord's energies are inconceivable. No one can estimate them by mental speculation or by word. Now, this is a conclusion that is drawn by Pariksit Maharaj. The Lord's energies are inconceivable. The Lord's energies are working, especially in this material world and even in the spiritual world, but in the spiritual world, it is the spiritual energy, internal potency, which is working, and in the material world, it is the external potency, the material energy, which is working. Both of them are controlled, owned, and directed by the Supreme Lord only. Krishna only is the owner, controller, director, and master of all the energies. So, by mental speculation, we cannot figure out how the Lord's energies are working, how the Lord's energies are working in different circumstances. We cannot make calculation, material calculation. That's impossible. So, in the Purport, Srila Prabhupada explains, a question may be raised as to why a devotee should refrain from identifying an actor, although he knows definitely that the Lord is the ultimate doer of everything. A devotee does not blame anyone for any difficulty or danger. A devotee does not blame anyone for any difficulty or danger. A devotee does not blame anyone for any difficulty or danger. A doer knows the problem he may be facing. Why? Even though he knows that the Lord is the ultimate doer, but that doesn't mean he blames the Lord, neither he blames the person who is the immediate cause, and therefore he also blames the Lord as the person who is the immediate cause, because there are a chain of causes and defects, because there are a chain of causes and defects, and every effect there is a cause, and every cause there is a particular justification why that cause is actually being sanctioned by the law. Knowing the ultimate doer, one should not pose himself as ignorant of the actual performer.

### karma, free will, maya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xsLpu-f0ZTY?t=231
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The material energy is always provoking doubts about the supreme authority of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the concept of karma, the illusion created by Maya Shakti, and how the Lord sanctions actions through his material energy, while not being directly responsible for them.

So, it's not that because anyway, ultimately, every person suffers because of his or her own past bad karma, I or anyone else doesn't have the license to inflict harm on others, that is not a license. It's just like sometimes people say, anyway, the animal is going to die, so if I kill it for some food, what is wrong there? It's going to die. Animal is not the body. After all, I can only kill the body. I cannot kill the soul. Animal or the living entity is the soul. So, this reasoning or argument is not correct, is invalid. The perpetrator of a sinful activity is definitely responsible for this sinful action and is going to be punished by God's laws, the laws of karma. Of this, there is no doubt at all. Yet, when the sinful action is sanctioned by the Lord, then there is a higher cause. So, it is said here, to answer this doubt, the reply is that the Lord is also not directly responsible for everything is done by his deputed Maya Shakti or material energy. In the material world, Maya Shakti, material energy is working. In every case, every material action, material energy is working. The material energy is always provoking doubts about the supreme authority of the Lord, because we directly don't see the Lord's hand in every action in this material world, but still, the fact is, the Lord is always in the background, and he only is directing the material energy. The personality of religion knew perfectly well that nothing can take place without the sanction of the Supreme Lord, and still he was put into doubts by the deluding energy. And thus, he refrained from mentioning the supreme cause.

---
### illusion, responsibility, divine will
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xsLpu-f0ZTY?t=388
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The whole atmosphere of the age of Kali is magnified by the deluding energy, and the proportion of measurement is inexplicable."
**Summary:** This segment delves into the complexities of responsibility in the age of Kali, the role of Maya, and the inexplicable nature of the illusory energy, using the example of Dharma and Kali Purusha. It then introduces the story of Markandeya Rishi's desire to witness the Lord's illusory energy in action.

This doubtfulness was due to the contamination of both Kali and the material energy. When Dharma does not complain against the Kali Purusha, so, does it mean that oh, Kali Purusha is not directly responsible, so he is not a culprit. So, Pariksit need not punish him? No, that is incorrect. At the same time, the Lord is sanctioning, only then Kali Purusha can do some harm. So, Lord is responsible? No, the Lord is not responsible. The whole atmosphere of the age of Kali is magnified by the deluding energy, and the proportion of measurement is inexplicable. The material world always, Maya's actions are definitely the direct cause of any suffering that anybody may undergo. But then, our own past sinful actions are also responsible. We are responsible. So, how does this illusory energy work? Is the question that may be raised. So, it is not easy to understand the working of the illusory energy in each and every case. So, I want to briefly narrate a particular description in the 12th Kant of the Bhagavatam. Tiruvannamalai. There was one Markandeya Rishi. He once prayed to the Supreme Lord, I want to see your illusory energy in action, because he is himself a great tapasvi, he is situated above the influence of the illusory energy, but he is seeing the illusory energy working. So many people are under the clutches of Maya and they are engaged in either material enjoyment or they sometimes suffer and they are in ignorance and they are perpetually in the cycle of birth and death. They have no clue how to get out because of ignorance. So how is this material energy working? I wanted to see. This is what Markandeya wanted to actually ask the Lord.

### devotion, benediction, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xsLpu-f0ZTY?t=532
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They mistake this material energy itself to be the absolute truth."
**Summary:** This segment narrates the arrival of Naranarayanarishi at Markandeya Rishi's ashram, the offering of benedictions, and Markandeya's specific request to witness the Lord's illusory potency, leading to his continued spiritual practice and meditation.

So once when Markandeya Rishi was sitting in his ashram performing his spiritual practice, the Supreme Lord appeared in his incarnation as Naranarayanarishi, the twin sages. And Markandeya Rishi received Naranarayanarishi very nicely. And after they had been offered all kinds of good reception, according to Vedic culture, then when they were seated, Markandeya Rishi offered nice prayers to them and the Lord, being satisfied, asked him, you can ask any benediction that you desire. So he said, I am not interested in any material benefit, knowing very well that they are useless from a spiritual perspective, but I want to, I wish to see your illusory potency, by whose influence the entire world, together with its ruling demigods, consider reality to be materially variegated. They mistake this material energy itself to be the absolute truth. No. Material energy is not the absolute truth. Krishna is the absolute truth. Krishna is in the background. Material energy is completely controlled, directed, owned by Krishna. Now, Markandeya's prayer to the Supreme Lord was accepted, and Supreme Lord said, so be it. And they departed for their ashram in Badrikashram. Thinking always of his desire to see the Lord's illusory energy, Markandeya Rishi remained in his ashram, meditating constantly upon the Lord within fire, the sun, the moon, the water, the earth, the air, lightning, his own heart, and worshipping him with paraphernalia conceived in his mind. So he was engaged in his specific spiritual practice.

---
### illusion, divine power, deluge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xsLpu-f0ZTY?t=691
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Remember, Markandeya Rishi had prayed to the Lord, I want to see your illusory energy in action."
**Summary:** This segment describes the onset of a great deluge, a manifestation of the Lord's illusory energy, and the terrifying experiences of Markandeya Rishi as he is tossed about in the flooded universe, encountering monstrous creatures and facing immense hardship.

One day, while Markandeya Rishi was performing his evening worship, suddenly a great wind, the wind arose. That wind created a terrible sound and brought in its wake fearsome clouds that were accompanied by lightning and roaring thunder. And the clouds poured down torrents of rain. Then on all sides, Markandeya Rishi saw the four great oceans swelling up. And there was wind, gusty wind. And these oceans were filled with sea monsters. And they were very, very fearsome. There were whirlpools. The sage saw all the inhabitants of the universe, including himself, tormented by the harsh winds. The bolts of lightning, the great waves rising beyond the sky, the whole earth became flooded, and he became perplexed and fearful. Remember, Markandeya Rishi had prayed to the Lord, I want to see your illusory energy in action. And now, actually the Lord is showing him what is my illusory energy like. Even as Markandeya looked on, the rain pouring down from the clouds filled the ocean more and more and more until the great sea covered up the earth's islands, mountains, continents. Completely the earth became submerged. Not only that water flooded the earth, but it also flooded the outer space, heaven, celestial region. Entire expanse of the universe was flooded in all directions. And it appeared like only Markandeya Rishi remained alive in that flood, great deluge, devastating flood. His matted hair scattered. The great sage wandered about alone in the water as if dumb and blind. He didn't know where to go, what to do. He's alive. He's somehow floating, and he doesn't know where to go and what to do. He was also tormented by hunger and thirst. He was attacked by monstrous makaras and timingala fish. Makaras means crocodiles, and very dangerous crocodiles which are huge. You can easily swallow up hundreds of Markandeya Rishis. And timingala fish. These fishes are very, very dangerous. They can devour an entire big blue sea whale. Huge. As big as ocean liners. That is attacked by a timingala. Timingala is very, very dangerous. So he saw timingala fishes. He saw monstrous crocodiles.

---
### divine revelation, illusion, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xsLpu-f0ZTY?t=887
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Suddenly the child inhaled everything, and Markandeya found himself within the body of the child."
**Summary:** This segment concludes the story of Markandeya Rishi, describing his encounter with the infant form of the Lord on a banyan leaf, his experience of being absorbed into the Lord's body and witnessing the universe within, and his eventual return to his ashram, signifying the end of the illusory display.

And he was battered by the wind and waves. He moved aimlessly in the infinite darkness into which he had fallen. Suddenly, he saw millions of years pass like that, wandering in that huge flood. His mind bewildered by the illusory energy of the Supreme Personality of Godhead Lord Vishnu. Once while wandering in the water, millions of years have passed. This Brahmana Markandeya Rishi discovered a small island. Now on this small island was a young banyan tree, and it was having, it was unaffected by that flood, by the hurricane wind, by the violent storm. Everything. This stood with nice fruits and nice blossoms of flowers. Now on a branch of that tree, he saw an infant boy, small boy, lying within a leaf. The child's effulgence was swallowing up the darkness. The infant's dark blue complexion was the color of a flawless emerald. His lotus face shone with a wealth of beauty. His throat bore marks like the lines of a conch shell. This is the Supreme Lord appearing in that form, Vata Patra Shai. He is lying on a banyan leaf, and he was sucking the toe within his mouth. So Markandeya when he saw the child, all his fatigue vanished, and just looking at the child, he felt great joy within his heart. Now who is this infant? He tried to go near the infant, and suddenly the child inhaled everything, and Markandeya found himself within the body of the child. And inside he found the universe was as it was before the dissolution. He was astonished. He saw the heavens. He saw the earth. He saw the stars. He saw the mountains. He saw everything. And he saw before him his own Badra river, his own hermitage. Now as he was kind of thinking, oh now the illusion is over, suddenly the infant exhaled and expelled the sage out of its abdomen, and he was back into the ocean of dissolution. At that moment, that child, when he came out, became again visible, invisible, became invisible, and suddenly everything vanished, the whole storm, and the, and the, oceans which were flooded, everything vanished, and Markandeya Rishi found himself in his ashram as he was seated. So this was the display of the illusory energy by the Lord. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### dharma, yugas, truthfulness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mlUDkSsg7SA?t=46
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.24
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In Kali yuga, at the beginning of Kali yuga, Dharma is left with only one principle that is truthfulness."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 24th verse of the 17th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining how Dharma, initially established on four legs (austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness) in Satya Yuga, gradually loses its strength as the ages progress, leaving only truthfulness in Kali Yuga.

In this session we will be discussing from the 24th verse of the 17th chapter of the 1st canto of Shri Madbhagavatam: Tapashaucham Dayasatyam Iti Pada Kriteyam Kriyate Krutah Adharmam Shais Trayobhagna Smaya Sanga Madais Tava. The meaning of this verse is: In the age of Satya, truthfulness, your four legs were established by the four principles of austerity, cleanliness, mercy and truthfulness. Parikshit Maharaj is speaking to the personality of religious principle dharma, the bull in the shape of the bow. That dharma in the beginning of the cycle of four yugas in Satya yuga was established on four legs, four pillars of dharma, what are they austerity, cleanliness, mercy and truthfulness: Tapaha, Shaucham, Daya, Satyam. Tapa means austerity, Shaucham means cleanliness, Daya means mercy, Satyam means truthfulness.

---
### kali yuga, degradation, irreligion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mlUDkSsg7SA?t=131
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.24
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So in Kali yuga, at the beginning of Kali yuga, Dharma is left with only one principle that is truthfulness."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the decline of Dharma through the yugas, explaining that by the time Kali Yuga arrives, only truthfulness remains as a pillar of religion, while the other three are broken due to pride, lust, and intoxication.

But by the time the ages rotate, time passes by, Satya, Treta, Dwapara, Kali. So from Satya yuga to Treta yuga, one of the leg is broken. Then from Treta yuga to Dwapara yuga, transition, one more leg is broken. And from Dwapara yuga to Kali yuga, yet one more pillar of religion is broken. So in Kali yuga, at the beginning of Kali yuga, Dharma is left with only one principle that is truthfulness. The other three principles and mercy are broken. Now, how are they broken? It is described here, they are broken due to rampant irreligion in the form of pride, lust for women and intoxication.

---
### maya, sinful activities, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mlUDkSsg7SA?t=187
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But one who completely surrenders unto the Lord can easily do so, can easily actually overcome."
**Summary:** The speaker references Prabhupada's purport, explaining that the influence of Maya is proportional to one's attraction to sinful activities, particularly intoxication, meat-eating, illicit sex, and gambling, and that surrendering to the Lord is the means to overcome Maya's power.

So, Prabhupada explains this in the purport very nicely. In the purport, Srila Prabhupada says, the deluding energy or material nature can act upon the living beings proportionately in terms of the living beings falling prey to the deluding attraction of maya. We are in the material world, the material energy, maya is acting. But maya's action or influence is possible only if we become attracted by maya's allurements to enjoy. Engage in sinful activities. Otherwise, if we refrain from sinful activities, particularly in Kali Yuga, four kinds of sinful activities: Intoxication, meat eating, illicit sex and gambling. If we refrain from these four pillars of sinful life, then we are in the material world. We are in the material world. Then, we can remain aloof from the influence of maya. So, that will be explained now by Srila Prabhupada in a little more detail. Prabhupada says, there is a standard example given in the scriptures. Moths are captivated by the glaring brightness of light and thus they become prey to the fire. Similarly, the deluding energy, deluding energy means the energy which influences us to become illusioned, is always captivating the conditioned souls to become prey to the fire of delusion. And the various scriptures warn the conditioned souls not to become prey to delusion, but to get rid of it. Don't get attracted. Don't get captivated by some illusion, illusory energies display of some opportunity for enjoyment in sinful ways. Don't get captivated. The Vedas warn us not to go to the darkness of ignorance, but to the progressive path of light, tamasoma jyotir gama. Tamasoma. Don't remain in darkness or don't go more into darkness. Come out of darkness and go towards light. The Lord himself also warns that the deluding power of material energies is too powerful to overcome. Daivi, Hesha, Gunamayi, Mamamaya, Durakyaya. It's too powerful. Durakyaya. But one who completely surrenders unto the Lord can easily do so, can easily actually overcome. Ma mevaye prapadyante maya metam tarantite. But to surrender unto the lotus feet of the Lord is also not very easy.

### austerity, cleanliness, mercy, truthfulness, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mlUDkSsg7SA?t=397
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that surrender to Krishna is possible through austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness, which are the pillars of religious principles, and that simply avoiding sinful activities is not enough.

Such surrender is possible by persons of austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness. So on the one side are four things. So on the one side are four pillars of sinful life. The deluding energy, the illusory energy is attracting the conditioned souls come enjoy in sinful ways. Other side are the four pillars of religion, religious principles. Now simply avoiding sinful activities alone does not enable a person to surrender to Krishna. That is required. In addition to avoiding sinful activities one has to practice the principles of austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness. Then it is possible to surrender to Krishna.

### satya yuga, brahmanical culture, degradation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mlUDkSsg7SA?t=465
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In that age every human being was practically a qualified Brahman of the highest order."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the remarkable features of Satya Yuga, where everyone was a qualified Brahman and free from Maya's influence, and how the curtailment of Brahmanical culture (austerity, cleanliness, mercy, truthfulness) led to the retreat of transcendental bliss.

And by surrendering to Krishna one can overcome the influence of the illusory energy completely. These four principles of advanced civilization were remarkable features in the age of Satya. The first age in the cycle of four yugas. Satya Yuga. In that age every human being was practically a qualified Brahman of the highest order. And in the social orders of life they were all Paramahamsas or the topmost in the renounced order. All human beings were of the topmost class in the Satya Yuga. By cultural standing the human beings were not at all subjected to deluding energy. They were not affected by Maya in Satya Yuga. All the human beings. Such strong men of character were competent enough to get away from the clutches of Maya. But gradually as the principles of Brahmanical culture, what are the principles of Brahmanical culture? Austerity, cleanliness, mercy, truthfulness. Austerity is Tapa. Shaucham. Tapa is austerity. Shaucham is cleanliness. Daya is mercy. Satya is truthfulness. As these principles became curtailed by proportionate development of pride, attachment for women and intoxication, the path of salvation or the path of transcendental bliss retreated far far away from human society.

---

With the progression of the age of Kali people are becoming very proud. They are becoming too much attached to women. Women means I will explain this. And intoxication. Here women means objects of enjoyment, especially objects of sex pleasure. This characteristic of Kali Yuga. People are more and more and more after sex pleasure. And they are very easily become attached and attracted. Attracted and hence attached to enjoyment with the member of the opposite sex. Whether women or men. Women are attracted to men and men are attracted to women for sex pleasure. This is the characteristic of Kali Yuga. By the influence of the age of Kali. Srila Prabhupada explains further. Even a pauper is proud of his penny. As far as pride is concerned. There is no reason to be proud for a person who has very very very little money in his possession. But even the poorest of poor fellow, if he gets some money, little, few rupees let's say nowadays, few rupees, he is very proud. Oh I have so much wealth. I am a very rich person. He thinks like that. He becomes proud. The women are always dressed in an overly attractive fashion to victimize the minds of men. You see. The dressing fashion of the women in Kali Yuga, simply to attract men for sex enjoyment.

### intoxication, corruption, mind control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mlUDkSsg7SA?t=671

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker continues describing Kali Yuga, focusing on intoxication and its connection to corruption, explaining that the root cause of corruption lies in these irreligiosities and that the cure is to follow the principles of austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness.

They dress in a very bad way. And the man is addicted to drinking wine, smoking, drinking tea, chewing tobacco, etc. All varieties of intoxication. Hmm. All these habits or so called advanced under civilization are the root causes of all irreligiosities. And therefore it is not possible to check corruption, bribery and nepotism. Lot of people talk about too much corruption. Too much corruption. How to root out this corruption? So the root cause of corruption is being pointed out here. What is it? All these irreligiosities. If people are addicted to intoxication, they are addicted to sex pleasures, they are addicted to more and more possessions, greed for possessions, because of which they become overly proud of possessing many things, then it is not possible to avoid corruption. Man cannot check all these evils simply by statutory acts and police vigilance. But he can cure the disease of the mind by the proper medicine. It is actually a disease which is within the mind. This propensity for enjoyment by sinful activities is a disease of the mind. And what is the cure for this? The proper medicine for this disease of the mind is the principles of austerity, cleanliness, mercy and truthfulness.

### Maya, desire, sinful enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/x_w_GwAJ0tU?t=131
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Maya is alluring. Come, enjoy sinfully."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how the influence of Maya is directly related to one's desire for sinful enjoyment, emphasizing that the scriptures warn against being captivated by Maya's allurement.

So in the purport which we discussed in the previous session, part 1 of this presentation of the same verse, it is explained that the influence of Maya is proportionate to the extent to which I become captivated by Maya's allurement for enjoying sinfully. Understand this very carefully. Maya is alluring. Come, enjoy sinfully. And if I think, oh let me enjoy because I am seeking happiness. Let me enjoy, even if it is in a sinful way, let me enjoy. Then I become prey to the influence of Maya. So the scriptures are warning, don't be captivated by Maya's allurement. Now somebody may say, why should Krishna arrange Maya to allure us in the first place? That is because we have desire for enjoying in that sinful way. We have developed that desire. Why are we here in the material world? Because of our desire to enjoy materially. Materially means sinfully. Materially means sinfully. So, if we have desire for enjoyment, then only we will become captivated by Maya's allurement.

### analogy, desire, religious principles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/x_w_GwAJ0tU?t=240
**Verse References:** BG 7.11
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna says, I am sex life, which is in accordance with religious principles."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of a teetotaler versus a liquor addict, the speaker illustrates how desire influences attraction to Maya. He then explains Krishna's statement in the Bhagavad Gita about regulated sex life for Krishna conscious progeny.

To give a very simple example, if, let us say, you are a complete teetotaller, you don't, you completely abstain from drinking any liquor. You don't take any liquor. You are strict in never touching even liquor. Now, you are walking on, in the market, on the street, when there are so many shops. Now, when you pass by a liquor store, either you don't even pay attention, or if you see, oh, liquor store. Okay, I have nothing to do with it. You just go by, pass by. You have nothing to do with liquor store. But somebody who is addicted to drinking liquor, or liquor store, moment he sees, or get the smell, or get some idea of a liquor store, immediately so much attraction to enter the liquor store, to find out what is new, to buy something, or to taste something, or to consider enjoying some drink, etc. So, like that, the same principle that works in this material world is, if I have a desire in my heart, I want to enjoy sex pleasure. If I have a desire. And that too, I am having a desire to enjoy in sinful ways. Because, Krishna says in Bhagavad Gita, dharma aviruddha kamosmi bhara tarshama dharma aviruddha bhuteshu kamosmi bhara tarshama. Krishna says, I am sex life, which is in accordance with religious principles. Sex life has a place in this material world to bring forth Krishna conscious progeny, to bring forth devotees. So, sex life is required.

### devatas, laws of nature, utilization of resources

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker explains that devatas are bound by the laws of nature and cannot regulate how wealth obtained through them is utilized, emphasizing that it is the individual's responsibility to use resources in a religious or irreligious way.

And the devatas, they simply are bound by the laws of nature to award according to certain rules, certain regulations. So, they cannot consider or they do not consider or they cannot consider the merit of the person who is praying for certain benefit. They cannot consider the devatas. So, if somebody is approaching a devata, according to the Vedic descriptions, for getting some wealth, then they will get the wealth if they follow all the rules and regulations. Now, after getting the wealth, how they utilize the wealth, the devata cannot actually regulate. Devata cannot regulate. So, it is up to the person who has got wealth or who has got power or who has got some knowledge, or who has got some resource. How to utilize that resource?

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on how disseminating the knowledge of the Bhagavatam, along with practices like Sankirtana and prasadam distribution, can cleanse people's minds and defeat Maya. He emphasizes that true peace cannot be achieved without addressing the contamination of the heart caused by sinful desires.

There is a solution. The rich people, the powerful people, should spend 50% of their wealth for educating the common people in Krishna Consciousness. Simply through disseminating this Bhagavatam, knowledge given in the Bhagavatam, the attractive pastimes of Krishna and his pure devotees, making people hear that, making people hear Sankirtana, chant Hare Krishna, nice music, nice japa, and distributing prasadam, then people's mind will become cleansed of the dirt accumulated due to the influence of Kali. And then Maya will be defeated. Kali will be defeated and Maya will be defeated. And also Prabhupada says here, false pride or too high an estimation of one's own values of life, undue attachment to women or association with them, simply for the sake of sex pleasure, and intoxication, addiction to intoxication, will divert human civilization from the path of peace. However much people may clamor for peace in the world, people want peace. But simply if you simply desire peace and have big peace conferences and peace talks and peace propagation of peaceful way of living, that's not going to work as long as people's heart is contaminated by desire for sex enjoyment, sinful activities, you cannot have peace in the society at all.

### Kali Yuga, Time, Godlessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uNxTVcIlg1A?t=60
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The day Krishna left this earth planet and went back to his personal kingdom, that is when Kali Yuga began."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the Kali Yuga, its duration, its beginning with Krishna's departure, and its influence of godlessness, which was initially mitigated by righteous kings.

This chapter is entitled Punishment and Reward of Kali. Kali represents the Kali Yuga, the present age. Time is passing on this earth planet and is measured in terms of the number of years, a particular age called Yuga. It's a very, very big time period. Like Kali Yuga, the present age, lasts 432,000 years or we can say 4,32,000 years. Kali Yuga began about 5,000 years ago when Krishna appeared at the end of Dwapar Yuga. Kali Yuga and Krishna went back to his personal abode after winding up his pastimes. The day Krishna left this earth planet and went back to his personal kingdom, that is when Kali Yuga began. But the influence of the age of Kali, which is to bring in godlessness, was actually not felt by the people because of the wonderful administration of Parikshit Maharaj and similar Krishna conscious kings. So that is what will be described in this chapter, Punishment and Reward of Kali.

### Cow protection, Dharma, Kali Yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uNxTVcIlg1A?t=168
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In a civilization where God is conspicuously banished and there is no devotee warrior like Arjuna, the associates of the age of Kali take advantage of this lawless kingdom and arrange to kill innocent animals like the cow in secluded slaughterhouses."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Parikshit Maharaj's reaction to the beating of a cow by Kali Purusha, emphasizing the importance of cow protection and contrasting it with the godless civilization of Kali Yuga where cows are killed in slaughterhouses.

So the sixth verse. The meaning of this verse is, you rogue. It is Parikshit Maharaj addressing that Kali Purusha, the personality of Kali, who is beating a common bull in a secluded place. Parikshit is addressing that Kali Purusha. The meaning of this verse is, you rogue. Do you dare beat an innocent cow because Lord Krishna and Arjuna, the carrier of Gandiva bow, are out of sight? Since you are beating the innocent in a secluded place, you are considered a culprit and therefore deserve to be killed. So the king is pointing out the action of this Kali Purusha is a criminal act and therefore he deserves to be killed, death sentence or death penalty because an innocent cow is beaten in a secluded place so the cow should be given all kinds of protection against any harm including even just beating the cow. So in the purport Srila Prabhupada says in a civilization where God is conspicuously banished and there is no devotee warrior like Arjuna, the associates of the age of Kali take advantage of this lawless kingdom and arrange to kill innocent animals like the cow in secluded slaughterhouses.

---
### Godless civilization, Lawlessness, Animal slaughter
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uNxTVcIlg1A?t=313
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Those in power, political power or those in governmental authority they don't conduct the state in terms of the codes given by God the codes given in our authorized Vedic scriptures."
**Summary:** This segment compares the present-day society to the beginning of Kali Yuga, highlighting the godless nature of rulers, their disregard for Vedic codes, and the resulting lawlessness that allows for the cruel slaughter of cows.

Srila Prabhupada is comparing the present day status of the society is somewhat similar to the situation being described which Parikshita encountered the beginning of Kali Yuga so our present civilization is more or less a godless civilization as far as the rulers are concerned. Those in power, political power or those in governmental authority they don't conduct the state in terms of the codes given by God the codes given in our authorized Vedic scriptures. They don't conduct the state in terms of the codes given by God the codes given in our authorized Vedic scriptures. In short they don't conduct the governance in accordance with the Dharma Shastras. The Manu Smriti, Parashara Smriti or Yajna Valkya Smriti. Perfect codes are given, perfect laws are given. They are not man-made laws. They are given by liberated souls. They are not man-made laws. They are given by liberated souls. They are not man-made laws. They are given by liberated souls. So there is no mistake in them. Neither there is any need to amend those laws from time to time. There is no need of any amendment. They are perfect laws. So these present day rulers in this Kali Yuga especially God is conspicuously banished from their governance. It's a godless state. And the person's ruling are not exactly like Arjuna or Pariksit Maharaj in terms of conducting the state affairs in order to maintain law and order. They don't conduct themselves properly. Then, as Prabhupada says here, the associates of the age of Kali take advantage of this lawless kingdom and arrange to kill innocent animals like the cow in secluded slaughterhouses.

---
### Animal cruelty, Justice, Punishment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uNxTVcIlg1A?t=477
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Such murderers of animals stand to be condemned to death by the order of a pious king like Maharaj Pariksit."
**Summary:** This segment condemns the cruel killing of animals in modern slaughterhouses, comparing it to the murder of an innocent child, and emphasizes that such acts deserve the death penalty according to pious kings like Parikshit Maharaj.

Nobody knows how the cow suffers in the slaughterhouse. Formerly the killing was done in the butcher shop if at all animals were to be killed for meat. But now, especially in the modernized states where modern technology is employed the people eat packed meat and they think it is like packed vegetables or packed biscuits or packed grains. Like that they think. No, it is not that. It is very cruel killing of the animals who suffer so much. Such murderers of animals. See the word Prabhupada uses is murder. That means somebody who is supposed to be protected is being killed unlawfully. Is being killed unlawfully. So such murderers of animals stand to be condemned to death by the order of a pious king like Maharaj Pariksit. Pariksit Maharaj will not tolerate such a killing. He doesn't even tolerate a beating of a cow. What to speak of killing an animal like cow. For a pious king the culprit who kills an animal in a secluded place is punishable by the death penalty exactly like a murderer who kills an innocent child in a secluded place. Prabhupada gives a comparison. If a child is killed it is helpless. Supposing it is killed by some rogue. That is the term used by Prabhupada for this Kali Purusha who is beating an innocent cow in a secluded place. So it is exactly like killing a child. Same way if a child is killed, innocent child is killed in a secluded place, then the murderer is punishable by the death sentence. Exactly like that. Even the one who harms a cow should be actually punished by death penalty.

---
### Inquiry, Compassion, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uNxTVcIlg1A?t=628
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Are you some demigod causing us grief in the form of a bull?"
**Summary:** This segment explains the seventh verse, where Parikshit Maharaj inquires about the bull's identity and condition, wondering if it's a demigod indicating future misfortune for cows and bulls.

The seventh verse Tvamva brunala dhavalaha paadai niunah. padacharan vrisharoopena kim kashchid devonah parikhedayan. The meaning is Then he, Maharaj Pariksit, asked the bull O who are you? Are you a bull as white as a white lotus? Or are you a demigod? You have lost three of your legs and are moving on one only. Are you some demigod causing us grief in the form of a bull? So Pariksit Maharaj is next questioning the bull. Because the bull was not exactly like an ordinary bull. As white as a white lotus. So very very beautiful to look at. Except that it had lost three legs and it was moving on only one leg. So what was this bull trying to communicate? So he is wondering whether it is simply a bull or is it a devata who has come in the form of a bull to communicate something. Are you some demigod causing us grief in the form of a bull? Indicating some misfortune, some bad time is going to come or is foreboding some danger for such innocent animals.

## Disappearance Day of Sri Murari Gupta | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | ISKCON Bangalore Sanga
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Devotion, Murari Gupta, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hoeoWS0w8sI?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Today is the disappearance day anniversary of Shri Murari Gupta, who was an intimate associate of Shri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu."
**Summary:** This segment introduces Shri Murari Gupta, his association with Shri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, his qualities, his profession as a physician, and instances of his service to the Lord.

Today is the disappearance day anniversary of Shri Murari Gupta, who was an intimate associate of Shri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. Let us hear about his wonderful devotional qualities from Shri Chaitanya Charitamrita. Murari Gupta was a storehouse of love of Godhead. His great humility and meekness melted the heart of Lord Chaitanya. Shri Murari Gupta wrote a book called Shri Chaitanya Charita. He belonged to a Vaidya physician family of Shri Hatha, the paternal home of Lord Chaitanya and later became a resident of Navadvipa. He was among the elders of Shri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. Shri Murari Gupta never accepted charity from friends nor did he accept money from anyone. He practiced as a physician and maintained his family. With his earnings, as Murari Gupta treated his patients, by his mercy, both their bodily and spiritual diseases subsided. Once Sivananda Sena offered food to Lord Chaitanya that had been cooked with excessive ghee, and the next day, Lord Chaitanya became sick and went to Murari Gupta for treatment. Lord Chaitanya accepted some water from the water pot of Murari Gupta, and thus, he was cured. The natural remedy for indigestion is to drink a little water, and since Murari Gupta was a physician, he gave the Lord some drinking water and cured him. When Chaitanya Mahaprabhu appeared in the house of Srivastakur in his Chaturbhuja murti, Murari Gupta became his carrier in the form of Garuda and in these pastimes of ecstasy, the Lord then got up on his feet and passed down the back of Murari Gupta. When Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu one day appeared in ecstasy as the Varaha Murti,

---
### Devotion, Lord Ramachandra, Testing of Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hoeoWS0w8sI?t=119
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Hearing this pure devotion Shri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu became overjoyed."
**Summary:** This segment narrates how Lord Chaitanya tested Murari Gupta's unwavering devotion to Lord Ramachandra, and how Murari Gupta's steadfastness pleased the Lord.

Murari Gupta offered him nice prayers. Murari Gupta gave Zuvar posso kepada Sat-Siat Rao one time. Once Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu glorified Murari Gupta for his unf constellating devotion and loyalty to Lord Rama Chandra because Murari Gupta was a great devotee of Lord Rama. Once Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu glorified Murari Gupta for his unf constellating devotion and loyalty to Lord Rama Chandra because Mahaprakasha form, Lord Chaitanya appeared before Murari Gupta as Lord Ramachandra. Just to test Murari's faith, he repeatedly encouraged Murari Gupta to shift his worship from Lord Rama to Lord Krishna, describing the unparalleled sweetness, supremacy and all attractive nature of Shri Krishna, who is the origin of all incarnations and the reservoir of all transcendental qualities and pleasure. Although Murari heard these divine glories of Krishna from the Lord himself, his heart remained steadfastly fixed on his eternal master Lord Ramachandra. Murari being utterly devoted could not imagine giving up the service of Lord Rama's lotus feet. That night he was deeply troubled, weeping continuously and praying that death might take him before he was forced to abandon his worship of Lord Ramachandra. He spent the entire night awake, overwhelmed by distress. The next morning Murari approached Lord Chaitanya, fell at his feet and humbly confessed that though he desired to please the Lord, he could not forsake Lord Ramachandra for he had sold his head and heart to Lord Rama's service. Torn between obedience to Chaitanya's command and loyalty to Lord Ramachandra Murari begged for death as his only release. Hearing this pure devotion Shri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu became overjoyed. Embracing Murari Gupta, Lord Chaitanya praised his unwavering faith declaring that such steadfast devotion is a true hallmark of a servant. The Lord revealed that his repeated persuasion was merely a test to demonstrate Murari's deep unbreakable attachment to Lord Ramachandra. Recognizing him as an incarnation of Hanuman, the eternal servant of Rama, Mahaprabhu accepted Murari Gupta as his very life and soul moved by his humility and unalloyed devotion.

---

## Disappearance Day of Srila Narottama Dasa | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | ISKCON Bangalore Sanga
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### biography, devotion, Gaudiya Vaishnavism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ugj5n_4y1b8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The songs of Narottama Das Thakur are spiritual thunderbolts that crack open the heart. They descend directly from the spiritual world."
**Summary:** This segment narrates the life and significance of Srila Narottama Das Thakur, highlighting his devotion, teachings, and contributions to the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition.

Today is the disappearance day of Srila Narottam Das Thakur. Let us hear his glorious biography from the Chaitanya Charita Amrita. Srila Narottam Das Thakur, a preeminent Acharya in the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition, was an eternal associate of Lord Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. In his spiritual identity, he is known as Champaka Manjari, an intimate maidservant of Srimati Radharani, who eternally assists Sri Rupa Manjari in serving Radha and Krishna. Out of compassion for the conditioned souls, Champaka Manjari descended to earth as Narottam Das Thakur in the village of Kheturi in present-day Bangladesh around the mid-16th century. Narottama appeared on the full moon day of Magha as the son of King Krishnananda Datta and Queen Narayani Devi. His birth was marked by auspicious omens, and the entire kingdom rejoiced with his father distributing generous charity to the local brahmanas. Even as a child, Narottama demonstrated profound devotion during his Annaprashana ceremony, which is the first grain feeding ceremony for the infant child. He refused to eat until the food was first offered to Lord Krishna, thus teaching the sanctity of Prasadam. An astrologer observing his divine features declared that the child would become the greatest among men and thus should be named Narottama. Gifted with an extraordinary memory, Narottama was a Shruti Dharam, able to retain everything he heard. He mastered Sanskrit, grammar and the Vedas with ease, but his heart was fixed on serving Krishna. Remaining a lifelong Brahmachari, he was indifferent to the comforts of royal life. Concerned by his growing detachment, his parents placed guards to keep watch over him. But Narottama's only happiness lay in hearing the divine pastimes of Lord Chaitanya and Lord Nityananda, narrated to him daily by Shri Krishnadas Kaviraj Goswami. Immersed in remembrance of the Lord, he once had a dream in which Shri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu instructed him to travel to Vrindavan. When his father was away, Narottama escaped from home to fulfill the Lord's instruction. As he journeyed toward Vrindavan, his saintly nature attracted everyone's affection and people offered him food and shelter along the way. Upon reaching Vrindavan, he met Srila Jeeva Goswami and Srinivasa Acharya. Their meeting was filled with spiritual joy and affection and Narottama and Srinivasa soon became inseparable friends united in their love for the Lord. Srila Jeeva Goswami introduced Narottama to Shri Loknath Goswami. Narottama humbly fell at his feet and Loknath Goswami moved by his sincerity blessed him and later agreed to initiate him. Srila Jeeva Goswami also introduced Narottama to Shri Gopalabhatta Goswami and Shri Bhuggadbhag Goswami who received him affectionately. Narottama visited the holy temples of Rathadamoda and Madan Mohan and upon seeing the samadhis of Rupa and Sanatana Goswamis, he wept uncontrollably in love and devotion. Later with Srinivasa Acharya, he visited Radhakunda and Shamakunda and received the blessings of Sri Raghunath Das Goswami who was deeply touched by his devotion. Under the care of Jeeva Goswami, Narottama studied deeply the scriptures of Bhakti and soon became renowned for his humility, scholarship and realization. Srila Lokarnath Goswami, seeing Narottama's pure-hearted service finally initiated him into the chanting of the Maha Mantra. His exceptional understanding of devotional literature impressed all the Vaishnavas of Vrindavan and Sri Jeeva Goswami bestowed upon him the title Sri Thakur Mahasaya. From then on, he became a leading teacher and preacher in the Gaudiya Vaishnava movement. Srila Prabhupada said of his songs, "The songs of Narottama Das Thakur are spiritual thunderbolts that crack open the heart. They descend directly from the spiritual world." Srila Narottama Das Thakur's two principal works are Prarthana and Prema Bhakti Chandrika which beautifully express the essence of Gaudiya Vaishnava philosophy guiding devotees from Srinivasa Acharya to the spiritual world. They have the ability to take to the higher stages of divine love. Srila Narottama Das Thakur organized the grand Keturi Mahotsava festival at his birthplace, the first large-scale Gaud Purnima celebration which commemorates Lord Chaitanya's appearance. This event united Lord Chaitanya's followers from many regions and established the foundation for his worship across Bengal. Although born into a Kayastha community, traditionally considered of lower caste, Narottama became the spiritual master of thousands including many learned brahmanas such as Ganga Narayan Chakravarthi and Ramachandra Kaviraj. These exalted disciples humbly served him, acknowledging his transcendental position. Despite criticism from caste conscious brahmanas, Narottama remained compassionate, embracing everyone equally and demonstrating that spiritual qualification is measured by devotion not by birth. In his later life, Narottama became a spiritual master of the old caste. This is the reason why he became a spiritual master of the new caste. After his death, Narottama experienced deep separation from the Lord and his associates. Having witnessed the departure of the six Goswamis, Srinivas Acharya and Ramachandra Kaviraj, he poured his heart out in the famous song Jai Anilo Prema Dhana, expressing grief over the loss of the great devotees who brought divine love to the world. His disappearance was miraculous. Aware that bathe him by pouring Ganga water over his body. As they did this, his body turned white like milk and gradually melted into the sacred Ganges. Heartbroken but obedient, his disciples collected the mixture of milk, tears, and Ganga water and enshrined it at Kheturi, where a beautiful Samadhi was built, known as Dut Samadhi, the Samadhi of Milk. Srila Narottam Das Thakur remains a radiant example of pure devotion, humility, and compassion. His life and songs reveal the heart of Prema Bhakti, unconditional love for Krishna, and his teachings transcend all material distinctions. Srila Prabhupada revered his bhajans as spiritual scripture and they continue to inspire generations of devotees worldwide.

## Exclusive Q&A Session 73| Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami |22-12-22
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### social order, qualification, misuse of skills
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/R7S-sbd5kgU?t=89
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, General public
**Key Quote:** "If a wrong person has got some skill, he can misuse it."
**Summary:** The speaker explains why Dronacharya asked Ekalavya for his thumb as Guru Dakshina, comparing it to the restriction of Vedic knowledge for Shudras, arguing it's to prevent misuse of power by the unqualified.

Why did Dronacharya ask Ekalavya to give his thumb as Guru Dakshina? Is it to show the importance of surrendering to Guru or is it because Ekalavya was violating social order? Actually, Ekalavya was not a proper qualified person to have that skill of shooting arrows. If a wrong person has got some skill, he can misuse it. It is just like it is said in the scriptures itself: Shudras are not taught the Vedas. Why? That's because the Shudras don't have the intelligence to properly utilize the Vedic knowledge and use it for everyone's welfare, good. They may misuse it for some bad purposes or wrong purposes. It is just like somebody is a terrorist, he should not have weapons because he will misuse it like that. So, Ekalavya was not actually qualified to have the skill of shooting arrows. So, by asking him to give his thumb as Guru Dakshina, Dronacharya actually cut down his ability to use that skill, because the thumb is very important in actually shooting using a bow and arrow. So, that way he curtailed his usage for the social good, for the good of everyone else. That's the reason.

### Vaikuntha, goal of life, spiritual body
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/R7S-sbd5kgU?t=232
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Attaining Vaikuntha means you understand I am spirit, soul. I don't die at the time of death. I change body."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the significance of Vaikuntha Dwaara and Vaikuntha Ekadashi, emphasizing that the ultimate goal of life is to attain Vaikuntha by understanding oneself as a spirit soul and preparing for a spiritual body.

It is said that by passing through Vaikuntha Dwaara, one can attain Vaikuntha. Where can we find the source of the statement? This is said in one of the Puranas. Now, the significance of Vaikuntha Ekadashi, the day on which this festival is celebrated, especially in Vishnu temples, that one can pass through Vaikuntha Dwaara, that is to remind us that our destination should be Vaikuntha after quitting this body. And to make our destination as Vaikuntha is the correct goal of life. Otherwise, people either they do not have a goal of life in the sense that when we quit this body, which we are forced to do at the time of death, then where do I go? That people do not even know that they are spirit, soul and they are eternal. That itself they do not know. So, this is a particular important point that you understand. Attaining Vaikuntha means you understand I am spirit, soul. I don't die at the time of death. I change body. So, if I prepare myself, I can get a spiritual body and in that spiritual body I can attain Vaikuntha Loka. For eternal life full of bliss and knowledge that is awaiting us. If we want to go there, if we understand the importance of practicing Bhakti in order to go there, then we can certainly go there.
