### purification, material contamination, spiritual world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/R7S-sbd5kgU?t=350
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As long as we have material contamination or material desires, we have to keep taking birth in this material world."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the necessity of purifying consciousness from material contamination to attain the spiritual world, emphasizing that material desires bind one to repeated births in the material realm.

So, merely doing a ritual of passing through Vaikuntha Dwaara is not enough. Passing through Vaikuntha Dwaara is to remind ourselves that we should prepare our consciousness by purifying it of all material contamination. So, the Supreme Lord is very eager to take us back to his personal abode called Vaikuntha. And he wants us to qualify. Qualify in what sense? Due to long association with this material nature, our consciousness has become polluted by material desires, by material reactions, by material contamination. So, to purify this is essential. As long as we have material contamination or material desires, we have to keep taking birth in this material world. Because those desires can be fulfilled only in this material world. Material contamination is not permitted in the spiritual world. Spiritual world, everything is spiritual. The Supreme Lord is completely spiritual. His abode is completely spiritual. The devotees are completely spiritually situated. So, everything is spiritual there. Nothing is material. So, therefore, we have to prepare ourselves. And anyway, that is our original position. As spirit soul, we belong to the spiritual world. We have a relationship with the Supreme Lord in his personal abode. And he is our maintainer. He is our protector. He is our well-wisher. He is our everything in the spiritual world. But then if we remain with this material desires or material comforts or material aspirations, then we cannot leave this world and go away. Not possible.

**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker explains how to remember Krishna's form, emphasizing the importance of temple visits, concentrating on the deity, and associating with Krishna through scriptures, prayers, and prasadam to prepare for the spiritual kingdom.

So, please remember, this going through Vaikuntha Dwara is to remind us that prepare yourself before death comes. Become Krishna conscious. And be ready to go to the abode of the Supreme Lord Vaikuntha. Next question. In the introduction to Bhagavad Gita As It Is, Srila Prabhupada says, if one remembers the form of Krishna at a time of death, he can approach the spiritual kingdom. What is this form and how to remember? Okay. The form of Krishna, Krishna is present in several different forms. The easiest way to understand form of Krishna is the form of the deity of Krishna, Krishna Vikraha, Archa Vikraha. You go to any temple of Krishna, there is the Krishna deity. Deity, Vikraha. So, that is one form of Krishna, the particular deity in a particular temple. So, remembering that form, how do you remember that form? When you go to the temple, you completely concentrate your attention on the form of Krishna as the deity and get the impression in your heart. And carry that impression with you even after you die. Even after you leave the temple and go away to your home or office and remember that form and pray and hear the glories of Krishna from the Bhagavatam. Learn the instructions of Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita. All this you connect with that remembrance of the form of Krishna. Krishna is my supreme well-vision. Krishna is my maintainer. Krishna is my beloved Lord. Krishna is the ultimate source of everything. Krishna is full of all unlimited good qualities. Like this, thinking of Krishna, talking about Krishna, remembering Krishna's form within the heart and hearing about Krishna, taking Krishna Prasadam, worshipping the deity of Krishna or picture of the deity of Krishna, all this actually prepares us to approach the spiritual kingdom.

But remembering at the time of death is made possible by the mercy of the Supreme Lord who is situated in our heart. If we endeavor to try to remember while we are in this material world doing so many different activities, simultaneously we try to remember Krishna, practice remembering Him constantly, then Krishna will give us remembrance of Him at the time of death and take us back to God. Next question. In the third canto, when Lord Brahma offers His praise to Vishnu, did He offer the prayers before the material creation or after the creation? Now the material creation it's a long process. It begins with the Supreme Lord incarnating in this material world, in the material energy. The Lord enters as the first Vishnu form from the spiritual world who descends into this material energy. So that is called Karanodaka Shaini Vishnu, also called Maha Vishnu. So Maha Vishnu first appears, enters the material energy which is lying in a dormant condition, unmanifest condition. That means at that time there are no universes. Previous destruction took place and there was only the material energy. So that energy first of all Maha Vishnu enters. Then Maha Vishnu agitates that energy and the energy becomes activated by Maha Vishnu's glance.

### material creation, Vishnu, Brahma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/R7S-sbd5kgU?t=776
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The primary creation is the creation of the ingredients for the creation."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the process of material creation, starting with Maha Vishnu entering the dormant material energy, agitating it, and leading to the birth of Brahma, who then performs the secondary creation of living entities.

And then after Maha Vishnu's glance, first of all the agitation produces Mahatatva. And Mahatatva differentiates the elements. And those elements are combined to form varieties of material objects, material planets. And then from there is one expansion of this Maha Vishnu called Padmanabha Vishnu or Garbhodaka Shaini Vishnu. So that Garbhodaka Shaini Vishnu from his navel one lotus flower is born. On that Brahma is created by Vishnu. And Brahma after his creation, he doesn't know who he is, he doesn't know where he is, he doesn't know what he is supposed to do. So he began searching out for a source and he begins contemplation. Who am I? Where have I come from? What am I supposed to do? Etc, etc. So then Lord Vishnu actually who is seated in the heart of Brahma, he enlightens Brahma, gives knowledge to Brahma and Brahma then understands that he is to do the secondary creation. What is secondary creation? The primary creation is the creation of the ingredients for the creation. That is all the elements earth, water, fire, air, sky, mind, intelligence, objects, everything is the elements. That is done by Maha Vishnu through agitation of the material energy. Then Brahma, birth of Brahma is done by Garbhoda Darsahi Vishnu. Then Brahma is given knowledge and enlightenment by Vishnu. Then Brahma can create the living entities, the bodies, living entities means the bodies, he can give the bodies for the living entities. By bringing forth the different varieties of the species. So that gives the bodies to the living entities or helping them take birth in this material world. So that is the work of creation of Brahma.

**Summary:** The speaker explains that Brahma offers prayers to Vishnu before generating living entities, feeling sorry for their inevitable suffering in the material world and their cycle of birth and death, emphasizing the need for rescue through devotional service.

After Brahma is born and already Vishnu has done the work of creation to the point of generating the elements, then Brahma actually does the further work of creation of generating the living beings with their proper bodies in the different planets that take birth according to their past karma. That is work of Brahma to generate the living entities. So before he can actually generate the living entities, he offers prayers to Vishnu that he may not become entangled. That he feels sorry for the living beings that he has to be responsible for giving them bodies through which they will no doubt get some enjoyment but they will also have to suffer so many miseries. He feels very sorry for them and he also sees their condition is going to be like they are born in when they are born they are ignorant and foolishly they become busy with material enjoyment and they remain in ignorance and after death again and again they keep taking birth but at the time of dissolution of the whole universe they again are helplessly absorbed in the material energy and they remain dormant like that for billions and billions of years and again they come out again they are busy with material enjoyment. So they can remain in this cycle unless they are rescued by a pure devotee or the Lord himself. So therefore the prayers offered by Brahma is after the initial phase of, different phases of the creation are done by the two Vishnus and then Brahma's work has to begin of generating the living beings given the material body that is the work of creation of Brahma and that happened before he has to do that he offers nice prayers to Lord Vishnu.

## How Can Lord Krishna or Rama Become Visible to Us? | HH Stoka Krishna Swami
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### transcendental form, perception, spiritual vision
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZSLPRGfVwN8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna Rama appears as the son of his devotees apparently taking birth like an ordinary human being, it's not at all ordinary, it is not at all material."
**Summary:** Lord Krishna and Rama appear in transcendental form, which is beyond our material perception, but they grant us the ability to see them.

Now, when Krishna Rama appears as the son of his devotees, apparently taking birth like an ordinary human being, it's not at all ordinary; it is not at all material. He doesn't accept a material form. He comes in his original transcendental form, meaning the form in the spiritual world, which is eternal and completely spiritual. But we cannot see him in that form. But then they become visible as Krishna, as Rama, as Narasimha, as Varaha, by giving us the power to see them because our eyes can only perceive material forms, and that too, not all material forms. Like if the devatas were to come, and do come to this earth planet sometimes, we cannot see them with these eyes because these eyes can't see them. These eyes can only perceive gross form. Devatas have subtle material form, sukshma sharira, just like we cannot see the form of a ghost because a ghost has got only a subtle form.

## How Krishna Relates Personally with Every Soul? | His Holiness Stoka Krishna Swami
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Krishna's relationship, unlimited nature, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AQH7z6tYGjw?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna relates with every Jeeva, with every living entity, with every living being."
**Summary:** Krishna has a specific and unlimited relationship with every living being, expanding into numerous forms to receive their devotional service.

Krishna relates with every Jeeva, with every living entity, with every living being. He has a very specific relationship with each individual Jeeva. I should be interested in what is my specific relationship with Krishna. And I should also be aware that Krishna is unlimited. If He relates with me, it's not that He is absent in some other relationship. That's the case in the material world. Like Krishna, He had 16,108 queens in Dwaraka when He incarnated here. So He was expanding into 16,108 forms, and nobody is deprived of His association. We have no experience of some person being unlimited in this material world, or even for a matter in the spiritual world, no Jeeva is unlimited. Everyone is limited. Krishna is the only unlimited person. And as the unlimited person, He has unlimited number of forms, and He expands in those forms and relates with devotees for the sake of receiving their service.

## How can I develop self control for spiritual progress | AAQ: HH Stoka Krishna Swami
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### sense control, devotional service, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/15-Za6vfXGY?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Like that all the senses, if we engage in Krishna's service, that is the way to control the senses."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the way to control the senses is to engage them in devotional service to Krishna, giving examples of how to engage each sense.

How to control senses to focus on spiritual journey in time of crisis? Engaging the senses in devotional service means to engage the senses in devotional service. Just like there is an example given in Bhagavad Gita of one Ambarish Maharaj. He was a pure devotee of Krishna. So he engaged all his senses in Krishna's service. As I said, beginning with the tongue, what do you do with the tongue to engage in Krishna's service? We chant Hare Krishna. Ears, we hear the chanting of Hare Krishna. With the tongue we can also taste Krishna Prasadam. With the eyes we can see beautiful form of Krishna, either the picture of Krishna or the deity of Krishna. The eyes we can read Bhagavad Gita. And then with the nose we can smell nice fragrant flowers offered to Krishna or incense offered to Krishna. With the sense of touch, you can engage in so many activities with the hands in Krishna's service. You can engage these hands and the sense of touch also. Like that all the senses, if we engage in Krishna's service, that is the way to control the senses.

**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that Krishna is beyond material energy and understanding His divine birth and activities leads to liberation from the cycle of birth and death.

So this has to be very, very clear to us that Krishna is not of this material energy. Even though He is coming on my tongue, He is not of the material energy. 5000 years back also when Krishna came here, He is not of this material energy. Just because my material eyes could see Him, He is not material. That is the reason in Bhagavad Gita Krishna says, simply by knowing what is the nature of Krishna's janma karma as divine, anybody who knows, means in truth, one understands this, he has no more birth in this material world. So, what we are discussing today, what we are discussing yesterday, exactly same thing. We are trying to understand the janma karma, appearance of the nama on my tongue, and what are the activities of the nama avatara. Once you understand this, there is no more birth and breath, old age disease. Krishna is promising that simply by understanding that Lord is not material, yet how He is in the material world. This is the understanding of the tattva of avatara or incarnation. So, it is very essential for us to understand this.

### transcendental nature of Krishna, nama bhajana, maya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/o1N77UxgyQ4?t=86
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is especially important to remember the transcendental nature of the Lord Krishna when we approach His service, of which chanting His holy name is foremost."
**Summary:** This segment quotes Bhaktivinoda Thakur from Chaitanya Siksha Amrita, emphasizing that Krishna's form, name, service, and servants are eternally free from maya, and that understanding this is crucial before engaging in nama bhajana.

So, it is very essential for us to understand this. You cannot shun away from all these things. Because spiritual world is beyond this material reality. Krishna is beyond this material reality. This material reality is only one of the energies of Krishna. So, we will see some quotes which again reinforces Krishna's transcendental nature. And then we will go into rest of it. See, in Chaitanya Siksha Amrita, Bhaktivinoda Thakur says, even before entering the field of nama bhajana, that is nama bhajana is what we are doing, japa, one should first know for certain that Krishna's form, Krishna's name, Krishna's service, Krishna's servants are all eternally free. That is they are beyond the grasp of maya, the illusory energy. Krishna, His transcendental abode and His paraphernalia are totally beyond material imagination. Similarly, His throne, His rooms, His gardens, forests, Gordhan Hill and river Yamuna. In fact, everything about Krishna cannot be touched by maya. It is especially important to remember the transcendental nature of the Lord Krishna when we approach His service, of which chanting His holy name is foremost. This understanding is not based on blind belief but on absolute eternal truth. So, Prabhupada. So this is, that was from Siksha Amrita.

### purification of senses, devotional service, Shuddha Sattva
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/o1N77UxgyQ4?t=256
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Out of costless mercy, the Lord appears in different ages in His original Shuddha Sattva transcendental body to elevate human society to the platform upon which they can truly benefit."
**Summary:** This segment explains that purifying the mind leads to purified senses, enabling devotional service and awakening dormant love for Krishna, and emphasizes that Krishna and Chaitanya Mahaprabhu appear in Shuddha Sattva bodies out of mercy to elevate humanity.

When mind is purified, the senses are also purified. Just see. Instead of using the senses for sense gratification, the awakened devotee employs the senses in the transcendental service of the Lord. This is a process by which dormant love of Krishna, Supreme Personality of Godhead, dormant love for Krishna. The Supreme Personality of Godhead appears in order to teach us Vedic principles intended to understanding Him. Vedeshya Sarvair Aham Ev Vedyah. We should always know that when Krishna and Lord Chaitanya appeared, they appeared in Shuddha Sattva bodies. Please note that. We should always know that when Krishna and Lord Chaitanya appeared, they appeared in Shuddha Sattva bodies. One should not mistake the body of Krishna or Chaitanya Mahaprabhu to be a material body like ours. For Krishna and Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu appeared as needed for the benefit of the entire human society. Out of costless mercy, the Lord appears in different ages in His original Shuddha Sattva transcendental body to elevate human society to the platform upon which they can truly benefit.

### spiritual energy, Krishna's power, deity worship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/o1N77UxgyQ4?t=338
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Never think of the deity as made of stone or wood. Every worshipper must remember that Krishna is personally present."
**Summary:** This segment presents a conversation with Srila Prabhupada, explaining that matter is Krishna's energy and can be transformed into spirit, emphasizing Krishna's power and the importance of recognizing His personal presence in the deity form.

Conversation Srila Prabhupada, ultimately everything is spiritual because matter is Krishna's energy. If Krishna is the original cause, therefore the matter can be changed into spirit and spirit can be changed to matter. Just as in the same electrical energy, acting is heater and cooler because the original cause is electricity. Similarly, the original cause of Krishna, so Krishna can change matter into spirit and spirit into matter. This is His power. The rascals and fools, when Krishna appears in His own body, Atma Maya, they think it is just like material body. But they do not know that to Krishna, there is no distinction of material and spiritual. Never think of the deity as made of stone or wood. Every worshipper must remember that Krishna is personally present. He is simply kindly presenting Himself before us in a way so that we can handle Him. That is His mercy. Otherwise, He is unapproachable. There is no difference between matter and spirit for the Lord, although there is a gulf of difference between the two in the case of condition living being. For the Lord, there is nothing but spiritual existence. Similarly, there is nothing except spiritual existence for the pure devotee of the Lord in His intimate relationship with the Lord.

## S.B. 3.15.28-29 | By His Holiness Stoka Krishna Swami | 04-07-2020 | Hare Krishna Movement Chennai
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Vaikuntha, Four Kumaras, Doorman
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8k5rtFdLJno?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Brahma is describing to the Devatas what is happening as the cause for the darkness spreading everywhere in the universe."
**Summary:** Brahma describes Vaikuntha and the four Kumaras' travels, setting the stage for the story of their encounter with the doormen.

Entered all the six doors of the palace and no one checked them. Therefore, when they attempted to enter the seventh door and were forbidden by the doorman who checked them with their sticks, they naturally became very angry and sorrowful. An orderly child would cry, but because these were not orderly children, they immediately made preparations to punish the doorman, for the doorman had committed a great offense. Even to this day, a saintly person is never checked from entering anyone's door in India. The sense the Bhakti Vilasa purport purports to purpose. Om ajnana timirandhasya jnana anjana shalakaya chakshur unmilitam yena tasmai shri gurave namaha. Namah Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Prishtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pachatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Galadharan Shiva Sadhi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. So Brahma is describing to the Devatas what is happening.

---
### Liberation, Yogic Siddhi, Spiritual Sky
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8k5rtFdLJno?t=113
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Exceptionally advanced yogis like the four Kumaras can even cross the coverings of the universe."
**Summary:** Brahma describes the four Kumaras as liberated souls and advanced yogis who can travel freely throughout the material and spiritual skies, even beyond the coverings of the universe.

As the cause for the darkness spreading everywhere in the universe. So Brahma is describing, first of all he described Vaikuntha. And he has already described that the four Kumaras while wandering carefree, because they were wandering carefree, because they were liberated souls. And they didn't have any definite desire. The description is there. My four sons, Sanakya, Sanatana, Sanantara and Sanat Kumara who are born from my mind are your predecessors. They are born much before all the demigods. As they travel throughout the material and spiritual skies without any definite desire. So then Brahma is describing as they travel all over the universes. They also travel in the spiritual sky. So they happen to enter the spiritual sky. And because they were freed from all material contamination, they could go there. And they also had the yogic siddhi. It requires yogic siddhi to go to specific places. Otherwise they are restricted to the planet. They are supposed to stay there. Now normally yogic siddhi means they can travel within the universe. But exceptionally advanced yogis like the four Kumaras can even cross the coverings of the universe.

---
### Liberation, Material Contamination, Desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8k5rtFdLJno?t=256
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To become free from material desire is possible only if one is free from all types of material contamination."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that only liberated souls, free from material contamination and desire, can cross the coverings of the universe, emphasizing that consciousness is spiritual but can be contaminated by material desires.

So elsewhere it is explained that to cross the coverings of the universe, one has to be fully free from all material contamination. Fully purified and should be liberated soul. Only liberated soul can cross the coverings of the universe. Even among the yogis, those yogis who are not liberated, they cannot go beyond the coverings of the universe. They cannot go. So from this description it is very clear that the four Kumaras were liberated souls. And they were liberated souls who were also yogis. Because they were yogis, they could travel freely. If one is not a yogi, one cannot travel. So they were liberated souls. Therefore they could cross the coverings of the universe and they could reach the Vaikuntha planet because they were yogis. Yogis and liberated souls. So then Brahma gives the elaborate description of Vaikuntha that we have been hearing in the past several verses. We have been hearing about the Vaikuntha. So, without any definite desire, Prabhupada explains, means no material desires. No material desires. So to become free from material desire is possible only if one is free from all types of material contamination. Even if there is a small tinge of material contamination, there is material desire. So many times people wonder what is this material contamination? It is nothing but material desire. Whenever it is referred to, material contamination in the heart or consciousness. Consciousness is completely spiritual. So how can consciousness be contaminated? It is the desire which becomes contaminated. Naturally, as spirit souls, it is the desire which becomes contaminated. Because we all have consciousness. That consciousness is the symptom of spirit. Symptom of spirit soul, consciousness. The characteristic of consciousness is desire. Consciousness means desire. So a living being cannot be without desire. Therefore, many times Prabhupada explains, if it is said, one has to become desireless, it means that one has to become free from material desires. That's the meaning, desireless. Nispruhaha, many places in the Bhagavad Gita, nispruhaha, nispruhasarvakamebhyo, free from all kinds of material desires.

---
### Desirelessness, Spiritual Desire, Spirit Soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8k5rtFdLJno?t=484
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is the characteristic of the living being to be always having desires."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that desirelessness means freedom from material desires, not the absence of all desires, as spirit souls are characterized by spiritual desires and activities.

So people who don't know about devotional service or bhakti yoga, they think desirelessness means no desire, become a stone, become like a stone, no material desire, no activity. That is not correct. So they try to become desireless. That is not correct. They try to become desireless, try to become inactive. But that is never possible for a living being. Never possible. So without any definite desire means without any material desire. So without any material desire means there is spiritual desire. There is spiritual desire. Not spiritual desire, one desire, spiritual desires. So the Kumaras are traveling with a spiritual desire. At least one spiritual desire, there could be more. It is the characteristic of the living being to be always having desires. Always having. That's a characteristic. That is the natural symptom of living. It is like this table. Can it have any desire? Material object, can it have desire? Material substance. Is there anything like that? No. Is there anything like desire? No. So that is characteristic of spirit, soul, desire, consciousness, activity, senses, perception, thinking, feeling, willing, all these are characteristics of spirit, living person.

---
### Absolute Truth, Impersonal Brahman, Spiritual Qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8k5rtFdLJno?t=687
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whole understanding is completely different if you properly understand what is the spirit."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the Vaishnava understanding of the Absolute Truth with the Mayavadi philosophy, emphasizing that the Absolute Truth possesses ultimate emotions, power, consciousness, senses, and spiritual qualities, unlike the impersonal Brahman.

See, whole understanding is completely different if you properly understand what is the spirit. He is the ultimate, the ultimate, the ultimate emotions. He is the ultimate power. He is the ultimate consciousness. So these Mayavadi philosophers, they study so much the Vedas and after all that the conclusion, because they are stuck up with one philosophy, absolute truth is impersonal, that's why they come to a wrong conclusion. Either living being or absolute truth, no desires at all, no senses, no activity and to justify that, they say see scriptures describe absolute truth as nirguna, nirvikara, nirvishesha. But unless they hear the meaning, the explanation of all these descriptions in the scriptures from a proper source, they will not understand. They take the literal meaning. Nirguna means without qualities. Nirguna means without material qualities. Nirguna means nirakar, without any material form, so there is transcendental form, there is transcendental qualities, there is transcendental senses, transcendental activities, transcendental abode, transcendental paraphernalia, everything is there, this material world, 15th chapter of Bhagavad Gita is.

### Spiritual World, Material World, Perverted Reflection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8k5rtFdLJno?t=817
**Verse References:** BG 15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In this material world, even though it's a reflection of the spiritual world, it is not a proper reflection. It's a perverted reflection."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the material world is a perverted reflection of the spiritual world, not an exact inverted reflection, and uses the analogy of a convex or concave mirror to illustrate the distorted nature of material reality.

Described by Krishna as an inverted reflection of the spiritual world, so in the spiritual world, unless there is variety, how can there be a difference? How can there be variety in the material world? But Krishna also explains in the 15th chapter, it's a perverted reflection. Literally, if you take, urdha-moolam-adashakam, inverted reflection, just like this. Just like if you want to see inverted reflection in the mirror, if you are standing on your feet, in the mirror it will appear like you're standing on your head, shirsasana, what is that? Shirsasana. So it's not exactly inverted reflection. See this also requires an acharya to explain, what is this urdha-moolam-adashakam? It's a perverted reflection. There's a difference between perverted reflection and inverted reflection. Perverted reflection is, it's like, to give an example of perverted reflection, if you look in a convex mirror or a concave mirror, your face, let us say, your face will appear either very big or very small. Correct? Have you seen that? Sometimes you would have seen, some of you would have seen at least. So like that, in this material world, even though it's a reflection of the spiritual world, it is not a proper reflection. It's a perverted reflection.

---
### Material Quality, Spiritual Quality, Proper Guidance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8k5rtFdLJno?t=958
**Verse References:** BG 2
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The nature of spirit, to properly understand, you have to study the scriptures under proper guidance."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts material and spiritual qualities, emphasizing the need for studying scriptures under proper guidance to avoid misinformation and understand the true nature of spirit.

So everything is topsy-turvy. Right? There everything is of the spiritual quality, in the spiritual world. Here everything is of the material quality. So material quality and spiritual quality, very clearly Krishna has distinguished in the second chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, by distinguishing the soul from the body or spirit from matter. Very clearly Krishna has given the distinction. In other places in the scriptures also, many, many, many, many places it is there. The descriptions are there. Matter and spirit, very clearly it is there. So perverted reflection means of the material quality as opposed to spiritual quality in the spiritual world. So Prabhupada explains, since there is variety here. And there is a variety here. And because the material world is a reflection of the spiritual world, there is variety in the spiritual world. But because this reflection is a perverted reflection, we should not think just like there is material variety here, there is material variety in the spiritual world. No. There is spiritual variety. So the nature of spirit, to properly understand, you have to study the scriptures under proper guidance. Otherwise all kinds of interpretation or misinformation, just like these mayavadis, there are so many wrong understandings.

### Kumaras, Impersonal Brahman, Paramatma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8k5rtFdLJno?t=1081
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If a yogi is not having proper knowledge about who or what is that form in the heart of the Supersoul, Paramatma, then their conclusion will be, what is that form in the heart?"
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the Kumaras' desire to see the Lord and contrasts it with the Mayavadi understanding of the Paramatma, emphasizing that without proper knowledge, yogis may misinterpret the form of the Supersoul as a manifestation of the impersonal Brahman.

So it is described that the Kumaras, they had desire, they were eager to see the Lord. They were eager to see the Lord in Vaikuntha. They had already seen the Lord in the heart. They had already seen the Lord in the heart. In perfection. In perfection. In perfect Samadhi, a yogi is able to see the form of the Lord in the heart, Paramatma form, Supersoul. But if a yogi is not having proper knowledge about who or what is that form in the heart of the Supersoul, Paramatma, then their conclusion will be, what is that form in the heart? Their conclusion will be, oh, this is the manifestation of the impersonal Brahman, the absolute truth or God. This Paramatma feature is a manifestation of the impersonal Brahman. Similarly, they also conclude. They also conclude that we are not the body. We are spirit soul. We are eternal. We are spiritual by nature. So they conclude that this spiritual nature or spirit soul is also manifestation of impersonal Brahman. Now again, some places in the scriptures, there is description. Absolute truth is Brahman. We are also Brahman. And the qualities of Brahman are given, identical. Nirvikara, nirguna, nirvishesha, nirvikalpa. Whether you apply that to the soul or the Supersoul or the impersonal Brahman, the absolute truth, same qualities. So they equalize the soul with the impersonal Brahman.

**Summary:** The speaker explains that devotees avoid the mistake of equating the soul with the impersonal Brahman by understanding the perverted reflection of the spiritual world in the material world and emphasizes the importance of hearing from a perfect source in the parampara system.

With the absolute truth, with God, with Brahman in the spiritual world. So this mistake, devotees never make. Because devotees understand that in this material world, there is an inverted reflection, perverted reflection. The perverted reflection of the spiritual world. So the variety here is material, their variety is spiritual. Here there are living beings, so many millions and billions and trillions of living beings, who have material desires, material form, covering their spirit, covering the spirit, material form, material possessions. In the spiritual world, there also there are millions of living beings. But they are having spiritual form, they are having spiritual desires, they have spiritual activity, they have spiritual senses, their intelligence is spiritual. Their identity is spiritual. Do you understand? You see, how perfect, clear understanding can be there, only when you hear from the perfect source. That's why the perfect source is described in the Bhagavad Gita and all the important scriptures. That you have to hear in the parampara system. From a guru who is coming in parampara, beginning from Krishna.

The seventh door, Jaya and Vijaya. Those who do Narasimha Homa, you have some mantras, no? Chanda Prachanda Abhyam Namaha. What are the names there? Huh? Bala Prabala Abhyam Namaha. And then follow these Brahmachari principles even after attaining adulthood. Continue to follow the Brahmacharya principles. So that one will not become a victim of this sex desire. Now, these Kumaras are very intelligent transcendentalists. They thought that this sex impulse is going to bother us. And Brahmacharya principles have to be followed whole life. So, using the yogic siddhi, they maintained the body of five-year-olds.

## S.B. 3.15.30 | By His Holiness Stoka Krishna Swami | 05-07-2020 | Hare Krishna Movement Chennai
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Kumaras, Vaikuntha, doorkeepers
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Purport and the purport."
**Summary:** This segment presents the translation and purport by Srila Prabhupada of S.B. 3.15.30, describing the Kumaras' arrival at Vaikuntha and their encounter with the doorkeepers.

Translation: The four boy sages who had nothing to cover their bodies but the atmosphere looked only five years old, even though they were the oldest of all living creatures and had realized the truth of the self. But when the porters, who happened to possess a disposition quite unpalatable to the Lord, saw the sages, they blocked their way with their staffs, despising their glories, although the sages did not deserve such treatment at their hands. Purport by Srila Prabhupada: The four sages were the firstborn sons of Brahma; therefore, all other living entities, including Lord Shiva, are born later and are therefore younger than the four Kumaras. Although they looked like five-year-old boys and traveled naked, the Kumaras were older than all other living creatures and had realized the truth of the self such that they could not remain inedible and thus became known as the king of all the world. However, the sages were not to be forbidden to enter the kingdom of Vaikuntha, but by chance, the doorman objected to their entrance. This is not fitting. The Lord is always anxious to serve sages like the Kumaras, but in spite of knowing this fact, the doorman astonishingly and outrageously prohibited them from entering. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Purport and the purport.

---

So this is a word spoken by Brahma. Brahma is speaking to Devatas. So the description continues what happened when the sages reached the Vaikuntha planet. They were trying to enter the personal abode of the Supreme Lord, that particular palace. In the Vaikuntha, everyone lives in palaces. Just like when Brahma came to visit Dwaraka, he saw that all the Vishnus, they are living in wonderful palaces, wonderful palaces. Not Brahma, Sudhama. Brahma also came to Dwaraka, but it is described Sudhama saw so many palaces. So since he had to meet Krishna, so he was shown Krishna's palace after he passed through so many security guards, guarding persons. You know that. First, he had to pass through some gates to enter the Dwarka township itself. And after that, he had to pass through so many palaces of the Vrishnis, Yadus. And then he had to be shown the particular palace in which Krishna was residing. In fact, Krishna doesn't reside in one palace. He resides in 16,108 palaces because he has those many queens. So like that in the Vaikuntha also, all the devotees live in palatial buildings. But the Kumaras were particularly eager to see the Supreme Lord. They were eager to see the Supreme Lord. Okay.

### Kumaras, doorkeepers, agitation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=321
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So when they tried to enter, six gates nobody blocked."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the Kumaras' entry through six gates without obstruction, but their encounter with agitated doorkeepers at the seventh gate, who raised their eyebrows and breathed heavily, contrasting this with the cool glance of the Lord.

So when they tried to enter, six gates nobody blocked. Six gates nobody blocked. The seventh gate, it is already described, with open minds they entered the seventh door out of their own will, just as they had passed through the six other doors, which were open ones which are made of gold and diamonds. But the two doormen of the seventh door, they first of all raised their eyebrows. Who are you? And they breathe little heavily discontented nostrils, reddish eyes. They appear deep. His eyes are red, appeared somewhat agitated. These reddish eyes is common for the Lord and the inhabitants of Vaikuntha. But when the Lord glances with his reddish eyes in his transcendental form, it is as cool as a lotus. That glance is as cool as a lotus. This is described in the first canto, the seventh chapter. The son of Drona punished. This is Ashwatthama at the end of the battle of Kurukshetra. He went and beheaded the sleeping sons of Draupadi. So Draupadi was very much heartbroken, naturally. So then Arjuna, he decided to punish this Ashwatthama who had done this unlawful killing. So Arjuna chased. Now Arjuna was accompanied by Krishna because even the end of the battle, still Krishna was unharmed. He chased the chariot driver of Arjuna.

### Krishna, Ashwatthama, glance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=474
**Verse References:** SB 1.7
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna was also looking but with cool eyes, Krishna's eyes are reddish, but he was looking with cool eyes, as cool as a lotus in a beautiful reservoir of water, like that, Krishna's glance is very cool."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Arjuna chasing Ashwatthama, contrasting Arjuna's red-hot eyes with Krishna's cool, lotus-like glance, and then returns to the description of the doormen's agitated demeanor towards the Kumaras, emphasizing their unfavorable disposition to the Lord.

So Krishna was there with Arjuna when Arjuna chased this Ashwatthama. And Arjuna was very angry against this Ashwatthama. So when Arjuna managed to catch this Ashwatthama, then he brought him to the chariot driver of Draupadi, to the chariot, to take him into Draupadi, to his camp to punish him. So Arjuna was looking, it is described with red hot eyes. He was very angry at Ashwatthama because Ashwatthama was a murderer of innocent boys. Krishna was also looking but with cool eyes, Krishna's eyes are reddish, but he was looking with cool eyes, as cool as a lotus in a beautiful reservoir of water, like that, Krishna's glance is very cool. So, these two doormen at the seventh door, even though reddish eyes, Kumaras could make out they are agitated, they are agitated, they are not casting a cool glance, they are agitated. And not only they were looking in an agitated way at the Kumaras, but they blocked their way using their staff, as if despising their glories, who are you, simply creating nuisance here, go away. Don't enter. In that mood, they stopped, despising their glories. Tejo vihasya bhagavat pratikul shilau, and they are blocking with their staff and despising their glories. Bhagavata was quite unpalatable to the Lord. Bhagavata pratikul shilau, that which is not pleasing to the Lord. Why is this particularly mentioned? Because in Vaikuntha, everyone is serving the Supreme Lord in a favorable way. All are devotees. All are serving Anukula.

### Bhakti, Anukula, Pratikula
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=671
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Here, it is the opposite. Bhagavata pratikula shilau."
**Summary:** The speaker references Rupa Goswami's definition of pure devotional service (Bhakti) as 'anukuliena krishnanu shilanam' and contrasts it with the doorkeepers' 'pratikula shila,' a disposition displeasing to the Lord, highlighting the anomaly of such behavior in Vaikuntha.

Rupa Goswami describes Bhakti in Bhakti Rishams Sindhu. What does he say? The definition of pure devotional service, according to Rupa Goswami: Anyabhilashita shunyam, Jnana karmadi anavrta, Anukuliena krishnanu shilanam. Here, it is the opposite. Bhagavata pratikula shilau. Anukula shila, pratikula shila. Pratikula shila is having a nature displeasing to the Lord. Anukula shila, either Bhagavata Anukula shila or Krishna Anukula shila. That is pleasing to the Lord. A disposition, a behavior, a proper way of interacting in a favorable way to the Lord. That is Bhagavata Anukula. Here it is Bhagavata pratikula shila. A disposition quite unpalatable to the Lord.

### Kumaras, Vriddhan, Atad Arhanam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=770
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy

**Summary:** The speaker explains that the doorkeepers were stopping 'Vriddhan' (the oldest of all living creatures), referring to the Kumaras, who, despite appearing as five-year-old boys, are self-realized souls and do not deserve such treatment.

And who are they stopping? They were stopping Vriddhan. Vriddhan means, Vriddhan means, Vriddhan means, the oldest of all living creatures. Most elderly personalities, the four kumaras. Because Prabhupada says in the purport, the four sages, the four kumaras, they were the first born sons of Brahma. So all others, sons of Brahma, are later, after the four kumaras. So they are the oldest, means eldest, elderly, in the sense of elderly. Vriddhan, but Dasha, Ardha, Vayasaha, they appear like five-year-old boys, half of ten is five. Vayasan, Vayasaha, Dasha, Ardha, Vayasaha, five-year-old, they appear like five-year-olds. And they are, even though appearing like five-year-olds, they are Vidita Atma Tattva, they are realized the truth of the Self. They have realized the truth of the Self, the self-realized souls. So such self-realized, eldest of all sons of Brahma, and they are sages, they are Naishitika Brahmacharis. So such great personalities never deserve such treatment at the hands of the doorkeepers.

### Kumaras, Vaikuntha, renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=903
**Verse References:** —

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that the doorkeepers should have welcomed the Kumaras with respect, as they are self-realized souls eager to see the Lord, and explains that the Kumaras' nakedness signifies their complete renunciation.

Atad Arhanam Stau, Atad Arhanam, that which is not proper, undeserving. So, the two doorkeepers, they should not have stopped the Kumaras. In fact, they should have welcomed them or received them with great respect and honor because they are self-realised souls and it is also said they were eager to see the Supreme Lord. They were eager to see the Supreme Lord. So Srila Prabhupada explains the purport, the justification for the Kumaras entering that Vaikuntha abode of the Supreme Lord. The four sages were the first born sons of Brahma, therefore all other living entities including Lord Shiva are born later and therefore younger than the four Kumaras, younger means in terms of birth in this material world. Kumaras are the oldest, all others are younger. But the Kumaras, they look like five-year-old boys, they maintain that way, as I explained yesterday, they maintain that type of form and they travel naked. The Sanskrit that is used to describe them, Vata Rasha Naan, covered by the atmosphere or air, that means they are not wearing anything, they are naked, small boys, five-year-old boys, nobody will mind, nobody will say why are you not dressed, five-year-old boys. So they are traveling naked because they are completely renounced, completely renounced.

Generally these four Kumaras are not visible to everybody because they are residents of Brahma Loka. So residents of Brahma Loka first of all don't have a gross body, they only have a subtle body. And not only that they have a subtle body, in the upper planets their body has growth, their body has got a glow, an effulgence. And that effulgence is so dazzling that even if somebody had the ability to see a subtle form, just like all the residents of Swarga, they have subtle form, they don't have gross form, Swarga and above, all subtle form, but even then, relatively speaking they have relatively different degree of bodily effulgence. There are different degrees of bodily effulgence, so more advanced spiritually, more the effulgence, more the effulgence, more the effulgence, more the effulgence. So there's a description that when Vaman Deva walked into the sacrificial arena of Bali Maharaj, that time Vaman Deva was Supreme Lord, so Supreme Lord's effulgence is the supreme effulgence and that is ultimately Brahma Josa Jyoti, how bright it illuminates the entire spiritual sky and the entire existence. So nobody's effulgence can be brighter than the Supreme Lord's effulgence. But the Supreme Lord when he incarnates, he does not display his effulgence all the time. Most of the time he doesn't display.

### Krishna, Gokula, butter
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=1220
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna when in Gokula, he would go round as a small boy trying to steal butter."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Krishna in Gokula stealing butter, despite Mother Yashoda's efforts to provide him with specially flavored butter, illustrating how Krishna often conceals his full effulgence during his incarnations.

Krishna when in Gokula, he would go round as a small boy trying to steal butter. So the gopis would try to prevent Krishna from stealing butter by hiding the butter pots. Mother Yashoda would receive complaints, Krishna is stealing butter. So Mother Yashoda first would say, why will my son steal butter? There is plenty of butter in our house. And not only that, plenty of butter, Yashoda thinking that these complaints are becoming too much, she specially was feeding flavored grass to some limited number of cows. Nandamaharaja had 9 lakh cows. So out of that she had selected some cows and those cows she was feeding flavored grass. And that flavored grass means the milk will be having special flavor. That special flavored grass personally she would feed, personally she would milk the cow. And that milk she would boil nicely, make it yogurt. And that yogurt should personally churn and make butter to feed Krishna specially flavored butter. So that Krishna will not go to neighboring houses to steal butter. Because Yashoda has to wonder, why will he go to somebody else's house and eat butter? Maybe their butter is more tasty. So she said, I will feed Krishna. Why? Because Krishna has special flavored butter. So Yashoda used to prepare this kind of butter. So after she started feeding this kind of butter, Krishna very nicely used to eat. Then Yashoda is feeding lovingly this flavored butter. Then complaints are still coming in spite of this. So she will tell the gopis, why don't you hide the pots, keep it in a dark place so that he cannot see. Small boy, you are complaining he is stealing butter.

**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Krishna stealing butter, describing how the gopis hid the butter in dark places, but Krishna still found it, leading them to believe that his jewelry was illuminating the area, though they ultimately realized it was a natural glow from his body due to his Brahman effulgence.

So then they used to hide it in a dark place. Still Krishna was stealing. So next time they came with complaint, they said, actually we are hiding. Yashoda said, I told you. I told you. I told you. I told you. I told you to hide. No. How can he come and take if you are hiding? No, there is, I think he is able to see even if it is in a dark place because you are decorating him with nice jewelry. And those jewelry you have got from precious stones. And the stones naturally give out light. So then Yashoda said, okay, I will remove him, his special ornament. They said, no, no, no, no, no. Don't remove his ornaments. Don't remove his ornaments. He looks so beautiful with those wonderful ornaments. Don't remove his ornaments. But then they began to think that why were they telling don't remove the ornaments? Because he looks very beautiful with the ornaments. Secondly, they are telling these two boys, Krishna and Balarama, even if you remove ornaments, he will not come. He will not come. Even if you remove ornaments, no use. Because their body, some kind of glow is there in their body. Some kind of glow is there. They couldn't understand that Krishna and Balarama have a natural glow because of their Brahman effulgence. They are Brahman, Supreme Brahman. So naturally they have got the effulgence. So naturally. So naturally. So sometimes Krishna and Balarama will display little effulgence. Little effulgence. That time some of the gopis will see, oh, there is some effulgence coming out of their bodies. So all these are loving pastimes of Krishna and Balarama. Loving pastimes.

### Vamanadeva, Bali Maharaj, austerity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=1511
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So everybody thought, who is this Brahmachari with so much Tejas he is walking in?"
**Summary:** The speaker relates the story of Vamanadeva appearing in Bali Maharaj's sacrificial arena, initially concealing his effulgence but later displaying a dazzling light that surpassed the assembled sages, who recognized his austerity and stood up to receive him.

So Varman Deva similarly. Varman Deva. When he appeared as the son of Aditi and Kashyapa, he appeared like their son, but he was not displaying his effulgence. He was not displaying his effulgence. But when he walked into the sacrificial arena of Bali Maharaj, then all the assembled sages, because that sacrificial arena, there were very, very powerful sages who were performing the sacrifice, especially Bali Maharaj had invited very powerful sages to perform the sacrifices after defeating Indra. So these sages were displaying their effulgence, natural effulgence, very, very bright. So when Vamande walked into that assembly, he appeared to be dazzling, dazzling, having dazzling effulgence, brighter than the effulgence, the effulgence of the most powerful sages assembled there. So everybody thought, who is this Brahmachari with so much Tejas he is walking in? Because the Tejas also depends on how much Tapasya one has performed. More the Tapasya, more the effulgence from their body. So if these two, if this Brahmachari has performed severe austerities, more than what those sages have performed, then their bodily effulgence, this Brahmachari's bodily effulgence will be more. Tejas will be more. So they thought, here is some very, very, very, very powerfully austere Brahmachari. So they all stood up to receive him out of respect for his austerity, Tejas.

### austerity, self-realization, Paramahamsas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=1650
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So what will they do after realizing the absolute truth? No more need to perform any austerity, but they will be absorbed in meditation on the supreme Brahman truth."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that sages, after realizing the absolute truth, become absorbed in meditation on Brahman, exemplified by Shukadeva Goswami's renunciation, and contrasts this with the Varna Ashrama Dharma, where individuals dress according to their position, highlighting the Paramahamsas as the topmost among Sanyasis.

So, these sages, why I mention this is, these sages, they had performed so much austerity. They are completely dedicated for realizing the absolute truth. So what will they do after realizing the absolute truth? No more need to perform any austerity, but they will be absorbed in meditation on the supreme Brahman truth, just like Shukadeva Goswami. He was Brahman realized, self-realized. So after his birth, he simply walked away, simply walked away from home, not interested in all the rituals and study, studying Vedic literature, going to Gurukul. He has no use for all that. He is liberated. So why will he go and study? Why will he undergo all the samskara? No need. So he walked away from home. What was he doing? Wandering? Aimlessly, apparently aimlessly wandering. Again, he was also nakedly walking around everywhere. He was not dressed. Why not dressed? Not interested, completely renounced. Otherwise, according to the Ashrama and Varna, they have to dress themselves. That is the very culture. Varna Ashrama Dharma, everybody is dressed according to their position, according to their Varna, Ashrama. So Brahmachari is dressed a particular way. Grahastha is dressed a particular way. Vanaprastha is dressed a particular way. Sanyasi is dressed a particular way. Sanyasi is dressed generally in saffron. Others don't dress in saffron. So the saffron-dressed Sanyasis, they are the topmost among the four Ashramas. And among them, the Paramahamsas, among the Sanyasis also, are the greatest of the Sanyasis even. So such Paramahamsas, they are offered respect by everyone, including the other Sanyasis who are not Paramahamsas.

And their bodily effulgence is very, very, very, very bright because they are the most austere, completely absorbed in meditation on Brahman. No other business. They are not interested in dressing themselves, in any formalities, in any activities, material activities, even activities of self-realization. They are already self-realized. So why will they do any activity for attaining self-realization? They don't want to do anything. So they are simply aimlessly going around everywhere, absorbed in thought of Brahman. So when these sages try to enter, what is said here by Prabhupada? Such saints were not to be forbidden to enter the kingdom of Vaikuntha. But by chance the doorman objected to their entrance. This is not fitting. This is not proper.

### doorkeepers, Kumaras, Lord's service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=1904
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord is always anxious to serve sages like the Kumaras."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that the doorkeepers, being enlightened devotees in Vaikuntha, should not have mistaken the Kumaras for ordinary boys, and highlights the Lord's eagerness to serve sages like the Kumaras, as He is the well-wisher of all beings.

By chance the doorman objected. See the doorman, they are not ignorant people. They are themselves enlightened. That's how they are devotees in Vaikuntha. They are enlightened. So they can make out, even though these Kumaras look like five-year-old boys, naked, wandering naked, there is no possibility the doorman would have thought, why are these small boys simply coming here? No. They couldn't have thought like that because the Kumaras were self-realized. They were having that effulgence of great personalities. They were the oldest personalities. So there is no possibility that the doorman would have, out of ignorance, mistaken them to be small boys. No. That is not possible. Therefore it is said that by chance the doorman objected to their entrance. It should not have happened. By chance it happened. The Lord is always anxious to serve sages like the Kumaras. Because the Supreme Lord is Surudam Sarvabhutanam. He is the topmost well-wisher of every living being. Now the Kumaras, they are the greatest among all the created beings. So how much the Lord will be inclined towards them? Can you just imagine? The Lord is the best well-wisher even of the most fallen condition soul. So how much more will he be a well-wisher of the Kumaras? So Prabhupada says, the Lord is always anxious to serve sages like the Kumaras.

### Vaikuntha, devotees, astonishment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=2046
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "And in spite of knowing this fact, the doorman astonishingly and outrageously prohibited them from entering."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the reciprocal service between the Lord and his devotees in Vaikuntha, where the Lord seeks opportunities to serve his devotees, and emphasizes the astonishment and outrage caused by the doormen's prohibition of the Kumaras, despite knowing the Lord's eagerness to serve them.

I explained in earlier class that the devotees in Vaikuntha always want to serve the Lord to please him. And the Lord is looking for opportunity to serve his devotees and please them. This is what is the reciprocation between the Lord and the devotees in the spiritual world. So rightly Prabhupada is telling here, the Lord is always anxious to serve sages like the Kumaras. And in spite of knowing this fact, the doorman astonishingly and outrageously prohibited them from entering. Astonishing. It is very astonishing. Why? Everybody will think, why are they stopping? It is said here, in the next two words it is said, as other divinities were looking on, they stopped. Everybody else is looking. Why are they stopping? The six doormen, six pairs of doormen, first six doors, never stopped. They knew, these Kumaras are most eligible to enter. They are very eager to see the Lord. So the sixth doorman never stopped. So why this seventh door, these doormen are stopping? Others are looking. Why are they stopping? So astonishingly and outrageously, others would not have approved. Outrageously means shockingly bad behaviour. It is shocking. It is bad behaviour. Somebody deserving, why are they being stopped? Why are these doormen stopping? It is very wrong. It is not befitting. It is not proper behaviour. So astonishingly and outrageously prohibited them.

### Vaikuntha, Pratikulashilav, unprecedented
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=2183
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the extraordinary and unprecedented nature of the doormen's actions, even within the extraordinary realm of Vaikuntha, highlighting that their behavior is not pleasing to the Lord and is considered outrageous by others.

Prohibited them from entering. So this incident is an extraordinary incident, even in Vaikuntha. In Vaikuntha everything is extraordinary. Nothing is ordinary. But still, this sort of extraordinary incident is astonishing. It is not in line with something done to please the Lord. By stopping them, the doormen are not pleasing the Supreme Lord Narayana. No. It is not pleasing the Lord. That is what is described here. Bhagavat Pratikulashilav. They are not expected to behave like this. They are pure devotees. Why are they stopping? Why are they doing something which is not pleasing to the Lord? So others are all astonished. And others are considering this as outrageous behaviour. Not proper behaviour. Not proper behaviour. Because they know. These doormen, they know what is pleasing to the Lord. They know. And they are standing there to serve the Lord in a way to please the Lord. They are standing there for serving the Lord only. And somebody has come very eager to see the Lord, who deserves to be actually welcomed, such persons, who should be welcomed, who are eager to see the Lord, who have no other selfish motive. Why are they being stopped? Astonishingly and outrageously they are being prohibited. So this is something very, very, very, very unprecedented. So it will be described further. How. The. Events. Unfold. Which leads to. The pastimes of the Supreme Lord. Extraordinary pastimes of the Supreme Lord. It will lead to.

### Yoga Maya, Krishna, expansion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EKiUi9ClDW0?t=2406
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is the power of yoga maya. Yoga maya arranges in such a way that each one will think Krishna is in this palace."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how Yoga Maya arranges for Krishna to appear in multiple palaces simultaneously in Dwaraka, and contrasts Krishna's independent expansion with the limited and uniform expansion of a Yogi.

This is the power of yoga maya. Yoga maya arranges in such a way that each one will think Krishna is in this palace. They will not know. Prabhupada describes elsewhere that when Krishna used to come out of his palace, in front of every palace there will be one person waiting to take Krishna to the assembly hall every day morning after he finishes morning duties. So, 16108 palaces Krishna will come out and become one. 16108 form will become one. One Krishna will go to the assembly hall. But when he comes back, he will divide himself into 16108 different forms. Not divide, he multiplies actually, he expands. And then each palace he will go. But this is not seen ordinarily. Only when Narada wanted to see how the Lord is living in Dwaraka, Narada knew that Krishna will be actually performing some extraordinary Leela there. That's time. Leela. So Narada Muni went to see Krishna in Dwaraka. That time Krishna gave darshan to Narada. Every palace he saw Krishna is present simultaneously. And in each palace he is doing totally different activity. Why is it mentioned that he is doing something extraordinary? Why is it mentioned that he is doing something extraordinary? Why is it mentioned that he is doing something extraordinary? Why is he doing different activity when Narada Muni saw? Because a Yogi can expand himself into at most nine or ten forms. But each form, expanded form by the Yogic power will do the same activity. Just like in one form the Yogi is lifting his hand, all nine other forms, expanded forms, the same action. It will not be different. No possibility of different actions in different forms simultaneously not possible.

## S.B. 3.15.31 | By His Holiness Stoka Krishna Swami | 06-07-2020 | Hare Krishna Movement Chennai
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### anger, devotion, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "According to the Vedic system, a sanyasi, a person in the renounced order of life, is dressed in sanyasi, is dressed in sanyasi, and is worn in saffron coloured garments."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse about the Kumaras becoming angry and sets the stage for discussing anger in the context of devotion and liberation.

When the Kumaras, although by far the fittest persons, were thus forbidden entrance by the two chief doorkeepers of Sri Hari, while other divinities looked on, their eyes suddenly turned red because of anger, due to their great eagerness to see their most beloved master Sri Hari, the personality of Godhead. Please repeat this translation. When the Kumaras, although by far the fittest persons, were thus forbidden entrance by the two chief doorkeepers of Sri Hari, while other divinities looked on, their eyes suddenly turned red because of anger, because of anger, due to their great eagerness to see their most beloved master Sri Hari, the personality of Godhead, performed by Srila Prabhupada.

---
### sanyasa, preaching, Vedic culture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=79
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The sanyasi's duty is to enlighten people in Krishna consciousness."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the role of a sanyasi in Vedic culture, emphasizing their duty to preach Krishna consciousness and their freedom of movement.

According to the Vedic system, a sanyasi, a person in the renounced order of life, is dressed in sanyasi, is dressed in sanyasi, and is worn in saffron coloured garments. This saffron dress is practically a passport for the mendicant and sanyasi to go anywhere. The sanyasi's duty is to enlighten people in Krishna consciousness. Those in the renounced order of life have no other business but preaching the glories and supremacy of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Therefore, the Vedic sociological conception is that a sanyasi should not be restricted. He is allowed to go anywhere and everywhere he wants, and he has not refused any gift he might demand from a householder.

---
### Krishna, friendship, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=136
**Verse References:** BG 5.29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As Lord Krishna states in the Bhagavad Gita, he is the best friend of all living entities, suhrdam sarva bhutanam."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes Krishna's role as the best friend of all living entities and his efforts to reclaim fallen souls.

The four Kumaras came to see the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Narayana. The word suhrtama, best of all friends, is important. As Lord Krishna states in the Bhagavad Gita, he is the best friend of all living entities, suhrdam sarva bhutanam. No one can be a greater well-wishing friend to any living entity than the Supreme Personality of Godhead. He is so kindly disposed towards everyone, that in spite of our completely forgetting our relationship with the Supreme Lord, he comes himself, sometimes personally as Lord Krishna appeared on this earth, and sometimes as his devotee, as did Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, and sometimes he sends his bona fide devotees to reclaim all the fallen souls. Therefore, he is the greatest well-wisher.

---
### anger, desire, Kumaras
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=194
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If once desire is not fulfilled, the younger brother anger follows."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the doorkeepers should not have restricted the Kumaras and introduces the concept of anger as the younger brother of desire.

He is the best well-wishing friend of everyone, and the Kumaras wanted to see him. The doorkeepers should have known that the four sages had no other business, and therefore to restrict them from entering the palace was not apt. In this verse, it is figuratively stated that the younger brother of desire suddenly appeared in person when the sages were forbidden to see their most beloved personality of Godhead. The younger brother of desire is anger. If once desire is not fulfilled, the younger brother anger follows. Here we can mark that even great saintly persons like the Kumaras were also angry.

---
### anger, liberation, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=238
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The difference between the anger of an ordinary person and that of a liberated person is that an ordinary person becomes angry because his sense desires are not being fulfilled, whereas a liberated person like the Kumaras becomes angry when restricted in the discharge of duties for serving the Supreme Personality of Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker distinguishes between the anger of ordinary and liberated persons, explaining that the Kumaras' anger stemmed from being restricted in their service to the Lord.

But they were not angry for their personal interests. They were angry because they were forbidden to enter the palace to see the personality of Godhead. Therefore, the theory that in the perfectional stage one should not have anger is not supported in this verse. Anger will continue even in the liberated stage. These four mendicant brothers, the Kumaras, were considered liberated persons, but still they were angry because they were restricted in their service to the Lord. The difference between the anger of an ordinary person and that of a liberated person is that an ordinary person becomes angry because his sense desires are not being fulfilled, whereas a liberated person like the Kumaras becomes angry when restricted in the discharge of duties for serving the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

---
### liberation, self-realization, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=305
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Becoming liberated does not necessitate losing one's sensual activities."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that liberation does not mean losing sensual activities, but rather engaging them in connection with Krishna consciousness.

In the previous verse, it has been clearly mentioned that the Kumaras were liberated persons. Vidhita Atma Tattva means one who understands the truth of self-realization. One who does not understand the truth of self-realization is called ignorant. But one who understands the self, the super-self, their interrelation and activities in self-realization is called Vidhita Atma Tattva. Although the Kumaras were already liberated persons, they nevertheless became angry. This point is very important. Becoming liberated does not necessitate losing one's sensual activities. Sense activities continue even in the liberated stage. The difference is, however, that sense activities in liberation are accepted only in connection with Krishna Consciousness, whereas sense activities in the conditioned stage are enacted for personal sense gratification. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta purport.

---
### Kumaras, anger, spiritual world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=446
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So these kumaras are very eager to see their most beloved master most beloved master shri hari the supreme personality of god but they were stopped by the door keepers"
**Summary:** The speaker reiterates the unusual nature of the Kumaras' anger in the spiritual world, emphasizing their eagerness to see Krishna and the doorkeepers' obstruction.

Very interesting verse which describes kumaras who are liberated souls in vaikuntha they are becoming angry. Most unusual. Very very rare to find such a instance in the spiritual world everyone is fully satisfied always engaged in trying to please the supreme lord and the lord reciprocally always wants to please his devotees so it's a very cordial atmosphere very nice atmosphere prevails in the spiritual world so in such a place the kumaras have reached and astonishing and outrageously they were prohibited from entering the abode of the lord by two door keepers who stopped them even though they were the fittest persons the fittest persons huh and the lord is described as the best well-wisher he is the topmost well-wisher of everyone and more so of his devotees so these kumaras are very eager to see their most beloved master most beloved master shri hari the supreme personality of god but they were stopped by the door keepers.

And therefore they became angry suddenly they became angry and it is described their anger was very much visible that their eyes were open and they could see the anger and their eyes were suddenly turned red. The Vaikuntha Nivasis, their eyes are naturally red. But generally in the material world we don't see somebody with red eyes except when they become angry or they have some problem with the eye. So Prabhupada elaborately explains in the purport this point of anger and very poetic description. kamanujena. Sudden appearance of the younger brother of desire in the personality of the Kumaras. So this is a description of anger as something which Anuja means also to follow. Lakshmana is Rama Anuja. So both the meanings are valid. Anuja means younger brother. Anuja. Ja means one who takes birth. Anu means afterwards, following. So younger brother takes birth after the older one is born. Correct? So Anuja means younger brother. And Anuja also means one who follows. One who follows. So here kamanuja, kama-anuja, the younger brother of desire means if desire is unfulfilled, it is generally followed by anger. Anger is younger brother of desire. This is a poetic description. It actually means anger follows desire which is unfulfilled.

Anger in the conditioned state, which is a negative quality, which is detrimental for our spiritual progress, which can result in criminal activities being performed, laws being broken, uncontrolled anger. Sometimes they say murders happen simply because the person who committed that murder was unable to control his anger. So, and among devotees, what happens if anger is uncontrolled? Especially by neophyte devotees. Uncontrolled anger may lead to Vaishnava Prath. Being committed by the neophyte who becomes angry. So, anger in the conditioned state is very, very negative quality. It's very negative quality. But in the liberated state,
