### anger, love, rasa
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=855
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In anger there is love."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that in the liberated state, anger can be a spiritual emotion expressing love, referencing the twelve kinds of rasa.

Anger is one of the many spiritual emotions. If you carefully study the twelve kinds of loving relationships, love is expressed indirectly in seven ways. In anger there is love. Raudra, rasa. Twelve kinds of rasa. Five are primary rasas. Seven secondary rasas. Sometimes Radharani becomes angry with Krishna. That anger is out of love for Krishna. It is out of love for Krishna. So, in that anger also there is love. But that love is always in the liberated state. There is no question of love in the conditioned state. In the conditioned state, love is reflected as lust. Prema is reflected as, pervertedly reflected as kama. So, in the Chaitanya Charita Amrita, Krishnadas Kaviraj Goswami distinguishes between kama and prema.

### kama, prema, Krishna's senses
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=945
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "And a very strong desire to please Krishna's senses, Krishna yendriya, preeti vancha, prema nama dhare. It is called prema. It is called love."
**Summary:** The speaker defines kama as the desire to satisfy one's own senses and prema as the desire to please Krishna's senses.

What is the definition of kama he gives and what is the definition of prema? Atma yendriya, preeti vancha. That's called kama. A strong desire to satisfy my own senses is called kama. Is called lust. And a very strong desire to please Krishna's senses. Krishna yendriya, preeti vancha, prema nama dhare. It is called prema. It is called love. So, prema is the strong desire to please Krishna's senses. So, in prema, in trying to please Krishna, in trying to satisfy Krishna, sometimes, when there is an obstacle, then there could be anger, even in the liberated state.

### sanyasa, Vedic culture, saffron dress
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=1057
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This is the Vedic culture, that saffron color indicates the person is in the renounced order."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to elaborate on Prabhupada's explanation, starting with the Vedic system of sanyasa and the significance of saffron-colored garments.

So, elaborately, Prabhupada describes. First of all, Prabhupada says, according to the Vedic system, a sanyasi, a person in renounced order of life, which is dressed in saffron-colored garments. This is the Vedic culture, that saffron color indicates the person is in the renounced order. Of course, now, Vedic culture is not so much followed. Somebody wants to go to Ayyappa temple, and has taken some kind of vow, to follow some rules. So, then they wear saffron. Sometimes they wear black. Some of them wear red. Some of them wear saffron. Like that. So, but strictly in the Vedic culture, except sanyasis, nobody dresses in saffron-colored garments. It is reserved for sanyasis.

### sanyasa, mendicant, preaching
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=1141
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Those in the renounced order of life have no other business but preaching the glories and supremacy to the Supreme Personality of Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the saffron dress serves as a passport for sanyasis, allowing them to travel and preach Krishna consciousness.

Now, that is because, this saffron dress is practically a passport for the mendicant and sanyasi to go anywhere. He is identified as a sanyasi by his saffron dress, which means that he can go anywhere without any restriction. Mendicant. Do you understand what is mendicant? What is the meaning of the word mendicant? It is an English word. Beggar. One who begs. Now, sanyasis, sometimes, they go begging from householders, simply to get an opportunity to enter the householder's house. And why are they trying to enter? In order to enlighten them in Krishna consciousness. That's why Prabhupada says, those in the renounced order of life have no other business but preaching the glories and supremacy to the Supreme Personality of Godhead. The Vedic culture, the sanyasis are simply meant to travel and preach. Especially to the householders who become busy with their family life and may forget the prime goal of life, self-realization. To awaken such householders, to enlighten them in Krishna consciousness, a sanyasi travels and preaches.

---
### sanyasa, parivrajaka, renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=1227
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They are begging because they want to actually give an opportunity for the householders to do some seva to the Supreme Lord through giving something to the sanyasi."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the four phases of sanyasa: kutichaka, bahudhaka, parivrajaka, and paramahamsa, focusing on the preparatory stages for full renunciation.

So, this phase of sanyas, there are three, four phases of sanyas. This third phase of sanyas is called parivrajaka. Four stages of sanyas, or four phases of sanyas. Kutichaka, bahudhaka, parivrajaka and paramahamsa. So, kutichaka is the stage where a sanyasi, especially after giving up the household life, in different stages, the sanyasi is able to develop complete renunciation. Full renunciation is difficult right in the beginning of accepting sanyas. So, the preparatory stage for full renunciation is called kutichaka, where one stays just outside the village or outside his home, and the food keeps coming from home, but he does not visit his house. He does not maintain any connection with his former family members. They keep sending through somebody some food. So, he doesn't have to go and beg, because he is not yet accustomed to begging. He would have been living a very honorable life. He would have been earning his own livelihood. He might have been a respectable person in the society. So, if a respectable person suddenly starts, especially in the same village, he starts going and begging. People think, what's wrong with this guy? Why is he begging? Of course, in the Vedic culture, they understand that sanyasis beg not because they are in want of food. They are begging out of humility. They are begging because they want to actually give an opportunity for the householders to do some seva to the Supreme Lord through giving something to the sanyasi. So many different reasons can be given like that, why sanyasis beg. But in the Vedic culture, the general people understand that a sanyasi is not simply begging because he is a parasite in the society or he has become lazy.

---
### sanyasa, bahudhaka, Madhukari
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=1377
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The profession of a bee."
**Summary:** The speaker continues describing the stages of sanyasa, focusing on the bahudhaka stage where the sanyasi begs from many houses like a bee collecting nectar.

But the sanyasi himself may have some hesitation to go and beg. So, in the beginning, the food comes from home. And then, bahudaka is the stage where he is now ready to not get food from home but to go and beg little, little from many different houses. Madhukari, it's called. The profession of a bee. What does a bee do? It goes to different flowers and takes little, little, little, little. Because every flower, how much it can give? Nectar. Very, very little. So, the flower doesn't become completely useless and dead when the bee takes little nectar. But it collects nectar from many flowers and ultimately so much of honey. It makes wonderful honey out of the nectar it collects from flowers. Only the bee can do. No scientist can do this. No scientist can do this. Only the bees can do this. So, the bees collect nectar from so many flowers. So, similarly, the sanyasi in the bahudaka stage, he goes to many different houses, begs little, little, little. So that the householders will not feel a burden. And he himself is able to get his minimum requirement without any difficulty. A sanyasi is generally not allowed to cook. He should not cook. He should go and beg and take whatever is given and just eat for just maintaining his body and soul together. That's the recommendation for the sanyasi, renounced daughter of life.

---
### sanyasa, bahudhaka, parivrajaka
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=1485
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A sanyasi should not be restricted knowing very well that a sanyasi is coming to give spiritual enlightenment."
**Summary:** The speaker continues describing the bahudhaka stage and transitions to the parivrajaka stage, where the sanyasi travels and preaches, emphasizing that a sanyasi should not be restricted.

So, bahudaka stage is the second stage where he goes and begs. And that also he'll beg in places generally known to him. Generally he does not go to an unknown place. Just like he goes to a house where they know, oh, he was a very respectable person, he was working, he is not a lazy bum. Now he has taken sanyas, therefore he is coming and begging. So, very happily they will give. Very respectably, honorably, nicely they will give. So, such places he will go and beg, he will not have any hesitation in begging. Neither he will be refused. He will not have difficulty in getting his some minimum requirement of food. He will not have any hesitation. So, he becomes accustomed to begging and getting whatever his minimum needs. Specially food. Then after that, he is now ready to travel. Even go to unknown places. Specially traveling means going to unknown places. That stage is called parivrajaka. Literally means traveling. Everywhere. Everywhere. Traveling all over the country or all over the world. As the case may be. As it is feasible. So, traveling for the sake of preaching. Traveling for the sake of preaching. And by the way, accepting some food or some gift or something like that. So, Prabhupada says here, a sanyasi should not be restricted knowing very well that a sanyasi is coming to give spiritual enlightenment.

### sanyasa, gift, Narada Muni
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=1614
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If at all he makes a demand, it is for the benefit of the householder."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that a sanyasi generally will not demand anything, but if he does, it is for the benefit of the householder, illustrating this with the story of Narada Muni and Mrigari the hunter.

With that understanding a sanyasi is not restricted. And he is allowed to go anywhere and everywhere he wants and he has not refused any gift he might demand from a householder. A sanyasi generally will never demand from any householder. If at all he makes a demand, it is for the benefit of the householder. It is for the benefit of the householder. Just like Narada Muni was traveling, he came across this hunter, Mrigari. You have heard this story. So, this Mrigari had the habit of half killing animals. And he used to take pleasure when the animals suffer so much pain. And they don't immediately die when he half kills them using his poison arrows. So, Narada Muni, he saw that these poor animals are suffering in pain. So, out of compassion for the suffering animals, Narada Muni nicely removed the arrow and then with his lotus hand, his mystic power, he touched and healed that wound. And the animal would just go away happily walking away. Oh, I am relieved now. So, the hunter saw this and said, why is he bothering me? So, he approached Narada Muni and said, why are you unnecessarily bothering me? If you want any gift of any skin, deer skin or tiger skin, these are sadhus, no? They want some tiger skin or deer skin for their sitting and doing tapasya. So, I will give you. I have got lot of skins. So, please don't disturb my business, my livelihood. Don't disturb me in my livelihood. So, Narada Muni said, I have no intention of disturbing you in your livelihood. And I don't want any gift of any skin, deer skin, tiger skin, nothing. But I want to ask you one thing. What is that? As a hunter, you are supposed to kill for your livelihood. But I request you, don't half kill these animals. You fully kill them and then you take the dead animal, do whatever. Don't half kill them. So, the hunter simply by this brief association with Narada Muni, the pure devotee, powerful, spiritually powerful devotee,

---
### Narada Muni, Mrigari, compassion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=1776
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Killing is sinful. But half killing is very, very, very, very sinful."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Narada Muni and Mrigari, highlighting Narada Muni's compassion and his instruction to the hunter.

He began to consider the words of Narada Muni very seriously. So, he asked Narada Muni, is there a difference between half killing and full killing? Narada Muni was very pleased. Such a nice question he is asking. So, Narada Muni explained, killing is sinful. But half killing is very, very, very, very sinful. Half killing is very, very, very sinful. So, at least, even if you kill animals, don't give them so much pain. And become implicated sinfully too much. So, then, Vrigari began to think, what a horrible occupation I am engaged in. So, then he began to inquire, how can I give up this business of killing? As a hunter, I only know killing. No other way I know how to maintain myself. So, then Narada Muni instructed him, and he became a devotee. So, the point is, Narada Muni, he didn't want anything from the hunter. But still he said, I want to beg you for something. That don't half kill the animals. So, the idea is that when a sanyasi demands or begs, generally sanyasi will not beg. But if he begs or demands something from a householder, or from somebody, it is for that person's spiritual benefit.

---
### sanyasa, gift, inauspiciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=1894
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Especially a householder should not refuse a gift which a sanyasi asks."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that a householder should never refuse a gift requested by a sanyasi, as refusal can be inauspicious.

So, therefore, when a sanyasi asks something, he's never refused. He's never refused. And especially a householder should not refuse a gift which a sanyasi asks. He's never refused a gift which a sanyasi asks. He's never refused a gift which a sanyasi asks. He's never refused a gift which a sanyasi asks. He's never refused a gift which a sanyasi asks. It's very interesting that Prabhupada, when he went to the West, and even before going to the West, in India also, as a sanyasi, he was never going and begging anybody for his needs, minimum needs. How was he maintaining himself? By printing books and selling them. He did this even in America. Even in America he did this. So, a sanyasi, a genuine sanyasi, will not generally ask something because if a sanyasi begs or asks something, from a householder, and the householder does not give or cannot give, it is very, very inauspicious for that householder. It's very inauspicious. So sanyasi, before asking, he will think. If I ask, if he does not give, then it will be very inauspicious for him. So if he is not in a position to give, or he doesn't want to give, I will not ask. So sanyasis don't ask so easily. A genuine sanyasi will not simply go and beg or ask.

### brahmanas, Sudama, Madhavendra Puri
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KHe8E-ZPO-4?t=2007
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna will provide, whatever I require."
**Summary:** The speaker provides examples of enlightened brahmanas and paramahamsas, like Sudama and Madhavendra Puri, who depended on the Lord and did not beg easily.

Even brahmanas sometimes, Sudama, Krishna's friend Sudama, he would not go and beg. If somebody would not come and give him some dana, he will not have anything to eat. He will just drink some water and sleep. So at one time, continuously for many days, I don't know how many days or weeks or months, no, he has not eaten anything. He is becoming very, very, very, very weak. And reduced to skin and bones. And then his wife is worried, what is going to happen to him? And you know the story after that, what happened. So there are enlightened brahmanas, the Bhagavatam describes, why was he not going and begging for some food? When there was no dana coming. So because Bhagavatam says, Shukadeva Goswami says this, he was very enlightened. He is simply depending on the Supreme Lord. If somebody comes and gives some, Supreme Lord sends something, it's fine. If he doesn't send something, it's still okay. So a sanyasi also, sometimes, as a paramahamsa, he may not beg anywhere. Madhavendra Puri was like that. Once he went to Mathura, and he was just sitting. He had fasted for, some time, two, three days. He had not taken anything. And then, he was sitting, and he was just chanting. Suddenly one cowherd boy came, and gave him some milk. And said, why are you fasting? Why don't you beg for some, food? In this village, no one fasts. And if somebody is, in need, I supply. So it is Krishna himself, who had come. Later on, Madhavendra Puri came to know. It is Krishna himself, who had come, to give him. So, this is the, mood of, a paramahamsa. This is the mood of, enlightened Brahmanas. Krishna will provide, whatever I require. Satisf

## SB 1.15.50-51 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami |  06-01-2023
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### invocation, prayer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The lecture begins with traditional Vaishnava prayers and mantras, invoking blessings and paying respects to gurus and deities.

Om Agyanati Miran Dhasya Gyananjan Shalakaya Chakshurun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnu Padaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Ganadhara Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare.

---
### Srimad Bhagavatam, Pandavas, Renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=40
**Verse References:** SB 1.15.50, SB 1.15.51
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In this session we will be discussing from two verses, the 50th and the 51st verses of the 15th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces the topic of the lecture, which is based on verses 50 and 51 of the 15th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on the Pandavas' retirement.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing from two verses, the 50th and the 51st verses of the 15th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. These two verses are the last verses of this chapter. This chapter was entitled The Pandavas Retired Timely.

---
### Draupadi, Subhadra, Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=69
**Verse References:** SB 1.15.50
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Both she and Subhadra became absorbed in thoughts of Krishna and attained the same results as their husbands."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the meaning of verse 50, describing how Draupadi and Subhadra, understanding their husbands' detachment, became absorbed in thoughts of Krishna and attained the same spiritual destination.

So the Pandavas retired and they prepared themselves to go back to Godhead. Now we will hear the description of even the wives of the Pandavas also went back to Godhead following in the footsteps of their husbands. So the 50th verse: Draupadi Chatad Aagnyaya Patinam Anapekshatam Vasudeve Bhagavati Hekantamatir Apatam. The meaning of this 50th verse is Draupadi also saw that her husbands, without caring for her, were leaving home. She knew well about Lord Vasudeva and the love of Krishna, the personality of Godhead. Both she and Subhadra became absorbed in thoughts of Krishna and attained the same results as their husbands.

---
### Preparation, Death, Spiritual Practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=127
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When flying an airplane, one cannot take care of other planes. Everyone has to take care of his own plane."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces an analogy of flying an airplane to illustrate the importance of individual preparation for death and going back to Godhead, emphasizing that one must be ready and cannot rely on others at the final moment.

So the description now is being given about the wives of the Pandavas. Draupadi was the wife of all the five Pandavas and Subhadra was the wife of Arjuna. So the description is given about their going back to Godhead. How did they go back to Godhead? So in the purport Srila Prabhupada says, 'When flying an airplane, one cannot take care of other planes. Everyone has to take care of his own plane and if there is any danger, no other plane can help another in that condition.' Now this is an example being given just to help us understand that before death comes, we must be prepared.

---
### Spiritual Guidance, Guru, Practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=185
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "At the time of death, if you are not ready to go back to Godhead, we have to accept another body."
**Summary:** The speaker stresses the need for training, hearing, clarifying doubts, and practicing spiritual principles with the help of a bona fide spiritual master, scriptures, and association of devotees before death arrives, as it will be too late to start preparing at the time of death.

By taking training, by taking help, by hearing, by clarifying doubts, by practicing etc. For all that help is available, we must take help. The bona fide spiritual master, the scriptures, the instructions of the previous Acharyas, Guru Sadhu Shastra, and the association of devotees, and our own practice, everything is required to be done before death comes. It's not that at the time of death I'll start practicing, I'll start preparing. No. At the time of death, if you are not ready to go back to Godhead, we have to accept another body.

---
### Reincarnation, Individual Effort, Spiritual Master
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=248
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Similarly at the end of life, when one has to go back home, back to Godhead, everyone has to take care of himself without help rendered by another."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the airplane analogy, emphasizing that at the time of death, one must rely on their own preparation and cannot depend on others for help, highlighting the importance of individual effort in spiritual advancement.

We have to accept another birth. So therefore it is said here that the example is given of flying an airplane. Now each one has to fly his or her own plane to go back to Godhead. Of course this is not literal, but you can literally... It is the process or the method or the way to go back to Godhead should be known to each person before death comes. So Srila Prabhupada says, 'Similarly at the end of life, when one has to go back home, back to Godhead, everyone has to take care of himself without help rendered by another. At the time of quitting the body, there is no question of taking help of another devotee or spiritual master or anybody like that. No.'

---
### Guidance, Responsibility, Family
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=318
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Don't accept the position of becoming a father, or a mother, or a husband, or a teacher, or a devata, or a king, if you cannot deliver your dependents from the cycle of birth and death."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that while ultimate responsibility lies with the individual, help and guidance are available during one's lifetime from spiritual masters, parents, relatives, and others, emphasizing the responsibility of superiors to guide their dependents towards spiritual liberation.

Proper clarifies, 'The help is however offered on the ground, before flying in space.' Similarly, the spiritual master, the father, the mother, the relatives, the husband, and others can all render help during one's lifetime. While we are living in this present body, we should take help, we should take guidance, we should practice, we should live in the association of people who are seriously practicing. So we take help from who all? We take help from the spiritual master. We take help from the father, mother, the relatives, all our superiors. There is a verse in the Bhagavatam that says, 'Don't accept the position of becoming a father, or a mother, or a husband, or a teacher, or a devata, or a king, if you cannot deliver your dependents from the cycle of birth and death.'

---
### Training, Education, Dependence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=390
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The superior has to train, educate, and help the subordinate to actually prepare them for going back to Godhead at the end of life."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the responsibility of superiors to train, educate, and help their dependents prepare for going back to Godhead, reiterating Srila Prabhupada's emphasis on the help available during one's lifetime.

So there is a responsibility towards a dependent or a subordinate that the superior has to train, educate, educate, and help the subordinate to actually prepare them for going back to Godhead at the end of life. So therefore they are specifically mentioned here by Srila Prabhupada. The spiritual master, the father, the mother, the relatives, the husband, and others can all render help during one's lifetime. But while crossing the sea, one has to take care of himself and utilize the instructions formally received while actually going back to Godhead, when quitting the body. After quitting the body, one has to utilize those instructions, one has to have had sufficient practice and that will be also explained what is that practice, what is that we have to do is clearly explained in the later part of this purport.

### Draupadi, Individual Responsibility, Detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=461
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Draupadi had to take care of herself without waiting for her great husbands."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the individual responsibility for spiritual progress, using the example of Draupadi, who had to take care of her own spiritual advancement without relying on her husbands.

Continue to read the purport by Srila Prabhupada. Draupadi had five husbands and no one asked Draupadi to come when they were going back to Godhead, the five husbands, the five Pandavas, Yudhishthira, Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, Sahadeva, none of them asked Draupadi, 'please come with me, let us together go back to Godhead.' No. Why? Because Draupadi had to take care of herself without waiting for her great husbands. This is the principle.

---
### Material Relationships, Spiritual Relationships, Eternal Soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=507
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The soul has eternal spiritual relationship with Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that material relationships are temporary and based on the body, while the soul has an eternal, spiritual relationship with Krishna, which connects it to other souls.

So it is very clear while in this body, while in this material existence, the relationships are between the bodies. It is not between the souls. The material relationships are always between two bodies. So this we have to remember and when we quit the body, the relations are finished. There is no more relationship. Now the soul however has eternal spiritual relationship with Krishna. The individual soul has eternal particular loving relationship with Krishna and through Krishna, through the relationship with Krishna, the soul is related to other souls also.

---
### Duty, Concentration, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9LVbYEbZIL8?t=583
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Draupadi's relationship with her five husbands was as long as the bodies were there."
**Summary:** The speaker reiterates that material duties and relationships end with the body's demise, and Draupadi's example shows the importance of individual concentration on Krishna for spiritual liberation.

So those relationships are spiritual, eternal, they are not temporary, they are not just based on the material body, no. So Draupadi's relationship with her five husbands was as long as the bodies were there. Even in terms of duty of the wife towards the husband or husband towards the wife, it is only as long as the body is there. Once one quits the body, there is no more duty, there is no more relationship, material relationship I mean, material relationship. So therefore Srila Prabhupada said Draupadi had to take care of herself without waiting for her great husbands and because she was already trained, she at once took to concentration upon the lotus feet of Lord Vasudeva, Krishna, the personality of God.

## SB 1.16.25 - 30 Part 5 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami |  05-02-2023
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### chivalry, Kshatriya, responsibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_8Hx3BFK-YU?t=45
**Verse References:** BG 18.43
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A Kshatriya means he should have the courage to fight to the extent that if required he is willing to give up his life, but he will not give up the fight."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the quality of responsibility in Krishna, exemplified by his chivalrous actions as a Kshatriya, highlighting the courage and willingness to fight even to the death.

In this session, we will be discussing verses 25 to 30 of the 16th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, which we were discussing in the previous four sessions. These are the verses describing the 40 transcendental qualities that are to be found in the Supreme Lord, which Mother Earth is describing to Dharma. Among the qualities of Krishna, one of the qualities described is his responsibility, the quality of accepting responsibility. Even in his transcendental pastimes, his different role in his transcendental pastimes, he accepts responsibility and carries out his responsibility very, very wonderfully. Sri Ramaprabha is describing when he is playing the part of a Kshatriya as the son of Maharaj Vasudeva, he displayed that Kshatriya quality of being chivalrous. Sri Ramaprabha says, a Kshatriya king has to secure a wife by fighting or kidnapping a princess, sometimes. This sort of behavior for a Kshatriya is praiseworthy in the sense that a Kshatriya must show his power of chivalry to his would-be wife, so that the daughter of a Kshatriya is not taken away from him. The daughter of a Kshatriya can see the valor of her would-be husband. A Kshatriya princess, she wants demonstration before she marries another Kshatriya king. She wants to see his power of chivalry, his bravery, not that he is dressed up and he is having some wealth, but actually in his heart he is not having that courage to fight. A Kshatriya means he should have the courage to fight to the extent that if required he is willing to give up his life, but he will not give up the fight. Yuddhecha api apalayanam. This is described in the Bhagavad Gita by Krishna in the 18th chapter, in the 43rd verse, describing the qualities of a Kshatriya. A Kshatriya is courageous in battle. He never retreats from a battle.

---
### chivalry, Krishna's pastimes, marriage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_8Hx3BFK-YU?t=226
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna came to rescue and he had to fight against the army of Shishupala, and he fought very bravely and kidnapped Rukmini and brought her safely to Dwaraka."
**Summary:** This segment narrates instances of Krishna and Rama displaying chivalry to secure their wives, including Rama breaking the bow of Haradhanu to win Sita, and Krishna fighting to rescue Rukmini from marrying Shishupala.

So, in displaying his courage in battle, Krishna, in order to marry some princess, he actually displayed his courage by actually fighting in some cases. Srila Prabhupada says even the personality of Godhead Sri Rama displayed such a chivalry, a spirit of chivalry during his marriage. He broke the strongest bow called Haradhanus and achieved the hand of Sita Devi, the mother of all opulence. Lord Rama is the supreme lord, but he appeared as the son of a Kshatriya, as Dasharatha's son. And when Vishwamitra took Lord Ramachandra, he was a small boy, a young prince. Vishwamitra took him to protect his sacrificial arena. Dasharatha initially was hesitant, but when Vishwamitra explained to Dasharatha, your son is no ordinary person. He is a supreme lord incarnated as your son. So don't have any doubts whether your son can give protection to my sacrificial arena. Anyway, Dasharatha agreed and finally sent Rama and Lakshmana. So Rama and Lakshmana were young princes, but they fought against 14,000 rakshasa associates of Ravana who were disturbing the sacrificial arena of Vishwamitra Rishi and other saintly persons. So Rama killed all of them and delivered all the 14,000 rakshasa. So that's only possible for the Supreme Personality of Godhead. But he displayed that, that chivalry. And when Sita Swayamvara, Janaka had declared that whichever king wants to marry my daughter, beautiful daughter, the mother of all opulence, Sita Devi, Lakshmiji, then they have to actually break one very, very, very heavy bow, Haradhanu, bow of Lord Shiva. Three hundred men had to carry it to bring it to the place where the competition was to take place. And single-handedly of course Lord Rama lifted it. Not only lifted it as a heavy bow, but he also broke that strongest bow easily, very effortlessly he broke it. So he actually demonstrated his strength as a powerful Kshatriya. Lord Krishna also he had to fight like a chivalrous Kshatriya to secure a wife. Just like Rukmini, it was scheduled, Rukmini was scheduled to marry someone else, Shishupala, against her wish and against her father's wish also, her elder brother decided to marry Rukmini to Shishupala. And Rukmini wanted to marry Krishna because she is a devotee of Krishna. So she sent a secret letter to Krishna, kindly rescue me. Krishna came to rescue and he had to fight against the army of Shishupala. Army of Shishupala. And he fought very bravely and kidnapped Rukmini and brought her safely to Dwaraka.

---
### responsibility, Krishna's supremacy, demonstration
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_8Hx3BFK-YU?t=478
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When he displayed his universal form he proved it. When he fought against all the demons he proved it. When he accepted the challenge of different Kshatriyas and defeated them he proved his supremacy."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes Krishna's responsibility and perfection, demonstrated through his actions and pastimes, proving his supremacy beyond mere claims.

So like that he had to secure so many different wives. A Kshatriya generally marries more than one wife. So Krishna as part of his Leela he married 16,108 princesses. Among them 16,108 princesses were all held captive by one Bhaumasura. So Krishna fought against Bhaumasura and rescued these princesses. So like that he displayed full responsibility in every action of his different transcendental pastimes. He is not irresponsible at any time. And of course he is all perfect. Krishna is the Supreme Lord, is all perfect. But he demonstrates that also. Not simply by way of proclaiming or claiming by words, I am the greatest, or I am the Supreme, or I am the Lord of everything. Krishna declares in Bhagavad Gita I am the Lord of all the planets in the entire creation. So when he declares that he also proves it. When he displayed his universal form he proved it. When he fought against all the demons he proved it. When he accepted the challenge of different Kshatriyas and defeated them he proved his supremacy. Like that.

---
### knowledge, gratitude, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_8Hx3BFK-YU?t=586
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Only fools are ungrateful to their benefactors."
**Summary:** This segment delves into the quality of knowledge, its five subheadings, and the importance of gratitude, especially in the context of devotional service to Krishna, who reciprocates the unconditional love of his devotees.

Then there is one more quality called knowledge. The 14th quality called knowledge. It can be analyzed under five subheadings. So the first one is intelligence. Second one, second subheading of knowledge is gratefulness. Third one is power of understanding the circumstantial environments of place, object and time. And fourth one is perfect knowledge of everything in this world. And the fifth one, the last one, under the five subheadings. The fifth one is knowledge of the self is the most confidential knowledge. We all look like we are some material body, product of matter, and we think that way also. I am this body. But actually we are not a body. We are not material in nature. We are spiritual. We are spirit soul. So this knowledge of the self, the real I, a spirit soul, this knowledge is the most confidential one. It is called Brahmavidya, Atmavidya, Adhyatmavidya. Different names are same. Knowledge of the self. So these five subheadings, Prabhupada explains briefly regarding gratitude. Prabhupada says, only fools are ungrateful to their benefactors. What does it mean? If I take some benefit from somebody, I should be grateful to that person who is offering me some benefit. That's the least I can do after receiving some benediction. I should at least be grateful to that person. So the Supreme Lord's position is that he does not require any benefit from anyone, because he is completely full in himself. Atma Rama is self-satisfied, because he is the owner of every resource. He is the supreme proprietor of everything and everyone. So what is the need for him? There is no need for him of anything. So there is no question of the Lord being in want. But still when his pure devotees render him service, he feels benefited by their pure devotional service, and therefore he feels grateful to his devotees for their unsophisticated, unconditional service, and tries to reciprocate that service of the devotees by himself rendering service to the devotee. Although the devotee does not expect anything in return from the Lord.

---
### gratitude, omnipresence, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_8Hx3BFK-YU?t=778
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord is all pervading, present everywhere by the omnipresent rays of his effulgence called Brahma Jyoti."
**Summary:** This segment continues the discussion on gratitude and then focuses on the Lord's all-knowing nature, explaining that because he is present everywhere through his Brahma Jyoti, he is conscious and knows everything.

See how pure devotees serve Krishna out of love for him and they don't want anything in return. Krishna accepts his service and he wants to repay his devotees. And therefore he renders service to him though the devotees don't want any service. Anyway he is the only maintainer of everyone. Still Krishna feels a sense of gratitude for his devotees rendering him service. Now regarding the Lord being all knowing, one of the subheadings of knowledge was that gratefulness and another subheading was perfect knowledge of everything. Perfect knowledge of everything. So regarding that Srila Prabhupada explains here the Vedas say Brahma Brahma Brahma Brahma. That means the Lord is all pervading, present everywhere by the omnipresent rays of his effulgence called Brahma Jyoti. There is a Brahma Jyoti that is effulgence of the Lord that spread everywhere in the entire creation. And because he is present everywhere he is also he knows everything. Simply because he is conscious. He is conscious being. So since he is conscious and he is present everywhere he knows everything. So that is the way it is explained he is all knowing the Srila Prabhupada.

### Mahat Tattva, Yoga Nidra, Maha Vishnu
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RaykGB0KLdU?t=100
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This material world is created when the Lord lies down on the causal ocean and goes to sleep."
**Summary:** This segment explains the creation of the material world, starting with Maha Vishnu lying down on the causal ocean in yoga nidra, and the significance of this 'mystic slumber'.

So Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, the last part of the purport, this material world is a product of the Mahat Tattva, which is a state of the Lord's presence, which is a state of the Lord's dreaming condition in his yoga nidra mystic slumber in the causal ocean. A lot of technical terms in here. Now the Supreme Lord, he incarnates in this material world for the purpose of the material creation, maintenance, destruction, for the sake of attracting the conditioned souls to go back to Godhead, for the sake of giving transcendental knowledge, for displaying his transcendental pastimes and so many purposes. So different incarnations of the Lord appear from time to time. But at the beginning of the creation or even before the creation takes place, the Lord, first of all, he incarnates as Maha Vishnu. That is the first incarnation of the Lord before the creation of this entire material cosmic manifestation. So this Maha Vishnu, he appears in the portion of the spiritual sky which is covered by the material energy. Material energy. The material energy is like a cloud in the brilliantly effulgent spiritual sky. And in that cloud-like region, the Lord enters. Vishnu means one who enters. He enters into that material region covered by the material energy. And he lies down on the causal ocean. And he lies down on the causal ocean. And he lies down on the causal ocean. So right in that causal ocean lies water. The causal ocean. And he lies down and he goes to sleep. And his sleeping is not like ours. When we sleep we go into forgetfulness of our wakeful state. But the Lord, he is never forgetful. So his sleeping is caused by our sleep. Sleeping is called yoga nidra. Nidra means sleeping. So, Srila Prabhupada describes that yoga nidra as mystic slumber. Slumber means sleeping. So, it's mystical how he goes to sleep. It is his pastime. It's his leela.

---
### Creation, Mahat Tattva, Dreaming
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RaykGB0KLdU?t=285
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This material world is created when the Lord lies down on the causal ocean and goes to sleep. And the Lord dreams about the material creation."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the process of creation as a result of the Lord's dreaming, emphasizing that this dreaming is not like human dreaming and that the creation, maintenance, and destruction of the material universes occur within this dreaming state.

So, this material world is created when the Lord lies down on the causal ocean and goes to sleep. And the Lord dreams about the material creation. Now, this dreaming of the Lord is not like our dreaming. It is entirely different. That in the dreaming condition also there is the creation, maintenance and destruction of the material universes. So, the creation begins when the Lord enters this material energy region like a cloud in the spiritual sky. And he lies down on the causal ocean. And he dreams. He goes to sleep. Mystic slumber. Yoga nidra. And he dreams and in that dreaming state there is the creation of the material world. It is a product of the mahat tattva. Mahat tattva is the material energy in a dormant condition when it is activated by the glass. The dance of Maha Vishnu. Then that inactive state of material energy transforms into activated condition. And in that activated condition the creation begins. By the mahat tattva differentiating into the different elements.

---
### Control, Consciousness, Factual Manifestation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RaykGB0KLdU?t=395
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though this material world is produced in the state of the Lord's dreaming condition, still the whole creation appears to be a factual presentation."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the factual nature of the Lord's dreaming condition and how it manifests as the material world. It highlights the difference between the Lord's consciousness and human consciousness, emphasizing the Lord's complete control and awareness.

And later on the elements are combined by the Supreme Lord in his form as Garbhodaka Shai Vishnu or Vishwatma in his Hiranyagarbha incarnation or expansion. Even though this material world is produced in the state of the Lord's dreaming condition, still the whole creation appears to be a factual presentation. It is a factual presentation of the Lord. That means the Lord's dreaming conditions are also factual manifestations. There is no difference between his dreaming condition and his wakeful condition. There is no difference. In our case it is entirely different. When we are in the wakeful state we are forgetful of our dreaming state. And when we are in the dreaming condition we are forgetful about our wakeful state. So that is our position. Whereas the Lord, for him he is ever cognizant. Always conscious. Always aware of everything. In every nook and corner of the entire creation. Spiritual world and material world. Because his consciousness is spread everywhere. So he is fully conscious. So therefore the creation of this world is also a factual manifestation of the Lord's dreaming condition. He can therefore bring everything under his transcendental control. You see, his complete control always exists. In the spiritual world, in the material world, in all conditions, in all states of existence.

---
### Control, Mercy, Liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RaykGB0KLdU?t=530
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He is complete controller of everything and everyone."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the Lord's complete control and fullness in all his appearances, even in his Vishnu form. It also discusses the Lord's mercy, extending even to demons who are killed by him, as this killing leads to their liberation.

He is complete controller of everything and everyone. Thus whenever and wherever he does appear he does so in his fullness. He is not bereft of any of his powers when he appears even as Vishnu lying down on the causal ocean and going to sleep. He is not bereft of any of his power. He is not forgetful. He does not go into ignorance. He is always completely full with perfect knowledge. So he appears in this world or anywhere in any form of incarnation, personal incarnation, in his fullness. He is always with his knowledge, with his beauty, with his energies, with his paraphernalia, with his associates. He is always full. He is never partial. He is never incomplete. So therefore Srila Prabhupada says further, the Lord being all that is described above maintains the affairs of the creation. He creates, he maintains. And by his so doing he gives salvation even to his enemies who are killed by him. We say the Lord is all merciful. So he is merciful even to the demons who are against the Lord. Because they are also his part and parcel. They are also his children. Though they are disobedient or they are misled or they are desirous of independent enjoyment or they have got enviousness, whatever be their condition, they are ultimately part and parcel of the Supreme Lord only as much as anyone else is. So Krishna never neglects them. So he, when they create some disturbance for the devotees, then Krishna comes to stop that disturbance and Krishna kills the demons. But his killing is good for the demons because the demons get salvation or mukti or moksha, liberation from material bondage. So it's a favor of the Lord upon the demons when the demons are killed by the Lord personally.

---
### Attraction, Transcendence, Inconceivability
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RaykGB0KLdU?t=690
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Thus, the Supreme Lord is worshipable even by Brahma and Shiva, the greatest of all demigods."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the Lord's attractiveness to even liberated souls and his worship by demigods like Brahma and Shiva. It also touches on the Lord's creative energy and the countless incarnations, emphasizing the unfathomable and inconceivable nature of the Lord's qualities.

And further, he, the Supreme Lord, is attractive even to the topmost liberated soul. That liberated souls are also attracted to Krishna. They don't have any attraction for anything material. Those are liberated. It's only the conditioned souls who are running after matter and material products. The liberated soul is not interested in any matter or material objects. Even they are attracted by Krishna. When they have no attraction for anything material, still they are attracted to Krishna, which means that Krishna is not at all material personality. He is completely transcendental. Only then can he be attractive to the transcendentalists. Thus, the Supreme Lord is worshipable even by Brahma and Shiva, the greatest of all demigods. That is the position. Shiva virenchi nutam. Bhagavatam's eleventh canto describes this. He is worshiped even by the greatest demigods, Shiva and Brahma. Virenchi means Brahma. Now, regarding his creative energy. Even in his incarnation of Purushavatara, he is the lord of the creative energy. This creative energy is working under his direction. And to control the material energy, he is the root cause of innumerable incarnations in all the universes. How many incarnations are there? Countless. We cannot count. To give an example, just the incarnation of Manu. There are five hundred thousand incarnations of Manu in the lifetime of Brahma in one universe. Like this, there are millions of universes. So who can count how many incarnations of the lord appear in the material world? Nobody can count. And as far as the spiritual world is concerned, beyond this material creation, there is no incarnation, but there are plenary expansions of the lord, and they are perfectly carrying out their activities in the spiritual world. So, nobody can actually estimate or count the qualities of the lord, nor can anybody estimate in what quantity he has got those qualities. All of them are unfathomable, inconceivable, cannot be estimated by anyone. So that is the unique position of the supreme lord. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### Governance, Protection, Duty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/A5YG2qs98AU?t=51
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.10, SB 1.17.11
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "O chaste one, the king's good name, duration of life and good rebirth vanish when all kinds of living beings are terrified by miscreants in his kingdom."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses from Srimad Bhagavatam that will be discussed, focusing on the king's duty to protect all living beings and the consequences of failing to do so.

In this session we will be discussing from two verses, the 10th and the 11th verses of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Both these verses are given together by Srila Prabhupada, so I shall read both of them, the 10th verse and the 11th verse. Yasya Rashtre Prajasarvaha Trasyante Sadhya Asadubihi Tasya Mattasya Nashyanti Keertir Ayur Bhagogatihi Esha Ragnam Parodharmo Hyartanam Aarti Nigraha Ataenam Vadishyami Bhutadruham Asaptamam. The meaning of these two verses is as follows: O chaste one, the king's good name, duration of life and good rebirth vanish when all kinds of living beings are terrified by miscreants in his kingdom. It is certainly the prime duty of the king to subdue first the sufferings of those who suffer. Therefore, I must kill this most wretched man because he is violent against other living beings. So Srila Prabhupada writes elaborate purport for these two verses.

---
### Protection, Responsibility, Law and Order
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/A5YG2qs98AU?t=145
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Any living being who takes birth in the state has the primary right to live under the protection of the king."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Srila Prabhupada's purport, emphasizing the king's responsibility to protect all citizens, including animals, and to punish those who cause terror or harm.

Purport is as follows: When all kinds of living beings are terrified by miscreants in his kingdom, when there is some disturbance caused by wild animals in a village or town, the police or others take action to kill them. Similarly, it is the duty of the government to kill at once all bad social elements such as thieves, decoits and murderers. The same punishment is also due to animal killers because the animals of the state are also called praja. The word praja that is used, citizen. In English we say citizen, praja. Praja means one who has taken birth in the state. Ja means to take birth. So this praja includes both men and animals, especially the cows and bulls. They are reared by human beings, especially for Brahmanical culture. Any living being who takes birth in the state has the primary right to live under the protection of the king. Any living being who takes birth in the state, be it an animal or a human being. The jungle animals are also subject to the king and they also have a right to live. What to speak of, as Prabhupada says, what to speak of the domestic animals like the cows and bulls, even the jungle animals, they cannot be indiscriminately, they cannot be indiscriminately killed by anyone. So, especially the cows and bulls must be protected by the king. Also, as Prabhupada further explains, any living being, if he terrifies other living beings, is a most wretched subject and the king should at once kill such a disturbing element. To maintain peace, to maintain law and order, the king should impose very strict punishment in order to protect the innocent. As the wild animal is killed when it creates disturbances, similarly, any man who unnecessarily kills or terrifies the jungle animals or other animals must be punished at once.

---
### Karma, Subsistence, Vegetarianism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/A5YG2qs98AU?t=291
**Verse References:** SB 1.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "By the law of the Supreme Lord, all living beings in whatever shape they may be are the sons of the Lord, and no one has any right to kill another animal unless it is so ordered by the codes of natural law."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the laws of nature regarding subsistence, explaining why humans should not kill animals for food, as they can subsist on grains, fruits, and milk ordained by God, except in extreme circumstances.

By the law of the Supreme Lord, all living beings in whatever shape they may be are the sons of the Lord, and no one has any right to kill another animal unless it is so ordered by the codes of natural law. What is wrong in killing an animal by a human being? Prabhupada points out that the tiger can kill a lower animal for his subsistence because that is the food for the tiger ordained by the higher laws. But a man cannot kill an animal for his subsistence. That is the law of God who has created the law that a living being subsists by eating another living being. Jeevo Jeevasya Jeevanam. This is explained in the earlier chapter, 13th chapter of the first canto of Bhagavatam, that we cannot eat some matter, dead matter, we cannot eat. We have to eat something for our subsistence, something which is like a living being. Thus the vegetarians are also living by eating other living beings. Therefore the law is that one should live only by eating specific living beings as ordained by the law of God. And that is explained in the Isopanishad as follows. The Isopanishad directs that one should live by direction of the Lord and not at one's sweet will. People say, my preference to eat meat. You may prefer to eat vegetables, but my preference to eat meat. This is the argument that is given by some people who are non-vegetarians. But Prabhupada says, a man can subsist on varieties of grains, fruits and milk ordained by God. There is no need of animal food. For a human being, save and accept in particular cases. Let's say there is no availability of food grains or fruits or vegetables or milk. Then under those extreme circumstances one may eat some animal flesh. Otherwise there is no reason to eat animal flesh.

---
### Governance, Karma, Responsibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/A5YG2qs98AU?t=441
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The illusion king or executive head, even though sometimes advertised as a great philosopher and learned scholar, will allow slaughterhouses in the state without knowing that torturing poor animals clears the way to hell to such foolish kings or executive heads."
**Summary:** The speaker condemns leaders who allow animal slaughter, emphasizing that they face karmic consequences and that true governance should be based on divine law, not personal whims.

The illusion king or executive head, even though sometimes advertised as a great philosopher and learned scholar, will allow slaughterhouses in the state without knowing that torturing poor animals clears the way to hell to such foolish kings or executive heads. See, above the laws enforced by a government or a king, there are laws of God operating in this material world. So, their future is very very very very dark. Those who are animal killers. The executive head must always be alert to the safety of the prajas, both man and animal, and inquire whether a particular living being is harassed at any place by another living being. The harassing living being must be at once caught and put to death as shown by Maharaj Parichar. The king's duty is to give protection and punish the culprits without showing any sentimental compassion or mercy. No. So Srila Prabhupada says, the people's government or government by the people should not allow killing of innocent animals by the sweet will of foolish government men. These government people don't care if animals are killed indiscriminately. And actually, the government should not allow that. If they want proper law and order, if they want peace in the society, they must know the codes of God as mentioned in the revealed scriptures, Dharma Shastra. Manusmriti, Parashara Smriti, Agnya Varkya Smriti. They should know that. Perfect. Correct directions are given in the revealed scriptures for governance, how to rule the kingdom or the state. Maharaj Parikshit quotes here that according to the codes of God, the irresponsible king or state executive jeopardizes his good name, duration of life, power and strength and ultimately his progressive march towards a better life and salvation after death.

---
### Ignorance, Karma, Suffering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/A5YG2qs98AU?t=586
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** The speaker discusses the ignorance of those who do not believe in the next life or the laws of God, explaining that their suffering is a result of past sinful activities and a violation of divine codes.

See, this is very important. This understanding that every living being is spirit-soul, not the body. And the soul actually is meant for making progress. The actual living being inside the body, the living entity as we say, the particular person inside the body is meant for progressing towards a better life. And that is the opportunity in this material world. Such foolish men do not even believe in the existence of the next life. They don't even know there is a next life. They are so ignorant. Or if they are informed, they think, oh, we don't believe in that. Now this belief system under the situation where they are ignorant people, certain laws that are working, they do not know. Just like foolish people, they do not know their suffering is caused by their own previous sinful activities. That they do not know. Many foolish people are there, ignorant people are there. And all kinds of non-believers, as we say, atheists, agnostics, upstarts, ignorant people, they suffer, but they do not know why they are suffering. Especially when they are afflicted by some disease. Or they are afflicted by some pain. Some natural calamity. They do not know why they were subject to some disaster, natural disaster. They do not know. But, it is not that sometimes there is some natural calamity and we have to simply face it. No. It is not like that. It is completely controlled. Nature is controlled by God. And nature, under God's direction, gives punishment to people who are sinful. And they suffer in proportion to their past sinful activities. Violation of the codes of God. So,

### Governance, Scripture, Conditioned Soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/A5YG2qs98AU?t=765
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the piety of Maharaj Parikshit with the ignorance of modern politicians who disregard scriptural codes, emphasizing that even great men are conditioned souls bound by material nature.

Srila Prabhupada says, politicians, they are ignorant and one such politician has written, I do not believe in any such ceremonies and to submit to them even as a matter of form would be hypocrisy in an attempt to delude ourselves and others. I have no religious sentiment in the matter. Some politician has said that he doesn't believe in life after death. He doesn't believe in the codes of God as given in the authentic scriptures. Such people are totally ignorant unlike Maharaj Pariksit. So, Maharaj Pariksit was pious according to scriptural codes. He was having a solid basis for governance. Whereas, the modern politician goes by his personal belief and sentiments. He just conducts himself and he enforces law based on his personal sentiments, which is very very dangerous. Because, I may differ from another person. Some other person may differ from somebody else. Is that the way to actually conduct the government? It is totally irrational. It's meaningless. So, Prabhupada says, after all, even the so-called great man in this material world is a conditioned soul. He is bound by his hands and feet by the ropes of material nature. And still he thinks that he can act whimsically. So the conclusion is, during Pariksit Maharaj's time, people were happy. Animals were given proper protection. And, there was peace and prosperity in the kingdom. And it was experienced by everyone.

### illusion, reality, mayavada

**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, this imaginary form is based on the wrong understanding that this material world itself is illusion, is false."
**Summary:** The speaker criticizes the concept of an imaginary form in meditation, linking it to the Mayavadi philosophy and the misunderstanding of the material world as an illusion.

Imaginary form, something imaginary. So, this imaginary form is also taught by the Mayavadi philosophers. Mayavadi philosophers, they also talk of imaginary form. So, this imaginary form is based on the wrong understanding that this material world itself is illusion, is false. The material world has got an illusory aspect that everything is a presentation of the interactions of the three gunas as reality but it is not reality. Because it is constantly changing, it is not reality. Reality is what remains or what is behind the change. That is the material energy itself which is working as per the direction of the Supreme Lord Krishna according to his will. So, that is reality. That reality is missed out by all these Mayavadis and the philosophers who follow Patanjali's yoga sutra etc.

---
### guidance, parampara, self-realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c5UrO3fWmFA?t=856
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing the necessity of guidance from a self-realized spiritual master in parampara to overcome illusion and attain self-realization, understanding the material world as part of the Lord's energies.

They miss out this reality. So, it is not easy to arrive at the truth. While we are in the grip of illusion, while we have this material body, while we are in ignorance, unless we take help of a self-realized bona fide spiritual master coming in parampara who is himself situated in transcendence and who can properly explain everything and enlighten us beyond matter, know our self as spiritual reality, as spirit soul, and Supreme Lord as a Supreme Spirit and the totality, some total understanding of everything in existence, at least the material existence itself as a part of the manifestation of the Lord's energies. In this session, we will be discussing from the 25th verse of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This chapter is entitled Punishment and Reward of Kali. It's interesting that the chapter title says Punishment and Reward. Punishment we can understand because Kali Purusha was the root cause of all kinds of irreligiosities. ### Dharma, truthfulness, Kali Yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/usmclZZ2p8k?t=89
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Earlier it was described Dharma which in Satya Yuga was standing on four legs, four pillars: austerity, cleanliness, mercy and truthfulness."
**Summary:** The meaning of the verse is explained in terms of Dharma standing on one leg (truthfulness) in Kali Yuga, while Kali tries to destroy it. The four pillars of Dharma (austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness) are described as they existed in Satya Yuga and diminished over time.

The meaning of this verse is, you are now standing on one leg only, which is your truthfulness, and you are somehow or other hobbling along, but quarrel personified Kali, flourishing by deceit, is also trying to destroy that leg. Earlier it was described Dharma which in Satya Yuga was standing on four legs, four pillars: austerity, cleanliness, mercy and truthfulness. So in Satya Yuga, four pillars of religion perfectly intact, and then by the time Satya Yuga progressed and came to Treta Yuga, one leg was destroyed, tapasya, austerity. Then from Treta Yuga to Dwapar Yuga, the second leg was destroyed, that is cleanliness. Now, beginning of Kali Yuga, only that fourth leg, only one leg, the fourth leg, that is truthfulness, is remaining, and somehow still some Dharma is there at the beginning of Kali Yuga, but this Kali Purusha, quarrel personified, flourishing by deceit, by unwritten, by lying propensity, lying propaganda, is trying to destroy that leg, that leg of truthfulness of Dharma. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains the principles of religion do not stand on some dogmas or man-made formulas, but they stand on four primary regulative observances, namely austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness. Earlier we had discussed religion, Dharma, means the codes given by God. Nobody can manufacture Dharma. Real Dharma, it is what is given by God in the beginning of creation and coming down in human society as a result of that. It is not possible to do that, it is impossible to get that.

---
### Dharma, austerity, parampara
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/usmclZZ2p8k?t=271
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Real religion means there are four primary regulative observances: austerity, cleanliness, mercy and truthfulness."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that real religion comes through the parampara system and is not man-made. He defines the four regulative principles, focusing on austerity as voluntarily accepting inconvenience for spiritual realization, using fasting as an example. He distinguishes this from fasting for political or other purposes, which is condemned in the Bhagavad-gita.

Through maturity, through the parampara system, bona fide parampara system. So there are no man-made religions. Man-made religion means that is a cheating religion, or somebody is very strong belief in some system of not real religion. So real religion means there are four primary regulative observances: austerity, cleanliness, mercy and truthfulness. The mass of people, Srila Prabhupada says, must be taught to practice these principles from childhood. Austerity means what are the meaning of these four principles in reality? Austerity means to accept voluntarily things which may be not very comfortable for the body but are conducive for spiritual realization. Tapasya, tapasya, what does tapasya mean? Voluntarily accept inconvenience for the body for a higher purpose, that is spiritual realization. And an example is given: fasting. Fasting twice or four times a day is not good for the body, it is not good for the mind, but at least a month is a sort of austerity which may be voluntarily accepted for spiritual realization only and not for any other purposes, political or otherwise. We know one great freedom fighter in this country used to go on fast to put pressure on the government to yield to some of their demands. That is not good for the body. The real austerity that one should take up, fasting is done, austerity means only for the sake of spiritual realization. Fasting which are meant for not for self-realization but for some other purposes are condemned in the Bhagavad-gita in the 17th chapter, fifth and sixth verses. Fasting in the mode of passion, fasting in the mode of ignorance, that is condemned in the Bhagavad-gita. It is not going to result in any spiritual benefit and materially even if it is in some way beneficial, ultimately is the cause of material entanglement. Already we are entangled, so we should not do something by which you get more entangled. That is not very wise, that's not very intelligent. Similarly, cleanliness is necessary both for the mind and the body.

This is achieved by glorifying the Supreme Lord, primarily through chanting Hare Krishna. A godless civilization cannot cleanse the mind because it lacks the concept of God.

Simply bodily cleanliness may help to some extent, people know about bodily cleanliness, external cleanliness, but more important is cleansing the mind. Bodily cleanses is good, but it's not enough, it's not sufficient. Cleanliness of the mind is absolutely essential for a human being. And how is it possible to clean the mind? It is affected by glorifying the Supreme Lord. By glorifying the Supreme Lord, no one can cleanse the accumulated mental without glorifying the Supreme Lord. Chanting Hare Krishna primarily cleanses our mind. Primary a godless civilization cannot cleanse the mind because it is no idea of God. Modern civilization is a godless civilization. People say, oh, we are very nice people, we like to follow some principles without any reference to God or scriptures. They say, why do you bring in God? If I cut your throat, how will you feel? Same way the cow also feels. And the cow is the most useful animal in the human society, the most useful animal. Even the cow dung and cow urine are useful for human beings. And such an animal they go and kill just because it stops giving milk or just because they like to eat cow flesh. ### peace of mind, Sathya Yuga, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/usmclZZ2p8k?t=657
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everyone is anxious for peace of mind but how can they get this peace of mind without cleansing the mind?"
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the lack of peace of mind in Kali Yuga and contrasts it with the complete peace in Sathya Yuga due to the presence of austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness. He notes the gradual decline of these attributes over the yugas, with truthfulness being the last remaining leg of Dharma in Kali Yuga.

In this age of Kali, people are mostly dissatisfied. So everyone is anxious for peace of mind but how can they get this peace of mind without cleansing the mind? So Srila Prabhupada says this peace of mind was complete in the Sathya Yuga, Sathya age because of the existence of the above mentioned attributes of the human beings. Everyone was austere, everyone was clean internally and externally, everyone was having mercy and everyone was truthful. So in Sathya Yuga, total peace of mind complete. Gradually these attributes have diminished in Trita Yuga to three-fourths, in Dwapar Yuga to half and in Kali Yuga to one-fourth which is also gradually diminishing on account of prevailing untruthfulness. The last leg of Dharma, truthfulness is still there in the beginning of Kali Yuga.

### Srimad Bhagavatam, Krishna's Advent, Auspiciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bed3_T287po?t=47
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The burden of the earth was certainly diminished by the personality of Godhead and by others as well."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces verses 26, 27, and 28 of the 17th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Parikshit Maharaj's inquiry to Dharma and the diminishing of the earth's burden due to Krishna's presence and auspicious footprints.

In this session, we will be discussing from 3 verses, 26, 27, and 28 of the 17th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 26th verse, the meaning of this 26th verse is: The burden of the earth was certainly diminished by the personality of Godhead and by others as well, when he was present as an incarnation. All good was performed because of his auspicious footprints. These are words spoken by Parikshit Maharaj in response to Dharma's reply. Parikshit asked Dharma, 'Who or what is the cause of your suffering?' And Dharma did not give a direct reply. He gave a very philosophical reply. So then Parikshit immediately replied, and he recognized this bull is no ordinary bull. It is the personality of religious principles. And he also understood the cow was mother earth in the shape of a cow. So now he's talking about how the burden of the earth, which was due to so many demoniac persons posing as kings at the time of Krishna's advent, just before Krishna's advent, that was definitely reduced by Krishna appearing and killing so many demons. When Krishna was present as an incarnation, all good was performed because of his auspicious footprints, merely Krishna setting foot on the surface of the earth. By that, there was all auspiciousness for everyone in the entire world.

---
### Kali Yuga, Degradation, Lamentation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bed3_T287po?t=181
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.27
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker explains verse 27, describing how Mother Earth laments her future rule by lower-class men in Kali Yuga, who lack Kshatriya qualities and exploit the innocent, contrasting it with the righteous rule of Yudhishtira Maharaj.

Then in the 27th verse, it is said here, the meaning of this 27th verse is: Now she, that is Mother Earth, the chaste one, being unfortunately forsaken by the personality of Godhead, laments her future with tears in her eyes, for now she is being ruled and enjoyed by lower class men who pose as rulers. Krishna came and the demons posing as rulers were completely destroyed and Krishna installed the most righteous Yudhishtira Maharaj as the emperor of the world. The Pandavas were ruling, but Kali Purusha is entering, Kali Yuga is beginning, and in Kali Yuga, the earth will be ruled by lower class men who pose as Kshatriyas but don't have Kshatriya qualities, and because the people and the animals, innocent people and innocent animals, will be exploited by these rulers, Mother Earth is having tears in her eyes, she's lamenting.
