Untrained lower-class men or men without ambition to protect the sufferers cannot be placed on the seat of an administrator. Unqualified person should not be sitting on the seat of the administrator. Unfortunately, in the age of Kali, the lower class men without training occupied a post of a ruler by strength of popular votes is called the democratic system. Votes, who are gets a maximum votes, majority works properly as it said in Latin by majority vote a person comes to power and instead of protecting the sufferers, these untrained people, unqualified people, create a situation quite intolerable for everyone. Such rulers illegally gratify themselves at the cost of all comforts with citizens. They collect taxes, don't use it for the citizens welfare entirely. They become themselves very rich. They misuse the tax collected in this way and they throw some crumbs for the people in the name of welfare activity. They don't do real welfare for the people and thus the chaste Mother Earth cries to see the pitiable condition of her sons, both men and animals. Innocent men and women are not allowed to live in the country.

### Parikshit Maharaj, Kali, Irreligion

**Verse References:** SB 1.17.28

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker introduces verse 28, describing Maharaj Parikshit's readiness to kill Kali, the cause of irreligion, and emphasizes that leaders should not be indifferent to the reactions of Kali Yuga.

iti dharmam maheem chaiva santvayitva maharatha nishatam adade khadgam kalaye adharma hetave. The meaning of this verse is: Maharaj Pariksit who could fight one thousand enemies single handedly thus pacified the personality of religion and the earth then he took up his sharp sword to kill the personality of Kali who is the cause of all irreligion. So Srila Prabhupada explains as described above the personality of Kali is he who deliberately commits all kinds of sinful acts which are forbidden in the revealed scriptures. There is no license to kill animals, there is no license to kill animals, innocent animals, no way, but they are doing that in the name deliberately committing sinful activities even though it is forbidden in the scriptures. This age of Kali will certainly be full of all activities of Kali, but this does not mean that the leaders of society, the executive heads, the learned and intelligent men or above all the devotees of the Lord should sit down tightly and become callous to the reactions of the age of Kali.

**Key Quote:** "The administrators should not simply pass resolutions for anti-corruption steps but they must be ready with sharp swords to kill the persons creating corruption from the angle of vision of the recognized shastras."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of taking action against the activities of Kali, using the analogy of protecting oneself from rain, and advocates for strong measures against corruption and intoxication, following the example of Maharaj Parikshit.

Sometimes people say what can we do we are following we don't kill animals we don't harm people but we cannot do anything about others indulging in animal killing or others harming innocent people we cannot do anything so it's not that I cannot control somebody else therefore I don't do anything that's not correct. So Prabhupada gives a nice example here in the rainy season certainly there will be profuse rainfalls but that does not mean that men should not take means to protect themselves from the rains very nice example you cannot stop the rain but you can take precaution for protecting yourself against the rain. Similarly it is the duty of the executive heads of state and others to take all necessary actions against the activities of Kali or the persons influenced by the age of Kali and Maharaj Parikshit is the ideal executive head of state for at once he was ready to kill the personality of Kali with his sharp sword because Kali was harming an innocent cow and an innocent bull so he was ready to kill Kali Purusha. I have an example Prabhupada is giving they must at once close all shops of intoxicating drugs and wine and force punishment even by death for those who indulge in habits of intoxication of all description as long as people are indulging in intoxication they cannot have any good behavior good principles they cannot be truthful it is not possible so government has to stop totally stop. ## SB 1.17.29-30 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 11-03-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse

### surrender, fear, kshatriya dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PQD0lukvUMQ?t=48
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That was the only way he could have saved his life otherwise Parikshit would have killed him."
**Summary:** This segment explains the 29th verse of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Kali Purusha's surrender to Maharaj Parikshit out of fear.

In this session we will be discussing from two verses, the 29th and the 30th verses of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Shreemad Bhagavatam. The 29th verse: Tam Jighamsum Abhipretya Vihaya Drupalanchanam Tadpada Moolam Shirasa Samagatbhaya Vikvalaha. The meaning of this verse is when the personality of Kali understood that the king was willing to kill him, he at once abandoned the dress, and under pressure of fear completely surrendered to him bowing his head. So what is Kali Purusha's reaction to Parikshit Maharaj being ready to kill Kali Purusha because Kali Purusha was harming an innocent cow and a bull? So Kali Purusha understood that he cannot face the king, the emperor Parikshit and he at once gave up the dress of a king. He is actually not a king, he is dressed as a king for external show and he completely surrendered to Parikshit Maharaj. That was the only way he could have saved his life otherwise Parikshit would have killed him.

So they don't have the courage, they don't have the mentality to sacrifice their life for a higher cause. That is the meaning of lower-class person. You see, no qualification, no training, no proper understanding what is the duty of a ruler, simply wants to enjoy power, enjoy some money, enjoy some facilities, exploit people, you know, send people because they are little weak, did not find their home, they can't retaliate, so exploit them. This is not a Kshatriya. So when a real Kshatriya encounters such an imposter ruler, immediately the real Kshatriya kill him if he does not give up his false position. A real Kshatriya never surrenders. This is the term. This is the real quality of a Kshatriya. A Kshatriya never surrenders. He accepts the challenge of a rival Kshatriya and he fights either to die or to win. All such battles between Kshatriyas, real Kshatriyas is decisive, just like in the battle of Kurukshetra. Duryodhana challenged the Pandavas, if you want your kingdom you fight and take. So when the battle happened then Pandavas completely destroyed all the Kauravas. 100 sons of Dhritarashtra were all killed. Because that is the way of Kshatriya. That is the Kshatriya spirit. Because Duryodhana was challenging him. Surrender is unknown to a real Kshatriya.

## SB 1.17.31-32 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 12-03-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### surrender, irreligion, welfare state
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TnkpjshcIVA?t=48
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.31
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But you cannot remain in my kingdom, for you are the friend of irreligion."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the context of Parikshit Maharaj's response to Kali Purusha's surrender, focusing on the principle of excusing a surrendered soul while preventing them from negatively influencing society.

In this session, we will be discussing from two verses, the 31st and 32nd verses of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavata. The 31st verse is as follows: Rajavacha, after the Kali Purusha surrendered to Parikshit Maharaj, when he is about to be killed, he surrendered. And what did Parikshit say? He definitely pardoned Kali Purusha, did not kill him. But what did he say? Na te gudakesha yaso dharanam baddhanjalervai bhayam astikinchit na vartitavyam bhavata katanchana kshetremadiyetvam adarmabandhuhu. This is Parikshit's reply to Kali Purusha, the action of Kali Purusha. The translation of this 31st verse: The king thus said, it's King Parikshit, 'We have inherited the fame of Arjuna. Therefore, since you have surrendered yourself with folded hands, you need not fear for your life, but you cannot remain in my kingdom, for you are the friend of irreligion.' Adarmabandhu. Hmm.

### irreligion, religious principles, welfare state

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

So, Shri La Prabhupada explains, 'The personality of Kali, who is the friend of all kinds of irreligiosities, may be excused if he surrenders, but in all circumstances, he cannot be allowed to live as a citizen in any part of a welfare state.' Those who are violators of the fundamental principles of religion, fundamental principles of religion are not based on whether somebody is Hindu or Christian or Muslim. No, it is independent of that. To practice austerity, cleanliness, truthfulness, and mercy is there in all religious systems, bonafide religious systems. Hmm. So, they have to follow that. If they become irreligious by breaking these principles, then they will not be allowed to live as a proper citizen in the kingdom or in the welfare state, being ruled by a proper administrator. Hmm. So, the principle of excusing or rather sparing the life of a surrendered soul is there. Hmm. But he cannot be allowed to live and influence the innocent people in a wrong way.

Srila Prabhupada explains further, 'The Pandavas were entrusted representatives of the personality of God and Lord Krishna, who practically brought into being the battle of Kurukshetra.' It is Krishna who arranged the battle of Kurukshetra because before the battle of Kurukshetra, Krishna was engaged in killing so many demons. But in spite of killing, Krishna killing so many demons, Krishna began killing right on the lap of his mother as a small ten day old infant. Poorthana came to kill Krishna, but Krishna delivered her. And after that so many demons came, Pakasura, Denukasura, Aghasura, Trinavarta, so many demons came, Shakatasura, so many demons. Krishna killed, killed, killed. Of course Krishna's killing is delivering them, liberating them, purifying them, but still Krishna had to deal with these demons. And Krishna went on killing, killing, killing, but the number of demons were so many, those who escaped, Krishna brought them all together on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. And through this battle of Kurukshetra, Krishna arranged for all these remaining demons to be killed to bring down the burden of the earth. So he arranged.

### ideal ruler, Krishna consciousness, duty of a ruler
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TnkpjshcIVA?t=326
**Verse References:** BG 4.1
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna wanted an ideal king like Maharaj Yudhishthira and his descendants like Maharaj Pariksit to rule the world."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes Krishna's desire for ideal rulers like Yudhishthira and Parikshit to govern the world according to the principles of Bhagavad Gita, with the primary duty of protecting citizens from material bondage by preaching Krishna consciousness.

And Pandavas, even though Yudhishthira said I am not interested in ruling a kingdom and enjoying royal power, no, even though it was his rightful claim, but he said, 'If Duryodhana wants to rule, let him rule. I don't want to unnecessarily fight and I be the cause of so much killing happening in the battle.' But Krishna wanted this battle. Why? To clear away the earth of the burden of these demons. So through the battle of Kurukshetra, Krishna arranged the remaining demons to be killed by the agency of the Pandavas. So Krishna did not have any personal interest, not that he wanted to rule or he wanted to enjoy, nothing. So Krishna, what did Krishna want? Through the battle of Kurukshetra, Krishna wanted an ideal king like Maharaj Yudhishthira and his descendants like Maharaj Pariksit to rule the world. Krishna wanted that. Krishna always wants that. It is explained in the Bhagavad Gita by Srila Prabhupada, Krishna first instructed the Bhagavad Gita to this sun god Vivaswan. Why? Because Krishna wants the rulers to rule over the citizens according to the principles of Bhagavad Gita. So the first duty of a ruler is to protect the citizens against material bondage to lust by preaching Krishna consciousness, God consciousness, making them God conscious, and thereby human life becomes successful for everyone. Everyone can live peacefully by following the religious principles, God conscious principles, and everyone is able to make spiritual progress to ultimately attain the perfection of human life simply by following the principles of Bhagavad Gita. So.

### irreligion, greed, religious principles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TnkpjshcIVA?t=586
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.32
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If the personality of Kali, irreligion, is allowed to act as a man god or an executive head, certainly irreligious principles like greed, falsehood, robbery, incivility, deceit will spread."
**Summary:** This segment focuses on the 32nd verse, explaining that allowing Kali to act as a leader will lead to the spread of negative qualities like greed and falsehood, and that the fundamental principles of religion (austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness) are universal and not limited to any specific faith.

Tvam vartamanam Naradeva Deheshu Anupravrttoyam Adharma Pugaha Lobho Anrutam Chauriyam Anaryam Jestha Chamaya Kalahascha Dambhaha. The meaning of this 32nd verse is, if the personality of Kali, irreligion, is allowed to act as a man god or an executive head, certainly irreligious principles like greed, falsehood, robbery, incivility, deceit will spread. Okay. Kali Purusha surrendered, so his life was spared, but he cannot be allowed to live. Why? If he lives, all these bad qualities will spread among the people in the kingdom of Pariksit: greed, falsehood, robbery, treachery, misfortune, that I am great without actually having the real qualities. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada says, 'The principles of religion, namely austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness, as we have already discussed, may be followed by the follower of any faith.' As regard religious principles, nobody can say Hindus. No. There is no such thing as Bhagavatam is meant for Hindus. These four principles are the fundamental principles of God consciousness, no matter which religious system somebody may follow, which bona fide religious system they may follow. The same principles are given in all scriptures. So Prabhupada says, 'There is no need to turn from Hindu to Mohammedan to Christian or some other faith and thus become a renegade and not follow the principles of religion.' Renegade means a separatist or a rebel. No need. So it is not that people in the name of I don't believe or don't accept some particular religious system. No. You cannot live in a civilized society and spread irreligion among the innocent people and thereby corrupt the atmosphere, pure atmosphere of the state. The Bhagavatam religion urges following the principles of religion.

So God is one, though different names, He may be referred to in different bonafide religious systems, but God is one. Supreme means only one person. Nobody is equal to him. Nobody is above him. ### secular state, religious principles, corruption
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TnkpjshcIVA?t=884
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The corruption is the natural consequence of people breaking the fundamental religious principles."
**Summary:** This segment explains that while a secular state is impartial to different faiths, it cannot be indifferent to fundamental religious principles like austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness, and that corruption is a natural consequence of breaking these principles.

But the state cannot be indifferent to the principles of religion as above mentioned. Secular state means the state will allow people to follow either Christianity or Islam or whatever, but they cannot break the fundamental principles like austerity, cleanliness, mercy, truthfulness. They cannot break that. They are punishable if they break these principles, irrespective of what their belief system is. In the age of Kali, the executive heads of state will be indifferent to such religious principles, and therefore under their patronage, the opponents of religious principles will follow, and so there will be no meaning to propaganda crying to stop corruption in the state. People want to root out corruption, and they are harassed by this corruption, but the corruption is the natural consequence of people breaking the fundamental religious principles. You see, there is a connection. If people don't practice austerity, somebody may think, 'Oh he doesn't believe in doing tapasya, the form of fasting, so it's okay. Let him not follow.' No. That will result in the person becoming afflicted by greed, by falsehood, by cheating, so many bad qualities the person will develop. So therefore, to protect people, the enforcement, the law enforcing authority should enforce that everyone should follow, irrespective of their religious affiliation, the four fundamental pillars of dharma: austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### irreligion, sacrifice, dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0fGa9aq5Azw?t=61
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.33
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Therefore, O friend of irreligion, you do not deserve to remain in a place where experts perform sacrifices according to truth and religious principles for the satisfaction of the Supreme Personality of Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam 1.17.33, explaining its meaning about irreligion not being allowed where sacrifices are performed for the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

In this session we will be discussing from the 33rd verse of the 17th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Navarthi Tavyam Tad Adharma Bandho Dharmena Satyencha Varthi Tavye Brahma Varte Yatra Yajanti Yajner Yajneshwaram Yajna Vitaam Vigna Vitana Vignaha. The meaning of this verse is: Therefore, O friend of irreligion, you do not deserve to remain in a place where experts perform sacrifices according to truth and religious principles for the satisfaction of the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

---
### Parikshit Maharaj, Kali Purusha, Brahmavarta
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0fGa9aq5Azw?t=114
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, he is addressing in this verse why is a personality like Kalipurusha not allowed to live in the kingdom of Pariksit Maharaj."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Parikshit Maharaj encountering Kali Purusha and explains why Kali Purusha was not allowed to stay in Parikshit's kingdom, which is described as Brahmavarta, a place where sacrifices are performed according to truth and religion.

These are words spoken by the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Shri Gautama Shetty Parijkshit Maharaj. For your refresh memory, while Parikshit was touring the kingdom, he came across this Kali Purusha trying to beat a bull and a cow. The bull was standing just on one leg, somehow hobbling along, the cow had tears in her eyes and she seemed to be unprotected and this Kali Purusha was actually a low-class person dressed as a king but his activity his action was harming the cow and the bull so immediately Parikshit took out a sword to kill this Kali Purusha but Kali Purusha actually he completely surrendered to the king when he saw Kali Purusha. there was no way he could escape. So since Kalipurusha surrendered, Pariksit did not want to kill him but he said you cannot remain in my kingdom because you are the friend of irreligion, atharma bandhu. So, he is addressing in this verse why is a personality like Kalipurusha not allowed to live in the kingdom of Pariksit Maharaj. So, the Kingdom of Pariksit Maharaj is a place where experts perform sacrifices according to truth and religion and morality, filial routine and morality, affinity and morality. So, all these principles for the satisfaction of the Supreme Personality, if you got it. Such a place is called Brahmavarta. It is not only one place, but it is wherever, any place on the surface of this globe, where the Supreme Lord is worshipped according to the principles of the authorized scriptures.

---
### sacrifice, scriptures, yugas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0fGa9aq5Azw?t=282
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Such sacrificial ceremonies are performed according to the scriptural directions, are prescribed differently in the scriptures for different ages."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the Supreme Lord is the beneficiary of all sacrifices and that the scriptures prescribe different types of sacrifices for different ages (yugas).

And that is explained in the purport by Srila Prabhupada. Agneshwara, or the Supreme Personality, if you got it, is the beneficiary of all kinds of sacrificial ceremonies. This is a statement of the Bhagavad Gita. The Supreme Lord is called Yajneshwara. Yajneshwara. He is the enjoyer, the beneficiary, the object of all sacrifices, which are performed according to the scriptural directions, which are authorized by the standard scriptures, which are not manufactured by some person of this world, no. So, Srila Prabhupada says, such sacrificial ceremonies are performed according to the scriptural directions, are prescribed differently in the scriptures for different ages. Now the shastras are recommending different types of sacrifice for different yugas in this earth planet. Different yugas, the conditions are different, the living conditions are different.

---
### Satya Yuga, meditation, spirituality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0fGa9aq5Azw?t=376
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In Satya yuga, most people are situated in the mode of goodness."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the characteristics of Satya Yuga, where people are spiritually inclined and the recommended method of sacrifice is meditation on the Supreme Lord within the heart.

In Satya yuga, most people are situated in the mode of goodness. They are all spiritually inclined, they are very much religious minded, they follow the scriptural directions. So for that age the method of sacrifice was? God. Absolutely correct. the performance of meditation on the Supreme Lord within the heart. Vishnu seated in the heart is Paramatma, meditation on Paramatma. And that was feasible in that age in Satyayuga. There was a long duration of life for people and people were very, very sober, peaceful. They were satisfied with all the necessities being met with by the arrangement of the Supreme Lord through the material nature. So even now if you go to the Himalayas, of course it is rare. But there are people who are living in certain secluded places, remote places in the Himalayas where there is plenty of fruits available, nice water for bathing, drinking, everything, pure water. And there are caves for living under the, under the, under the, under the, under the water. And they are there as a place of shelter. And then their mind is completely controlled, so they are able to direct their mind, focus their mind on meditation on this Supreme Lord in the heart as directed by the shastras. So the Bhagavatam says, kriteyadhyayato vishnum. Kriteyadhyayato vishnum. Kriteyadhyayato vishnum.

### Treta Yuga, Dwapar Yuga, Kali Yuga, sacrifice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0fGa9aq5Azw?t=531
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Yajna ultimately means to please the Supreme Lord after accepting his supremacy."
**Summary:** The speaker continues describing the methods of sacrifice for Treta Yuga (fire sacrifice), Dwapar Yuga (temple worship), and Kali Yuga (Sankirtan Yajna), emphasizing that all sacrifices aim to please the Supreme Lord.

That is the recommendation and in the second age Treta Yuga the duration of life reduces and there is some irreligiosity because with time there is a degradation in the consciousness of the people of the human beings so vice is introduced irreligion is introduced in Treta Yuga and gradually the religious principles reduce by one-fourth in Treta Yuga so the method is performance of five-fold. Fire sacrifice Treta Ayaam Yajato Makhaihi people are recommended to perform fire sacrifices as again given in the authorized Vedic scriptures and there are varieties of sacrifices for varieties of people different purposes. But ultimately all such sacrifices are aimed at satisfying the Supreme Personality of Godhead only. And in Dwapar Yuga it is temple worship Dwapare Parijari Ayaam temple worship of the Lord in different Deity forms installed Deity forms so that is the method in Dwapar Yuga and Kali Yuga Kalavata Hari Kirtanath the method is a performance of Sankirtan Yajna so Aarshala Prabhupada says in other words sacrifice means to accept the supremacy of the Lord and thereby perform acts by which the Lord may be satisfied in all respects religion means to please the Supreme Lord by the recommended method as given in the scriptures because scriptures are given by the Supreme Lord.

So Yajna ultimately means to please the Supreme Lord after accepting his supremacy he is the Supreme Personality, he is the Supreme Controller, he is Supreme Maintainer, he is Supreme well wisher, he is Supreme Lord. Every perspective you see he is the Supreme. Now let us noodle it. nobody is equal to him nobody is greater than him so therefore he is meant to be obeyed by all not that somebody believes that everyone is god or supreme or that somebody else believes there is no god or somebody else believes such and such a person is god it's not like that god means one and only one supreme personality and all bona fide religions accept this particular or all bona fide religions teach and propagate and and and also have got documented in their scriptures that there is one supreme lord who is the lord of all everyone without exception but he is described according to the people for whom the scriptures were actually written so there is only one lord he may be called as krishna or buddha in the buddhist system in the vedic system krishna of course krishna is not present in only one form the supreme lord krishna rama narsimha etc and in the christian religion he is called as christ or father in heaven and in the islam religion is allah and like that in the buddhist religion as buddha and this is uh all the religions aim at worship of this supreme lord obey his orders and ultimately as proper points out all religions ultimately aim at developing love for god that is the highest perfection of religion.

## SB 1.17.34 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 14-03-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Vedic worship, Demigods, Supreme Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1RiTxupCZR4?t=57
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Basically the followers of the Vedas, the Vedic scriptures, among them there are some who worship God as one Supreme person, like the Vaishnavas."
**Summary:** This segment explains the different types of worship within the Vedic tradition, including worship of the Supreme Lord, impersonal Brahman, and demigods.

In this session, we will be discussing from the thirty-fourth verse of the seventeenth chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is, in all sacrificial ceremonies, although sometimes a demigod is worshipped, the Supreme Lord, Personality of Godhead, is worshipped because He is the Supersoul of everyone and exists both inside and outside like the air. Thus it is He only who awards all welfare to the worshipper. This is a very important verse because a certain secret is revealed about the nature of God, because people sometimes get confused, especially in the Hindu religion as they call it. The Vedas don't describe any... where that our religious system is called a Hindu religious system, but it has come into practice to label our system as the Hindu religious system. Basically the followers of the Vedas, the Vedic scriptures, among them there are some who worship God. They worship God. They worship God as one Supreme person, like the Vaishnavas. And there are impersonalists who worship God as the Supreme Brahman, impersonal Brahman. They don't exactly describe what is God or who is God. They basically say God is without form, without any qualities, without any attributes, Nirmisesha, Nirvikara, Nirmikalpa, like that. They go on describing in a negative way and ultimately they don't say what is God. And then there are some people who worship a representation of God, which is the Supreme Lord, the Supreme Beholder. And there are some who worship God as the Supreme God, a material representation in the scriptures they are called devatas or as Prabhupada says demigods. Demigods means almost like God but not equal to God so demigods of worship like Indra Chandra, varuna or Ganesha or Shiva or Durga in so many forms. Durga is worshipped in so many forms, Shiva is worshipped in so many forms, the other devatas are worshiped and represented by sometimes even the elements like fire, fire, fire or even bodies of water, worshipping the river, or worshipping a tree, or worshipping the ocean, worshipping fire, worshipping the sun, worshipping the moon, etc. So, all these different types of worship are in a nutshell explained here by Srila Prabhupada.

---
### Demigod worship, Sacrifice, Material benefit
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1RiTxupCZR4?t=312
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In the scriptures, there is a recommendation that somebody may worship a particular devata for a particular purpose, for a particular material benefit."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the worship of demigods for specific material benefits, mentioning various deities and associated sacrifices.

So, Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, it is even sometimes seen that demigods like Indra and Chandra worshipped and offered sacrificial awards. In the scriptures, there is a recommendation that somebody may worship a particular devata for a particular purpose, for a particular material benefit. And the sacrifices offered in the name of such devata, Indraya Swaha or Agnaya Swaha, like that, different demigods. There are so many demigods. Actually, according to our authentic Vedic scriptures, there are 33 crore devatas, though most people do not worship or do not know about 33 crore devatas, but some prominent ones are worshipped. Especially nowadays, you can even see temples dedicated for worship of deities of Shiva, Durga, Ganesha, Kartikeya. And, of course, the Vaishnavas worship Krishna, Rama, Narsimha, Varaha, Vamana. So many different temples for these deities. And even sacrifices, fire sacrifices for pleasing Narsimha, Narsimha Homa, or for pleasing Krishna as Krishna Yagna, or Ramathar Ka Homa, like that. So many different types of worship of different forms of the Supreme Lord, Krishna, Rama, Narsimha, Varaha. Authorized. And then the forms of the demigods.

---
### Supreme Lord, Paramatma, Vedic Directions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1RiTxupCZR4?t=450
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Because it is the Supreme Lord who is situated in the heart of everyone, including the Devatas."
**Summary:** This segment explains that the Supreme Lord is the ultimate giver of rewards, as He is present in everyone's heart, including the demigods.

Like Indra, Chandra, Varuna, Vayu. So Srila Prabhupada says, yet the rewards of all such sacrifices are awarded to the worshipper by the Supreme Lord only. If somebody follows the Vedic directions and worships either a deity of Ganesha or performs a sacrifice for pleasing Ganesha, Gana Homa, they say, Ganapati Homa. In any case, the ultimate reward is given only by the Supreme Lord Krishna. How is that? Because it is the Supreme Lord who is situated in the heart of everyone, including the Devatas. And he is everywhere present in the heart of everyone. He has entered into everything. Vishnu means one who enters into everything after his creation takes place. The Supreme Lord expands as Paramatma and enters into the heart of all living entities and even enters into every atom, material atom. Andantarastha paramanu chayantarastham. He enters the created universe. He enters every planet. He enters the heart of every created being. He enters into every material atom, Paramanu. Anda means the universal shell, Vishwatma. So, like that, the Supreme Lord as Paramatma is present in everyone's heart, everywhere, even the Devata's heart, Indra, Chandra, Varuna, Vayu, everyone's heart. The Supreme Lord is present as Paramatma. And it is this Lord who is present in the heart of everyone, who is also present as the maintainer Vishnu, who is also present as Maha Vishnu, the origin of this creation itself, the creator, maintainer and destroyer of all the universes and present in the spiritual world as Narayana, as Krishna. So, that Supreme Lord is present in everyone's heart, is present everywhere, is in the spiritual world, is in the material world. So, he actually is the only one who can avoid the results of all such bona fide sacrifices. He is the one who maintains everyone. He is the one who accepts the sacrifices, if it is offered according to the Vedic directions. So, when somebody offers a sacrifice to Indra, it is through Indra that they are actually offering to the Supreme Lord.

---
### Bhagavad Gita, Supreme Lord, Demigod worship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/1RiTxupCZR4?t=652
**Verse References:** BG 9.23, BG 9.24
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That whatever a man may sacrifice to other gods, gods here mean demigods, O son of Kunti is really meant for me alone."
**Summary:** This segment uses verses from the Bhagavad Gita to clarify that all sacrifices are ultimately meant for the Supreme Lord, even when offered to demigods.

So, in the Bhagavad Gita this is clarified. Bhagavad Gita is the final scripture which clarifies all such apparent confusions or contradictions in the directions given in the original Vedas. Bhagavad Gita clarifies this. Bhagavad Gita it is said, that whatever a man may sacrifice to other gods, gods here mean demigods, O son of Kunti is really meant for me alone. Me, Krishna is speaking. It is meant for Krishna only, but it is offered without true understanding. Yajante avidhi purvakam. They are not following the proper method of offering the sacrifice, because sacrifice means to ultimately please the Yajneshwara, the Supreme Lord of all sacrifices. That is the Supreme Lord Vishnu. Vishnu. Yajna vai Vishnu. Yajna means Vishnu. So, there is only one Supreme Lord. He is one without a second. Ekameva advitiya. This is the statement of the Vedas. One only. Supreme Lord is one only. There is no other God other than the Supreme Lord himself. Thus the Supreme Lord is eternally transcendental to the material creation. This is a very important point. There is this material creation consisting of different planets, different personalities, and different powers, different heads of the rulers, head of the particular living entities in a particular planet, the chief of the planet, planetary systems. Indra is the king of heaven, and then there is Indraloka. Then there is Chandra, the moon god, the predominant deity of the moon planet. The sun god, Suryadeva, predominant deity of the sun planet. Like that there are so many planets, so many personalities. But Krishna is above this material creation, and he is the lord of everyone. He is seated in everyone's heart, Suryadeva's heart, Chandra Deva's heart, Ganesha's heart, and Shiva's heart, Parvati or Durga Devi's heart. He is seated in heart of everyone. He is the lord of everyone. He is the lord of everyone. He is above everybody. So Prabhupada says the demigods are indirect qualitative representation of supreme lord. So a learned devotee or a learned scholar in the Vedas knows who is who. He knows what is the position of the devatas and what is the position of supreme lord Krishna. So the really learned people they worship only Krishna, but others may worship Indra, Chandra, Varuna, etc. as a representative of Krishna, accepting on behalf of Krishna and passing on to Krishna the supreme lord. So this is the correct understanding about the recommendations for worship of Indra, Chandra, Ganesha, Shiva, etc. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## SB 1.17.35 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 15-03-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Srimad Bhagavatam, Sūta Goswāmī, Parīkṣit Mahārāja, Śukadeva Goswāmī
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mzx64cBygH0?t=35
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So all the chapters practically in the first canto are descriptions of Parikshit Maharaj, Shukadev Goswami and the meeting, how the meeting happened, the events leading to that."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces the verses to be discussed, focusing on Sūta Goswāmī's narration to the sages of Naimiṣāraṇya about the events leading to the meeting between Parīkṣit Mahārāja and Śukadeva Goswāmī.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Deviim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing from three verses, verses 35, 36 and 37 of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Sūta Uvāca Parīkṣitāivam ādiṣṭaha Sakalir jātavē patuhu Tam udyatāsim āhedam Dandapāṇim ivaudhyatam. The meaning of this verse is Śrī Sūta Goswāmī said. Now this is a verse spoken by Sūta Goswāmī who is describing these events leading to Parīkṣit Mahārāja meeting Śukadeva Goswāmī. Remember the Bhagavatam began with a conversation between Sūta Goswāmī and the sages of the Sūta Goswāmī. The words Sūnai Mishār 달라, so Sūta Goswāmī is narrating the events leading to the original Bhagavatam is spoken by Shukadeva Goswāmī to Parīkṣit Mahārāja. The same Bhagavatam is going to be recited by Sūta Goswāmī to the sage Sūnai Mishārana, but before reciting the events, the Bhagavatam is mentioned that first and foremost, the sages have requested him kindly tell us something about who is Parikshit Maharaj, who is Shukadev Goswami, how did they meet each other. So all the chapters practically in the first canto are descriptions of Parikshit Maharaj, Shukadev Goswami and the meeting, how the meeting happened, the events leading to that.

### Kali Yuga, Irreligion, Sinful Activities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sa8LGWNdsiw?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.38
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Parikṣit Mahārāja gave permission to Kali to reside in a place where there is gambling, drinking, prostitution and animal slaughter were performed."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 38th verse of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining how Parikṣit Mahārāja allowed Kali to reside in places where gambling, drinking, prostitution, and animal slaughter occur, which are the sources of irreligion.

Om Gyanati Miran Dhasya Gyanan Janishalakaya Chaksharun Militam Yena Tasvain Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnu Padaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Paschatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shri Vasadi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 38th verse of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Sūta uvāca abhyartitas tadātasmai sthānāni kalaye dadau tyūtaṁ pānam sthriya-sūnā yatra adharmas chatur vidhahā. Sūta Goswami said, Mahārāja Parikṣit, thus being petitioned by the personality of Kali, gave him permission to reside in places where gambling, drinking, prostitution and animal slaughter were performed. This is a very, very, very important verse in the entire Bhagavatam, I would say, because this describes the four places where Kali was given permission to reside by Parikṣit Mahārāja, where life was spared. And who is Kali? Kali is the one who spreads irreligion. He spreads evil influence. He influences people in an evil way to commit sinful activities even though they may not be sinful otherwise. So what are those four places? Parikṣit Mahārāja gave permission to Kali to reside in a place where there is gambling, drinking, prostitution and animal slaughter were performed. Parikṣit Mahārāja gave permission to Kali to reside in a place where there is gambling, drinking, prostitution and animal slaughter were performed.

Suna animal killing for the sake of eating the animal flesh so these four places were the places designated by Pariksit Maharaj for Kali Purusha to reside after his life was spent which means that in Kali Yuga wherever these four activities are going on is a place where Kali breeds all kinds of irreligiosity means breaking the the principles of Dharma and thereby committing various types of sinful activities and becoming implicated in sinful reactions to some of the activities that are going on in Kali Yuga so if at all there is any suffering it is only because of sin and sin means to break the principles of Dharma and this Dharma is given by the Supreme Lord and where is it explained over is it described in the authentic scriptures again given by God and anybody who violates they are actually punished and Kali Purusha particularly in this Age the influence of the the atmosphere prevailing atmosphere is an evil god one of a kind influence Kali Kalmasa it is described the Kali Santra Upanishad especially to overcome the bad influence the evil influence in Kali Yuga of committing sinful activities or becoming implicated in different types of sinful activities or becoming victim of these four sinful activities gambling consuming intoxication prostitution and animal slaughter animal killing so these four sinful activities particularly are the places designated for Kali.

**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Fiwg9d4D8SA?t=43
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.38
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Maharaj Pariksit, thus being petitioned by the personality of Kali, gave him permission to reside in places where gambling, drinking, prostitution and animal slaughter were performed."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the session, focusing on Srimad Bhagavatam 1.17.38, explaining how Maharaj Pariksit allowed Kali to reside in places of gambling, drinking, prostitution, and animal slaughter.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam. Devi Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayam Udhirayam. In this session we will be discussing from the 38th verse of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This is a fresh session which is continuing from the previous session. Previous session we discussed part 1 of this 38th verse, now we will discuss part 2 and in the next session we will discuss part 3 because there is a very big purport here written by Srila Prabhupada. So I will just read this verse once again, 38th verse of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, Suta Vacha Abhyarthitas Tadatasmai Sthanani Kalaye Dadau Deutam Panam Striya Suna Yatra Adharma Chatur Vidhah. The meaning of this verse is, Suta Goswami said, Maharaj Pariksit, thus being petitioned by the personality of Kali, gave him permission to reside in places where gambling, drinking, prostitution and animal slaughter were performed. So Maharaj Pariksit gave Kali Purusha four places, Deutam means gambling, Panam means drinking, Pras and Striya means prostitution and Suna means animal slaughter.

### Dharma, Adharma, scriptural injunctions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Fiwg9d4D8SA?t=158
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.38
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Drinking against the principles of scriptures, association with women outside of marriage, killing animals against the injunctions of scriptures, are irreligious."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the basic principles of irreligiosity and how they counteract the principles of religion, referencing Srila Prabhupada's purport and Srila Jiva Goswami's commentary, emphasizing that actions like drinking, illicit relations, and animal slaughter are adharma only when against scriptural injunctions.

So we discussed in the previous session that the basic principles of irreligiosity, they counteract the four principles of religion. And we discussed how counteraction happens in the previous session. Now, Srila Prabhupada says in the purport further, the personality of Kali was given permission to live in four places particularly mentioned by the king. Namely, the place of gambling, the place of prostitution, the place of drinking, and the place of animal slaughter. Four specific places were given for Kali Purusha by Maharaj Pariksit. So, now something about what is Dharma and what is Adharma as regards these four activities are specifically explained by Srila Prabhupada. With reference to Srila Jeeva Goswami's commentary on this verse in his writings. Srila Jeeva Goswami is one of the six Goswamis, the direct disciples of Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. So, Srila Prabhupada says in the next part of the purport, Srila Jeeva Goswami directs the drunken people and the unmarried woman with the five principles of the scriptures that drinking against the principles of scriptures, such as the Sautramaaniyagna, association with woman outside of marriage, killing animals against the injunctions of scriptures, are irreligious. Irreligious means Adharma. Three things are specifically mentioned. Drinking against the principles of scriptures, association with women outside marriage, killing animals against the injunction of scriptures are all adharma or all irreligious.

---
### scriptural injunctions, pravrittas, nivrittas, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Fiwg9d4D8SA?t=309
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Vedic injunction for the pravartas, the ordinary people those engaged in material enjoyment is to gradually regulate their activities towards the path of liberation."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that these acts are irreligious only if against the scriptures, explaining what scriptures allow in terms of drinking, association with women, and killing animals, differentiating between injunctions for 'pravrittas' (materially engaged) and 'nivrittas' (liberated), emphasizing that Vedic injunctions aim to regulate activities towards liberation.

Now we have to carefully understand what does it mean that these acts are irreligious only if they are against the scriptures and what do scriptures allow in terms of drinking, illicit connection with women and or rather association with women and killing animals. What do scriptures allow? scriptures say so Prabhupada first of all clarifies in the Vedas two different types of injunctions are there for the pravrittas and for the nivrittas so dharma or scriptural injunctions are there differently explained for two different categories of persons who are the pravrittas pravrittas are those who are engaged in material enjoyment and nivrittas are those who are liberated for from material bondage so pravrittas who are regular ordinary people they are somehow in ignorance and they are engaged in material enjoyment so what is the scriptural injunctions of practicing Dharma for the normal people ordinary people the pravartas those engaged in material enjoyment and what are those scriptural injunctions specifically meant for nivrittas or those liberated from material bondage there are different injunctions so Srila Prabhupada further says the Vedic injunction for the pravartas the ordinary people those engaged in material enjoyment is to gradually regulate their activities towards the path of liberation so the injunctions in this scriptural injunctions are the same as the pravartas scriptures are meant to regulate the activities of the ordinary common people so that these common people progress towards liberation not that they follow the scriptures and they remain in bondage no the aim of the Vedas whether it is for the pravartas or nivrittas is ultimately to free them from material bondage and to help them progress after becoming liberated to go back to Godhead we should not forget this merely attaining liberation from material bondage is not enough there is a further progress to be made after liberation to go back to Godhead where we all belong.

it's not meant for everyone so these pravrittas for whom such allowance is given are those in the lowest stage of ignorance they are tamasic people they are situated in tamoguna so it is not meant for those who are not in tamoguna it's not meant for those in rajoguna or sattvaguna no so when the people who are thumbsick they indulge in drinking liquor or eating flesh animal flesh or they indulge in yajna or they may associate with women or they may associate with women or they may associate with women or they may after getting married to them according to the Vedic injunctions so for such thumbsick people under Vedic regulation which is meant for thumbsick people pravrittas there is no consideration of sin I'm sorry there's no consideration of being punished by being sent to hell if they kill a goat and offer it in sacrifice to Kali or they offer wine in a Sautramani yajna and then partake of the remnants of such offering or they indulge in sex with a woman after being duly ### modes of nature, adharma, regulation, scriptural interpretation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Fiwg9d4D8SA?t=772
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.38
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One man's food may be poisoned for others similarly what is recommended for those in the mode of ignorance may be poisoned for those in the mode of goodness."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing that these injunctions are only for tamasic people, not for everyone, and that what is recommended for those in ignorance can be detrimental to those in goodness, stressing the importance of careful scriptural interpretation and that these allowances are not for sattvic or rajasic individuals, referencing Srila Prabhupada's clarification on the purport of verse 38.

Do not pray to Lord Shiva for anything else but only to improve one's health in hell so these injunctions in the scriptures are only for thumbsick people it is not meant for every one so Shruthi Prabhupada says one man's food may be poisoned for others similarly what is recommended for those in the mode of ignorance may be poisoned for those in the mode of goodness Srila Jiva Goswami Prabhupada says that recommendations in the scriptures for certain class of men are never to be considered adharma or irreligious for them but in general for people who are not in the tamasic stage all such activities are never to be encouraged and they are factually adharma for sattvic people and rajasic people the recommendations like soutramani yajna etc are actually not to be followed for them all sorts of recommendations like that are not to be followed for them all sorts of meat-eating and all sorts of drinking liquor are prohibited so therefore one has to be very careful that simply because the shastras allow for a certain class of people some animal sacrifice or offering of wine in some yagna and then partaking the remnants doesn't mean everyone can do it no so this clarification Srila Prabhupada is giving in the purport to this particular verse the 38th verse which says that Kali Purusha was given four places and one has to be careful and the later verses and purports will clarify this more I will stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna.

### dharma, irreligion, statecraft
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8VRwECv5WuY?t=45
**Verse References:** SB 1.17.38
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Following in the footsteps of Maharaj Pariksit, it is the duty of all executive heads of states to see that the principles of religion namely austerity, cleanliness, mercy and truthfulness are established in the state and that the principles of irreligion namely pride, illicit female association of prostitution, intoxication and falsity are checked by all means."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of discussion, verse 38 of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on the four sinful activities and the duty of the state to uphold Dharma by promoting austerity, cleanliness, mercy, and truthfulness, while curbing pride, illicit relations, intoxication, and falsity.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Devi Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we continue to discuss from the 38th verse of the 17th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This session will be part 3 of the discussion on the 38th verse. Previous two sessions we discussed different aspects of these four sinful activities: Deutam Panam Striya Suna, gambling, drinking liquor, illicit connection with women, and animal slaughter, animal killing for the sake of eating the animal flesh. These four sinful activities we discussed in the previous two sessions. Prabhupada now is in the last part of this purport. He is describing what is the duty of the executioner. The duty of the executioner is to protect the executive heads of state to actually counteract this evil influence of Kali so that there is religiosity which is Dharma is protected in the state. So Prabhupada says following in the footsteps of Maharaj Pariksit it is the duty of all executive heads of states to see that the principles of religion namely austerity, cleanliness, mercy and truthfulness, tapasyaucham, dayasatyam are established in the state and that the principles of irreligion namely pride, illicit female association of prostitution, intoxication and falsity are checked by all means.

**Summary:** The speaker explains that because Pariksit Maharaj ruled strictly, there were no places for sinful activities in his kingdom. He then discusses how those addicted to sinful habits can be regulated by scriptural injunctions, but the state should never encourage such activities.
